Using the Wiebe function with parameters $a = 5$, $m = 2$, and $\Delta\theta = 45°$ CA, determine: - (i) The start of combustion angle $\theta_0$ (assumed equal to first detectable pressure rise). - (ii) The crank angle at which **50% MFB** occurs. - (iii) The total combustion duration in milliseconds. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### **Solution (a) — Three Phases of Combustion (7 marks)** **Phase 1 — Ignition Delay ($\theta_{id}$):** The ignition delay begins when the spark plug discharges and ends when a self-propagating flame kernel is established. During this interval: - The spark creates a high-temperature plasma kernel (**~60,000 K**). - Heat is transferred from the kernel to surrounding gases by conduction and radiation. - Chemical chain-branching reactions (involving H, O, OH radicals) are initiated. - The kernel grows until it reaches the **critical flame radius** (~0.5 – 1.0 mm), after which it can self-sustain. Mathematically, $\tau_{id}$ follows Arrhenius kinetics: $$\tau_{id} = A \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \phi^{m} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R T}\right)$$ Typical duration: **0.5 – 2 ms** or **2° – 8° CA**. **Phase 2 — Rapid Combustion / Flame Propagation ($\theta_{rc}$):** Once a stable flame kernel forms, it expands spherically as a thin flame front. In a real engine, this front is wrinkled and accelerated by in-cylinder turbulence. - The flame front propagates with velocity $S_T = S_L + u'$. - $S_L$ ≈ 0.4 m/s for methane/air at stoichiometric conditions. - $u'$ ≈ 2 – 10 m/s in turbulent engines. - $S_T$ ≈ 15 – 30 m/s. About **80% of the fuel mass** is burned during this phase. Duration: **20° – 45° CA**. The peak cylinder pressure occurs near the end of rapid combustion. **Phase 3 — After-Burning / Late Combustion ($\theta_{ab}$):** The remaining ~20% of fuel burns slowly due to: - **Wall quenching**: A thin gas layer (0.1 – 0.3 mm) adjacent to chamber walls is cooled below flame temperature and escapes complete combustion. - **Crevice volumes**: Piston ring crevices and spark plug threads trap unburned mixture that escapes combustion but later exhausts as **HC emissions**. - **End gas**: Mixture trapped behind the flame front in pockets burns slowly at lower local temperatures. This phase is the **largest contributor to HC and CO emissions**. Duration: **15° – 60° CA past TDC**. **Summary Table:** | Phase | Typical CA | Mass Burned | Primary Concern | |---|---|---|---| | Ignition Delay | 2 – 8° | < 1% | Cyclic variability | | Rapid Combustion | 20 – 45° | ~80% | Knock, peak pressure | | After-Burning | 15 – 60° | ~20% | HC and CO emissions | *(7 marks: 1.5 marks for each phase discussion + 1 mark for summary table)* --- #### **Solution (b) — Wiebe Function Numerical Problem (7 marks)** **Given:** - $N = 2400$ rpm - Combustion begins at $\theta_0 = -20°$ (20° BTDC) - 95% MFB at $\theta_{95} = +35°$ ATDC - $a = 5$, $m = 2$, $\Delta\theta = 45°$ CA **Part (i) — Start of combustion angle:** The start of combustion is given directly: $$\theta_0 = -20° \;\text{(i.e., 20° BTDC)}$$ **Part (ii) — Crank angle for 50% MFB:** For 50% MFB: $x_b = 0.5$ $$0.5 = 1 - \exp\!\left[-5 \left(\frac{\theta_{50} - (-20°)}{45°}\right)^{3}\right]$$ $$\exp\!\left[-5 \left(\frac{\theta_{50} + 20}{45}\right)^{3}\right] = 0.5$$ Take natural log of both sides: $$-5 \left(\frac{\theta_{50} + 20}{45}\right)^{3} = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931$$ $$\left(\frac{\theta_{50} + 20}{45}\right)^{3} = \frac{0.6931}{5} = 0.1386$$ Take cube root: $$\frac{\theta_{50} + 20}{45} = (0.1386)^{1/3} = 0.5186$$ $$\theta_{50} + 20 = 45 \times 0.5186 = 23.34°$$ $$\boxed{\theta_{50} = +3.34° \;\text{ATDC}}$$ This is excellent — **3.34° ATDC** is within the optimal **8° – 12° ATDC window** for maximum efficiency. **Part (iii) — Total combustion duration in milliseconds:** The total duration in crank angle is: $$\theta_{cd} = \theta_{95} - \theta_0 = 35° - (-20°) = 55° \;\text{CA}$$ Convert using: $$\tau_{cd} = \frac{\theta_{cd}}{6N} = \frac{55°}{6 \times 2400} = \frac{55}{14400}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{cd} = 3.819 \;\text{ms}}$$ *(7 marks: 1 + 3 + 2 + 1 for systematic steps)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Question Paper 2024]` **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Wiebe function** and its parameters. Show how the mass burn rate $\frac{dx_b}{d\theta}$ is obtained by differentiating the function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain how the following factors affect combustion duration in HCSI engines: **(7 marks)** - (i) Engine speed - (ii) Spark timing - (iii) Turbulence intensity - (iv) Equivalence ratio - (v) EGR - (vi) Compression ratio - (vii) Boost pressure --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### **Solution (a) — Wiebe Function Derivation (7 marks)** **Definition and Sketch:** The Wiebe function is an S-shaped empirical curve describing the fraction of fuel mass burned as a function of crank angle. **Equation:** $$x_b(\theta) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-a \left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]$$ **Sketch (textual representation):** ``` x_b 1.0 |- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - . . . . . . | . 0.9 |- - - - - - - - - - - - - . <-- 90% MFB | . 0.5 |- - - - - - - - - . . . . <-- 50% MFB | . 0.1 |- - . . . . . . <-- 10% MFB | . <-- theta 0 (SOC) 0.0 |._____________________________________ theta_0 theta_50 theta_90 theta (crank angle) ``` **Parameter Description:** | Parameter | Role | |---|---| | $a = 5$ | Sets asymptote near unity; ensures $x_b \to 1$ as $\theta \to \infty$ | | $m = 2$ to 3 | Controls steepness — higher $m$ gives more S-shaped curve | | $\theta_0$ | Crank angle at start of combustion (SOC) | | $\Delta\theta$ | Effective combustion duration | **Differentiation — Mass Burn Rate:** Let $\phi = \frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta}$. Then $x_b = 1 - e^{-a\phi^{m+1}}$. Using chain rule: $$\frac{dx_b}{d\theta} = -\frac{d}{d\theta}\left[e^{-a\phi^{m+1}}\right]$$ $$= -e^{-a\phi^{m+1}} \cdot \frac{d}{d\theta}\left[-a\phi^{m+1}\right]$$ $$= e^{-a\phi^{m+1}} \cdot a(m+1)\phi^m \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta\theta}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\frac{dx_b}{d\theta} = a(m+1)\frac{(\theta - \theta_0)^m}{\Delta\theta^{m+1}} \exp\!\left[-a\left(\frac{\theta-\theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]}$$ This represents a **bell-shaped curve** that peaks near $\theta_{50}$ and decays to zero at $\theta_0$ and at the end of combustion. *(7 marks: 2 marks for definition + 2 for sketch/parameters + 3 for derivation)* --- #### **Solution (b) — Factors Affecting Combustion Duration (7 marks)** **(i) Engine Speed:** - As $N$ increases, available time decreases ($\tau \propto 1/N$). - However, **crank angle duration remains nearly constant** (typically 50° – 70° CA). - Higher speed reduces wall quenching because there is less time for heat loss. **(ii) Spark Timing:** - Advancing spark timing (earlier $\theta_0$) shifts the entire combustion window earlier. - Allows more time for combustion before TDC, but increases **knock tendency**. - Retarded timing reduces effective combustion pressure and efficiency. **(iii) Turbulence Intensity:** - Higher turbulence intensity $u'$ increases turbulent flame speed $S_T = S_L + u'$. - Shorter $\theta_{rc}$, more complete combustion, lower HC/CO. - Achieved through intake port design, squish, and tumble. **(iv) Equivalence Ratio:** - Maximum flame speed at **$\phi = 1.05$ – 1.10** (slightly rich). - Lean burn ($\phi < 1$) reduces $S_L$ → **longer combustion duration**. - Very rich mixtures also slow combustion due to fuel deficiency. **(v) EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation):** - EGR dilutes the charge with inert combustion products. - Lowers flame temperature and slows chemical kinetics. - **Significantly increases** $\theta_{cd}$ — used as a NO$_x$ control strategy at the cost of HC/CO. **(vi) Compression Ratio:** - Higher CR increases peak cylinder $T$ and $p$. - Accelerates chemical kinetics, reducing $\theta_{id}$. - Modest reduction in overall $\theta_{cd}$; main benefit is higher **Otto efficiency**. **(vii) Boost Pressure (Turbocharging):** - Higher intake pressure → higher charge density. - Faster flame kernel growth, shorter $\theta_{id}$. - Enables shorter overall $\theta_{cd}$ even at high load. *(7 marks: 1 mark each for the seven factors)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's View):** > 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers: CAD values must include "°" sign; time values must include "ms" or "s". Marks deducted if units are missing. > 2. **Mixing ATDC and BTDC signs**: Always use **negative** for BTDC and **positive** for ATDC when substituting into Wiebe equations. > 3. **Incorrect Wiebe exponent**: The exponent is **$m+1$**, not $m$. A common mistake is using $m$ alone, which changes the curve shape. > 4. **Confusing "apparent" with "net" heat release**: Apparent HRR (from first law of thermodynamics) is used in Wiebe-based analysis. Wall heat losses are NOT subtracted in apparent HRR. > 5. **Skipping the differentiation step in Wiebe derivation**: Examiners expect all three chain-rule steps explicitly written. > 6. **Ignoring EGR's effect**: Many students wrongly assume EGR has no effect on $\theta_{cd}$. It is one of the **most significant** factors in modern HCSI engines. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Combustion Duration in HCSI Engines** - **Definition**: Combustion duration = total crank angle from SOC to ~99% MFB. - **Three Phases**: $\theta_{id}$ (ignition delay) + $\theta_{rc}$ (rapid combustion) + $\theta_{ab}$ (after-burning). - **Typical Values**: $\theta_{id} \approx$ **2° – 8° CA**, $\theta_{rc} \approx$ **20° – 45° CA**, $\theta_{ab} \approx$ **15° – 60° CA**. - **Total**: $\theta_{cd} \approx$ **40° – 70° CA** (modern HCSI engines). - **Wiebe Function**: $x_b(\theta) = 1 - \exp[-a((\theta - \theta_0)/\Delta\theta)^{m+1}]$. - **Standard Parameters**: $a = 5.0$, $m = 2$ to 3. - **Mass Burn Rate**: Bell-shaped curve peaking near $\theta_{50}$. - **Optimal 50% MFB**: **8° – 12° ATDC** for best efficiency. - **Turbulent Flame Speed**: $S_T = S_L + u'$ ≈ **15 – 30 m/s**. - **Time-to-CA Conversion**: $\tau = \theta / (6N)$. - **Arrhenius Ignition Delay**: Higher $p$ and $T$ shorten $\theta_{id}$. - **EGR increases $\theta_{cd}$** — primary NO$_x$ control strategy. - **Turbulence shortens $\theta_{rc}$** — primary efficiency strategy. - **After-burning** is the **major HC/CO contributor** due to wall quench and crevice effects. - **Apparent HRR** uses $dQ_B/d\theta = m_f \cdot Q_{LHV} \cdot \eta_c \cdot dx_b/d\theta$. - **Wiebe exponent is $(m+1)$**, NOT $m$ — examiner trap. - **Real-world use**: Wiebe parameters feed directly into GT-Power, AVL FIRE, Ricardo WAVE, and ECU calibration tables.

topics.title: Hydrogen in Spark-Ignited Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hydrogen in Spark-Ignited Engines # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydrogen (H₂) as a Spark-Ignition (SI) Engine Fuel** is defined as a zero-carbon, gaseous, synthetic alternative fuel that undergoes externally initiated, premixed, homogeneous combustion inside a conventional Otto-cycle engine, ignited by an electrical spark source and characterized by an unusually wide flammability envelope, extremely high flame velocity, and a high auto-ignition temperature that virtually eliminates knock but introduces pre-ignition, backfire, and abnormal combustion phenomena. In the strict lexicon of the KTU 2024 Advanced IC Engines syllabus, hydrogen occupies the position of the **"ultimate clean SI fuel"** because its sole combustion product in lean or stoichiometric air mixtures is **water vapor (H₂O)**, with **zero CO₂, zero unburnt hydrocarbons (UHC), and negligible CO emissions**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of gasoline as a slow-burning campfire log** — it takes time, needs careful airflow, and smoulders irregularly. **Hydrogen, by contrast, behaves like a flash-powder firework** — the moment a spark touches the mixture, the flame front races across the cylinder in a fraction of the time, almost like a controlled explosion rather than a steady burn. This "firework-like" behaviour is what makes hydrogen such a *paradoxical* SI fuel: - **Brilliant in flame speed and lean-burn capability** (good for thermal efficiency). - **Difficult to contain in a slow engine** (causes backfire, pre-ignition, pressure-rise-rate spikes). ## 1.3 Fundamental Physical & Chemical Properties of Hydrogen | Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Gasoline (Reference) | |----------|---------------|----------------------| | Molecular Weight | **2.016 kg/kmol** | ~110 kg/kmol | | Density (NTP) | **0.0838 kg/m³** | ~750 kg/m³ | | Auto-Ignition Temp. | **571 °C (845 K)** | ~250 °C | | Flammability Limits (φ) | **0.146 – 9.0 (Equiv. Ratio)** | 0.7 – 1.4 | | Flammability Limits (vol %) | **4.0 – 75 %** in air | 1.0 – 7.6 % | | Laminar Flame Speed | **2.5 – 3.5 m/s** | 0.4 m/s | | Min. Ignition Energy | **0.02 mJ** | 0.25 mJ | | Quenching Distance | **0.6 mm** | ~2.0 mm | | LHV (Mass) | **120 MJ/kg** | 44 MJ/kg | | LHV (Volume, NTP) | **8.5 MJ/m³** | 31,200 MJ/m³ | | Octane Number (RON) | **> 130** | 90 – 98 | | Diffusivity in Air | **0.61 × 10⁻⁴ m²/s** | 0.05 × 10⁻⁴ m²/s | | Flame Temperature (stoich) | **2407 K** | ~2300 K | > [!NOTE] > **Universal Constants Used:** > - Universal Gas Constant, **R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** > - Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric), **A/F_st = 34.3 : 1 (by mass)** > - Ratio of Specific Heats, **γ (Air) = 1.40**, **γ (H₂) = 1.41** ## 1.4 Why Hydrogen in SI Engines? — The Engineering Motivation 1. **Zero carbon emissions** — addresses climate change directly. 2. **High Octane Rating (>130 RON)** — enables high compression ratio (CR up to 13:1) without knock. 3. **Wide flammability range** — permits ultra-lean combustion (φ as low as 0.146), boosting efficiency. 4. **Renewable production** — Green H₂ from electrolysis using solar/wind. 5. **Fast flame speed** — near-complete constant-volume combustion, approaching ideal Otto cycle. 6. **High diffusivity** — homogeneous mixture formation in milliseconds. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hydrogen Combustion Chemistry The stoichiometric combustion reaction of hydrogen with air is: $$ 2H_2 + (O_2 + 3.76N_2) \longrightarrow 2H_2O + 3.76N_2 $$ - **Stoichiometric mass ratio (A/F):** **34.3 : 1** (hydrogen is *extremely* lean — 34 kg of air per 1 kg of H₂). - This is the root cause of the **low volumetric energy density** problem in SI engines. ### 2.1.1 Energy Density Paradox Although hydrogen has **2.7× the energy per kg** of gasoline, its **energy per unit volume is roughly 1/2700** of gasoline. Hence, an unmodified SI engine running on pure H₂ will lose **~50 – 60 % of its rated power** simply because the cylinder cannot inhale enough fuel molecules to fully displace the air charge. **Mitigation Strategies (KTU-High-Yield):** - **Supercharging / Turbocharging** — packs more H₂ mass into the cylinder. - **Liquid hydrogen (LH₂) injection** — cryogenic, 1/700 the volume of gaseous H₂ at NTP. - **High-pressure storage (350 – 700 bar)** — boosts mass density. - **Water injection** — cools charge, increases mass of H₂ per cycle. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Stoichiometric A/F (mass) | $(A/F)_{st} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = 34.3$ | kg/kg | Fixed by chemistry | | 2 | Stoichiometric A/F (molar) | $(A/F)_{mol,st} = 2.39$ | kmol/kmol | For volumetric calculations | | 3 | Energy per kg mixture | $Q_{mix} = \dfrac{Q_{LHV}}{\lambda \cdot (A/F)_{st} + 1}$ | kJ/kg | Where λ = equivalence ratio⁻¹ | | 4 | Relative Air-Fuel Ratio (λ) | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{st}}$ | – | λ < 1 → rich; λ > 1 → lean | | 5 | Equivalence Ratio (φ) | $\phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | – | φ = 1 → stoich; φ < 1 → lean | | 6 | Theoretical Otto Efficiency | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | – | γ = 1.40 (air) | | 7 | Indicated Thermal Eff. | $\eta_{it} = \dfrac{W_i}{m_f \cdot Q_{LHV}}$ | – | W_i in kJ | | 8 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{a,actual}}{\rho_a \cdot V_d}$ | – | Affected by gaseous H₂ | | 9 | Density of H₂ at (P,T) | $\rho_{H_2} = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{H_2}}{R_u \cdot T}$ | kg/m³ | Use ideal gas law | | 10 | Laminar Flame Speed Ratio | $S_L / S_{L,ref} \approx f(\phi, T, P)$ | – | H₂ ~ 8× gasoline | | 11 | Quenching Distance Ratio | $d_q \propto \dfrac{\alpha}{S_L}$ | mm | H₂ = 0.6 mm; gasoline = 2.0 mm | | 12 | Min. Ignition Energy | $E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | mJ | H₂ needs only 0.02 mJ | | 13 | Power Derating Factor | $P_{H_2} / P_{gas} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (A/F)_{st} \cdot (1/\lambda)}$ approx. | – | Mass-based | | 14 | Flame Temperature | $T_{ad} = T_i + \dfrac{Q_{rxn}}{m_{mix} \cdot c_{p,avg}}$ | K | 2407 K at stoich | | 15 | NOx (Zeldovich) Rate | $\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_f \cdot [O][N_2]$ | mol/(L·s) | ∝ exp(-E_a/RT) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always quote the **stoichiometric A/F = 34.3** and the **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** — these two values alone answer 60 % of numerical questions on hydrogen SI engines. ## 2.3 Abnormal Combustion Phenomena in H₂ SI Engines (The "Three-Headed Monster") Hydrogen's *exceptional* properties create three notorious operational defects that KTU examiners repeatedly emphasize: ### A. Backfire - **Definition:** Combustion of the air-hydrogen mixture in the **intake manifold** or during the **intake stroke**, ignited by residual hot spots or an open hot exhaust valve. - **Root Cause:** H₂ has **very low quenching distance (0.6 mm)** and **extremely low minimum ignition energy (0.02 mJ)** → any residual flame kernel, glowing carbon deposit, or hot spot survives long enough to ignite the incoming charge *before* it reaches TDC. - **Mitigation:** Hot-spot-free valves (sodium-cooled), careful valve timing, exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) to cool residual gases, anti-flame arrestors in the intake. ### B. Pre-ignition - **Definition:** Ignition of the charge **before** the spark plug fires, initiated by an overheated component (spark plug tip, exhaust valve, carbon deposit). - **Root Cause:** Hydrogen's **very high auto-ignition temperature (571 °C)** is paradoxically irrelevant here — pre-ignition in H₂ engines is driven by **local catalytic surface ignition**, not bulk auto-ignition. Hydrogen dissociates on hot metal surfaces (catalytic effect) and ignites well below its bulk AIT. - **Mitigation:** Cold-rated spark plugs, water-methanol injection, ceramic coatings, optimised combustion chamber design. ### C. Knock / Pressure Rise Rate (dP/dθ) Spikes - **Definition:** A near-instantaneous pressure rise (very steep dP/dθ > 6 bar/deg) producing audible "knock" and mechanical stress. - **Root Cause:** Hydrogen's **laminar flame speed (3 m/s)** is 6–8 times faster than gasoline, so the cylinder volume is consumed in a very short crank-angle interval → near-detonation pressure wave. - **Mitigation:** Heavy inert dilution (EGR, water injection), ultra-lean operation (φ ≈ 0.4 – 0.5), lower compression ratio (8–10:1 instead of 13:1), turbulence enhancement. ## 2.4 Why Lean-Burn Operation is the Hallmark of H₂ SI Engines Because hydrogen has: - A flammability range spanning **φ = 0.146 → 9.0** (an extraordinarily wide range) - A flame speed that remains high even in ultra-lean mixtures - Zero soot formation tendency - A high octane number preventing knock at high dilution …the optimal strategy is **ultra-lean operation at φ ≈ 0.4 – 0.5**, yielding: - **Higher thermal efficiency** (lean mixtures have higher γ_effective). - **Lower peak flame temperature** → **drastic NOx reduction** (NOx peaks near φ = 1.0). - **Improved part-load fuel economy** (only the necessary air mass is compressed; throttling losses vanish). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trade-off:** As φ decreases below ~0.4, combustion becomes unstable and cycle-to-cycle variance (COV_imep) rises above 10 %, which KTU examiners mark as the **"lean misfire limit."** ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |--------|-------------| | Automotive | BMW Hydrogen 7, Toyota Mirai (FCEV), Hyundai NEXO | | Aerospace | Cryogenic LH₂ in rocket engines (RL-10, SSME) | | Stationary Power | Hydrogen-fuelled gensets for telecom backup | | Marine | Norway's Hydra ferry (H₂ fuel cells + SI gensets) | | Research | Dual-fuel micro-pilot ignition systems | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Power Derating of an SI Engine Switched to Pure H₂ **Problem Statement:** A 4-stroke, single-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has a displacement volume V_d = 500 cm³ and runs at N = 3000 rpm on gasoline with a stoichiometric A/F ratio of 14.7. Calculate the **percentage power derating** when the same engine is operated on pure hydrogen at the same λ (relative A/F), assuming the same volumetric efficiency and equal energy conversion. ### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation **Step 1 — Define the working principle.** For the same intake condition (same mass of air per cycle, m_a), the fuel mass changes because (A/F)_st differs between the two fuels. **Step 2 — Mass of air per cycle.** $$ m_a = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_d $$ For air at NTP, ρ_a = 1.181 kg/m³, and we take η_v = 1.0 for ideal comparison. **Step 3 — Mass of gasoline fuel per cycle.** $$ m_{f,gas} = \dfrac{m_a}{(A/F)_{st,gas}} = \dfrac{m_a}{14.7} $$ **Step 4 — Mass of hydrogen fuel per cycle (same air mass).** $$ m_{f,H_2} = \dfrac{m_a}{(A/F)_{st,H_2}} = \dfrac{m_a}{34.3} $$ **Step 5 — Energy released per cycle (gasoline).** $$ Q_{gas} = m_{f,gas} \cdot LHV_{gas} = \dfrac{m_a}{14.7} \cdot 44{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 6 — Energy released per cycle (hydrogen).** $$ Q_{H_2} = m_{f,H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2} = \dfrac{m_a}{34.3} \cdot 120{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 7 — Power ratio (assuming equal mechanical efficiency).** $$ \dfrac{P_{H_2}}{P_{gas}} = \dfrac{Q_{H_2}}{Q_{gas}} = \dfrac{120{,}000 / 34.3}{44{,}000 / 14.7} $$ **Step 8 — Numerical evaluation of the numerator.** $$ \dfrac{120{,}000}{34.3} = 3498.54 \text{ kJ per kg of air} $$ **Step 9 — Numerical evaluation of the denominator.** $$ \dfrac{44{,}000}{14.7} = 2993.20 \text{ kJ per kg of air} $$ **Step 10 — Form the ratio.** $$ \dfrac{P_{H_2}}{P_{gas}} = \dfrac{3498.54}{2993.20} = 1.1687 $$ **Step 11 — Logical Conclusion.** Because the same mass of air is present, hydrogen actually delivers **16.87 % MORE energy per cycle** when both fuels are run at their stoichiometric A/F with the same air charge. This is the *theoretical* ideal. > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In real engines, hydrogen is always run **lean** (λ > 1) to control NOx and knock. At λ = 1.5, for example, the effective energy density drops by ~33 %, and practical power derating of **20 – 50 %** is observed. KTU answers must distinguish *stoichiometric* from *lean* operation. **Step 12 — Corrected expression for arbitrary λ.** If both engines run at the same λ: $$ \dfrac{P_{H_2}}{P_{gas}} = \dfrac{LHV_{H_2} \cdot (A/F)_{st,gas}}{LHV_{gas} \cdot (A/F)_{st,H_2}} = \dfrac{120{,}000 \times 14.7}{44{,}000 \times 34.3} $$ $$ = \dfrac{1{,}764{,}000}{1{,}509{,}200} = 1.1689 $$ Same result. **Therefore, at stoichiometric, hydrogen is theoretically 16.9 % more energetic per unit air mass** — a brilliant property for forced-induction (turbocharged) applications. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Adiabatic Flame Temperature of a Stoichiometric H₂–Air Mixture **Step 1 — Reaction enthalpy at 298 K (LHV basis):** $$ \Delta H_{rxn} = -241.8 \text{ MJ/kmol H}_2 $$ **Step 2 — Number of moles in products (per 1 mol H₂ burned).** Reaction: 1 H₂ + ½ O₂ + ½(3.76) N₂ → 1 H₂O + 1.88 N₂ Total moles of products per mol H₂ = 1 + 1.88 = **2.88 mol**. **Step 3 — Molar mass of the products mixture.** $$ \bar{M}_{prod} = \dfrac{(1 \times 18) + (1.88 \times 28)}{2.88} = \dfrac{18 + 52.64}{2.88} = 24.53 \text{ kg/kmol} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the energy balance Q = 0 (adiabatic, constant pressure baseline).** $$ \sum n_i h_i(T_{ad}) = \sum n_i h_i(298 \text{ K}) $$ For a constant-pressure flame with sensible enthalpy rise: $$ \Delta H_{rxn}(298) = \int_{298}^{T_{ad}} \left[ n_{H_2O} c_{p,H_2O} + n_{N_2} c_{p,N_2} \right] dT $$ **Step 5 — Use average specific heats (T range 298 → 2400 K):** $$ \bar{c}_{p,H_2O} = 41.2 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)} \quad \bar{c}_{p,N_2} = 30.6 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)} $$ **Step 6 — Substitute the values:** $$ 241{,}800 = (1 \times 41.2 + 1.88 \times 30.6) (T_{ad} - 298) $$ $$ 241{,}800 = (41.2 + 57.53)(T_{ad} - 298) $$ $$ 241{,}800 = 98.73 (T_{ad} - 298) $$ **Step 7 — Solve for T_ad:** $$ T_{ad} - 298 = \dfrac{241{,}800}{98.73} = 2449.4 \text{ K} $$ $$ T_{ad} = 2747 \text{ K} $$ > [!NOTE] > The textbook-cited value of **~2400 K** corresponds to a constant-volume flame with dissociation losses (~300 K lower). The exact value depends on whether dissociation is included and whether the calculation is constant-V or constant-P. **Step 8 — Dissociation Correction (advanced KTU elective topic):** Above ~2000 K, the reaction H₂O → H₂ + ½ O₂ begins to occur significantly, absorbing energy. The corrected equilibrium temperature is: $$ T_{ad,corr} \approx 2407 \text{ K (with dissociation)} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Knock Resistance — Critical Compression Ratio Limit **Step 1 — Knock criterion.** For an engine, knock occurs when end-gas auto-ignites before flame arrival. For hydrogen, the end-gas auto-ignition delay is so long (high AIT) that *theoretically* CR can exceed 18:1 without knock. **Step 2 — Empirical safe CR for H₂ SI engines:** $$ CR_{H_2} \le 13{:}1 \text{ (recommended)} $$ **Step 3 — Reasoning via Octane Number.** Required Octane Number (RON) ≥ (CR/3.5) — empirical for spark-ignition knock limit. For CR = 13: RON_required = 13/3.5 = **3.7** — trivially satisfied by hydrogen's RON > 130. **Step 4 — Why we don't go to CR = 18?** Because at CR > 13, **pre-ignition** (not knock) becomes the limiting phenomenon. H₂ auto-ignites on hot surfaces long before the bulk AIT, forcing practical CR back to 8 – 13. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: H₂ Engine Operational Map | Operating Mode | Equivalence Ratio (φ) | λ | Power Output | NOx | Efficiency | Use Case | |----------------|----------------------|---|--------------|-----|------------|----------| | Ultra-Lean (KTU-preferred) | 0.30 – 0.50 | 2.0 – 3.3 | Low (50 – 70 % rated) | Very low (< 50 ppm) | High (> 38 %) | Cruise / light load | | Lean | 0.50 – 0.80 | 1.25 – 2.0 | Moderate (70 – 85 %) | Low | High | Part load urban | | Stoichiometric | 1.00 | 1.0 | High (rated) | **High** (~2000 ppm) | Moderate | WOT / full power | | Rich (rare) | 1.20 – 1.50 | 0.67 – 0.83 | High | Moderate | Lower | Cold start / transient | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Note:** The **NOx emission peak** occurs *exactly* at φ = 1.0 because flame temperature is maximum. Moving to φ = 0.5 cuts NOx by **~80 %** while sacrificing ~30 % of the power output — the central design compromise of all hydrogen SI engines. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 4: Mass of H₂ Required for a Given Engine Output **Given:** Engine rated power P = 50 kW, brake thermal efficiency η_bt = 30 %, runs on hydrogen (LHV = 120 MJ/kg). **Step 1 — Output energy per second.** $$ \dot{W}_b = 50 \text{ kW} = 50 \text{ kJ/s} $$ **Step 2 — Input energy rate (fuel).** $$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_b}{\eta_{bt}} = \dfrac{50}{0.30} = 166.67 \text{ kJ/s} $$ **Step 3 — Mass flow rate of H₂.** $$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{LHV_{H_2}} = \dfrac{166.67}{120{,}000} = 1.389 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to g/s and kg/h.** $$ \dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.389 \text{ g/s} = 4.998 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 5 — Air consumption (stoichiometric).** $$ \dot{m}_{air} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times 34.3 = 47.65 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 6 — Total exhaust mass flow (products of combustion).** Products: 1 kg H₂ + 8.94 kg O₂ + 25.36 kg N₂ → 9.94 kg H₂O + 25.36 kg N₂ = **35.30 kg** $$ \dot{m}_{exh} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times 35.30 = 4.998 \times 35.30 = 176.4 \text{ kg/h} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A 50 kW hydrogen engine consumes **5 kg of H₂ per hour** — to run for 1 hour, you need a 70 MPa (700 bar) tank of approximately 65 L (since 1 kg of H₂ ≈ 13 L at 700 bar). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System Architecture: Hydrogen SI Engine — Functional Block Diagram The following Mermaid diagram presents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of a hydrogen-fuelled SI engine, mapping the integration of all major subsystems. How do their values for H₂ differ from gasoline, and why is this difference significant for SI engine design?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE):** the smallest electrical energy that can ignite a combustible mixture. For H₂ it is **0.02 mJ**, for gasoline ~0.25 mJ (1 mark). - **Quenching Distance:** the minimum gap that a flame can propagate through. For H₂ it is **0.6 mm**, for gasoline ~2 mm (1 mark). - **Significance:** H₂ ignites *far more easily* and survives in *narrower gaps*, causing backfire and pre-ignition, requiring flame arrestors and hot-spot-free valve design (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q1]** > **(a)** Discuss in detail the combustion characteristics of hydrogen as an SI engine fuel, comparing it with gasoline on the basis of (i) flame speed, (ii) flammability limits, (iii) minimum ignition energy, (iv) quenching distance, and (v) auto-ignition temperature. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A 4-stroke, single-cylinder hydrogen SI engine has a displacement of 600 cm³, runs at 2400 rpm at an equivalence ratio of 0.6, with a volumetric efficiency of 85 % and an indicated thermal efficiency of 32 %. Calculate: > (i) Indicated power, > (ii) Mass of H₂ consumed per hour, > (iii) Brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC), assuming mechanical efficiency = 80 %. > Take LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg, ρ_air = 1.181 kg/m³, (A/F)_st = 34.3. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** | # | Property | Hydrogen | Gasoline | Comparison | Marks | |---|----------|----------|----------|------------|-------| | 1 | Flame Speed | 2.5–3.5 m/s | 0.4 m/s | H₂ is **6–8× faster** → near-constant-V combustion (1) | 1 | | 2 | Flammability (φ) | 0.146 – 9.0 | 0.7 – 1.4 | H₂ range is **~30× wider** → ultra-lean operation possible (1) | 1 | | 3 | MIE | 0.02 mJ | 0.25 mJ | H₂ ignites **~12× easier** → backfire risk (1) | 1 | | 4 | Quench Distance | 0.6 mm | 2.0 mm | H₂ propagates in **narrower gaps** → hot-spot risk (1) | 1 | | 5 | AIT | 571 °C | 250 °C | H₂ is **knock-resistant** but pre-ignites on hot surfaces (1) | 1 | | 6 | Concluding remark | — | — | H₂ is ideal for lean burn SI; needs EGR, flame arrestor, cold plugs (2) | 2 | **Total: 7 marks** **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Mass of air per cycle.** $$ m_a = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_d = 0.85 \times 1.181 \times 600 \times 10^{-6} = 6.023 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/cycle} $$ **Step 2 — Actual A/F ratio.** $$ (A/F)_{act} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{st}}{\phi} = \dfrac{34.3}{0.6} = 57.17 $$ **Step 3 — Mass of H₂ per cycle.** $$ m_f = \dfrac{m_a}{(A/F)_{act}} = \dfrac{6.023 \times 10^{-4}}{57.17} = 1.0535 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/cycle} $$ **Step 4 — Energy per cycle.** $$ Q_{cycle} = m_f \cdot LHV = 1.0535 \times 10^{-5} \times 120{,}000 = 1.264 \text{ kJ/cycle} $$ **Step 5 — Cycles per second (4-stroke).** $$ n = \dfrac{N}{2 \times 60} = \dfrac{2400}{120} = 20 \text{ cycles/s} $$ **Step 6 — Indicated Power.** $$ P_i = Q_{cycle} \times n \times \eta_{it} = 1.264 \times 20 \times 0.32 = 8.09 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 7 — Mass of H₂ per hour.** $$ \dot{m}_f = m_f \times n \times 3600 = 1.0535 \times 10^{-5} \times 20 \times 3600 = 0.759 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 8 — Brake Power.** $$ P_b = P_i \times \eta_m = 8.09 \times 0.80 = 6.47 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 9 — BSFC.** $$ BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \dfrac{0.759}{6.47} = 0.1173 \text{ kg/kWh} $$ **Final Answers:** (i) P_i = **8.09 kW**; (ii) m_f = **0.759 kg/h**; (iii) BSFC = **0.117 kg/kWh** (or 117 g/kWh) --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1 (Alternative)]** > **(a)** Explain the three abnormal combustion phenomena (backfire, pre-ignition, knock) encountered in hydrogen-fuelled SI engines, giving their root causes and mitigation strategies in each case. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** An engine rated at 75 kW on gasoline is converted to run on pure hydrogen at the *same λ (relative air-fuel ratio)* and at the *same brake thermal efficiency*. Calculate: > (i) The new power output, > (ii) The percentage change in specific fuel consumption (SFC) on an energy basis, > (iii) Comment on the volumetric implication. > Given: LHV of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg, LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg, (A/F)_st (gasoline) = 14.7, (A/F)_st (H₂) = 34.3. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Backfire (2 marks):** Definition (0.5) + Cause: hot residual gas/valve, low MIE of H₂ (0.75) + Mitigation: sodium-cooled valves, anti-flame arrestors, EGR (0.75). 2. **Pre-ignition (2 marks):** Definition (0.5) + Cause: hot surface catalytic ignition, low quench distance (0.75) + Mitigation: cold plugs, water injection, ceramic coatings (0.75). 3. **Knock / dP/dθ (2 marks):** Definition (0.5) + Cause: high flame speed → near-detonation pressure rise (0.75) + Mitigation: lean burn, high EGR, lower CR, swirl enhancement (0.75). 4. **Synthesis statement (1 mark):** All three phenomena stem from H₂'s *exceptionally* low MIE and high flame speed, requiring active combustion control. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Energy released per kg of air (stoichiometric basis).** For gasoline: $$ q_{gas} = \dfrac{LHV_{gas}}{(A/F)_{st,gas}} = \dfrac{44{,}000}{14.7} = 2993.2 \text{ kJ/kg air} $$ For hydrogen: $$ q_{H_2} = \dfrac{LHV_{H_2}}{(A/F)_{st,H_2}} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{34.3} = 3498.5 \text{ kJ/kg air} $$ **Step 2 — New power (same air mass, same η_bt).** $$ P_{H_2} = P_{gas} \times \dfrac{q_{H_2}}{q_{gas}} = 75 \times \dfrac{3498.5}{2993.2} = 75 \times 1.169 = 87.66 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3 — Energy-based SFC comparison.** SFC (energy basis) = $\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV / P_b$ Since η_bt and P_b are related by the same η_bt, the SFC on an energy basis is **identical** — that is the whole point of "energy basis" comparison. The SFC on a *mass* basis, however, would be: $$ SFC_{mass,H_2} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{bt} \cdot LHV_{H_2}} \times 3600 = \dfrac{3600}{0.30 \times 120{,}000} = 0.100 \text{ kg/kWh} $$ $$ SFC_{mass,gas} = \dfrac{1}{0.30 \times 44{,}000} \times 3600 = 0.273 \text{ kg/kWh} $$ **Step 4 — Percentage change in mass SFC.** $$ \Delta SFC = \dfrac{0.100 - 0.273}{0.273} \times 100\% = -63.4\% $$ Hydrogen reduces **mass-based SFC by 63.4 %** but **does not improve energy-based SFC**. **Step 5 — Volumetric implication (2 marks for (iii) part).** For the same air mass per cycle, the gaseous H₂ occupies much more volume than liquid gasoline. At NTP, the H₂ fuel volume needed per cycle is **~2700×** the gasoline volume (because of the density ratio 750/0.0838 ≈ 8950 and the 1/34.3 vs 1/14.7 mass ratio). Therefore, the engine **cannot ingest the same air charge with the original intake system** — a supercharger or LH₂ injection is required to realise the theoretical 16.9 % power gain. **Final Answers:** (i) P_H₂ = **87.66 kW**; (ii) ΔSFC = **−63.4 %** (mass basis) or **0 %** (energy basis); (iii) volumetric penalty necessitates forced induction. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Triggers:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to cycles/s** for a 4-stroke engine (use N/120, NOT N/60). > 2. **Confusing φ and λ** — KTU answers must use φ = 1/λ consistently. > 3. **Mixing mass-based and energy-based SFC** — examiners award partial credit only if the student explicitly states the basis. > 4. **Forgetting dissociation correction** in flame temperature derivations — penalised only in 14-mark questions, not in 3-mark ones. > 5. **Stating "hydrogen has high auto-ignition temperature"** without explaining the paradox of catalytic pre-ignition — a 2-mark penalty trap. > 6. **Omitting the volumetric energy density argument** when discussing power derating — KTU's most repeated "partial answer" complaint. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):** - 🔑 **Stoichiometric A/F of H₂ = 34.3** (mass basis); **2.39** (molar basis). - 🔑 **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg**; HHV = 142 MJ/kg. - 🔑 **Laminar flame speed of H₂ = 2.5 – 3.5 m/s** (≈ 8× gasoline). - 🔑 **Flammability range: 4 – 75 vol %** in air, or **φ = 0.146 – 9.0**. - 🔑 **MIE = 0.02 mJ**; **Quenching distance = 0.6 mm** — both the smallest of common fuels. - 🔑 **Auto-ignition temperature = 571 °C** — high, but catalytic pre-ignition occurs at lower temperatures. - 🔑 **RON > 130** — enables high CR (theoretical 18:1, practical 10–13:1). - 🔑 **Lean-burn optimum φ = 0.4 – 0.5** balances NOx and stability. - 🔑 **NOx peak occurs at φ = 1.0** — lean operation is the principal NOx mitigation. - 🔑 **Three abnormal combustion phenomena:** Backfire, Pre-ignition, High dP/dθ (knock). - 🔑 **Power derating at lean operation = 20 – 50 %** of gasoline rating. - 🔑 **Volumetric energy density = 1/2700 of gasoline** — root cause of power derating. - 🔑 **Mitigation toolkit:** Supercharging, LH₂ injection, high-pressure storage, water injection, EGR, cold spark plugs, flame arrestors, sodium-cooled valves, swirl/tumble enhancement. - 🔑 **Combustion products = pure H₂O (no CO₂, no CO, no UHC)** when run lean. - 🔑 **For numericals, always use 4-stroke cycle frequency = N/120.**

topics.title: Emission Control Devices in Modern SI Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Emission Control Devices in Modern SI Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Emission Control Devices in Modern SI Engines

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Emission Control Devices** in modern Spark-Ignition (SI) engines refer to the integrated hardware and control subsystems that treat, recirculate, or prevent the formation of regulated exhaust pollutants — namely **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)**, **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)**, and to a lesser extent Particulate Matter (PM) and Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) — so that tailpipe emissions comply with the statutory limits prescribed under prevailing **Bharat Stage (BS-VI / BS-VII)** norms, which are harmonized with European Euro 6 / Euro 7 standards.

These devices are broadly classified into three categories:

1. **In-Cylinder / Pre-Combustion Controls** — techniques that reduce pollutant *formation* (e.g., EGR, retarded timing, stratified charge).
2. **In-Engine / Crankcase Controls** — systems that prevent *escape* of pollutants from non-exhaust pathways (e.g., PCV, EVAP).
3. **After-Treatment Devices** — systems that *destroy* pollutants after they leave the combustion chamber (e.g., Three-Way Catalytic Converter, Diesel Oxidation Catalyst, NOx Trap, GPF/DPF).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the module on SI engines, the primary after-treatment device of interest is the **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)**, supported by **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)**, **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)**, and the **Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP)** system. The Oxygen (λ) sensor and closed-loop electronic fuel injection form the *enabling* subsystem without which the TWC cannot operate at peak efficiency.

### 1.2 The Three Major Pollutants — Source Mechanism

| Pollutant | Symbol | Formation Mechanism | Health Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | Incomplete combustion of fuel due to localized fuel-rich zones (quenching at walls, crevices) | Binds to haemoglobin → asphyxiation |
| Unburnt Hydrocarbons | HC | Flame quenching, wall absorption, crevice volumes, misfire | Carcinogenic (e.g., Benzene, PAH) |
| Oxides of Nitrogen | NOx | High in-cylinder peak temperatures (>1800 K) dissociate N₂ and O₂ → Zeldovich mechanism | Acid rain, smog, respiratory illness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Three-Headed Monster"):** Imagine a bonfire. The smoke that escapes unburned represents **HC** (incomplete combustion). The slight choking smell near the flames represents **CO** (incomplete oxidation). The brownish plume high above represents **NOx** (formed because the fire was *too* hot). An emission control system attacks all three heads simultaneously — a **Three-Way Catalyst** neutralizes the smoke and choking smell in a single chamber, while **EGR** cools the flame so the brown plume never forms in the first place.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used (KTU 2024 Mandatory)

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFRₛₜₒᵢcₕ):** 14.7:1 by mass for gasoline (iso-octane reference).
- **Equivalence Ratio (φ):** φ = (F/A)ₐcₜᵤₐₗ / (F/A)ₛₜₒᵢcₕ
- **Lambda (λ):** λ = AFRₐcₜᵤₐₗ / AFRₛₜₒᵢcₕ = 1/φ
- **Conversion Efficiency (η):** η = (Cᵢₙ − Cₒᵤₜ) / Cᵢₙ × 100 %

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Constant:** The **stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = 14.7** is the single most important number in modern SI engine emission control. The TWC only works efficiently when λ is held within a narrow band of **0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01** (called the "Lambda Window" of ±1 %).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) — Operational Core

A TWC is a stainless-steel honeycomb monolith (typically cordierite 2MgO·2Al₂O₃·5SiO₂, with **400–600 cells/in²**) coated with a porous **washcoat** of γ-Al₂O₃ (gamma alumina). Within the washcoat, two precious-metal catalysts are dispersed:
- **Platinum (Pt)** and **Palladium (Pd)** — promote *oxidation* of CO and HC.
- **Rhodium (Rh)** — promotes *reduction* of NOx.

The washcoat also contains stabilizers like **Ceria (CeO₂)**, which acts as an oxygen storage medium (OSC — Oxygen Storage Capacity), allowing the catalyst to "ride through" transient air-fuel excursions.

### 2.2 The Three Simultaneous Reactions in a TWC

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Oxidation of CO:} \quad & 2\,\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 \\
\text{Oxidation of HC:} \quad & \text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{Reduction of NO:} \quad & 2\,\text{NO} \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2 \quad \text{(or } \text{NO} + \text{CO} \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\text{N}_2 + \text{CO}_2\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The third reaction is what makes the TWC a *three-way* catalyst — it handles **both** oxidation (CO, HC → CO₂, H₂O) and reduction (NOx → N₂). For this to happen, the engine must be operated very near stoichiometry, because:
> - **Rich (λ < 1):** insufficient O₂ → CO and HC not fully oxidized; NOx reduced efficiently.
> - **Lean (λ > 1):** abundant O₂ → CO and HC oxidized efficiently; **NOx reduction stalls** (no reductant).

## 2.2.1 Why a Lambda Sensor is Mandatory

The **Heated Exhaust Gas Oxygen (HEGO) sensor** is a zirconia (ZrO₂) ceramic element that generates a voltage step (≈ 0.1 V lean ↔ 0.9 V rich) precisely at λ = 1. This signal is fed to the **ECU**, which trims the injector pulse-width at 1–2 Hz to "dither" λ around 1.0. The dithering frequency and amplitude determine the TWC's average efficiency.

## 2.3 Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)

EGR routes a metered portion (typically **5–25 %** by mass) of exhaust gas back into the intake manifold. The exhaust is inert (mostly CO₂, H₂O, N₂) and dilutes the fresh charge, which:

1. **Lowers the peak flame temperature** (because the specific heat of CO₂ and H₂O is high → more heat absorbed per degree of temperature rise).
2. **Reduces the in-cylinder O₂ partial pressure**, slowing the Zeldovich NOx formation.

### 2.3.1 Quantitative Effect on NOx

The Zeldovich (extended) mechanism gives the steady-state NO formation rate as:

$$
\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = 2 k_1 [\text{O}][\text{N}_2] \frac{1 - (k_{-1}/k_1)([\text{NO}]/[\text{O}][\text{N}_2])}{1 + (k_{-1}/k_2)([\text{NO}]/[\text{O}_2])}
$$

A simpler engineering relation used in KTU is:

$$
[\text{NOx}]_{\text{out}} \propto \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T_{\text{peak}}}\right)
$$

where $E_a$ is the apparent activation energy (≈ 285 kJ/kmol for the Zeldovich path) and $T_{\text{peak}}$ is the peak cycle temperature. A **100 K drop** in $T_{\text{peak}}$ (achievable with ~15 % EGR) typically reduces NOx by **60–80 %**.

## 2.4 Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)

Blow-by gases (HC-rich) leak past the piston rings into the crankcase. Without PCV, these are vented to the atmosphere (a major HC source). The PCV valve, a spring-loaded metered orifice, draws these gases into the intake manifold at a controlled rate proportional to manifold vacuum. This eliminates **~20 % of total HC emissions** from older engines.

## 2.5 Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP)

Fuel vapours from the tank and lines are captured in a **charcoal canister** and purged (during closed-loop operation) into the intake manifold. The purge solenoid is duty-cycle controlled by the ECU to avoid perturbing λ.

## 2.6 Secondary Air Injection

A pump (or pulse-driven reed valve) injects fresh air downstream of the exhaust port during cold start (and brief warm-up). The extra O₂ oxidizes HC and CO in the exhaust manifold before the catalyst lights off, slashing the **cold-start HC spike** (which can be 60–80 % of total HC in a FTP-75 cycle).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols & Units | Typical Values / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{air}}}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$ for gasoline | $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$ g/mol, $M_{\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}} = 114.23$ g/mol | **= 14.7** (must know) |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Dimensionless | λ = 1 at stoichiometry |
| 3 | $\phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Equivalence ratio | φ = 1 at stoichiometry |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{conv}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\,\%$ | % conversion efficiency | TWC peak **> 98 %** for CO, HC, NOx |
| 5 | TWC Light-off Temperature | $T_{\text{lo}}$ | **~ 250 °C** (HC), **~ 400 °C** (NOx) |
| 6 | NO formation (Arrhenius-type) | $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$ | $E_a \approx 285$ kJ/kmol |
| 7 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | g/kWh | Used in BS-VI CO₂ correlation |
| 8 | EGR rate | $r_{\text{EGR}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{exh,recirc}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{exh,total}}} \times 100\,\%$ | 5–25 % typical |
| 9 | PCV flow | $\dot{m}_{\text{PCV}} \propto \Delta P_{\text{manifold-crankcase}}$ | kg/s |
| 10 | Stoichiometric O₂ demand (per kg fuel) | $m_{\text{O}_2} = 3.42$ kg O₂/kg fuel (gasoline) | For combustion stoichiometry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The combined TWC + EGR + PCV + EVAP system is the reason a modern BS-VI petrol car emits < 100 mg/km of NOx versus the 1.5–2 g/km from an uncontrolled 1970s engine — a **> 95 %** reduction achieved without changing the basic Otto cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈)

This is a KTU favourite. We balance the combustion equation and compute the mass ratio.

### Step 1: Write the unbalanced molecular equation

$$
\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + a\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### Step 2: Balance Carbon (C)

C atoms on LHS = 8 → b = 8.

### Step 3: Balance Hydrogen (H)

H atoms on LHS = 18 → 2c = 18 → c = 9.

### Step 4: Balance Oxygen (O)

O atoms on RHS = 2(8) + 9 = 25 → 2a = 25 → a = 12.5.

### Step 5: Final balanced equation

$$
\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

### Step 6: Convert mole ratio to mass ratio

Mass of fuel = 1 mol × 114.23 g/mol = 114.23 g.
Mass of O₂ = 12.5 mol × 32.00 g/mol = 400.00 g.

$$
(\text{F}/\text{O})_{\text{mol}} = \dfrac{114.23}{400.00} = 0.2856
$$

### Step 7: Convert O₂ mass to air mass

Air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass (by weight), so:

$$
m_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{0.232} = \dfrac{400.00}{0.232} = 1724.14 \text{ g}
$$

### Step 8: Compute the stoichiometric AFR

$$
\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \dfrac{1724.14}{114.23} \approx 15.1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Refinement:** Using the more accurate "air" composition (21 % O₂ by volume, 23.3 % by mass), and accounting for the argon content, the textbook value of **14.7** is obtained. For exam purposes, **14.7 : 1** is the standard answer.

## 3.2 Derivation: Lambda Window of a TWC

The TWC efficiency is a strong function of λ. Define conversion efficiency for each species as:

$$
\eta_{\text{CO}} = \dfrac{[\text{CO}]_{\text{in}} - [\text{CO}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{CO}]_{\text{in}}}
$$

Similarly for HC and NOx. The characteristic TWC efficiency curve is bell-shaped, peaking near λ = 1. The HEGO sensor + ECU dither system keeps λ within:

$$
\lambda \in [\lambda_L, \lambda_R] = [0.99, 1.01]
$$

This ±1 % band ensures **> 90 %** simultaneous conversion of all three pollutants.

## 3.3 Numerical Example: NOx Reduction via EGR

> A naturally aspirated SI engine runs at WOT, λ = 1.0, with peak cycle temperature $T_{\text{peak}}$ = 2400 K. The NOx concentration in the exhaust is measured at 1800 ppm. If 15 % EGR is introduced, the peak temperature drops to 2260 K. Using the Arrhenius relation, estimate the new NOx concentration.

**Given:** $E_a$ = 285 kJ/kmol, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T_1$ = 2400 K, $T_2$ = 2260 K, [NOx]₁ = 1800 ppm.

**Step 1:** Form the ratio using the Arrhenius approximation (valid when the pre-exponential factor cancels):

$$
\dfrac{[\text{NOx}]_2}{[\text{NOx}]_1} = \exp\left[-\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $1/T_2 - 1/T_1$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{2260} - \dfrac{1}{2400} = (4.4248 \times 10^{-4}) - (4.1667 \times 10^{-4}) = 2.581 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the exponent:

$$
-\dfrac{285{,}000}{8.314} \times 2.581 \times 10^{-5} = -34270 \times 2.581 \times 10^{-5} = -0.8844
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the exponential:

$$
\exp(-0.8844) \approx 0.413
$$

**Step 5:** Compute new NOx:

$$
[\text{NOx}]_2 = 1800 \times 0.413 \approx 743 \text{ ppm}
$$

**Step 6:** Percentage reduction:

$$
\%\,\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{1800 - 743}{1800} \times 100 = 58.7\,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 140 K drop in peak temperature via 15 % EGR reduces NOx by **~ 59 %**, validating the Zeldovich kinetic model and confirming EGR as the most cost-effective NOx control strategy for SI engines.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Overall SI Engine Emission Control Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fuel Tank] -->|Liquid Fuel| B[High-Pressure Fuel Rail]
    A -.->|Vapours| C[Charcoal Canister EVAP]
    C -->|Purge Solenoid Duty Cycle| D[Intake Manifold]
    B -->|Injectors Pulsed by ECU| D
    D -->|Fresh Charge + EGR| E[Combustion Chamber]
    F[Exhaust Manifold] -->|Hot Gas 400 900 C| G[Three Way Catalytic Converter TWC]
    H[Crankcase Blow by Gases] -->|PCV Valve Metered| D
    I[Exhaust Gas Recirculation EGR] -->|Cooled 5 to 25 percent| D
    F --> I
    E --> F
    G --> J[Lambda Oxygen Sensor HEGO]
    J -->|Voltage Feedback 0.1 to 0.9 V| K[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    K -->|Pulse Width Modulation| B
    K -->|Purge Duty Cycle| C
    K -->|EGR Valve Position| I
    G --> L[Tailpipe]
    M[Cold Start Air Pump] -->|Secondary Air Injection| F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Internal Structure of a Three-Way Catalytic Converter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INLET [Exhaust Inlet at 400 to 600 C]
        A1[CO]
        A2[HC]
        A3[NOx]
    end

    subgraph SUBSTRATE [Cordierite Honeycomb 400 to 600 cpsi]
        B1[Gamma Alumina Washcoat]
        B2[Ceria CeO2 Oxygen Storage]
    end

    subgraph CATALYSTS [Precious Metal Sites]
        C1[Platinum Pt Oxidation]
        C2[Palladium Pd Oxidation]
        C3[Rhodium Rh Reduction]
    end

    subgraph OUTLET [Exhaust Outlet at 350 to 500 C]
        D1[CO2]
        D2[H2O]
        D3[N2]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B1 --> C3
    B2 -.->|Buffers lambda transients| B1
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Closed-Loop Lambda Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Target Lambda = 1.000] --> B[ECU Comparator]
    C[HEGO Sensor Voltage] --> B
    B --> D[Error Signal e]
    D --> E[PI Controller Kp plus Ki]
    E --> F[Injector Pulse Width Adjustment]
    F --> G[Engine Combustion]
    G --> H[Exhaust Composition]
    H --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The PI controller continuously trims the injector pulse-width so that the HEGO sensor output oscillates symmetrically around the stoichiometric switching voltage (~ 0.45 V). The frequency of this dither (typically 0.5–2 Hz) sets the catalyst's average operating point within the Lambda Window.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define the term "Lambda (λ)" in the context of SI engine emission control. Why is it critical to maintain λ = 1.000 ± 0.01 for a Three-Way Catalytic Converter to operate efficiently?**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Definition:** $\lambda = \text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} / \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}}$. For gasoline, $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 14.7$. So $\lambda = 1$ at stoichiometry, $\lambda < 1$ is rich, $\lambda > 1$ is lean.
- **Why critical:** A TWC performs three simultaneous reactions — CO and HC oxidation, and NOx reduction. Oxidation needs O₂ (lean), but NOx reduction needs a reductant (rich). The *only* condition where all three can be ~98 % converted simultaneously is within the narrow **Lambda Window of 0.99 to 1.01**.
- **Realisation:** This is achieved by the HEGO (O₂) sensor + ECU closed-loop dithering of injector pulse-width.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
**List any three emission control devices used in a modern port-injection SI engine. State the pollutant each one primarily targets.**

**Model Answer (Table Format Expected):**

| # | Device | Pollutant Targeted | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) | CO, HC, NOx (all three) | Catalytic oxidation + reduction at λ ≈ 1 |
| 2 | Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) | NOx | Dilutes charge, lowers peak flame temperature |
| 3 | Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) | HC (blow-by) | Routes crankcase vapours to intake |
| 4 | Evaporative Canister (EVAP) | HC (fuel vapours) | Charcoal adsorption + timed purge |
| 5 | Secondary Air Injection | HC, CO (cold start) | Post-manifold oxidation during warm-up |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **PCV** (for crankcase blow-by gases) with **EVAP** (for fuel-tank vapours). PCV handles gaseous leakage past piston rings; EVAP handles liquid-fuel evaporation from the tank. Examiners *do* deduct marks for this swap.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A [14 Marks] — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** used in modern SI engines. Discuss the role of platinum, palladium, and rhodium. (7 Marks)

**(b)** What is a **HEGO sensor**? Explain how the ECU uses its signal to maintain the air-fuel ratio within the Lambda Window. Why is a "dither" necessary instead of holding λ exactly at 1.000? (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working of TWC:**

**Construction:**
- A **ceramic honeycomb substrate** made of **cordierite** (2MgO·2Al₂O₃·5SiO₂) with 400–600 cells per square inch, providing a large geometric surface area in a small volume.
- The substrate is coated with a porous **washcoat** of **γ-alumina (γ-Al₂O₃)**, which increases the true surface area to ~ 20,000 m² per litre of catalyst.
- Within the washcoat, three precious metals are dispersed as nano-particles: **Platinum (Pt) ~ 1–3 g/L**, **Palladium (Pd) ~ 2–5 g/L**, and **Rhodium (Rh) ~ 0.2–0.5 g/L**.
- **Ceria (CeO₂)** is added as an **oxygen-storage component (OSC)** that buffers lambda transients.
- The whole assembly is housed in a stainless-steel can with a flexible mat to allow for thermal expansion.

**Working — Three Simultaneous Reactions:**

1. **CO oxidation (Pt, Pd):** 2CO + O₂ → 2CO₂
2. **HC oxidation (Pt, Pd):** CₓHᵧ + (x + y/4)O₂ → xCO₂ + (y/2)H₂O
3. **NO reduction (Rh):** 2NO → N₂ + O₂ ; NO + CO → ½N₂ + CO₂

**Role of Each Metal:**
- **Pt:** Highly active for cold-start HC/CO oxidation; excellent low-temperature light-off.
- **Pd:** More thermally stable than Pt; good for HC oxidation under high-temperature conditions.
- **Rh:** Unique in catalysing NO → N₂ reduction; without Rh, NOx conversion is negligible.
- **Ceria (CeO₂):** Stores O₂ under lean excursions (2CeO₂ → Ce₂O₃ + ½O₂) and releases it under rich excursions, smoothing the lambda dither.

**Operating Conditions:**
- Light-off temperature: **~ 250 °C** for CO/HC, **~ 400 °C** for NOx.
- Optimal efficiency: **> 95 %** conversion in a properly warmed-up, closed-loop engine.
- Space velocity: ~ 30,000–100,000 h⁻¹.

**(b) HEGO Sensor & Lambda Control:**

**HEGO Sensor:**
- A **zirconia (ZrO₂)** ceramic thimble, coated inside and outside with porous platinum electrodes.
- The exhaust gas (outside) is at a different O₂ partial pressure than the reference air (inside).
- The Nernst equation gives the generated EMF:

$$
E = \dfrac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{air}}}{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{exh}}}\right)
$$

where F = 96,485 C/mol (Faraday's constant).

- Output: **~ 0.1 V** in lean exhaust (high O₂) and **~ 0.9 V** in rich exhaust (low O₂). The switch occurs sharply at λ = 1.000 (the " stoichiometric voltage " of ~ 0.45 V).

**Closed-Loop ECU Operation:**

1. ECU reads HEGO voltage every ~ 10 ms.
2. If V > 0.45 V (rich), ECU *shortens* injector pulse-width → leaner mixture.
3. If V < 0.45 V (lean), ECU *lengthens* injector pulse-width → richer mixture.
4. The correction is applied through a **PI (Proportional + Integral) controller** to eliminate steady-state offset.

**Why Dither Instead of Holding λ = 1.000?**
- The HEGO sensor is a **binary (switching) device** — it cannot measure *how far* from stoichiometry the mixture is; it only says "rich" or "lean."
- Without a dither, the system would settle at one limit and stay there, *never* crossing λ = 1.000.
- The dither (typical amplitude ±1 %, frequency 0.5–2 Hz) ensures the catalyst is **periodically** exposed to both slightly rich and slightly lean conditions. The ceria OSC + Rh then achieves the *average* high conversion of all three pollutants.
- Additionally, the dither keeps the catalyst thermally active and prevents it from being "stuck" on one reaction regime.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write that "the TWC reduces CO, HC, and NOx to 100 %." This is impossible — even the best TWC achieves a *peak* of **~ 98 %** for each pollutant, and *simultaneously* only within the lambda window. Marks are lost for not mentioning the **window** and the **dither**.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain the **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** system in an SI engine with a suitable diagram. How does EGR reduce NOx formation? Quantitatively justify using the Zeldovich mechanism. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Describe the **Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV)** and **Evaporative Emission Control (EVAP)** systems. Why is the EVAP purge typically enabled only during closed-loop operation? (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) EGR System:**

**Description:**
- A controlled quantity (5–25 % by mass) of exhaust gas is extracted from the exhaust manifold (or pre-TWC) and routed through a **cooled EGR tube** back into the intake manifold, downstream of the throttle body.
- A **vacuum-modulated EGR valve** (older systems) or a **stepper-motor-actuated EGR valve** (modern systems) regulates the flow rate based on the ECU's command.
- In modern engines, the EGR cooler reduces the recirculated gas temperature from ~ 600 °C to ~ 100 °C, maximising its density and heat-absorption capacity.

**Mechanism of NOx Reduction:**
- The exhaust gas contains CO₂ (≈ 15 %), H₂O (≈ 12 %), and N₂ (≈ 70 %) — all inert, high specific-heat species.
- When mixed with the fresh charge, EGR:
  1. **Dilutes O₂:** lowers the O₂ partial pressure, reducing the O atom concentration (needed for the Zeldovich step NO + N → N₂ + O and N + O₂ → NO + O).
  2. **Increases specific heat:** the higher heat capacity of CO₂ and H₂O absorbs more energy per unit temperature rise, lowering the adiabatic flame temperature and the peak cycle temperature.
  3. **Reduces flame speed slightly:** gives more time for heat loss, further lowering Tₚₑₐₖ.

**Quantitative Justification (Zeldovich):**

The simplified Zeldovich NO formation rate is:

$$
\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = k_f [\text{O}][\text{N}_2] - k_r [\text{NO}][\text{N}]
$$

with $k_f = 1.8 \times 10^{11} \exp(-38370/T)$ cm³/(mol·s) and $k_r = 3.8 \times 10^{9} \exp(-425/T)$ cm³/(mol·s).

The Arrhenius-type temperature dependence gives:

$$
[\text{NO}]_{\text{equilibrium}} \propto \exp(-E_a / RT)
$$

For $E_a = 285$ kJ/mol, a temperature drop of 140 K (e.g., 2400 K → 2260 K) reduces the equilibrium NO by a factor of:

$$
\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{285{,}000}{8.314}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2260} - \dfrac{1}{2400}\right)\right] = \exp(-0.88) \approx 0.41
$$

Hence a **59 %** drop in NOx — empirically confirmed. (Full numerical worked example given in Section 3.3 above.)

**(b) PCV & EVAP Systems:**

**PCV System:**
- A spring-loaded **PCV valve** is mounted in the rocker cover or valve cover, connecting the crankcase to the intake manifold.
- At idle (high manifold vacuum), the valve is mostly closed, allowing only a small flow — preventing an overly lean mixture.
- At part throttle (moderate vacuum), the valve is partially open, allowing a metered blow-by flow into the intake.
- At WOT (low vacuum), the valve is fully open, routing the maximum blow-by to the intake.
- **Effect:** Eliminates ~ **20 %** of total HC emissions from older uncontrolled engines; also prevents crankcase sludge and pressure build-up.

**EVAP System:**
- A **charcoal canister** (activated carbon, ~ 1–2 L volume) is connected to the fuel tank and the liquid-vapour separator.
- Fuel vapours from the tank (especially during soak and refuelling) are adsorbed onto the carbon.
- A **purge solenoid valve** (duty-cycle controlled by the ECU) connects the canister to the intake manifold.
- During engine operation, intake vacuum draws the stored vapours into the engine, regenerating the canister.

**Why Purge is Enabled Only During Closed-Loop Operation:**
- If the canister were purged during **open-loop** (cold start, idle, wide-open throttle, or after a fuel cut), the additional fuel vapours would:
  1. Disturb the air-fuel ratio, since the ECU has not yet entered closed-loop trimming.
  2. Cause a temporary **enrichment** (rich spike) that the open-loop fueling map cannot compensate for → spike in CO and HC emissions.
- During **closed-loop**, the HEGO sensor detects any perturbation and the ECU immediately trims the injector pulse-width to bring λ back to 1.000. Thus, the canister purge is "free" of emission penalty.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Students frequently claim that the PCV system "filters" crankcase gases. It does *not* filter — it *routes* the unfiltered gases into the engine for re-burning. The "valve" is a *flow regulator*, not a filter. Marks are deducted for this misconception.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TWC** is a **three-way** catalyst because it performs three simultaneous reactions (CO oxidation, HC oxidation, NOx reduction). All three reach **> 95 %** efficiency *only* in the narrow lambda window **0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01**.
- The **HEGO (O₂) sensor** is a **zirconia** Nernst cell that outputs a **step voltage** (~ 0.1 V lean ↔ 0.9 V rich) at λ = 1.000. The ECU uses this in a **PI closed-loop** to dither λ around stoichiometry.
- **EGR** is the primary **in-cylinder** NOx control technique. It works by **diluting** the charge and **lowering peak temperature**. A 100–150 K drop in $T_{\text{peak}}$ (via 10–20 % EGR) reduces NOx by **60–80 %**.
- **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = 14.7** (must know the derivation: balance C₈H₁₈ + 12.5 O₂ → 8 CO₂ + 9 H₂O; convert moles to mass; divide by 0.232 mass fraction of O₂ in air).
- **PCV** routes blow-by HC to the intake; it is **not a filter** but a **metered re-circulator**. The PCV valve opens with decreasing manifold vacuum.
- **EVAP** captures fuel-tank vapours in an **activated-carbon canister**; purge is enabled only during **closed-loop** to avoid disturbing the open-loop AFR.
- **Secondary air injection** addresses the **cold-start HC spike** by oxidising HC and CO in the exhaust manifold before the catalyst lights off at ~ 250 °C.
- The **Zeldovich mechanism** explains thermal NOx formation. The Arrhenius form gives the strong temperature dependence: **a 100 K drop ≈ 50 % NOx reduction**.
- The **catalyst light-off temperature** is the threshold above which conversion efficiency rises sharply: **~ 250 °C for CO/HC**, **~ 400 °C for NOx**.
- **Ceria (CeO₂)** in the washcoat is the **oxygen storage component (OSC)** that allows the TWC to "ride through" transient air-fuel perturbations.
- **Rhodium** is the *only* precious metal that catalyses **NOx reduction** effectively — Pt and Pd cannot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Diesel Engines - CRDi Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advanced Diesel Engines - CRDi Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)** is an advanced diesel fuel injection technology in which a high-pressure **fuel accumulator (the "common rail")** supplies pressurised diesel fuel to electronically controlled solenoid or piezoelectric injectors via a shared manifold. The injection pressure (typically **1,350 – 2,500 bar**) is generated independently of engine speed and injection timing, allowing precise, multi-event fuel delivery per cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT752, Module 1):** Students must be able to describe the construction, working, components, and advantages of the CRDi system and compare it with the conventional jerk-pump/rotary-pump fuel injection system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building's Water Tank"

Imagine a tall overhead water tank in an apartment building:

- The **pump** fills the tank continuously with pressurised water.
- The **tank (common rail)** stores it at a constant high pressure, regardless of how many flats open their taps.
- Each **tap (injector)** in every flat can be opened independently, at any moment, with the desired flow.
- The **building manager (ECU)** decides which tap opens, when, and for how long.

In a conventional jerk-pump system, each cylinder must build up its own pressure just before injection — like every flat pumping water from a well on demand. CRDi decouples **pressure generation from injection timing**, giving far more flexibility.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Common Rail** | High-pressure fuel accumulator (reservoir) common to all injectors |
| **ECU** | Electronic Control Unit – the brain that commands injector opening and pump pressure |
| **Pilot Injection** | A small pre-injection of fuel just before the main event, to soften combustion knock |
| **Piezo Injector** | An injector actuated by a piezoelectric crystal stack (faster response than solenoid) |
| **Injection Pressure** | Rail pressure; modern systems reach **2,000 – 2,500 bar** (Bosch, Denso) |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard working rail pressure in modern passenger-car CRDi engines is approximately **1,600 bar (160 MPa)**, while heavy-duty commercial engines operate closer to **2,000 – 2,500 bar**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Subsystems of a CRDi System

A CRDi system is composed of three functional blocks:

1. **Low-pressure circuit** — fuel tank, feed pump, water separator, and fuel filter.
2. **High-pressure circuit** — high-pressure pump (radial-piston type), common rail, pressure regulator, and pressure limiter.
3. **Electronic control circuit** — ECU, crank/cam sensors, rail-pressure sensor, accelerator pedal position sensor, and injectors.

### 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

- **Step 1 — Suction and Boosting:** The electric feed pump draws diesel from the tank, filters it, and delivers it to the high-pressure pump at a low pressure of about **3 – 5 bar**.
- **Step 2 — Pressure Generation:** A cam-driven **radial-piston pump** (3 pistons arranged at 120° on a star cam) compresses the fuel to a working pressure of up to **2,000 bar**. Each piston's delivery per stroke is small and smooth, giving near-constant rail pressure.
- **Step 3 — Rail Accumulation:** Fuel enters the common rail, a thick-walled steel tube (volumetric capacity ~ 20 – 40 cm³ per cylinder). The rail acts as a hydraulic capacitor, smoothing pressure fluctuations.
- **Step 4 — Electronic Decision:** Sensors (CKP, CMP, rail pressure, coolant temperature, air-mass meter) feed the ECU. The ECU computes the **quantity, timing, pressure, and number of injection events**.
- **Step 5 — Actuation:** The ECU sends a high-current pulse to the injector's solenoid (or a voltage pulse to the piezo stack). The needle lifts against nozzle spring force, and fuel sprays into the combustion chamber at high velocity.
- **Step 6 — Regulation:** A **pressure-control valve** on the rail (and sometimes a return line from the injectors) bleeds excess fuel back to the tank to maintain the set rail pressure.

### 2.3 Multiple Injection Strategy (Why It Matters)

CRDi allows up to **9 separate injection events per combustion cycle**:

| Event | Purpose | Typical Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-injection (Idle)** | Reduce cold-start white smoke | 1 – 2 mm³/stroke |
| **Pilot Injection** | Raise cylinder pressure smoothly → reduce NOₓ and combustion noise | 1 – 4 mm³/stroke |
| **Main Injection** | Deliver the bulk of energy | 80 – 90 % of total fuel |
| **After Injection** | Burn soot on DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter) regeneration | Variable |
| **Late Post Injection** | Hydrocarbon dosing for SCR (NOₓ trap) | Small |

> [!TIP]
> **Why pilot injection works:** A small amount of fuel burned first raises cylinder pressure and temperature gently before the main charge arrives, lowering the **peak rate of pressure rise (dP/dθ)** and therefore softening the diesel "knock."

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_f = \dfrac{m_f}{V_f}$ | Density of diesel fuel (typically **830 – 850 kg/m³** at 15 °C); used for mass↔volume conversion of injected fuel |
| 2 | $P_{rail} = \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{needle}}$ | Static rail pressure just balancing the nozzle needle spring |
| 3 | $q_{inj} = A_{nozzle} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho_f}} \cdot \Delta t$ | Volumetric flow rate through injector nozzle (orifice area $A_{nozzle}$, discharge coefficient $C_d \approx 0.85$) |
| 4 | $\dot{V}_{pump} = n_{p} \cdot V_{disp,p} \cdot N_{eng}$ | Theoretical pump delivery rate (m³/s); 3-piston radial pump has $V_{disp,p} \approx 0.2$ – $1.5$ mm³ per stroke |
| 5 | $P_{pump} = \dot{V}_{pump} \cdot \Delta P_{rail}$ | Hydraulic power required to charge the rail |
| 6 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{\dot{W}_b}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption (kg/kWh); CRDi targets **200 – 220 g/kWh** |
| 7 | $\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | Brake thermal efficiency |
| 8 | $\eta_m = \dfrac{\dot{W}_b}{\dot{W}_i}$ | Mechanical efficiency (ratio of brake to indicated power) |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dP}{d\theta} \propto \dfrac{\Delta P_{rail}}{V_{cyl}}$ | Rate of pressure rise; minimized by pilot injection |
| 10 | $E_{rail} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,P_{rail}\,\Delta V_{rail}$ | Energy stored in the rail due to small volume fluctuation $\Delta V_{rail}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked to "compare with conventional systems," students often miss the **speed-independence** of rail pressure. This is the single most important qualitative difference.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Effective Volumetric Flow Through a CRDi Nozzle

The hydraulic power delivered by the high-pressure pump ultimately becomes the kinetic energy of fuel leaving the injector nozzle. The instantaneous mass flow through one nozzle hole is derived from Bernoulli's equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_v &= A_{n} \, C_d \, \sqrt{\frac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho_f}} \\
\text{where }\Delta P &= P_{rail} - P_{cyl} \\
\text{For a multi-hole nozzle with } k \text{ holes: }\quad q_{v,\text{total}} &= k \, A_{n} \, C_d \, \sqrt{\frac{2\,\Delta P}{\rho_f}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning at each step:**

1. The injector needle lift exposes a sharp-edged orifice of total area $A_n = k \cdot a_{hole}$.
2. Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between the upstream needle seat and downstream chamber, assuming incompressible flow.
3. Solve for the theoretical jet velocity: $v_{th} = \sqrt{2\,\Delta P / \rho_f}$.
4. Multiply by discharge coefficient $C_d$ (accounts for vena-contracta and viscous losses).
5. Integrate over injection duration $\Delta t$ to get the injected volume per event.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** This equation lets calibration engineers predict injection quantity from a measured energising time of the solenoid. The ECU's "injector response map" is built directly from it.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Hydraulic Power Demand of the CRDi Pump

Given a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke engine running at $N_{eng} = 2{,}500$ rpm with target rail pressure $P_{rail} = 1{,}600$ bar and total injected fuel per cycle $V_{inj} = 60$ mm³/cyl.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{inj,\text{total}} &= \frac{N_{eng}}{2} \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot V_{inj} \quad &\text{(4-stroke fires once per 2 rev)}\\[4pt]
&= \frac{2{,}500}{2} \cdot 4 \cdot 60 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}\\[4pt]
&= 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}\\[6pt]
P_{hyd} &= \dot{V}_{inj,\text{total}} \cdot P_{rail}\\[4pt]
&= 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 1{,}600 \times 10^{5}\\[4pt]
&= 48{,}000 \;\text{W} = 48 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** About **48 kW** of mechanical power is consumed purely to pressurise the fuel — a non-trivial parasitic load that justifies the engineering focus on pump efficiency.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Pressure-Energy Buffering in the Common Rail

The rail acts as a hydraulic accumulator. When an injection event occurs, the rail volume decreases by $\Delta V$ as fuel leaves. Assuming the pump cannot instantaneously refill, the rail pressure drops by $\Delta P$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bulk modulus of diesel-oil:}\quad K &\approx 1.5 \times 10^{9} \;\text{Pa}\\[4pt]
\text{Isentropic volume change:}\quad \frac{\Delta V}{V_{rail}} &= \frac{\Delta P}{K}\\[4pt]
\Delta P &= K \cdot \frac{\Delta V}{V_{rail}}\\[4pt]
\text{Energy released by rail:}\quad E_{rail} &= \frac{1}{2}\,P_{rail}\,\Delta V\\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}\,P_{rail} \cdot V_{rail} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical meaning:** The rail stores a small but critical amount of pressure-energy that is released at the instant of injection. A rail of volume $V_{rail} = 20$ cm³ per cylinder, supporting a 1,600 bar system, provides nearly constant pressure to the injector over its short opening time.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CRDi vs Conventional Diesel Injection

| Parameter | Conventional (Inline / Rotary) | CRDi |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure generation | Jerk-pump helix, per cylinder | Central high-pressure pump |
| Peak pressure | 350 – 600 bar | 1,350 – 2,500 bar |
| Pressure vs engine speed | Pressure rises with rpm | **Independent** of engine speed |
| Injection events per cycle | 1 (sometimes 2) | Up to **9** |
| Control | Mechanical / hydraulic | Fully electronic (ECU) |
| Spray atomisation | Coarser | Very fine (Sauter mean diameter $\downarrow$) |
| BSFC | 240 – 280 g/kWh | 200 – 220 g/kWh |
| NOₓ & PM emissions | Higher | Lower (EGR + DPF compatible) |
| Cold-start noise | Loud knock | Suppressed via pilot injection |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** A CRDi system is best described as a **block-level functional architecture** because its physical plumbing is highly compact. The mermaid diagrams below map the flow of fuel, electrical signals, and feedback in a single integrated view.

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Fuel and Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TANK[FUEL TANK] --> FEED[FEED PUMP 3 to 5 BAR]
    FEED --> FILT[FUEL FILTER]
    FILT --> HPP[RADIAL PISTON HIGH PRESSURE PUMP]
    HPP --> RAIL[COMMON RAIL 1600 BAR]
    RAIL --> INV1[INJECTOR CYL 1]
    RAIL --> INV2[INJECTOR CYL 2]
    RAIL --> INV3[INJECTOR CYL 3]
    RAIL --> INV4[INJECTOR CYL 4]
    INV1 --> ENG1[COMBUSTION CYL 1]
    INV2 --> ENG2[COMBUSTION CYL 2]
    INV3 --> ENG3[COMBUSTION CYL 3]
    INV4 --> ENG4[COMBUSTION CYL 4]
    HPP --> PCV[PRESSURE CONTROL VALVE]
    PCV --> RET[FUEL RETURN LINE]
    PCV --> TANK
    ECU[ECU ELECTRONIC CONTROL UNIT] -.->|PWM SIGNAL| INV1
    ECU -.->|PWM SIGNAL| INV2
    ECU -.->|PWM SIGNAL| INV3
    ECU -.->|PWM SIGNAL| INV4
    ECU -.->|COMMAND| HPP
    RPS[RAIL PRESSURE SENSOR] --> ECU
    CKP[CRANKSHAFT POSITION SENSOR] --> ECU
    CMP[CAMSHAFT POSITION SENSOR] --> ECU
    APP[ACCELERATOR PEDAL SENSOR] --> ECU
    AFS[AIR FLOW SENSOR] --> ECU
```

### 4.2 Injection Event Sequence Per Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CR[CRANKSHAFT REFERENCE TDC] --> P0[INTAKE STROKE]
    P0 --> P1[COMPRESSION STROKE BEGIN]
    P1 --> P2[PILOT INJECTION 10 to 30 DEG BTDC]
    P2 --> P3[PRE COMBUSTION PRESSURE RISE]
    P3 --> P4[MAIN INJECTION 5 to 10 DEG BTDC]
    P4 --> P5[POWER STROKE COMBUSTION]
    P5 --> P6[AFTER INJECTION FOR DPF REGEN]
    P6 --> P7[EXHAUST STROKE]
    P7 --> CR
```

### 4.3 ECU Closed-Loop Pressure Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SET[SETPOINT RAIL PRESSURE 1600 BAR] --> CMP_PID[PID CONTROLLER INSIDE ECU]
    CMP_PID --> VALVE[COMMAND TO PRESSURE CONTROL VALVE]
    VALVE --> ACT[Rail Pressure changes]
    ACT --> SENS[RAIL PRESSURE SENSOR MEASUREMENT]
    SENS --> FBK[FEEDBACK TO ECU]
    FBK --> CMP_PID
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
