The SCV is a normally-open proportional valve on the inlet of the high-pressure pump. By modulating its duty cycle (PWM signal from the ECU), the ECU controls how much fuel enters the pumping chamber. A higher duty cycle admits more fuel and increases rail pressure; a lower duty cycle restricts fuel inflow and reduces rail pressure.

**Step 5 — PI/PID Control Algorithm:**
The ECU applies a Proportional-Integral (PI) or PID algorithm to minimize the error:
$$
u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}
$$
where $u(t)$ is the SCV duty cycle command.

**Step 6 — Iterative Correction:**
This loop executes every **5 to 10 ms**, ensuring rail pressure stays within $\pm$ **2 MPa** of target even during rapid transients (tip-in, tip-out).

**Step 7 — Fault Handling:**
If $P_{actual}$ deviates by more than a threshold (e.g., $\pm$ 10 MPa), the ECU stores a DTC (Diagnostic Trouble Code), illuminates the MIL, and enters **limp-home mode** to protect injectors and pump.

**Role Summary:**
- **RPS** $\rightarrow$ Provides real-time pressure feedback.
- **SCV** $\rightarrow$ Acts as the final control element (actuator).
- **ECU** $\rightarrow$ Computes error, executes control law, drives SCV, manages diagnostics.

**[Marks Distribution: Target pressure — 1, Sensing — 1, Error calculation — 1, SCV function — 1.5, PID control — 1.5, Iteration and diagnostics — 1]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing RPS with MAP sensor:** The RPS measures *fuel* rail pressure, NOT intake manifold pressure. MAP sensors typically operate at 0.1–0.4 MPa.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Rail pressure is often quoted in *bar* or *MPa*. Mixing units is the #1 cause of numerical mistakes. Always convert to SI (Pa) before substitution.
> 3. **Sign convention in Wheatstone bridge:** Students often write $V_{out} = V_s (\Delta R / R)$ without the minus sign. The polarity depends on the bridge orientation; both signs are technically correct if consistent.
> 4. **Missing the isolation diaphragm detail:** Many students draw the silicon diaphragm in direct contact with fuel — this is WRONG. Always mention the **isolation diaphragm** and **silicone oil fill**.
> 5. **Ignoring ratiometric output:** The 0.5 V–4.5 V range is NOT arbitrary — it is ratiometric to the 5 V supply, ensuring noise immunity and supply-independent calibration.
> 6. **Not mentioning OBD-II diagnostics:** For 14-mark questions on RPS, always include the role in OBD-II / emission monitoring. Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [x] The **Rail Pressure Sensor** is a piezoresistive pressure transducer mounted on the common rail.
- [x] It uses a **Wheatstone bridge** of four piezoresistors diffused on a silicon diaphragm.
- [x] Typical operating range: **25 – 200 MPa** (modern systems up to 250 MPa).
- [x] Typical output: **0.5 – 4.5 V** ratiometric to a **5 V** supply.
- [x] The sensor has **three wires**: supply (5 V), ground, and signal.
- [x] The sensing diaphragm is **isolated from fuel** by a stainless-steel diaphragm and silicone oil.
- [x] Diaphragm deflection is in the **micrometer range** (typically 50–150 $\mu$m at full scale).
- [x] Key formula: $V_{out} = V_s \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ for a fully active bridge.
- [x] Diaphragm deflection: $w_{max} = \dfrac{3 P r^4 (1 - \nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$.
- [x] The RPS is part of a **closed-loop PI/PID control** with the SCV on the HP pump.
- [x] OBD-II fault codes: **P0190, P0191, P0192, P0193, P0194**.
- [x] Failure triggers **limp-home mode** to protect injectors and pump.
- [x] Accuracy requirement: **$\pm$ 0.5 % to $\pm$ 2 %** of Full Scale.
- [x] Response time: **less than 2 ms** to track rapid pressure transients.
- [x] Operating temperature: **$-$40°C to +150°C** with ASIC-based temperature compensation.
- [x] ECU calibration equation: $P_{rail} = m \cdot V_{signal} + c$ where $m$ is the slope (MPa/V) and $c$ is the offset.
- [x] The sensor enables **multi-pilot injection** strategies for BS-VI / Euro-VI emission compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Width
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pulse Width

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Width ($t_p$)** in the context of advanced Internal Combustion (IC) engines is defined as the **temporal duration** (measured in milliseconds, $\mu s$, or degrees of crank rotation) for which the **electronic control unit (ECU)** energizes the **fuel injector solenoid** (or piezo actuator), thereby commanding the injector needle to remain lifted and fuel to be delivered into the combustion chamber.

In the **2024 Scheme KTU syllabus** for *Advanced IC Engines and Combustion*, Pulse Width is treated as the **master control variable** of any **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)** system — be it:
- **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)**
- **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)** for diesel
- **Port Fuel Injection (PFI)**

The ECU manipulates $t_p$ in real-time (closed-loop) based on inputs from the **Mass Air Flow (MAF)** sensor, **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP)** sensor, **Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT)** sensor, **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)**, **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)**, and the **lambda ($\lambda$) oxygen sensor** feedback.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🚰

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Pulse Width as a WATER TAP held open by a TIMED ROBOT HAND.** 🤖🚰
>
> Imagine you are extremely thirsty and a robotic hand is holding a water tap open. The *longer* the robot keeps its hand on the tap (i.e., the longer the "pulse"), the *more water flows*. The ECU is that robot. It "pulses" the injector open for a precise microsecond duration. Doubling the pulse = doubling the fuel = doubling the energy released during combustion.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pulse Width (Gasoline PFI) | $t_p$ | **1.5 – 20** | ms |
| Pulse Width (Diesel CRDi) | $t_p$ | **0.2 – 1.0** | ms |
| Injector Static Flow Rate | $q$ | **100 – 500** | cm³/min |
| Battery Voltage Compensation | $V_c$ | **8 – 16** | V |
| Energizing Time (Pull-in) | $t_{on}$ | **0.1 – 0.5** | ms |

## 1.4 Why Pulse Width Matters in Advanced IC Engines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse width is the single most manipulated parameter for achieving the precise air–fuel ratio ($\lambda$) demanded by modern emission norms (BS-VI / Euro 6).** Even a deviation of **$\pm 0.1$ ms** can shift $\lambda$ by **$\pm 1\%$**, which directly affects tailpipe **HC, CO, NOx, and PM emissions**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition — How Pulse Width Works

The injection event is not a single rectangular pulse but a **structured sequence** governed by electromechanical and fluid-dynamic laws:

1. **ECU Triggering:** The CKP sensor sends a 60-minus-2 tooth wheel signal. The ECU identifies the **Top Dead Center (TDC)** reference and schedules an injection event at a preset crank-angle window.
2. **Solenoid Energization:** A 12V (or boosted 50–90V for piezo) signal is applied across the injector coil. The **pull-in current** (peak) magnetically lifts the needle against the spring preload.
3. **Hold Phase:** The current drops to a lower **hold current** to prevent coil overheating, while the needle remains fully open.
4. **Fuel Delivery:** Fuel flows through the nozzle at the **static flow rate $q$** for the duration $t_p$.
5. **De-energization:** The signal is cut, the spring snaps the needle shut, and injection ends.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Sub-Distinction:** The ECU "commanded" pulse width is NOT the same as the "effective" injection duration. There exists an **injector dead time** ($t_d$ or $t_{offset}$), typically 0.6–1.2 ms, during which the solenoid is energizing but no fuel flows.

## 2.2 Injection Strategies Governed by Pulse Width

| Strategy | Description | Pulse Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Pulse** | One pulse per cycle (idle/low load) | ▭ |
| **Double Pulse** | Pilot + Main (diesel HCCI) | ▭▭ |
| **Multi-Pulse** | Pilot + Main + After + Post (CRDi) | ▭▭▭▭ |
| **Modulated Pulse Width (PWM)** | Variable $t_p$ to control lambda feedback | ▭▱▭▱▭ |
| **Phased Pulse** | Camshaft-referenced for individual cylinder control | ▭ ▭ ▭ ▭ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Cheat Code" Equations for Pulse Width Problems** — Memorize these for Part A and Part B.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = q \cdot t_p$ | Total fuel delivered per injection pulse | mm³ or mg |
| 2 | $Q_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} q \cdot t_{p,i}$ | Sum of multi-pulse injections per cycle | mm³ |
| 3 | $t_{p,eff} = t_{p,cmd} - t_{d}$ | Effective pulse width after dead time | ms |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_f = q \cdot \rho_f \cdot \frac{t_p}{60}$ | Mass fuel flow rate (per second, per injector) | kg/s |
| 5 | $\frac{A}{F} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Air–Fuel ratio from fuel quantity | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_a = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2 \cdot 60}$ | Air mass flow (4-stroke, 1 cyl) | kg/s |
| 7 | $t_p = K \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda_{target}} \cdot \frac{1}{V_{comp}}$ | ECU base pulse width algorithm (simplified) | ms |
| 8 | $\Delta t_p = t_{p,new} - t_{p,old}$ | Short-term fuel trim (STFT) correction | ms |
| 9 | $E_{inj} = \frac{1}{2} L \cdot I^2$ | Inductive energy stored in solenoid coil | Joules |
| 10 | $t_{d} = f(V_{bat})$ | Dead time as function of battery voltage (lookup) | ms |

> **Legend:** $Q$ = fuel quantity, $q$ = static flow rate, $t_p$ = pulse width, $t_d$ = injector dead time, $\rho_f$ = fuel density, $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency, $V_d$ = displacement, $N$ = RPM, $L$ = coil inductance, $I$ = current.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emissions Control:** The diesel **NOx–Particulate Matter (PM) trade-off** is managed by splitting fuel into a small **pilot pulse** (~0.05 ms) followed by the **main pulse** — reducing peak in-cylinder temperature.
- **Cold Start Enrichment:** At $-20^\circ C$, $t_p$ may be increased by **200–400\%** to compensate for poor fuel atomization and wall-wetting.
- **Altitude Compensation:** ECU increases $t_p$ at high altitude (low MAP) to maintain stoich $\lambda = 1$.
- **Engine Braking Deceleration Fuel Cut:** $t_p$ is set to **0 ms** during over-run to save fuel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Fuel Quantity from Pulse Width

We now derive the master equation $Q = q \cdot t_p$ from first principles of fluid mechanics.

### Step 1 — Definition of Static Flow Rate
The injector manufacturer specifies the **static flow rate** $q$ as the volumetric flow of fuel (typically at 3.0 bar pressure differential, $43.5$ psi) over a 60-second interval.

$$
q = \frac{V_{fuel}}{t_{ref}}
$$

where $t_{ref} = 60$ s and $V_{fuel}$ is the volume delivered.

### Step 2 — Linear Assumption for Short Pulses
For pulse widths much shorter than 60 s (which is always the case — typical $t_p$ is in **milliseconds**), the volumetric flow is assumed **linear** with respect to time. This is the *quasi-steady* assumption valid for $t_p \geq 1$ ms in modern injectors.

$$
\frac{V_{fuel}}{t_{ref}} = \frac{V_{inj}}{t_p}
$$

### Step 3 — Cross-Multiplication
Solving for the injected volume $V_{inj}$:

$$
V_{inj} = q \cdot t_p
$$

Since the volume of fuel delivered per injection is our quantity of interest, we obtain the master equation:

$$
\boxed{Q = q \cdot t_p}
$$

### Step 4 — Conversion to Mass
Fuel mass is obtained by multiplying by fuel density $\rho_f$ (gasoline ≈ **750 kg/m³**, diesel ≈ **835 kg/m³**):

$$
m_f = \rho_f \cdot q \cdot t_p
$$

### Step 5 — Per-Cycle and Per-Second Fuel Flow
For an engine running at $N$ RPM with $n_{inj}$ injections per cycle and $N_c$ cylinders:

$$
\dot{m}_{f,total} = \frac{n_{inj} \cdot N_c \cdot N \cdot \rho_f \cdot q \cdot t_p}{2 \cdot 60}
$$

The factor 2 accounts for the 4-stroke cycle (one firing per 2 revolutions).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke gasoline engine runs at $3000$ RPM. Each cylinder uses a PFI injector with static flow rate $q = 200$ cm³/min. The ECU commands a pulse width $t_p = 8$ ms. The injector dead time $t_d = 1.0$ ms. Fuel density $\rho_f = 750$ kg/m³. Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.85$. Displacement $V_d = 1.998$ L. Ambient conditions: $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, $T_{amb} = 300$ K. Find:
> (a) Effective fuel mass per injection per cylinder.
> (b) Total fuel mass flow rate.
> (c) Air mass flow rate.
> (d) Resulting Air–Fuel ratio.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Effective Pulse Width:**

$$
t_{p,eff} = t_{p,cmd} - t_d = 8.0 - 1.0 = 7.0 \text{ ms}
$$

**Effective Fuel Volume per Injection:**

First, convert flow rate: $q = 200$ cm³/min $= \dfrac{200}{60 \cdot 1000} = 0.003333$ cm³/ms

$$
V_{inj} = q \cdot t_{p,eff} = 0.003333 \cdot 7.0 = 0.02333 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Effective Fuel Mass per Injection:**

$$
m_{f,inj} = \rho_f \cdot V_{inj} = 750 \cdot 0.02333 \cdot 10^{-6} = 1.75 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg}
$$

**Part (b) — Total Fuel Mass Flow Rate:**

For 4-stroke: one injection per cylinder per 2 revolutions.

$$
\dot{m}_{f,total} = \frac{N_c \cdot m_{f,inj} \cdot N}{2 \cdot 60}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{f,total} = \frac{4 \cdot 1.75 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 3000}{120} = 1.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Part (c) — Air Mass Flow Rate:**

Using ideal gas: $\rho_a = \dfrac{p_{atm}}{R_a \cdot T_{amb}} = \dfrac{101325}{287 \cdot 300} = 1.177$ kg/m³

$$
\dot{m}_a = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2 \cdot 60}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a = 0.85 \cdot 1.177 \cdot 1.998 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{3000}{120}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a = 0.85 \cdot 1.177 \cdot 1.998 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 25 = 0.04996 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Part (d) — Air–Fuel Ratio:**

$$
\frac{A}{F} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_{f,total}} = \frac{0.04996}{0.00175} = 28.55
$$

> **Interpretation:** Stoichiometric $A/F$ for gasoline is **14.7**. Here $A/F = 28.55$, which is nearly **2× stoich** — indicating a **lean-burn or part-throttle** condition. ✅ Result is consistent with cruise-mode operation.

---

## 3.3 Multi-Pulse Analytical Matrix

For a **CRDi diesel** with Pilot + Main + After injection strategy:

| Pulse | Timing (° BTDC) | $t_p$ (ms) | $q$ (cm³/min) | $Q_i$ (cm³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pilot 1 | 25° | 0.20 | 400 | 0.00133 |
| Pilot 2 | 15° | 0.25 | 400 | 0.00167 |
| Main | 5° | 0.80 | 400 | 0.00533 |
| After | 40° ATDC | 0.30 | 400 | 0.00200 |

$$
Q_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} q \cdot t_{p,i} = q \cdot \sum t_{p,i}
$$

$$
\sum t_{p,i} = 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.80 + 0.30 = 1.55 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 400 \cdot \frac{1.55}{60 \cdot 1000} = 0.01033 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ per cycle per cylinder}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_SENSORS["INPUT SENSORS"]
        A1[MAF Sensor]:::sensor
        A2[MAP Sensor]:::sensor
        A3[TPS Sensor]:::sensor
        A4[ECT Sensor]:::sensor
        A5[CKP Sensor]:::sensor
        A6[Lambda O2 Sensor]:::sensor
    end

    subgraph ECU_PROCESSING["ECU PROCESSING UNIT"]
        B1[Signal Conditioning & ADC]:::proc
        B2[Base Pulse Width Map 3D Lookup]:::proc
        B3[Correction Multipliers]:::proc
        B4[Closed Loop PID Controller]:::proc
        B5[Injector Dead Time Lookup]:::proc
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_STAGE["OUTPUT DRIVER STAGE"]
        C1[PWM Driver IC]:::driver
        C2[Boost Converter 12V to 90V]:::driver

        C4[Injector Needle Mechanism]:::driver
    end

    subgraph ENGINE_FEEDBACK["ENGINE OUTPUT"]
        D1[Combustion Chamber]:::engine
        D2[Lambda Feedback to O2 Sensor]:::engine
    end

    A1 --> B1

    A4 --> B1

```

## 4.2 Pulse Width Timing Waveform — Single vs. Multi-Pulse

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SP["SINGLE PULSE WAVE"]
        SP1[Time t equals 0]:::time
        SP2[Command t_p 8.0 ms]:::pulse
        SP3[Dead Time t_d 1.0 ms]:::dead
        SP4[Effective Flow 7.0 ms]:::flow
        SP1 --> SP2
        SP2 --> SP3
        SP3 --> SP4
    end

    subgraph MP["MULTI PULSE DIESEL CRDi"]
        MP1[Pilot 0.2 ms]:::pulse
        MP2[Pilot 0.25 ms]:::pulse
        MP3[Main 0.80 ms]:::pulse
        MP4[After 0.30 ms]:::pulse
        MP1 --> MP2
        MP2 --> MP3
        MP3 --> MP4
    end

    classDef time fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pulse fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dead fill:#FF6347,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef flow fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 ECU Pulse Width Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Cranking]:::start --> B{ECT greater than 80C}
    B -->|NO Cold Start| C[Apply Enrichment Multiplier 2x to 4x]:::cold
    B -->|YES Warm| D[Use Base Map from 3D Lookup]:::base
    C --> E[Add Cranking Pulse 10 to 20 ms]:::crank
    D --> F{MAF greater than threshold}
    E --> F
    F -->|YES High Load| G[Schedule Multi Pulse Injection]:::multi
    F -->|NO Low Load| H[Schedule Single Pulse PFI]:::single
    G --> I[Apply Battery Voltage Compensation]:::comp
    H --> I
    I --> J[Subtract Dead Time from t_p]:::sub
    J --> K[Output Final t_p to Driver]:::output
    K --> L[Lambda Sensor Reads A or F]:::lambda
    L -->|Off Stoich| M[Apply STFT LTFT Correction]:::correct
    L -->|At Stoich| N[Hold Pulse Width Steady]:::hold
    M --> D

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef cold fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    classDef multi fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef single fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#40E0D0,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef lambda fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef correct fill:#FF6347,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef hold fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define pulse width in the context of an Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) system. State the typical range of pulse width for port-fuel-injected gasoline engines.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> Pulse width ($t_p$) is the duration (in milliseconds) for which the ECU energizes the fuel injector solenoid, keeping the injector open and allowing fuel to flow into the engine.
>
> For port fuel injection (PFI) gasoline engines, the typical range of pulse width is **1.5 to 20 ms**, depending on engine load, RPM, and operating conditions. **(3 marks)**

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is injector dead time, and why is it significant in pulse-width calculations?**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> Injector dead time ($t_d$) is the brief interval (typically **0.6 – 1.2 ms**) between the moment the ECU energizes the injector coil and the actual start of fuel flow. During this interval, the magnetic field builds up, the needle overcomes spring preload, and fuel begins to atomize.
>
> It is significant because the *effective* pulse width — which determines actual fuel delivery — equals the *commanded* pulse width minus $t_d$:
> $$t_{p,eff} = t_{p,cmd} - t_d$$
> Ignoring dead time leads to incorrect fueling and air–fuel ratio errors. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

**A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine operates at 3500 RPM under closed-loop stoichiometric control. The PFI injectors have a static flow rate of 250 cm³/min. The commanded pulse width is 7.5 ms, and the injector dead time is 0.9 ms. Fuel density is 750 kg/m³. The engine displacement is 2.0 L with a volumetric efficiency of 0.88. Intake air conditions: 100 kPa, 305 K.**

**(a)** Calculate the effective fuel mass per injection per cylinder and the total fuel mass flow rate of the engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the air mass flow rate and verify whether the engine is operating at stoichiometric air–fuel ratio. Comment on the lambda value. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Effective Fuel Mass and Total Fuel Mass Flow Rate**

**Step 1: Effective Pulse Width**

$$
t_{p,eff} = 7.5 - 0.9 = 6.6 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Static Flow Rate in Compatible Units**

$$
q = 250 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} = \frac{250}{60 \times 1000} = 0.004167 \text{ cm}^3/\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3: Fuel Volume per Injection**

$$
V_{inj} = q \times t_{p,eff} = 0.004167 \times 6.6 = 0.0275 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 4: Fuel Mass per Injection**

$$
m_{f,inj} = \rho_f \times V_{inj} = 750 \times 0.0275 \times 10^{-6} = 2.0625 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5: Total Fuel Mass Flow Rate (4-stroke, 4 cylinders)**

$$
\dot{m}_{f,total} = \frac{N_c \times m_{f,inj} \times N}{2 \times 60} = \frac{4 \times 2.0625 \times 10^{-5} \times 3500}{120}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{f,total} = 2.406 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Air Mass Flow Rate and Lambda Verification**

**Step 1: Air Density Using Ideal Gas Law**

$$
\rho_a = \frac{p}{R_a \times T} = \frac{100{,}000}{287 \times 305} = 1.142 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Air Mass Flow Rate**

$$
\dot{m}_a = \eta_v \times \rho_a \times V_d \times \frac{N}{2 \times 60}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a = 0.88 \times 1.142 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{3500}{120}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a = 0.88 \times 1.142 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 29.167 = 0.05862 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 3: Air–Fuel Ratio**

$$
\frac{A}{F} = \frac{0.05862}{0.002406} = 24.36
$$

**Step 4: Lambda Calculation**

Stoichiometric $A/F$ for gasoline = 14.7

$$
\lambda = \frac{A/F_{actual}}{A/F_{stoich}} = \frac{24.36}{14.7} = 1.657
$$

**Comment:** $\lambda = 1.657 > 1$, the engine is operating in a **lean-burn** condition. This is inconsistent with "closed-loop stoichiometric control," which should hold $\lambda \approx 1.0 \pm 0.02$. The discrepancy suggests either (i) incorrect $\eta_v$ assumption, (ii) the pulse width is miscomputed, or (iii) the lambda sensor feedback has a malfunction. **The system would normally correct this within 50–100 ms via STFT adjustment.** **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> Students often forget to convert cm³/min to cm³/ms before multiplying by $t_p$ in ms. This single error causes a **60× wrong answer** and full marks lost in step 2. Always unit-check!

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`

**A modern CRDi diesel engine uses a multi-pulse injection strategy with three pulses: a pilot pulse of 0.18 ms, a main pulse of 0.75 ms, and an after pulse of 0.32 ms. The injector static flow rate is 380 cm³/min at 1350 bar rail pressure. The engine is a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke unit running at 2800 RPM. The injection dead time is 0.4 ms.**

**(a)** Calculate the total fuel volume delivered per cycle per cylinder, and the total fuel mass flow rate. Take diesel density as 835 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the engine brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) is 220 g/kWh and brake power is 75 kW, verify whether the calculated fuel flow rate is consistent with the indicated power output, and discuss the role of multi-pulse injection in controlling NOx and noise. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Total Fuel Volume and Mass Flow Rate**

**Step 1: Sum of Pulse Widths (with dead time applied to first pulse only — approximation)**

$$
t_{p,total} = 0.18 + 0.75 + 0.32 = 1.25 \text{ ms}
$$

> Note: The dead time $t_d = 0.4$ ms occurs only once at the *start* of the energizing event. For multi-pulse separated by intervals, $t_d$ applies to the leading edge of the entire event.

**Step 2: Static Flow Rate in Compatible Units**

$$
q = 380 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} = \frac{380}{60 \times 1000} = 0.006333 \text{ cm}^3/\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3: Total Fuel Volume per Cycle per Cylinder**

$$
V_{cycle} = q \times t_{p,total} = 0.006333 \times 1.25 = 0.007917 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 4: Fuel Mass per Cycle per Cylinder**

$$
m_{f,cycle} = \rho_f \times V_{cycle} = 835 \times 0.007917 \times 10^{-6} = 6.611 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5: Total Fuel Mass Flow Rate (4 cylinders, 4-stroke)**

$$
\dot{m}_{f,total} = \frac{N_c \times m_{f,cycle} \times N}{2 \times 60} = \frac{4 \times 6.611 \times 10^{-6} \times 2800}{120}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{f,total} = 6.170 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} = 2.221 \text{ kg/h} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Verification of Power Output and Multi-Pulse Discussion**

**Step 1: Theoretical Fuel Consumption from BSFC**

$$
\dot{m}_{f,BSFC} = \frac{BSFC \times P_b}{1000} = \frac{0.220 \times 75}{1000} = 0.0165 \text{ kg/s} = 59.4 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 2: Comparison**

Our calculated fuel flow = **2.22 kg/h**, but the BSFC-derived requirement is **59.4 kg/h**.

**Discrepancy Analysis:**

The huge gap (≈ **27× lower**) indicates that the pulse width values given (0.18 + 0.75 + 0.32 = 1.25 ms) correspond to a **low-load or idle condition**, NOT the 75 kW full-load condition. At full load, a CRDi injector at 1350 bar rail pressure would have $t_{p,total} \approx 8$–12 ms (nearly **10× higher**). The problem statement contains an inconsistency that the student should flag.

> Alternatively, the "0.18 ms" might be a **typo for 1.8 ms** (a common mistake in KTU problem statements). If $t_{p,total} = 1.8 + 7.5 + 3.2 = 12.5$ ms, the calculation yields 19.78 kg/h, closer to 59.4 kg/h (the difference being attributable to mechanical efficiency, typically 90%, accounting for ~10% loss).

**Step 3: Role of Multi-Pulse in NOx and Noise Control**

| Pulse | Purpose | Effect on NOx | Effect on Noise |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pilot** | Pre-mixture preparation, low-temperature heat release | ↑ Slightly | ↓ Significantly (ramps up cylinder pressure gradually) |
| **Main** | Main energy release, work output | Main contributor | Main contributor (sharp rise) |
| **After** | Burn off soot, regenerate DPF | ↓ (soot oxidation) | ↑ Slightly |

By **splitting** the main combustion event into multiple small pulses, the **peak in-cylinder temperature is reduced**, which suppresses **thermal NOx** formation (Zeldovich mechanism). Simultaneously, the **pressure rise rate (dP/dθ)** is gentler, which reduces **combustion noise and engine knock**. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:**
> Many students apply the dead time $t_d$ to *each* pulse in a multi-pulse sequence, leading to $t_{p,eff} = (0.18 - 0.4) + (0.75 - 0.4) + (0.32 - 0.4) < 0$. **NEVER** do this! Dead time applies to the **leading edge of the entire injection event**, not to each pulse. Partial credit is awarded if you state this clearly.

---

## ⚡ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU 14-Mark Rapid Revision Checklist for "Pulse Width"**

- ✅ Pulse width $t_p$ = duration of injector energization by ECU
- ✅ Master equation: $Q = q \cdot t_p$ (fuel volume per injection)
- ✅ Effective pulse width: $t_{p,eff} = t_{p,cmd} - t_d$ (always subtract dead time)
- ✅ Dead time $t_d \approx 0.6$–$1.2$ ms (voltage-dependent, lookup table based)
- ✅ Multi-pulse strategies: Pilot + Main + After = control of **NOx, PM, and noise**
- ✅ $A/F$ stoich for gasoline = **14.7**; for diesel = **14.5**
- ✅ Lambda $\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow$ stoich; $\lambda > 1 \Rightarrow$ lean; $\lambda < 1 \Rightarrow$ rich
- ✅ Cold start enrichment: $t_p$ multiplied by **2× to 4×**
- ✅ Closed-loop feedback: O2 sensor → STFT → corrects $t_p$ in real-time
- ✅ Static flow rate $q$ is specified at 3.0 bar $\Delta P$ — must convert cm³/min to cm³/ms
- ✅ 4-stroke factor: divide RPM by **120** (not 60) for events per second
- ✅ Engine management: $\dot{m}_a = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot N / (2 \cdot 60)$
- ✅ High-pressure CRDi (1350+ bar) allows **smaller droplets, finer atomization, lower pulse widths**
- ✅ Battery voltage compensation: lower $V_{bat}$ → higher $t_p$ (slows solenoid response)

> 🎓 **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question gives you pulse width + flow rate + RPM, your *first written line* should be the **unit conversion** of $q$ from cm³/min to cm³/ms. This single step wins you 1 mark instantly and shows the examiner you're methodically trained.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duty Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Duty Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT752):**
> **Duty Cycle** of an Internal Combustion (IC) engine is defined as the **ratio of the crankshaft angle (or time duration) during which net positive work is produced on the piston to the total crankshaft angle (or time duration) of one complete thermodynamic cycle.**

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\text{Duty Cycle (DC)} = \frac{\theta_{\text{power stroke}}}{\theta_{\text{total cycle}}}$$

Where:
- $\theta_{\text{power stroke}}$ = Angular duration of the expansion (power) stroke in degrees
- $\theta_{\text{total cycle}}$ = Total angular displacement of the crankshaft for one complete working cycle

The duty cycle is a **dimensionless quantity**, conventionally expressed as a **percentage (%)** or a **fraction**.

---

## 1.2 Standard Classification Values

| Engine Type | Power Stroke Duration | Total Cycle Duration | Duty Cycle |
|:-----------:|:---------------------:|:--------------------:|:----------:|
| **4-Stroke SI/CI Engine** | 180° (½ revolution) | 720° (2 revolutions) | **25 % (1/4)** |
| **2-Stroke SI/CI Engine** | 180° (½ revolution) | 360° (1 revolution) | **50 % (1/2)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The duty cycle is **NOT** the same as the firing frequency. It strictly refers to the **fractional utilization of the cycle** for producing useful positive work.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

**The "Factory Worker" Analogy:**

Imagine two factory workers (engines) operating for an **8-hour shift**:

- **Worker A (4-Stroke Engine):** Out of every 4 hours of work, he produces output for **only 1 hour**, and spends the other 3 hours on preparatory tasks (intake, compression, exhaust). His **duty cycle = 1/4 = 25 %**.
- **Worker B (2-Stroke Engine):** Out of every 2 hours of work, he produces output for **1 hour**, and spends only 1 hour on support tasks. His **duty cycle = 1/2 = 50 %**.

Worker B clearly "works harder" per cycle — but as we will see later, working harder per cycle comes with engineering trade-offs (cooling, scavenging losses, etc.).

This analogy explains *why* a 2-stroke engine theoretically produces **twice the power** of a comparable 4-stroke engine at the same speed and displacement — it is paid for its work **twice as often**.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board-Exam Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Firing frequency (4-stroke):** Once every **2 revolutions** of the crankshaft
> - **Firing frequency (2-stroke):** Once every **1 revolution** of the crankshaft
> - **Standard power stroke angle:** **180°** (crank angle)
> - **Mechanical efficiency penalty in 2-stroke:** typically **20–30 % lower** due to scavenging losses (despite higher duty cycle)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

### Step 1 — Identification of the Power Stroke
In any reciprocating IC engine, **only the expansion (combustion/power) stroke** delivers net positive work on the piston. All other strokes (intake, compression, exhaust) require work input from the flywheel.

### Step 2 — Determination of Crankshaft Angle per Stroke
For a simple crank-slider mechanism, one complete piston stroke corresponds to **180°** of crank rotation.

### Step 3 — Counting Strokes per Cycle
- A **4-stroke engine** requires **4 piston strokes = 720°** of crank rotation for one thermodynamic cycle.
- A **2-stroke engine** requires **2 piston strokes = 360°** of crank rotation for one thermodynamic cycle.

### Step 4 — Formation of the Duty Cycle Ratio

> **Logical Conclusion:**
> "How much of the cycle is actually *productive* (producing positive work)?"

This fraction is the **duty cycle**.

### Step 5 — Engineering Implication
A higher duty cycle means:
- More frequent power impulses per unit time
- Theoretically higher specific power output (kW/litre)
- Higher mean effective pressure (MEP) requirement for the same crank speed
- Increased thermal loading on combustion chamber walls (in 2-strokes)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference — Duty Cycle Module**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|:-:|:------------------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $DC = \dfrac{\theta_{ps}}{\theta_{cycle}}$ | General definition of duty cycle | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $DC_{4s} = \dfrac{180°}{720°} = \dfrac{1}{4}$ | Duty cycle of a 4-stroke engine | — |
| 3 | $DC_{2s} = \dfrac{180°}{360°} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ | Duty cycle of a 2-stroke engine | — |
| 4 | $\dfrac{DC_{2s}}{DC_{4s}} = \dfrac{1/2}{1/4} = 2$ | 2-stroke has **2×** the duty cycle of 4-stroke | — |
| 5 | $P = W_{net} \cdot N \cdot k$ | Indicated Power where $k = 1$ (2-stroke), $k = 1/2$ (4-stroke) | kW |
| 6 | $N_{firing} = \dfrac{N}{2}$ (4-stroke) | Firing frequency in revolutions/sec | Hz |
| 7 | $N_{firing} = N$ (2-stroke) | Firing frequency in revolutions/sec | Hz |
| 8 | $P_{2s,\text{theoretical}} = 2 \cdot P_{4s}$ | Theoretical power ratio at same $N$, $V_d$ | — |

> **Notation Key:** $W_{net}$ = net work per cycle (kJ), $N$ = crank speed (rps), $k$ = firing factor, $V_d$ = displacement volume, $P$ = indicated power.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The duty cycle concept is **not merely academic** — it directly determines:

1. **Power Density** of small engines (chainsaws, marine outboards, mopeds) — these overwhelmingly use 2-stroke designs because of their higher duty cycle.
2. **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) Tuning** — 4-stroke engines have lower duty cycle, hence longer intervals between firings, resulting in smoother torque delivery (preferred for automotive applications).
3. **Cooling System Sizing** — A 50% duty cycle (2-stroke) means **double** the heat flux per unit time on the cylinder head, requiring more aggressive cooling.
4. **Fuel Metering Strategy** — In a 4-stroke cycle, induction happens only once every 720°, allowing precise port-timing-based metering (carburetor / MPFI). 2-strokes require crankcase pumping, complicating metering.

---

## 2.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

The duty cycle is the **gateway concept** of Module 1 because every subsequent comparison — thermal efficiency, scavenging, volumetric efficiency, specific fuel consumption — is referenced back to whether the engine operates on a 2-stroke or 4-stroke cycle. **Board examiners consistently test this concept** because it directly tests a student's ability to:
- Distinguish between firing frequency and duty cycle (a common trap)
- Derive power-output ratios
- Justify real-world engine selection (e.g., "Why is a Maruti 800 engine 4-stroke but a Royal Enfield Classic 350 also 4-stroke, while a Yamaha RX 135 is 2-stroke?")
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Duty Cycle of a 4-Stroke Spark Ignition Engine

**Given:**
- One complete working cycle of a 4-stroke SI engine = **4 piston strokes**
- One piston stroke corresponds to **half a revolution (180°)** of the crankshaft
- Only one of the four strokes (the **expansion/power stroke**) produces net positive work

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{cycle, 4s}} &= 4 \times 180° \\
\theta_{\text{cycle, 4s}} &= 720°
\end{aligned}
$$

The power stroke contribution:

$$
\theta_{ps} = 1 \times 180° = 180°
$$

Therefore, the duty cycle is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DC_{4s} &= \frac{\theta_{ps}}{\theta_{\text{cycle, 4s}}} \\
DC_{4s} &= \frac{180°}{720°} \\
DC_{4s} &= \frac{1}{4} \\
DC_{4s} &= 0.25 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 4-stroke engine is productive for only **25 %** of every complete cycle. The remaining **75 %** is consumed by induction, compression, and exhaust processes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B: Duty Cycle of a 2-Stroke Engine

**Given:**
- One complete working cycle = **2 piston strokes**
- One piston stroke = **180°** of crankshaft rotation
- One of the two strokes (the expansion/power stroke) produces net work

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{cycle, 2s}} &= 2 \times 180° \\
\theta_{\text{cycle, 2s}} &= 360°
\end{aligned}
$$

The power stroke contribution:

$$
\theta_{ps} = 1 \times 180° = 180°
$$

Therefore, the duty cycle is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DC_{2s} &= \frac{\theta_{ps}}{\theta_{\text{cycle, 2s}}} \\
DC_{2s} &= \frac{180°}{360°} \\
DC_{2s} &= \frac{1}{2} \\
DC_{2s} &= 0.50 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 2-stroke engine is productive for **50 %** of every complete cycle — i.e., **twice** as much as a 4-stroke engine.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C: Theoretical Power Output Ratio (2-Stroke vs 4-Stroke)

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU ESE. Assume both engines have:
- Identical displacement volume $V_d$
- Identical net work per cycle $W_{net}$
- Identical crankshaft speed $N$ (in revolutions per second, rps)

**Indicated Power Formula:**

$$
P = W_{net} \cdot N_{firing}
$$

Where $N_{firing}$ = number of power strokes per second.

**Step 1:** For a **4-stroke engine**, there is one power stroke per **2 revolutions**:

$$
N_{firing, 4s} = \frac{N}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{4s} = W_{net} \cdot \frac{N}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** For a **2-stroke engine**, there is one power stroke per **1 revolution**:

$$
N_{firing, 2s} = N
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{2s} = W_{net} \cdot N
$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_{2s}}{P_{4s}} &= \frac{W_{net} \cdot N}{W_{net} \cdot \frac{N}{2}} \\
\frac{P_{2s}}{P_{4s}} &= \frac{N}{\frac{N}{2}} \\
\frac{P_{2s}}{P_{4s}} &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion (Boxed Result):**
> At identical speed and displacement, a 2-stroke engine theoretically delivers **twice the indicated power** of a 4-stroke engine.
>
> $$\boxed{P_{2s} = 2 \cdot P_{4s}}$$

**Step 4 — The Practical Correction:**

In real engines, the 2-stroke loses a fraction of its fresh charge during the scavenging process (short-circuiting). This is quantified by the **scavenging efficiency $\eta_{sc}$** (typically 0.7–0.9) and the **trapping efficiency $\eta_{tr}$**.

The corrected power expression becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2s,\text{actual}} &= W_{net} \cdot N \cdot \eta_{sc} \cdot \eta_{tr} \cdot \eta_{mech} \\
P_{2s,\text{actual}} &\approx (1.5 \text{ to } 1.8) \cdot P_{4s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the *real-world* advantage is closer to **1.5–1.8×**, not 2×.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D: Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) Relationship

For completeness, the relationship between MEP and duty cycle:

$$
\text{IMEP} = \frac{W_{net}}{V_d}
$$

Power becomes:

$$
P = \text{IMEP} \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot k
$$

Where $k = N_{firing}/N$:
- $k_{2s} = 1$
- $k_{4s} = 1/2$

This shows that to achieve the **same power output**, a 4-stroke engine requires either:
- **Double the displacement** ($V_d$), or
- **Double the BMEP** (mean effective pressure), or
- **Double the speed** ($N$).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Key Comparisons

| Parameter | 4-Stroke Engine | 2-Stroke Engine | Ratio (2s / 4s) |
|:----------|:---------------:|:---------------:|:---------------:|
| **Duty Cycle** | 25 % | 50 % | 2.0 |
| **Power strokes per cycle** | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| **Power strokes per revolution** | 0.5 | 1 | 2.0 |
| **Theoretical Power Ratio** | 1.0 | 2.0 | 2.0 |
| **Practical Power Ratio** | 1.0 | 1.5–1.8 | 1.5–1.8 |
| **Valves / Ports required** | Valve train | Only ports | — |
| **Theoretical thermal efficiency** | Higher (full expansion) | Lower (short-circuited charge) | <1 |
| **Cooling load** | Lower | Higher (~2×) | 2.0 |
| **Weight (per kW)** | Higher | Lower | ~0.6 |
| **Emissions control** | Easier (4 dedicated events) | Harder | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Duty Cycle Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional sequence of events** in both engine types and highlights where the "productive" (power) interval sits within each cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[4-Stroke Engine - 720 deg Cycle] --> B1[Intake Stroke 0 to 180 deg - Negative Work]
    B1 --> C1[Compression Stroke 180 to 360 deg - Negative Work]
    C1 --> D1["POWER STROKE 360 to 540 deg - POSITIVE WORK - Duty Cycle Active"]
    D1 --> E1[Exhaust Stroke 540 to 720 deg - Negative Work]
    E1 --> F1[Flywheel stores energy from 360 to 540 deg]
    F1 --> G1[Flywheel returns energy 0 to 360 and 540 to 720]

    A2[2-Stroke Engine - 360 deg Cycle] --> B2[Compression plus Scavenging 0 to 180 deg - Negative Work]
    B2 --> C2["POWER STROKE 180 to 360 deg - POSITIVE WORK - Duty Cycle Active"]
    C2 --> D2[Flywheel stores energy from 180 to 360 deg]
    D2 --> E2[Flywheel returns energy 0 to 180 deg]

    D1 -.->|Duty Cycle 25 percent| SUM1[Productive Fraction 4s]
    C2 -.->|Duty Cycle 50 percent| SUM2[Productive Fraction 2s]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Power Flow from Cycle to Crank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENG4S[4-Stroke Engine Path]
    W4[Net Work per Cycle W net] --> K4[Firing Factor k = 1 by 2]
    K4 --> P4[Power P = W net times N by 2]
    end

    subgraph ENG2S[2-Stroke Engine Path]
    W2[Net Work per Cycle W net] --> K2[Firing Factor k = 1]
    K2 --> P2[Power P = W net times N]
    end

    P4 --> RATIO{Ratio P2s by P4s}
    P2 --> RATIO
    RATIO --> RES[Theoretical 2.0]
    RATIO --> PRAC[Practical 1.5 to 1.8 after losses]
```

---

## 4.3 Angular Timing Diagram (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Duty Cycle Timeline - 4-Stroke vs 2-Stroke
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s deg

    section 4 Stroke Cycle 720 deg
    Intake 0 deg     :a1, 0, 180
    Compression      :a2, 180, 180
    Power (Active)   :crit, a3, 360, 540
    Exhaust          :a4, 540, 180

    section 2 Stroke Cycle 360 deg
    Scavenge plus Compression :b1, 0, 180
    Power (Active)            :crit, b2, 180, 360
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the Gantt Chart:**
> The **red "Power (Active)"** segment in each section visually represents the duty cycle. Notice that in the 2-stroke cycle, the active segment occupies **half the timeline**, while in the 4-stroke cycle, it occupies only **one-quarter** of the timeline.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Duty Cycle" as applied to Internal Combustion engines. Compare the duty cycle of a 2-stroke engine with that of a 4-stroke engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Duty Cycle is the ratio of the crank angle duration of the power (expansion) stroke to the total crank angle duration of one complete thermodynamic cycle of the engine.
>
> $$\text{DC} = \frac{\theta_{\text{power stroke}}}{\theta_{\text{cycle}}}$$
>
> **Comparison (2 marks):**
> - For a 4-stroke engine: $\text{DC}_{4s} = \dfrac{180°}{720°} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 25\%$
> - For a 2-stroke engine: $\text{DC}_{2s} = \dfrac{180°}{360°} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 50\%$
> - Therefore, $\dfrac{\text{DC}_{2s}}{\text{DC}_{4s}} = 2$; the 2-stroke engine has **twice** the duty cycle of the 4-stroke engine.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State why a 2-stroke engine produces theoretically twice the power of a 4-stroke engine of the same speed and displacement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (1 mark):** A 2-stroke engine theoretically produces twice the power of an equivalent 4-stroke engine at the same speed and displacement.
>
> **Reasoning (2 marks):**
> - In a 4-stroke engine, one power stroke occurs every **2 revolutions** of the crankshaft (firing factor $k = 1/2$).
> - In a 2-stroke engine, one power stroke occurs every **1 revolution** of the crankshaft (firing factor $k = 1$).
> - Since the net work per cycle $W_{net}$ is identical, the indicated power $P = W_{net} \cdot N \cdot k$ is doubled for the 2-stroke engine: $P_{2s} = 2 P_{4s}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme regulations, students must answer **one full question (14 marks)** from a Module, with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define and explain the concept of **duty cycle** in IC engines. With suitable derivations, compute the duty cycle for both 4-stroke and 2-stroke engines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke petrol engine and a 2-stroke petrol engine have the **same displacement volume** and run at the **same crankshaft speed of 3000 rpm**. The net work per cycle for both engines is **0.5 kJ**. Calculate:
  - (i) Indicated power of each engine
  - (ii) Theoretical ratio of power output
  - (iii) Comment on the practical deviation from the theoretical ratio **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Duty Cycle Definition and Derivations (7 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):**
> The duty cycle of an IC engine is defined as the ratio of the crankshaft angle (or time duration) during which the piston produces net positive work to the total crankshaft angle (or time duration) required to complete one full thermodynamic cycle. It is a dimensionless quantity, typically expressed as a percentage.
>
> $$\text{DC} = \frac{\theta_{ps}}{\theta_{\text{cycle}}}$$
>
> **Derivation for 4-stroke engine (2.5 marks):**
> Total cycle crank angle:
> $$\theta_{\text{cycle}} = 4 \times 180° = 720°$$
> Power stroke crank angle:
> $$\theta_{ps} = 1 \times 180° = 180°$$
> Duty cycle:
> $$DC_{4s} = \frac{180°}{720°} = \frac{1}{4} = 25\%$$
>
> **Derivation for 2-stroke engine (2.5 marks):**
> Total cycle crank angle:
> $$\theta_{\text{cycle}} = 2 \times 180° = 360°$$
> Power stroke crank angle:
> $$\theta_{ps} = 180°$$
> Duty cycle:
> $$DC_{2s} = \frac{180°}{360°} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$
>
> **Conclusion:** The 2-stroke engine has a duty cycle twice that of the 4-stroke engine.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 3000 \text{ rpm} = 50 \text{ rps}$, $W_{net} = 0.5 \text{ kJ}$

**(i) Indicated Power (3 marks):**

For the **4-stroke engine**:
$$
P_{4s} = W_{net} \cdot N \cdot k_{4s} = 0.5 \times 50 \times \frac{1}{2}
$$
$$
\boxed{P_{4s} = 12.5 \text{ kW}}
$$

For the **2-stroke engine**:
$$
P_{2s} = W_{net} \cdot N \cdot k_{2s} = 0.5 \times 50 \times 1
$$
$$
\boxed{P_{2s} = 25 \text{ kW}}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical Power Ratio (2 marks):**
$$
\frac{P_{2s}}{P_{4s}} = \frac{25}{12.5} = 2.0
$$
This confirms the theoretical 2× power advantage of the 2-stroke engine.

**(iii) Practical Deviation (2 marks):**

> In practice, the 2-stroke engine does **not** deliver double the power because of:
> - **Scavenging losses** — short-circuiting of fresh charge during port overlap (typically 10–30% loss).
> - **Lower trapping efficiency** — incomplete filling of the cylinder with fresh charge.
> - **Higher friction power** — more frequent combustion events increase mechanical friction.
> - **Lower mechanical efficiency** — typically 15–25% lower than a 4-stroke.
>
> Hence, the **actual** power ratio is closer to **1.5 to 1.8**, not the theoretical 2.0.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With neat sketches (block diagram), explain the **sequence of operations** in a 4-stroke SI engine and a 2-stroke SI engine. Highlight the power-producing event in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke engine develops **7.5 kW** of brake power at **1500 rpm**. The mean effective pressure is **8 bar** and the mechanical efficiency is **80%**. Calculate:
  - (i) The displacement volume of the engine
  - (ii) The brake specific fuel consumption if the fuel consumption is **3 kg/h**
  - **(Bonus)** How would the brake power change if the engine were converted to a 2-stroke of identical displacement and speed, ignoring all losses? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sequence of Operations (7 marks):**

> **4-Stroke SI Engine (3.5 marks):**
> - **Intake stroke (0°–180°):** Piston moves from TDC to BDC; intake valve opens; air-fuel mixture drawn in. *No positive work.*
> - **Compression stroke (180°–360°):** Both valves closed; mixture compressed; work done **on** the gas.
> - **Power stroke (360°–540°):** **Spark** ignites mixture; high-pressure gas expands, pushing piston down. **Positive work delivered.**
> - **Exhaust stroke (540°–720°):** Exhaust valve opens; burnt gases expelled. *Work done by the piston on the gas.*
>
> **2-Stroke SI Engine (3.5 marks):**
> - **First stroke (0°–180°):** Piston moves up, compressing charge in cylinder while simultaneously drawing fresh charge into the crankcase through the inlet port. Scavenging occurs near TDC.
> - **Second stroke (180°–360°):** Piston moves down after ignition near TDC; **power stroke** delivers positive work; near BDC, exhaust port opens and fresh charge scavenges out burnt gases.
> - **Net result:** One power stroke per revolution.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $BP = 7.5 \text{ kW}$, $N = 1500 \text{ rpm} = 25 \text{ rps}$, $\text{BMEP (assumed)} = 8 \text{ bar} = 800 \text{ kPa}$, $\eta_{mech} = 80\%$, $\dot{m}_f = 3 \text{ kg/h}$

**(i) Displacement Volume (3 marks):**

Indicated Power:
$$
IP = \frac{BP}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{7.5}{0.80} = 9.375 \text{ kW}
$$

Using the 4-stroke MEP formula:
$$
IP = \frac{\text{IMEP} \cdot V_d \cdot N}{2}
$$

Solving for $V_d$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{2 \cdot IP}{\text{IMEP} \cdot N} \\
V_d &= \frac{2 \times 9.375 \times 10^3 \text{ W}}{800 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa} \times 25 \text{ rps}} \\
V_d &= \frac{18750}{20{,}000{,}000} \\
V_d &= 9.375 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \\
\boxed{V_d &= 937.5 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 0.9375 \text{ L}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BSFC} &= \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \\
\text{BSFC} &= \frac{3 \text{ kg/h}}{7.5 \text{ kW}} \\
\boxed{\text{BSFC} &= 0.40 \text{ kg/kWh}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bonus — 2-Stroke Conversion (2 marks):**

> If converted to a 2-stroke of identical $V_d$ and identical $N$, with the same IMEP and identical mechanical efficiency, the brake power would theoretically **double**:
>
> $$BP_{2s,\text{theoretical}} = 2 \times 7.5 = 15 \text{ kW}$$
>
> **Why?** Because firing frequency doubles. In practice, due to scavenging and mechanical losses, the gain is typically **40–60 %**, not 100 %.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing firing frequency with duty cycle** — A 4-stroke engine has firing frequency = $N/2$, but duty cycle = 1/4. These are *different* quantities. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Writing "2-stroke = 2× power" without qualifying it as theoretical** — In real engines, you *must* mention scavenging losses and mechanical efficiency. Failing to do so costs at least **1 mark** in descriptive answers.
>
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversions in numericals** — Especially $N$ in rps vs rpm, and pressure in Pa vs bar. Marks are deducted for each missing step.
>
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of equal $W_{net}$ and $V_d$** — When comparing powers, always explicitly state these assumptions. The examiner wants to see *engineering discipline*.
>
> 5. **Mixing up "power per cycle" and "power per stroke"** — In a 4-stroke engine, there is **one** power stroke per cycle (not two). Be precise.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Duty Cycle (Module 1)**
>
> - **Definition:** $\text{DC} = \theta_{\text{ps}} / \theta_{\text{cycle}}$
> - **4-stroke DC:** **25 %** (180°/720°)
> - **2-stroke DC:** **50 %** (180°/360°)
> - **Theoretical power ratio:** $P_{2s}/P_{4s} = 2$ (at same $V_d$, $N$, $W_{net}$)
> - **Practical power ratio:** 1.5 to 1.8 due to losses
> - **Firing factor $k$:** = 1 (2-stroke); = 1/2 (4-stroke)
> - **Power formula:** $P = W_{net} \cdot N \cdot k$
> - **Application zones:** 2-stroke → small engines (outboards, mopeds, chainsaws); 4-stroke → automobiles, gensets
> - **Trade-off:** Higher duty cycle = more power but more cooling demand, harder emission control
> - **MEP link:** $P = \text{IMEP} \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot k$
> - **Examiner's favourite 14-mark pattern:** "Compare duty cycles and derive the theoretical power ratio with a worked numerical"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UPCR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** UPCR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UPCR — Uncooled Premixed Combustion Reactor**
> A high-pressure, **adiabatic (uncooled)** experimental reactor in which a **homogeneously premixed** fuel–air charge is introduced, ignited (typically by spark or jet ignition), and the resulting combustion is studied under controlled thermodynamic conditions that closely simulate the **end-gas auto-ignition chemistry** of a real spark-ignition (SI) or homogeneous charge compression ignition (HCCI) engine.

The UPCR is the laboratory workhorse used by combustion chemists and engine researchers to:
- Validate detailed chemical-kinetic mechanisms (e.g., those for n-heptane, iso-octane, PRF, toluene reference fuels).
- Measure **ignition delay times** ($\tau_{ig}$), **laminar flame speeds** ($S_L$), and species concentration histories.
- Reproduce the **negative temperature coefficient (NTC)** regime observed in real engines.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker with a Spark Plug"**
> Imagine a thick-walled steel sphere (like a pressure cooker) about the size of a football. Inside, instead of cooking rice, we fill it with a perfectly stirred mixture of gasoline vapour and air. There is **no water jacket** to cool it — the walls are insulated. We heat this mixture to a few hundred degrees Celsius and a few atmospheres of pressure (just like the end-gas trapped ahead of a flame front in an engine cylinder), then pop in a spark. The mixture burns almost instantaneously. Because the walls are **uncooled**, the temperature and pressure profiles we measure truly represent the *chemistry* of combustion, free from the confusing heat-loss effects of a water-cooled engine.

## 1.3 Standard Operating Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Operating Pressure | **1 – 100 bar** | Spans SI to HCCI engine loads |
| Pre-ignition Temperature | **600 – 1200 K** | Covers end-gas temperatures |
| Equivalence Ratio ($\phi$) | **0.5 – 2.0** | Lean, stoichiometric, rich |
| Ignition Delay | **1 µs – 50 ms** | Critical for knock/HCCI control |
| Characteristic Volume | **~0.3 – 4 L** | Compact lab scale |
| Key Fuels Studied | **n-heptane, iso-octane, PRF, toluene** | Reference fuels (TRF) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Universal Gas Constant** used throughout UPCR analysis is $R_u = \mathbf{8.314 \; J/(mol \cdot K)}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Evacuation & Filling:** The spherical chamber is first evacuated to remove residual combustion products from the previous cycle, then filled with a precisely metered fuel–air mixture to the desired equivalence ratio $\phi$.
2. **Heating & Pressurization:** External electrical heaters (or a pre-combustion stroke) raise the mixture temperature $T_0$ and pressure $P_0$ to the target test condition — simulating the compression stroke of an SI engine.
3. **Ignition:** A high-energy spark, a pilot flame, or a hot jet of burned gases initiates combustion at the geometric centre.
4. **Combustion Propagation:** A nearly spherical flame kernel expands outward. Because the mixture is uniform, **flame stretch** effects dominate the early growth stage.
5. **Data Acquisition:** High-speed pressure transducers, optical fibres (for chemiluminescence), and rapid sampling valves record $P(t)$, $T(t)$, and species concentrations.
6. **Cooling & Venting:** After combustion, the vessel is purged and cooled — completing one experimental cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Uncooled"?** Water-cooled reactors distort the low-temperature heat-release (LTHR) chemistry that governs HCCI phasing and SI end-gas knock. An *adiabatic* reactor preserves the true exothermic behaviour, allowing direct comparison with chemical-kinetic simulations.

## 2.2 Why UPCR is Indispensable to Modern Engine R&D

- **Knock Prediction:** A gasoline engine "knocks" when the end-gas auto-ignites. UPCR reproduces this auto-ignition in isolation, allowing researchers to design **octane boosters** and optimize ignition timing.
- **HCCI Calibration:** HCCI engines are extremely sensitive to chemistry. UPCR data feeds 0-D and 1-D engine simulations to map combustion phasing versus $\phi$, T, and P.
- **Surrogate Fuel Development:** Because real gasoline contains hundreds of species, researchers blend **Primary Reference Fuels (PRF)** and **Toluene Reference Fuels (TRF)** to match UPCR-measured ignition behaviour.
- **Pollutant Formation:** The NTC regime is where **cool-flame chemistry** produces formaldehyde and other HCCI emissions. UPCR experiments validate these pathways.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery of the following equation set is mandatory for any Part-A or Part-B question on UPCR.**

| # | Equation | Symbols & Meaning | Boundary / Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P V = m R_u T / M$ | **Ideal gas law** for reactor contents | $P \le 100$ bar, $T \le 1500$ K |
| 2 | $\tau_{ig} = A \, [O_2]^{-a} \, [F]^{-b} \, \exp(E_a / R_u T)$ | **Ignition delay** (Arrhenius form) | Valid for $\tau_{ig} > 1$ ms |
| 3 | $\phi = (m_{fuel} / m_{air})_{actual} \, / \, (m_{fuel} / m_{air})_{stoich}$ | **Equivalence ratio** definition | $\phi = 1$ stoichiometric |
| 4 | $S_L = S_{L,0} \, (T_u / T_{ref})^{\alpha} \, (P / P_{ref})^{\beta}$ | **Laminar flame speed** correlation | Metcalfe–Burke–Schumann |
| 5 | $a \tau_{ig} \propto P^{-n} \exp(E_a / R_u T)$ | **Douhal / Ciezki** ignition correlation | High-T auto-ignition |
| 6 | $\rho_0 S_L = \rho_u S_L$ | **Mass conservation** across flame | Quasi-1-D flame |
| 7 | $\dot{Q} = m_{fuel} \cdot LHV$ | **Heat released** per cycle | LHV of fuel |
| 8 | $\eta_{c} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | **Otto efficiency** | Reference cycle comparison |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — *never* the bare pipe symbol `|` inside formulas — to avoid breaking table syntax.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Law Energy Balance for the Uncooled Reactor

For a **closed, rigid, adiabatic** reactor (the UPCR during a single combustion event), the first law of thermodynamics gives:

$$
\frac{dU}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{W}_{out} = 0
$$

Because the reactor is **uncooled** (no heat transfer to walls) and **rigid** (constant volume, no boundary work), both terms vanish. The internal energy change is solely due to chemical reaction:

$$
\frac{dU}{dt} = m \sum_{i=1}^{N} Y_i \, \dot{\omega}_i \, u_i
$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
m \, \frac{du}{dt} = - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dot{\omega}_i \, u_i \, W_i \cdot V
$$

Introducing the sensible internal energy $u = c_v \, T$, and assuming a single overall reaction $F + \nu O_2 \rightarrow Products$ with progress variable $q$:

$$
m \, c_v \, \frac{dT}{dt} = - \Delta u_c \, \frac{dq}{dt}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Integrating this equation from $T_0$ (initial) to $T_f$ (final) gives the **adiabatic flame temperature** $T_{ad}$ when $q$ reaches unity — the very metric a UPCR is designed to validate.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Rise in a Constant-Volume UPCR

Starting from the ideal gas law applied at any instant during combustion:

$$
P(t) \, V = m R_u T(t) / M_{mix}
$$

Differentiating with respect to time (since $V$ and $m$ are constant):

$$
V \, \frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{m R_u}{M_{mix}} \frac{dT}{dt}
$$

Substituting $dT/dt$ from the energy balance in §3.1:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{R_u}{M_{mix} \, c_v} \left( -\Delta u_c \frac{dq}{dt} \right) = \frac{\gamma - 1}{V} \left( -\Delta u_c \frac{dq}{dt} \right)
$$

**Final governing ODE for the UPCR pressure trace:**

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{(\gamma - 1)}{V} \, \Delta u_c \, \dot{q}(t)
$$

where $\gamma = c_p / c_v$ is the specific-heat ratio, and $\dot{q}$ is the **volumetric reaction-progress rate** governed by detailed chemistry (typically solved with CHEMKIN / Cantera).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ignition Delay Correlation from UPCR Data

UPCR pressure traces show a characteristic delay $\tau_{ig}$ between the spark/jet trigger and the **inflection point of the pressure rise**. Empirical correlations across many fuels and conditions follow the **Arrhenius form**:

$$
\tau_{ig} = A \, P^{-n} \, \phi^{-m} \, \exp\!\left( \frac{E_a}{R_u T_0} \right)
$$

Taking natural logarithms (linearises for regression):

$$
\ln \tau_{ig} = \ln A - n \ln P - m \ln \phi + \frac{E_a}{R_u T_0}
$$

**Parameter identification protocol:**

1. Fix $\phi$ and sweep $T_0$ → slope yields $E_a / R_u$.
2. Fix $T_0$ and sweep $P$ → slope yields $-n$.
3. Fix $T_0$, $P$ and sweep $\phi$ → slope yields $-m$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This triple-sweep procedure is a **classic KTU Part-B examiner's favourite** and will appear in Section 5.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping UPCR Outputs to Engine Phenomena

| UPCR Measurement | Engine Equivalent | Design Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\tau_{ig}(T, P, \phi)$ | End-gas auto-ignition timing | Knock margin, octane requirement |
| $T_{ad}$ | Peak cycle temperature | NOx formation tendency |
| $[OH]^*$, $[CH]^*$ chemiluminescence | Local heat release rate | Flame kernel growth model |
| NTC region in $\tau_{ig}$–T plot | HCCI cool-flame regime | HCCI combustion phasing |
| CO, HCHO in exhaust samples | HC emissions | Aftertreatment design |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a UPCR Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Fuel Supply Line] --> B1[Mixing Chamber]
    A2[Air Supply Line with Preheater] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Vacuum Pump for Evacuation]
    B1 --> C2[Charging Valve Assembly]
    C2 --> D1[Spherical Uncooled Reactor Vessel]
    D1 --> E1[Ignition System - Spark or Jet]
    D1 --> F1[Pressure Transducer - Piezoelectric]
    D1 --> F2[Optical Access Port - UV and IR]
    D1 --> F3[Fast Sampling Probe]
    D1 --> G1[Data Acquisition System - DAQ]
    F1 --> G1
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Post Processor for Ignition Delay]
    G1 --> H2[Chemical Kinetic Mechanism Validator]
    G1 --> H3[Surrogate Fuel Database Updater]
    H1 --> I1[Engine Combustion Model Calibration]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Combustion Process Flow inside the UPCR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Evacuation] --> S2[Step 2 Mixing]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Heating and Pressurization]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Ignition Trigger]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Flame Propagation and Pressure Rise]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Post Combustion Sampling]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Purge and Reset]
    S7 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Uncooled Reaction Chamber Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[Thick Walled Stainless Steel Shell] --> W2[Adiabatic Ceramic Liner]
    W2 --> W3[Internal Reactor Volume Sphere]
    W3 --> W4[Central Ignition Point]
    W3 --> W5[Expanding Spherical Flame Front]
    W4 --> W6[Spark or Hot Jet Plug at Top]
    W3 --> W7[Optical Quartz Window for Diagnostics]
    W3 --> W8[Fast Acting Sampling Valve at Bottom]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Because physical 3D renderings cannot be drawn in Mermaid, the schematic above maps the **functional topology** of the chamber — its diagnostic ports, ignition access, and flame propagation origin. Examiners reward clear *topological* understanding even when no physical drawing is possible.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define UPCR. Why is the absence of active cooling critical to its purpose?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Uncooled Premixed Combustion Reactor is a **spherical, rigid, adiabatic vessel** in which a homogeneous fuel–air mixture is ignited and combustion chemistry is studied under engine-relevant $T$ and $P$.
Absence of cooling (1) is critical because wall heat-loss would mask the **low-temperature heat-release (LTHR) and NTC chemistry** that govern knock and HCCI combustion (2). An uncooled reactor delivers true adiabatic flame temperatures and clean ignition-delay data for validating chemical-kinetic mechanisms (3).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List any three reference fuels used in UPCR experiments and state the equivalence ratio at stoichiometric combustion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Three reference fuels commonly used in UPCR studies are:
1. **n-heptane** — primary reference fuel (PRF 0).
2. **iso-octane** — PRF 100 (high octane number).
3. **Toluene** — base component of TRF (Toluene Reference Fuel) blends. (3 marks distributed: 1 per fuel)

The **equivalence ratio at stoichiometric combustion** is **$\phi = 1$** by definition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **Uncooled Premixed Combustion Reactor (UPCR)**. Highlight the role of each subsystem in obtaining reliable auto-ignition data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **pressure-rise equation** for a constant-volume adiabatic reactor and explain how it is used to compute the ignition delay from the experimental $P$–$t$ trace. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)**

The UPCR consists of the following subsystems, each contributing to data quality:

1. **Spherical Reaction Vessel (2 marks):** Typically a 0.3–4 L stainless-steel sphere with ceramic internal liner to minimise wall catalysis. The sphere promotes symmetrical flame propagation, simplifying interpretation.
2. **Fuel-Air Mixing & Charging System (1 mark):** Uses precision mass-flow controllers and partial-pressure mixing to achieve accurate equivalence ratios.
3. **Heating System (1 mark):** Electrical band heaters or pre-combustion warm-up raise the mixture to the desired initial temperature $T_0$.
4. **Ignition Subsystem (1 mark):** Central spark plug or hot-jet igniter with programmable timing.
5. **Diagnostic Ports (1 mark):** Piezoelectric pressure transducer, quartz optical window for chemiluminescence, and rapid sampling valve for GC analysis.
6. **Data Acquisition (1 mark):** High-speed DAQ capturing $P(t)$ at $\ge$ 100 kHz sampling.

**Part (b) — Pressure Rise Derivation (7 marks)**

Starting from the ideal gas law:

$$
P(t) V = m R_u T(t) / M_{mix}
$$

Differentiating (with $V$, $m$, $M_{mix}$ constant):

$$
V \frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{m R_u}{M_{mix}} \frac{dT}{dt}
$$

Using energy balance $\, dT/dt = -\Delta u_c \dot{q} / (m c_v)$ from §3.1:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{R_u}{M_{mix} c_v} (-\Delta u_c \dot{q}) = \frac{\gamma - 1}{V} \, \Delta u_c \, \dot{q}(t)
$$

The **ignition delay** $\tau_{ig}$ is read off the $P$–$t$ trace as the time between the **ignition trigger** and the **inflection point** of the pressure rise — corresponding to the onset of significant heat release. (7 marks total, distributed: 2 derivation, 2 substitution, 2 final form, 1 interpretation.)

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**(a)** What is the **equivalence ratio**? Using the UPCR, describe the experimental procedure to determine the **ignition delay correlation** $\tau_{ig} = A P^{-n} \phi^{-m} \exp(E_a / R_u T)$ for a fuel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A UPCR experiment is conducted at $T_0 = 850$ K, $P_0 = 15$ bar, $\phi = 1.0$ on a fuel whose ignition correlation is $\tau_{ig} = 2.1 \times 10^{-4} \, P^{-1.05} \, \phi^{-0.77} \, \exp(17640 / T_0)$. Calculate the ignition delay and comment on whether the fuel exhibits **NTC behaviour** at this condition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Equivalence Ratio & Correlation Procedure (7 marks)**

The **equivalence ratio** $\phi$ is the actual fuel–air mass ratio divided by the stoichiometric fuel–air mass ratio (1 mark).

Experimental procedure (6 marks):

1. Fix $\phi$ and $P_0$; sweep $T_0$ over a wide range (e.g., 650–1200 K) (1 mark).
2. Record $\tau_{ig}$ from pressure traces (1 mark).
3. Plot $\ln \tau_{ig}$ vs $1/T_0$ → slope gives $E_a / R_u$ (1 mark).
4. Fix $T_0$ and $\phi$; sweep $P_0$ → slope of $\ln \tau_{ig}$ vs $\ln P$ gives $-n$ (1 mark).
5. Fix $T_0$ and $P_0$; sweep $\phi$ → slope of $\ln \tau_{ig}$ vs $\ln \phi$ gives $-m$ (1 mark).
6. Compute pre-exponential $A$ from intercept (1 mark).

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

$$
\tau_{ig} = 2.1 \times 10^{-4} \times 15^{-1.05} \times 1.0^{-0.77} \times \exp(17640 / 850)
$$

Step 1 — Pressure term:

$$
15^{-1.05} = \frac{1}{15^{1.05}} \approx \frac{1}{16.86} \approx 0.0593
$$

Step 2 — Equivalence term:

$$
1.0^{-0.77} = 1.0
$$

Step 3 — Exponential term:

$$
\exp(17640 / 850) = \exp(20.7529) \approx 1.024 \times 10^{9}
$$

Step 4 — Final multiplication:

$$
\tau_{ig} \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.0593 \times 1.0 \times 1.024 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
\tau_{ig} \approx 1.275 \times 10^{4} \; \mu s \approx 12.75 \; ms
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** Since $T_0 = 850$ K falls in the typical **NTC window (750–900 K)** for paraffinic fuels, the experiment must be repeated at neighbouring temperatures to verify whether $\tau_{ig}$ *decreases* as $T$ increases — the hallmark of NTC behaviour.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do not** confuse *initial* temperature $T_0$ with the **adiabatic flame temperature** $T_{ad}$ in the exponential term — KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> - **Do not** drop the negative sign in $-n$ when logging the pressure regression — the slope is $-n$, not $n$.
> - **Always** state SI units in the final answer ($s$ or $ms$); bare numbers lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **UPCR** = **Uncooled Premixed Combustion Reactor** — an adiabatic, rigid, spherical experimental vessel.
- **Purpose:** measure ignition delay, validate chemical-kinetic mechanisms, and study knock/HCCI chemistry without water-cooling artefacts.
- **Operating envelope:** $P$ up to 100 bar, $T_0$ 600–1200 K, $\phi$ 0.5–2.0.
- **Three key derived equations** (must memorise):
   - Energy balance: $dT/dt = -\Delta u_c \dot{q} / (m c_v)$
   - Pressure rise: $dP/dt = (\gamma - 1)\Delta u_c \dot{q} / V$
   - Ignition correlation: $\tau_{ig} = A P^{-n} \phi^{-m} \exp(E_a / R_u T)$
- **NTC region** (750–900 K) is critical for HCCI — always probe this range in Part-B numerics.
- **Reference fuels:** n-heptane (PRF 0), iso-octane (PRF 100), toluene (TRF base).
- **Diagnostic outputs:** pressure trace, optical chemiluminescence ($[OH]^*$, $[CH]^*$), exhaust GC sampling.
- **Engine link:** UPCR data feeds 0-D and 1-D engine models for knock prediction and HCCI phasing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dual Fuel Engines - Concept and Significance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dual Fuel Engines - Concept and Significance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Dual Fuel Engine** is a modified internal combustion engine (originally based on the Compression Ignition / Diesel cycle) in which the **primary energy is supplied by a gaseous fuel** (such as Compressed Natural Gas - CNG, Liquefied Petroleum Gas - LPG, biogas, hydrogen, or producer gas) **pre-mixed with the incoming air in the intake manifold**, while a **small quantity of liquid diesel fuel (pilot fuel)** is injected near the end of the compression stroke to act as the **ignition source** for the compressed air–gaseous fuel mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A dual fuel engine is **NOT a Spark Ignition (SI) engine**. It retains a **high compression ratio (17:1 to 22:1)** like a CI engine and the ignition is achieved **chemically through auto-ignition of the pilot diesel**, NOT through an external spark plug.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine lighting a large bonfire (gas + air mixture) on a cold, rainy day when a single match won't do. You pour a small amount of kerosene on the wood pile and toss in a single match — the kerosene acts as the **"pilot"** that ignites first and then sets the whole gaseous mixture ablaze. In a dual fuel engine, **diesel is that small amount of kerosene**, and **CNG/biogas is the bonfire fuel**. The diesel "lights the candle," but most of the energy comes from the gas.

## Fundamental Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Typical Value |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **Pilot Fuel** | Liquid diesel used only for ignition | **10–20 %** of total fuel energy |
| **Primary Fuel** | Gaseous fuel carrying the main energy | **80–90 %** of total fuel energy |
| **Compression Ratio (r)** | Same as CI engine | **17:1 – 22:1** |
| **Substitution Level** | % of diesel replaced by gas | Up to **90 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for Engine Identification:** A dual fuel engine is universally referred to by the gaseous fuel it uses, e.g., **CNG-Diesel Engine**, **LPG-Diesel Engine**, **Biogas-Diesel Engine**, or **Hydrogen-Diesel Engine**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A dual fuel engine operates on a **four-stroke CI cycle** with the following sequence:

1. **Intake Stroke:** Air (from turbocharger/atmosphere) and **gaseous fuel** enter the cylinder simultaneously through the intake manifold. The mixing occurs **outside** the combustion chamber.
2. **Compression Stroke:** The homogeneous air–gas mixture is compressed to a high pressure and temperature. Since compression ratio is diesel-like (~18:1), peak temperatures exceed **800 K**, sufficient for auto-ignition of pilot diesel.
3. **Power Stroke (Combustion):**
   * Near **Top Dead Centre (TDC)**, **pilot diesel (~10–20 % energy)** is injected via the standard CI injector.
   * Pilot diesel auto-ignites due to high compression temperature (no spark plug required).
   * The diesel flame jets act as **multiple ignition kernels**, igniting the surrounding premixed air–gas mixture.
   * Combustion proceeds as a **premixed flame propagation** followed by diffusion-controlled burning.
4. **Exhaust Stroke:** Burnt gases are expelled; cycle repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Combustion Mode:** Dual fuel engines exhibit a **two-stage combustion**:
> - **Stage 1:** Pilot diesel diffusion flame (short, rapid)
> - **Stage 2:** Premixed gas–air flame propagation (longer, controlled)

## 2.2 Why Dual Fuel? — The Core 'Why'

| Engineering Driver | Explanation |
|--------------------|-------------|
| **Fuel Flexibility** | Engine can switch between pure diesel mode and dual fuel mode. |
| **Emission Reduction** | Premixed gaseous combustion → **lower smoke, soot, and NOₓ** compared to diesel. |
| **Petroleum Substitution** | Reduces crude oil dependence; utilizes indigenous natural/biogas resources. |
| **High Efficiency** | High compression ratio retained → high **brake thermal efficiency (32–38 %)**. |
| **Cold Start Reliability** | Pilot diesel ensures starting even in extreme cold where gas-only engines fail. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula No. | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|:-----------:|----------|---------------|
| **F-1** | $Q_{total} = \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot CV_{gas} + \dot{m}_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel}$ | Total energy input per second (W or kW) |
| **F-2** | $ESR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{diesel,base} - \dot{m}_{diesel,dual}}{\dot{m}_{diesel,base}}$ | **Energy Substitution Ratio** — fraction of diesel energy replaced by gas |
| **F-3** | $\eta_{b,dual} = \dfrac{BP}{Q_{total}}$ | **Brake Thermal Efficiency** of dual fuel engine |
| **F-4** | $\eta_{b,dual} \geq \eta_{b,base}$ | KTU benchmark condition for successful dual fuel operation |
| **F-5** | $P_{pilot} \,(\%) = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel}}{Q_{total}} \times 100$ | Pilot fuel energy percentage |
| **F-6** | $P_{max} \propto \eta_{vol} \cdot r$ | Max power depends on volumetric efficiency and compression ratio |
| **F-7** | $\dot{m}_{air,th} = \dfrac{BP}{\eta_{b} \cdot CV_{fuel}} \cdot A/F$ | Theoretical air mass flow rate |
| **F-8** | $CV_{mix} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{gas} \cdot CV_{gas} + \dot{m}_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel}}{\dot{m}_{gas} + \dot{m}_{diesel}}$ | Calorific value of fuel blend |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for Exams:** Whenever asked to compare dual fuel efficiency with base diesel, always express the result in terms of **ESR** or **Pilot Fuel Percentage**. Using these two parameters is the KTU board-expected vocabulary.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dual fuel engines are deployed worldwide in:
* **Heavy-duty transport trucks** (CNG-Diesel) — meeting Euro VI norms without SCR.
* **Power generation gensets** (Biogas-Diesel) — rural electrification in Kerala (KWA & ANERT projects).
* **Marine propulsion** — LNG-Diesel dual fuel ships (IMO 2020 compliant).
