* **Agricultural pumpsets** — Dual fuel retrofit kits popular in Indian irrigation sector.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR)

The **Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR)** is the single most important KTU-mandated performance metric for dual fuel engines. Below is the complete, line-by-line derivation.

### Given:
* $\dot{m}_{diesel,base}$ = mass flow rate of diesel when the engine runs in **pure diesel mode** (kg/s)
* $\dot{m}_{diesel,dual}$ = mass flow rate of diesel when the engine runs in **dual fuel mode** (kg/s)
* $CV_{diesel}$ = Calorific value of diesel (**≈ 43,000 kJ/kg**)
* $\dot{m}_{gas}$ = mass flow rate of gaseous fuel (kg/s)
* $CV_{gas}$ = Calorific value of gaseous fuel (kJ/kg)

### Derivation Steps:

**Step 1:** Write the total energy input in the **base (pure diesel) mode**.

$$Q_{base} = \dot{m}_{diesel,base} \cdot CV_{diesel}$$

This represents the total energy supplied when the engine runs only on diesel (no gas substitution).

**Step 2:** Write the total energy input in the **dual fuel mode**. Now the system has TWO energy contributors: diesel (pilot) + gas (primary).

$$Q_{dual} = \dot{m}_{diesel,dual} \cdot CV_{diesel} + \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot CV_{gas}$$

**Step 3:** Define the **energy saving in diesel mass flow** as the difference between diesel used in base mode and diesel used in dual fuel mode.

$$\Delta \dot{m}_{diesel} = \dot{m}_{diesel,base} - \dot{m}_{diesel,dual}$$

**Step 4:** Express the diesel energy that has been "substituted" or saved.

$$\Delta Q_{diesel} = \Delta \dot{m}_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel}$$

**Step 5:** Form the **ratio** of substituted diesel energy to the base diesel energy. This ratio is defined as the **Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR)**.

$$ESR = \dfrac{\Delta Q_{diesel}}{Q_{base}}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute Step 4 and Step 1 into Step 5.

$$ESR = \dfrac{(\dot{m}_{diesel,base} - \dot{m}_{diesel,dual}) \cdot CV_{diesel}}{\dot{m}_{diesel,base} \cdot CV_{diesel}}$$

**Step 7:** Cancel the common term $CV_{diesel}$ from numerator and denominator.

$$ESR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{diesel,base} - \dot{m}_{diesel,dual}}{\dot{m}_{diesel,base}}$$

**Step 8:** Final compact form (KTU-board-acceptable).

$$\boxed{ESR = 1 - \dfrac{\dot{m}_{diesel,dual}}{\dot{m}_{diesel,base}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** ESR = 0 means **no substitution** (pure diesel mode). ESR → 1 means **almost complete substitution** (engine runs almost entirely on gas). A typical operating value for CNG-Diesel engines is **ESR = 0.80 to 0.90**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Brake Thermal Efficiency of Dual Fuel Mode

**Step 1:** Start with the universal definition of brake thermal efficiency.

$$\eta_{b,dual} = \dfrac{BP}{Q_{total,dual}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the total energy input from F-1 in the formula sheet.

$$\eta_{b,dual} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_{diesel,dual} \cdot CV_{diesel} + \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot CV_{gas}}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to express gas energy as a function of ESR for analytical use. We know from Step 7 above:

$$\dot{m}_{diesel,dual} = \dot{m}_{diesel,base} \cdot (1 - ESR)$$

**Step 4:** Also, energy conservation requires the **total energy input** in dual fuel mode to be approximately equal to (or slightly higher than) the base mode, because the engine must deliver the same brake power.

$$\dot{m}_{gas} \cdot CV_{gas} = \dot{m}_{diesel,base} \cdot CV_{diesel} \cdot ESR$$

**Step 5:** Substitute these into the efficiency expression:

$$\eta_{b,dual} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_{diesel,base} \cdot CV_{diesel} \cdot (1 - ESR) + \dot{m}_{diesel,base} \cdot CV_{diesel} \cdot ESR}$$

**Step 6:** Factor out the common term:

$$\eta_{b,dual} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_{diesel,base} \cdot CV_{diesel} \cdot [(1-ESR) + ESR]}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify the bracket (the two ESR terms cancel):

$$(1 - ESR) + ESR = 1$$

**Step 8:** Final simplified result:

$$\boxed{\eta_{b,dual} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_{diesel,base} \cdot CV_{diesel}} = \eta_{b,base}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In **ideal iso-energy substitution**, dual fuel efficiency equals base diesel efficiency. In practice, **$\eta_{b,dual}$ is slightly lower** (2–5 %) due to slower gas flame propagation and incomplete combustion at high loads. KTU board questions often ask you to justify this deviation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Dual Fuel vs. Conventional CI vs. SI

| Parameter | Conventional CI (Diesel) | SI (Petrol) | **Dual Fuel Engine** |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Compression ratio | 17–22 | 8–12 | **17–22 (CI-like)** |
| Ignition source | Auto-ignition of diesel | Spark plug | **Auto-ignition of pilot diesel** |
| Fuel state | Liquid only | Liquid (vaporized) | **Liquid pilot + premixed gas** |
| Throttling | No | Yes | **Partially required** at low load |
| Thermal efficiency | 32–38 % | 25–30 % | **30–36 %** |
| Smoke emissions | High | Low | **Very Low** |
| NOₓ emissions | High | Moderate | **Moderate** |
| Fuel flexibility | Low | Low | **Very High** |
| Starting in cold weather | Excellent | Good | **Excellent (diesel pilot)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Dual Fuel Engine Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CNG / LPG / Biogas Cylinder] --> B[Pressure Regulator]
    C[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> D[Turbocharger]
    D --> E[Intake Manifold]
    B --> E
    E --> F[Engine Cylinder - Premixed Charge]
    G[Diesel Fuel Tank] --> H[High Pressure Injection Pump]
    H --> I[Pilot Injector near TDC]
    F --> J[Combustion Chamber]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Crankshaft - Brake Power Output]
    J --> L[Exhaust Manifold]
    L --> M[Turbocharger Turbine]
    M --> D
    K --> N[Load or Generator]
    O[ECU / Control Unit] -. Monitors .-> B
    O -. Controls .-> H
    O -. Switches Modes .-> G
```

**Description of the Flow:**
* The gaseous fuel and air are mixed in the **intake manifold** (left side of cylinder).
* The **pilot diesel** is injected directly into the cylinder near TDC.
* Both combustion products drive the **crankshaft** to deliver brake power.
* The **ECU** modulates gas admission and diesel pilot quantity based on load demand.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Combustion Process in a Dual Fuel Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Intake Stroke: Air + Gaseous Fuel enter cylinder] --> step2[Compression Stroke: Mixture compressed to 17:1 to 22:1 ratio]
    step2 --> step3[Pilot Diesel Injection near TDC - 5 to 10 degrees BTDC]
    step3 --> step4[Auto-Ignition of Pilot Diesel - Stage 1 Combustion]
    step4 --> step5[Premixed Gas Air Mixture Ignites - Stage 2 Combustion]
    step5 --> step6[Power Stroke - Piston descends, BP generated]
    step6 --> step7[Exhaust Stroke - Burnt gases expelled]
    step7 --> step8{Cycle repeats}
    step8 --> step1
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Comparison: Three Combustion Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Diesel Only - Diffusion Combustion] -->|Compare| M2[SI Engine - Premixed Flame Propagation]
    M2 -->|Compare| M3[Dual Fuel - Two Stage Combustion]
    
    style M1 fill:#ffe6e6
    style M2 fill:#e6f3ff
    style M3 fill:#e6ffe6
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
* **Red block:** Pure diesel — heterogeneous diffusion flame.
* **Blue block:** Pure petrol — homogeneous premixed flame.
* **Green block:** Dual fuel — **hybrid** of both, which is why it offers the **best compromise** between efficiency and emissions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Define a dual fuel engine. List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A dual fuel engine is a compression ignition engine that operates on a mixture of a **primary gaseous fuel** (CNG/LPG/biogas) and **air**, with a small quantity of **liquid diesel** injected as pilot fuel to ignite the mixture.

**Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Reduced smoke and particulate emissions** due to premixed gaseous combustion.
2. **Higher fuel flexibility** — can switch to pure diesel mode when gas supply is unavailable.
3. **Lower operating cost** when gaseous fuel is cheaper than diesel.
4. **Indigenous fuel utilization** — supports national energy security.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**What is the role of pilot fuel in a dual fuel engine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The pilot fuel (typically diesel) serves **three critical roles** (1 mark each):
1. **Ignition Source:** It auto-ignites due to high compression temperature, providing the initial flame kernel to ignite the air–gas mixture.
2. **Cold Start Aid:** It enables reliable starting of the engine even in cold conditions where gas-only combustion becomes difficult.
3. **Load Stabilizer:** At low loads, it ensures stable combustion when the gas–air mixture may become too lean to ignite on its own.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often wrongly state that pilot fuel is the "main energy source." This is incorrect. Pilot fuel contributes only **10–20 %** of the total energy; the gaseous fuel is the primary contributor.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Each 14-mark question is split into sub-parts. Typically **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Either Question A OR Question B must be answered (internal choice).

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a **CNG-Diesel dual fuel engine**. Compare its performance with a conventional diesel engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR)**. A diesel engine consumes **10 kg/hr** of diesel in pure diesel mode. When converted to dual fuel mode, it consumes **2 kg/hr** of diesel and **6 kg/hr** of CNG. Given $CV_{diesel} = 43,000 \, kJ/kg$ and $CV_{CNG} = 50,000 \, kJ/kg$, calculate: **(i)** ESR, **(ii)** Pilot fuel percentage, **(iii)** Whether the engine meets the KTU efficiency criterion $\eta_{b,dual} \geq 0.90 \cdot \eta_{b,base}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Question A:

**(a) Working Principle (3.5 marks) and Comparison (3.5 marks):**

**Working Principle:**
* In a CNG-Diesel dual fuel engine, **CNG** is the primary gaseous fuel and **diesel** is the pilot fuel.
* **Air** enters the cylinder via the turbocharger. CNG is admitted through a **pressure regulator** into the intake manifold where it mixes with air.
* The homogeneous air–CNG mixture is compressed to a **compression ratio of 17:1 to 20:1**.
* Near TDC, a small quantity of diesel is injected via the high-pressure injector.
* Diesel auto-ignites due to high compression temperature (~800 K). The diesel flame front spreads and ignites the surrounding premixed air–CNG charge.
* Combustion proceeds in two stages: pilot diesel diffusion flame, followed by premixed gas–air flame.
* The high-pressure gases drive the piston, and exhaust is expelled.

**Comparison with Conventional Diesel:**

| Aspect | Conventional Diesel | CNG-Diesel Dual Fuel |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Smoke emissions | High | **80–90 % lower** |
| Fuel cost | Higher | **20–40 % lower** |
| NOₓ emissions | High | **Moderate** |
| CO₂ emissions | Higher | **20–25 % lower** |
| Cold starting | Excellent | Excellent (diesel pilot) |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Calculation of ESR (2.5 marks):**

Using the derived formula from Section 3.1:

$$ESR = 1 - \dfrac{\dot{m}_{diesel,dual}}{\dot{m}_{diesel,base}}$$

Substituting values:

$$ESR = 1 - \dfrac{2}{10} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$$

$$\boxed{ESR = 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%}$$

This means **80 %** of the diesel energy has been substituted by CNG.

**(ii) Pilot Fuel Percentage (2 marks):**

First calculate total energy input per hour using F-1:

$$Q_{diesel} = 2 \times 43{,}000 = 86{,}000 \, kJ/hr$$

$$Q_{CNG} = 6 \times 50{,}000 = 300{,}000 \, kJ/hr$$

$$Q_{total} = 86{,}000 + 300{,}000 = 386{,}000 \, kJ/hr$$

Pilot fuel percentage using F-5:

$$P_{pilot} = \dfrac{86{,}000}{386{,}000} \times 100 = 22.28\%$$

$$\boxed{P_{pilot} \approx 22.3\%}$$

This is within the **acceptable KTU range of 10–25 %**.

**(iii) Efficiency Check (2.5 marks):**

Total fuel energy in base diesel mode:

$$Q_{base} = 10 \times 43{,}000 = 430{,}000 \, kJ/hr$$

Assuming **same brake power output** in both modes, the efficiency ratio:

$$\dfrac{\eta_{b,dual}}{\eta_{b,base}} = \dfrac{Q_{base}}{Q_{dual}} = \dfrac{430{,}000}{386{,}000} = 1.114$$

Since $1.114 \geq 0.90$, **the engine meets the KTU efficiency criterion**.

$$\boxed{\eta_{b,dual} \approx 1.114 \cdot \eta_{b,base} \, \text{(i.e., 11.4\% higher)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** In practice, the efficiency gain is partially offset by incomplete gas combustion. So actual measured gain is closer to **5–8 %**.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the **significance of dual fuel engines** in the context of **(i)** energy security, **(ii)** emission control, and **(iii)** sustainable development. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **p-V diagram**, compare the **theoretical cycles** of a dual fuel engine with a standard Otto and Diesel cycle. What are the **limitations** of dual fuel engines? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Question B:

**(a) Significance (2.5 + 2.5 + 2 marks distribution):**

**(i) Energy Security:**
* India imports **~85 %** of its crude oil requirement. Dual fuel engines reduce this dependency by enabling use of **indigenous natural gas, biogas, and producer gas**.
* Aligns with the **National Hydrogen Mission** and **SATAT scheme** (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation).
* Critical for KTU-aligned sustainable engineering curriculum.

**(ii) Emission Control:**
* Premixed gaseous combustion produces **near-zero soot** and **70–90 % lower particulate matter**.
* Helps automotive OEMs meet **BS-VI / Euro VI** norms without expensive after-treatment.
* Lower **unburned hydrocarbons (HC)** due to better mixture homogeneity.

**(iii) Sustainable Development:**
* Enables utilization of **agricultural waste biogas** — supports rural economy and circular economy.
* Reduces **carbon footprint** by 20–25 % per km in transport sector.
* Supports UN SDG-7 (Affordable Clean Energy) and SDG-13 (Climate Action).

**(b) Cycle Comparison and Limitations:**

**Theoretical Cycle Comparison:**

| Cycle | Process 1-2 (Compression) | Process 2-3 (Heat Addition) | Process 3-4 (Expansion) |
|-------|--------------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|
| **Otto (SI)** | Isentropic, r = 8–12 | Constant Volume (spark) | Isentropic |
| **Diesel (CI)** | Isentropic, r = 17–22 | Constant Pressure | Isentropic |
| **Dual Fuel** | Isentropic, r = 17–22 | **Mixed (premixed + diffusion)** | Isentropic |

The dual fuel cycle is a **hybrid** — heat addition occurs at near-constant volume initially (premixed gas ignition) followed by near-constant pressure (pilot diesel diffusion burning).

**Limitations of Dual Fuel Engines (3.5 marks):**
1. **Low Load Operation Problem:** At low loads, gas–air mixture becomes too lean → misfiring and unstable combustion. Pilot fuel quantity must be increased, reducing ESR.
2. **Knock Tendency:** At high loads, uncontrolled auto-ignition of the premixed charge may cause diesel knock.
3. **Reduced Efficiency at High Loads:** Slower flame speed of gaseous fuels → incomplete combustion → **2–5 % drop** in brake thermal efficiency.
4. **Storage and Refueling Issues:** Gaseous fuels require high-pressure cylinders (CNG at 200 bar) which are bulky.
5. **Power Derating:** Typically 10–15 % lower maximum power compared to pure diesel mode.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In numerical problems, **always include units** in the final answer (e.g., kJ/hr, %). Marks are deducted for missing units.
> - In descriptive answers, **draw the diagram first**, then explain. A neat, labelled block diagram fetches at least 1.5 bonus marks.
> - Do **NOT** confuse dual fuel engines with **bifuel engines**. Bifuel = can run on EITHER fuel separately. Dual fuel = runs on BOTH fuels SIMULTANEOUSLY.
> - In efficiency comparison questions, always justify the deviation using **flame speed, mixture homogeneity, or combustion duration** arguments.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Points — Read Before Exam:**

- A **dual fuel engine** is a **modified CI engine** running on a **gaseous primary fuel + diesel pilot**.
- **Compression ratio** is **CI-like (17:1 to 22:1)**, NOT SI-like.
- **Ignition** is by **auto-ignition of pilot diesel**, NOT by spark plug.
- **Pilot fuel** contributes only **10–20 %** of total energy; its role is **ignition**, not propulsion.
- **ESR** (Energy Substitution Ratio) is the **key metric** for measuring substitution level. Typical range: **0.70 to 0.90**.
- **Pilot fuel percentage** is given by F-5; typical operating range: **10–25 %**.
- **Two-stage combustion** is the hallmark of dual fuel engines: pilot diffusion + premixed gas flame.
- **Main advantages:** low smoke, fuel flexibility, lower operating cost, indigenous fuel utilization.
- **Main limitations:** poor low-load operation, knock at high load, ~10–15 % power derating, gaseous fuel storage challenges.
- **Ideal efficiency rule:** $\eta_{b,dual} = \eta_{b,base}$ for iso-energy substitution (in theory).
- **Practical efficiency rule:** $\eta_{b,dual}$ is **2–5 % lower** than $\eta_{b,base}$ at high loads.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always state the **gaseous fuel name** explicitly (CNG, LPG, biogas) when defining a dual fuel engine — generic answers lose marks.
- **Bifuel ≠ Dual Fuel** — this distinction is a frequent KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Combustion in Dual Fuel Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Combustion in Dual Fuel Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Dual Fuel Engine** is a compression-ignition (CI) engine that operates on the principle of **Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI)** or **Premixed Charge Compression Ignition (PCCI)**, in which a primary gaseous fuel (low reactivity — typically CNG, LNG, biogas, LPG, or hydrogen) is mixed with the inducted air to form a homogeneous lean premixture, while a secondary high-reactivity liquid pilot fuel (usually diesel) is directly injected near the end of the compression stroke to act as the ignition source.

> [!NOTE]
> **Combustion in Dual Fuel Engines** is fundamentally a **two-stage, multi-source process**: (i) auto-ignition of the pilot fuel droplet spray, followed by (ii) flame propagation and diffusion-controlled burning of the premixed gaseous fuel cloud surrounding the pilot spray. The quality, stability, and completeness of this combined process are governed by a tightly coupled set of thermodynamic, chemical, and fluid-mechanical factors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine lighting a large bonfire in a foggy field. You first throw in a small, fast-burning **matchbox** (the diesel pilot) to create an ignition kernel. The fog surrounding it is the **gaseous fuel-air mixture** that was already pre-mixed. The matchbox provides the heat, and the surrounding fog (gaseous fuel) catches fire and burns. Now imagine changing the **density of the fog**, the **size of the matchbox**, the **timing** of when you throw it, the **temperature** of the surroundings, and the **amount of air** — each of these will change how fast, how completely, and how cleanly the fire burns. The "factors affecting combustion" are exactly these physical and chemical knobs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** Dual fuel engines are NOT spark-ignited, even though the main fuel is gaseous. Combustion is **initiated by compression ignition of the pilot fuel** and NOT by an external spark plug. This distinction is a frequently tested conceptual point.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

- **Pilot Fuel Energy Share (PFES)** = Ratio of pilot fuel energy to total fuel energy (typically **5–30%**).
- **Equivalence Ratio (\(\phi\))** of the premixed gaseous fuel charge (typically **0.2–0.8**, lean operation).
- **Substitution Ratio (SR)** = Ratio of gaseous fuel mass to the mass of diesel that would have been used in a baseline CI engine for the same energy output.
- **Indicated Thermal Efficiency (\(\eta_{it}\))** — for natural gas dual fuel operation, typically **\mathbf{30\%–42\%}\)**.
- **Ignition Delay Period (IDP)** — measured in **crank angle degrees (°CA)**, normally **\mathbf{4°–10°CA}\)** for diesel pilot.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Factors Affecting Combustion

The combustion in a dual fuel engine is sensitive to a large number of interacting parameters. These are systematically classified below.

#### A. Fuel-Related Factors
1. **Type and Composition of Gaseous Fuel**
   - Methane number, calorific value, auto-ignition temperature, flame speed.
   - Higher methane number = higher resistance to knock = better controlled combustion.
2. **Quantity of Pilot Fuel (Pilot Energy Share)**
   - Too little pilot → misfire, partial combustion, high HC/CO emissions.
   - Too much pilot → defeats the purpose of dual fuelling; approaches pure diesel operation.
3. **Pilot Fuel Properties (Cetane Number, Volatility, Viscosity)**
   - Higher CN → shorter ignition delay → more controlled combustion onset.
4. **Gaseous Fuel Substitution Level / Energy Share**
5. **Air-Fuel Equivalence Ratio of the Premixed Charge**

#### B. Engine Operational Factors
6. **Compression Ratio**
   - Higher CR → higher peak temperature and pressure → better pilot ignition and gaseous fuel burning.
7. **Injection Timing of Pilot Fuel (Injection Advance Angle)**
8. **Injection Pressure of Pilot Fuel**
9. **Engine Load**
10. **Engine Speed (RPM)**
11. **Intake Air Temperature and Pressure (Boost/Supercharging)**
12. **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)**

#### C. Combustion Chamber Design Factors
13. **Combustion Chamber Geometry** (open, pre-chamber, swirl chamber).
14. **Swirl and Turbulence Intensity**
15. **Spray Pattern and Spray Penetration of Pilot Injector**
16. **Number and Location of Injector Nozzle Holes**

### 2.2 Detailed Mechanism of Combustion

The combustion process in a dual fuel engine proceeds through **four overlapping stages**:

**Stage 1 — Physical Induction:** The gaseous fuel and air are inducted/mixed during the intake stroke, forming a homogeneous lean premixture.

**Stage 2 — Compression Heating:** Both the premixed gas-air charge and a small quantity of pilot fuel are compressed. The gaseous mixture alone typically does NOT auto-ignite because its auto-ignition temperature is too high.

**Stage 3 — Pilot Ignition:** Near TDC, the pilot diesel is injected. After the (short) ignition delay of diesel, the pilot spray auto-ignites, forming multiple high-temperature ignition kernels.

**Stage 4 — Gaseous Fuel Combustion:** Flame fronts from the kernels propagate through the surrounding gaseous premixture. The combustion is partially premixed and partially diffusion-controlled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The controlling factor of dual fuel combustion is the interaction between pilot ignition energy release rate and the local equivalence ratio of the premixed gaseous charge.**

### 2.3 Effect of Each Major Factor (Examination-Oriented Analysis)

| Factor | Effect on Combustion | Direction of Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Increase in **Compression Ratio** | Raises peak T & P, promotes pilot ignition and gaseous fuel burning | **Improves** combustion efficiency and reduces HC/CO |
| Increase in **Pilot Fuel Quantity** | Provides more ignition kernels, more reliable ignition | **Reduces** HC/CO at low load; efficiency approaches diesel |
| Increase in **Gaseous Fuel Energy Share** | More premixed charge; leaner overall | Risk of **misfire** at low load; higher HC and CO |
| Increase in **Pilot Injection Advance** | Earlier start of combustion | More time for gaseous fuel burn → smoother but risk of high pressure rise rate |
| Increase in **Engine Load** | Higher in-cylinder T, better pilot ignition | **Improves** combustion completeness and reduces HC/CO |
| Increase in **Engine Speed** | Less time for mixing, shorter ignition delay window | **Degrades** combustion stability, especially at low load |
| Higher **Swirl/Turbulence** | Better air-fuel mixing, better flame propagation | **Improves** combustion rate and reduces cycle-to-cycle variation |
| Higher **Boost Pressure** | Higher density, better pilot ignition | **Improves** gaseous fuel energy density and combustion |
| Higher **Methane Number** of gaseous fuel | Higher knock resistance | **Enables** higher compression ratio and load |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are testable in KTU ESE numerical/analytical questions. Memorize the variable definitions and the units carefully.

**Table 2.1 — Core Combustion Formulas for Dual Fuel Engines**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi_g = \dfrac{m_{g}/m_{a}}{(A/F)_{stoich,g}}$ | Equivalence ratio of gaseous fuel | dimensionless |
| 2 | $PFES = \dfrac{m_{p} \cdot CV_{p}}{m_{p} \cdot CV_{p} + m_{g} \cdot CV_{g}}$ | Pilot Fuel Energy Share (fraction) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $SR = \dfrac{m_{g} \cdot CV_{g}}{m_{diesel,base} \cdot CV_{diesel}}$ | Substitution Ratio (diesel replaced basis) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $E_{total} = m_{p} \cdot CV_{p} + m_{g} \cdot CV_{g}$ | Total energy input per cycle | kJ/cycle |
| 5 | $\eta_{it} = \dfrac{W_{indicated}}{E_{total}}$ | Indicated thermal efficiency | % |
| 6 | $AFR_{total} = \dfrac{m_{a}}{m_{p} + m_{g}}$ | Overall air-fuel ratio (combined) | kg/kg |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{g} = \rho_{g} \cdot V_{d} \cdot \dfrac{N}{2} \cdot \eta_{vol}$ | Gaseous fuel mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{p} = \rho_{p} \cdot n_{holes} \cdot A_{noz} \cdot C_{d} \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_{p}}$ | Pilot fuel mass flow rate (orifice) | kg/s |
| 9 | $P_{max} = f(CR, PFES, \phi_{g}, \theta_{inj})$ | Peak cylinder pressure | bar |
| 10 | $(dP/d\theta)_{max} = \dfrac{\gamma \cdot P}{V} \cdot \dfrac{dV}{d\theta} + \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{V} \cdot \dfrac{dQ_{chem}}{d\theta}$ | Pressure rise rate (combustion roughness) | bar/°CA |
| 11 | $Q_{HR,f} = \dfrac{1}{W_{f}} \sum_{i} m_{i} \cdot Q_{i}$ | Heat release from fuel mass $W_{f}$ | kJ/kg-fuel |
| 12 | $\theta_{ign} = f\left(T_{cyl}, \tau, CN, P_{cyl}\right)$ | Ignition delay (often fit by Arrhenius) | °CA or ms |
| 13 | $T_{ad} = T_{1} \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} \cdot \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1} \cdot r^{\gamma}}$ | Adiabatic flame / compression temperature | K |
| 14 | $\tau = A \cdot P^{-n} \cdot \exp(E_a / R T)$ | Ignition delay (Arrhenius form) | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equation 10 (pressure rise rate) is the single most examination-relevant diagnostic** in dual fuel combustion problems. Excessive $dP/d\theta$ causes **combustion knock / roughness**, while too low values cause **misfire**. The KTU examiner often tests the student's ability to relate $dP/d\theta$ to gaseous fuel energy share and pilot timing.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Heavy-duty trucks, buses, and off-road equipment in cities with strict emission norms.
- **Power Generation:** Remote gas-field captive power, well-head generators using flare gas.
- **Marine:** Large 2-stroke dual fuel engines using LNG (e.g., MAN B&W ME-GI, Wärtsilä 31DF).
- **Agricultural Tractors:** Biogas-based dual fuel operation in rural areas.
- **Aviation (R&D):** Hydrogen-diesel dual fuel concepts for decarbonization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pilot Fuel Energy Share (PFES)

The Pilot Fuel Energy Share represents the fraction of total energy supplied by the pilot fuel (diesel) per cycle.

**Starting from the definition of energy from each fuel stream:**

$$E_p = m_p \cdot CV_p \quad \text{[kJ/cycle]}$$

$$E_g = m_g \cdot CV_g \quad \text{[kJ/cycle]}$$

The total energy input is the sum of the two energy contributions:

$$E_{total} = E_p + E_g = m_p \cdot CV_p + m_g \cdot CV_g$$

The Pilot Fuel Energy Share is the ratio of pilot energy to total energy:

$$PFES = \dfrac{E_p}{E_{total}}$$

Substituting the expressions for $E_p$ and $E_{total}$:

$$PFES = \dfrac{m_p \cdot CV_p}{m_p \cdot CV_p + m_g \cdot CV_g}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $m_p \cdot CV_p$:

$$PFES = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{m_g \cdot CV_g}{m_p \cdot CV_p}}$$

Defining the energy ratio $\beta = m_g CV_g / m_p CV_p$:

$$PFES = \dfrac{1}{1 + \beta}$$

> **Interpretation:** When gaseous fuel contribution is zero ($\beta = 0$), PFES = 1 (pure diesel). When pilot is small ($\beta \rightarrow \infty$), PFES → 0 (full gas operation). For typical KTU numerical problems, PFES = 0.10–0.20.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Substitution Ratio (SR)

Substitution Ratio quantifies the mass of baseline diesel replaced per unit mass of gaseous fuel.

**Baseline Diesel Energy per cycle (pure diesel mode, same output):**

$$E_{base} = m_{d,base} \cdot CV_d$$

**Actual Energy Supplied by Gaseous Fuel:**

$$E_g = m_g \cdot CV_g$$

By definition of substitution ratio:

$$SR = \dfrac{E_g}{E_{base}} = \dfrac{m_g \cdot CV_g}{m_{d,base} \cdot CV_d}$$

If we assume the energy output is preserved (same indicated work, $W_{i,base} = W_{i,DF}$), then:

$$SR = \dfrac{\eta_{it,base} \cdot m_{d,base} \cdot CV_d}{\eta_{it,DF} \cdot (m_p \cdot CV_p + m_g \cdot CV_g)} \cdot \dfrac{m_g \cdot CV_g}{m_{d,base} \cdot CV_d}$$

Simplifying by cancelling $m_{d,base} \cdot CV_d$ in numerator and denominator:

$$SR = \dfrac{\eta_{it,base}}{\eta_{it,DF}} \cdot \dfrac{m_g \cdot CV_g}{m_p \cdot CV_p + m_g \cdot CV_g}$$

> **Engineering Note:** Real engines rarely achieve SR = 1 because of the pilot fuel requirement. Typical values: SR = 0.70–0.90 for natural gas dual fuel, meaning 70–90% of diesel is replaced.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ignition Delay in Dual Fuel Combustion

The ignition delay of the pilot fuel determines the start of combustion. It is governed by the Arrhenius-type equation:

$$\tau = A \cdot P_{cyl}^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{cyl}}\right)$$

Where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential constant (fuel-specific)
- $P_{cyl}$ = cylinder pressure at injection (bar)
- $n$ = pressure exponent (≈ 1.0–1.5 for diesel)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **\mathbf{8.314 \; J/(mol \cdot K)}**
- $T_{cyl}$ = cylinder temperature at injection (K)

**Expressing cylinder temperature using the compression polytropic relation:**

$$T_{cyl} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} \cdot \eta_{pol}$$

Where:
- $T_1$ = temperature at start of compression (K)
- $r$ = compression ratio
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats ≈ **\mathbf{1.35}** for hot compressed gas
- $\eta_{pol}$ = polytropic efficiency of compression (≈ 0.90)

**Substituting back:**

$$\tau = A \cdot P_{cyl}^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R \cdot T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} \cdot \eta_{pol}}\right)$$

> **Key Insight for KTU:** Higher compression ratio (larger $r$) → higher $T_{cyl}$ → smaller $\tau$ (faster ignition). This is precisely why dual fuel engines **require a higher compression ratio than spark-ignition engines** to ignite the pilot fuel reliably.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pressure Rise Rate (Combustion Roughness)

The first law of thermodynamics applied to the cylinder contents (per unit crank angle) gives:

$$\dfrac{dU}{d\theta} = \dfrac{dQ_{chem}}{d\theta} - \dfrac{dW}{d\theta} - \dfrac{dQ_{loss}}{d\theta}$$

With $U = m \cdot c_v \cdot T$ and $W = \int P \, dV$:

$$m \cdot c_v \cdot \dfrac{dT}{d\theta} = \dfrac{dQ_{chem}}{d\theta} - P \cdot \dfrac{dV}{d\theta} - \dfrac{dQ_{loss}}{d\theta}$$

Using the ideal gas law $P V = m R T$ and differentiating, and combining terms, the classical Heywood expression emerges:

$$\dfrac{dP}{d\theta} = -\gamma \cdot \dfrac{P}{V} \cdot \dfrac{dV}{d\theta} + \dfrac{(\gamma - 1)}{V} \cdot \dfrac{dQ_{net}}{d\theta}$$

**Step-by-step simplification:**

- The first term $-\gamma (P/V)(dV/d\theta)$ is the **motoring term** (purely geometric, due to piston motion).
- The second term $\dfrac{(\gamma - 1)}{V} \cdot \dfrac{dQ_{net}}{d\theta}$ is the **combustion term** (positive when $dQ > 0$).
- The peak rate of pressure rise occurs when **the combustion term dominates**, near TDC during the premixed burn phase of the gaseous fuel.

**For dual fuel engines, the premixed burn fraction controls the magnitude of $(dP/d\theta)_{max}$:**

$$\left(\dfrac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{max} \propto \dfrac{1}{V_{TDC}} \cdot (\gamma - 1) \cdot m_{g,premixed} \cdot CV_g \cdot \dfrac{1}{\Delta \theta_{b}}$$

Where:
- $m_{g,premixed}$ = mass of premixed gaseous fuel
- $\Delta \theta_{b}$ = burn duration (crank angle)
- $V_{TDC}$ = clearance volume

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This equation is the heart of dual fuel combustion theory:** Higher gaseous fuel premixed mass with shorter burn duration → very high $dP/d\theta$ → combustion knock. The KTU examiner loves asking how to *reduce* this — answer: **lower gaseous energy share, advance pilot timing, increase swirl, or retard injection**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Factor on Key Outputs

The following table cross-relates each combustion-affecting factor with its impact on key engine outputs. Memorize this matrix for KTU 14-mark analytical questions.

| Factor ↑ | PFES | $\eta_{it}$ | HC/CO | NOx | $(dP/d\theta)_{max}$ | BSFC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compression Ratio ↑ | ↓ (less pilot needed) | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Pilot Fuel Quantity ↑ | ↑ | ↓ toward diesel | ↓ at low load | ↑ | ↓ (smoother) | ↑ |
| Gaseous Energy Share ↑ | ↓ | ↑ (peak at ~80%) | ↑ at low load | ↓ (lean) | ↑ sharply | ↓ |
| Pilot Injection Advance ↑ | — | (optimum exists) | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | (optimum) |
| Engine Load ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Engine Speed ↑ | — | (optimum) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | — |
| Swirl/Turbulence ↑ | — | ↑ | ↓ | — | ↓ smoother | — |
| Boost Pressure ↑ | — | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |

---

### 3.6 Illustrative Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A dual fuel engine running on CNG with diesel pilot has the following data per cycle:
- Mass of diesel pilot: $m_p = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg
- Calorific value of diesel: $CV_p = 42{,}000$ kJ/kg
- Mass of CNG: $m_g = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg
- Calorific value of CNG: $CV_g = 50{,}000$ kJ/kg
- Indicated work per cycle: $W_i = 1.4$ kJ/cycle

**Compute (a) PFES, (b) $\eta_{it}$.**

**Solution (a) — PFES:**

$$E_p = m_p \cdot CV_p = (0.5 \times 10^{-3})(42{,}000) = 21 \text{ kJ/cycle}$$

$$E_g = m_g \cdot CV_g = (1.5 \times 10^{-3})(50{,}000) = 75 \text{ kJ/cycle}$$

$$E_{total} = 21 + 75 = 96 \text{ kJ/cycle}$$

$$PFES = \dfrac{21}{96} = 0.2187 \approx 21.87\%$$

**Solution (b) — Indicated Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{it} = \dfrac{W_i}{E_{total}} = \dfrac{1.4}{96} = 0.01458 = 1.458\%$$

> **Note:** This very low efficiency is for illustration of formula usage. In practice, a full cycle (1800 RPM, 4-stroke) gives indicated work of tens of kJ/cycle. Adjust accordingly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Dual Fuel Combustion Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Intake Stroke: Air Inducted] --> B[Gaseous Fuel Inducted and Mixed]
    B --> C[Homogeneous Lean Premixture Formed in Cylinder]
    C --> D[Compression Stroke: T and P Rise Polytropically]
    D --> E[Near TDC: Pilot Diesel Injected at High Pressure]
    E --> F[Pilot Droplets Atomize, Vaporize, Mix with Air]
    F --> G[Ignition Delay: Chemical Reaction Kinetics]
    G --> H[Pilot Auto-Ignition: Multiple Kernels Formed]
    H --> I[Flame Propagation Through Gaseous Premixture]
    I --> J[Diffusion Burn of Remaining Gaseous Fuel]
    J --> K[Expansion: Heat Converted to Work]
    K --> L[Exhaust: Residual Gases Released]

    M[Fuel Properties] -.modulates.-> E
    N[Compression Ratio] -.modulates.-> D
    O[Injection Timing and Pressure] -.modulates.-> E
    P[Engine Load and Speed] -.modulates.-> G
    Q[Swirl and Turbulence] -.modulates.-> I
    R[Air Fuel Equivalence Ratio] -.modulates.-> C
    S[Combustion Chamber Geometry] -.modulates.-> I
    T[Boost and EGR] -.modulates.-> D
```

### 4.2 Influence Diagram — Factors Affecting Dual Fuel Combustion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FUEL_FACTORS[FUEL RELATED FACTORS]
        F1[Gaseous Fuel Type]
        F2[Pilot Fuel Quantity]
        F3[Air Fuel Ratio]
    end

    subgraph OP_FACTORS[ENGINE OPERATING FACTORS]
        O1[Compression Ratio]
        O2[Injection Timing]
        O3[Engine Load]
        O4[Engine Speed]
        O5[Boost Pressure]
    end

    subgraph DESIGN_FACTORS[DESIGN FACTORS]
        D1[Chamber Geometry]
        D2[Swirl Turbulence]
        D3[Pilot Injector Spray]
    end

    FUEL_FACTORS --> OUT[COMBUSTION QUALITY OUTPUTS]
    OP_FACTORS --> OUT
    DESIGN_FACTORS --> OUT

    OUT --> P1[Ignition Reliability]
    OUT --> P2[Combustion Efficiency]
    OUT --> P3[Pressure Rise Rate]
    OUT --> P4[Emissions HC CO NOx]
    OUT --> P5[Thermal Efficiency]
    OUT --> P6[Cycle to Cycle Variation]
```

### 4.3 Stage-by-Stage Crank-Angle Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage1: Induction of Air and Gaseous Fuel 0 to 180 CA] --> S2[Stage2: Polytropic Compression 180 to 360 CA]
    S2 --> S3[Stage3: Pilot Injection and Ignition Delay 355 to 365 CA]
    S3 --> S4[Stage4: Pilot Ignition and Premixed Burn 365 to 385 CA]
    S4 --> S5[Stage5: Diffusion Controlled Burn of Gas 385 to 430 CA]
    S5 --> S6[Stage6: Expansion 430 to 540 CA]
    S6 --> S7[Stage7: Exhaust 540 to 720 CA]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Combustion Quality Control)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT[Inputs: Fuel Properties, Air, Operating Conditions] --> CTRL[Combustion Control System]
    CTRL --> M1[Module 1: Pilot Injection ECU]
    CTRL --> M2[Module 2: Gaseous Fuel Metering]
    CTRL --> M3[Module 3: Air Intake Throttle]
    M1 --> CYL[Cylinder Combustion Chamber]
    M2 --> CYL
    M3 --> CYL
    CYL --> SENSE[In-Cylinder Pressure and Temperature Sensors]
    SENSE --> FB[Feedback Signal]
    FB --> CTRL
    CYL --> OUTPUT[Outputs: Power, Efficiency, Emissions, Noise]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Define a dual fuel engine. With a neat sketch, explain the working principle and identify the two fuels used."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **dual fuel engine** is a modified compression-ignition engine that uses two fuels simultaneously:

1. **Primary fuel:** A low-reactivity gaseous fuel (CNG, LNG, biogas, LPG, or hydrogen) inducted with air to form a homogeneous lean premixture.
2. **Secondary fuel:** A high-reactivity liquid pilot fuel (diesel or biodiesel) injected near TDC to initiate combustion.

**Working Principle:** The gaseous fuel-air mixture is compressed to a high pressure and temperature. Since the gaseous fuel alone has a high auto-ignition temperature, it does not auto-ignite. A small quantity of diesel is injected near TDC, which auto-ignites due to compression heating. The diesel flames then ignite the surrounding premixed gaseous fuel charge, producing power.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for two-fuel identification, 1 for working principle)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List any six major factors that affect combustion in a dual fuel engine."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — list and brief explanation):**

1. **Compression ratio** — higher CR improves pilot ignition and gaseous fuel combustion.
2. **Pilot fuel quantity** — must be sufficient to ensure reliable ignition at all loads.
3. **Type and composition of gaseous fuel** — methane number and calorific value affect combustion rate.
4. **Injection timing of pilot fuel** — influences peak pressure and rate of pressure rise.
5. **Engine load** — low load conditions cause poor combustion due to low in-cylinder temperatures.
6. **Swirl and turbulence** — enhances air-fuel mixing and flame propagation through the premixed gas.

(3 marks: 0.5 per factor with one-line justification)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **four stages of combustion** in a dual fuel engine with the help of a crank-angle-based schematic. Discuss the role of the **pilot fuel** in each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the effect of the following on dual fuel combustion: (i) Compression ratio, (ii) Pilot fuel quantity, (iii) Gaseous fuel energy share. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution:**

**(a) Four Stages of Combustion (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Induction (0° to 180° CA):**
Air and gaseous fuel are inducted through the intake manifold. The mixing is passive and produces a **homogeneous lean premixture** with $\phi_g \approx 0.3$–$0.7$. No pilot fuel is involved.

**Stage 2 — Compression (180° to ~360° CA):**
The premixed charge is compressed polytropically. By the time the piston approaches TDC, the temperature and pressure reach values that satisfy the auto-ignition condition of the diesel pilot, but not of the gaseous fuel (which has higher AIT). No combustion occurs yet.

**Stage 3 — Pilot Injection and Ignition Delay (~355° to 365° CA):**
The diesel pilot is injected at high pressure (200–1000 bar) through the injector. The droplets undergo atomization, vaporization, and mixing with the surrounding hot air. After the (short) chemical ignition delay, the pilot auto-ignites, forming **multiple high-temperature ignition kernels**. The size and number of kernels depend on pilot quantity, injection pressure, and chamber design.

**Stage 4 — Premixed and Diffusion Burn of Gaseous Fuel (~365° to 430° CA):**
Flame fronts from the kernels propagate through the surrounding gaseous premixture at speeds determined by local $\phi_g$, turbulence, and temperature. The first part is **premixed burn** (fast, can cause high $dP/d\theta$); the latter part is **diffusion-controlled burn** of the gas that has not yet been reached by the flame (slower, controlled by mixing).

**Role of Pilot Fuel:** Acts as the **ignition source** for the otherwise non-auto-igniting gaseous fuel. The pilot must provide enough ignition energy, and the kernels must be sufficiently distributed to ignite all the gaseous charge.

(7 marks: 1.5 per stage + 1 for pilot role)

---

**(b) Effect of Three Factors (7 marks):**

**(i) Compression Ratio (2.5 marks):**
- Higher CR raises the polytropic compression end temperature: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$.
- This reduces the pilot ignition delay and improves the reliability of pilot ignition.
- Allows reduction in pilot fuel quantity (lower PFES).
- Enables higher gaseous fuel energy share to be burned completely.
- Trade-off: too high CR causes combustion knock (high $dP/d\theta$) and high mechanical stress.
- Typical dual fuel engines operate at CR = **14:1 to 18:1**, higher than SI engines.

**(ii) Pilot Fuel Quantity (2.5 marks):**
- Governed by PFES, typically **5–25%**.
- At very low pilot quantity: misfire, high HC and CO, low efficiency.
- At high pilot quantity: combustion approaches pure diesel mode; gaseous fuel benefit is lost.
- **Optimum PFES depends on load:** higher pilot needed at low loads to ensure ignition.
- Acts as a "combustion stabilizer" in dual fuel engines.

**(iii) Gaseous Fuel Energy Share (2 marks):**
- Represents the fraction of energy supplied by gas: $1 - PFES$.
- Higher share → more lean premixed charge → smoother, more complete combustion at high load.
- At low load: very high gas share leads to over-leaning, low in-cylinder temperatures, **misfire and high HC/CO**.
- High gas share also → higher $dP/d\theta$ → combustion knock risk.
- Practical limit: ~80–85% gas energy share in natural gas dual fuel engines.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **(i) Pilot Fuel Energy Share (PFES)** and **(ii) Substitution Ratio (SR)**. State the typical numerical ranges for a CNG-diesel dual fuel engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **engine operating parameters** (injection timing, load, speed, boost pressure) and **design parameters** (swirl, chamber geometry) collectively determine the combustion quality in a dual fuel engine. Include the role of $(dP/d\theta)_{max}$. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution:**

**(a) Derivations (7 marks):**

**(i) PFES Derivation (3.5 marks):**

$$E_p = m_p \cdot CV_p, \quad E_g = m_g \cdot CV_g$$

$$E_{total} = E_p + E_g$$

$$PFES = \dfrac{E_p}{E_{total}} = \dfrac{m_p \cdot CV_p}{m_p \cdot CV_p + m_g \cdot CV_g} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \beta}$$

where $\beta = (m_g CV_g)/(m_p CV_p)$.

For a typical **CNG-diesel** dual fuel engine: **PFES = 0.05–0.25** (i.e., 5–25%).

**(ii) SR Derivation (3.5 marks):**

$$SR = \dfrac{m_g \cdot CV_g}{m_{d,base} \cdot CV_d} = \dfrac{\eta_{it,base}}{\eta_{it,DF}} \cdot \dfrac{m_g \cdot CV_g}{m_p \cdot CV_p + m_g \cdot CV_g}$$

For CNG-diesel dual fuel: **SR = 0.70–0.90** (70–90% diesel replaced by gas on energy basis).

---

**(b) Combined Effect of Parameters (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Mechanism | Effect on Combustion |
|---|---|---|
| **Injection Timing** | Earlier injection → longer time for pilot mixing before ignition → earlier start of combustion | Higher peak P, can increase $dP/d\theta$ |
| **Engine Load** | Higher load → higher in-cylinder T → better pilot ignition | Low load: poor combustion (HC/CO ↑); High load: efficient |
| **Engine Speed** | Higher RPM → less time for mixing and combustion | Cycle-to-cycle variation ↑, especially at low load |
| **Boost Pressure** | Higher intake density → higher compression end T and P | Allows higher gaseous energy share to be burned |
| **Swirl** | Organized rotation of charge → better pilot spray-gas mixing | Faster flame propagation, lower $dP/d\theta$ |
| **Chamber Geometry** | Open vs. pre-chamber vs. swirl chamber | Pre-chamber and swirl chambers: better low-load combustion |

**Role of $(dP/d\theta)_{max}$:**

The maximum rate of pressure rise is the **key diagnostic** for combustion quality:

$$\left(\dfrac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{max} \propto \dfrac{m_{g,premixed} \cdot CV_g}{V_{TDC} \cdot \Delta \theta_{b}}$$

- **Too high $dP/d\theta$** → combustion knock, mechanical stress, noise.
- **Too low $dP/d\theta$** → misfire, partial burn, high HC and CO.

The goal of the design and control strategy is to **maintain $dP/d\theta$ within 4–10 bar/°CA** for smooth, complete, and knock-free combustion.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing dual fuel with spark-ignition (SI) engines.** Dual fuel engines IGNITE THE GASEOUS FUEL VIA THE DIESEL PILOT, not by a spark plug. Writing "spark plug ignites the CNG" will fetch **0 marks** for the working principle question.
>
> 2. **Confusing PFES with SR.** PFES is a *fraction* of energy from pilot; SR is a *ratio* of gas energy to baseline diesel energy. These are **NOT the same** and substituting one for the other loses full marks in numericals.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the unit of $dP/d\theta$.** It is **bar per degree crank angle (bar/°CA)**, not bar/second. Converting incorrectly yields numerical errors.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the role of compression ratio.** A common incomplete answer is "high CR is good for dual fuel." The complete answer is "high CR raises T₂, reduces pilot ignition delay, but also increases the risk of high $dP/d\theta$ — there is an optimum."
>
> 5. **Not drawing the schematic.** For a 14-mark question asking for stages of combustion, **a crank-angle diagram is mandatory**. Text-only answers lose up to **2 marks** even if the content is correct.
>
> 6. **Wrong formula for $PFES$** — students often write $\frac{m_p}{m_p + m_g}$ (mass ratio) instead of energy ratio. Always use the **calorific-value-weighted** form.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **dual fuel engine** is a CI engine in which a **gaseous primary fuel** is mixed with air and a **liquid pilot fuel** (diesel) ignites the mixture via **compression ignition**.
- The combustion has **four stages**: induction, compression, pilot ignition, and premixed + diffusion burn of gas.
- **Key governing factors:** Compression ratio, pilot quantity (PFES), gaseous energy share, injection timing, engine load, speed, boost, swirl, and chamber geometry.
- **Most important performance metric:** $(dP/d\theta)_{max}$ — too high causes knock, too low causes misfire. Optimum = **4–10 bar/°CA**.
- **Pilot Fuel Energy Share (PFES):** $\dfrac{m_p CV_p}{m_p CV_p + m_g CV_g}$ — typical range **5–25%** for CNG-diesel.
- **Substitution Ratio (SR):** $\dfrac{m_g CV_g}{m_{d,base} CV_d}$ — typical range **70–90%** for natural gas dual fuel.
- **Ignition delay** follows Arrhenius kinetics: $\tau = A P^{-n} \exp(E_a/RT)$.
- **High gaseous energy share** + **low load** = **misfire and high HC/CO**.
- **High CR** + **advanced pilot timing** = **high $dP/d\theta$ (knock)**.
- **Swirl and turbulence** are the primary design tools to **lower $dP/d\theta$** while maintaining combustion efficiency.
- **Methane number** of the gaseous fuel determines the **knock resistance** and hence the **maximum allowable CR**.
- For KTU problems, always state **units** clearly: PFES (dimensionless), $dP/d\theta$ (bar/°CA), $W_i$ (kJ/cycle), $CV$ (kJ/kg).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multifuel Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multifuel Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Multifuel Engine** is an internal combustion engine — typically derived from a **compression-ignition (CI) diesel platform** — that has been purposefully engineered, modified, and calibrated to operate efficiently and reliably on a **wide spectrum of liquid and gaseous fuels** of varying physical and chemical properties, often *without major structural changes* to the basic engine block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim-aligned):** A multifuel engine is one that is designed to run on a variety of fuels (such as petrol, kerosene, diesel, alcohol, vegetable oils, producer gas, biogas, CNG, LPG, and hydrogen), either by changing the compression ratio, the combustion chamber geometry, or by adding auxiliary fuel-conditioning and dual-injection subsystems.

The **single most defining characteristic** of a multifuel engine is *fuel flexibility* — the ability of one engine hardware configuration to consume multiple fuels across the **entire operating map (load × speed)** with acceptable performance, emissions, and durability.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a multifuel engine like a versatile human stomach/digestive system:** Just as a healthy person can digest rice, bread, meat, or vegetables (each with totally different nutritional content, texture, and digestion time), a multifuel engine can "digest" petrol, kerosene, alcohol, or gas. The "stomach" (combustion chamber) is somewhat adaptable, but it needs "digestive aids" (modifications to compression ratio, injection timing, heating value) to properly break down each type of fuel into useful energy.

A **normal diesel engine** is like a person who can only eat one specific dish. A **multifuel engine** is the same person who has trained their metabolism to handle many different foods, perhaps with the help of specific condiments (fuel additives) or cooking methods (combustion chamber shape changes).

## 1.3 Historical and Strategic Context

Multifuel engines gained prominence during **World War II** (1939–1945), when military vehicles (notably German Panzer tanks and U.S. Army trucks like the **GMC CCKW** and **Studebaker US6**) were required to operate on whatever fuel could be locally captured, refined, or improvised — kerosene, diesel, gasoline, low-grade tars, or even alcohol.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever the question asks *"Why are multifuel engines still relevant today?"*, mention the **three modern drivers**:
> 1. **Energy security** — Reduces dependence on a single fuel source.
> 2. **Renewable/biofuel integration** — Allows use of locally produced fuels (ethanol, biodiesel, biogas) in remote/rural areas.
> 3. **Emergency/strategic reserve flexibility** — Military and disaster-relief operations.

## 1.4 The Three Fundamental Properties That Make a Fuel "Different" in an Engine

A multifuel engine must be able to handle dramatic differences in these three properties:

| Property | Affects | Range Across Fuels |
|----------|---------|---------------------|
| **Cetane Number (CN) / Octane Number (ON)** | Ignition quality | Hydrogen ≈ high CN; Methanol = high ON |
| **Calorific Value (CV)** | Energy per kg | Hydrogen ≈ **142 MJ/kg**; Diesel ≈ **43 MJ/kg** |
| **Viscosity & Volatility** | Spray atomization | Kerosene = thin; Vegetable oil = thick |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The biggest engineering challenge in a multifuel engine is **ignition** — fuels with low cetane number (e.g., gasoline, methanol, ethanol) will **NOT auto-ignite** at normal diesel compression ratios (14–18). This single fact drives **all** the major design modifications discussed in Section 2.

## 1.5 Core Categories of Multifuel Engine Approaches (Classification)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes three primary architectural approaches:

1. **Approach A — Adjustable Compression Ratio (ACR) engines** — Mechanical systems that vary the clearance volume (e.g., movable piston crowns, split heads, eccentric cranks) to suit the fuel's octane/cetane rating.
2. **Approach B — Dual-Combustion / Dual-Fuel engines** — A small quantity of pilot diesel (or pilot glow plug) ignites a lean mixture of a low-cetane primary fuel (gas, alcohol).
3. **Approach C — Spark-Assisted / Hot-Plate Assisted multifuel** — A spark plug or hot bulb assists ignition of otherwise hard-to-ignite fuels, allowing conventional diesel hardware to be retained.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" of Multifuel Engine Design — Core Operating Logic

A standard **diesel engine** relies on the **auto-ignition principle**: as the piston compresses the air-fuel mixture, the temperature at top dead center (TDC) must exceed the **auto-ignition temperature** of the fuel. For pure diesel, this is satisfied at compression ratios (CR) of **14:1 to 18:1**.

When a multifuel engine switches to a fuel like **gasoline (ON ≈ 88–98)**, the auto-ignition temperature is *higher* than what 14:1 CR can produce, and the fuel may **knock** or fail to ignite. Conversely, when switching to a high-cetane fuel like **kerosene (CN ≈ 40–45)**, the lower auto-ignition resistance causes *premature* ignition and rough running.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Design Rule:** The required compression ratio of an engine is inversely related to the **knock resistance** (octane number) of the fuel. Higher ON → Lower CR acceptable; Higher CN → Higher CR acceptable.

The relationship is approximately governed by:

$$CR_{req} \;\approx\; k_1 \cdot \frac{1}{ON} + k_2 \cdot T_{ign}$$

where $k_1$ and $k_2$ are experimentally determined constants and $T_{ign}$ is the auto-ignition temperature.

## 2.2 The Three Major Engine Modifications Required

For an engine to be classified as a *true* multifuel engine, the following subsystems are typically modified:

### (i) Combustion Chamber Modifications
- **Open combustion chamber** (direct injection) — simplest, used in multifuel applications due to wide fuel flexibility.
- **Swirl chambers / pre-chambers** — improve atomization of viscous fuels like vegetable oils and heavy oils.
- **Variable geometry chambers** — some advanced designs use hydraulically actuated inserts to alter the chamber volume and swirl intensity.

### (ii) Injection System Modifications
- **Higher injection pressures (200–400 bar, or even 1000+ bar in CRDI systems)** — essential to atomize high-viscosity fuels.
- **Larger injectors and modified nozzle geometries** — multi-hole nozzles for finer spray.
- **Variable injection timing (VIT)** — advances or retards injection based on fuel cetane rating.
- **Heated fuel lines** — for high-viscosity fuels (biodiesel, vegetable oils, heavy fuel oil).

### (iii) Ignition System Modifications
- **Glow plugs** — assist cold-starting with low-cetane fuels.
- **Spark plugs** — for gasoline/petrol mode (some dual-fuel multifuel engines).
- **Hot-bulb / hot-spot** — retained heat ignites air-fuel mixture (used in some kerosene/methanol engines).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-priority formula reference** for solving any KTU numerical on multifuel engines. Memorize the variables and their units.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Standard Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Equivalence Ratio** | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | Lean ($\phi \lt 1$), Stoich ($\phi = 1$), Rich ($\phi \gt 1$) |
| 2 | **Lower Calorific Value (LCV)** | $Q = m \cdot LCV$ | kJ or MJ | Energy released per kg of fuel |
| 3 | **Indicated Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{it} = \dfrac{W_{indicated}}{m_f \cdot LCV}$ | dimensionless | Energy conversion to work |
| 4 | **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption** | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot 3600}{P_{brake}}$ | kg/kWh | Fuel used per unit brake power |
| 5 | **Compression Ratio** | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c}$ | dimensionless | Volume ratio (clearance to TDC) |
| 6 | **Air-Fuel Ratio by Mass** | $(A/F) = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | dimensionless | Mass of air per mass of fuel |
| 7 | **Stoichiometric A/F (Carbon-Hydrogen-Oxygen fuel $C_xH_yO_z$)** | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{34.56\,(x + y/4 - z/2)}{12x + y + 16z}$ | dimensionless | For complete combustion |
| 8 | **Relative Efficiency** | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{\eta_{actual}}{\eta_{air-standard}}$ | dimensionless | How close to ideal cycle |
| 9 | **Energy Share by Pilot Fuel** | $f_{pilot} = \dfrac{Q_{pilot}}{Q_{pilot} + Q_{primary}}$ | dimensionless | Pilot fuel fraction in dual-fuel |
| 10 | **Auto-ignition temp relation (qualitative)** | $T_{ig} \propto \dfrac{1}{CN}$ | K | Higher CN → lower ignition temp |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** When computing the stoichiometric A/F ratio for a multifuel engine running on **methanol ($CH_3OH$)**, **ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$)**, or **hydrogen ($H_2$)**, students often forget the **oxygen already present in the fuel molecule**. Use the general formula in row 7 — the $z$ term accounts for this internal oxygen.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multifuel engine technology is critically applied in:

1. **Military vehicles** — tanks, trucks, armored personnel carriers operating across geographies.
2. **Agricultural tractors** in developing nations — can burn locally produced biofuels, kerosene, or even non-standard refinery products.
3. **Remote power generation** — rural electrification using biogas or producer gas with diesel pilot.
4. **Marine and locomotive prime movers** — bunker fuel flexibility.
5. **Disaster response vehicles** — where conventional fuel supply chains are disrupted.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Question papers frequently test the **modifications needed to convert a standard diesel engine into a multifuel engine**, and the **impact of fuel properties (CN, ON, CV, viscosity) on compression ratio and injection timing**. Always link a fuel property to a specific engine modification in your answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for a General Hydrocarbon Fuel

> **Problem:** A multifuel engine is to operate on a generic fuel with chemical formula $C_x H_y O_z$. Derive the stoichiometric mass of air required per kg of fuel for **complete combustion**.

**Step 1 — Write the general combustion equation.**

The complete combustion of one kmol of fuel is:

$$C_x H_y O_z + a\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + b\,O_2 + 3.76a\,N_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply oxygen balance.**

Oxygen supplied in air = oxygen in $CO_2$ + oxygen in $H_2O$ + free $O_2$. For *stoichiometric* combustion, $b = 0$:

$$2a = 2x + \frac{y}{2} - z$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $a$ (kmol of $O_2$ per kmol of fuel).**

$$a = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass basis.**

- Mass of air per kmol of fuel = $a \times (32 + 3.76 \times 28) = a \times 137.28$ kg
- Mass of fuel per kmol = $(12x + y + 16z)$ kg

**Step 5 — Form the A/F ratio.**

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{a \times 137.28}{12x + y + 16z} = \frac{34.56\,(x + y/4 - z/2)}{12x + y + 16z}$$

This is the **master stoichiometric A/F formula** in row 7 of the cheat sheet.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Multifuel Engine on Methanol

> **Problem:** A multifuel CI engine runs on pure **methanol** $CH_3OH$ (i.e., $x=1, y=4, z=1$). Calculate the stoichiometric A/F ratio. If the actual A/F supplied is 6.0, find the **equivalence ratio** $\phi$ and the **percent excess air**.

**Step 1 — Apply the stoichiometric formula.**

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{34.56 \times (1 + 4/4 - 1/2)}{12(1) + 4 + 16(1)}$$

$$= \frac{34.56 \times (1 + 1 - 0.5)}{12 + 4 + 16} = \frac{34.56 \times 1.5}{32} = \frac{51.84}{32}$$

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = 1.62 \text{ (by mass)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the equivalence ratio.**

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}} = \frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}} = \frac{1.62}{6.0} = 0.27$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

Since $\phi = 0.27 \ll 1$, the mixture is **highly lean** — this is consistent with methanol's *lean-burn capability* and is a key reason multifuel engines love methanol as a primary fuel.

**Step 4 — Compute percent excess air.**

$$\%\,Excess\,Air = \left(\frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} - 1\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{6.0}{1.62} - 1\right) \times 100$$

$$= (3.7037 - 1) \times 100 \approx 270.4\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for the student:** Methanol needs roughly **6.5× less air mass** than diesel because the oxygen is *built into the fuel molecule*. This drastically changes intake manifold design and is a favourite KTU question topic.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Energy Share by Pilot Fuel in Dual-Fuel Mode

> **Problem:** A dual-fuel multifuel engine operates on **80% biogas + 20% pilot diesel by energy**. If the calorific values are: LCV(biogas) = **20 MJ/m³** and LCV(diesel) = **43 MJ/kg**, and the engine consumes **0.3 m³ of biogas per second** and **0.05 kg of diesel per second**, verify the energy share.

**Step 1 — Compute heat released by each fuel per second.**

$$Q_{biogas} = \dot{V}_{biogas} \times LCV_{biogas} = 0.3 \times 20 = 6.0\ \text{MJ/s}$$

$$Q_{diesel} = \dot{m}_{diesel} \times LCV_{diesel} = 0.05 \times 43 = 2.15\ \text{MJ/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total energy and percentages.**

$$Q_{total} = 6.0 + 2.15 = 8.15\ \text{MJ/s}$$

$$f_{biogas} = \frac{6.0}{8.15} = 0.7362 = 73.62\,\%$$

$$f_{diesel} = \frac{2.15}{8.15} = 0.2638 = 26.38\,\%$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

Even though biogas has only **20 MJ/m³** (very low energy density), its *volumetric flow* is **6× that of diesel**, so the **energy share comes out roughly 74:26**, not 80:20 as given in the problem. The problem statement's "80% by energy" assumption is therefore an *idealized* design intent, not a measured value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is high-yield for KTU:** Dual-fuel energy-share analysis is one of the most frequently repeated 14-mark problems in Module 1. The examiner expects to see (1) identification of pilot fuel, (2) calculation of energy fractions, and (3) discussion of how pilot fraction varies with load.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Multifuel Choices

| Fuel | CN | ON | LCV (MJ/kg) | Stoich A/F | Auto-Ignition Issue | Modification Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Diesel** | 45–55 | — | 42–45 | 14.5 | None (baseline) | Baseline |
| **Kerosene** | 40–45 | — | 43.4 | 14.7 | Slightly early | Slight CR reduction |
| **Gasoline** | — | 88–98 | 44 | 14.7 | Will not auto-ignite at 14:1 | Add spark plug OR raise CR to 22:1 |
| **Methanol** | ~3 | 108 | 19.9 | 6.43 | Will not auto-ignite | Hot plate + glow plug + larger injectors |
| **Ethanol** | ~8 | 108 | 26.9 | 9.0 | Hard cold-start | Glow plug + heated manifold |
| **CNG / Methane** | 0 | 130 | 50 | 17.2 | Will not auto-ignite | Spark plug + pressurized gas injection |
| **Hydrogen** | 0 | 130+ | 142 | 34.3 | Backfire & pre-ignition | Special injectors, low CR, vented crankcase |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Octane Number* and *Cetane Number* when assigning fuels to multifuel engines. **Remember:** ON ↔ gasoline engines (resistance to knock); CN ↔ diesel engines (ease of auto-ignition). They are *inverse* properties of the same fundamental chemical behaviour.

## 3.5 Compression Ratio Adjustment Derivation (Qualitative)

A fuel's required compression ratio can be related to its **research octane number (RON)** and **motor octane number (MON)** by the empirical Knocking-Limited Compression Ratio relation:

$$CR_{max,\,fuel} \approx a \cdot \frac{MON}{100} + b \cdot \frac{(RON - MON)}{100} + c$$

where $a, b, c$ are engine-specific calibration constants.

> For multifuel applications, a designer selects $CR$ such that:
> $$CR_{design} = \min_{fuels} \left( CR_{max,\,fuel} \right)$$
> i.e., the *most knock-resistant* fuel determines the upper limit of $CR$ acceptable. This is why multifuel engines **rarely** exceed CR of 17:1, even when running on diesel.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multifuel Engine System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a multifuel engine — that is, how the *fuel selection*, *conditioning*, *combustion*, and *control* subsystems interact. (A physical schematic with full engine components is best studied from your textbook figures, but the architecture below is what KTU questions actually test.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FuelSourceA] --> F1[FuelConditioningUnit]
    B[FuelSourceB] --> F1
    C[FuelSourceC] --> F1
    D[FuelSourceD] --> F1
    F1 --> G[FuelSelectionValve]
    G --> H[HeatedFuelRail]
    H --> I[HighPressureInjectionPump]
    I --> J[MultiHoleInjector]
    K[GlowPlugAndOrSparkPlug] --> J
    J --> L[CombustionChamber]
    M[AirIntakeAndTurbocharger] --> L
    L --> N[PistonAndCrankshaft]
    N --> O[PowerOutputShaft]
    P[EngineControlUnitECU] --> G
    P --> I
    P --> K
    P --> M
    L --> Q[ExhaustAndAfterTreatment]
    Q --> R[EmissionsToAtmosphere]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **four fuel sources (A, B, C, D)** can be diesel, kerosene, alcohol, or gas.
- The **Fuel Conditioning Unit (F1)** handles viscosity reduction (heating), pressure regulation, and filtration — *critical* for high-viscosity fuels.
- The **Fuel Selection Valve (G)** is electronically or manually switched.
- The **Glow Plug and Or Spark Plug (K)** is the *multifuel ignition key* — diesel-only engines omit this.
- The **ECU (P)** receives signals about fuel type and dynamically adjusts **injection timing, EGR rate, and boost pressure** to optimize combustion for the active fuel.

## 4.2 Decision-Flow Diagram — How to Select a Multifuel Modification Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[FuelSelectionStage] --> S2{IsCNHigh}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[UseStandardDieselHardware]
    S2 -- No --> S4{FuelIsGaseous}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[UseDualFuelWithPilotDiesel]
    S4 -- No --> S6{AutoIgnitionTempIsHigh}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[AddGlowPlugOrHotPlate]
    S6 -- No --> S8{FuelIsHighViscosity}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[AddHeatedFuelRailAndHighPressurePump]
    S8 -- No --> S10[AdjustInjectionTimingAndCR]
    S3 --> S11[MultifuelEngineReady]
    S5 --> S11
    S7 --> S11
    S9 --> S11
    S10 --> S11
```

**Interpretation of the decision flow:**

| Decision Node | Engineering Criterion | Practical Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| Is CN high? | Standard diesel hardware sufficient | CN ≥ 40 |
| Fuel is gaseous? | Requires pilot fuel approach | Boiling point < -40 °C |
| Auto-ignition temp is high? | Needs glow plug or hot plate | T_ig > 350 °C at 14:1 CR |
| Fuel is high-viscosity? | Needs heated rail + high-pressure pump | μ > 10 cSt at 40 °C |

## 4.3 Multifuel Engine Subsystem Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FE[Fuel Energy Subsystem]
        FE1[LiquidFuelTank] --> FE2[FuelPump]
        FE3[GasCylinder] --> FE4[GasPressureRegulator]
        FE2 --> FE5[FuelRail]
        FE4 --> FE5
    end
    subgraph AC[Air And Combustion Subsystem]
        AC1[AirFilter] --> AC2[TurbochargerOrSupercharger]
        AC2 --> AC3[IntakeManifold]
        AC3 --> AC4[CombustionChamber]
        FE5 --> AC4
        AC4 --> AC5[ExhaustManifold]
    end
    subgraph CT[Control Subsystem]
        CT1[EngineControlUnit] --> CT2[Sensors]
        CT2 --> CT1
        CT1 --> FE2
        CT1 --> FE4
        CT1 --> AC2
    end
    AC5 --> OUT[ExhaustToAfterTreatment]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this in an exam answer:** If asked to "describe the modifications needed in a diesel engine to convert it into a multifuel engine," draw a simplified version of the above block diagram and **explicitly label the added/modified blocks** (FE4, FE5, AC2, CT1). Examiners give 2–3 marks just for a clear, labelled block diagram.

---
