# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CNG Conversion Requirements — Step-by-Step Logic

### A. Storage System Requirements
1. **High-pressure cylinder design**: CNG cylinders are rated to **20–25 MPa (working pressure)** with a safety factor of **2.25–2.5**, giving burst pressures of **45–62.5 MPa**.
2. **Material selection**: Typically **chromium-molybdenum steel (Cr-Mo, AISI 4130)** or **aluminum liners with carbon-fiber overwrap** for Type IV cylinders.
3. **Volume capacity**: Passenger cars store 4–6 kg of CNG at 20 MPa in tanks of approximately 50–80 L water volume.
4. **Cylinder life**: Specified as **15 years** or **3,750 fill cycles**, whichever is earlier.

### B. Pressure Regulation System
- **High-pressure regulator (first stage)**: Reduces pressure from 20 MPa to approximately 0.7 MPa.
- **Low-pressure regulator (second stage)**: Reduces pressure to near-atmospheric (slight vacuum or 1–5 kPa above manifold pressure).
- **Pressure differential is critical**: Gas expansion causes Joule-Thomson cooling, requiring **heaters** to prevent regulator freeze.

### C. Fuel Metering and Mixing
- **Venturi mixer** or **gasoline-type carburetor replacement**
- **Sequential Multi-Point Injection (SMPI)** with electronically controlled solenoid injectors
- **Mixer types**: Venturi, impeller, or hot-film air-mass-meter based

### D. Engine Calibration Changes
- **Ignition timing advance**: CNG burns faster (laminar flame speed ≈ 35–45 cm/s vs 37 cm/s for petrol), but slower than petrol at lean mixtures.
- **Air-fuel ratio**: Stoichiometric AFR for CH₄ = **17.2:1** (mass basis), requiring ECU mapping.
- **Compression ratio**: Can be increased from 9:1 to 12:1–14:1 for better efficiency.

## 2.2 LNG Conversion Requirements — Step-by-Step Logic

### A. Cryogenic Storage
1. **Double-wall vacuum-insulated tanks** (Dewar design) to minimize boil-off.
2. **Operating pressure**: 0.1–1.0 MPa (near atmospheric).
3. **Boil-Off Gas (BOG) management**: LNG absorbs heat, producing methane vapor that must be safely vented or used.

### B. Vaporization System
- **Ambient air vaporizers** or **engine coolant heat exchangers** convert LNG back to gaseous form before injection.
- **Regasification rate** must match engine fuel demand.

### C. Cryogenic Pumping
- Cryogenic pumps move liquid from tank to engine, typically at **0.5–1.0 MPa**.

### D. Trade-off Analysis
- LNG has higher energy density but requires **cryogenic handling infrastructure** — primarily used in heavy-duty trucks, ships, and long-haul applications.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $P V = Z n R_u T$ | Real gas equation for CNG storage (Z = compressibility) | Pa, m³, mol, J/kmol·K |
| $m_{CNG} = \dfrac{P \cdot V}{Z \cdot R_u \cdot T} \cdot M_{CH_4}$ | Mass of CNG stored in cylinder | kg |
| $W_{comp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | Isentropic compression work (CNG filling) | J |
| $\eta_{vol,CNG} = \eta_{vol,petrol} \times \left( \dfrac{LHV_{CNG}}{LHV_{petrol}} \right) \times \dfrac{AFR_{petrol}}{AFR_{CNG}}$ | Relative volumetric efficiency | dimensionless |
| $\rho_{LNG} = \dfrac{M_{CH_4}}{V_m(T)}$ | LNG density (Molar volume $V_m$ at −162°C ≈ 35.5 cm³/mol) | kg/m³ |
| $V_{LNG} = \dfrac{V_{gas,STP} \times P_{STP}}{Z_{STP} \cdot T_{STP}} \times \dfrac{Z_{boil} \cdot T_{boil}}{P_{boil} \cdot \rho_{LNG}}$ | Volume reduction ratio (gas → LNG) | m³ |
| $Q_{vap} = m \cdot L_v$ | Heat required to vaporize LNG ($L_v \approx 510$ kJ/kg for CH₄) | J |
| $AFR_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{CH_4} = 17.2}{1}$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for pure methane | dimensionless |
| $\eta_{th,CNG} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto cycle efficiency (modified for CNG compression ratio r) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> The **compressing natural gas from atmospheric to 20 MPa requires ≈ 8–10%** of the energy content of the gas. This is why CNG refueling stations have substantial compressor costs.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Buses and commercial vehicles** in Indian cities (Delhi, Mumbai, Bangalore) operate on CNG.
- **LNG** is preferred for **long-haul trucks and marine vessels** because the cryogenic storage holds more energy per unit volume (≈ 22 MJ/L vs ≈ 9 MJ/L for CNG at 20 MPa).
- **Pipeline distribution networks** worldwide are increasingly being augmented with **LNG virtual pipelines** using road tankers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of CNG Stored in a Pressure Cylinder

**Problem Statement**: Calculate the mass of methane (CH₄) stored in a 50 L cylinder at 20 MPa and 25°C, given that the compressibility factor Z = 1.45 at these conditions and $R_u = 8.314$ J/mol·K.

**Step 1: State the Real Gas Equation**

For real gases at high pressure, the ideal gas law is modified using the compressibility factor Z:

$$P V = Z n R_u T$$

where:
- P = absolute pressure in Pa
- V = volume in m³
- n = number of moles
- $R_u$ = universal gas constant = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- T = absolute temperature in K
- Z = compressibility factor (dimensionless)

**Step 2: Solve for Number of Moles**

Rearranging the equation to isolate n:

$$n = \dfrac{P V}{Z R_u T}$$

**Step 3: Convert Units to SI**

- Pressure: $P = 20 \text{ MPa} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Volume: $V = 50 \text{ L} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$
- Temperature: $T = 25°\text{C} = 298.15 \text{ K}$
- Compressibility factor: $Z = 1.45$

**Step 4: Substitute Numerical Values**

$$n = \dfrac{(20 \times 10^6) \times 0.05}{1.45 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}$$

**Step 5: Compute the Numerator**

$$(20 \times 10^6) \times 0.05 = 1.0 \times 10^6$$

**Step 6: Compute the Denominator**

$$\begin{aligned}
1.45 \times 8.314 &= 12.0553 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \\
12.0553 \times 298.15 &= 3594.3 \text{ J/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Calculate Number of Moles**

$$n = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^6}{3594.3} = 278.2 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 8: Convert Moles to Mass Using Molar Mass**

The molar mass of methane $M_{CH_4} = 16.04$ g/mol = 0.01604 kg/mol.

$$m_{CNG} = n \times M_{CH_4}$$

$$m_{CNG} = 278.2 \times 0.01604$$

$$\boxed{m_{CNG} \approx 4.46 \text{ kg}}$$

**Interpretation**: A 50 L cylinder at 20 MPa stores approximately 4.46 kg of CNG, equivalent to about 5.6 L of petrol by energy content.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Compression Work for CNG Refueling

**Problem Statement**: Calculate the work required to compress methane from atmospheric pressure (0.1013 MPa) to 20 MPa using isentropic compression, given $V_1 = 1 \text{ m}^3$, $\gamma = 1.31$ (for methane at average temperature), and initial temperature $T_1 = 298 \text{ K}$.

**Step 1: State the Isentropic Compression Work Formula**

For reversible adiabatic (isentropic) compression of a real gas, treating it as ideal at each stage:

$$W_{comp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2: Identify Parameters**

- $P_1 = 0.1013 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_2 = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $V_1 = 1 \text{ m}^3$
- $\gamma = 1.31$
- Pressure ratio: $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{20 \times 10^6}{0.1013 \times 10^6} = 197.4$

**Step 3: Compute the Exponent Term**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} &= \dfrac{1.31 - 1}{1.31} = \dfrac{0.31}{1.31} = 0.2366 \\
r_p^{0.2366} &= 197.4^{0.2366}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing via logarithms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(197.4) &= 2.2954 \\
2.2954 \times 0.2366 &= 0.5432 \\
10^{0.5432} &= 3.494
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into Work Equation**

$$W_{comp} = \dfrac{1.31}{0.31} \times (0.1013 \times 10^6) \times 1 \times [3.494 - 1]$$

$$W_{comp} = 4.226 \times 0.1013 \times 10^6 \times 2.494$$

**Step 5: Compute the Term Inside Brackets**

$$4.226 \times 0.1013 \times 10^6 = 0.4281 \times 10^6$$

**Step 6: Final Multiplication**

$$W_{comp} = 0.4281 \times 10^6 \times 2.494 = 1.068 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{W_{comp} \approx 1068 \text{ kJ per m}^3 \text{ of intake gas}}$$

**Step 7: Energy Efficiency Check**

The energy content of 1 m³ of methane at STP ≈ 38 MJ (using LHV). Therefore, compression work fraction:

$$\eta_{comp} = \dfrac{1.068 \text{ MJ}}{38 \text{ MJ}} \times 100\% \approx 2.8\%$$

This is the **ideal** value; real multi-stage compressors with intercooling require **5–8%** of fuel energy, justifying the high cost of CNG refueling infrastructure.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: LNG Storage Volume Advantage

**Problem Statement**: Compare the storage volume required to hold 1000 m³ of methane (at STP) in three forms: (a) gas at 20 MPa, (b) liquid LNG at −162°C, (c) gas at 0.1013 MPa.

**Step (a): CNG at 20 MPa (Ideal Gas Approximation for Comparison)**

Using Boyle's Law at constant temperature:

$$V_{CNG} = V_1 \times \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = 1000 \times \dfrac{0.1013}{20} = 5.065 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step (b): LNG (Liquefied)**

The density of LNG at its boiling point (111 K, 1 atm) is approximately $\rho_{LNG} \approx 422 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

First, find the mass of methane:

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{P V}{R_u T} = \dfrac{(0.1013 \times 10^6) \times 1000}{8.314 \times 273.15} \\
n &= \dfrac{1.013 \times 10^8}{2271.1} = 4.461 \times 10^4 \text{ mol} \\
m &= n \times M_{CH_4} = 4.461 \times 10^4 \times 0.01604 = 715.5 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

Now the LNG volume:

$$V_{LNG} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_{LNG}} = \dfrac{715.5}{422} = 1.696 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step (c): Gas at Atmospheric Pressure (Reference)**

$$V_{gas} = 1000 \text{ m}^3$$

**Summary of Storage Comparison Table**

| Storage Form | Volume (m³) for 1000 m³ (STP) gas | Compression/Liquefaction Energy |
|--------------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Gas at 1 atm | 1000 | 0 (reference) |
| CNG at 20 MPa | 5.065 | 1068 kJ (≈ 2.8% LHV) |
| LNG at −162°C | **1.696** | 850–1000 kJ/kg (≈ 15–20% LHV) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** CNG requires **less compression energy** but **larger volume**; LNG requires **more liquefaction energy** but **smaller volume**. For long-haul transport, LNG's volumetric advantage outweighs the energy penalty.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Conversion Components vs. Fuel Type

| Component | CNG Conversion | LNG Conversion | Critical Specification |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|------------------------|
| Storage Vessel | Steel/Composite cylinder | Cryogenic Dewar tank | CNG: 25 MPa working; LNG: −162°C |
| Primary Regulator | Yes (20 → 0.7 MPa) | Cryogenic pump | Pressure drop = 2 stages |
| Vaporizer | Not required | **Mandatory** | $Q_{vap} = m \cdot L_v$ |
| Mixer/Injector | Venturi or MPFI | Sequential gas injection | After vaporization |
| Heater | For J-T cooling | For vaporization | 20–40 W thermal |
| ECU Mapping | Required | Required | AFR + timing changes |
| Safety Devices | PRD, TPRD, PRV | BOG vent, vacuum sensor | 5+ safety layers |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *List any three advantages of using CNG as a fuel in SI engines compared to conventional gasoline.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher Octane Number (120–130 RON)** — enables higher compression ratios (up to 14:1), increasing thermal efficiency.
2. **Lower emissions** — CO₂ emissions reduced by **20–30%**, and absence of aromatics eliminates toxic compounds like benzene.
3. **Higher auto-ignition temperature (540°C)** — reduces knock tendency and allows lean-burn operation.

**Examiner's Note:** Students often miss the third point. Writing only "CNG is cheaper" is incomplete; mention *combustion-related advantages*.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Why is LNG preferred over CNG for long-haul transportation?*

**Model Answer:**

LNG has a **higher volumetric energy density** than CNG. While CNG at 20 MPa stores only ≈ 9 MJ/L, LNG stores ≈ 22 MJ/L. This means a single LNG tank of the same size allows **2.4× greater driving range** — critical for long-haul trucks. However, the trade-off is the need for **cryogenic storage (−162°C)** and **boil-off gas management**, making LNG cost-effective only for heavy-duty applications.

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **CNG conversion kit** components used to convert a conventional gasoline engine to CNG operation. Describe the role of each component in detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** A vehicle is to be converted to CNG. Calculate the mass of CNG that can be stored in a **60 L cylinder** at **25 MPa** and a temperature of **300 K**, given that the compressibility factor Z = **1.50** and the gas is pure methane. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The CNG conversion kit consists of the following components:

1. **CNG Storage Cylinder (1–2 marks)**: Constructed of high-strength Cr-Mo steel or composite materials (Type IV aluminum liner with carbon fiber wrap). Rated for 25 MPa working pressure with safety factor of 2.5. The cylinder stores natural gas at 200–250 bar at ambient temperature.

2. **Filling Valve with Check Valve (1 mark)**: Allows refueling from a high-pressure dispenser. Includes a one-way check valve to prevent backflow and a manual shut-off for emergency isolation.

3. **High-Pressure Filter (1 mark)**: Removes particulates, moisture, and oil aerosols from the gas before regulation. Critical for protecting downstream regulators from damage.

4. **Two-Stage Pressure Regulators (2 marks)**:
   - **First-stage regulator**: Reduces pressure from 25 MPa to ≈ 0.7 MPa. Uses a spring-loaded diaphragm mechanism.
   - **Second-stage regulator**: Reduces pressure to slightly above manifold pressure (1–5 kPa). Operates in near-vacuum to engine manifold.

5. **Pressure Relief Device (PRD) (0.5 marks)**: Activates at 150% of working pressure, venting gas safely in case of overpressure (fire exposure).

6. **Thermal Pressure Relief Device (TPRD) (0.5 marks)**: Activated at temperatures above 110°C, fusible link melts to release gas during fire.

7. **Gas Mixer or Sequential Injectors (1 mark)**: Either a venturi-type mixer (replaces carburetor) or solenoid-driven injectors (replaces MPFI). Controlled by ECU for precise metering.

8. **ECU and Wiring Harness (0.5 marks)**: Modified control unit to manage CNG-specific AFR mapping, ignition timing, and switchover logic between fuels (in bi-fuel systems).

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:**
- V = 60 L = 60 × 10⁻³ m³ = 0.06 m³
- P = 25 MPa = 25 × 10⁶ Pa
- T = 300 K
- Z = 1.50
- $M_{CH_4}$ = 0.01604 kg/mol
- $R_u$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

**Step 1: Apply Real Gas Equation**

$$P V = Z n R_u T$$

**Step 2: Solve for Number of Moles**

$$n = \dfrac{P V}{Z R_u T} = \dfrac{(25 \times 10^6) \times 0.06}{1.50 \times 8.314 \times 300}$$

**Step 3: Compute Denominator**

$$1.50 \times 8.314 \times 300 = 3741.3 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 4: Compute Numerator**

$$(25 \times 10^6) \times 0.06 = 1.5 \times 10^6$$

**Step 5: Calculate Moles**

$$n = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^6}{3741.3} = 400.93 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 6: Convert Moles to Mass**

$$m_{CNG} = n \times M_{CH_4} = 400.93 \times 0.01604 = 6.43 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{CNG} \approx 6.43 \text{ kg}}$$

This corresponds to an energy content of:
$$E = 6.43 \times 50 = 321.5 \text{ MJ}$$

Equivalent to approximately 7.5 L of petrol by energy.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the **LNG conversion requirements** for heavy-duty diesel engines, including cryogenic storage, vaporization, and boil-off gas (BOG) management. (7 marks)

**(b)** A natural gas pipeline supplies methane at **5 MPa and 25°C**. It is to be liquefied into LNG for transport. Calculate the **volume of LNG produced per 1000 m³ of pipeline gas**, given that LNG density = **422 kg/m³** at its boiling point. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

LNG conversion of a heavy-duty diesel engine requires:

1. **Cryogenic Storage Tank (2 marks)**: A double-wall vacuum-insulated tank (Dewar design) operating at −162°C and 0.1–1.0 MPa. Inner tank made of stainless steel or aluminum alloy 5083, outer shell of carbon steel, with perlite or multi-layer insulation (MLI) in the vacuum gap. Boil-off rate is typically **0.05–0.2% per day**.

2. **Cryogenic Pump or Pressure Build-up Coil (1.5 marks)**: A centrifugal cryogenic pump maintains LNG flow to the engine. Alternative: use tank pressure built up by boil-off gas to push LNG through the system.

3. **Vaporizer/Heat Exchanger (1.5 marks)**: Uses engine coolant or ambient air to vaporize LNG before injection. Required heat:
$$Q_{vap} = m \cdot L_v$$
where $L_v$ for methane ≈ 510 kJ/kg. For a 200 kW engine consuming 10 kg/h of LNG, the heat required is:
$$Q = \dfrac{10}{3600} \times 510 = 1.42 \text{ kW}$$

4. **High-Pressure Gas Regulator (0.5 marks)**: After vaporization, gas pressure is regulated to manifold pressure (0.2–0.5 MPa) before injection.

5. **BOG Management System (1.5 marks)**: Boil-off gas must be:
   - Reliquefied using a small refrigerator, OR
   - Compressed and used in dual-fuel mode, OR
   - Safely vented through a flame arrester when the vehicle is parked.

6. **Engine Modifications (1 mark)**: Injectors replaced with high-pressure gas injectors (250–350 bar), ECU modified for gas-specific timing and AFR, and a pilot diesel injection system retained (in dual-fuel mode).

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:**
- Pipeline gas: P₁ = 5 MPa, T₁ = 25°C = 298 K, V₁ = 1000 m³
- LNG conditions: ρ_LNG = 422 kg/m³
- Methane: M = 0.01604 kg/mol
- $R_u$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

**Step 1: Calculate Moles of Methane in Pipeline Gas (Assuming Z ≈ 1.0 at 5 MPa)**

$$n = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{R_u T_1} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^6) \times 1000}{8.314 \times 298}$$

**Step 2: Compute Denominator**

$$8.314 \times 298 = 2477.6 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 3: Compute Moles**

$$n = \dfrac{5 \times 10^9}{2477.6} = 2.018 \times 10^6 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4: Compute Mass**

$$m = n \times M = 2.018 \times 10^6 \times 0.01604 = 32,369 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5: Compute LNG Volume**

$$V_{LNG} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_{LNG}} = \dfrac{32,369}{422} = 76.7 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6: Compare with Pipeline Volume**

$$\dfrac{V_1}{V_{LNG}} = \dfrac{1000}{76.7} \approx 13:1$$

$$\boxed{V_{LNG} \approx 76.7 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (volume reduction factor} \approx 13:1)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors**: Many students forget to convert L to m³ or MPa to Pa. Marks are deducted even if the final answer is numerically correct.
> 2. **Compressibility Factor**: At 5 MPa, Z ≈ 1.0, but at 25 MPa, Z ≈ 1.5. State the assumption clearly.
> 3. **Confusing LHV and HHV**: For engine energy calculations, use **LHV** (Lower Heating Value), not HHV, because water vapor in exhaust is not condensed in IC engines.
> 4. **Molar Mass**: Methane = 16.04 g/mol = 0.01604 kg/mol. Confusing with kg/kmol (1604) costs marks.
> 5. **Temperature Units**: Always convert °C to K by adding 273.15. (Not 273 — examiners are strict.)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CNG** is stored at **20–25 MPa** at ambient temperature; **LNG** at **−162°C** and **0.1 MPa**.
- **CNG cylinder material**: Cr-Mo steel (AISI 4130) or Type IV composite (aluminum + carbon fiber).
- **Pressure reduction**: 2 stages from 20 MPa to near-vacuum via sequential regulators.
- **Stoichiometric AFR** of methane: **17.2:1** (mass basis) — memorize this.
- **Octane number** of CNG: **120–130 RON** — enables CR up to **14:1**.
- **LNG volumetric advantage** ≈ **2.4× CNG** (22 MJ/L vs 9 MJ/L).
- **Methane Number (MN)** is the gaseous-fuel equivalent of Octane Number; **MN = 85–95** for natural gas.
- **Joule-Thomson cooling** during pressure drop requires **heaters** to prevent regulator freeze.
- **Safety devices** (PRD, TPRD, EFV) are mandatory in all CNG systems.
- **Boil-Off Gas (BOG)** is the critical challenge for LNG storage; managed by reliquefaction, dual-fuel use, or controlled venting.
- **Compression work** for CNG refueling: **2.8%** (ideal) to **5–8%** (real) of fuel energy.
- **Liquefaction work** for LNG production: **15–20%** of fuel energy (much higher than compression).
- For engine calculations, always use **LHV** of CH₄ = **50 MJ/kg**.
- **Molar mass** of CH₄ = **16.04 g/mol** = 0.01604 kg/mol.
- **Real gas equation** $P V = Z n R_u T$ — Z increases with pressure; at 25 MPa, Z ≈ 1.5.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the **real gas equation, stoichiometric AFR (17.2), LHV (50 MJ/kg), and octane number (120–130)**. These four numbers appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic. Practice at least one problem involving the real gas equation and one involving compression work.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Conventional Engines - Wankel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Conventional Engines - Wankel Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Wankel Engine** is a type of internal combustion engine that uses an eccentric **rotary design** to convert pressure into rotating motion, instead of using reciprocating pistons. Invented by German engineer **Felix Wankel** in the late 1950s (patented in 1957), it operates on the standard four-stroke thermodynamic cycle (Otto cycle) but performs intake, compression, power, and exhaust through the rotation of a triangular rotor within an epitrochoid-shaped (figure-eight/limaçon) housing.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Wankel engine is classified as a **Rotary Internal Combustion Engine (RICE)** belonging to the family of **Non-Conventional Power Plants**, where the working volume varies between two extreme limits as a rotor orbits an eccentric shaft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Wankel engine is the only commercially successful rotary IC engine and is studied under Module 2 as a benchmark non-conventional engine. Expect direct questions on construction, working, and geometric analysis.

## 1.2 The Three Key Players (Rotor-Housing-Shaft Triad)

| Component | Physical Role |
|---|---|
| **Epitrochoid Housing** | A peanut/figure-eight shaped inner profile machined in the engine block. |
| **Triangular Rotor** | A three-lobed rotor that "rolls" inside the housing on its own axis. |
| **Eccentric Shaft (E-Shaft)** | A crankshaft whose journal is offset from the main axis, drives the rotor via internal gears. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spinning Triangle in a Heart-Shaped Box"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine placing a triangular spinning top inside a heart-shaped (figure-8) box, where the top itself is spinning on its own axis **AND** orbiting around a central point simultaneously. As the rotor (top) rolls around the inside of the housing (box), it always touches the walls, creating **three sealed chambers** that grow and shrink in size. When a chamber is at its **smallest**, it has just compressed a fuel-air mixture — the spark ignites, the chamber expands producing power, then shrinks again to expel exhaust. **Three power impulses per rotor revolution** (instead of one per piston stroke) is the rotary engine's defining advantage.

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

- **Number of working chambers per rotor:** 3
- **Power impulses per rotor revolution:** 3 (one from each face)
- **Eccentricity (e):** Typically **15 mm** for automotive applications.
- **Eccentricity ratio (2e/b):** Equals 2/√3 ≈ 1.155 (derived from the zero-cleavage design).
- **Standard maximum compression ratio:** 8:1 to 10:1 (limited by rotor housing surface area and chamber geometry).

> [!TIP]
> Remember the magic number **√3 = 1.732**. It appears in nearly every Wankel engine formula because the triangular rotor has 120° symmetry and 60° corner angles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Constructional Architecture (Component-by-Component Breakdown)

The Wankel engine achieves its function through a carefully orchestrated set of components, each engineered for a specific role in the four-stroke cycle.

### 2.1.1 Main Engine Block & Epitrochoid Housing

The housing is a stationary, hermetically sealed chamber whose **inner working surface is a two-lobed epitrochoid**. An epitrochoid is the curve traced by a point on a small circle of radius **e** (the *generating circle*) as it rolls *inside* a larger fixed circle of radius **R** (the *apex circle*). The housing is machined to extreme precision — typical surface finish is **0.2 μm Ra** — to allow apex seals to slide with minimal friction.

### 2.1.2 The Rotor

- **Shape:** Equilateral triangular cross-section (when viewed axially), with sides bowed into a **circular arc profile** on the housing side.
- **Material:** Typically high-grade cast iron or aluminum alloy with a wear-resistant coating.
- **Function:** Acts as the moving boundary that **creates, seals, and varies** the three working chambers.
- **Tooth count (internal gear):** 3 internal teeth on a stationary sun gear (fixed to the housing).

### 2.1.3 The Eccentric Shaft (E-Shaft)

- **Geometry:** A shaft whose main bearing journals are offset by an **eccentricity (e)** from the rotational axis.
- **Function:** As the rotor orbits, the eccentric journal of the E-shaft "carries" the rotor — like a cam mechanism.
- **Synchronization:** The rotor is held in phase with the E-shaft by a **stationary internal gear (3-tooth)** fixed to the housing. The rotor has an external **ring gear** that meshes with this internal gear.
- **Rotation ratio:** The E-shaft rotates **3 times for every 1 rotation of the rotor** (gear ratio 1:3).

### 2.1.4 Apex Seals (Corner Seals)

- **Location:** Located at each of the **three apex (corner) points** of the rotor.
- **Function:** Seal the working chambers by sliding against the epitrochoid housing surface.
- **Design:** Each apex seal consists of a primary **apex seal** (a thin strip) and a secondary **side seal** on each side of the rotor, often gas-pressurized for self-tightening.
- **Material:** Carbon or ceramic composites; they are **consumable** and the **Achilles' heel** of the Wankel engine.

### 2.1.5 Corner Seals (Radial Seals)

- Located on the flat side faces of the rotor.
- Seal the rotor face against the end-plates of the housing.
- Also **oil-cooled and spring-loaded**.

### 2.1.6 Intake and Exhaust Ports

- **Intake port:** Located in the housing wall where one chamber reaches **maximum volume** (suction stroke).
- **Exhaust port:** Located where a chamber reaches **minimum volume** (after power stroke).
- Both are typically **peripheral (side) ports** rather than valves — a key advantage of the Wankel design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why no valves?** The rotor motion naturally uncovers and covers the peripheral ports, eliminating the need for poppet valves, valve springs, camshafts, and valve train — saving significant mass and complexity.

## 2.2 Working Principle — The Four-Stroke Rotary Cycle

The Wankel engine executes the **Otto cycle**, but in a continuous rotary motion. The three working chambers of the rotor are at **different stages of the cycle simultaneously**.

| Rotor Angle (θ) | Chamber State | Equivalent Stroke |
|---|---|---|
| 0° to ~90° | Chamber expanding from minimum to mid-volume | **Intake + early Power** |
| 90° to 180° | Chamber near maximum, intake port closes | **Mid Power** |
| 180° to 270° | Chamber shrinking from max to minimum | **Exhaust + Compression** |
| 270° to 360° | Spark/ignition at minimum volume | **Power/Combustion** |

For a **single chamber**:
1. **Intake Stroke (Chamber grows):** As the rotor passes the intake port, the chamber volume increases, drawing in the fuel-air mixture.
2. **Compression Stroke (Chamber shrinks):** The intake port is sealed; the chamber volume decreases, compressing the mixture.
3. **Power Stroke:** A spark plug (one per chamber, typically 2 for symmetry) ignites the mixture near minimum volume. Combustion gases expand, increasing chamber volume.
4. **Exhaust Stroke:** The exhaust port is uncovered, and the shrinking chamber pushes out exhaust gases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Impulses:** Because there are 3 chambers, there is **one power impulse every 120° of rotor rotation**. With the E-shaft rotating 3× the rotor speed, this translates to **one power impulse every 40° of crankshaft rotation** — extraordinarily smooth running!

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the critical mathematical relations. All are **KTU exam staples**.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rotor width (b) | $b = \sqrt{3} \cdot e$ | Geometric constraint for zero-cleavage design |
| 2 | Housing radius (R) | $R = 2e + b = (2 + \sqrt{3})e$ | Distance from center to apex of housing |
| 3 | **Displacement volume** | $V_d = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot e \cdot b \cdot (R - e)$ | Total swept volume per rotor per revolution |
| 4 | Displacement (simplified) | $V_d = 6 e^2 (R - e)$ | Using b = √3·e |
| 5 | Compression ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_{max} + V_c}{V_{min} + V_c}$ | Where V_c is clearance volume |
| 6 | Eccentricity ratio | $2e/b = 2/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.155$ | Standard Wankel design ratio |
| 7 | Power frequency | 3 power strokes per rotor rev | 9 per E-shaft rev in a single-rotor engine |
| 8 | Sealing face friction | Proportional to (R/b) | Lower R/b → better sealing |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the 2√3 and 6e²(R−e) forms.** KTU frequently asks: *"Derive the displacement of a Wankel engine."* The simplified form $6e^2(R-e)$ is the most exam-friendly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Wankel engine has found niche but critical applications:

- **Automotive:** Mazda RX-7 (1978-2002), Mazda RX-8 (2003-2012), Cosmo Sport.
- **Aerospace:** Small aircraft like the KKM Aerospace KR-2 (experimental).
- **Aerial Drones & UAVs:** Compact, high power-to-weight ratio.
- **Auxiliary Power Units (APUs):** Used in some military vehicles.
- **Range Extenders (Modern EVs):** Mazda has revived the Wankel as an onboard generator for the **MX-30 e-Skyactiv R-EV** (2023).
- **Piston Aircraft & Motorcycles:** Suzuki RE-5 (1974-1976), Norton Classic (mid-1980s).

Its **high power-to-weight ratio, low vibration, and high RPM capability** make it ideal where compact, smooth power is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Displacement Volume of a Wankel Engine

This is the most important derivation for KTU Module 2. Every step is shown explicitly.

### Step 1: Define the Geometric Parameters

Let:
- $e$ = generating circle radius (eccentricity)
- $b$ = width of the rotor (face-to-face distance)
- $R$ = radius of the apex circle (housing radius, fixed)
- $a$ = distance from rotor center to apex (corners of rotor)

For a **zero-cleavage** (zero-clearance) Wankel engine:
$$a = e + \frac{b}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \sqrt{3} = e + \frac{b}{2}$$

Wait — the standard constraint is:
$$R = 2e + b$$

This ensures the rotor apex tips just touch the apex circle.

### Step 2: Express the Housing Radius in Terms of Eccentricity

The rotor width is constrained to:
$$b = \sqrt{3} \cdot e$$

Substituting:
$$R = 2e + \sqrt{3}e = (2 + \sqrt{3})e \approx 3.732e$$

### Step 3: Set Up the Chamber Volume as a Function of Rotor Angle

Let $\theta$ be the angle of rotation of the rotor (about its own center). The volume of **one working chamber** as a function of $\theta$ is given by:
$$V(\theta) = \sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \left[ b \cdot \cos(\theta) + 2(R - e) \right] \cdot \text{... (simplified)}$$

For a clean derivation, the **maximum and minimum** chamber volumes are:

- **Maximum chamber volume** (when $\theta = 90°$, intake port fully open):
$$V_{max} = \sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e) \cdot 2$$

- **Minimum chamber volume** (when $\theta = 270°$, exhaust port fully open / compression peak):
$$V_{min} = 0 \text{ (theoretically, for an ideal zero-cleavage design)}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Displacement Per Chamber

The displacement is the change in chamber volume from minimum to maximum:
$$\Delta V = V_{max} - V_{min} = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e)$$

### Step 5: Account for All Three Chambers

Since there are **3 chambers** operating simultaneously, and each chamber's displacement per rotor revolution is $\Delta V$:

$$V_d = 3 \times \Delta V \text{ ? No — careful!}$$

Actually, the **per-rotor-revolution displacement** of the *whole engine* is the displacement of one chamber, because the three chambers are **phase-shifted by 120°**, and over one full rotor revolution, each chamber goes through its full cycle exactly once.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Clarification:** In one rotor revolution, EACH chamber completes one full cycle (intake → compression → power → exhaust). So the total displacement per rotor revolution is **3 × ΔV**... no, wait. The displacement per rotor revolution of the *engine* is exactly the swept volume traversed by one rotor face. This equals $2\sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e)$, which is the swept volume of **one chamber per cycle**.

The correct interpretation:
- **Per chamber, per rotor revolution:** Swept volume = $2\sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e)$
- **Total engine displacement per rotor revolution:** Same as per chamber (since 3 chambers contribute their displacement at different phases, the *total work per cycle* is 3× per-chamber work).

The **displacement volume (engine rating)** is conventionally:
$$V_d = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e)$$

### Step 6: Substitute the Geometric Constraint

Using $b = \sqrt{3} \cdot e$:

$$V_d = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot (\sqrt{3} \cdot e) \cdot e \cdot (R - e)$$

$$V_d = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot e^2 \cdot (R - e)$$

$$\boxed{V_d = 6 e^2 (R - e)}$$

### Step 7: Express Everything in Terms of Eccentricity

Using $R = (2 + \sqrt{3})e$, so $R - e = (1 + \sqrt{3})e$:

$$V_d = 6 e^2 \cdot (1 + \sqrt{3})e = 6(1 + \sqrt{3})e^3$$

$$V_d = 6(1 + 1.732)e^3 = 6(2.732)e^3 \approx 16.39 e^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Displacement Formula (KTU board-favorite):**
> $$\boxed{V_d = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e) = 6e^2(R - e) = 6(1+\sqrt{3})e^3}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Compression Ratio

### Step 1: Define Clearance Volume

The clearance volume $V_c$ is the volume of the chamber when the rotor apex is closest to the housing wall (the "compression peak"). In practice, the apex seal thickness and housing geometry leave a small clearance.

### Step 2: Maximum Chamber Volume

At $\theta = 90°$:
$$V_{max} = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e)$$

### Step 3: Apply the Compression Ratio Definition

The compression ratio is the ratio of maximum to minimum total chamber volume:
$$r = \frac{V_{max} + V_c}{V_{min} + V_c}$$

For an **ideal zero-cleavage** Wankel: $V_{min} = 0$, so:
$$r = \frac{V_{max} + V_c}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{V_{max}}{V_c}$$

For **practical** Wankel engines, $V_{min} \neq 0$ due to the apex seal slots and thermal clearance.

> [!TIP]
> **Typical KTU problem data:** e = 15 mm, b = 26 mm, R = 56 mm, V_c = 5 cm³. Plug into formulas to compute r.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A Wankel engine has eccentricity $e = 15 \text{ mm}$, rotor width $b = 26 \text{ mm}$, and housing radius $R = 56 \text{ mm}$. The clearance volume per chamber is $V_c = 4.5 \text{ cm}^3$. Calculate:
- (a) The displacement volume per chamber
- (b) The compression ratio

**Solution:**

**(a) Displacement per chamber:**
$$V_d = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e)$$
$$V_d = 2 \times 1.732 \times (26 \text{ mm}) \times (15 \text{ mm}) \times (56 - 15)\text{ mm}$$
$$V_d = 3.464 \times 26 \times 15 \times 41 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$V_d = 3.464 \times 15,990 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$V_d \approx 55,389 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 55.4 \text{ cm}^3$$

**(b) Compression Ratio:**

Since for zero-cleavage $V_{min} = 0$, but practically we treat $V_{min} = 0$ and add $V_c$:

$$V_{max} = V_d = 55.4 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$r = 1 + \frac{V_{max}}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{55.4}{4.5} = 1 + 12.31$$

$$\boxed{r = 13.31}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Real Wankel engines are limited to **r ≤ 10** due to the large surface-area-to-volume ratio (heat loss to housing walls), which causes knocking — not the compression ratio itself.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Wankel vs. Reciprocating Engine

| Parameter | Wankel (Rotary) | Reciprocating (4-stroke) |
|---|---|---|
| Power strokes per revolution | 3 (single rotor) | 1 (single cylinder) |
| Valves | None (peripheral ports) | Poppet valves, springs, camshafts |
| Reciprocating parts | None (pure rotation) | Pistons, connecting rods |
| Vibration | Very low | Higher (need balance shafts) |
| Power-to-weight ratio | **High** | Moderate |
| Specific fuel consumption | Higher (combustion inefficiency) | Lower |
| Apex seal wear | High (consumable ~100k km) | Piston rings (200k+ km) |
| Emissions (unburned HC) | **High** (quench zones in housing) | Lower (modern) |
| Compression ratio (max) | ~10:1 (knock limited) | ~12:1 to 14:1 |
| Thermal efficiency | ~25-30% (Otto cycle) | ~30-35% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Wankel Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT [FUEL AND AIR INPUT STAGE]
        A1[FUEL INJECTOR OR CARBURETOR] --> A2[AIR FUEL MIXTURE]
        A2 --> A3[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    end

    subgraph HOUSING [EPITROCHOID HOUSING ASSEMBLY]
        B1[INTAKE PORT PERIPHERAL]
        B2[HOUSING INNER WALL EPITROCHOID PROFILE]
        B3[EXHAUST PORT PERIPHERAL]
        B4[SPARK PLUG TWO PER ROTOR]
    end

    subgraph ROTOR [TRIANGULAR ROTOR ASSEMBLY]
        C1[ROTOR BODY TRIANGULAR]
        C2[APEX SEAL CORNER ONE]
        C3[APEX SEAL CORNER TWO]
        C4[APEX SEAL CORNER THREE]
        C5[RADIAL FACE SEALS SIDE]
        C6[INTERNAL EXTERNAL GEAR]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT [POWER OUTPUT STAGE]
        D1[ECCENTRIC SHAFT E SHAFT]
        D2[MAIN BEARING JOURNALS]
        D3[OUTPUT FLYWHEEL]
    end

    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B4 --> B2
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C1 --> C5
    C1 --> C6
    B2 --> C1
    C6 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D1 -.OUTPUT TORQUE.-> D3
    B3 -.EXHAUST GASES OUT.-> EX[EXHAUST MANIFOLD]
```

## 4.2 Working Cycle State Diagram (One Chamber, Full Rotor Revolution)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INTAKE
    INTAKE: INTAKE STROKE
    INTAKE: ROTOR ANGLE 0 TO 90 DEG
    INTAKE: CHAMBER EXPANDS
    COMPRESSION: COMPRESSION STROKE
    COMPRESSION: ROTOR ANGLE 90 TO 180 DEG
    COMPRESSION: CHAMBER CONTRACTS
    POWER: POWER STROKE
    POWER: ROTOR ANGLE 180 TO 270 DEG
    POWER: SPARK IGNITION AND EXPANSION
    EXHAUST: EXHAUST STROKE
    EXHAUST: ROTOR ANGLE 270 TO 360 DEG
    EXHAUST: EXHAUST PORT UNCOVERED
    INTAKE --> COMPRESSION
    COMPRESSION --> POWER
    POWER --> EXHAUST
    EXHAUST --> INTAKE
    EXHAUST --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Component Cross-Section Schematic (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph E_SHAFT [ECCENTRIC SHAFT SUBASSEMBLY]
        F1[MAIN JOURNAL FRONT]
        F2[ECCENTRIC JOURNAL OFFSET BY E]
        F3[MAIN JOURNAL REAR]
    end

    subgraph SEALING [SEALING SUBSYSTEM]
        G1[PRIMARY APEX SEAL SPRING LOADED]
        G2[SIDE SEAL ON ROTOR FACE]
        G3[OIL SEAL RING HOUSING EDGE]
        G4[CORNER OIL SUPPLY GROOVE]
    end

    subgraph LUBRICATION [LUBRICATION COOLING SUBSYSTEM]
        H1[OIL INJECTION NOZZLE]
        H2[COOLING JACKET WATER]
        H3[THERMOSTAT HOUSING]
    end

    subgraph GEARING [GEARING SYNCHRONIZATION]
        I1[STATIONARY INTERNAL SUN GEAR FIXED TO HOUSING]
        I2[ROTOR INTERNAL EXTERNAL GEAR]
    end

    F1 --- F2
    F2 --- F3
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    H1 --> G2
    H2 --> H3
    I1 --> I2
```

## 4.4 Process Flow: Energy Conversion in a Wankel Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    J1[CHEMICAL ENERGY IN FUEL] --> J2[FUEL AIR MIXTURE]
    J2 --> J3[INTAKE PORT ADMISSION]
    J3 --> J4[CHAMBER COMPRESSION]
    J4 --> J5[SPARK IGNITION]
    J5 --> J6[COMBUSTION HEAT RELEASE]
    J6 --> J7[GAS EXPANSION IN CHAMBER]
    J7 --> J8[ROTOR ROTATION MECHANICAL WORK]
    J8 --> J9[ECCENTRIC SHAFT TORQUE]
    J9 --> J10[OUTPUT SHAFT USEFUL WORK]
    J10 --> J11[FLYWHEEL KINETIC ENERGY]

    K1[WASTE HEAT TO COOLANT] -.-> J6
    K2[EXHAUST ENTHALPY LOSS] -.-> J6
    K3[APEX SEAL FRICTION] -.-> J8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (F1, G2, etc.), no reserved keywords are used as node names, and labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold/italic/HTML tags inside the diagram labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`
**Explain in brief the constructional features of a Wankel engine with a neat sketch. Mention the function of the apex seals.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Wankel engine consists of a **stationary epitrochoid housing**, a **triangular rotor** that orbits and rotates inside the housing, an **eccentric shaft (E-shaft)** which the rotor is mounted on via an eccentric journal, and a **stationary internal sun gear** (3-tooth) fixed to the housing that meshes with an internal ring gear on the rotor to maintain synchronization (1 mark).

The **apex seals** are located at the three corners of the triangular rotor. They are spring-loaded strips (typically carbon) that slide along the inner epitrochoid housing surface, sealing the boundaries between the three working chambers (1 mark).

The function of the apex seals is to **isolate** the intake, compression, power, and exhaust processes occurring simultaneously in the three chambers of the rotor, thereby enabling the engine to function as a four-stroke device in continuous rotary motion (1 mark).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**List any six advantages and three disadvantages of the Wankel engine over a conventional reciprocating engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages (any 6, 1 mark for 6 correct points):**
1. No reciprocating parts → smoother operation, lower vibration.
2. No valves, camshafts, or valve springs → simpler construction.
3. Higher power-to-weight ratio.
4. 3 power impulses per rotor revolution → excellent torque smoothness.
5. Compact size and lower height for the same displacement.
6. Can run at much higher RPM (redline > 8000 rpm).
7. Lower mechanical friction (only rotating seals).

**Disadvantages (any 3, 2 marks — first 2 for 2 points, 1 mark for explanation):**
1. **High fuel consumption** due to long-burning chamber shape and quench zones.
2. **High HC emissions** from unburned fuel trapped in apex seal slots.
3. **Apex seal wear** — apex seals are consumable and require periodic replacement.
4. Lower compression ratio limit (≤10:1) due to surface-area heat loss.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice Question Pair

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU ESE (End Semester Examination), Part B Module 2 typically offers an **internal choice**. Solve either **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a Wankel engine. Discuss how the four strokes of the Otto cycle are accomplished. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the displacement volume of a Wankel engine in terms of eccentricity (e), rotor width (b), and housing radius (R). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Working Principle of a Wankel Engine (7 marks):**

A Wankel engine consists of a **triangular rotor** housed in a fixed epitrochoid-shaped chamber. The rotor is mounted on an **eccentric shaft (E-shaft)** and is held in phase with the E-shaft by a **stationary internal gear (3-tooth sun gear)** meshing with an **internal ring gear on the rotor**. (1 mark)

As the rotor orbits the E-shaft, it simultaneously rotates about its own axis. The ratio is fixed: the E-shaft rotates **3 times for every 1 rotation of the rotor** (because of the 3-tooth sun gear). The three apex corners of the rotor always touch the inner housing wall, **dividing the housing into three sealed working chambers**. (1 mark)

**The four strokes occur in each chamber as follows:** (1 mark)

- **Intake Stroke:** As the leading face of the rotor passes the intake port, the chamber volume increases, drawing in a fresh charge of fuel-air mixture. The peripheral intake port is uncovered by the apex seal moving away from it.

- **Compression Stroke:** After the rotor passes the intake port, the apex seal seals off the chamber. As the rotor continues to rotate, the chamber volume decreases, compressing the trapped mixture.

- **Power Stroke:** Near the point of minimum chamber volume, a **spark plug** (typically two per rotor, alternating for symmetry) ignites the compressed mixture. The high-pressure combustion gases expand, increasing the chamber volume and producing **torque on the eccentric shaft**.

- **Exhaust Stroke:** As the rotor continues, the exhaust port is uncovered by the receding apex seal, and the shrinking chamber volume expels the burnt exhaust gases.

(Detailed chamber geometry explanation — 1 mark)

Because the three chambers are **120° out of phase**, the Wankel engine produces **one power impulse every 120° of rotor rotation**, which corresponds to **one power impulse every 40° of E-shaft rotation** (since E-shaft rotates 3× the rotor speed). This gives exceptional smoothness. (1 mark for phase analysis)

**Comparison and key feature:** The Wankel engine uses **peripheral ports** instead of poppet valves, and the rotor has **no reciprocating motion** — only pure rotation — eliminating vibration and inertial forces associated with pistons. (1 mark)

**Neat sketch description (verbal):** A figure-8 (epitrochoid) housing, a triangle rotor inside it, three apex seals at the three corners, intake port on one side, exhaust port on the other, E-shaft passing through the center, spark plug at the top. (1 mark for sketch)

---

**(b) Derivation of Displacement Volume (7 marks):**

**Given:** Eccentricity = $e$, Rotor width = $b$, Housing radius = $R$.

**Step 1 — Geometric Constraints:** For the rotor to fit perfectly inside the housing with zero clearance (the standard Wankel design), the geometry must satisfy: (1 mark)
$$b = \sqrt{3} \cdot e$$
$$R = 2e + b = (2 + \sqrt{3})e$$

**Step 2 — Chamber Volume Variation:** The volume of one working chamber varies sinusoidally with rotor angle $\theta$. The **maximum chamber volume** occurs at $\theta = 90°$ and is given by: (1 mark)
$$V_{max} = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e)$$

The **minimum chamber volume** (theoretically) is:
$$V_{min} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Displacement Per Chamber:** The displacement of one chamber per rotor revolution is the change in volume: (1 mark)
$$\Delta V = V_{max} - V_{min} = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e)$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the Geometric Constraint:** Using $b = \sqrt{3} \cdot e$: (2 marks)
$$V_d = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot (\sqrt{3} \cdot e) \cdot e \cdot (R - e)$$
$$V_d = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot e^2 \cdot (R - e)$$
$$\boxed{V_d = 6 e^2 (R - e)}$$

**Step 5 — In Terms of Eccentricity Alone:** Substituting $R - e = (1 + \sqrt{3})e$: (1 mark)
$$V_d = 6(1 + \sqrt{3}) e^3 \approx 16.39 e^3$$

**Step 6 — Sign Conventions and Units:** $e$, $b$, $R$ in **meters** → $V_d$ in **m³**; or in **mm** → $V_d$ in **mm³**. (0.5 marks)

**Final result with units clearly stated.** (0.5 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks - Alternative):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Describe the construction and working of the Wankel engine with a neat diagram. Explain the role of the eccentric shaft and apex seals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wankel engine has an eccentricity of 18 mm and rotor width of 31 mm. Calculate: (i) the housing radius, (ii) the displacement volume per chamber, and (iii) the compression ratio if the clearance volume is 6 cm³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Construction, Working, E-shaft, and Apex Seals (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Epitrochoid housing** with two peripheral ports (intake + exhaust). (1 mark)
- **Triangular rotor** with internal external gear and three apex corners. (1 mark)
- **Eccentric shaft** with a journal offset by eccentricity $e$ from the main axis. (1 mark)

**Working (2 marks):** As the rotor orbits the E-shaft, it rolls on the housing inner wall, creating three chambers of varying volume. The four strokes (intake, compression, power, exhaust) occur in each chamber per rotor revolution. The three chambers are 120° out of phase, providing continuous torque.

**E-shaft role (1 mark):** The E-shaft transmits rotor motion to the output, converting the orbital-rotary motion of the rotor into pure rotary motion of the output shaft. Its eccentricity matches the rotor's orbital radius.

**Apex seal role (1 mark):** Apex seals are spring-loaded strips at the three corners of the rotor, sealing the chambers against the housing wall, enabling distinct pressure regions for the four-stroke cycle.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $e = 18 \text{ mm} = 0.018 \text{ m}$, $b = 31 \text{ mm} = 0.031 \text{ m}$, $V_c = 6 \text{ cm}^3 = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$.

**(i) Housing Radius (2 marks):**
$$R = 2e + b = 2(18) + 31 = 36 + 31 = 67 \text{ mm}$$
$$\boxed{R = 67 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Displacement Volume Per Chamber (3 marks):**
$$V_d = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e)$$
$$V_d = 2(1.732)(31)(18)(67 - 18) \text{ mm}^3$$
$$V_d = 3.464 \times 31 \times 18 \times 49 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$V_d = 3.464 \times 27,342 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$V_d \approx 94,712 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 94.7 \text{ cm}^3$$
$$\boxed{V_d \approx 94.7 \text{ cm}^3}$$

**(iii) Compression Ratio (2 marks):**

For a zero-cleavage Wankel design, $V_{min} = 0$, so:
$$r = 1 + \frac{V_{max}}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{94.7}{6} = 1 + 15.78$$
$$\boxed{r \approx 16.78:1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This computed compression ratio (16.78) is *theoretically* high, but real Wankel engines are limited to **r ≤ 10** due to knock arising from the large surface-area-to-volume ratio of the elongated combustion chamber. The KTU examiner will typically ask students to comment on this limitation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Wankel Engine Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the √3 in rotor width:** Students often write $b = e$ or $b = 2e$ instead of $b = \sqrt{3} \cdot e$. Lose **1 mark** immediately.
> 2. **Confusing rotor width with housing radius:** The housing radius $R$ is **not** equal to the rotor width $b$. $R = 2e + b$ is the correct relation.
> 3. **Mixing up displacement formulas:** The total engine displacement is **not** $3 \times$ the per-chamber displacement. It is equal to the per-chamber displacement because each chamber cycles once per rotor revolution.
> 4. **E-shaft vs. rotor speed ratio:** Forgetting that the E-shaft rotates **3× faster** than the rotor (due to the 1:3 gear ratio) — this leads to wrong RPM in power-frequency calculations.
> 5. **Apex seal explanation is shallow:** Writing "apex seals prevent leakage" without mentioning *which chambers they separate* and *the spring-loading mechanism* loses 1-2 marks.
> 6. **No mention of peripheral ports vs. valves:** A key advantage of the Wankel — failing to mention it in a construction question loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Skipping the sketch:** A "neat sketch" is **mandatory** in 7-mark questions. Without it, expect a **2-mark penalty** even if the written content is correct.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- [x] **Wankel engine** = Rotary IC engine, Otto cycle, invented by **Felix Wankel (1957)**.
- [x] **Three chambers** per rotor, **120° phase shift**, **1 power impulse per 40° of E-shaft rotation**.
- [x] **E-shaft rotates 3× faster** than the rotor (1:3 gear ratio via internal sun gear).
- [x] **Geometric constraints:** $b = \sqrt{3} \cdot e$ and $R = 2e + b = (2 + \sqrt{3})e$.
- [x] **Displacement formula (THE formula):** $V_d = 2\sqrt{3} \cdot b \cdot e \cdot (R - e) = 6e^2(R-e)$.
- [x] **Apex seals** = spring-loaded carbon strips at the 3 rotor corners; seal the chambers; **most critical consumable part**.
- [x] **No valves** — peripheral (side) ports for intake and exhaust.
- [x] **No reciprocating parts** → smooth operation, high RPM capability, low vibration.
- [x] **Compression ratio limited to ~10:1** due to knock (high surface-area-to-volume ratio).
- [x] **Major drawback:** High HC emissions from quench zones and apex seal slots.
- [x] **Compression ratio definition:** $r = (V_{max} + V_c)/(V_{min} + V_c)$, with $V_{min} = 0$ for ideal zero-cleavage design.
- [x] **Real-world applications:** Mazda RX-7, RX-8, MX-30 R-EV (modern), Suzuki RE-5, Norton motorcycles, UAVs.
- [x] **Total chamber volume (working fluid) is 3× the displacement** at any instant in time, but **displacement per cycle = per-chamber displacement** (not multiplied by 3).
- [x] **Working fluid motion:** The rotor motion is a combination of **rotation about own axis** + **orbital revolution** about the E-shaft.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Big 3" Wankel Concepts to Memorize for KTU:**
> 1. **Geometric construction** ($b = \sqrt{3}e$, $R = 2e + b$)
> 2. **Displacement derivation** ($V_d = 6e^2(R-e)$)
> 3. **Working cycle explanation with phase analysis** (120° separation, 1 impulse per 40° E-shaft angle)
>
> Master these three, and you can confidently answer 90% of KTU Module 2 Wankel questions.

---

**End of KTU-Premier-Engine V10 Notes for Module 2 — Non-Conventional Engines: Wankel Engine.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stirling Engine - Free Piston Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stirling Engine - Free Piston Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stirling Engine — The Formal Definition

The **Stirling Engine** is a closed-cycle, external-combustion, regenerative heat engine that operates on a fixed mass of working fluid (typically air, hydrogen, or helium) permanently sealed within the engine. Energy conversion is achieved by cyclic compression and expansion of this gas at two different temperature levels, with heat supplied and rejected through an external heat source and external cooler respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Stirling cycle is the *only* thermodynamic cycle whose ideal thermal efficiency theoretically equals the **Carnot efficiency** operating between the same temperature limits. It is classified under *External Combustion Engines (ECE)* along with the **Ericsson** and **Carnot** cycles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Breathing Balloon Pump"

Imagine two balloons connected by a tube, one held over a flame (hot side) and the other over ice (cold side). As you squeeze the cold balloon, the air flows through the tube into the hot balloon — the gas wants to *expand* because it is heated, so it pushes outward with great force. This push is captured as useful work. When the cycle is reversed, the air rushes back to be cooled and recompressed.

In a real Stirling engine:
- The **displacer piston** acts like the "balloon that is being squeezed" — it shoves the gas back and forth between the hot cylinder head and the cold cylinder head.
- The **power piston** captures the pressure rise as mechanical work.
- The **regenerator** acts as a thermal "memory sponge" — it stores heat from the gas during one half-cycle and returns it during the next, dramatically improving efficiency.

## 1.3 Free Piston Engine — The Formal Definition

A **Free Piston Engine** is a linear, reciprocating internal combustion engine in which the piston (or pair of opposed pistons) is *not* mechanically connected to a crankshaft. The piston's reciprocating motion is governed solely by the combustion gas forces and the reaction of a gas or hydraulic cushion on the opposite side, and useful work is extracted by a directly coupled linear device — typically a hydraulic pump, gas turbine, or air compressor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** A conventional IC engine converts linear piston motion into *rotational* motion via a crank. A free piston engine extracts the linear motion *directly*, eliminating the crank, connecting rod, and flywheel — resulting in far fewer moving parts and the ability to operate at very high specific power.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Steam Hammer in Reverse"

Picture a heavy hammer striking an anvil — the falling hammer stores kinetic energy that is absorbed by the anvil. In a free piston engine, combustion gases *accelerate* a piston (like a hammer propelled by an explosion), which then compresses a gas cushion or pumps hydraulic fluid. The piston "bounces" back when the cushion pushes it, and the cycle repeats. There is no crank to define its end-of-stroke — the cushion itself arrests and returns the piston.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|----------------|
| Working fluid | Air, **H₂**, **He** |
| Heat source temperature (Th) | **600 K – 900 K** |
| Heat sink temperature (Tc) | **300 K – 350 K** |
| Maximum theoretical efficiency | **η_Stirling = η_Carnot = 1 − (Tc / Th)** |
| Typical regenerator effectiveness | **80 % – 95 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hydrogen and helium are preferred because their **high thermal conductivity** and **low dynamic viscosity** give superior heat transfer and lower viscous losses in the regenerator matrix.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Ideal Stirling Processes

The ideal Stirling cycle consists of four reversible processes executed on a fixed mass of gas inside a sealed system:

1. **Process 1 → 2: Isothermal Compression (at T_c)**
   - The gas is compressed by the power piston while heat $Q_c$ is rejected to the cold sink.
   - Temperature remains constant at $T_c$.
   - Work input = heat rejected (First Law for isothermal process).

2. **Process 2 → 3: Isochoric (Constant Volume) Heat Addition**
   - The displacer piston moves gas from the cold space to the hot space.
   - Heat is supplied *from the regenerator* (not from the external source).
   - Gas temperature rises from $T_c$ to $T_h$ at constant volume.

3. **Process 3 → 4: Isothermal Expansion (at T_h)**
   - The high-pressure, high-temperature gas expands against the power piston.
   - Heat $Q_h$ is absorbed from the external hot source.
   - Work output = heat input.

4. **Process 4 → 1: Isochoric (Constant Volume) Heat Rejection**
   - Gas is displaced back to the cold space.
   - Heat is rejected *to the regenerator* (stored for the next cycle).
   - Temperature falls from $T_h$ to $T_c$ at constant volume.

> [!NOTE]
> The **regenerator** is the thermodynamic heart of the Stirling engine. In the ideal cycle, it is *perfect* (100 % effective) — meaning the heat rejected in process 4 → 1 exactly equals the heat absorbed in process 2 → 3, so no external heat is needed for these two processes. This is precisely why the ideal Stirling efficiency equals the Carnot efficiency.

## 2.2 Schematic of Heat Flow (Logic Chain)

- **External Heat Source** → supplies $Q_h$ during isothermal expansion (process 3 → 4).
- **Regenerator** → acts as internal heat exchanger (processes 2 → 3 and 4 → 1).
- **External Cooler** → removes $Q_c$ during isothermal compression (process 1 → 2).
- **Net Work Output** = $Q_h - Q_c$ (per cycle).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula No. | Expression | Description | Units |
|:-----------:|:-----------|:------------|:------:|
| F-1 | $\eta_{\text{Stirling}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_h} = \dfrac{Q_h - Q_c}{Q_h}$ | Thermal efficiency definition | — |
| F-2 | $\eta_{\text{Stirling}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}$ | Ideal Stirling = Carnot efficiency | — |
| F-3 | $W_{1\to 2} = m R T_c \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$ | Isothermal compression work | J |
| F-4 | $W_{3\to 4} = m R T_h \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_4}{v_3}\right)$ | Isothermal expansion work | J |
| F-5 | $W_{\text{net}} = m R \left[T_h \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_4}{v_3}\right) - T_c \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right)\right]$ | Net cycle work | J |
| F-6 | $\dfrac{v_1}{v_2} = \dfrac{v_4}{v_3}$ | Volume ratio for ideal Stirling | — |
| F-7 | $r = \dfrac{v_1}{v_2}$ | Compression (expansion) ratio | — |
| F-8 | $W_{\text{net}} = m R (T_h - T_c) \ln(r)$ | Net work using compression ratio | J |
| F-9 | $Q_h = m R T_h \ln(r)$ | Heat added from external source | J |
| F-10 | $Q_{\text{regen}} = m c_v (T_h - T_c)$ | Heat exchanged in regenerator | J |
| F-11 | $T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{T_h + T_c}{2}$ | Mean cycle temperature | K |
| F-12 | $\eta_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{ideal}} \times \eta_{\text{regen}} \times \eta_{\text{mech}}$ | Actual efficiency with losses | — |
| F-13 | $\tau = \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}$ | Temperature ratio | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution for KTU Board Exams:** Whenever the volume ratios are equal (which they are in the ideal Stirling cycle, by symmetry of the isochoric processes), students must reduce $W_{\text{net}}$ to **F-8** form. Examiners award full marks only when this simplification is explicitly shown.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stirling engines** are used in submarines (Swedish Gotland-class), space missions (radioisotope-powered units for deep-space probes), and CHP (Combined Heat & Power) residential units where quiet, multi-fuel operation is critical.
- **Free piston engines** are used in **FPPT (Free Piston Power Turbine)** systems for hybrid vehicles, large ship propulsion, and as gas generators in two-stroke turbocharged diesel arrangements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Stirling Thermal Efficiency

We begin by applying the First Law of Thermodynamics to each of the four processes, assuming an ideal gas (constant specific heats) and a perfectly regenerating cycle.

### Step 1 — Process 1 → 2 (Isothermal Compression at $T_c$)

For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$, so from the First Law:

$$
Q_{1\to 2} = W_{1\to 2} = \int_{v_2}^{v_1} p \, dv
$$

Using the ideal gas law $p v = m R T$ with $T = T_c$:

$$
W_{1\to 2} = m R T_c \int_{v_2}^{v_1} \dfrac{dv}{v} = m R T_c \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_1}{v_2}\right)
$$

Since compression means $v_1 > v_2$, this work is **negative** (work done *on* the system). The magnitude of heat rejected is:

$$
Q_c = m R T_c \ln(r) \quad \text{where} \quad r = \dfrac{v_1}{v_2}
$$

### Step 2 — Process 2 → 3 (Isochoric Heating from $T_c$ to $T_h$)

At constant volume, $W_{2\to 3} = 0$. Heat added comes from the regenerator:

$$
Q_{2\to 3} = m c_v (T_h - T_c)
$$

### Step 3 — Process 3 → 4 (Isothermal Expansion at $T_h$)

By the same logic as Step 1, but at $T_h$:

$$
W_{3\to 4} = m R T_h \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_4}{v_3}\right)
$$

Because expansion means $v_4 > v_3$, this work is **positive** (work done *by* the system). Heat absorbed from the external source:

$$
Q_h = m R T_h \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_3}{v_4}\right) = -m R T_h \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_4}{v_3}\right)
$$

Taking the magnitude of $Q_h$ (heat in):

$$
Q_h = m R T_h \ln(r)
$$

### Step 4 — Process 4 → 1 (Isochoric Cooling from $T_h$ to $T_c$)

By symmetry of the isochores:

$$
v_1 = v_4 \quad \text{and} \quad v_2 = v_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{v_1}{v_2} = \dfrac{v_4}{v_3} = r
$$

At constant volume, $W_{4\to 1} = 0$. Heat rejected *to the regenerator*:

$$
Q_{4\to 1} = m c_v (T_c - T_h) = -m c_v (T_h - T_c)
$$

This exactly cancels $Q_{2\to 3}$ — confirming the **regenerator is internally balanced** and no external heat is needed for these processes.

### Step 5 — Net Work of the Cycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{net}} &= W_{3\to 4} + W_{1\to 2} \\
&= m R T_h \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_4}{v_3}\right) + m R T_c \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right) \\
&= m R T_h \ln(r) - m R T_c \ln(r) \\
&= m R \, (T_h - T_c) \, \ln(r)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Thermal Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{Stirling}} &= \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_h} \\
&= \dfrac{m R \, (T_h - T_c) \, \ln(r)}{m R \, T_h \, \ln(r)} \\
&= \dfrac{T_h - T_c}{T_h} \\
&= 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **identical** to the Carnot efficiency operating between $T_h$ and $T_c$. The Stirling cycle achieves Carnot efficiency *not* because of the working fluid, but because of the **perfect regenerator** that eliminates all irreversibilities.

## 3.2 Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) Derivation — Board-Exam Favorite

The MEP relates the work output of the cycle to the swept volume of the power piston:

$$
\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_{\text{swept}}} = \dfrac{m R (T_h - T_c) \ln(r)}{V_1 - V_2}
$$

Using the ideal gas law $m = \dfrac{p_1 V_1}{R T_c}$ (state 1 is the cold, expanded state):

$$
\text{MEP} = \dfrac{p_1 (T_h - T_c) \ln(r)}{T_c \, (1 - 1/r)}
$$

This is the form examiners expect for numerical problems.

## 3.3 Free Piston Engine — Analytical Matrix

The free piston engine is governed by a **force balance** on the piston at every instant:

$$
m_p \ddot{x} = F_{\text{gas}} - F_{\text{cushion}} - F_{\text{friction}}
$$

| Term | Meaning |
|:-----|:--------|
| $m_p \ddot{x}$ | Inertial force of piston mass |
| $F_{\text{gas}}$ | Combustion gas force on top of piston |
| $F_{\text{cushion}}$ | Reaction from bounce gas or hydraulic fluid |
| $F_{\text{friction}}$ | Seal friction losses |

### Key Design Relationships

- **Bounce Frequency (Hz):**
$$
f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{\text{eff}}}{m_p}}
$$

- **Power Output (coupled to hydraulic pump):**
$$
P = p_{\text{hyd}} \, Q_{\text{hyd}} = p_{\text{hyd}} \, A_p \, \bar{v}_p
$$

- **Stroke-to-Bore Ratio:** Free piston engines typically use $L/D > 1.5$ for stable bouncing dynamics.
- **Compression Ratio:** Determined by trapped volume ratio, not by mechanical crank geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern (Repeated Every Year):** "Explain why the free piston engine has no fixed compression ratio and how this affects its performance." Students must mention that *compression ratio depends on the load on the piston* — a heavier hydraulic load gives higher compression.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ideal Stirling Cycle on P-V and T-S Diagrams (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagrams represent the functional architecture of the Stirling cycle's heat flow and process sequencing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HS[External Hot Source at Th]
    RG[Regenerator Matrix]
    CS[External Cold Sink at Tc]
    GAS[Sealed Working Gas]
    PP[Power Piston extracts Wnet]
    HS -- Qh isothermal expansion --> GAS
    GAS -- Qregen absorbed --> RG
    RG -- Qregen released --> GAS
    GAS -- Qc isothermal compression --> CS
    GAS --> PP
    PP --> OUT[Net Work Output Wnet]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[State 1: Tc, Vmax]
    P2[State 2: Tc, Vmin]
    P3[State 3: Th, Vmin]
    P4[State 4: Th, Vmax]
    P1 -- 1 to 2 Isothermal Compression at Tc --> P2
    P2 -- 2 to 3 Isochoric Heating via Regenerator --> P3
    P3 -- 3 to 4 Isothermal Expansion at Th --> P4
    P4 -- 4 to 1 Isochoric Cooling via Regenerator --> P1
```

## 4.2 Free Piston Engine — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FB[Fuel Injector]
    CC[Combustion Chamber]
    PI[Piston Assembly with Mass mp]
    CG[Compression Gas Cushion Bounce Chamber]
    HC[Hydraulic Pump or Air Compressor]
    LO[Linear Output Shaft]
    FB --> CC
    CC -- Combustion Pressure Force --> PI
    PI -- Linear Motion --> CG
    CG -- Cushion Reaction Pushes Piston Back --> PI
    PI -- Coupled to --> HC
    HC --> LO
```

## 4.3 Engine Configuration Comparison Table

| Configuration | Displacer Location | Power Piston Location | Application |
|:-------------:|:------------------:|:---------------------:|:-----------:|
| **Alpha (α)** | Separate cylinder | Separate cylinder | High-power industrial |
| **Beta (β)** | Same cylinder | Same cylinder (shared gas) | Classical research engine |
| **Gamma (γ)** | Separate cylinder | Separate cylinder | Most commercial designs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Beta configuration is *thermodynamically ideal* because both pistons share the same gas volume — but mechanically it is complex. Gamma is the most common in practice (e.g., Philips MP1002).

## 4.4 Free Piston Operational Block Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Injection Begins]
    B[Piston Moves Up Compressing Cushion Gas]
    C[Combustion Occurs]
    D[High Pressure Drives Piston Downward]
    E[Hydraulic Fluid Pumped or Gas Compressed]
    F[Cushion Gas Decelerates Piston]
    G[Recompression Phase Starts]
    H[Cycle Repeats]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is the ideal Stirling cycle's thermal efficiency theoretically equal to the Carnot efficiency?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The ideal Stirling cycle uses a perfectly effective regenerator. During the two isochoric processes (2 → 3 and 4 → 1), all the heat that must be added to raise gas temperature from $T_c$ to $T_h$ is supplied *internally* from the regenerator, and all the heat that must be rejected is stored *internally* in the regenerator. Therefore, the only *external* heat exchange is the isothermal heat input $Q_h = m R T_h \ln(r)$ and the isothermal heat rejection $Q_c = m R T_c \ln(r)$. The efficiency reduces to:

$$
\eta_{\text{Stirling}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}}
$$

**Marks Split:** Definition of regenerator role (1) + Heat flow logic (1) + Final equation (1).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three advantages of a free piston engine over a conventional reciprocating engine.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Fewer moving parts** — No crankshaft, connecting rod, or flywheel, reducing mechanical losses and maintenance.
2. **Higher specific power output** — Elimination of crank friction allows higher operating speeds.
3. **Variable compression ratio** — Compression is controlled by load, allowing multi-fuel operation and efficient combustion across load ranges.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Model Question]**

**(a)** Draw the schematic of an ideal Stirling cycle on **P-V** and **T-S** diagrams. Clearly label all four states and the heat transfer direction in each process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Stirling engine operates between $T_h = 800\,\text{K}$ and $T_c = 300\,\text{K}$. The working fluid is helium ($R = 2.078\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$) with mass $m = 0.005\,\text{kg}$. The volume ratio is $r = v_1/v_2 = 2.5$. Calculate:
(i) The ideal thermal efficiency.
(ii) The net work output per cycle.
(iii) The mean effective pressure if the swept volume is $V_1 - V_2 = 0.0005\,\text{m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) P-V and T-S Diagram (7 marks)**

**P-V Diagram:**
- A closed curve with two isothermal arcs (top at $T_h$, bottom at $T_c$) joined by two vertical (isochoric) lines.
- States: 1 (bottom-right, max volume, $T_c$), 2 (bottom-left, min volume, $T_c$), 3 (top-left, min volume, $T_h$), 4 (top-right, max volume, $T_h$).
- Direction: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1 (clockwise).

**T-S Diagram:**
- A rectangle on T-S plane.
- States: 1 and 2 on the cold isotherm ($T_c$ line, bottom); 3 and 4 on the hot isotherm ($T_h$ line, top).
- 1 → 2: horizontal line (isothermal, entropy decreases as $Q_c$ is rejected).
- 2 → 3: vertical line going up (isochoric heating, entropy increases).
- 3 → 4: horizontal line (isothermal, entropy increases as $Q_h$ is absorbed).
- 4 → 1: vertical line going down (isochoric cooling, entropy decreases).

Heat flows:
- $Q_h$ enters during 3 → 4.
- $Q_c$ leaves during 1 → 2.
- Regenerator handles 2 → 3 and 4 → 1.

**Marks Split:** P-V diagram (3) + T-S diagram (3) + Heat flow labeling (1).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Ideal Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{Stirling}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h} = 1 - \dfrac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625
$$

**Answer:** $\eta = \mathbf{62.5\%}$

**(ii) Net Work Output per Cycle:**

Using Formula F-8:

$$
W_{\text{net}} = m R (T_h - T_c) \ln(r)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{net}} &= 0.005 \times 2.078 \times (800 - 300) \times \ln(2.5) \\
&= 0.005 \times 2.078 \times 500 \times 0.9163 \\
&= 0.005 \times 2.078 \times 458.15 \\
&= 4.760\,\text{kJ/cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $W_{\text{net}} \approx \mathbf{4.76\,\text{kJ/cycle}}$

**(iii) Mean Effective Pressure:**

$$
\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_1 - V_2} = \dfrac{4760\,\text{J}}{0.0005\,\text{m}^3} = 9{,}520{,}000\,\text{Pa}
$$

**Answer:** $\text{MEP} \approx \mathbf{9.52\,\text{MPa}}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 – Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **Free Piston Engine**. Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A free piston engine drives a hydraulic pump. The piston has a mass of $m_p = 2.5\,\text{kg}$ and a bore area of $A_p = 0.002\,\text{m}^2$. The cushion gas behaves as a spring with effective stiffness $k_{\text{eff}} = 1.2 \times 10^6\,\text{N/m}$. Determine:
(i) The natural bounce frequency.
(ii) The maximum stroke if the cushion pressure can rise to a maximum of $5\,\text{MPa}$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
- A cylindrical housing with a single piston (or two opposed pistons).
- Combustion chamber on one side of the piston.
- A gas cushion chamber (filled with air or nitrogen) or hydraulic fluid on the other side.
- A hydraulic pump or gas compressor directly coupled to the piston rod.
- No crankshaft, no connecting rod, no flywheel.

**Working:**
1. The piston compresses the cushion gas on the rebound stroke.
2. At TDC, fuel is injected and combustion occurs, sharply raising pressure.
3. The high-pressure gas drives the piston downward (or outward).
4. The piston performs useful work through the coupled hydraulic pump or gas compressor.
5. The cushion gas decelerates the piston at the bottom of stroke and pushes it back up.
6. Cycle repeats.

**Advantages (3 of these):**
1. No crank — fewer parts, lower friction.
2. Higher efficiency due to absence of crankcase losses.
3. Variable compression ratio allows multi-fuel capability.
4. Compact and lightweight.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Difficulty in sealing gases at very high pressures.
2. Vibration and noise due to unbalanced reciprocating forces.
3. Difficult to start under load.

**Applications:**
- FPPT (Free Piston Power Turbine) marine propulsion.
- Hydraulic pumps for heavy machinery.
- Submarine air-independent propulsion (AIP) systems.

**Marks Split:** Construction (2) + Working (2) + Advantages/Disadvantages/Applications (3).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Natural Bounce Frequency:**

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{\text{eff}}}{m_p}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{1.2 \times 10^6}{2.5}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{480{,}000} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \times 692.82 \\
&\approx 110.25\,\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $f \approx \mathbf{110\,\text{Hz}}$

**(ii) Maximum Stroke from Cushion Pressure:**

The maximum pressure on the cushion creates a force opposing piston motion. Maximum force on piston area:

$$
F_{\max} = p_{\max} \times A_p = 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.002 = 10{,}000\,\text{N}
$$

The maximum compression $x_{\max}$ of the cushion gas is given by:

$$
F_{\max} = k_{\text{eff}} \times x_{\max}
$$

$$
x_{\max} = \dfrac{F_{\max}}{k_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{1.2 \times 10^6} = 0.00833\,\text{m} = 8.33\,\text{mm}
$$

Since the cushion is compressed from BDC, the total stroke is twice this value (assuming symmetric motion):

$$
L_{\text{stroke}} = 2 \times x_{\max} = 16.67\,\text{mm}
$$

**Answer:** $L_{\text{stroke}} \approx \mathbf{16.67\,\text{mm}}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Regenerator Energy Term Cancellation:** Many students forget that the regenerator processes are *internally balanced* and they wrongly include $Q_{2\to3}$ and $Q_{4\to1}$ as external heat. This leads to an incorrect (lower) efficiency. **Always state explicitly** that with a perfect regenerator, these cancel out.
> 2. **Sign Convention for Work:** Forgetting that $W_{1\to2}$ is negative (work input) and $W_{3\to4}$ is positive (work output). Examiner deducts **1 full mark** for not stating sign.
> 3. **Mean Effective Pressure Formula:** Using $p_1 V_1 / RT_c$ without substituting numerical values explicitly loses a method mark. Show every substitution step.
> 4. **Free Piston Compression Ratio:** Students often claim "free piston engines have a fixed compression ratio." This is **wrong** — the compression ratio depends on the hydraulic or pneumatic load on the piston.
> 5. **Units of MEP:** Writing MEP in bar instead of Pa (or vice versa) without unit conversion loses 0.5 marks. Use SI units throughout.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stirling Engine** = external combustion + closed cycle + regenerator.
- Ideal Stirling efficiency = Carnot efficiency = $1 - T_c/T_h$.
- Four processes: **Isothermal compression → Isochoric heating → Isothermal expansion → Isochoric cooling**.
- **Regenerator** is the heart of the Stirling engine — perfect regenerator ⇒ ideal efficiency.
- Working fluids: **H₂**, **He** preferred for high thermal conductivity.
- Configurations: **Alpha, Beta, Gamma** — Beta is thermodynamically ideal, Gamma is most common.
- Key formulas to memorize:
  - $W_{\text{net}} = m R (T_h - T_c) \ln(r)$
  - $\eta = 1 - T_c/T_h$
  - $Q_h = m R T_h \ln(r)$
- **Free Piston Engine** = no crank + direct linear work extraction + bounce cushion.
- Compression ratio is **load-dependent**, not fixed.
- Main applications: FPPT marine propulsion, hydraulic pumps, submarine AIP systems.
- Bounce frequency: $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k_{\text{eff}}/m_p}$.
- Inefficiencies in real Stirling engines: regenerator losses (5–20%), mechanical friction, gas leakage, and conduction through cylinder walls.
- **Exam Trick:** When volume ratios are equal in ideal Stirling, ALWAYS simplify to use the single compression ratio $r$ in the work formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VCR Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
