> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** VCR Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VCR (Variable Compression Ratio) Engine:** An internal combustion engine in which the **geometric compression ratio** can be **deliberately and continuously varied** during operation (or between test runs) without requiring a mechanical redesign of the basic engine block.

The compression ratio **r** of any IC engine is mathematically defined as:

$$r = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c}$$

where:
- **V_s** = Swept Volume (volume displaced by the piston between TDC and BDC)
- **V_c** = Clearance Volume (volume above the piston at TDC)

In a conventional engine, **V_s** and **V_c** are fixed by the geometry of the cylinder, piston, and combustion chamber, making **r** a constant. In a **VCR engine**, one (or both) of these volumes is made adjustable so that the operator can sweep through a range of compression ratios — typically **r ≈ 5 to 22** — depending on the engine.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Telescope Eyepiece" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a syringe with a movable plunger. If you push the plunger all the way down, you compress the air a lot (high r). If you stop the plunger slightly short, the trapped air has more room, so compression is less (low r). A **VCR engine is essentially a syringe whose plunger can be told "stop here" or "stop there" at will** — letting the engine engineer dial in exactly the squeeze ratio the fuel needs, the way a photographer changes eyepieces on a telescope to change magnification.

This is critically important because **every fuel has its own "sweet-spot" compression ratio**:
- Petrol (Gasoline) — knocks above **r ≈ 10:1**
- Diesel — needs very high compression for auto-ignition (**r ≈ 14:1 to 22:1**)
- CNG (Compressed Natural Gas) — tolerates higher CR (**r ≈ 12:1 to 14:1**)
- LPG — moderate (**r ≈ 10:1 to 12:1**)
- Methanol / Ethanol — high octane, tolerates **r ≈ 12:1 to 16:1**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Hook:** Whenever a question asks "Why VCR?" — the answer is always: **multi-fuel flexibility + optimum efficiency across the entire load-speed range.**

---

## 1.3 Classification of VCR Engines

VCR engines can be classified based on **which parameter is varied**:

| **Type** | **What is Changed** | **Typical Range of r** | **Commercial Use** |
|----------|---------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Clearance-Volume VCR** | V_c is varied (extra piston crown / spacer) | 5 : 1 to 20 : 1 | Research, Kirloskar lab engines |
| **Swept-Volume VCR** | Stroke length varied via eccentric crank | 8 : 1 to 18 : 1 | Prototype automotive |
| **Dual-Adjustable VCR** | Both V_s and V_c varied | Wide continuous range | Concept engines |

The **Kirloskar VCR Engine** (single-cylinder, water-cooled, multi-fuel, commonly used in KTU-affiliated engineering labs across Kerala) is the standard reference for university examinations.

### 1.4 Why a Variable Compression Ratio? — Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single fixed CR is always a **compromise**. The optimum CR differs for:
> 1. Different fuels (multi-fuel vehicles)
> 2. Different loads (part-load knock vs. full-load efficiency)
> 3. Different ambient conditions (cold start vs. warmed-up)
> 4. Different speeds (knock tendency rises with rpm in SI engines)

By varying **r** adaptively, the engine can always operate **near its peak efficiency point**, knocking is suppressed, and cold-start cranking torque is improved because the engine can start at low CR and progressively climb to high CR as combustion stabilizes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Mechanism of VCR — How is the Compression Ratio Actually Variated?

### 2.1.1 The Kirloskar "Spinning-Crown" Mechanism

This is the **single most-asked mechanism in KTU exams** for this topic.

**Operating Principle:**

1. The piston is built as a **two-part assembly**:
   - A **main piston body** (attached to the connecting rod via the gudgeon/wrist pin).
   - An **additional crown / disc** (sometimes called a "spacer ring" or "extra piston head") screwed onto the top of the main piston via a **trapezoidal spiral groove**.

2. The spiral groove is cut such that when the crown is **rotated relative to the main piston body**, it is forced to **move axially up or down** along the piston axis (screw-and-nut action).

3. **Rotating the crown one way** lifts it → clearance volume **V_c increases** → compression ratio **r decreases**.

4. **Rotating the crown the other way** lowers it → clearance volume **V_c decreases** → compression ratio **r increases**.

> [!NOTE]
> The crown rotation is achieved from outside the cylinder using a **lever/crankshaft** that engages with a rack-and-pinion or fork arrangement on the piston via the cylinder wall slot (only when piston is at a specific position near TDC — safety interlock).

**Geometric Relationship:**

$$\boxed{\,r = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c \pm \Delta V}\,}$$

where **ΔV** is the small extra volume added or removed by raising/lowering the crown. The **±** sign indicates the bidirectional adjustability.

### 2.1.2 Alternative VCR Mechanisms

| **Mechanism** | **How CR is Varied** | **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** |
|---------------|---------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Piston with Extra Crown** (Kirloskar) | Rotating crown moves it axially | Simple, reliable, lab-friendly | Discrete steps, requires external access |
| **Two-Piece Connecting Rod** | Upper rod length adjustable | Continuous variation | Mechanical complexity, vibration |
| **Eccentric Crank-pin** | Effective stroke changes → V_s changes | Truly continuous | Requires complex bearing design |
| **Hydraulic Lash Adjustment** | Hydraulic fluid in a chamber changes effective TDC | Fully automatic, modern | Requires high-pressure hydraulics |
| **Multi-Link Articulated Piston** | Articulation angle varies effective V_c | Used in modern Nissan VC-Turbo | Cost, NVH tuning difficulty |

### 2.1.3 The Nissan VC-Turbo (Modern Production VCR)

For contemporary relevance: Nissan's **2.0 L VC-Turbo (KR20DDET)** automotive engine (2018+) uses a **multi-link system** that continuously varies the stroke between **8.0 mm and 14.0 mm**, giving a compression ratio range of **8:1 to 14:1**. High CR is used for economy; low CR for turbo boost and knock resistance.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Beyond IC engines, the concept of "variable geometry" extends to **variable-geometry turbochargers**, **variable valve timing (VVT)**, and **variable-length intake manifolds** — all designed to keep the engine at its peak efficiency point.

---

## 2.2 Effects of Compression Ratio Variation on Engine Performance

### 2.2.1 Effect on Otto Cycle (SI Engine) Air-Standard Efficiency

The air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle is:

$$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

where **γ = C_p / C_v = 1.4** for air. Therefore:

$$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{0.4}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As r increases, η_otto increases monotonically.** So a VCR engine running at the highest tolerable CR always gives the best theoretical efficiency — but is constrained by **knock** in SI engines.

### 2.2.2 Effect on Diesel Cycle Efficiency

For the Diesel cycle:

$$\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)} \right]$$

where **ρ = V_1 / V_2 = cut-off ratio**.

$$\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{0.4}} \left[ \frac{\rho^{1.4} - 1}{1.4(\rho - 1)} \right]$$

### 2.2.3 Effect on Dual Cycle (Limited-Pressure / Mixed Cycle)

For the dual (Sabathe) cycle used in modern high-speed diesels:

$$\eta_{dual} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \frac{\beta \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(\beta - 1) + \gamma \beta (\rho - 1)} \right]$$

where **β = p_3 / p_2 = pressure ratio at constant-volume heat addition**.

### 2.2.4 Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) and Specific Work

The indicated mean effective pressure is:

$$P_{im} = \frac{W_{indicated}}{V_s}$$

For a VCR engine with constant **V_s**, varying **r** changes the **peak pressure** and **peak temperature** reached in the cycle. Higher r → higher P_3 → higher P_im → higher indicated work per cycle (until knock or thermal limits intervene).

### 2.2.5 Knock Tendency

For SI engines, knock occurs when end-gas auto-ignites ahead of the flame front. The empirical **K-factor (or "knock resistance index")** of a fuel is related to **r** as:

$$K \propto r^n \cdot p_1 \cdot T_1 \cdot e^{E / (R T_{end-gas})}$$

where **n ≈ 1.5 to 2** for octane-related parameters. **Lowering r reduces knock**, which is why high-boost turbo SI engines use low CR (≈ 8:1 to 10:1) at high load.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols follow IS/ISO 3046 engine-test nomenclature** — same as KTU examiner's expected notation.

| **#** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|-------|------------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Geometric Compression Ratio | $r = (V_s + V_c) / V_c$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Swept Volume | $V_s = (\pi / 4) D^2 L$ | m³ (D = bore, L = stroke) |
| 3 | Clearance Volume (variable) | $V_c = V_s / (r - 1)$ | m³ |
| 4 | Otto-cycle Efficiency | $\eta_o = 1 - r^{1 - \gamma}$ | $\gamma = 1.4$ for air |
| 5 | Diesel-cycle Efficiency | $\eta_d = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | $\rho = V_1 / V_2$ cut-off |
| 6 | Dual-cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{du} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\beta \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(\beta - 1) + \gamma \beta (\rho - 1)}$ | $\beta = p_3 / p_2$ |
| 7 | Indicated Power (4-stroke) | $IP = (P_{im} \, L \, A \, n \, k) / 60$ | kW (k = 1 for 2-stroke, ½ for 4-stroke) |
| 8 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = m_f / BP$ | kg/kWh |
| 9 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = BP / (m_f \cdot CV)$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ith} = IP / (m_f \cdot CV)$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = BP / IP$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = m_{actual} / m_{ideal}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Air-Fuel Ratio (stoichiometric) | $(A/F)_{st} = 14.7$ for petrol | — |
| 14 | Specific Gas Constant (air) | $R = 0.287$ | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 15 | Mean Effective Pressure Relation | $P_{im} = W / V_s$ | kPa or bar |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use the vertical pipe character `\|` inside any table row** — use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of VCR Engines

| **Domain** | **Application of VCR** |
|------------|------------------------|
| **Multi-fuel military vehicles** | Single engine accepts petrol, diesel, JP-8 jet fuel |
| **Hydrogen ICE prototypes** | Hydrogen needs r < 12:1 to prevent backfire/knock |
| **Alcohol-fueled engines** | Ethanol/Methanol: r can be 14–16:1 |
| **Hybrid powertrains** | Atkinson vs. Otto cycle on demand (Toyota Dynamic Force) |
| **Cold-start improvement** | Engine starts at low r, climbs to high r as warm-up completes |
| **Knock control at WOT** | Low r under boost, high r at part-load cruise |
| **Research / Academic Labs** | Plotting efficiency vs. r for student experiments |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Otto Cycle Efficiency with Variable Compression Ratio

We derive the air-standard efficiency of an Otto cycle operating between **T_1** (state 1, BDC start of compression) and **T_2** (state 2, TDC end of compression).

### Step 1 — Identify the processes

| **Process** | **State Transition** | **Thermodynamic Path** | **Heat Transfer** |
|-------------|----------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 → 2 | Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression | $pV^{\gamma} = \text{const}$ | $Q = 0$ |
| 2 → 3 | Constant-volume heat addition | $V = \text{const}$ | $Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$ |
| 3 → 4 | Isentropic expansion | $pV^{\gamma} = \text{const}$ | $Q = 0$ |
| 4 → 1 | Constant-volume heat rejection | $V = \text{const}$ | $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$ |

### Step 2 — Apply the isentropic relation for processes 1→2 and 3→4

For process 1 → 2 (isentropic compression):

$$T_2 \, V_2^{\gamma - 1} = T_1 \, V_1^{\gamma - 1}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

For process 3 → 4 (isentropic expansion):

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left( \frac{V_4}{V_3} \right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

### Step 3 — Define heat input and heat rejection

$$Q_{in} = m \, C_v \, (T_3 - T_2)$$

$$Q_{out} = m \, C_v \, (T_4 - T_1)$$

### Step 4 — Apply thermal efficiency definition

$$\eta = \frac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the isentropic temperature ratios

From Step 2:

$$T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$$
$$T_3 = T_4 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$$

Substituting in the numerator and denominator:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_4 \, r^{\gamma - 1} - T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{r^{\gamma - 1} (T_4 - T_1)}$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}\,}$$

For air, **γ = 1.4**, so:

$$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{0.4}}$$

### Step 6 — Sample Numerical Evaluation for a VCR Engine

A VCR engine operates with **V_s = 480 cm³** and **V_c** adjustable from **30 cm³ to 80 cm³**.

| **Setting** | **V_c (cm³)** | **r** | **η_otto (%)** |
|-------------|---------------|-------|-----------------|
| Low CR (start/wide-open-throttle) | 80 | $1 + 480/80 = 7.0$ | $1 - 1/7^{0.4} = 1 - 1/2.178 = 54.08\%$ |
| Medium CR (cruise) | 50 | $1 + 480/50 = 10.6$ | $1 - 1/10.6^{0.4} = 1 - 1/2.738 = 63.48\%$ |
| High CR (part-load economy) | 30 | $1 + 480/30 = 17.0$ | $1 - 1/17^{0.4} = 1 - 1/3.317 = 69.85\%$ |

> [!TIP]
> Notice the efficiency gain from **54% to nearly 70%** purely by varying r — this is the **commercial justification** for VCR technology in modern luxury/hybrid vehicles.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Diesel Cycle Efficiency with Variable r and Cut-off ρ

### Step 1 — Processes of the Diesel Cycle

| **Process** | **Description** | **Equation** |
|-------------|-----------------|--------------|
| 1 → 2 | Isentropic compression | $T_2 / T_1 = r^{\gamma - 1}$ |
| 2 → 3 | Constant-pressure heat addition | $T_3 / T_2 = \rho$ (cut-off ratio) |
| 3 → 4 | Isentropic expansion | $T_3 / T_4 = \rho^{\gamma} / r^{\gamma - 1}$ → $T_4 = T_3 (r/\rho)^{\gamma - 1}$ |
| 4 → 1 | Constant-volume heat rejection | $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$ |

### Step 2 — Heat Input

At constant pressure: $Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$

### Step 3 — Heat Rejection

At constant volume: $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$

### Step 4 — Build the efficiency expression

$$\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

### Step 5 — Express all temperatures in terms of T_1, r, ρ

- $T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$
- $T_3 = T_2 \, \rho = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \, \rho$
- $T_4 = T_3 (r / \rho)^{\gamma - 1} = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \, \rho \cdot (r / \rho)^{\gamma - 1} = T_1 \, r^{2(\gamma - 1)} \rho^{2 - \gamma}$

Wait — let me re-derive carefully.

$$T_4 = T_3 \left( \frac{V_3}{V_4} \right)^{\gamma - 1}$$

But $V_3 = \rho V_2$ and $V_4 = V_1 = r V_2$, so:

$$\frac{V_3}{V_4} = \frac{\rho V_2}{r V_2} = \frac{\rho}{r}$$

Therefore:

$$T_4 = T_3 \left( \frac{\rho}{r} \right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \, \rho \cdot \left( \frac{\rho}{r} \right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_1 \, \rho^{\gamma}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute back into efficiency

$$\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_1 \rho^{\gamma} - T_1}{T_1 r^{\gamma - 1} \rho - T_1 r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

$$\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_1 (\rho^{\gamma} - 1)}{T_1 r^{\gamma - 1} (\rho - 1)}$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — VCR Engine vs. Conventional Engine

| **Parameter** | **Conventional Engine** | **VCR Engine** |
|---------------|-------------------------|----------------|
| Compression ratio | Fixed at design stage | Continuously variable (typically 6:1 to 18:1) |
| Multi-fuel capability | Limited to one fuel family | Excellent (petrol, diesel, CNG, LPG, alcohol, hydrogen blends) |
| Part-load efficiency | Lower (compromised CR) | Higher (optimum r at every load) |
| Knock resistance | Fixed by design fuel octane | Adaptive via r reduction |
| Cold-start | Difficult at high CR | Easy (start at low r, ramp up) |
| Mechanical complexity | Low | Moderate to high |
| Cost | Low | Higher (~10–15% premium historically) |
| Real-world examples | All mass-production cars | Nissan VC-Turbo, Toyota Dynamic Force, Kirloskar lab engines |

---

## 3.4 Problem-Solving Template for KTU Numerical Questions

**Given:** A VCR engine with bore **D = 87.5 mm**, stroke **L = 110 mm**, clearance volume can be set to **V_c1 = 25 cm³** or **V_c2 = 65 cm³**.

**Required:** (i) The two compression ratios; (ii) Air-standard Otto efficiency at each; (iii) Comment on which is suitable for petrol.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Swept volume:
$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0875)^2 (0.110) = 6.614 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3 = 661.4 \, cm^3$$

Step 2 — Compression ratio at V_c1 = 25 cm³:
$$r_1 = 1 + \frac{661.4}{25} = 1 + 26.456 = 27.456 \approx 27.5$$

Step 3 — Compression ratio at V_c2 = 65 cm³:
$$r_2 = 1 + \frac{661.4}{65} = 1 + 10.175 = 11.175 \approx 11.2$$

Step 4 — Otto efficiency at r₁:
$$\eta_1 = 1 - \frac{1}{27.5^{0.4}}$$

Computing $27.5^{0.4}$:
$$27.5^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 27.5} = e^{0.4 \times 3.314} = e^{1.3256} = 3.764$$

$$\eta_1 = 1 - \frac{1}{3.764} = 1 - 0.2657 = 73.43\%$$

Step 5 — Otto efficiency at r₂:
$$\eta_2 = 1 - \frac{1}{11.2^{0.4}}$$

Computing $11.2^{0.4}$:
$$11.2^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 11.2} = e^{0.4 \times 2.4159} = e^{0.9664} = 2.629$$

$$\eta_2 = 1 - \frac{1}{2.629} = 1 - 0.3804 = 61.96\%$$

Step 6 — Comment:
- $r_1 \approx 27.5$ is too high for petrol (knock limit ~10:1 to 12:1) → **suitable for diesel or alcohol**.
- $r_2 \approx 11.2$ is suitable for **petrol/gasoline** operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution Hint (KTU):** Numerical step-by-step gets **7 marks**; the conceptual comment on fuel-CR matching gets the **remaining 7 marks** — always write the comment explicitly.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Variable Compression Ratio (VCR) engine. Why is it preferred for multi-fuel operation?**

**Model Answer:**

A VCR engine is an internal combustion engine in which the **geometric compression ratio (r)** can be **deliberately varied** during operation by adjusting either the clearance volume (V_c) or the swept volume (V_s), without changing the basic engine block.

It is preferred for multi-fuel operation because **different fuels require different optimum compression ratios** for best efficiency and knock-free combustion:
- Petrol needs r ≈ 8–10
- Diesel needs r ≈ 14–22
- CNG needs r ≈ 12–14
- Alcohols need r ≈ 12–16

By varying r, a single VCR engine can run any of these fuels at its optimum point. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Mention any three methods of varying the compression ratio in a VCR engine.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Adjustable piston crown / extra disc** (Kirloskar type) — rotating a threaded crown disc on the piston changes the clearance volume.
2. **Eccentric crank-pin mechanism** — offsetting the crank-pin changes effective stroke length, varying swept volume.
3. **Multi-link articulated piston system** (e.g., Nissan VC-Turbo) — articulating joint changes effective stroke.
4. (Bonus) Hydraulic lash adjustment with movable piston deck. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a **Kirloskar-type VCR engine**. Show how compression ratio is varied. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An experimental VCR single-cylinder, 4-stroke, SI engine has a bore of **85 mm** and stroke of **100 mm**. The clearance volume can be set at **35 cm³** or **70 cm³**. Determine:
1. The swept volume.
2. The two possible compression ratios.
3. The air-standard Otto efficiency at each compression ratio (take γ = 1.4).
4. Comment on which compression ratio is more suitable for petrol (RON 91) operation. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Kirloskar VCR Engine — Working Principle (7 marks)

**Construction:**
- The piston is fabricated in **two parts** — a **main piston body** and an **additional crown disc**.
- The crown disc engages with the piston body via a **trapezoidal spiral groove thread** machined on the outer surface of the piston body.
- A **lever-and-rack mechanism** operated from outside the cylinder (via a slot in the cylinder wall, accessible only when the piston is at TDC) rotates the crown.

**Operation:**
1. When the lever is rotated clockwise, the spiral groove **lifts the crown disc upward**, away from the piston body, **increasing V_c** and **reducing r**.
2. When rotated counter-clockwise, the disc is **pulled down** toward the piston body, **reducing V_c** and **increasing r**.
3. Between trials, the crown is locked in position before starting the engine.

**Effect:**
- Compression ratio range typically 5:1 to 18:1.
- Same engine can be tested on petrol, diesel, CNG, LPG, kerosene, methanol, ethanol, etc.

**Sketch** — refer to **Section 4.2** Mermaid schematic for the equivalent functional diagram. **(7 marks — full marks require mechanism explanation + sketch + operating range + multi-fuel advantage)**

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Bore D = 85 mm = 0.085 m
- Stroke L = 100 mm = 0.100 m
- V_c1 = 35 cm³; V_c2 = 70 cm³
- 4-stroke SI engine; γ = 1.4

**Step 1 — Swept volume:**
$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.085)^2 (0.100)$$
$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.007225 \times 0.100$$
$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0007225 = 5.6746 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3 = 567.46 \, cm^3$$

**Step 2 — Compression ratio at V_c1 = 35 cm³:**
$$r_1 = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_{c1}} = 1 + \frac{567.46}{35} = 1 + 16.213 = 17.213 \approx 17.21$$

**Step 3 — Compression ratio at V_c2 = 70 cm³:**
$$r_2 = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_{c2}} = 1 + \frac{567.46}{70} = 1 + 8.107 = 9.107 \approx 9.11$$

**Step 4 — Air-standard Otto efficiency at r₁:**
$$\eta_1 = 1 - \frac{1}{r_1^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{17.21^{0.4}}$$

Computing $17.21^{0.4}$:
$$17.21^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 17.21} = e^{0.4 \times 2.846} = e^{1.1384} = 3.122$$

$$\eta_1 = 1 - \frac{1}{3.122} = 1 - 0.3203 = 0.6797 = 67.97\%$$

**Step 5 — Air-standard Otto efficiency at r₂:**
$$\eta_2 = 1 - \frac{1}{r_2^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{9.11^{0.4}}$$

Computing $9.11^{0.4}$:
$$9.11^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 9.11} = e^{0.4 \times 2.2097} = e^{0.8839} = 2.421$$

$$\eta_2 = 1 - \frac{1}{2.421} = 1 - 0.4131 = 0.5869 = 58.69\%$$

**Step 6 — Suitability for RON 91 petrol:**

Petrol with RON 91 has a knock-limited compression ratio of approximately **9:1 to 10:1**. Therefore:

- $r_1 = 17.21$ is **far too high** — would cause severe knock.
- $r_2 = 9.11$ is **suitable** for petrol RON 91.

**Conclusion:** The setting **V_c = 70 cm³** (giving r ≈ 9.11) is appropriate for petrol RON 91. **(7 marks)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** What are the **advantages and limitations of VCR engines**? Compare them with conventional fixed-CR engines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke VCR diesel engine has bore **95 mm**, stroke **110 mm**, and clearance volume **40 cm³** when set to maximum compression. If the engine operates on the dual cycle with cut-off ratio ρ = 1.6 and pressure ratio β = 1.4, compute:
1. The swept volume.
2. The compression ratio.
3. The air-standard dual-cycle efficiency (γ = 1.4). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) Advantages and Limitations of VCR Engines (7 marks)

**Advantages:**

| **#** | **Advantage** | **Engineering Impact** |
|-------|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Multi-fuel capability** | One engine, many fuels (petrol/diesel/CNG/LPG/alcohol/hydrogen) |
| 2 | **Optimum efficiency at every load** | Part-load BSFC drops significantly |
| 3 | **Knock suppression** in SI engines | Allows operation near knock limit safely |
| 4 | **Better cold start** | Starts at low r, ramps up as warm |
| 5 | **Reduced cyclic variation** | More stable combustion |
| 6 | **Lower emissions at part load** | Higher r → higher T → less CO/HC |
| 7 | **Useful for research** | Maps efficiency curves vs. r vs. fuel |

**Limitations:**

| **#** | **Limitation** | **Engineering Impact** |
|-------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical complexity** | Extra piston components, adjustment hardware |
| 2 | **Higher cost** | 10–15% premium historically |
| 3 | **NVH issues** | Variable geometry can introduce vibration |
| 4 | **Durability concerns** | Adjustable parts under high pressure/temperature |
| 5 | **Sealing difficulties** | Variable clearance volume harder to seal |
| 6 | **Slow response** | Manual VCR is slow; needs hydraulic actuation |
| 7 | **Limited commercialization** | Few mass-production vehicles use it (Nissan VC-Turbo, Infiniti Q50) |

**Comparison with conventional fixed-CR engines:**

| **Parameter** | **Conventional** | **VCR** |
|---------------|------------------|---------|
| Compression ratio | Fixed | Variable |
| Flexibility | Single fuel | Multi-fuel |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Efficiency curve | Single peak | Plateau over wide range |
| Cold start | Difficult at high CR | Easy (low r at start) |
| Knock margin | Limited | Wide (adaptive) |

#### (b) Numerical Solution — Dual Cycle (7 marks)

**Given:**
- D = 95 mm = 0.095 m
- L = 110 mm = 0.110 m
- V_c = 40 cm³ (at maximum compression)
- ρ = 1.6 (cut-off ratio)
- β = 1.4 (pressure ratio at constant-volume heat addition)
- γ = 1.4

**Step 1 — Swept volume:**
$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.095)^2 (0.110)$$
$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.009025 \times 0.110$$
$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.00099275$$
$$V_s = 7.7982 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3 = 779.82 \, cm^3$$

**Step 2 — Compression ratio:**
$$r = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{779.82}{40} = 1 + 19.4955 = 20.4955 \approx 20.5$$

**Step 3 — Dual-cycle efficiency:**

$$\eta_{dual} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\beta \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(\beta - 1) + \gamma \beta (\rho - 1)}$$

Compute each piece:
- $r^{\gamma - 1} = 20.5^{0.4}$
- $\rho^{\gamma} = 1.6^{1.4}$
- $\beta \rho^{\gamma} = 1.4 \times 1.6^{1.4}$
- $\beta - 1 = 1.4 - 1 = 0.4$
- $\gamma \beta (\rho - 1) = 1.4 \times 1.4 \times 0.6 = 1.176$

Step 3a — Compute $20.5^{0.4}$:
$$20.5^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 20.5} = e^{0.4 \times 3.0204} = e^{1.2082} = 3.348$$

Step 3b — Compute $1.6^{1.4}$:
$$1.6^{1.4} = e^{1.4 \ln 1.6} = e^{1.4 \times 0.4700} = e^{0.6580} = 1.931$$

Step 3c — Numerator term:
$$\beta \rho^{\gamma} - 1 = 1.4 \times 1.931 - 1 = 2.703 - 1 = 1.703$$

Step 3d — Denominator term:
$$(\beta - 1) + \gamma \beta (\rho - 1) = 0.4 + 1.176 = 1.576$$

Step 3e — Assemble:
$$\eta_{dual} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.348} \cdot \frac{1.703}{1.576}$$

$$\eta_{dual} = 1 - (0.2987) \cdot (1.0806)$$

$$\eta_{dual} = 1 - 0.3228 = 0.6772 = 67.72\%$$

**Final Answer:** Dual-cycle efficiency ≈ **67.72%** at r ≈ 20.5. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in VCR Questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing the sketch label** — Drawing the Kirloskar VCR piston without labelling the **spiral groove**, **crown disc**, and **direction of rotation** costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Units inconsistency** — Using cm³ for V_s but m³ for V_c (or mixing), losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting γ in Otto formula** — Writing $r^{\gamma}$ instead of $r^{\gamma - 1}$. The exponent MUST be **(γ − 1)**, not γ.
> 4. **Wrong cut-off definition** — ρ = V_3 / V_2 (expanding ratio during combustion), NOT V_2 / V_3.
> 5. **No comment after numerical** — KTU examiner rewards the **interpretive comment** ("which CR suits which fuel") for 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up β and ρ in dual cycle** — β = pressure ratio at **constant-V** heat addition; ρ = cut-off ratio at **constant-p** heat addition.
> 7. **Confusing indicated vs. brake efficiency** — Always specify which one is asked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Read Before Exam:**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **VCR Engine** = IC engine with adjustable compression ratio **r**.
- ☐ **r = (V_s + V_c) / V_c**; vary V_c to vary r (most common method).
- ☐ **Kirloskar VCR** uses a **rotating extra crown disc** on a spiral thread.
- ☐ **Rotating crown** lifts it → V_c ↑ → r ↓ ; rotating other way → V_c ↓ → r ↑.
- ☐ **Multi-fuel** because different fuels have different optimum r.
- ☐ **Typical r range:** 5:1 (low) to 22:1 (high) in lab engines.

### Formula Essentials
- ☐ **Otto:** $\eta = 1 - r^{1 - \gamma}$ ; γ = 1.4 → exponent = **−0.4** (sign convention).
- ☐ **Diesel:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$
- ☐ **Dual:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\beta \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(\beta - 1) + \gamma \beta (\rho - 1)}$
- ☐ **V_s = (π/4) D² L**; **r = 1 + V_s / V_c**.
- ☐ **BSFC = m_f / BP**; **η_bth = BP / (m_f × CV)**; **η_mech = BP / IP**.

### Fuel ↔ Optimum r Mapping
- ☐ Petrol: **8–10**, Diesel: **14–22**, CNG: **12–14**, LPG: **10–12**, Methanol/Ethanol: **12–16**, Hydrogen: **6–10**, Biodiesel: **16–20**.

### Modern Real-World Reference
- ☐ **Nissan VC-Turbo (KR20DDET)** — mass-production VCR engine with r = **8:1 to 14:1**.
- ☐ **Toyota Dynamic Force** uses Atkinson-cycle-like high CR on demand.

### Lab Experiment Quick Facts
- ☐ Engine: **Kirloskar VCR single-cylinder, water-cooled, multi-fuel**.
- ☐ Dynamometer: **hydraulic / eddy-current** (rope brake only for very small engines).
- ☐ Software: **Legra** for PV diagram; **pressure transducer** for in-cylinder pressure.
- ☐ Fuel measurement: **burette with stopwatch**.
- ☐ Outputs computed: **BSFC, η_bth, η_ith, η_mech, η_vol, P_im, P_bm**.
- ☐ Vary **load** at fixed r; vary **r** at fixed load (two sets of readings).

### High-Yield Exam Triggers
- ☐ If asked "Sketch Kirloskar VCR mechanism" → label **spiral thread, crown disc, gudgeon pin, connecting rod, clearance volume change direction**.
- ☐ If asked "Why VCR for hydrogen?" → Hydrogen has wide flammability limits and high flame speed → low r prevents backfire and abnormal combustion.
- ☐ If asked "Effect of increasing r on efficiency" → efficiency ↑ (Otto/Diesel/dual all rise with r at fixed cut-off).
- ☐ If asked "Limitation" → mechanical complexity, sealing, cost, NVH, durability.

---

**END OF NOTES — VCR ENGINES, MODULE 2 (KTU 2024 SCHEME)**

topics.title: Flexi Fuel Engines (FFV) - Description Only
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Flexi Fuel Engines (FFV) - Description Only # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Flexi Fuel Vehicle (FFV)** — also termed a **Flexible-Fuel Vehicle** or **Multi-Fuel Engine** — is an internal combustion engine (typically a spark-ignition petrol engine) that has been specifically calibrated, hardware-modified, and electronically re-mapped to operate efficiently on a **wide spectrum of fuel blends**, ranging from **100% gasoline (petrol) to 85% ethanol (E85)**, and in some advanced configurations, even up to **E100 (pure ethanol) or methanol blends (M85)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Flexi Fuel Engine is a powerplant designed to run on varying proportions of alternative fuels (alcohols like ethanol/methanol) mixed with conventional gasoline, by employing an **engine control unit (ECU)** with fuel-flex sensors, modified fuel-system components, and corrosion-resistant alloys to handle the differing physico-chemical properties of alcohol-gasoline blends. The defining engineering parameter of an FFV is the ability of its **Engine Management System (EMS)** to **automatically detect the alcohol-to-petrol ratio** in the fuel currently in the tank using a **fuel composition sensor**, and to dynamically adjust the **injection pulse width (IPW)**, **ignition timing advance**, and **cold-start enrichment** strategies in real time. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction from Bi-Fuel Vehicles:** In a **Bi-Fuel** vehicle, two *separate* fuel tanks carry two *distinct* fuels, and the driver manually switches between them. In a **Flexi-Fuel** vehicle, there is only *one* fuel tank containing a *homogeneous blend*, and the ECU automatically adapts — making FFV technologically superior and operationally seamless. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Pourer" Coffee Machine Imagine a modern automatic coffee vending machine (like those in airports). You can insert any coin — a 1-rupee coin, a 5-rupee coin, a 10-rupee coin, or even a foreign currency token. The machine has a **smart sensor** that detects the weight, diameter, and metallic composition of what you inserted. Internally, it does not care *which* coin you used; it simply adjusts the dispensing logic to deliver exactly **one cup of coffee's worth of ingredients** — be it a small coin giving a small pour or a large coin giving a large pour. A **Flexi Fuel Engine operates on the exact same principle**: - The **coin** = whatever ethanol-gasoline blend the refueling station pumps in (E20, E40, E85, or pure petrol). - The **smart sensor** = the **Fuel Composition Sensor** in the fuel rail. - The **dispensing logic** = the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** recalculating injection duration. - The **coffee** = the equivalent **combustion energy output** the driver demands via the accelerator pedal. The driver never asks, "What blend is in my tank right now?" The engine simply **figures it out** and delivers consistent performance. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Boundaries | Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---| | **Fuel Blends Supported** | E0 to E85 (typical), up to E100 (advanced) | The "E" denotes % ethanol by volume | | **Octane Rating Range** | **RON 95 (petrol) to RON 105+ (E85)** | Alcohols raise effective octane | | **Calorific Value** | Petrol ≈ **44 MJ/kg**, Ethanol ≈ **26.8 MJ/kg** | Alcohols carry ~40% less energy per kg | | **Stoichiometric AFR** | Petrol ≈ **14.7:1**, Ethanol ≈ **9.0:1** | Ethanol needs much more air per kg | | **Operating Principle** | Spark Ignition (Otto Cycle) | Modified for high latent heat fuels | | **Detection Method** | Fuel Composition Sensor (FCS) — dielectric/capacitive | Real-time ethanol % measurement | | **Required Hardware Mods** | Stainless fuel lines, alcohol-resistant seals, larger injectors, cold-start hardware | Combat ethanol's hygroscopic & corrosive nature | > [!NOTE] > Ethanol's **latent heat of vaporization (≈ 904 kJ/kg)** is roughly **3.4 times** that of gasoline (≈ 305 kJ/kg). This is why FFV engines exhibit dramatically improved **knock resistance** and allow higher compression ratios in pure alcohol mode. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Conventional Engines *Cannot* Run on Alcohol Blends To understand *why* an FFV is engineered the way it is, we must first analyze the **three critical physico-chemical differences** between ethanol and gasoline that break a standard SI engine: ### 2.1.1 Difference 1: Air-Fuel Ratio Discrepancy A stoichiometric petrol engine requires a mass ratio of **14.7:1 (air to fuel)**. Pure ethanol requires **9.0:1**. A standard ECU, mapped only for 14.7:1, will **over-fuel** the engine on an ethanol blend, causing: - Flooding of the intake manifold - Carbon deposits on spark plugs - **Drastic drop in fuel economy (km/L)** - Excessive unburnt hydrocarbon (HC) emissions ### 2.1.2 Difference 2: Calorific Value Disparity Pure ethanol carries only **26.8 MJ/kg** vs petrol's **44 MJ/kg** — a **~39% deficit**. Without compensation, the engine loses power on every alcohol percentage point above 0%. ### 2.1.3 Difference 3: Cold Start Difficulty Ethanol's **high latent heat of vaporization (904 kJ/kg)** causes severe charge cooling. Below **10°C ambient**, an alcohol-rich blend cannot vaporize sufficiently to form a combustible mixture, leading to **cold-start failure** — the single biggest reason a standard car cannot run E85 in winter. ## 2.2 The FFV Solution Architecture — Four Engineering Pillars To overcome the above challenges, every Flexi Fuel Engine is built on **four hardware/software pillars**: ### Pillar 1: Fuel Composition Sensor (FCS) — The "Brain's Eyes" - Mounted in the **fuel rail** between the high-pressure pump and the injector rail. - Operates on the **dielectric / capacitive principle**: alcohol and gasoline have different **dielectric constants** (ethanol ≈ 24.3, petrol ≈ 2.0–2.2). The sensor measures the **fuel's dielectric permittivity** in real time and outputs a voltage signal (typically 0–5 V) proportional to ethanol %. - Sends this signal to the **ECU at a refresh rate of ~10 Hz**. ### Pillar 2: Modified Engine Control Unit (ECU) — The "Adaptive Brain" - Contains a **multi-dimensional fuel map (3D map)** with axes for: (RPM, Manifold Pressure, Ethanol %). - Dynamically recalculates **Injector Pulse Width (IPW)** using the formula: $$\text{IPW}_{\text{blend}} = \text{IPW}_{\text{petrol}} \times \left[ 1 + k \cdot \frac{(E\%)}{100} \right]$$ where $k \approx 1.45$ empirically compensates for the combined effect of lower calorific value and lower stoichiometric AFR. - Advances **ignition timing** by **3° to 7° crank-angle** at high ethanol content to leverage the higher octane rating. ### Pillar 3: Cold-Start Hardware Augmentation - **Grid Heater** in the intake manifold: electrically heats incoming air by 30–50°C to vaporize alcohol. - **Auxiliary Fuel Injector** (in some designs): injects a precise squirt of pure petrol from a small secondary tank during cold starts to ensure ignition. - Higher **cranking RPM** commanded by the ECU to overcome charge cooling. ### Pillar 4: Corrosion-Resistant Material Engineering - **Stainless steel fuel lines, rails, and tank** (instead of terne-coated steel). - **Fluorocarbon (Viton/FKM) seals and O-rings** — resistant to ethanol's solvent action. - **Aluminum or stainless fuel pump internals** — ethanol attacks plain steel and certain plastics. - **Larger diameter fuel injectors** with higher flow rate (~30% more) to deliver the greater fuel mass required at high ethanol blends. ## 2.3 Operational Sequence — What Happens When You Refuel **Step 1:** Driver fills tank with **E40 (40% ethanol + 60% petrol)** at the station. **Step 2:** Engine is started. ECU reads FCS signal → "Ethanol content = 40%". **Step 3:** ECU consults its 3D fuel map → looks up the cell at (RPM = 850, MAP = 50 kPa, E% = 40) → outputs a corrected IPW and ignition advance. **Step 4:** Engine runs seamlessly. Driver notices **no difference** (slight power drop compensated by advance). **Step 5:** Driver refuels next time with **E85**. FCS detects the change in real time (within 1–2 minutes of driving). ECU seamlessly transitions to the E85 map cell. No driver action required. **Step 6:** If driver switches to **pure petrol (E0)** — also automatic. ECU smoothly de-rates the IPW and retards timing. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > For "Description Only" topics, formulas are primarily for **numerical analysis support questions** in ESE. The following are the high-yield relations examiners love to test. | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | KTU Exam Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stoichiometric AFR | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$ | Mass ratio (dimensionless) | Part A definitions | | 2 | Stoichiometric for petrol | $\text{AFR}_p = 14.7$ | — | Direct recall | | 3 | Stoichiometric for ethanol | $\text{AFR}_e = 9.0$ | — | Direct recall | | 4 | Stoichiometric for blend | $\text{AFR}_{\text{blend}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{x}{\text{AFR}_e} + \dfrac{(1-x)}{\text{AFR}_p}}$ | $x$ = ethanol fraction (decimal) | Numerical 14-mark problems | | 5 | Energy density of blend | $Q_{\text{blend}} = x \cdot Q_e + (1-x) \cdot Q_p$ | $Q$ in MJ/kg | Comparison problems | | 6 | Mass fuel for given energy | $m_f = \dfrac{Q_{\text{required}}}{Q_{\text{blend}}}$ | kg | Numerical | | 7 | Volume fuel consumed ratio | $\dfrac{V_{\text{blend}}}{V_{\text{petrol}}} = \dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_{\text{blend}}} \cdot \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_{\text{blend}}}$ | Ratio (dimensionless) | Fuel economy questions | | 8 | Densities (must know) | $\rho_p = 740 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_e = 789 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | kg/m³ | Numerical base data | | 9 | Calorific values (must know) | $Q_p = 44 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $Q_e = 26.8 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | MJ/kg | Numerical base data | | 10 | Latent heat of vaporization | $L_p = 305 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $L_e = 904 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | kJ/kg | Cold start reasoning | | 11 | Energy equivalent fuel correction | $\text{IPW}_{\text{correction}} \propto \dfrac{\text{AFR}_{\text{blend}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{petrol}}} \cdot \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_{\text{blend}}}$ | Ratio | ECU mapping questions | | 12 | Octane rating of blend (linear approx.) | $\text{RON}_{\text{blend}} = x \cdot \text{RON}_e + (1-x) \cdot \text{RON}_p$ | RON unitless | Knock resistance | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields - **Automotive R&D (e.g., Bosch, Continental, Delphi):** FFV calibration is a multi-million dollar ECU mapping exercise. - **Agricultural economies (Brazil, USA, India):** FFVs allow sugarcane/corn-based ethanol to be blended into the national fuel pool without requiring dedicated ethanol-only vehicles. - **Emissions engineering:** FFVs can be tuned to exploit ethanol's high octane and oxygen content, slashing **CO and particulate emissions** by up to 30% in E85 mode. - **Defence / Military:** Multi-fuel capability is a battlefield necessity — vehicles must run on any locally available fuel (JP-8, kerosene, ethanol, gasoline). Many military engines are essentially FFV-class hardware. - **KTU relevance:** This topic is a direct feeder into **Module 5 (Alternative Fuels)** and **Module 4 (Emissions & After-treatment)**. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Since the KTU 2024 syllabus tags this topic as **"Description Only"**, the derivations below focus on **quantitative descriptive problems** — the kind that appear in 14-mark ESE questions where the examiner asks you to *numerically compare* petrol vs. an alcohol blend and justify why FFV hardware is needed. No design derivations are required. ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem #1 — Stoichiometric AFR of a Blend **Question:** A Flexi Fuel Engine is operating on **E60 fuel** (60% ethanol + 40% petrol by volume). The stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of pure ethanol is **9.0:1** and that of pure petrol is **14.7:1**. Calculate the **stoichiometric AFR of the E60 blend**, assuming the components mix ideally. **Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):** **Step 1 — Convert volume fractions to mass fractions.** We are given volume fractions: $V_e = 0.60$ and $V_p = 0.40$. Densities: $\rho_e = 789 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\rho_p = 740 \text{ kg/m}^3$. $$\text{Mass of ethanol (per 1 m}^3 \text{ blend)} = V_e \times \rho_e = 0.60 \times 789 = 473.4 \text{ kg}$$ $$\text{Mass of petrol (per 1 m}^3 \text{ blend)} = V_p \times \rho_p = 0.40 \times 740 = 296.0 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 2 — Compute total blend mass.** $$m_{\text{blend}} = 473.4 + 296.0 = 769.4 \text{ kg per m}^3 \text{ blend}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to mass fractions.** $$x_e = \dfrac{473.4}{769.4} = 0.6153$$ $$x_p = \dfrac{296.0}{769.4} = 0.3847$$ Note: Even though the volume is 60:40, the mass fractions are **61.5 : 38.5** because ethanol is denser than petrol. **Step 4 — Apply the mass-weighted stoichiometric AFR formula.** The principle: 1 kg of blend needs $x_e$ kg of ethanol (requiring $9.0 \cdot x_e$ kg of air) plus $x_p$ kg of petrol (requiring $14.7 \cdot x_p$ kg of air). The blend AFR is total air per kg of blend: $$\text{AFR}_{\text{blend}} = x_e \cdot \text{AFR}_e + x_p \cdot \text{AFR}_p$$ $$\text{AFR}_{\text{blend}} = (0.6153 \times 9.0) + (0.3847 \times 14.7)$$ $$\text{AFR}_{\text{blend}} = 5.538 + 5.655$$ $$\boxed{\text{AFR}_{\text{blend}} = 11.19 : 1}$$ **Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation.** A standard ECU mapped for petrol (14.7:1) would feed 14.7 kg of air against only 11.19 kg required. The injector would over-fuel by: $$\text{Over-fuel \%} = \dfrac{14.7 - 11.19}{11.19} \times 100 = 31.4\%$$ This **31.4% excess fuel** is precisely what an FFV's ECU must correct via **Injector Pulse Width (IPW) extension** — justifying the need for a fuel composition sensor. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem #2 — Energy Density and Fuel Consumption Penalty **Question:** Compare the **volumetric fuel consumption** of an engine running on E85 (85% ethanol, 15% petrol by volume) versus the same engine running on pure petrol, when both deliver the **same indicated work output**. **Given Data:** - Calorific value of petrol, $Q_p = 44 \text{ MJ/kg}$ - Calorific value of ethanol, $Q_e = 26.8 \text{ MJ/kg}$ - Density of petrol, $\rho_p = 740 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Density of ethanol, $\rho_e = 789 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):** **Step 1 — Establish the energy demand.** Let the engine deliver an indicated work of $W = 100 \text{ MJ}$ (a convenient reference value). **Step 2 — Mass of fuel needed if running on pure petrol.** $$m_{f,p} = \dfrac{W}{Q_p} = \dfrac{100}{44} = 2.273 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3 — Energy density of the E85 blend (mass basis).** For E85 by volume: $V_e = 0.85$, $V_p = 0.15$. $$m_{e,\text{per m}^3} = 0.85 \times 789 = 670.65 \text{ kg}$$ $$m_{p,\text{per m}^3} = 0.15 \times 740 = 111.0 \text{ kg}$$ $$m_{\text{blend}} = 670.65 + 111.0 = 781.65 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ $$x_e = \dfrac{670.65}{781.65} = 0.8580, \quad x_p = \dfrac{111.0}{781.65} = 0.1420$$ $$Q_{\text{blend}} = (0.8580 \times 26.8) + (0.1420 \times 44)$$ $$Q_{\text{blend}} = 22.99 + 6.25 = 29.24 \text{ MJ/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Mass of E85 fuel needed.** $$m_{f,\text{E85}} = \dfrac{W}{Q_{\text{blend}}} = \dfrac{100}{29.24} = 3.420 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 5 — Convert both fuel masses to volumes (the metric a driver sees in km/L).** $$V_p = \dfrac{m_{f,p}}{\rho_p} = \dfrac{2.273}{740} = 3.071 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 3.071 \text{ L}$$ $$V_{\text{E85}} = \dfrac{m_{f,\text{E85}}}{\rho_{\text{blend}}} = \dfrac{3.420}{781.65} = 4.375 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 4.375 \text{ L}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the volumetric penalty ratio.** $$\dfrac{V_{\text{E85}}}{V_p} = \dfrac{4.375}{3.071} = 1.425$$ $$\boxed{\text{Volumetric fuel consumption increases by } 42.5\% \text{ on E85 vs. petrol}}$$ **Step 7 — Practical Implication.** A vehicle returning **15 km/L on petrol** will return only: $$\text{km/L on E85} = \dfrac{15}{1.425} = 10.5 \text{ km/L}$$ This **42.5% range penalty** is the primary reason FFVs must have **larger fuel tanks** and why pure E85 vehicles are less popular despite the fuel being cheaper per litre. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component-by-Component Comparison: Standard SI Engine vs. FFV | Subsystem | Standard Petrol Engine | Flexi Fuel Engine (FFV) | Reason for Modification | |---|---|---|---| | **Fuel Tank** | Terne-coated steel or HDPE | Stainless steel or HDPE only | Ethanol corrodes unprotected steel; HDPE compatible with both | | **Fuel Pump** | Standard internal components | Stainless/aluminum internals, higher flow rate (~30%) | Ethanol is a solvent; attacks plain steel; needs more mass flow | | **Fuel Lines** | Coated steel or rubber | Stainless steel braided lines | Ethanol leaches plasticizers from standard rubber hoses | | **Seals / O-rings** | Nitrile rubber (NBR) | Fluorocarbon (FKM / Viton) or PTFE | NBR swells and degrades in ethanol | | **Injectors** | Standard flow rate | Higher flow rate (~+30%) | Greater mass of fuel needed for stoichiometric combustion | | **ECU Mapping** | Single 2D map (RPM × MAP) | 3D map (RPM × MAP × Ethanol %) | Must compensate for AFR and energy density variations | | **Cold Start** | Standard cranking + idle | Grid heater + auxiliary petrol squirt + higher cranking RPM | Ethanol's high latent heat prevents vaporization below 10°C | | **Spark Timing** | Fixed advance curve | Adaptive advance (+3° to +7° at high E%) | Higher octane of ethanol permits more advance | | **Fuel Rail** | Standard pressure (~3.5 bar) | Higher pressure (~4.0 bar) or larger rail volume | Accommodate higher mass flow demand | | **Fuel Composition Sensor** | Absent | Present (dielectric/capacitive type) | Real-time ethanol % detection | | **Emissions Calibration** | Optimized for petrol | Flexible λ-sensor strategy + purge control | Alcohols alter exhaust species profile (less CO, more aldehydes) | --- # 4.
