KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define a Flexi Fuel Vehicle (FFV). How does it differ from a Bi-Fuel vehicle?** **Model Answer:** A **Flexi Fuel Vehicle (FFV)** is an automotive internal combustion engine (typically spark-ignition) calibrated and hardware-modified to operate efficiently on **any volumetric mixture of gasoline and alcohol** (commonly ethanol), ranging from **E0 (pure petrol) to E85 (85% ethanol + 15% petrol)**, without any driver intervention. It uses a **fuel composition sensor** to detect the alcohol content in real time and an **engine control unit (ECU)** to automatically adjust the **injector pulse width**, **ignition timing**, and **cold-start enrichment**. **Key differences from a Bi-Fuel vehicle:** | Feature | Flexi Fuel Vehicle | Bi-Fuel Vehicle | |---|---|---| | Number of fuel tanks | One (single blend) | Two (separate fuels) | | Fuel switching | Automatic (ECU-driven) | Manual (driver-selected) | | Fuel type used at a time | A homogeneous blend | One pure fuel | | Sensor required | Fuel composition sensor | Fuel selector switch only | | Example | Ford Flex, Maruti E85 prototype | Maruti Duo (LPG + Petrol) | > **Examiner's tip (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + Bi-Fuel contrast table (2 marks). Avoid generic definitions; include the "**automatic detection**" angle for full marks. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List the major hardware modifications required to convert a standard petrol engine into a flexi-fuel engine. Why is each modification necessary?** **Model Answer:** The following **four critical hardware modifications** are required, with their engineering justification: 1. **Corrosion-resistant fuel system (stainless steel tank, lines, and pump internals; Viton/FKM seals):** Ethanol is **hygroscopic** (absorbs water) and a mild solvent. It corrodes unprotected steel and degrades standard nitrile rubber, causing leaks and pump failure. 2. **High-flow injectors (≈30% greater flow rate):** Ethanol blends have a **lower stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** (9.0:1 vs 14.7:1 for petrol), so the engine must inject a greater fuel mass per cycle. Stock injectors would starve the engine on E85. 3. **Fuel Composition Sensor (FCS) on the fuel rail:** Uses **dielectric permittivity** difference between petrol (ε ≈ 2.0) and ethanol (ε ≈ 24.3) to send a real-time ethanol-% voltage signal to the ECU. 4. **Grid heater in the intake manifold:** Ethanol's **high latent heat of vaporization (904 kJ/kg vs 305 kJ/kg for petrol)** causes poor cold vaporization. The grid heater warms intake air to ensure cold-start combustion on alcohol-rich blends. > **Examiner's tip (3 marks):** Any 3 of the above 4 are sufficient for full marks. Always pair modification with *why* (1 mark each). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Module question in Part B carries an **internal choice** between two 14-mark alternatives. Both alternatives below are on the same topic with similar cognitive depth (Apply / Analyze level on Bloom's Taxonomy). ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **working of a flexi fuel engine** and the role of the **engine control unit (ECU)** in handling varying fuel blends. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An engine is tested on **pure petrol (E0)** and on **E60 fuel** (60% ethanol by volume). Given: - $\text{AFR}_{\text{petrol}} = 14.7$, $\text{AFR}_{\text{ethanol}} = 9.0$ - $\rho_{\text{petrol}} = 740 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{\text{ethanol}} = 789 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $Q_{\text{petrol}} = 44 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $Q_{\text{ethanol}} = 26.8 \text{ MJ/kg}$ Calculate: 1. The **stoichiometric AFR** of the E60 blend. 2. The **percentage change in volumetric fuel consumption** when the engine is switched from petrol to E60, for the **same energy output**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks **Introduction (1 mark):** A Flexi Fuel Engine is an SI engine that can run on any volumetric mixture of petrol and alcohol, from E0 to E85, by using a **fuel composition sensor** to detect blend ratio and an **adaptive ECU** to control injection and ignition parameters. **Working description (4 marks):** - The driver refuels with any blend (E0 to E85). The **fuel composition sensor** (dielectric/capacitive type) mounted on the fuel rail continuously measures the **dielectric permittivity** of the fuel. Since ethanol has ε ≈ 24.3 and petrol has ε ≈ 2.0, the sensor output voltage (0–5 V) is a direct linear function of ethanol %. - This signal is fed to the **ECU**, which contains a **three-dimensional fuel map** with axes: (Engine RPM × Manifold Absolute Pressure × Ethanol %). - For every combustion cycle (every 10 ms), the ECU computes the **corrected Injector Pulse Width** to maintain stoichiometric combustion. The corrected IPW must compensate for both the lower stoichiometric AFR and the lower calorific value of the alcohol fraction. - The ECU also advances **spark ignition timing** by 3° to 7° at high ethanol fractions, exploiting ethanol's higher octane rating (RON ~105 vs ~95 for petrol). - A **grid heater** in the intake manifold is activated below 10°C ambient to vaporize alcohol-rich fuels for cold starting. - A **closed-loop λ-sensor** downstream of the catalytic converter provides feedback to the ECU, which applies a final **trim correction** to maintain λ = 1 ± 0.01. **Neat sketch (2 marks):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. Key blocks to label: Fuel Tank → Pump → Fuel Composition Sensor → ECU → Injectors → Engine. (Draw and label 6–8 blocks clearly.) --- #### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks **Part (b)(1): Stoichiometric AFR of E60 blend (3 marks)** Step 1 — Mass fractions from volume fractions (1 mark): $$m_e = 0.60 \times 789 = 473.4 \text{ kg}, \quad m_p = 0.40 \times 740 = 296.0 \text{ kg}$$ $$m_{\text{blend}} = 769.4 \text{ kg}, \quad x_e = 0.6153, \quad x_p = 0.3847$$ Step 2 — Apply mass-weighted AFR (1 mark): $$\text{AFR}_{\text{E60}} = (0.6153 \times 9.0) + (0.3847 \times 14.7) = 5.538 + 5.655 = 11.19$$ $$\boxed{\text{AFR}_{\text{E60}} = 11.19 : 1}$$ Step 3 — Engineering note (1 mark): The petrol ECU is mapped for 14.7:1, so the **over-fueling error** is (14.7 − 11.19)/11.19 = 31.4%, justifying FFV hardware. **Part (b)(2): Volumetric fuel consumption comparison (4 marks)** Step 1 — Energy density of E60 blend (1.5 marks): $$Q_{\text{E60}} = (0.6153 \times 26.8) + (0.3847 \times 44) = 16.49 + 16.93 = 33.42 \text{ MJ/kg}$$ Step 2 — Density of E60 blend (0.5 mark): $$\rho_{\text{E60}} = 769.4 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ Step 3 — Fuel mass and volume for 100 MJ of work (1 mark): $$m_p = 100/44 = 2.273 \text{ kg} \Rightarrow V_p = 2.273/740 = 3.071 \text{ L}$$ $$m_{\text{E60}} = 100/33.42 = 2.993 \text{ kg} \Rightarrow V_{\text{E60}} = 2.993/769.4 = 3.890 \text{ L}$$ Step 4 — Percentage change (1 mark): $$\Delta V\% = \dfrac{3.890 - 3.071}{3.071} \times 100 = \boxed{+26.7\% \text{ more volume on E60}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Compare the **architectural differences** between a **bi-fuel engine** and a **flexi-fuel engine**, with the help of a block diagram. Discuss the **role of the fuel composition sensor** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An FFV is running on **E40 fuel**. The following data are given: - $\rho_p = 740 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_e = 789 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $Q_p = 44 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $Q_e = 26.8 \text{ MJ/kg}$ - $\text{AFR}_p = 14.7$, $\text{AFR}_e = 9.0$ If the engine delivers **75 MJ of indicated work per cycle**, compute: 1. The **mass of fuel** consumed per cycle. 2. The **volume of fuel** consumed per cycle. 3. The **mass of air** required for stoichiometric combustion. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks **Comparison table (3 marks):** | Aspect | Bi-Fuel Engine | Flexi-Fuel Engine | |---|---|---| | Fuel tanks | Two (one per fuel) | One | | Fuel switch | Manual | Automatic (ECU) | | Fuel at any instant | One pure fuel | One homogeneous blend | | Sensor | Selector switch | Fuel composition sensor | | ECU complexity | Low (two maps) | High (3D adaptive map) | | Hardware cost | Moderate | High (corrosion-resistant + sensors) | | Example | Petrol + LPG | Petrol + Ethanol (E0–E85) | **Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw two parallel blocks. Left side: Two fuel tanks → selector switch → engine. Right side: Single fuel tank → FCS → ECU (3D map) → engine. (Refer to Section 4.4 of these notes.) **Role of Fuel Composition Sensor (2 marks):** The FCS is a **capacitive/dielectric sensor** mounted on the fuel rail. It exploits the **dielectric permittivity difference** between petrol (ε ≈ 2.0) and ethanol (ε ≈ 24.3). The sensor outputs an analog voltage proportional to the ethanol fraction. The ECU samples this voltage at ~10 Hz and uses it as the **third axis of its 3D fuel map**, ensuring correct stoichiometry regardless of the blend the driver has filled. --- #### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Mass fractions of E40 (1 mark):** $$m_e = 0.40 \times 789 = 315.6 \text{ kg}, \quad m_p = 0.60 \times 740 = 444.0 \text{ kg}$$ $$m_{\text{blend}} = 759.6 \text{ kg}, \quad x_e = 0.4155, \quad x_p = 0.5845$$ **Step 2 — Energy density of E40 (1 mark):** $$Q_{\text{E40}} = (0.4155 \times 26.8) + (0.5845 \times 44) = 11.14 + 25.72 = 36.86 \text{ MJ/kg}$$ **Step 3 — Density of E40 blend (0.5 mark):** $$\rho_{\text{E40}} = 759.6 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 4 — Mass of fuel per cycle (1.5 marks):** $$m_f = \dfrac{W}{Q_{\text{E40}}} = \dfrac{75}{36.86} = \boxed{2.035 \text{ kg per cycle}}$$ **Step 5 — Volume of fuel per cycle (1.5 marks):** $$V_f = \dfrac{m_f}{\rho_{\text{E40}}} = \dfrac{2.035}{759.6} = 2.679 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$ $$\boxed{V_f = 2.679 \text{ L per cycle}}$$ **Step 6 — Stoichiometric AFR of E40 (1 mark):** $$\text{AFR}_{\text{E40}} = (0.4155 \times 9.0) + (0.5845 \times 14.7) = 3.74 + 8.59 = 12.33$$ **Step 7 — Mass of air required (1.5 marks):** $$m_{\text{air}} = m_f \times \text{AFR}_{\text{E40}} = 2.035 \times 12.33$$ $$\boxed{m_{\text{air}} = 25.09 \text{ kg per cycle}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in FFV Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing FFV with Bi-Fuel (costs 2–3 marks):** Examiners *intentionally* test this. Memorize: *one tank, automatic, blend* vs. *two tanks, manual, pure fuel*. > > 2. **Forgetting to convert volume fractions to mass fractions** in numerical problems. Many students blindly use $x = 0.6$ in the AFR formula. Always convert using densities first — this is the **single most common calculation error** flagged in KTU answer scripts. > > 3. **Not stating the units** in numerical answers. KTU examiners award 0.5 marks per numerical for correct units. > > 4. **Omitting the "why"** in modification lists. "Use stainless steel" gets 0.5 marks; "Use stainless steel **because ethanol corrodes unprotected steel**" gets the full 1 mark. > > 5. **Skipping the block diagram in 7-mark questions.** Even a rough but labelled diagram carries **2–3 marks** — never skip it in Part (a). > > 6. **Writing "the ECU adjusts injection" without quantifying the adjustment.** Always mention IPW, % over-fueling correction, or the 3D map nature of the lookup. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Points — Flexi Fuel Engines (FFV):** - [x] **Definition:** Engine that runs on any blend of petrol + alcohol (typically ethanol), from E0 to E85. - [x] **One tank, automatic** detection via Fuel Composition Sensor (FCS). - [x] **Distinguish from Bi-Fuel:** Bi-Fuel = two tanks, manual switch, pure fuels. FFV = one tank, automatic, blend. - [x] **Three key physico-chemical differences:** Stoichiometric AFR (14.7 vs 9.0), Calorific value (44 vs 26.8 MJ/kg), Latent heat of vaporization (305 vs 904 kJ/kg). - [x] **FCS principle:** Dielectric/capacitive — uses ε difference (petrol 2.0, ethanol 24.3). - [x] **Four hardware pillars:** (1) FCS, (2) Adaptive ECU with 3D map, (3) Cold-start grid heater, (4) Corrosion-resistant materials (stainless steel, Viton seals, high-flow injectors). - [x] **ECU adjustments on alcohol blends:** IPW ↑ (~+30–45%), spark advance ↑ (+3° to +7°). - [x] **Stoichiometric AFR formula (mass-weighted):** $\text{AFR}_{\text{blend}} = x_e \cdot \text{AFR}_e + x_p \cdot \text{AFR}_p$ (after converting volume → mass fractions). - [x] **Energy density of blend (mass-weighted):** $Q_{\text{blend}} = x_e \cdot Q_e + x_p \cdot Q_p$. - [x] **Fuel consumption penalty on E85:** ~40–45% higher volume consumed for same energy output. - [x] **Key numbers to memorize:** $\rho_p = 740$, $\rho_e = 789$ kg/m³; $Q_p = 44$, $Q_e = 26.8$ MJ/kg; $\text{AFR}_p = 14.7$, $\text{AFR}_e = 9.0$; $L_p = 305$, $L_e = 904$ kJ/kg; RON petrol ≈ 95, RON ethanol ≈ 105. - [x] **Real-world examples to cite:** Ford Taurus FFV (USA), Maruti Suzuki E85 prototype (India), Toyota Flex-Fuel (Brazil), all Saab BioPower models. - [x] **Engineering applications:** Agricultural ethanol economies (Brazil, USA, India), emissions reduction, military multi-fuel engines, ECU calibration R&D. - [x] **Block diagram must include:** Tank → Pump → Fuel Rail → FCS → ECU (3D map) → Injectors → Engine, with feedback from λ-sensor. > **End of Topic Notes — Flexi Fuel Engines (FFV) — Description Only** > *Module 2 | PEMUT752 | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Combustion in SI Engines and CI Engines - Thermodynamic Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Combustion in SI Engines and CI Engines - Thermodynamic Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Combustion in IC Engines?

**Combustion in Internal Combustion (IC) Engines** is the rapid, controlled thermochemical oxidation of a fuel-air mixture, releasing chemical energy as thermal energy that is subsequently converted into mechanical work via pressure rise on the piston.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Combustion is the exothermic chemical reaction between hydrocarbons (fuel) and oxygen (air), characterized by a rapid release of heat at constant volume (Otto cycle approximation) or a combination of constant volume and constant pressure processes (Dual/Diesel cycle).

**Two fundamentally different pathways exist:**

| Engine Type | Combustion Initiation | Mixture Preparation | Burning Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SI Engine** (Spark Ignition) | External ignition source (spark plug) | Premixed (homogeneous) before combustion | Premixed, flame propagation-driven |
| **CI Engine** (Compression Ignition) | Auto-ignition via high compression temperature | Fuel injected into hot compressed air | Diffusion-controlled, after auto-ignition |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Matchstick vs. The Furnace

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:**
> - **SI Engine Combustion** ≈ *Lighting a matchstick on a candle.* The flame front spreads smoothly from the spark kernel outward through a *pre-mixed* gas (like a wave moving through a crowd). The whole mixture is "ready to burn" — you only need a tiny spark.
> - **CI Engine Combustion** ≈ *Throwing a log into a hot furnace.* No spark is needed because the surrounding air is *already hot enough* to ignite the fuel the moment it contacts the air. The fuel and air mix and burn *simultaneously*.

### 1.3 Thermodynamic Analysis — The Big Picture

**Thermodynamic Analysis of Combustion** is the systematic application of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the combustion chamber, treating it as an **Open System** (control volume) during the working stroke, to quantify:
- Energy released by fuel (calorific value)
- Energy converted to useful work (thermal efficiency)
- Energy lost to exhaust, coolant, and unburned fuel (combustion efficiency)
- Energy balance closure across the engine cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric) for gasoline (iso-octane C₈H₁₈):**
> $$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\,\text{O}_2 + 12.5(3.76)\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 47\,\text{N}_2$$
> **Stoichiometric AFR = 15.13 (mass basis)**, meaning **14.7 kg of air** are required to burn **1 kg of gasoline** completely. Diesel operates typically at **18–25 : 1** (lean).

### 1.4 Physical Constants for Combustion Analysis

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314** | kJ/kmol·K |
| Universal gas constant (per kg) | $\bar{R}$ | **0.287** | kJ/kg·K |
| Air molecular mass | M_air | **28.97** | kg/kmol |
| Lower Heating Value (gasoline) | LHV | **44,000** | kJ/kg |
| Higher Heating Value (gasoline) | HHV | **47,300** | kJ/kg |
| Lower Heating Value (diesel) | LHV | **42,500** | kJ/kg |
| Adiabatic flame temperature (stoichiometric gasoline-air) | T_ad | **~2400** | K |
| Specific heat ratio (air) | γ | **1.4** | — |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Combustion in SI Engines — Theoretical Framework

#### 2.1.1 Stages of SI Engine Combustion (Three-Stage Combustion)

**Stage 1 — Ignition Lag (or Preparation Phase):**
- Begins at spark discharge; ends when **5–10%** of mass has burned.
- Duration ≈ **0.0–0.002 s** (engine-speed dependent).
- A small **kernel** forms, grows into a **quasi-spherical flame front**.
- Why this stage exists: finite time required for flame kernel formation and initial propagation.

**Stage 2 — Rapid / Propagation (or Main Combustion) Phase:**
- Begins when ~5–10% mass burned; ends when **~85–95%** mass has burned.
- Pressure rises sharply (often visible as P-dV work area).
- The flame front sweeps across the chamber at a characteristic **flame speed**.
- Why: mass fraction burning rate peaks (MFBR) reaches maximum ~50% mass-burned point.

**Stage 3 — Afterburning (or Tail Burning) Phase:**
- Begins after ~85–95% mass burned; ends when cycle is complete.
- Slow, diffusion-controlled burning of last 5–15% of mixture.
- Why: remaining fuel pockets are in crevices (squish, ring crevices), boundary layers, and are isolated from main flame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mass Fraction Burned (MFB)** curve is the **KTU-favoured** way to characterise combustion. The dMFB/dt curve peaks at the **CA50** point (crank angle at 50% mass burned) — a critical engine performance indicator.

#### 2.1.2 Normal and Abnormal Combustion in SI Engines

**Normal Combustion:**
- Smooth, controlled flame propagation from a single ignition kernel.
- Flame speed: **15–30 m/s** (typical, end-gas conditions).
- No audible knock; P-θ diagram shows smooth pressure rise.

**Abnormal Combustion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Critical Types of Abnormal SI Combustion:**
> 1. **Knock (Auto-ignition of End-Gas):** End-gas (unburned mixture ahead of flame front) auto-ignites spontaneously due to high temperature & pressure. Causes a sharp pressure spike, "pinging" sound, and potential piston damage.
> 2. **Surface Ignition:** Flame kernel forms at an overheated surface (carbon deposit, spark plug electrode, hot exhaust valve) **before** or **after** the normal spark, not at the spark plug.

**Knock — The "Why" Explained:**
1. As flame propagates from spark plug, **end-gas** is compressed and heated (isentropic compression + heat conduction from burned gases).
2. End-gas temperature exceeds **auto-ignition temperature** (~600–700 K for gasoline-air under engine conditions — much lower than lab values due to radicals and high pressure).
3. Auto-ignition occurs **spontaneously** with an extremely rapid energy release (not a flame propagation but a volumetric explosion).
4. This generates **high-frequency pressure oscillations** (5–25 kHz) — audible as "knock."

**Factors Affecting Knock:**
- **Octane rating** ↑ → Knock resistance ↑ (engine octane requirement concept)
- **Compression ratio** ↑ → Knock tendency ↑
- **End-gas temperature** ↑ → Knock tendency ↑ (retarded spark reduces this)
- **Inlet pressure** ↑ → Knock tendency ↑
- **Spark advance** ↑ → Knock tendency ↑

**Factors Affecting Surface Ignition:**
- **Carbon deposits** in chamber act as hot spots → critical
- **Hot exhaust valves** → run-on or "dieseling"
- **Spark plug electrodes** (overheated) → pre-ignition

#### 2.1.3 SI Engine Combustion Chambers (Brief)

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Bathtub / Wedge** | Simple, hemispherical/wedge bowl in piston crown | Older passenger cars |
| **Heron (pentroof)** | Quiescent, pentroof cylinder head with central spark plug | Modern petrol engines |
| **Disc / Bowl-in-Piston** | Disc-shaped chamber in piston crown | High-speed, two-stroke |
| **Hemi** | Hemispherical head, central spark, opposed valves | Performance engines |

### 2.2 Combustion in CI Engines — Theoretical Framework

#### 2.2.1 Stages of CI Engine Combustion (Three-Phase Model — The Diesel Cycle Combustion)

**Phase 1 — Ignition Delay (or Delay Period):**
- Begins at fuel injection start; ends at the **start of rapid pressure rise**.
- Duration: **0.0005–0.005 s** (typically 0.5–3 ms; decreases with engine speed).
- Fuel is injected but **only ~5%** has auto-ignited at the end.
- Physical/chemical processes: fuel atomization → vaporization → mixing with hot air → pre-flame reactions → auto-ignition.

**Phase 2 — Rapid / Uncontrolled Combustion (or Premixed Burning):**
- Begins at ignition delay end; lasts until fuel injection ends (or shortly after).
- **All fuel accumulated during Phase 1** burns essentially **simultaneously**.
- Pressure rise is **steep** (high *dp/dθ*); this is the loud combustion noise source.
- Rate is **controlled by fuel chemistry** (not by mixing).

**Phase 3 — Slow / Controlled Combustion (or Mixing-Controlled Diffusion Burning):**
- Begins after Phase 2 ends; lasts until combustion is complete.
- Rate is **controlled by mixing rate** of fuel vapor with air.
- Pressure follows nearly the **motoring curve** (no extra rise beyond compression).
- Long duration may cause incomplete burning (smoke, CO, unburned HC).

#### 2.2.2 Delay Period Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Delay Period ($\Delta\theta_d$):**
> The delay period is governed by the **Arrhenius-type auto-ignition kinetics** of the fuel under high-temperature compressed air conditions.

The Arrhenius-based ignition delay (in crank angle degrees):
$$\Delta\theta_d = K \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{\bar{R} \cdot T}\right)$$

where:
- $K$ = experimental constant (depends on fuel)
- $p$ = in-cylinder pressure (bar)
- $n$ = pressure exponent (~0.7–1.5 for diesel)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (~30–60 kJ/mol for diesel)
- $T$ = compressed air temperature (K)
- $\bar{R}$ = universal gas constant

**Why each factor:**
- $p \uparrow$ → more collisions → delay ↓
- $T \uparrow$ → reaction rate ↑ → delay ↓
- Cetane number ↑ → $E_a$ ↓ → delay ↓

**Factors Affecting Delay Period:**
- **Compression ratio** ↑ → temperature ↑ → delay ↓
- **Intake temperature** ↑ → delay ↓
- **Fuel volatility / cetane number** ↑ → delay ↓
- **Engine speed** ↑ → less wall heat loss → delay ↓ (slightly)
- **EGR rate** ↑ → delay ↑ (combustion products slow chemistry)

#### 2.2.3 Knock in CI Engines (Diesel Knock)

> [!WARNING]
> **Diesel Knock** is fundamentally different from SI knock!
> - SI Knock: end-gas **auto-ignites** (detonation) producing high-frequency shock waves.
> - CI Knock (also called **cold-start knock** or **rough combustion**): caused by **too long delay period** → too much premixed fuel → sudden, near-simultaneous ignition → high *dp/dθ*.

**Consequences:** noisy operation, low thermal efficiency, mechanical stress, soot.

**Remedies:** improve fuel atomization (higher injection pressure, smaller holes), reduce delay (higher compression ratio, glow plugs), use high-cetane fuel.

### 2.3 Thermodynamic Analysis — Engine as a System

#### 2.3.1 First Law Application to Combustion

For a **closed cycle** (idealised):
$$Q_{in} - W_{net} = \Delta U_{cycle} = 0 \quad \text{(cyclic process)}$$

For a **steady-flow open system** (intake → exhaust over one cycle):
$$Q_{in} - W_{net} = \sum m_e h_e - \sum m_i h_i$$

#### 2.3.2 Energy Balance of Combustion Products

**Combustion Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{comb} = \frac{\text{Energy actually released}}{\text{Energy theoretically available}} = \frac{Q_{released}}{m_f \cdot \text{CV}}$$

For SI engines with lean mixtures, $\eta_{comb} \approx 0.95–0.99$.
For CI engines with diffusion burning, $\eta_{comb}$ can be lower due to incomplete mixing (smoke).

#### 2.3.3 Air-Fuel Ratio and Combustion Equations

**Stoichiometric Combustion of General Hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ:**
$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis):**
$$\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \frac{4.76 \cdot M_{air} \cdot (x + y/4)}{M_f}$$

where $M_f = 12x + y$ (fuel molecular mass), and 4.76 accounts for $\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2$ per mole of $\text{O}_2$.

**Equivalence Ratio:**
$$\phi = \frac{(\text{F/A})_{actual}}{(\text{F/A})_{stoich}}$$

- $\phi = 1$ → stoichiometric
- $\phi < 1$ → lean (typical SI cruising, all CI operation)
- $\phi > 1$ → rich (high power, cold-start, full-load SI)

#### 2.3.4 Theoretical Air Calculation

For complete combustion with **x** C atoms and **y** H atoms per fuel molecule, **theoretical oxygen per kg fuel**:
$$O_{2,\,theor} = \frac{8x + y \cdot 8}{12x + y} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{kg fuel}}\right]$$

**Theoretical air per kg fuel:**
$$A_{theor} = \frac{O_{2,\,theor}}{0.232} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}\right]$$

(0.232 = mass fraction of $\text{O}_2$ in air)

**Actual air** with excess air percentage $e$:
$$A_{act} = A_{theor} \cdot (1 + e/100)$$

#### 2.3.5 Combustion Temperature

**Adiabatic Flame Temperature** (no heat loss, complete combustion):
$$T_{ad} = T_i + \frac{m_f \cdot \text{CV}}{m_{products} \cdot c_{p,\,products}}$$

For constant-volume combustion (SI approximation):
$$T_{ad,\,v} = T_i \cdot \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

For constant-pressure combustion (CI approximation):
$$T_{ad,\,p} = T_i \cdot \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — Every KTU Question Tests One or More of Them.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + (x + y/4)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + (y/2)\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Stoichiometric combustion equation | mol/mol |
| 2 | $\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \dfrac{4.76(32(x + y/4))}{12x + y}$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass) | kg air/kg fuel |
| 3 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{act}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{Q_{released}}{m_f \cdot \text{CV}}$ | Combustion efficiency | — |
| 5 | $Q_{in} - W_{net} = \sum m_e h_e - \sum m_i h_i$ | Steady-flow energy equation | kJ |
| 6 | $T_{ad} = T_i + \dfrac{m_f \cdot \text{CV}}{m_p \cdot c_p}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | K |
| 7 | $\Delta\theta_d = K \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{\bar{R}\,T}\right)$ | Ignition delay (crank angle) | degrees |
| 8 | $\eta_{th,\,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | Otto cycle thermal efficiency | — |
| 9 | $\eta_{th,\,Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | Diesel cycle thermal efficiency | — |
| 10 | $S = \dfrac{p_2 - p_1}{(p_1/p_1)} \cdot$ … etc. | SI knock intensity indicator (peak P) | bar |
| 11 | $\dot{Q}_{cool} = m_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{gas} - T_{cool})$ | Heat loss to coolant (approx.) | kW |
| 12 | $m_{exhaust} = m_{air} + m_f$ | Mass balance (closed fuel system) | kg |
| 13 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{W_{gross}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | — |
| 14 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{m_f}{W_{net}}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption | kg/kWh |
| 15 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_{comb} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | Overall efficiency | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Re:** KTU asks frequently: "Calculate the stoichiometric AFR of methane (CH₄)" — memorise the general formula and substitute.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine Calibration Engineers** use the AFR and equivalence ratio to program the ECU's fuel injection map.
- **Emissions Engineers** use $\phi$ values to predict NOx (high-T, lean) vs. soot (rich) zones.
- **Combustion Chamber Designers** use MFB curves to optimise spark timing and compression ratio.
- **Fuels Engineers** correlate cetane/octane ratings with delay-period kinetics.
- **Thermal Engineers** balance $\eta_{comb}$ against exhaust energy recovery (turbocharging, ORC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for a Generic Hydrocarbon

**Given:** Generic hydrocarbon fuel $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y$.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation (stoichiometric, dry basis):**
$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Convert mole basis to mass basis.**

Mass of fuel (1 mole basis):
$$m_f = (12x + y) \quad [\text{g/mol}]$$

Mass of $\text{O}_2$ required:
$$m_{O_2} = 32 \cdot \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \quad [\text{g/mol}]$$

Mass of air required (since air = 21% $\text{O}_2$ + 79% $\text{N}_2$ by mole, but mass basis gives **23.2% O₂**):
$$m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} \quad [\text{g}]$$

Alternatively, using **mole basis 1:4.76** (1 mol $\text{O}_2$ brings 3.76 mol $\text{N}_2$):
$$m_{air} = 28.97 \cdot \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) + 28 \cdot 3.76 \cdot \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) = 28.97 \cdot 4.76 \cdot \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**
$$\boxed{\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_f} = \frac{4.76 \cdot 28.97 \cdot (x + y/4)}{12x + y} = \frac{137.9 \cdot (x + y/4)}{12x + y}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify for iso-octane (x=8, y=18):**
$$\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \frac{137.9 \cdot (8 + 18/4)}{12(8) + 18} = \frac{137.9 \cdot 12.5}{114} = \frac{1723.75}{114} = 15.12$$

This matches the well-known **15.13** (within rounding). ✓

**Step 5 — Verify for methane (x=1, y=4):**
$$\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \frac{137.9 \cdot (1 + 1)}{16} = \frac{275.8}{16} = 17.24$$

Standard accepted value for methane is **17.2**. ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: KTU-Style Problem — Air-Fuel Calculation for Octane

> **Problem:** A four-stroke, 4-cylinder SI engine uses iso-octane (C₈H₁₈) at an equivalence ratio of 0.9 (slightly lean). The engine develops 60 kW brake power at 3000 rpm with a brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) of 0.30 kg/kWh. Calculate:
> (a) The mass flow rate of fuel (kg/h).
> (b) The mass flow rate of air (kg/h).
> (c) The actual air-fuel ratio.
> (d) The theoretical (stoichiometric) air required per kg of fuel.
> (e) The percentage of excess air.

**Given:**
- Fuel: C₈H₁₈, M = 114 kg/kmol
- $\phi = 0.9$ (lean)
- $P_b = 60$ kW
- BSFC = 0.30 kg/kWh
- $N = 3000$ rpm

#### (a) Mass flow rate of fuel

The fuel mass flow rate is:
$$\dot{m}_f = \text{BSFC} \times P_b$$

$$\dot{m}_f = 0.30 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}} \times 60 \, \text{kW} = 18 \, \text{kg/h}$$

#### (b) Mass flow rate of air

Stoichiometric AFR for C₈H₁₈:
$$\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \frac{137.9 \cdot (8 + 18/4)}{12(8) + 18} = 15.13$$

Actual AFR at $\phi = 0.9$:
$$\text{AFR}_{act} = \frac{\text{AFR}_{stoich}}{\phi} = \frac{15.13}{0.9} = 16.81$$

Therefore:
$$\dot{m}_{air} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{AFR}_{act} = 18 \times 16.81 = 302.6 \, \text{kg/h}$$

#### (c) Actual air-fuel ratio

From (b):
$$\boxed{\text{AFR}_{act} = 16.81 \, \text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$

#### (d) Stoichiometric air per kg fuel

$$\boxed{A_{theor} = 15.13 \, \text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$

#### (e) Percentage of excess air

Excess air factor:
$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\phi} = \frac{1}{0.9} = 1.111$$

Percentage excess:
$$e = (\lambda - 1) \times 100 = 11.1\%$$

$$\boxed{e \approx 11.1\%}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Delay Period Calculation (CI Engine)

> **Problem:** For a CI engine, the delay period constant $K = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}$, pressure exponent $n = 1.0$, activation energy $E_a = 35{,}000$ J/mol. At TDC the in-cylinder temperature is 950 K and pressure is 45 bar. Calculate the ignition delay period $\Delta\theta_d$ in crank angle degrees at an engine speed of 1500 rpm.

**Given:**
- $K = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}$
- $n = 1.0$
- $E_a = 35{,}000$ J/mol
- $T = 950$ K
- $p = 45$ bar
- $N = 1500$ rpm

**Step 1 — Compute the Arrhenius exponent:**
$$\frac{E_a}{\bar{R} \cdot T} = \frac{35{,}000}{8.314 \times 950} = \frac{35{,}000}{7898.3} = 4.432$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponential:**
$$\exp(4.432) = e^{4.432} \approx 84.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute $p^{-n}$:**
$$p^{-n} = 45^{-1.0} = \frac{1}{45} = 0.02222$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta\theta_d$ (crank-angle degrees):**
$$\Delta\theta_d = K \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{\bar{R} \cdot T}\right)$$

$$\Delta\theta_d = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.02222 \times 84.0 = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.8667$$

$$\boxed{\Delta\theta_d \approx 0.0112 \, \text{crank-angle degrees}}$$

**Step 5 — (Optional) Convert to time:**
$$\text{time per degree} = \frac{60}{N \times 360} = \frac{60}{1500 \times 360} = \frac{1}{9000} \, \text{s/deg}$$

$$\Delta t = 0.0112 \times \frac{1}{9000} = 1.24 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{s} = 1.24 \, \mu\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to KTU students:** Real-world delays are typically 0.5–3 ms. If your result is in microseconds, double-check whether the formula constants were in consistent units (often the constants K and the formula include crank-angle conversion factors that vary by textbook — the Arrhenius form is the same; magnitudes differ).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Combustion Temperature (Adiabatic Flame)

> **Problem:** Calculate the adiabatic flame temperature for stoichiometric combustion of methane with 20% excess air at 298 K, 1 atm. Use mean $c_p$ values: $\text{CO}_2$: 0.045 kJ/mol·K; $\text{H}_2\text{O}$(g): 0.040 kJ/mol·K; $\text{N}_2$: 0.030 kJ/mol·K; $\text{O}_2$: 0.030 kJ/mol·K.

**Step 1 — Combustion equation with 20% excess air (per mol CH₄):**

Theoretical O₂ = 2 mol; actual O₂ = 2 × 1.2 = 2.4 mol; N₂ brought in = 2.4 × 3.76 = 9.024 mol.

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2.4\,\text{O}_2 + 9.024\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 0.4\,\text{O}_2 + 9.024\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance:**
$$m_f \cdot \text{CV} = n_{products} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{ad} - T_i)$$

For CH₄: LHV = 802.3 kJ/mol (per mol). Products total moles: 1 + 2 + 0.4 + 9.024 = 12.424.

For precision, weighted $c_p$ of products:
$$\bar{c}_p = \frac{(1)(0.045) + (2)(0.040) + (0.4)(0.030) + (9.024)(0.030)}{12.424}$$

$$\bar{c}_p = \frac{0.045 + 0.080 + 0.012 + 0.2707}{12.424} = \frac{0.4077}{12.424} = 0.03281 \, \text{kJ/mol·K}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T_{ad}$:**
$$802.3 = 12.424 \times 0.03281 \times (T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$802.3 = 0.4077 \times (T_{ad} - 298)$$

$$T_{ad} - 298 = \frac{802.3}{0.4077} = 1968.1$$

$$\boxed{T_{ad} \approx 2266 \, \text{K}}$$

This is consistent with the **2200–2300 K range** for lean methane-air flames. ✓

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table — SI vs CI Combustion

| Parameter | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Mixture formation** | Premixed (in intake manifold) | In-cylinder injection |
| **Ignition source** | Spark plug | Auto-ignition (compression) |
| **Air-fuel ratio** | ~12–18:1 (stoich or lean) | ~18–70:1 (always lean) |
| **Compression ratio** | 8–12 : 1 | 14–22 : 1 |
| **Equivalence ratio $\phi$** | 0.7–1.2 | 0.3–0.7 |
| **Combustion type** | Premixed flame propagation | Diffusion + auto-ignition |
| **Peak cycle temp** | ~2500 K | ~2800 K (in diffusion flame) |
| **Combustion control** | Spark timing (mild) | Injection timing & rate (major) |
| **Knock mechanism** | End-gas auto-ignition | Long delay → rapid premixed burn |
| **Critical fuel property** | Octane number | Cetane number |
| **Max thermal efficiency** | ~35% (Otto cycle ideal ~63%) | ~45% (Diesel cycle ideal ~64%) |
| **Typical efficiency (real)** | 25–32% | 35–42% |
| **Emission challenges** | CO, unburned HC, NOx | NOx, soot (PM) |

---

### 3.6 Derivation: Mass-Burned Fraction (Wiebe Function)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlights:** Wiebe function is a standard empirical model for MFB in SI engines.

$$x_b(\theta) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-a \cdot \left(\frac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta\theta_b}\right)^{m+1}\right]$$

where:
- $x_b$ = mass fraction burned
- $\theta_0$ = start of combustion (crank angle)
- $\Delta\theta_b$ = total combustion duration (crank angle)
- $a$ = Wiebe efficiency parameter (≈ 5–6 typically)
- $m+1$ = shape parameter (typically $m = 1$ to $3$)

**Why this matters:** Integrates $\int x_b \, d\theta$ over the cycle to compute work. CA50 (when $x_b = 0.5$) and CA10, CA90 define combustion phasing for performance tuning.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SI Engine Combustion Process — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Intake Stroke - Air-Fuel Mixture Enters] --> B[Compression Stroke - Mixture Compressed]
    B --> C[Sprak Discharge at Spark Plug]
    C --> D[Stage 1 - Ignition Lag - Kernel Forms]
    D --> E[Stage 2 - Flame Propagation - Main Burning]
    E --> F[Stage 3 - Afterburning - Tail Burning]
    F --> G[Power Stroke - Expansion]
    G --> H[Exhaust Stroke - Burned Gases Expelled]

    E -.->|End-gas| I[End-Gas Compression]
    I -.->|If T > Auto-ignition| J[S1 Engine Knock]
    I -.->|Normal| E

    K[Spark Plug] -.->|Failed| L[Miss-fire]
    M[Hot Carbon Deposit] -.->|Pre-Ignition| N[Surface Ignition]
```

### 4.2 CI Engine Combustion — Three-Phase Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start of Injection SOI] --> B[Phase 1 - Ignition Delay Period]
    B --> C[Fuel Accumulates, Atomizes, Vaporizes]
    C --> D[AUTO-IGNITION of End-Gas]
    D --> E[Phase 2 - Rapid Premixed Combustion]
    E --> F[Steep Pressure Rise - dp/dtheta HIGH]
    F --> G[Phase 3 - Diffusion Controlled Burning]
    G --> H[Slow Mixing-Controlled Combustion]
    H --> I[End of Combustion - EOC]

    B -.->|Delay Too Long| J[Excessive Premixed Fuel]
    J -.->|Sudden Burn| K[CI Engine KNOCK]
    E -.->|Normal| G
```

### 4.3 First-Law Energy Balance — Combustion Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Energy In - m_f x CV] --> B[Combustion Chamber]
    C[Air Mass Energy - m_air x h_air] --> B
    B --> D[Work Output - W_net]
    B --> E[Exhaust Energy Loss - Q_exh]
    B --> F[Coolant Heat Loss - Q_cool]
    B --> G[Unburned Fuel Loss - Q_unburned]
    B --> H[Radiation and Convection Loss - Q_rad]

    A --> I[Energy Released Q_released]
    C --> I
    I --> B
```

### 4.4 Mass-Burned Fraction Curve (MFBR Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Time or Crank Angle theta] --> B[Mass Fraction Burned xb]
    B --> C[Stage 1 - Slow Initial Burn 0 to 5 percent]
    B --> D[Stage 2 - Rapid Rise 5 to 90 percent]
    B --> E[Stage 3 - Tail Burn 90 to 100 percent]

    F[5 percent burned - CA5] --> G[50 percent burned - CA50]
    G --> H[90 percent burned - CA90]

    I[dp/dtheta MAX] -.->|At CA50 approx| G
```

### 4.5 SI Engine Knock — Causal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spark Plug Fires] --> B[Flame Front Propagates]
    B --> C[End-Gas Compressed and Heated]
    C --> D{Temp Exceeds Auto-Ignition T ?}
    D -->|No| E[Smooth Combustion - No Knock]
    D -->|Yes| F[Spontaneous Auto-Ignition of End-Gas]
    F --> G[High-Frequency Pressure Waves]
    G --> H[Audible PING Knock]
    G --> I[Possible Piston Damage]
```

### 4.6 Comparative Architecture: SI vs CI Combustion Systems

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SI[S1 Engine Combustion]
        SI1[Carburettor or Port Injection] --> SI2[Premixed Homogeneous Charge]

    end

    subgraph CI[C1 Engine Combustion]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**"Distinguish between 'Normal Combustion' and 'Knock' in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Normal Combustion | Knock |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Smooth, controlled propagation of flame front from spark kernel outward | Spontaneous, uncontrolled auto-ignition of unburned end-gas ahead of flame front |
| **Pressure trace** | Smooth, rounded peak | Sharp, high-frequency oscillations superimposed |
| **Flame propagation** | Quasi-spherical at 15–30 m/s | Volumetric explosion (very high speed, near sound) |
| **Audible indicator** | Quiet operation | Metallic "ping" or "knock" sound |
| **Cause** | Proper spark timing, sufficient octane | End-gas exceeds auto-ignition temperature |
| **Remedy** | — | Retard spark, use higher-octane fuel, lower compression ratio |

**Self-contained 3-mark statement (write in exam):**

> Normal combustion in an SI engine proceeds as a smooth, controlled propagation of a flame front originating from the spark plug. Knock, in contrast, is the spontaneous auto-ignition of the unburned end-gas ahead of the flame front, producing sharp high-frequency pressure oscillations and a characteristic metallic sound. (3 marks)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
**"What is meant by 'Ignition Delay Period' in a Compression Ignition engine? List any four factors affecting it." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The ignition delay period in a CI engine is the time interval (or crank-angle interval) between the start of fuel injection and the **start of rapid pressure rise** due to combustion. During this period, the fuel is atomised, vaporised, mixed with hot compressed air, and undergoes pre-flame chemical reactions until auto-ignition occurs.

**Four factors affecting delay period (1 mark):**

1. **Compressed air temperature** — ↑ temperature → ↓ delay (Arrhenius kinetics)
2. **Compressed air pressure** — ↑ pressure → ↓ delay (more molecular collisions)
3. **Fuel cetane number** — ↑ cetane → ↓ delay (lower activation energy)
4. **Engine speed** — ↑ speed → slight ↓ in delay (less wall heat loss time)

(Alternative valid factors: compression ratio, intake temperature, EGR rate, fuel volatility.)

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain with a clear P-θ diagram the **three stages of combustion in a Spark Ignition engine**. Discuss how flame speed and cylinder geometry influence each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A four-cylinder, four-stroke SI engine runs at 3000 rpm and develops 75 kW brake power. The BSFC is 0.32 kg/kWh. Using iso-octane (C₈H₁₈) with 10% excess air, calculate:
   - (i) Stoichiometric AFR
   - (ii) Actual AFR
   - (iii) Mass flow rate of fuel (kg/h)
   - (iv) Mass flow rate of air (kg/h) **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Three Stages of SI Combustion (Refer to Mermaid block 4.1):**

**Stage 1 — Ignition Lag (1 mark):**
- Starts at spark discharge; lasts until ~5–10% mass burned.
- Duration ≈ 0–0.002 s.
- A small ignition kernel forms at the spark plug gap and grows into a quasi-spherical flame front.
- **Why it matters:** Mass-burned rate is slow initially because the kernel surface area is small.

**Stage 2 — Rapid / Main Combustion (2 marks):**
- Begins at ~5–10% mass burned, ends at ~85–95% mass burned.
- Pressure rises sharply; this is the phase doing useful work.
- **Flame speed effect:** Higher flame speed (better mixture motion, higher turbulence) → shorter Stage 2 → higher rate of pressure rise → more power.
- **Cylinder geometry effect:** Compact, central spark-plug locations (e.g., Heron chamber) shorten the flame travel distance → uniform propagation → less knock tendency.

**Stage 3 — Afterburning (1 mark):**
- Last 5–15% of mass burned, slow diffusion-controlled phase.
- Remaining fuel pockets in crevices (squish, ring-land, head gasket) burn slowly.

**P-θ Diagram (2 marks):**

```
P
|
|        ___
|       /   \___
|      /        \_____
|     /               \____
|    /                     \____
|___/                            \__________ Motoring
|   ↑
|  Spark
+----------> θ
   ATDC
```

- The smooth pressure peak above motoring represents combustion work.
- Knock shows as a sharp spike near peak pressure.
- Surface ignition shows as abnormal pressure rise before/after spark.

**Influence of flame speed and geometry (1 mark):** Higher flame speed + compact chamber = faster, more complete combustion → higher efficiency, less knock risk.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- C₈H₁₈ (iso-octane, x=8, y=18, M=114)
- $P_b = 75$ kW; BSFC = 0.32 kg/kWh; $N = 3000$ rpm; **10% excess air**

**Step (i) — Stoichiometric AFR (2 marks):**

$$\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \frac{137.9 \cdot (x + y/4)}{12x + y} = \frac{137.9 \cdot (8 + 4.5)}{114} = \frac{137.9 \cdot 12.5}{114}$$

$$\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \frac{1723.75}{114} = 15.12 \, \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step (ii) — Actual AFR (1 mark):**

With 10% excess air, $\lambda = 1.10$:
$$\text{AFR}_{act} = \lambda \cdot \text{AFR}_{stoich} = 1.10 \times 15.12 = 16.63 \, \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step (iii) — Mass flow rate of fuel (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m}_f = \text{BSFC} \times P_b = 0.32 \times 75 = 24 \, \text{kg/h}$$

**Step (iv) — Mass flow rate of air (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{AFR}_{act} = 24 \times 16.63 = 399.1 \, \text{kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{air} \approx 399.1 \, \text{kg/h}}$$

---

#### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **three phases of combustion in a Compression Ignition engine** with the help of a pressure-crank angle (P-θ) diagram. Discuss how the delay period affects engine roughness. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The combustion of methane (CH₄) is carried out with 30% excess air. Calculate:
   - (i) The stoichiometric air required per kg of methane
   - (ii) The actual air-fuel ratio
   - (iii) The equivalence ratio
   - (iv) The volumetric analysis of the wet flue gas (per kg of fuel) **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Three Phases of CI Combustion (Refer to Mermaid block 4.2):**

**Phase 1 — Ignition Delay Period (2 marks):**
- Interval from start of injection (SOI) to start of rapid pressure rise.
- During this period: fuel atomisation → vaporisation → mixing with hot air → pre-flame reactions → auto-ignition.
- Duration decreases with increasing compression ratio, inlet temperature, cetane number; increases with EGR.

**Phase 2 — Rapid / Premixed Combustion (2 marks):**
- All fuel accumulated in Phase 1 burns nearly simultaneously.
- Pressure rises steeply; high *dp/dθ* causes audible knock.
- Controlled primarily by **fuel chemistry**, not by mixing.

**Phase 3 — Diffusion-Controlled Combustion (1 mark):**
- After injected fuel has been consumed, remaining combustion is governed by fuel-air mixing rate.
- Pressure nearly follows the **motoring curve**.

**P-θ Diagram (1 mark):**

```
P
|
|     /\
|    /  \  ____
|   /    \/
|  /      \  <- Rapid phase (steep rise)
| /        \_____ <- Diffusion (mixing controlled)
|/                \_________
+----------------+----------------------→ θ
       Injection     TDC
       start
```

**Delay period and engine roughness (1 mark):**
- Longer delay → more accumulated fuel → bigger rapid-combustion spike → louder knock → engine roughness.
- Roughness is mitigated by: high cetane fuels, high injection pressure (better atomisation), combustion chamber design (e.g., bowl-in-piston), retarded injection timing.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** CH₄ (x=1, y=4, M=16); 30% excess air.

**Step (i) — Stoichiometric air per kg fuel (2 marks):**

$$\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \frac{137.9 \cdot (1 + 1)}{16} = \frac{275.8}{16} = 17.24 \, \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step (ii) — Actual AFR (1.5 marks):**

$$\lambda = 1.30; \quad \text{AFR}_{act} = 1.30 \times 17.24 = 22.41 \, \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step (iii) — Equivalence ratio (1 mark):**

$$\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.30} = 0.769$$

**Step (iv) — Volumetric analysis of flue gas (2.5 marks):**

Combustion equation with 30% excess air (per mol CH₄ = 16 g; we work per kg, so basis = 1000/16 = 62.5 mol CH₄):

Per mol CH₄: theoretical O₂ = 2 mol; with 30% excess = 2.6 mol O₂ supplied, of which 2 mol consumed; excess O₂ = 0.6 mol. N₂ brought in = 2.6 × 3.76 = 9.776 mol.

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2.6\,\text{O}_2 + 9.776\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 0.6\,\text{O}_2 + 9.776\,\text{N}_2$$

Total products per mol = 1 + 2 + 0.6 + 9.776 = 13.376 mol.

Volume percentages (since volume = mole × 22.4 at same T,P):

| Component | Moles | Volume % |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 1.0 | 7.48% |
| H₂O | 2.0 | 14.95% |
| O₂ | 0.6 | 4.49% |
| N₂ | 9.776 | 73.08% |
| **Total** | **13.376** | **100%** |

Per kg of CH₄: multiply moles by 62.5 — the **proportions remain unchanged**. So flue gas composition = CO₂ 7.48%, H₂O 14.95%, O₂ 4.49%, N₂ 73.08% (wet basis).

$$\boxed{\text{Wet Flue Gas Composition (vol \%) : CO}_2 = 7.48\%, \text{H}_2\text{O} = 14.95\%, \text{O}_2 = 4.49\%, \text{N}_2 = 73.08\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing air-fuel ratio and fuel-air ratio** — always state clearly which one. AFR = air/fuel; FAR = fuel/air = 1/AFR.
> 2. **Excess air vs. equivalence ratio** — mixing up leads to wrong answers. Remember: $\lambda = 1/\phi$ (excess air factor is the inverse of equivalence ratio).
> 3. **Wet vs. Dry flue gas** — if the question says "dry," you must subtract H₂O from both numerator and denominator.
> 4. **Energy balance questions** — students often forget to account for sensible heat of inlet air. Always write $Q_{in} = m_f \cdot \text{CV} + m_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot T_{air}$.
> 5. **Delay period units** — if asked for *time*, convert $\Delta\theta_d$ using $\Delta t = \Delta\theta_d \cdot 60/(N \cdot 360)$ for a four-stroke. Show this step explicitly.
> 6. **Cylinder count** — in part (b) of A-type, students confuse kW per cylinder vs. total; read carefully whether the question gives total or per-cylinder brake power.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Combustion in SI & CI Engines**

**🔹 SI Engine Combustion:**
- ☐ Three stages: **Ignition Lag → Propagation → Afterburning**
- ☐ Spark initiates kernel → flame propagates at **15–30 m/s**
- ☐ **Knock** = end-gas auto-ignition (NOT flame speed increase)
- ☐ **Surface ignition** = combustion initiated by hot spot (not spark plug)
- ☐ **Octane number** measures knock resistance (↑ = better)
- ☐ Retarded spark, lower compression ratio, lean mixture → reduce knock
- ☐ MFB curve — CA50 is the centre of combustion (key performance metric)
- ☐ Wiebe function: $x_b = 1 - \exp[-a((\theta-\theta_0)/\Delta\theta_b)^{m+1}]$

**🔹 CI Engine Combustion:**
- ☐ Three phases: **Ignition Delay → Rapid Premixed → Diffusion Controlled**
- ☐ Delay period governed by **Arrhenius kinetics**: $\Delta\theta_d = K \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp(E_a/\bar{R}T)$
- ☐ Long delay → excessive premixed fuel → **diesel knock** (rough combustion)
- ☐ **Cetane number** measures auto-ignition quality (↑ = shorter delay)
- ☐ Injection timing, rate shaping, and pressure are key control parameters

**🔹 Thermodynamic Analysis:**
- ☐ Stoichiometric combustion: $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + (x + y/4)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + (y/2)\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- ☐ Stoichiometric AFR formula: $\dfrac{137.9(x + y/4)}{12x + y}$
- ☐ Equivalence ratio $\phi = (\text{F/A})_{act}/(\text{F/A})_{stoich}$; $\lambda = 1/\phi$
- ☐ Adiabatic flame temperature from energy balance: $T_{ad} = T_i + m_f \cdot \text{CV}/(m_p \cdot c_p)$
- ☐ First Law for engine cycle: $Q_{in} - W_{net} = \sum m_e h_e - \sum m_i h_i$
- ☐ Combustion efficiency: $\eta_{comb} = Q_{released}/(m_f \cdot \text{CV})$
- ☐ Air = 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ (mole); 23.2% O₂ + 76.8% N₂ (mass); 4.76 mol air per mol O₂

**🔹 Comparison Hooks (Memory Aids):**
- ☐ SI = Spark = Stoichiometric = Octane (SSO)
- ☐ CI = Compression = Continuously lean = Cetane (CCC)
- ☐ SI is "pre-mixed fast flame"; CI is "diffusion slow mixing flame."

**🔹 Common Exam Mistakes:**
- ☐ Forgetting to convert BSFC × Power = fuel rate per hour (not per second)
- ☐ Using molecular mass in g/mol vs. kg/kmol inconsistently
- ☐ Misinterpreting "excess air" — 30% excess means $\lambda = 1.30$, not 0.30
- ☐ Forgetting to multiply by cylinder count or stroke factor when needed
- ☐ Mixing up air-fuel ratio (AFR) and fuel-air ratio (FAR)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Structure and Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flame Structure and Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Premixed Flame?

A **premixed flame** is a self-sustaining combustion wave that propagates through a homogeneous mixture of fuel vapour and oxidiser, liberating heat at a thin reaction zone. In the context of Spark-Ignition (SI) engines, this wave originates from the spark plug and travels through the combustible charge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Zel'dovich–Frank–Kamenetskii, 1938):**
> A *laminar premixed flame* is a steady, one-dimensional, planar, adiabatic wave in which a balance exists between **heat conduction upstream from the reaction zone** and **diffusion of reactants into it**, while the mass of burned gases flows downstream.

## 1.2 The Three Zones of a Premixed Flame

A 1-D adiabatic premixed flame has three distinct thermal/chemical regions:

| Zone | Temperature Range | Dominant Physics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unburned / Reactant Zone** | $T_{u}$ (≈ 300–700 K) | Cold charge, no chemistry |
| **Preheat Zone** | $T_{u} \rightarrow T_{i}$ | Conduction heating, mild fuel cracking |
| **Reaction Zone** | $T_{i} \rightarrow T_{b}$ | Radical recombination, rapid energy release |
| **Post-Flame / Burned Zone** | $T_{b}$ (≈ 2000–2500 K) | Equilibrium products, dissociation |

> [!NOTE]
> The **reaction zone thickness ($\delta_{r}$)** is typically **$\delta_{r} \approx 0.1$ – $1\,\text{mm}$** for hydrocarbon-air flames, while the **thermal preheat thickness ($\delta_{th}$)** is **$\delta_{th} \approx 0.5$ – $2\,\text{mm}$**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Ignition Paper Trail"

Imagine you set fire to one end of a tightly rolled newspaper:

- The **unburned end** = the reactant zone (paper is intact).
- The **smoldering ring right ahead of the flame** = the preheat zone (heat conductively drying the paper).
- The **bright glowing band** = the reaction zone (paper actually burning).
- The **charred section behind** = the post-flame zone (combustion products).

The flame front advances because the heat released in the glowing band is conducted forward and ignites the next layer. *The same principle governs every SI-engine flame kernel expanding from the spark.*

## 1.4 Laminar Burning Velocity – The Cardinal Quantity

The **Laminar Burning Velocity ($S_{L}$)** is the most important single parameter characterising a premixture. It is *not* the speed of the gas molecules—it is the speed at which the flame front moves **relative to the unburned gas ahead of it**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric – Stoichiometric Methane–Air at 1 atm, 300 K:**
> $$S_{L} \approx 0.38\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$$
> Stoichiometric iso-octane–air: $S_{L} \approx 0.27\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters in SI engines:** The flame speed controls the *mass-burn rate*, which in turn governs the **rate of pressure rise**, **heat-release rate (HRR)**, **cycle-to-cycle variability (COV of IMEP)**, and the **tendency for end-gas auto-ignition (knock)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inside the Flame – What Each Zone Physically Does

### (a) Preheat Zone
- Energy balance dominated by **conduction** from the reaction zone.
- $T$ rises monotonically; species concentrations essentially unchanged.
- Reactant temperature climbs to the **ignition temperature $T_{i}$**.
- Governing PDE (1-D, steady, no chemistry):
  $$\rho_{u} c_{p,u} S_{L} \dfrac{dT}{dx} = \dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\lambda_{u}\dfrac{dT}{dx}\right)$$

### (b) Reaction Zone
- Here the temperature shoots up from $T_{i}$ to the **adiabatic flame temperature $T_{b}$**.
- Radical pool ($\text{H}^{\bullet}, \text{OH}^{\bullet}, \text{O}^{\bullet}, \text{CH}_{3}^{\bullet}$) drives chain-branching.
- Heat generation rate $\dot{q}'' = -\Delta h_{c}\, \dot{\omega}_{F}$ where $\dot{\omega}_{F}$ is the volumetric fuel-consumption rate.
- Approximated as **infinitely thin** in the **thermal theory of Mallard–Le Chatelier**.

### (c) Post-Flame Zone
- Chemical equilibrium reached; only **dissociation reactions** of $\text{CO}_{2}$, $\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$, $\text{NO}$ remain.
- Temperature $T_{b}$ corresponds to the **adiabatic equilibrium temperature** $T_{ad}$ at constant pressure.

## 2.2 The Zel'dovich Number and the Role of Activation Energy

A dimensionless number captures how "sensitive" the chemistry is:

$$\beta \;=\; \frac{E_{a}}{R\,T_{b}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For hydrocarbon–air flames, $\beta \approx 8$ – $15$ (the **large-activation-energy asymptote**). This single assumption allows the entire flame structure to be solved analytically.

When $\beta \gg 1$:
- Reaction is **negligible** for $T < T_{b} - \Delta T$.
- Reaction is **concentrated** in a thin slab at the back of the preheat zone.
- Classic asymptote by **Zel'dovich & Frank–Kamenetskii (1938)** applies.

## 2.3 Factors Affecting Laminar Burning Velocity

| # | Factor | Trend on $S_{L}$ | Physical Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Equivalence ratio $\phi$** | Peaks at $\phi \approx 1.05$–$1.15$ | Maximum reaction rate + high $T_{b}$ |
| 2 | **Unburned temperature $T_{u}$** | $S_{L} \propto T_{u}^{\alpha}$, $\alpha \approx 2$ | $\exp(-E/RT_{u})$ sensitivity |
| 3 | **Pressure $p$** | $S_{L} \propto p^{-0.2}$ to $p^{-0.5}$ | Three-body termination steps |
| 4 | **Dilution (EGR)** | Monotonically reduces $S_{L}$ | Lower $T_{b}$ and reactant partial pressure |
| 5 | **Turbulence intensity $u'$** | Increases *apparent* $S_{T}$ | Wrinkled/fluted surface area |
| 6 | **Fuel molecular structure** | Smaller alkanes $>$ larger alkanes | $\text{H}_{2} > \text{CH}_{4} > \text{C}_{2}\text{H}_{6} > \text{C}_{8}\text{H}_{18}$ |
| 7 | **Radiation losses** | Marginal in laminar case | Small $\delta$ at engine pressures |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Memorise This)

| # | Symbol | Definition / Expression | Typical Units / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S_{L}$ | Laminar burning velocity relative to unburned gas | $\text{m·s}^{-1}$, $0.2$–$0.5$ (HC-air) |
| 2 | $\delta_{L}$ | Laminar flame thermal thickness $\delta_{L} = (T_{b}-T_{u}) \big/ \vert dT/dx \vert_{max}$ | $\approx 0.5$–$1.5\,\text{mm}$ |
| 3 | $\beta$ | Zel'dovich number $E_{a}/(R\,T_{b})$ | $8$–$15$ |
| 4 | $\alpha_{th}$ | Thermal diffusivity $\lambda / (\rho_{u} c_{p})$ | $\approx 2\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $D_{th}$ | Thermal diffusivity $D_{th} = \lambda/(\rho c_{p})$ | $\approx 2\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $\tau_{chem}$ | Characteristic chemical time $1/[A\,e^{-E_{a}/RT_{b}}]$ | $\mu\text{s}$–$\text{ms}$ |
| 7 | $\text{Da}$ | Damköhler number $\tau_{\text{flow}}/\tau_{\text{chem}}$ | $\gg 1$ engine-relevant |
| 8 | $K_{a}$ | Karlovitz number $\tau_{\text{chem}}/\tau_{\text{Kolmogorov}}$ | $0.1$ – $10$ in engines |
| 9 | $\text{Le}$ | Lewis number $\alpha_{th}/\mathcal{D}_{F}$ | $\approx 1$ for most HC-air |
| 10 | $u'$ | RMS turbulent velocity fluctuation | $1$–$5\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$ in engine |
| 11 | $l_{t}$ | Integral turbulence length scale | $1$–$3\,\text{mm}$ |
| 12 | $\eta_{K}$ | Kolmogorov micro-scale $(\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$ | $\approx 30$–$100\,\mu\text{m}$ |
| 13 | $S_{T}$ | Turbulent burning velocity (wrinkled-sheet) | $1$–$10\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$ in engine |
| 14 | $u_{b}$ | Burned-gas velocity $\approx S_{L}\cdot(\rho_{u}/\rho_{b})$ | $4$–$8 \times S_{L}$ |
| 15 | $\Delta t_{b}$ | Mass-burn duration (10–90 %) | $30$–$70^{\circ}\text{CA}$ (SI engines) |

> [!TIP]
> **Absolute-value warning:** Whenever you write magnitude in the cheat sheet, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ rather than `\vert` *inside* a table cell — write it as `\left\vert \cdot \right\vert` in display mode, or as the word **magnitude** in plain text within rows.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Engine calibration:** Ignition-timing optimisation relies on accurate $S_{L}(\phi,T_{u},p)$ maps to keep **CA50 ≈ 8–12 °ATDC** for best BSFC.
2. **Knock prediction:** Auto-ignition chemistry uses the **Zel'dovich mechanism** for $\text{NO}_{x}$ and **LLNL/Caltech** reduced mechanisms for end-gas.
3. **CFD combustion modelling:** LES and RANS use $S_{L}$ as the closure for **G-equation**, **Flamelet-Generated Manifold (FGM)**, or **TACOT** models.
4. **HCCI / RCCI:** Without flame propagation, $S_{L}$ loses meaning; the transition from premixed → auto-ignition regime is the very heart of KTU Module-2 topics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Laminar Burning Velocity by Thermal Theory (Mallard–Le Chatelier, Simplified)

We treat the flame as **stationary** in the lab frame by attaching a coordinate system moving with the flame. The unburned gas enters at velocity $S_{L}$, exits as burned gas at velocity $S_{b}$ (from continuity: $S_{b} = S_{L}\,\rho_{u}/\rho_{b}$).

**Governing energy equation** (steady, 1-D, equal diffusivities, adiabatic):

$$\rho_{u}\,c_{p}\,S_{L}\,\frac{dT}{dx} \;=\; \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\lambda\,\frac{dT}{dx}\right) \;+\; \dot{\omega}_{F}\,\Delta h_{c}$$

where $\dot{\omega}_{F}$ is the molar fuel consumption rate (negative, since fuel is destroyed) and $\Delta h_{c}$ is the lower heating value per mole of fuel.

**Step 1 — Apply the large-activation-energy asymptote.**

Assume the reaction is confined to a sheet of negligible thickness $\delta_{r} \to 0$. Integrate the energy equation across this sheet:

$$\rho_{u}\,c_{p}\,S_{L}\,(T_{b}-T_{u}) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \dot{\omega}_{F}\,\Delta h_{c}\,dx$$

**Step 2 — Use the Burke–Schumann / Chapman–Jouguet substitution.**

Define a single representative reaction time $\tau_{r}$ at $T \approx T_{b}$:

$$\dot{\omega}_{F}\,\Delta h_{c} \;\approx\; \rho_{u}\,c_{p}\,(T_{b}-T_{u})\;\frac{1}{\tau_{r}}\;e^{\beta(1-T_{b}/T)}$$

In the **high-activation limit** the exponential function collapses the integrand into a sharply peaked term of width $T_{b}/\beta$. Evaluating the integral by the method of steepest descent gives:

$$\int e^{\beta(1-T_{b}/T)}\,dT \;\approx\; \frac{T_{b}}{\beta}\,e^{-\beta/2}\, \sqrt{\frac{2\pi}{\beta}}\;\cdot\;\text{(Jacobian)}$$

The standard textbook result (Williams, *Combustion Theory*) reduces to:

$$\boxed{\;S_{L}^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\alpha_{th}}{\tau_{r}}\,\beta^{2}\,e^{-\beta}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Express $\tau_{r}$.**

Using an Arrhenius rate $\dot{\omega}_{F} = A\,[F]^{n}[O_{2}]^{m}\,\exp(-E_{a}/RT)$ evaluated at $T_{b}$:

$$\tau_{r} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_{u}\,c_{p}\,(T_{b}-T_{u})}{A\,[F]^{n}[O_{2}]^{m}\,\Delta h_{c}}\,e^{\beta}$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\boxed{\;S_{L}^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\alpha_{th}\,A\,[F]^{n}[O_{2}]^{m}\,\Delta h_{c}}{\rho_{u}\,c_{p}\,(T_{b}-T_{u})}\,\beta^{2}\,e^{-\beta}\,e^{\beta}\;=\; \dfrac{2\,\alpha_{th}}{\tau_{r}}\,\beta^{2}\,e^{-\beta}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Thermal flame thickness.**

By definition:

$$\delta_{L} \;=\; \frac{T_{b}-T_{u}}{\big(dT/dx\big)_{\max}} \;\approx\; \frac{\alpha_{th}}{S_{L}}$$

Substituting the $S_{L}$ expression:

$$\delta_{L} \;\approx\; \frac{T_{b}-T_{u}}{T_{b}}\;\frac{\delta_{r}}{\beta} \;\sim\; \frac{\alpha_{th}}{S_{L}}$$

The final closed-form expression for $S_{L}$ is the **Frank–Kamenetskii result**:

$$\boxed{\;S_{L} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,\lambda_{u}}{\rho_{u}^{2}\,c_{p}^{2}\,(T_{b}-T_{u})^{2}}\;\int_{T_{u}}^{T_{b}}\!\!\!\int_{T}^{T_{b}}\dot{\omega}_{F}(T')\,\Delta h_{c}\;dT'\,dT}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's key:** Notice that the entire flame is governed by **two timescales** — the chemical time $\tau_{r}$ and the diffusion time $\delta_{L}^{2}/\alpha_{th}$. Equating them produces the speed of propagation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Damköhler Analysis for Turbulent Flame Speed

Damköhler (1940) considered two limiting regimes using **integral turbulence length scale $l_{t}$** and **Kolmogorov micro-scale $\eta_{K}$**.

**Step 1 — Define the Damköhler number.**

$$\text{Da} \;=\; \frac{\tau_{\text{flow}}}{\tau_{\text{chem}}} \;=\; \frac{l_{t}/u'}{l_{t}/S_{L}} \;=\; \frac{S_{L}}{u'}$$

Inverse Damköhler: $u'/S_{L}$.

**Step 2 — Regime classification.**

| Regime | Condition | Reaction-Zone Physics | $S_{T}/S_{L}$ scaling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **I — Wrinkled flamelets** | $\eta_{K} \gg \delta_{L}$ | Reaction zone inside thin laminar sheet | $S_{T}/S_{L} \sim u'/S_{L}$ |
| **II — Distributed reaction** | $\eta_{K} \ll \delta_{L}$ | Reactions throughout eddies | $S_{T}/S_{L} \sim (u'\,\delta_{L}/\alpha_{th})^{1/2}$ |

**Step 3 — Wrinkled-sheet regime derivation.**

Assume a planar laminar flame element of surface area $\Sigma_{0}$ gets wrinkled into an enlarged surface $\Sigma$ by turbulence:

$$\frac{S_{T}}{S_{L}} \;=\; \frac{\Sigma}{\Sigma_{0}}$$

For isotropic turbulence with integral scale $l_{t}$ and rms velocity $u'$:

$$\frac{d\Sigma}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{u'}{l_{t}}\;\Sigma \;\Rightarrow\; \Sigma(t) \;=\; \Sigma_{0}\,e^{u't/l_{t}}$$

Convolving the curvature over the eddy turnover time $l_{t}/u'$ gives the effective area ratio:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{S_{T}}{S_{L}} \;=\; 1 + \frac{u'}{S_{L}}\;}\quad \text{(metgalkeck low-turbulence limit, 1980)}$$

**Step 4 — High-turbulence correction (Gülder, 1990).**

Using a fractal surface concept $A_{r}/A_{0} = (l_{t}/\eta_{K})^{D-2}$ with $D$ the fractal dimension:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{S_{T}}{S_{L}} \;=\; 1 + 0.62\!\left(\frac{u'}{S_{L}}\right)^{1/2}\,\text{Re}_{t}^{1/4}\;}$$

where $\text{Re}_{t} = u'\,l_{t}/\nu$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Metgalchi–Keck Correlation (KTU Favourite)

For engine conditions (moderate-to-high turbulence, $0.5 < u'/S_{L} < 5$):

$$\boxed{\;S_{T} \;=\; S_{L} + u' \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{u'}{S_{L}}\right)\right]\;}$$

**Derivation outline.**

Start with the Wrinkled-flamelet expression: $S_{T}/S_{L} = 1 + u'/S_{L}$. Apply a saturation correction when $u' \to \infty$ because the reaction zones cannot respond infinitely fast — chemical relaxation time $\tau_{chem}$ imposes a finite upper limit. Multiply by a saturation function $\sigma = 1 - \exp(-u'/S_{L})$:

$$S_{T} \;=\; S_{L} + u'\,\sigma \;=\; S_{L} + u'\left[1 - e^{-u'/S_{L}}\right]$$

**Limiting cases:**
- $u' \ll S_{L}$: $S_{T} \approx S_{L} + u'$ (linear)
- $u' \gg S_{L}$: $S_{T} \approx S_{L} + u' \to \infty$ asymptotically bounded by extinction chemistry.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem.** Stoichiometric methane–air mixture at 1 atm, 300 K, equivalence ratio $\phi = 1.05$. Given: $S_{L} = 0.40\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$, $u' = 2.5\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$, $l_{t} = 1.2\,\text{mm}$, $\nu = 1.5\times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{s}^{-1}$. Compute the turbulent burning velocity using Metgalchi–Keck and Gülder correlations.

**Solution — Metgalchi–Keck.**

$$S_{T} \;=\; 0.40 + 2.5\left[1 - e^{-2.5/0.40}\right]$$

$$e^{-6.25} \;=\; 0.00193 \;\Rightarrow\; S_{T} \;=\; 0.40 + 2.5\,(0.998) \;=\; 2.90\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$$

**Solution — Gülder.**

$$\text{Re}_{t} \;=\; \frac{u'\,l_{t}}{\nu} \;=\; \frac{2.5\times 1.2\times 10^{-3}}{1.5\times 10^{-5}} \;=\; 200$$

$$\frac{S_{T}}{S_{L}} \;=\; 1 + 0.62\,(6.25)^{1/2}\,(200)^{1/4} \;=\; 1 + 0.62 \times 2.50 \times 3.76 \;=\; 6.83$$

$$S_{T} \;=\; 6.83\times 0.40 \;=\; 2.73\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$$

Both methods agree within 6 %, demonstrating robustness of the **Gülder exponent**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Premixed Flame Structure — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This Mermaid block replaces a hand-drawn T–x profile. Each node describes one thermal/chemical function rather than a physical coordinate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1(["Cold Unburned Mixture  rho_u  T_u"])
    zone1["PREHEAT ZONE  T_rises  conduction_dominated"]
    zone2["REACTION ZONE  T_i to T_b  radical chemistry"]
    zone3["POST-FLAME ZONE  T_b  equilibrium products"]
    out1(["Burned Gas Products  rho_b  T_b"])

    in1 -- "S_L  relative velocity" --> zone1
    zone1 -- "T reaches T_i" --> zone2
    zone2 -- "exothermic jump" --> zone3
    zone3 -- "S_b = S_L rho_u/rho_b" --> out1

    zone1 -. "delta_th approx 1 mm" .-> zone1
    zone2 -. "delta_r approx 0.5 mm" .-> zone2
    zone3 -. "dissociation only" .-> zone3
```

## 4.2 Flame Propagation Inside an SI Engine Cylinder

The block diagram below tracks the **life-cycle of the SI flame kernel** from spark to end of combustion.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    spark(["Spark Discharge  E_gap approx 30 mJ"])
    ker["Kernel Growth  d approximately 1 to 2 mm  spherical laminar"]
    prop["Laminar Propagation  S_L  approx 0.3 to 0.5 m per s"]
    turb["Turbulent Propagation  S_T  approx 2 to 5 m per s"]
    wall["Wall Quenching  quench_layer approx 0.5 mm"]
    endg["End-Gas Region  pressure and T rise"]
    knock(["Knock or Normal Combustion"])

    spark --> ker
    ker --> prop
    prop --> turb
    turb --> wall
    turb --> endg
    endg --> knock
```

## 4.3 Turbulence–Chemistry Interaction Regime Map

Mapping **Karlovitz number $K_{a}$** against **Damköhler number $\text{Da}$** locates the combustion regime.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axD["Damköhler  Da"]
    axK["Karlovitz  Ka"]
    regA["Regime A  Wrinkled Flamelets  Ka less than 1  Da greater than 1"]
    regB["Regime B  Thin Reaction Zones  Ka approx 1"]
    regC["Regime C  Broken Reaction Zones  Ka greater than 1"]
    regD["Regime D  Distributed Reaction  Da less than 1"]

    axD --> regA
    axD --> regB
    axD --> regC
    axD --> regD
    axK --> regA
    axK --> regB
    axK --> regC
    axK --> regD
```

> [!TIP]
> **Borghi–Peters diagram connection:** Most SI engines operate in **Regime A → B** during the main combustion phase (30–60 °CA ATDC). HCCI/RCCI move towards **Regime C–D** because the reaction zone thickness grows with residence time.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define laminar burning velocity and explain how it differs from the absolute flame-front velocity measured in the laboratory frame.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Laminar burning velocity ($S_{L}$)** is the speed at which a planar, adiabatic, 1-D premixed flame propagates *relative to the unburned gas*.
- The **absolute flame-front velocity in the lab** $S_{abs} = S_{L} + u_{u}$ (where $u_{u}$ is the unburned-gas velocity toward the flame).
- The **burned gas leaves** the flame at $S_{b} = S_{L}\,\rho_{u}/\rho_{b}$ (continuity).
- **Distinction (3 marks):** $S_{L}$ is an *intrinsic thermo-chemical property* of the mixture; $S_{abs}$ depends on external flow, blow-off, etc. For a quiescent laboratory flame, $S_{abs} \approx S_{L}$.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**State the three thermal zones of a premixed flame and indicate which zone controls the propagation speed.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Preheat zone** — conduction dominates; $T: T_{u} \to T_{i}$.
2. **Reaction zone** — chemistry dominates; $T: T_{i} \to T_{b}$.
3. **Post-flame (burned) zone** — equilibrium; $T \approx T_{b}$.
- **Controller:** The **reaction zone** controls the propagation speed because it sets the *heat-release rate* which feeds back via conduction to the preheat zone. (Mark allocation: 1 + 1 + 1)

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Full-Mark Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the laminar burning velocity of a premixed flame using the **Frank–Kamenetskii thermal theory**, clearly stating the assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** List and briefly explain **four factors** that influence the laminar burning velocity of hydrocarbon–air mixtures. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Assumptions** (2 marks):
1. Steady, 1-D, planar, adiabatic.
2. Equal diffusivities ($\text{Le} = 1$).
3. Single-step Arrhenius chemistry.
4. Large activation energy ($\beta = E/(RT_{b}) \gg 1$).
5. Constant $\rho c_{p}$ and $\lambda$ across the flame.

**Step 2 — Governing equation** (1 mark):

$$\rho_{u}\,c_{p}\,S_{L}\,\frac{dT}{dx} \;=\; \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\lambda\,\frac{dT}{dx}\right) \;+\; \dot{\omega}_{F}\,\Delta h_{c}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate across the reaction sheet** (2 marks):

$$\rho_{u}\,c_{p}\,S_{L}\,(T_{b}-T_{u}) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\dot{\omega}_{F}\,\Delta h_{c}\;dx$$

**Step 4 — Apply large-$\beta$ asymptote**, leading to the result (2 marks):

$$\boxed{\;S_{L}^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\alpha_{th}}{\tau_{r}}\,\beta^{2}\,e^{-\beta}\;}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks, $4 \times 1.75$)

1. **Equivalence ratio** — $S_{L}$ peaks at $\phi \approx 1.05$–$1.15$ (slightly rich) due to combined effect of $T_{b}$ and $[\text{H}^{\bullet}]$ concentration.
2. **Unburned temperature** — $S_{L} \propto T_{u}^{2}$ (thermal effect on $\exp(-E/RT_{u})$).
3. **Pressure** — $S_{L} \propto p^{-0.2}$ to $p^{-0.5}$ for hydrocarbons (three-body termination).
4. **Turbulence intensity** — Increases the *apparent* turbulent $S_{T}$ by wrinkling the flame surface.
5. **Dilution / EGR** — Reduces $S_{L}$ via lowered $T_{b}$ and partial-pressure effects.
6. **Fuel molecular weight** — Lower alkanes have higher $S_{L}$ due to easier $\text{H}$-atom abstraction.
7. **Radiative heat loss** — Minor at engine-relevant high pressures.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Damköhler regime classification** for premixed turbulent combustion. Discuss the physical meaning of the Damköhler number and the Karlovitz number, and identify the regime applicable to a modern SI engine at top-dead-centre. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Metgalchi–Keck correlation** for turbulent burning velocity. A stoichiometric propane–air mixture has $S_{L} = 0.46\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$. In a spark-ignition engine at TDC, the rms turbulent velocity is $u' = 3.0\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$. Compute the turbulent burning velocity and comment on the enhancement ratio. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **Definitions (2 marks):**
   $$\text{Da} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau_{\text{flow}}}{\tau_{\text{chem}}} \;=\; \dfrac{S_{L}}{u'}$$
   $$K_{a} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau_{\text{chem}}}{\tau_{\text{Kolmogorov}}} \;=\; \delta_{L}\,u'^{2}/\alpha_{th}$$

2. **Regimes (3 marks):**
   - *Regime A — Wrinkled flamelets*: $K_{a} < 1$, $\text{Da} \gg 1$. Reaction zone thinner than Kolmogorov scale. $S_{T}/S_{L} \sim u'/S_{L}$.
   - *Regime B — Thin reaction zones*: $K_{a} \approx 1$. Eddies penetrate reaction zone but combustion still flamelet-like.
   - *Regime C — Broken / Distributed reaction*: $K_{a} > 1$, $\text{Da} \sim 1$. Chemical and turbulent scales comparable.

3. **Engine operation (2 marks):** Modern SI engines at TDC operate predominantly in **Regime A** during the main burn phase. As turbulence decays, the flame slowly relaxes to **Regime B** near end of combustion.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Derivation + Calculation

**Step 1 — Wrinkled-flamelet baseline** (2 marks):

$$\frac{S_{T}}{S_{L}} \;=\; 1 + \frac{u'}{S_{L}}$$

**Step 2 — Saturation correction** (1 mark): Replace the unbounded linear term by a saturation function $\sigma = 1 - \exp(-u'/S_{L})$:

$$S_{T} \;=\; S_{L} + u'\!\left[1 - e^{-u'/S_{L}}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation** (2 marks):

$$u'/S_{L} \;=\; \dfrac{3.0}{0.46} \;=\; 6.52$$

$$e^{-6.52} \;\approx\; 0.00148$$

$$S_{T} \;=\; 0.46 + 3.0\,(1 - 0.00148) \;=\; 0.46 + 2.996 \;=\; 3.456\,\text{m·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Enhancement ratio** (2 marks):

$$\dfrac{S_{T}}{S_{L}} \;=\; \dfrac{3.456}{0.46} \;\approx\; 7.5$$

**Comment:** A **7.5× enhancement** of the burning velocity is consistent with high-turbulence spark-ignition conditions. This corresponds to a flame crossing the bore in **3–5 ms**, which is exactly what is observed experimentally.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the activation-energy term $\beta^{2}e^{-\beta}$** in the $S_{L}$ derivation — examiners *always* allocate 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Confusing $S_{T}/S_{L}$ with $u'/S_{L}$** — the Metgalchi–Keck expression is *not* a pure linear ratio; the saturation exponential matters.
> 3. **Writing $\big\vert x \big\vert$ (with pipes) inside a markdown table** breaks the table render. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or the word *magnitude*.
> 4. **Ignoring Lewis-number effects** when EGR $> 15\,\%$: thermal-diffusive instability accelerates (or decelerates) $S_{L}$ — explicitly mention $\text{Le}$ for full marks.
> 5. **Lab-frame vs unburned-gas-frame confusion** in Part A — examiners love this trap; explicitly write the continuity jump $S_{b} = S_{L}\,\rho_{u}/\rho_{b}$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] Premixed flame has **three zones**: preheat, reaction, post-flame.
- [x] **Laminar burning velocity** is *intrinsic*; lab-frame velocity is $S_{L} + u_{u}$.
- [x] $\beta = E/(RT_{b}) \gg 1$ enables the **Zel'dovich asymptote**.
- [x] $S_{L} \propto T_{u}^{2}$, $\propto p^{-0.2\text{ to }-0.5}$, peaks at $\phi \approx 1.05$–$1.15$.
- [x] Thermal thickness: $\delta_{L} \approx \alpha_{th}/S_{L}$.
- [x] Damköhler regimes A / B / C / D are mapped on the **Borghi–Peters diagram**.
- [x] SI engines operate in **Regime A** at TDC main burn.
- [x] **Metgalchi–Keck:** $S_{T} = S_{L} + u'\!\left[1 - e^{-u'/S_{L}}\right]$.
- [x] **Gülder fractal:** $S_{T}/S_{L} = 1 + 0.62\,(u'/S_{L})^{1/2}\,\text{Re}_{t}^{1/4}$.
- [x] **Mass-burn rate** $\dot{m}_{b} = \rho_{u}\,A_{f}\,S_{L}\,(\Sigma/\Sigma_{0})$ governs HRR.
- [x] **End-gas auto-ignition** competes with flame arrival — knock metric.
- [x] Lewis-number $\text{Le} \neq 1$ causes **preferential diffusion** effects on $S_{L}$.
- [x] In CFD, **G-equation** propagates $G(\mathbf{x},t) = 0$ at $S_{T}|\nabla G| + \partial G/\partial t = 0$.
- [x] Always quote units ($S_{L}$ in **m·s⁻¹**, $\delta_{L}$ in **mm**) when solving numerical problems.
- [x] Use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ (not `\vert`) inside markdown tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cyclic Variations in Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cyclic Variations in Combustion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
