**Cyclic Variations in Combustion** refer to the cycle-to-cycle fluctuations in the combustion parameters of an Internal Combustion (IC) engine, even when the engine is operating under strictly steady-state (time-averaged) boundary conditions. These variations manifest as differences between successive pressure–crank angle ($p$–$\theta$) diagrams, peak cylinder pressures ($p_{max}$), peak pressure rise rates ($dp/d\theta_{max}$), ignition delays, mass fraction burned profiles, and the indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP) from one cycle to the next.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, cyclic variation is the **statistical dispersion of combustion events around their ensemble (cycle-averaged) mean**, characterized by the **Coefficient of Variation (COV)** of IMEP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT752 – Module 2):**
> Cyclic variations are a direct measure of combustion stability. They govern engine roughness, drivability, exhaust emissions (especially HC and CO), and the onset of abnormal combustion (knock / diesel knock).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine a trained musician playing the **same piano note repeatedly** in a quiet room. Although the note is *supposed* to be identical each time, an extremely sensitive microphone would reveal that the loudness and timbre vary ever so slightly from strike to strike. The pianist (engine) intends to be perfectly consistent, but tiny disturbances cause small fluctuations.

In an IC engine, these "disturbances" are:
- Slight unevenness in the **air–fuel mixture** entering the cylinder,
- Random fluctuations in **residual exhaust gas**,
- Variations in **in-cylinder turbulence**,
- Minor drifts in **spark timing** or **fuel injection timing**,
- Slight differences in **charge temperature and density**.

Just as the musician's note is *almost* identical each time, every combustion cycle is *almost* identical — but the tiny differences are critical for performance and emissions.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Mandatory)

The standard statistical metric is the **Coefficient of Variation (COV)** of IMEP, with the following standard limits for **acceptable operation**:

| Engine Type | Acceptable COV of IMEP | Rough Operation Threshold |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Spark Ignition (SI) | **2 – 5 %** | **> 10 %** |
| Compression Ignition (CI) | **1 – 3 %** | **> 5 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Coefficient of Variation (COV):**
> $$ \text{COV}_{\text{IMEP}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{IMEP}}}{\overline{\text{IMEP}}} \times 100\ \% $$
> where $\sigma_{\text{IMEP}}$ is the standard deviation of IMEP over $N$ consecutive cycles, and $\overline{\text{IMEP}}$ is the mean IMEP over the same $N$ cycles. Typically **$N \geq 100$** for statistical reliability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Classification of Cyclic Variations

Cyclic variations can be classified into two engine-family categories:

### A. SI Engine Cyclic Variations
1. **Variations in mixture strength** — non-uniform air–fuel distribution in the cylinder.
2. **Variations in residual gas fraction** — affects flame speed and ignition energy.
3. **Variations in turbulence intensity** — controls flame kernel growth.
4. **Variations in spark timing** — spark plug electrode wear, electrical drift.
5. **Variations in EGR rate** — dilutes charge, slows flame.
6. **Cycle-to-cycle variations in flame propagation speed.**

### B. CI Engine Cyclic Variations
1. **Variations in injection timing** (nozzle needle valve response).
2. **Variations in injection quantity** (mechanical / electronic injector tolerances).
3. **Variations in spray atomization quality** (nozzle hole wear / coking).
4. **Variations in air swirl and squish motion.**
5. **Variations in compression temperature** (wall heat transfer fluctuations).
6. **Cycle-resolved variations in ignition delay** — leads to "diesel knock".

## 2.2 Underlying Physical Causes — Structured Logic Steps

- **Step 1 — Stochastic Mixture Preparation:** In real cylinders, the air–fuel mixture is never perfectly homogeneous. Local equivalence ratio ($\phi$) variations follow a Gaussian distribution around the bulk mean.
- **Step 2 — Turbulence Stochasticity:** Turbulent eddies are random in space and time; the flame surface encounters varying stretch rates, causing local variations in burning velocity ($S_L$).
- **Step 3 — Ignition Variability:** In SI engines, the spark kernel forms with slight position shifts; in CI engines, the auto-ignition chemistry has stochastic induction times.
- **Step 4 — Heat Transfer Stochasticity:** In-cylinder wall temperatures vary slightly with prior cycles, modifying the compression end temperature ($T_c$) and hence the rate of heat release.
- **Step 5 — Statistical Manifestation:** All these micro-stochasticities accumulate into a measurable macro-dispersion in $p_{max}$, $dp/d\theta$, and IMEP.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every high-yield equation** for cyclic variation problems in KTU examinations.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{COV} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100$ | Coefficient of Variation (universal) | **%** |
| 2 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Sample standard deviation (Bessel-corrected) | bar, kW, … |
| 3 | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}x_i$ | Cycle-averaged (ensemble mean) value | same as $x_i$ |
| 4 | $\text{IMEP}_i = \dfrac{1}{V_d}\oint p_i \, dV$ | Cycle-resolved IMEP | bar |
| 5 | $\delta_p = \dfrac{p_{max,i} - \bar{p}_{max}}{\bar{p}_{max}} \times 100$ | Per-cycle peak pressure deviation | **%** |
| 6 | $\delta_{HRR} = \dfrac{(dp/d\theta)_{max,i} - \overline{(dp/d\theta)_{max}}}{\overline{(dp/d\theta)_{max}}} \times 100$ | Per-cycle max PRR deviation | **%** |
| 7 | $S_L \propto T^{0.5}\,(p)^{-0.5}$ | Laminar burning velocity dependence (Metghalchi–Keck) | m/s |
| 8 | $\tau_{id} = A\,p^{-n}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R_u T}\right)$ | Ignition delay (Arrhenius form) | s or °CA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention used in KTU PEMUT752:**
> $\bar{x}$ — arithmetic mean of quantity $x$ over $N$ cycles.
> $\sigma$ — true (or sample) standard deviation.
> $V_d$ — displaced volume.
> $R_u = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)** — universal gas constant.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding and controlling cyclic variations is **fundamental to modern engine calibration** because:

- **Drive-by-wire drivability** depends on smooth torque delivery (low COV).
- **Emissions Compliance (BS-VI / Euro 6)** — high COV correlates with elevated **unburnt HC** and **CO** spikes.
- **Knock Margin** — high COV of peak pressure can push individual cycles above the auto-ignition boundary, causing knock.
- **Cold-Start and Idle Quality** — most critical operating conditions where cyclic dispersion is at its maximum.
- **GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection) Engines** — wall-wetting and mixture preparation introduce higher cyclic variability than PFI (Port Fuel Injection).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Coefficient of Variation (COV) from First Statistical Principles

We aim to derive the **Coefficient of Variation of IMEP**, which is the **gold-standard KTU-board metric** for cyclic dispersion.

### Step 1: Define the IMEP of the $i^{\text{th}}$ cycle
From the first law applied to each individual cycle, the indicated work per cycle is:

$$ W_{i} = \oint p_i(\theta)\,dV $$

where $p_i(\theta)$ is the instantaneous in-cylinder pressure for the $i^{\text{th}}$ cycle as a function of crank angle $\theta$, and $V(\theta)$ is the instantaneous cylinder volume.

The cycle-resolved indicated mean effective pressure is therefore:

$$ \text{IMEP}_i = \dfrac{W_i}{V_d} = \dfrac{1}{V_d}\oint p_i(\theta)\,dV $$

**Reasoning (text):** This step converts the cycle work (energy) into an equivalent *mean* pressure acting over the full swept volume — a fair, displacement-normalized basis for comparing cycles of different geometries.

---

### Step 2: Form the arithmetic mean over $N$ consecutive cycles
For statistical convergence, we require a sufficient sample size ($N \geq 100$). The ensemble (cycle-averaged) IMEP is:

$$ \overline{\text{IMEP}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\text{IMEP}_i $$

**Reasoning:** The arithmetic mean minimizes the sum of squared deviations and is the **maximum-likelihood estimator** of the true mean when the underlying distribution is Gaussian.

---

### Step 3: Compute the squared deviations
The deviation of the $i^{\text{th}}$ cycle from the mean is:

$$ \Delta_i = \text{IMEP}_i - \overline{\text{IMEP}} $$

Squaring gives:

$$ \Delta_i^2 = (\text{IMEP}_i - \overline{\text{IMEP}})^2 $$

**Reasoning:** Squaring is mandatory because positive and negative deviations must both contribute positively to a measure of *spread*.

---

### Step 4: Sum the squared deviations
Summing over all $N$ cycles:

$$ S = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\Delta_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(\text{IMEP}_i - \overline{\text{IMEP}})^2 $$

---

### Step 5: Apply Bessel's correction ($N-1$ divisor)
For a finite sample drawn from a population, the *unbiased* estimator of variance uses $(N-1)$ instead of $N$ (Bessel's correction):

$$ \sigma_{\text{IMEP}}^{\,2} = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\text{IMEP}_i - \overline{\text{IMEP}})^2 $$

**Reasoning:** With $N$ observations we have only $N-1$ independent deviations (one constraint: $\sum \Delta_i = 0$). Using $N$ would systematically underestimate the true population variance.

---

### Step 6: Take the square root to obtain the standard deviation
$$ \sigma_{\text{IMEP}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\text{IMEP}_i - \overline{\text{IMEP}})^2} $$

---

### Step 7: Form the dimensionless Coefficient of Variation
Normalize the standard deviation by the mean to obtain a dimensionless measure of relative dispersion:

$$ \boxed{\;\text{COV}_{\text{IMEP}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{IMEP}}}{\overline{\text{IMEP}}} \times 100\ \%\;} $$

**Reasoning:** Absolute dispersion ($\sigma$ in bar) is *not* scale-invariant — a 0.5 bar deviation at low load (3 bar IMEP) is far more severe than at full load (12 bar IMEP). Dividing by the mean yields a **percentage** that is comparable across operating conditions.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cause → Mechanism → Effect

| # | Cause | Mechanism | Effect on COV of IMEP | Affected Engine |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Lean A/F mixture | Slower $S_L$, partial burn cycles | **Increases sharply** | SI |
| 2 | High EGR | Dilution, slower flame, more cycle-to-cycle variability | **Increases** | SI / CI |
| 3 | Low engine speed | Lower turbulence, less repeatable mixing | **Increases** | SI / CI |
| 4 | Retarded spark | Less time for complete burn before BDC | **Increases** | SI |
| 5 | Excessive diesel pilot + main | Auto-ignition interaction randomness | **Increases** | CI |
| 6 | High swirl / squish | More repeatable mixing | **Decreases** | CI |
| 7 | Higher compression ratio | More reliable ignition | **Decreases** | CI |
| 8 | Direct injection (improved spray) | Better atomization | **Decreases** | SI (GDI) |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Over 100 consecutive cycles, the IMEP values of a single-cylinder SI engine are recorded. The mean IMEP is **$\overline{\text{IMEP}} = 8.00$ bar**, and the sum of squared deviations is $\sum (\text{IMEP}_i - \overline{\text{IMEP}})^2 = 64.00\ \text{bar}^2$. Determine the COV of IMEP and comment on engine smoothness.

**Solution:**

$$ \sigma_{\text{IMEP}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{64.00}{100 - 1}} = \sqrt{0.6465}\ \text{bar} $$

$$ \sigma_{\text{IMEP}} = 0.804\ \text{bar} $$

$$ \text{COV}_{\text{IMEP}} = \dfrac{0.804}{8.00}\times 100 = 10.05\ \% $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Interpretation:** Since the COV exceeds **10 %**, the engine is operating at the threshold of **rough operation / misfire limit**. For an SI engine, the target is COV $\leq$ 5 %. The driver would perceive this as a perceptible engine roughness.

---

## 3.4 Linkage Between COV and Misfire Boundary (SI Engines)

The misfire boundary for SI engines is conventionally approximated (Heywood) as:

$$ \text{COV}_{\text{IMEP, misfire}} \approx 10\ \% $$

As the mixture becomes progressively leaner (or as EGR increases), the **mean IMEP drops** (less energy released) while $\sigma$ remains roughly constant or grows — leading to:

$$ \text{COV} \uparrow\ \text{as}\ \phi \downarrow $$

This explains why **lean-burn and high-EGR SI engines** are limited to modest dilution rates before cycle-by-cycle variability becomes unacceptable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Cyclic Variation Genesis

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **causal chain** of cyclic variation generation — from microscopic mixture non-uniformity to the macroscopic IMEP dispersion measured on the dynamometer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Engine Steady-State Operation] --> A1[Mixture Preparation Stage]
    A0 --> A2[Ignition / Injection Stage]
    A0 --> A3[In-Cylinder Thermal Stage]

    A1 --> B1[Local Equivalence Ratio Fluctuations phi]
    A1 --> B2[Residual Gas Fraction Variability]
    A1 --> B3[Turbulence Intensity Stochasticity]

    A2 --> C1[Spark Kernel Position Drift]
    A2 --> C2[Injection Timing Jitter]
    A2 --> C3[Spray Atomization Variability]

    A3 --> D1[Wall Temperature Fluctuation]
    A3 --> D2[Compression End Temperature T_c Drift]

    B1 --> E1[Cycle-Resolved Burning Velocity S_L]
    B2 --> E1
    B3 --> E1
    D2 --> E1

    C1 --> E2[Cycle-Resolved Ignition Delay tau_id]
    C2 --> E2
    C3 --> E2
    D1 --> E2

    E1 --> F1[Cycle-Resolved Mass Fraction Burned Profile x b theta]
    E2 --> F1

    F1 --> G1[Cycle-Resolved p theta Diagram]
    G1 --> H1[Cycle-Resolved IMEP i]
    H1 --> I1[Ensemble Mean IMEP]
    H1 --> I2[Standard Deviation sigma IMEP]
    I1 --> J1[COV IMEP percent]
    I2 --> J1
    J1 --> K1{Threshold Check}
    K1 -->|COV le 5 percent| L1[Smooth Operation SI]
    K1 -->|COV le 3 percent| L2[Smooth Operation CI]
    K1 -->|COV greater than 10 percent SI| L3[Rough Misfire Limit]
    K1 -->|COV greater than 5 percent CI| L4[Diesel Knock Limit]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the COV Measurement Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Engine on Dynamometer] --> R2[Cylinder Pressure Transducer Piezo]
    R2 --> R3[Charge Amplifier]
    R3 --> R4[High-Speed DAQ Card]
    R4 --> R5[Cycle Detection by TDC Reference Encoder]
    R5 --> R6[Cycle Resolved IMEP Calculation per Cycle]
    R6 --> R7[Store N greater than equal to 100 IMEP i Values]
    R7 --> R8[Compute Mean IMEP bar]
    R7 --> R9[Compute Standard Deviation sigma bar]
    R8 --> R10[COV equals sigma divided by mean times 100]
    R9 --> R10
    R10 --> R11[Compare with KTU Threshold Table]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Bubble Diagram — Causes of Cyclic Variation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SIROOT[SI Engine Sources]
        SIA[A F Ratio Variations]
        SIB[Residual Gas Fraction]
        SIC[Spark Timing Drift]
        SID[Turbulence Stochasticity]
        SIE[EGR Rate Variations]
    end

    subgraph CIROOT[CI Engine Sources]
        CIA[Injection Timing Jitter]
        CIB[Injection Quantity]
        CIC[Spray Atomization]
        CID[Air Swirl Squish]
        CIE[Compression Temperature]
    end

    SIROOT --> COMMON[Common Statistical Manifestation]
    CIROOT --> COMMON
    COMMON --> COV[COV of IMEP Pressure Peak and PRR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Architecture Rationale (for KTU 14-mark answers):**
> Always structure your long answers with a *Cause → Mechanism → Measurable Quantity* flow. The diagram above is the examiner-recommended visual scaffolding for a 14-mark question on cyclic variation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEMUT752]**
**Define cyclic variation in IC engines. Why is the Coefficient of Variation (COV) of IMEP preferred over the standard deviation of IMEP for quantifying cyclic dispersion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cyclic variation refers to the **cycle-to-cycle fluctuations** in combustion parameters (peak pressure, heat release, IMEP) of an IC engine operating under steady-state conditions.

**Standard deviation** $\sigma_{\text{IMEP}}$ measures absolute dispersion in bar — but it is *not scale-invariant*. A 1 bar deviation at low load is far more severe than at full load.

**COV of IMEP** normalizes the dispersion by the mean, yielding a dimensionless percentage:

$$ \text{COV}_{\text{IMEP}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{IMEP}}}{\overline{\text{IMEP}}} \times 100\ \% $$

This makes it **scale-invariant and comparable across operating conditions**, which is why KTU boards universally prefer it. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEMUT752]**
**List any three causes of cyclic variation in SI engines and three in CI engines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SI Engine Causes:**
1. Local air–fuel ratio (equivalence ratio $\phi$) fluctuations in the cylinder.
2. Cycle-to-cycle variation in residual exhaust gas fraction.
3. Spark timing jitter due to plug wear / electrical drift.

**CI Engine Causes:**
1. Injection timing jitter (nozzle needle valve response variability).
2. Variations in spray atomization (nozzle coking, hole wear).
3. Cycle-to-cycle variation in air swirl intensity. **(3 marks — 0.5 per correct point)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Rule (2024 Scheme):**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Full solutions are mandatory.

---

### **Q. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEMUT752, Module 2]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Explain in detail the various causes of cyclic variations in SI engines. Discuss how air–fuel ratio and residual gases influence the COV of IMEP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the causes of cyclic variations in CI engines. How do injection system parameters and swirl ratio influence cycle-to-cycle variability? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Causes of cyclic variations in SI engines (7 marks):**

1. **Mixture Strength Variations (1.5 marks):** Local equivalence ratio $\phi$ varies around the bulk mean. Lean pockets burn slowly → flame may not complete before exhaust valve opens → partial-burn cycles. Rich pockets burn fast → high peak pressure cycles. The resulting scatter in $p_{max}$ and IMEP drives COV upward, especially at the lean limit.

2. **Residual Gas Variations (1.5 marks):** Cycle-to-cycle residual fraction ($f_r$) varies by ~1–2 % in production engines. Higher $f_r$ dilutes the charge, lowers the laminar flame speed $S_L \propto T^{0.5}p^{-0.5}$ (Metghalchi–Keck), and degrades ignition kernel growth. Both effects amplify cyclic dispersion.

3. **Turbulence Intensity Variations (1.5 marks):** In-cylinder turbulence is stochastic. Turbulent burning velocity $S_T \propto u' \cdot S_L$ (where $u'$ is r.m.s. turbulence intensity) varies randomly cycle-to-cycle, producing scatter in burn duration.

4. **Spark Plug Variability (1 mark):** Electrode erosion, voltage drift, and fouling alter spark kernel formation. This shifts the location of flame initiation slightly cycle-to-cycle, producing variability in early flame growth.

5. **EGR and Charge Motion Variability (1.5 marks):** External or internal EGR introduces local concentration gradients of inert species, further amplifying mixture non-uniformity.

**Net effect:** All five mechanisms combine stochastically, yielding a roughly Gaussian distribution of IMEP around the cycle-mean, characterized by COV.

**(b) Causes of cyclic variations in CI engines (7 marks):**

1. **Injection Timing Jitter (2 marks):** The needle valve opening and closing pressure of the injector varies cycle-to-cycle by ~0.1–0.3 °CA. Even a 0.5 °CA shift at 1500 rpm changes the start of combustion timing, dramatically altering peak pressure and IMEP.

2. **Injection Quantity Variations (1.5 marks):** Mechanical tolerances and pressure fluctuations in the injection pump cause small cycle-to-cycle variations in fuel mass. Since CI combustion is mass-controlled (not mixture-controlled like SI), even a 1 % mass variation translates to ~1 % IMEP variation.

3. **Spray Atomization Variability (1.5 marks):** Nozzle hole wear, coking deposits, and variations in injection pressure alter the Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD) of the spray. Larger droplets increase ignition delay (longer $\tau_{id}$) and produce more variability.

4. **Air Swirl and Squish Ratio (2 marks):** Swirl is generated by the intake port geometry and varies slightly cycle-to-cycle. Higher and more consistent swirl *reduces* COV (typically 1–3 %) by improving air–fuel mixing. Insufficient swirl or excessive swirl (>3 ratio) can *increase* COV.

**Net effect:** CI engines intrinsically have lower COV than SI engines (~1–3 % vs 2–5 %) because of the more deterministic compression-ignition process, but they suffer from "diesel knock" when COV exceeds ~5 %.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Coefficient of Variation (COV) of IMEP from first principles. Define all terms clearly and justify each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The IMEP values for 100 consecutive cycles of a single-cylinder SI engine are recorded. The mean IMEP is 9.50 bar and the standard deviation is 0.78 bar. **(i)** Calculate the COV of IMEP and comment on engine smoothness. **(ii)** If the engine is now operated with 15 % EGR and the new COV becomes 9.2 %, what is the new standard deviation? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Derivation of COV of IMEP (7 marks):**

See **Section 3.1** for the full derivation. The expected answer must include:

- **Step 1:** Definition of cycle-resolved IMEP from $p$–$\theta$ diagram (1 mark)
- **Step 2:** Arithmetic mean over $N$ cycles (1 mark)
- **Step 3:** Definition of deviation $\Delta_i$ (1 mark)
- **Step 4:** Sum of squared deviations (1 mark)
- **Step 5:** Bessel's correction with $(N-1)$ divisor — justification required (1 mark)
- **Step 6:** Square root to obtain $\sigma$ (1 mark)
- **Step 7:** Normalization by mean to yield dimensionless COV % (1 mark)

**Final boxed expression:**
$$ \text{COV}_{\text{IMEP}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\text{IMEP}_i - \overline{\text{IMEP}})^2}}{\overline{\text{IMEP}}}\times 100\ \% $$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — COV calculation (3 marks):**

$$ \text{COV} = \dfrac{0.78}{9.50} \times 100 = 8.21\ \% $$

**Comment (1 mark):** Since 8.21 % > 5 % but < 10 %, the engine is operating in the **marginally rough region**. SI engines ideally require COV $\leq$ 5 % for smooth operation.

**Part (ii) — New standard deviation with EGR (3 marks):**

Given $\text{COV}_{\text{new}} = 9.2\ \%$, assuming mean IMEP stays approximately the same (acceptable engineering assumption at moderate EGR):

$$ \sigma_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{\text{COV}_{\text{new}} \times \overline{\text{IMEP}}}{100} = \dfrac{9.2 \times 9.50}{100} $$

$$ \sigma_{\text{new}} = 0.874\ \text{bar} $$

**Conclusion:** The new standard deviation is **0.874 bar**, indicating a worsening of cycle-to-cycle variability due to EGR-induced dilution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Bessel's correction** — using $N$ instead of $N-1$ in the variance formula loses **1 mark** in derivations.
> 2. **Confusing absolute and relative dispersion** — reporting $\sigma$ instead of COV when the question asks for "cycle-to-cycle variation" loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not specifying units (%) for COV** — examiners deduct **0.5 marks** if the percentage sign is omitted.
> 4. **Ignoring the EGR assumption** in numerical problems — explicitly state "assuming mean IMEP constant" to secure full credit.
> 5. **Confusing "residual gas" with "EGR"** — residual gas is *internal* (left from previous cycle); EGR is *external* (re-routed). Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PEMUT752 Module 2 — Cyclic Variations:**

- ✅ **Cyclic variation** = cycle-to-cycle fluctuations in $p_{max}$, $dp/d\theta$, IMEP, and burn duration under steady-state operation.
- ✅ **Standard metric:** COV of IMEP = $(\sigma/\bar{x}) \times 100$ in **%**.
- ✅ **Acceptable COV:** SI $\leq$ **5 %**, CI $\leq$ **3 %**.
- ✅ **Rough operation threshold:** SI > **10 %**, CI > **5 %**.
- ✅ **Minimum sample size for statistical reliability:** $N \geq 100$ cycles.
- ✅ **Bessel's correction:** always use $(N-1)$ in the denominator of variance.
- ✅ **SI causes:** mixture $\phi$ variations, residual gas, turbulence, spark drift, EGR.
- ✅ **CI causes:** injection timing jitter, quantity variation, atomization, swirl, compression temperature.
- ✅ **Lean mixtures and high EGR** increase COV (lean limit / misfire limit).
- ✅ **Higher compression ratio, swirl, and improved atomization** reduce COV.
- ✅ **High COV symptoms:** rough idle, drivability issues, **HC and CO emission spikes**, knock tendency.
- ✅ **Heywood's misfire threshold:** COV $\approx$ **10 %** for SI engines.
- ✅ **COV is dimensionless** — never report in bar; always in **%**.
- ✅ **Flame speed relation:** $S_L \propto T^{0.5}p^{-0.5}$ (Metghalchi–Keck) — key to SI cyclic variation analysis.
- ✅ **CI ignition delay:** $\tau_{id} = A\,p^{-n}\exp(E_a/R_u T)$ — Arrhenius form controls CI cyclic variation.
- ✅ **Sample IMEP formula:** $\text{IMEP}_i = (1/V_d)\oint p_i\,dV$ — must be quoted in long derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partial Burning and Misfire
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Partial Burning and Misfire

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Partial Burning** is an abnormal combustion phenomenon in Spark Ignition (SI) engines in which the air–fuel mixture fails to undergo complete, stoichiometric oxidation during the power stroke. A fraction of the charge exits the cylinder as **unburned hydrocarbons (uHC)**, **carbon monoxide (CO)**, or **partially oxidized intermediates (H₂, partial HCs, aldehydes)**, despite the occurrence of a successful ignition event and visible flame kernel propagation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Misfire** is the complete absence of combustion in one or more cylinders of a multi-cylinder engine during a power stroke. By definition, **zero work is extracted** from the misfiring cylinder, and the unburned charge passes directly into the exhaust system intact.

| Property | Partial Burning | Misfire |
|---|---|---|
| Ignition kernel formed? | Yes | No / Insufficient |
| Flame propagation | Incomplete or extinguished | Absent |
| Charge oxidation | 0% < η_c < 100% | η_c = 0% |
| Primary emission species | CO, uHC, partial oxidations | Unburned HC, O₂, fuel vapor |
| Cylinder work output | Reduced | Zero (negative during pump stroke) |
| Frequency in operating cycle | Often cyclic | Typically 1 in 50–200 cycles (legally defined) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Candle-In-A-Room Analogy**
> Imagine lighting a candle in a large, drafty hall. **Partial Burning** is like the flame sputtering weakly because of poor air supply (lean mixture) or because cold walls steal the heat — the wick glows red but the wax does not fully vaporize and combust. Some wax drips away unburned.
>
> **Misfire** is like striking a match that does not light at all — the wooden stick (fuel) remains a wooden stick, never converting to flame or heat.
>
> In an engine cylinder, "walls" (combustion chamber surfaces) "steal heat" (quenching), "drafts" are in-cylinder turbulence, and "air supply" is governed by the throttle and mixture quality.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

- **Mass Fraction Burned (MFB)**: ratio of charge mass that has undergone oxidation to total inducted charge. For full burn, MFB = **1.0**; for misfire, MFB = **0.0**; for partial burn, 0 < MFB < 1.
- **Cycle-to-Cycle Variation (CCV) of IMEP**: Coefficient of Variation CoV_IMEP = σ_IMEP / IMEP_mean. Misfire elevates CoV_IMEP above **~10%** threshold.
- **Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP)** drops to near zero for misfire.
- **Threshold equivalence ratio** for sustained partial burn: typically **φ < 0.6** (lean limit) or **φ > 1.4** (rich limit).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Root-Cause Analysis — Why Partial Burning Occurs

The combustion process in an SI engine depends on three coupled sub-processes:

1. **Flame Kernel Development** (0 → ~5% MFB): Controlled by spark energy, kernel radius, and local turbulence.
2. **Rapid Flame Propagation** (5% → 90% MFB): Laminar + turbulent flame speed governs burn rate.
3. **Late-Phase Burnout** (90% → 100% MFB): Wall-quenching, crevice volumes, and residual gas dilution dominate.

Partial burning arises when any of these sub-processes is interrupted. The principal mechanisms are:

- **Wall Quenching**: Flame cannot propagate into a boundary layer thinner than the **quench distance δ_q ≈ 0.7 mm** for hydrocarbons. Pockets of mixture trapped in the crevice volume (between piston, head gasket, and ring land) escape unburned.
- **Lean Misfire Limit**: As φ decreases, the adiabatic flame temperature T_ad falls, reducing flame speed S_L. Below φ ≈ **0.55–0.65** (for iso-octane), the flame cannot sustain propagation.
- **Excessive EGR (External or Internal)**: Residual exhaust gas acts as a thermal diluent, lowering T_ad and S_L. The product of dilution ratio and temperature reduction defines a critical EGR threshold (~25–30%).
- **Low Compression Ratio**: Reduces end-of-compression temperature T_2, lowering the post-ignition flame kernel temperature.
- **Poor Fuel Atomization/Vaporization**: Pools of liquid fuel on intake manifold walls or piston crown burn late or incompletely.
- **Low Engine Speed / Low Turbulence**: Reduces turbulent flame speed S_T and slows the burn rate (Weber-number dependent).

### 2.2 Root-Cause Analysis — Why Misfire Occurs

Misfire is the extreme case of partial burning (MFB → 0). It is triggered by:

- **Failed Ignition Hardware**: Fouled, cracked, or worn spark plug; weak ignition coil; open secondary circuit.
- **Mixture Outside Flammability Range**: φ < 0.4 or φ > 1.7 (stoichiometric limits of most hydrocarbons).
- **Contamination**: Oil dilution in cylinder, coolant ingress (head gasket failure), water ingestion.
- **Excessively Diluted Charge**: Beyond the lean-burn or EGR limit.
- **Cold Start and Warm-Up**: Cold walls and poor vaporization prevent kernel growth.
- **Carbon Loading / Deposits**: Localized quenching or pre-ignition near hot spots.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Ratio of actual to stoichiometric fuel-air ratio | dimensionless |
| Mass Fraction Burned (Wiebe) | $x_b = 1 - \exp\!\left[-a\left(\dfrac{\theta - \theta_0}{\Delta \theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]$ | Empirical burn profile, $a$≈5, $m$≈2–3 | — |
| Laminar Flame Speed | $S_L \propto T_{ad}^{\,n}$ where $n \approx 2$ | Metgalchi–Keck correlation | m/s |
| Turbulent Flame Speed | $S_T = S_L + u'$ | Damköhler-type closure | m/s |
| Quench Distance (Pearson) | $\delta_q = \dfrac{\lambda}{c_p \rho S_L}\dfrac{\dot{m}''/\rho}{1}$ | Thermal quench layer | mm |
| IMEP | $IMEP = \dfrac{1}{V_d}\displaystyle\oint p\,dV$ | Indicated work per unit displaced volume | bar |
| CoV of IMEP | $CoV_{IMEP} = \dfrac{\sigma_{IMEP}}{\overline{IMEP}}$ | Cyclic variability metric | % |
| Misfire Detection Threshold (OBD-II) | $\Delta\omega_{crank} > 0.4 \,\%$ per 200 revs | Federal EPA / Euro VI criterion | % |
| Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c = 1 - \dfrac{\sum \dot{m}_{i,\,exh}\,h_{i}}{\dot{m}_f\,LHV}$ | Fraction of fuel energy released | dimensionless |
| Hydrocarbon Emission Index | $EI_{HC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{HC}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}}$ | g HC per kg fuel | g/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight**: The Wiebe function is heavily used in KTU numerical problems. For a "normal" burn, $a = 5$ and $m = 2$ reproduce the 10–90% MFB window close to the empirical flame-propagation profile. For a *partial burn* problem, the parameter $\Delta \theta$ (combustion duration) is artificially lengthened or $a$ is reduced to fit the lower MFB achieved.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II)**: Detection of partial burn and misfire is mandated by EPA Tier 3 and Euro VI. Detection relies on crankshaft angular velocity fluctuation, exhaust HC spike, and catalyst thermal monitoring.
- **Hybrid Powertrain Design**: Engine downsizing combined with partial-burn operation (Atkinson / Miller cycle) intentionally operates on the lean-burn side to improve BSFC, accepting partial-burn HC to be handled by the **Three-Way Catalyst + GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)**.
- **Cold-Start Calibration**: Engineers enrich the mixture during cold start precisely to avoid partial burn / misfire due to wall condensation and slow flame kernel growth.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Combustion Efficiency Drop Due to Partial Burn

Consider an SI engine where only a fraction $\alpha$ (where $0 < \alpha < 1$) of the inducted charge undergoes complete stoichiometric oxidation. The remaining fraction $(1 - \alpha)$ leaves the cylinder as unburned hydrocarbons carrying their original chemical energy (LHV).

**Step 1 — Energy released by partial burn fraction:**
The energy released by the combusted fraction is:

$$E_{released} = \alpha \cdot \dot{m}_{f} \cdot LHV$$

**Step 2 — Energy carried away by unburned HC:**
The unburned fraction retains its full chemical energy and exits the exhaust:

$$E_{unburned} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot \dot{m}_{f} \cdot LHV$$

**Step 3 — Define combustion efficiency:**
By definition:

$$\eta_c = \dfrac{E_{released}}{E_{supplied}} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \dot{m}_{f} \cdot LHV}{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot LHV} = \alpha$$

So, in this idealized model, **combustion efficiency equals the mass fraction burned**.

**Step 4 — Effect on brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC):**
BSFC is inversely proportional to brake thermal efficiency. With reduced $\eta_c$:

$$BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{P_{brake}} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_b \cdot LHV}$$

Since $\eta_b = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_m$ and $\eta_{th} = \eta_c \cdot \eta_{indicated}$, a drop in $\eta_c$ from 0.98 to 0.85 produces a **~15.3% increase in BSFC**.

$$\dfrac{BSFC_{partial}}{BSFC_{full}} = \dfrac{\eta_{c,\,full}}{\eta_{c,\,partial}} = \dfrac{0.98}{0.85} \approx 1.153$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Quench Distance and Crevice HC Mass

**Step 1 — Pearson's Quench Distance:**
Energy balance at the wall gives:

$$\delta_q = \dfrac{2 \, k \, (T_{ad} - T_{wall})}{S_L \, \rho_u \, c_p \, (T_{ad} - T_u)}$$

**Step 2 — Typical values for iso-octane/air at φ = 1.0:**
Substituting $k \approx 0.026$ W/m·K, $T_{ad} = 2270$ K, $T_{wall} = 450$ K, $S_L = 0.8$ m/s, $\rho_u = 14.8$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1050$ J/kg·K, $T_u = 350$ K:

$$\delta_q = \dfrac{2 \times 0.026 \times (2270 - 450)}{0.8 \times 14.8 \times 1050 \times (2270 - 350)} \approx 6.3 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.63\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Crevice HC mass fraction:**
For a combustion chamber with total crevice volume $V_{cr}$ relative to clearance volume $V_c$:

$$f_{HC,\,crevice} = \dfrac{V_{cr}}{V_c} \cdot \dfrac{T_{u}}{T_{wall}} \cdot \dfrac{\phi}{\phi_{cr}}$$

Typical automotive $V_{cr}/V_c \approx 0.02$ → crevice HC contributes **1.5–3% of total uHC emissions**, the dominant source in warm, fully-developed engines.

### 3.3 Derivation: Misfire Limit from Critical Flame Stretch

Flame stretch rate $K$ is defined as:

$$K = \dfrac{1}{A}\dfrac{dA}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{dr}{dt} + \dfrac{u_n}{r}$$

The critical stretch leading to extinction is the inverse of the **Markstein length** $L_b$:

$$K_{ext} = \dfrac{S_L}{L_b}$$

**Step 1 — Substituting turbulent flame speed:**
The effective stretch experienced by a turbulent flame in an engine is roughly:

$$K_{engine} \approx \dfrac{u'}{L_I}$$

where $L_I$ is the integral length scale of turbulence.

**Step 2 — Extinction condition:**
Misfire / partial burn occurs when:

$$K_{engine} \ge K_{ext} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dfrac{u'}{L_I} \ge \dfrac{S_L}{L_b}$$

**Step 3 — Practical implication:**
This is the theoretical basis for the empirical observation that **excessive swirl or tumble** can actually *extinguish* a flame in highly dilute mixtures — a non-intuitive result that frequently appears in KTU essay questions.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder SI engine runs at 2400 rpm. Under normal operation, IMEP = 9.0 bar. During a cold-start, partial burning reduces the mass-burned fraction to α = 0.78. The displaced volume per cylinder is 500 cm³. Calculate (a) the percentage drop in IMEP, (b) the percentage rise in BSFC.

**Solution:**

(a) Assuming linear proportionality between MFB and IMEP (valid for low-to-moderate partial burn):

$$\dfrac{IMEP_{partial}}{IMEP_{full}} = 0.78$$

$$\Delta IMEP\,\% = (1 - 0.78) \times 100 = 22\%\,\text{drop}$$

(b) Since $BSFC \propto 1/\eta_c \propto 1/\alpha$:

$$\Delta BSFC\,\% = \left(\dfrac{1}{0.78} - 1\right) \times 100 = 28.2\%\,\text{increase}$$

The **fuel penalty** is therefore larger than the IMEP loss because partial burning also leaves chemical energy in the unburned HC.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Causal Network of Partial Burning and Misfire

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Engine Operating Condition] --> B1[Mixture Quality phi]
    A1 --> B2[Ignition System Health]
    A1 --> B3[Thermal Environment T wall]
    A1 --> B4[In-Cylinder Turbulence u prime]
    A1 --> B5[Residual Gas Fraction x r]
    A1 --> B6[Spark Energy E spark]

    B1 --> C1{Lean Limit phi < 0.6}
    B1 --> C2{Rich Limit phi > 1.4}
    B2 --> C3{Spark Plug Failure}
    B3 --> C4{Wall Quench}
    B4 --> C5{Low S T or High Stretch}
    B5 --> C6{Lean Misfire via EGR}
    B6 --> C7{Kernel Dies Out}

    C1 --> D1[Partial Burn]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2[Misfire]
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
    C6 --> D1
    C7 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C6 --> D2
    C3 --> D2

    D1 --> E1[HC and CO Emissions Rise]
    D1 --> E2[IMEP Drops]
    D1 --> E3[CoV IMEP Rises]
    D1 --> E4[BSFC Increases]
    D2 --> F1[Catalyst Thermal Damage]
    D2 --> F2[O2 Sensor Contamination]
    D2 --> F3[Pump Stroke Backflow]
    D2 --> F4[OBD-II MIL Illumination]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Engine Misfire Detection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Crank Position Sensor] --> S1[Signal Conditioning]
    P2[Camshaft Position Sensor] --> S1
    P3[Cylinder Pressure Sensor Optional] --> S1
    S1 --> U1[ECU Engine Control Unit]
    H1[Lambda Oxygen Sensor] --> U1
    H2[Exhaust HC Sensor] --> U1
    U1 --> D1[Misfire Detection Algorithm]
    D1 --> Q1{Crank Fluctuation > 0.4 percent}
    Q1 --> R1[Record Misfire Event]
    R1 --> M1[Illuminate MIL Malfunction Indicator Lamp]
    R1 --> M2[Disable Cylinder via Fuel Cut]
    R1 --> M3[Store Freeze Frame Data]
    R1 --> M4[Active Misfire Counter Increment]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Burn-Rate Comparison Chart (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Start of Combustion] --> N1[Normal Burn 0 to 100 percent MFB in 35 CAD]
    A0 --> P1[Partial Burn 0 to 78 percent MFB in 60 CAD]
    A0 --> M1[Misfire 0 percent MFB indefinitely]

    N1 --> X1[Peak Pressure 12 bar at 15 deg ATDC]
    P1 --> X2[Peak Pressure 8 bar at 22 deg ATDC]
    M1 --> X3[No Combustion Peak Pressure only Compression]
```

### 4.4 Misfire Decision Logic (Diagnostic Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z0[Start Diagnostics] --> Z1{Spark Plug Condition OK}
    Z1 -->|No| Z2[Replace Spark Plug]
    Z1 -->|Yes| Z3{Fuel Pressure in Spec}
    Z3 -->|No| Z4[Service Fuel Pump and Regulator]
    Z3 -->|Yes| Z5{Compression Pressure > 9 bar}
    Z5 -->|No| Z6[Check Rings Valve Seats Head Gasket]
    Z5 -->|Yes| Z7{Equivalence Ratio phi in 0.85 to 1.15}
    Z7 -->|No| Z8[Calibrate MAF and O2 Sensor]
    Z7 -->|Yes| Z9{Excessive Carbon Deposits}
    Z9 -->|Yes| Z10[Decoke Induction System]
    Z9 -->|No| Z11[Consult ECU Error Log for Sensor Faults]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> Define **partial burning** in an SI engine. List any **two causes** and **two consequences** of partial burning.

**Model Answer:**
Partial burning is the incomplete oxidation of the air–fuel mixture during the power stroke of an SI engine such that a fraction of the charge exits the cylinder as unburned or partially oxidized hydrocarbons.
*Causes*: (i) mixture too lean (φ < 0.6), (ii) wall quenching and crevice entrapment, (iii) excessive EGR.
*Consequences*: (i) rise in HC and CO emissions, (ii) fall in indicated mean effective pressure and rise in BSFC.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> Differentiate between **partial burn** and **misfire** in spark ignition engines.

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Partial Burn | Misfire |
|---|---|---|
| MFB achieved | 0 < x_b < 1 | x_b = 0 |
| Spark outcome | Successful kernel, incomplete propagation | No flame or immediate extinction |
| Cylinder work | Reduced | Zero / negative |
| Dominant emission | CO + uHC | uHC + O₂ in exhaust |
| OBD-II flag | Not necessarily | Always triggers MIL |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various **causes of partial burning** in SI engines. Use a Wiebe function sketch to show how the 10–90% burn interval widens under partial burn conditions.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A four-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine running at 3000 rpm suffers a partial burn such that α = 0.82. IMEP under complete burn is 9.5 bar. Calculate (i) the new IMEP, (ii) the percentage increase in BSFC, (iii) the HC emission index if the unburned fraction leaves as 220 ppm C₁ equivalent. Assume BSFC_full = 270 g/kWh and fuel flow rate = 4.5 g/s.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Causes (write any 5 with explanation): (1) Excessively lean or rich mixture, (2) Excessive EGR, (3) Wall quenching, (4) Low compression ratio, (5) Spark plug fouling, (6) Insufficient turbulence, (7) Cold-start conditions, (8) Poor fuel atomization.

Wiebe comparison:
- Normal: $x_b = 1 - \exp[-5(\theta/\Delta\theta_{norm})^3]$, $\Delta\theta_{norm} \approx 30$ CAD.
- Partial: same shape but $\Delta\theta_{partial} \approx 50$–60 CAD and $a$ reduced, so the curve flattens before reaching unity.

Sketch verbally: A normal-burn Wiebe curve approaches unity within ~50 CAD; a partial-burn curve plateaus at 0.82, and the asymptote is shown as a horizontal line $x_b = \alpha = 0.82$.

**(b)**

(i) $IMEP_{partial} = 0.82 \times 9.5 = 7.79$ bar.

(ii) $\Delta BSFC\,\% = (1/0.82 - 1) \times 100 = 21.95\% \approx 22\%$.

(iii) Mass of HC per second = $4.5 \times 0.18 = 0.81$ g/s.

$$EI_{HC} = \dfrac{0.81}{4.5} \times 10^3 = 180 \;\text{g HC/kg fuel}$$

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Define **misfire**. Discuss the **mechanisms of misfire detection** in modern OBD-II compliant engines.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for **combustion efficiency** under partial burning and show that BSFC is inversely proportional to the mass-burned fraction α. A 1.8 L four-cylinder engine loses 15% of its energy to partial burn. By what percentage does the indicated thermal efficiency drop? Take the indicated thermal efficiency at full burn as 38%.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Misfire = zero combustion in a cylinder during its power stroke. Detection mechanisms:
1. **Crankshaft angular acceleration fluctuation** (Δω > 0.4% over 200 revs triggers flag).
2. **Camshaft–crankshaft phase correlation** (determines which cylinder misfired).
3. **Exhaust HC spike** from the O₂ or HC sensor.
4. **Catalyst outlet temperature drop** (post-catalyst T_cat below threshold).
5. **Ion-current sensing** (in-cylinder) and **pressure-based detection** (research-grade).

**(b)** Derivation as in §3.1 above. $\eta_c = \alpha$. Hence $BSFC \propto 1/\alpha$.

For the numerical part, energy lost to partial burn = 15%:

$$\eta_{th,\,partial} = 0.85 \times 0.38 = 0.323 = 32.3\%$$

$$\Delta\eta_{th}\,\% = \dfrac{0.38 - 0.323}{0.38} \times 100 = 15\%$$

(The fractional loss in efficiency equals the fractional loss in α — a clean result that examiners love.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not** confuse **partial burn** with **knock (detonation)** — they are different abnormal-combustion phenomena. Knock = *too fast* combustion (auto-ignition of end gas). Partial burn = *too slow / incomplete* combustion.
> 2. **Do not** assume BSFC drops linearly with α. Always show the inverse relationship: $BSFC_{partial} = BSFC_{full} / \alpha$.
> 3. **Do not** omit units in numerical answers — losing 0.5–1 mark per question is common.
> 4. In Wiebe-based questions, **state the assumed values of $a$ and $m$** (default $a = 5$, $m = 2$) before applying the equation. Many students lose 2 marks by skipping this declaration.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Partial burning** = incomplete oxidation; MFB between 0 and 1.
- **Misfire** = zero oxidation; MFB = 0.
- Three principal cause categories: **mixture quality**, **thermal environment**, **ignition/turbulence**.
- **Lean limit** for most hydrocarbons: φ ≈ 0.55–0.65. **Rich limit**: φ ≈ 1.4–1.7.
- **Combustion efficiency η_c = α** (mass-burned fraction) in the ideal case.
- **BSFC increases hyperbolically** as α decreases: $BSFC_{partial} = BSFC_{full}/\alpha$.
- **Quench distance** ~0.6–0.8 mm for HC fuels at φ = 1.0.
- **Crevice volume** typically contributes 1.5–3% of total uHC emissions in warm engines.
- **OBD-II misfire threshold**: crank angular speed fluctuation > 0.4% over 200 revs.
- **Misfire detection** is mandated by EPA Tier 3 / Euro VI on all production engines.
- **Consequences**: ↑ HC, ↑ CO, ↓ IMEP, ↑ CoV_IMEP, ↑ BSFC, catalyst thermal damage, MIL illumination.
- **Mitigation strategies**: enrichment during cold start, EGR limits, spark timing advance, ignition coil redundancy, catalyst thermal management.
- **Wiebe function** is the canonical tool for modeling partial-burn profiles in KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abnormal Combustion - Reasons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Abnormal Combustion - Reasons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Abnormal Combustion** refers to any combustion phenomenon in an internal combustion (IC) engine that deviates from the intended, controlled, and orderly flame-front propagation initiated by the spark plug. In Spark Ignition (SI) engines, "normal" combustion proceeds as a smooth, roughly spherical flame kernel that grows at a controlled velocity. Abnormal combustion includes **engine knock (detonation), surface ignition, pre-ignition, and run-on (dieseling)**. These phenomena arise when the unburned end gas auto-ignites spontaneously or when combustion is triggered by a source other than the spark.

The KTU 2024 syllabus (PEMUT752, Module 2) requires a clear understanding of the **physical and chemical triggers** that cause each form of abnormal combustion, the **distinguishing features** between them, and the **engine and fuel design parameters** that influence them.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine lighting a single candle in a room full of fireworks with a long fuse. *Normal combustion* is the fuse burning steadily toward the fireworks — predictable and controlled. *Knock* is like the heat from the fuse being so intense that some fireworks nearby suddenly explode on their own before the fuse reaches them — a chaotic secondary ignition. *Pre-ignition* is like someone tossing a match into the fireworks pile before the fuse is even lit.

- **Engine Knock (Detonation):** Sudden, spontaneous, auto-ignition of the unburned end-gas due to high pressure and temperature, producing a sharp pressure wave (shock wave) inside the cylinder.
- **Surface Ignition:** Flammable mixture ignited by a **hot surface** (deposits, valve, spark plug electrodes) inside the combustion chamber.
- **Pre-Ignition:** A specific case of surface ignition where combustion starts **before** the spark plug fires (during compression).
- **Run-on / Dieseling / After-run:** Engine continues to run for a few seconds after ignition is switched off, because hot deposits ignite the mixture.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Values (SI Engine Abnormal Combustion):**
> - **Normal flame front velocity:** $\mathbf{15 - 30 \text{ m/s}}$ (laminar) and $\mathbf{50 - 80 \text{ m/s}}$ (turbulent)
> - **Knock-induced flame speed (detonation):** $\mathbf{300 - 1000 \text{ m/s}}$
> - **End-gas auto-ignition delay:** typically $\mathbf{1 - 5 \text{ ms}}$
> - **Auto-ignition temperature of gasoline (iso-octane):** $\mathbf{\approx 415 \text{ °C} \ (688 \text{ K})}$
> - **Octane Number (RON):** dimensionless rating; **2,2,4-Trimethylpentane (iso-octane) = 100**, **n-heptane = 0**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Abnormal Combustion

Abnormal combustion is broadly classified into four inter-related phenomena. KTU examiners frequently ask students to **distinguish** them in 3-mark questions.

#### 2.1.1 Engine Knock (Detonation)

- **What it is:** Spontaneous auto-ignition of the **end gas** (unburned mixture ahead of the flame front) producing a near-instantaneous release of chemical energy and a high-amplitude pressure oscillation.
- **Why it happens:** The end gas is compressed and heated by both the rising piston and the advancing flame front. If this combined temperature-time history exceeds the fuel's auto-ignition resistance, the end gas detonates.
- **How it is heard/seen:** Audible metallic "pinging" or "knocking" sound; vibration; slight loss of power; localized overheating.

#### 2.1.2 Surface Ignition

- **What it is:** Ignition of the air-fuel charge by **any hot surface** in the chamber other than the spark plug.
- **Why:** Carbon deposits, overheated valves, glowing spark plug electrodes, or protruding combustion chamber edges.
- **How:** Usually appears as a "rumble" — less sharp than knock but still undesirable.

#### 2.1.3 Pre-Ignition

- **What it is:** Surface ignition that occurs **before** the spark plug fires.
- **Why:** Persistent hot spots (typically > $700\text{ °C}$).
- **How:** Loss of timing control; can cause backfiring through the intake; can damage the engine severely because peak pressure rises much earlier than TDC.

#### 2.1.4 Run-on / Dieseling

- **What it is:** After the ignition is switched OFF, the engine continues running for a few cycles.
- **Why:** Hot carbon deposits ignite the charge without spark; high compression ratio aids it.
- **How:** Stopping is delayed; usually eliminated by lowering idle speed before shut-off.

### 2.2 Detailed Reasons for Engine Knock (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often frames Part-B questions as: *"Discuss the various factors responsible for knock in SI engines."* The following are the exhaustive reasons expected in the answer:

#### A. Fuel-Related Reasons
- **Low Octane Rating:** Paraffinic fuels (long straight-chain hydrocarbons) have low knock resistance.
- **High Self-Ignition Temperature Required:** Aromatics and branched paraffins have higher resistance.
- **High Volatility:** Easy vaporization increases charge density → higher peak pressure.
- **Presence of Lead or Aromatic Additives** → increases knock resistance.

#### B. Engine Design Parameters

1. **Compression Ratio:**
   As compression ratio $\rho$ increases, end-gas temperature increases roughly per the **ideal-gas isentropic relation**:

   $$\begin{aligned}
   T_2 &= T_1 \cdot \rho^{\gamma - 1}
   \end{aligned}$$

   where $\gamma = c_p / c_v \approx \mathbf{1.35}$ for combustion products. Higher $\rho$ → higher $T_2$ → higher knock tendency.

2. **Combustion Chamber Geometry:**
   - **Compact chamber** (e.g., hemispherical): short flame travel, less knock.
   - **Long narrow chamber** (e.g., bathtub): long flame travel, end gas stays longer under high $P$ and $T$ → more knock.
   - **Squish effect:** promotes turbulence, speeds up combustion → reduces knock.

3. **Spark Advance:**
   - Excessive spark advance means combustion pressure peak occurs well before TDC → high pressure and temperature push back on the still-rising piston → increases the end-gas compression tendency → promotes knock.

4. **Engine Speed (N):**
   - At higher speed, flame propagation has less time → end gas is exposed longer under high $T$ → higher knock.
   - But at very high speeds, increased turbulence and reduced time for auto-ignition chemistry actually **reduces** knock — this is why high-performance engines run rich and rev high.

5. **Engine Size (Bore):**
   - Larger bore → longer flame travel → higher knock tendency.

#### C. Operating Conditions

1. **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR):**
   - Knock is **maximum** near stoichiometric (AFR ≈ 14.7 for gasoline).
   - Both rich (AFR ≈ 12) and lean (AFR ≈ 18) mixtures have **higher knock resistance**.

2. **Intake Temperature:**
   - Hot intake air → higher end-gas temperature → higher knock.

3. **Intake Pressure (Boost/Turbocharging):**
   - Higher intake pressure → higher density → higher temperatures → greater knock tendency.

4. **Coolant Temperature:**
   - High coolant temperature → increases wall temperatures → increases end-gas temperature → more knock.

5. **Humidity:**
   - Higher humidity reduces knock (water vapor has high specific heat, lowers peak temperature).

6. **Combustion Chamber Deposits:**
   - Carbon deposits act as thermal insulators, increasing the local wall temperature and providing hot-spot-induced surface ignition that can transition to knock.

### 2.3 Detailed Reasons for Surface Ignition & Pre-Ignition

- **Carbon / Oil Deposits on chamber walls:** act as hot spots (temperatures up to $700 - 900 \text{ °C}$).
- **Overheated Spark Plug Electrodes:** improper heat range selection.
- **Overheated Exhaust Valves:** poor valve cooling, lean burn, retarded timing, EGR maldistribution.
- **Glowing Carbon Particles:** from incomplete combustion; ignite charge prematurely.
- **Sharp Edges / Protrusions in Chamber:** high local gas temperatures, enhanced surface ignition.
- **Detached Combustion Chamber Deposits:** at high load, deposits can dislodge and become mobile hot particles.

### 2.4 Reasons for Run-on (Dieseling)

- **High Compression Ratio:** enables auto-ignition of mixture after ignition-off.
- **Hot Carbon Deposits:** remain glowing for several seconds after shut-off.
- **Low Octane Fuel:** more prone.
- **High Idle Speed before shut-off:** more cycles run on residual heat.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises all high-yield formulas for Abnormal Combustion (Module 2):

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|---------|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| End-gas temperature after compression | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot \rho^{\gamma - 1}$ | $T$ in K; $\rho$ = compression ratio; $\gamma \approx 1.35$ | Predicts knock sensitivity with CR |
| Auto-Ignition Delay (Arrhenius) | $\tau = A \cdot e^{E_a / (R_u T)}$ | $\tau$ in s; $E_a$ in J/mol; $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $T$ in K | Evaluates chemical propensity to knock |
| Octane Number Correlation (empirical) | $ON \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Paraffin content}}$; $\propto \text{Aromatic branch}$ | dimensionless | Fuel selection |
| Mean Effective Pressure (knock-limited) | $P_m = \dfrac{W_{net}}{n \cdot V_d}$ | Pa; $V_d$ displacement volume | Defines knock-limited load |
| Flame front speed ratio | $S_{knock} / S_{normal} \approx \mathbf{10 \text{ to } 30}$ | m/s | Distinguishes knock from normal |
| Normal flame speed (turbulent) | $S_t = \mathbf{50 - 80 \text{ m/s}}$ | m/s | Reference baseline |
| Knock-limited compression ratio | $\rho_{kl} \approx f(\text{Octane Number})$ | dimensionless | Engine design constraint |

> [!WARNING]
> **MARK SCHEME PITFALL:** When writing $T_2 = T_1 \rho^{\gamma - 1}$, **never confuse** $\rho$ (compression ratio) with air density. In KTU valuation, examiners deduct 1 mark if units of $\rho$ are incorrectly stated as kg/m³.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel Refineries:** Set minimum Octane Number of petrol based on the highest CR passenger cars sold (typically 95–98 RON today).
- **Engine Designers:** Choose CR and chamber shape based on available fuel octane.
- **Turbocharged Engines:** Must include knock sensors (piezoelectric) and **retard timing** control via ECU.
- **Aviation:** 100LL Avgas uses TEL (tetraethyl lead) to deliver MON 100; future fuels use higher aromatics.
- **Racing (F1):** Knock-limited performance is the **single biggest performance constraint** in modern F1 engines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: End-Gas Temperature vs. Compression Ratio

We want to establish *quantitatively* why a higher compression ratio raises end-gas temperature and therefore the knock tendency.

**Step 1 — Starting assumption:** Treat the compression of the end gas (after the intake valve closes) as a reversible adiabatic (isentropic) compression.

**Step 2 — Apply isentropic relation between temperature and volume:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_2}{T_1} &= \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that the volume ratio $V_1/V_2$ is the compression ratio $\rho$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= T_1 \cdot \rho^{\gamma - 1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute typical numerical values (gasoline engine):**

- $T_1 = 350 \text{ K}$ (after induction)
- $\rho = 10$
- $\gamma = 1.35$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= 350 \times 10^{0.35}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $10^{0.35}$ using logarithmic expansion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
10^{0.35} &= e^{0.35 \ln 10} = e^{0.35 \times 2.3026} = e^{0.8059} \approx 2.239
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= 350 \times 2.239 \approx 783.6 \text{ K} \approx 510 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with auto-ignition temperature of iso-octane ($\approx 688$ K):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 \approx 783.6 \text{ K} > T_{auto} \approx 688 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** At CR = 10, end-gas temperature already exceeds iso-octane's auto-ignition temperature. This is why fuels used in modern high-CR engines need additives (or are highly branched/aromatic) to raise their effective auto-ignition resistance.

### 3.2 Derivation: Knock Limiting Effect of Charge Dilution

**Premise:** When the end gas is diluted with exhaust gas (EGR) or excess air (lean burn), its specific heat capacity increases and peak temperature drops.

**Step 1 — Energy balance per mole of mixture (idealized):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_i n_i c_{v,i} \, dT = -\sum_i U_i \, d\xi
\end{aligned}$$

where $\xi$ is reaction progress and $U_i$ is internal energy of species $i$.

**Step 2 — Effective specific heat of charge with dilution fraction $\alpha$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
c_{v,eff} &= (1 - \alpha) c_{v,fuel-air} + \alpha c_{v,diluent}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Adiabatic flame temperature drop with dilution (simplified):**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{ad,2} &= T_0 + \frac{\Delta U_{comb}}{c_{v,eff}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Because $c_{v,CO_2} > c_{v,N_2}$, diluents (especially $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$) absorb more energy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{ad} \propto \frac{1}{c_{v,eff}} \downarrow \quad \text{as } \alpha \uparrow
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Increasing EGR fraction or operating lean lowers peak flame temperature and therefore **lowers end-gas temperature at knock onset**, increasing knock resistance. This is the thermodynamic basis of EGR-based knock control in modern ECUs.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Engine Knock vs. Other Abnormal Combustion Forms

The following matrix is the **most-tested distinguishing feature** in KTU exams.

| Feature | Knock (Detonation) | Surface Ignition | Pre-Ignition | Run-on |
|---------|-------------------|------------------|--------------|--------|
| **Trigger source** | End-gas auto-ignition | Hot surface | Hot surface (before spark) | Hot deposits (after ignition off) |
| **Timing relative to spark** | After spark | After (or sometimes before) spark | Before spark | After ignition off |
| **Pressure rise rate** | Very high ($\approx 1$ MPa/° CA) | Moderate | Moderate | Very low |
| **Sound** | Sharp "ping" | Rumble / dull thud | Backfire / power loss | Continued idle |
| **Damage severity** | High (piston, ring, bearing) | Moderate | Severe (melted electrodes) | Low |
| **Octane relationship** | Strongly dependent | Independent | Independent | Moderate |
| **Corrected by** | Higher octane, lower CR, retarded timing | Clean deposits, cooler plugs | Lower CR, spark plug heat range | Lower idle, fuel cut-off |

### 3.4 Quantitative Link Between Octane Number and Allowable CR

**Empirical Heywood correlation** (light-duty SI engines):

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{max} \approx 1 + \frac{ON}{30}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-step check for ON = 95:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{max} &\approx 1 + \frac{95}{30} \approx 1 + 3.167 \approx 4.17
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **rough empirical estimate**; modern high-performance engines can exceed this with knock sensors, knock control, and direct injection.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Causal Flow of Abnormal Combustion — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **causal architecture** of how abnormal combustion events propagate. This serves as the functional schematic for KTU answer sheets where physical cross-sections cannot be drawn.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet Air-Fuel Charge] --> B[Compression Stroke Begins]
    B --> C[Spark Plug Fires at theta_s BTDC]
    C --> D[Flame Kernel Initiates]
    D --> E{End Gas Auto-Ignites?}
    E -- Yes --> F[KNOCK / DETONATION]
    E -- No --> G[Normal Combustion]
    F --> H[Pressure Wave Reflects in Chamber]
    H --> I[Piston, Ring, Bearing Damage]
    D --> J{Hot Surface Exists?}
    J -- Yes, before spark --> K[PRE-IGNITION]
    J -- Yes, after spark --> L[SURFACE IGNITION]
    K --> M[Loss of Timing Control]
    L --> N[Rumble / Mild Knock]
    G --> O[Ignition Switched OFF]
    O --> P{Hot Deposits Present?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[RUN-ON / DIESELING]
    P -- No --> R[Engine Stops]
    F --> S[Reduce CR / Increase Octane / Retard Spark]
    K --> S
    L --> T[Remove Deposits / Cooler Spark Plug]
    Q --> U[Lower Idle / Use Cleaner Fuel]
```

### 4.2 Flame-Front Propagation vs. Knock — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NormalCombustion
        N1[Spark Plug] --> N2[Flame Kernel]
        N2 --> N3[Smooth Spherical Flame Front]
        N3 --> N4[Combustion Chamber Walls]
    end
    subgraph AbnormalKnock
        K1[Spark Plug] --> K2[Flame Kernel]
        K2 --> K3[Flame Front Advancing]
        K3 --> K4[End Gas Reaches Auto-Ignition]
        K4 --> K5[Spontaneous Detonation of End Gas]
        K5 --> K6[High-Amplitude Pressure Wave]
        K6 --> K7[Piston / Bearing Damage]
    end
```

### 4.3 Root-Cause Categorisation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Abnormal Combustion Reasons] --> B[Fuel Properties]
    A --> C[Engine Design]
    A --> D[Operating Conditions]
    A --> E[Maintenance Issues]
    B --> B1[Low Octane Number]
    B --> B2[High Paraffin Content]
    B --> B3[High Volatility]
    C --> C1[High Compression Ratio]
    C --> C2[Poor Chamber Geometry]
    C --> C3[Excess Spark Advance]
    C --> C4[Large Bore]
    D --> D1[Stoichiometric AFR]
    D --> D2[High Intake Temp]
    D --> D3[High Boost Pressure]
    D --> D4[High Coolant Temp]
    D --> D5[Low Humidity]
    E --> E1[Carbon Deposits]
    E --> E2[Overheated Plugs or Valves]
    E --> E3[Oil Contamination]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each terminal leaf (B1, C1, D1, E1, …) represents a single examinable "reason" for abnormal combustion — together they form the **complete answer checklist** for KTU Part-B questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define engine knock. How does it differ from surface ignition?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engine Knock** is the spontaneous auto-ignition of the unburned end-gas ahead of the advancing flame front in an SI engine, producing a sharp pressure wave and a characteristic "pinging" sound. It occurs **after** the spark has fired.
>
> **Surface Ignition** is the ignition of the air-fuel mixture by **hot surfaces** in the combustion chamber (carbon deposits, overheated valves/plugs), independent of the spark. It can occur **before or after** the spark depending on whether it is pre-ignition or post-spark surface ignition.
>
> **Key Differences:**
>
> | Aspect | Knock | Surface Ignition |
> |--------|-------|------------------|
> | Trigger | End-gas auto-ignition | Hot surface |
> | Sound | Sharp "ping" | Dull rumble |
> | Timing | After spark | Before or after |
> | Pressure rise | Very steep | Moderate |

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for definition of knock, definition of surface ignition, distinguishing table)*

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six factors that influence knock in SI engines.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Octane rating of fuel (lower → more knock)
2. Compression ratio (higher → more knock)
3. Spark advance (advanced → more knock)
4. Air-fuel ratio (knock peaks near stoichiometric)
5. Combustion chamber geometry (long narrow → more knock)
6. Intake temperature (higher → more knock)
7. Engine speed and load
8. Combustion chamber deposits

*(3 marks — ½ mark per correct factor, minimum six required)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part-B questions for Module 2 carry **14 marks**, typically split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Below are TWO internal-choice questions (**A and B**) — students answer EITHER A OR B.

---

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the phenomenon of knock in SI engines with the help of a pressure–crank angle diagram. List the factors affecting knock. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Phenomenon of Knock (3 marks):**

After spark-ignition, a flame kernel grows into a roughly spherical flame front. The unburned gas ahead of this front — called the **end gas** — is heated and compressed by:
- The rising piston (compression stroke effect continues briefly)
- The advancing flame front (conductive and radiative heating)

If the **end-gas temperature** exceeds the **auto-ignition temperature** of the fuel before the flame front arrives, the end gas detonates spontaneously. This releases energy almost instantaneously, creating a **high-amplitude pressure wave** that travels at near-sonic speeds inside the chamber, reflecting off walls and producing the audible metallic "ping."

**2. Pressure–Crank Angle Diagram (2 marks):**

```
P (pressure)
 |
 |        Normal: smooth rise    Knock: sharp oscillation
 |            \                       /\
 |             \                      /  \
 |              \                    /    \___/
 |               \                  /
 |                \                /
 |                 \____________/  (Normal)
 |________________________ crank angle
              TDC
```

In normal combustion, pressure rises smoothly and peaks 10°–15° ATDC. In knock, an abrupt secondary pressure spike of 0.5–1.5 MPa appears 5°–15° after TDC, with high-frequency oscillation superimposed.

**3. Factors Affecting Knock (2 marks):**

- **Fuel:** Octane number, chemical composition (paraffins/aromatics)
- **Engine:** Compression ratio, chamber shape, spark timing, bore size
- **Operating:** AFR, intake temperature/pressure, coolant temperature, humidity, deposits

---

**(b) Discuss the effect of compression ratio, spark advance, and air-fuel ratio on knock in SI engines. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Compression Ratio (2 marks):**

A higher compression ratio raises end-gas temperature per $T_2 = T_1 \rho^{\gamma-1}$. Beyond a critical CR, the end gas auto-ignites. Hence, each fuel has a **knock-limited CR**.

**2. Spark Advance (2 marks):**

Advanced spark = combustion starts earlier = pressure peak occurs well before TDC = piston is still moving up = higher pressure opposes piston = end-gas compression is more severe = more knock.

Conversely, **retarded spark** shifts peak pressure toward or after TDC, reducing knock.

**3. Air-Fuel Ratio (3 marks):**

- **Stoichiometric mixture (AFR ≈ 14.7):** Peak flame temperature and pressure — **maximum knock**.
- **Rich mixture (AFR ≈ 12):** Excess fuel vaporizes, absorbing heat (latent heat of vaporization) → lowers flame temperature → **reduced knock**.
- **Lean mixture (AFR ≈ 17–18):** Excess air dilutes charge, lowers peak temperature → **reduced knock** until mixture becomes too lean to sustain combustion.
- Practical minimum knock AFR ≈ 12; practical maximum ≈ 18.

---

#### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a) What is surface ignition? How does it differ from pre-ignition? Explain the causes of surface ignition. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Surface Ignition (2 marks):**

Surface ignition is the **premature ignition** of the air-fuel mixture in the combustion chamber by a **hot surface** other than the spark plug. Hot surfaces include carbon deposits, overheated exhaust valves, glowing spark plug electrodes, and protruding metal edges.

**2. Difference between Surface Ignition and Pre-Ignition (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Surface Ignition | Pre-Ignition |
|--------|------------------|--------------|
| Definition | Any ignition by hot surface | Surface ignition occurring **before** the spark |
| Timing | Either before or after spark | Strictly before spark |
| Severity | Variable | Severe (loss of timing control) |

**3. Causes of Surface Ignition (3 marks):**

- **Carbon deposits** from poor-quality oil or rich operation acting as hot spots.
- **Overheated spark plug electrodes** (wrong heat range selection).
- **Overheated exhaust valves** from poor cooling, lean burn, or valve-guide oil leakage.
- **Glowing carbon particles** from incomplete combustion.
- **Sharp edges/protrusions** in combustion chamber geometry causing local high temperatures.

---

**(b) Explain the phenomenon of "run-on" or "dieseling" in SI engines. Why does it occur and how can it be prevented? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Phenomenon (2 marks):**

Run-on (also called **dieseling** or **after-run**) is the **continued operation of an SI engine for several cycles after the ignition switch has been turned OFF**. The engine behaves like a diesel — igniting the charge without spark — for a brief period before stopping.

**2. Reasons / Why It Occurs (3 marks):**

- **Hot carbon deposits** in the combustion chamber remain above the auto-ignition temperature of the fuel even after the spark is removed.
- These glowing deposits act as **ignition sources**, much like the hot compressed air in a diesel.
- **High compression ratio** aids this auto-ignition.
- **Low octane fuel** is more prone.
- **High idle speed before shut-off** leaves more residual heat.

**3. Prevention (2 marks):**

- Use **higher octane fuel**.
- **Lower the idle speed** before shut-off (fuel-cut strategy in modern ECUs).
- **Clean deposits** periodically (decarbonisation).
- Avoid **over-rich mixtures** that form carbon deposits.
- Use **synthetic oils** with low deposit-forming tendencies.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss pitfalls in Abnormal Combustion questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating knock with pre-ignition.** Knock is *after* spark; pre-ignition is *before* spark. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Listing fuel factors without specifying chemical reasons.** Saying "low octane causes knock" is incomplete; explain *why* (low octane = high paraffin = lower auto-ignition temperature).
> 3. **Forgetting the units in formula derivations.** Always state $T$ in K, $\rho$ is dimensionless, $\gamma$ is dimensionless.
> 4. **Missing the difference between "rich" and "lean" knock behaviour.** Knock peaks at stoichiometric, not at rich.
> 5. **Not drawing the pressure-crank angle diagram** when asked. A diagram is worth at least 2 marks out of 7.
> 6. **Confusing RON, MON, and PON.** KTU expects at least one mention of octane variants if the question is about fuel.
> 7. **Skipping engine design factors** like bore size and chamber geometry.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Abnormal combustion =** Knock + Surface ignition + Pre-ignition + Run-on.
- [x] **Knock** is end-gas auto-ignition **after** spark; produces sharp metallic ping.
- [x] **Pre-ignition** is hot-spot ignition **before** spark; causes loss of timing control.
- [x] **Run-on** is engine running after ignition-off due to glowing deposits.
- [x] **Knock increases with:** Higher CR, advanced timing, stoichiometric AFR, high intake T, high load, large bore, low octane, low humidity.
- [x] **Knock decreases with:** Retarded timing, rich/lean mixture, EGR, water/alcohol injection, high turbulence (squish), higher octane fuel.
- [x] **Surface ignition causes:** Carbon deposits, hot valves, hot plug electrodes, sharp chamber edges.
- [x] **Fuel-related reasons:** Paraffins → low octane → high knock; Aromatics & iso-paraffins → high octane → low knock.
- [x] **Key formula:** $T_2 = T_1 \rho^{\gamma-1}$ with $\gamma \approx 1.35$.
- [x] **Knock-limited CR:** $\rho_{max} \approx 1 + ON/30$ (Heywood empirical).
- [x] **Reference constants:** Auto-ignition temp of iso-octane ≈ 688 K; normal flame speed 15–80 m/s; knock flame speed 300–1000 m/s.
- [x] **Modern mitigation:** Knock sensors + ECU-controlled retarded timing + direct injection + cooled EGR.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lean Burn Engines - Fundamentals of Lean Combustion - Lean Burn SI Engines - Engine Combustion and Emissions - Lean Limit Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lean Burn Engines - Fundamentals of Lean Combustion - Lean Burn SI Engines - Engine Combustion and Emissions - Lean Limit Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lean Burn Engine:** An internal combustion engine (predominantly Spark Ignition type) that is intentionally designed and calibrated to operate with an *air–fuel mixture having a mass ratio significantly greater than the stoichiometric value* — i.e., with an absolute excess of air. The condition is mathematically captured using two dimensionless ratios:

**Equivalence Ratio:**
$$\phi = \frac{\left(F/A\right)_{actual}}{\left(F/A\right)_{stoich}}$$

**Relative Air–Fuel Ratio (Lambda, $\lambda$):**
$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\phi} = \frac{\left(A/F\right)_{actual}}{\left(A/F\right)_{stoich}}$$

- $\lambda < 1 \Rightarrow$ Rich mixture
- $\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow$ Stoichiometric (chemically correct) mixture
- $\lambda > 1 \Rightarrow$ **Lean mixture** (excess air, deficient fuel)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *Lean Burn SI Engine* is conventionally operated in the range $\lambda = 1.2$ to $\lambda = 1.6$. The upper limit is bounded by the **Lean Misfire Limit (LML)**, beyond which flame propagation becomes unstable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (Module 3):** Students must explicitly distinguish between *excess air operation* and *lean burn*. *Lean burn* is a calibrated, steady-state operating strategy with deliberate engine hardware modifications — not merely a transient over-lean condition.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of two campfires using identical wood:

- **Stoichiometric Fire (λ = 1):** You pile exactly the right amount of wood onto glowing embers. The flame is bright yellow-orange, vigorous, smoky, and very hot. It consumes fuel quickly, throwing away unburned soot (analogous to HC and CO emissions).
- **Lean Burn Fire (λ = 1.4):** You spread the same wood over a *much larger* bed of embers, with abundant airflow. The flame is faint blue, almost invisible, far less smoky, cooler in local zones, and remarkably fuel-efficient. However, the flame is fragile — a strong gust of wind can extinguish it (this fragility is exactly the **Lean Misfire Limit**).

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter for an engine?** The cooler peak flame temperature in a lean burn engine directly suppresses **NOx (oxides of nitrogen)** — historically the most stubborn pollutant to control. Simultaneously, complete oxidation of fuel in excess air reduces **CO and HC**. The penalty is operational: the engine approaches an unstable combustion cliff.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (gasoline) | $(A/F)_{stoich}$ | **14.7 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (methane) | $(A/F)_{stoich}$ | **17.2 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (iso-octane) | $(A/F)_{stoich}$ | **15.13 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Universal gas constant | $R_u$ | **8.314** | kJ/(kmol·K) |
| Molecular mass of air | $M_{air}$ | **28.97** | kg/kmol |
| Molecular mass of $\text{N}_2$ | $M_{N_2}$ | **28.014** | kg/kmol |
| Lean burn operating range (SI) | $\lambda_{op}$ | **1.2 – 1.6** | dimensionless |
| Typical Lean Misfire Limit (production SI) | $\lambda_{LML}$ | **1.3 – 1.5** | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All numerical values above are **standard reference figures** used in KTU board examination problems. Memorize the gasoline stoichiometric ratio **14.7:1** and the iso-octane value **15.13:1** — they appear in nearly every combustion calculation problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->
