$$12.8^{0.35} = \exp(0.35 \ln 12.8) = \exp(0.35 \times 2.5494) = \exp(0.8923) = 2.4407$$

$$\eta_{KL} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.4407} = 1 - 0.4097 = 0.5903 \; (59.03\%)$$

*(2 marks)*

**Gains:**

$$\Delta\eta_{abs} = 59.03\% - 56.09\% = 2.94 \text{ percentage points}$$

$$\Delta\eta_{\%} = \frac{2.94}{56.09} \times 100\% = 5.24\% \; \text{ relative gain in efficiency}$$

*(2 marks)*

**Practical implication (1 mark):** A 5.24% relative rise in indicated thermal efficiency translates, at fixed part-load torque, into roughly 5% lower indicated specific fuel consumption (ISFC), since $\text{ISFC} \propto 1/\eta_{th}$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (each costs 1–2 marks):**
> 1. **Confusing partial stratification with full stratification** — full stratification implies an extreme $\phi$ gradient with no rich spark pocket; partial has a *defined* rich kernel.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $\phi$ to $A/F$ when computing fuel mass** — examiners specifically check the step $(F/A) = 1/(A/F)_{stoich}$.
> 3. **Writing $r^{\gamma}$ instead of $r^{\gamma-1}$** in Otto efficiency — a 1-mark penalty every time.
> 4. **Skipping units in numericals** — always write mg or g explicitly.
> 5. **Claiming partial stratification eliminates knock** — it *suppresses* knock; total elimination is impossible.
> 6. **Omitting the Zeldovich reference** when explaining $\text{NO}_x$ — KTU examiners award partial credit only when the mechanism is named.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Partial stratification = lean global mixture ($\phi \approx 0.6$–$0.8$) + rich local pocket near spark ($\phi \approx 1.0$–$1.2$).
- **Key phrase "through partial stratification"** implies the *method* of charge preparation: late direct injection guided by piston bowl geometry — not pre-mixed intake manifold methods.
- **Otto efficiency formula** $\eta = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$ is the *single most important* equation on this topic.
- **Knock criterion:** end-gas ignition delay $\tau \propto \exp(E_a/R_u T) \cdot \phi^{-m}$. Lean bulk + low end-gas $T$ $\Rightarrow$ long $\tau$ $\Rightarrow$ knock suppressed.
- **$\text{NO}_x$ suppression:** lean flame temperature is low $\Rightarrow$ Zeldovich rate falls exponentially.
- **Fuel saving:** typically 25–30% vs stoichiometric homogeneous at part load.
- **Hardware needed:** high-pressure fuel pump (200–350 bar), multi-hole solenoid/piezo injector, piston with bowl/squish geometry, precise ECU for injection timing & spark.
- **NOT** the same as HCCI (no spark needed) or full stratified charge (no rich pocket maintained).
- **Practical limit:** particulate (PM) emissions rise in GDI partial-stratified mode — necessitates GPF (gasoline particulate filter) in modern vehicles.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme outcome mapping:** Topic primarily addresses **CO3** — *Analyze advanced combustion strategies and their thermodynamic implications in modern IC engines.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Microwave-Assisted Spark Ignition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Using Microwave-Assisted Spark Ignition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Microwave-Assisted Spark Ignition (MASI)** is an advanced ignition enhancement technology for Spark-Ignition (SI) internal combustion engines in which a high-frequency electromagnetic field (typically at the **2.45 GHz** ISM band) is superimposed on the conventional spark discharge at the spark plug. The microwave energy interacts with the in-cylinder air–fuel mixture, creating a non-thermal **plasma kernel** that lowers the activation energy barrier, expands the initial flame kernel volume, and stabilises combustion across a wider equivalence-ratio and load envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Cue:** MASI is classified under *Alternative / Plasma-Assisted Ignition Systems* in Module 3 (Advanced Ignition Systems) of PEMUT752. The examiner expects the term **"non-equilibrium plasma ignition"** to be used wherever microwave breakdown physics is discussed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Think of lighting a campfire with a single match versus using the same match after spraying a small cloud of lighter-gas over the kindling:

- The **match alone** (conventional spark) is a tiny, hot point — it must heat a small gas pocket until it auto-ignites. If the wood is damp (lean mixture, low EGR tolerance, cold start), the match often fails.
- The **match + lighter-gas cloud** (microwave + spark) is the same match, but the gas is *pre-activated*. The microwave pre-ionises a much larger volume of the mixture, so the spark now finds a "ready-to-burn" plasma cloud rather than cold molecules.

That is exactly what MASI does: the microwave pre-excites the gas surrounding the spark gap, so when the spark fires, the kernel is **larger, faster-growing, and more reproducible** — even in extremely lean or heavily diluted mixtures that would normally misfire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Key Real-World Metrics to Memorise (in bold):**
> - **Operating Frequency:** 2.45 GHz (also 915 MHz is used in heavy-duty systems)
> - **Typical Resonator Power Density:** 1–10 W/cm³ inside the ignition kernel
> - **Kernel Volume Enhancement:** Up to **~10×** larger than a conventional spark at the same ignition timing.

### 1.3 Why MASI Exists — Limitations of Conventional Spark Ignition

Before diving deep, it is vital to understand the *engineering problem* MASI solves:

1. **Lean-burn limit:** Conventional spark plugs cannot reliably ignite mixtures leaner than φ ≈ 0.6–0.7.
2. **High EGR tolerance:** Engines with cooled EGR (>25%) suffer misfire and cycle-to-cycle variation (COV of IMEP > 10%).
3. **Cold-start emissions:** Hydrocarbon (HC) spikes during cold start are partly due to poor kernel development.
4. **Knock sensitivity:** With retarded timing, flame propagation is unstable.
5. **Diluted combustion (HCCI/RCCI bridging):** Requires a reliable igniter for very lean or stratified mixtures.

MASI addresses all five by creating a **larger, more energetic, more reproducible ignition kernel**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of Microwave–Plasma Interaction in the Combustion Chamber

The MASI process can be broken down into **five sequential physical events**:

- **Step 1 — Microwave Generation:** A solid-state RF source (or compact magnetron) produces a continuous or pulsed 2.45 GHz signal at low power (typically 50–300 W peak).
- **Step 2 — Waveguide / Coaxial Delivery:** The signal is routed through a coaxial transmission line into a modified spark plug that doubles as a **quarter-wave coaxial resonator**.
- **Step 3 — Resonant Field Concentration:** The plug tip geometry acts as a cavity resonator. The electric field amplitude $E_0$ at the gap is amplified by the **Quality factor Q** of the resonator:

$$E_{\text{gap}} = E_{\text{feed}} \cdot \sqrt{Q \cdot \frac{Z_{\text{res}}}{Z_0}}$$

- **Step 4 — Non-thermal Plasma Formation:** Free electrons in the gas gain energy from the oscillating field. Once the reduced electric field $E/N$ exceeds the breakdown threshold (≈ **100 Td**, where 1 Td = $10^{-21}$ V·m²), electron avalanche occurs and a cold, non-equilibrium plasma forms.
- **Step 5 — Spark Trigger & Kernel Expansion:** A conventional high-voltage pulse (~25 kV) is applied synchronously. The spark now propagates through a pre-ionised, chemically activated gas cloud, producing a much larger flame kernel and faster early flame growth.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|------|----------------|
| Microwave Generation | Provides controllable, repeatable energy input without mechanical wear. |
| Resonant Delivery | Concentrates modest input power into extremely high local field strength. |
| Plasma Formation | Pre-excites radicals (O, H, OH) that accelerate chemistry. |
| Spark Trigger | Anchors combustion timing precisely to crank angle. |
| Kernel Expansion | Shortens the *initial flame-development period* (0–5° CA), which is the most cycle-variable phase. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exam)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise the symbol meaning, the typical numerical value, and the **units**.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Each Term | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $f_{\text{mw}} = 2.45 \text{ GHz}$ | Industrial, Scientific, Medical (ISM) band | $2.45 \times 10^9$ Hz |
| 2 | $v_{\text{kernel}} = S_L \cdot A_{\text{kernel}}$ | Laminar flame speed × kernel area | $S_L \approx 0.4$–$1.5$ m/s for hydrocarbons |
| 3 | $E_{\text{field}} = \frac{V_{\text{gap}}}{d_{\text{gap}}}$ | Gap electric field (V/m) | $d \approx 0.6$–$1.0$ mm |
| 4 | $\frac{E}{N} \geq 100 \text{ Td}$ | Reduced field for plasma sustain | $1 \text{ Td} = 10^{-21}$ V·m² |
| 5 | $P_{\text{density}} = \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{kernel}}}$ | Volumetric power density | 1–10 W/cm³ |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{plasma}} = \frac{E_{\text{chem}}}{E_{\text{mw,in}}}$ | Plasma-to-chemistry coupling efficiency | 30–60 % |
| 7 | $\text{COV}_{\text{IMEP}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{IMEP}}}{\overline{\text{IMEP}}} \times 100$ | Cycle-to-cycle variation | MASI reduces by 40–70 % |
| 8 | $\tau_{\text{ignition}} = \tau_{\text{spark}} + \tau_{\text{chem}}$ | Total ignition delay | MASI lowers $\tau_{\text{chem}}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

MASI is not merely academic. It enables:
- **Lean-burn SI engines** that can operate at φ = 0.5 with stable combustion → **3–5 % BSFC improvement**.
- **HCCI / RCCI hybrid systems** that need a controlled ignition trigger to bridge from chemical kinetics to flame propagation.
- **Hydrogen and ammonia-fuelled engines**, which have very high activation energies and wide flammability limits that benefit enormously from plasma pre-activation.
- **Aero-derivative gas turbines** for relight at altitude, where conventional spark plugs are unreliable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Take-away:** MASI does *not replace* the spark plug — it **augments** it. Any board answer that describes MASI as a substitute for the spark is **conceptually wrong** and will lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Resonator Field Amplification at the Spark Gap

The modified spark plug is modelled as a **quarter-wave coaxial resonator** terminated by a capacitive gap. For a resonator with quality factor Q, the stored energy oscillates between the source and the gap, building up the voltage. The relationship is derived from the definition of Q:

**Step 1 — Definition of Quality Factor:**
$$Q = 2\pi \cdot \frac{E_{\text{stored}}}{E_{\text{dissipated per cycle}}}$$

**Step 2 — Energy Stored in the Resonant Mode:**
For a coaxial resonator of characteristic impedance $Z_0$ and input power $P_{\text{in}}$:
$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{Q \cdot P_{\text{in}}}{\pi f_{\text{mw}}}$$

**Step 3 — Energy-to-Voltage Conversion at the Gap Capacitance:**
The gap behaves as a capacitor $C_{\text{gap}}$; stored energy also equals:
$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} C_{\text{gap}} V_{\text{gap}}^2$$

**Step 4 — Equating the Two Expressions for $E_{\text{stored}}$:**
$$\frac{1}{2} C_{\text{gap}} V_{\text{gap}}^2 = \frac{Q \cdot P_{\text{in}}}{\pi f_{\text{mw}}}$$

**Step 5 — Solving for $V_{\text{gap}}$:**
$$V_{\text{gap}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 Q P_{\text{in}}}{\pi f_{\text{mw}} C_{\text{gap}}}}$$

**Step 6 — Converting to Electric Field Using Gap Distance $d_{\text{gap}}$:**
$$E_{\text{gap}} = \frac{V_{\text{gap}}}{d_{\text{gap}}} = \frac{1}{d_{\text{gap}}}\sqrt{\frac{2 Q P_{\text{in}}}{\pi f_{\text{mw}} C_{\text{gap}}}}$$

This shows that even with a modest input power of ~100 W, the field at the gap can reach **MV/m** levels, easily exceeding the breakdown threshold of the air–fuel mixture.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Kernel Volume Enhancement

Assume the conventional spark produces a spherical kernel of radius $r_0$. With MASI, the plasma-activated region extends to a radius $r_{\text{MASI}}$ where the microwave field strength $E(r)$ equals the breakdown threshold $E_{\text{th}}$.

**Step 1 — Field Decay in a Coaxial Resonator (radial fall-off):**
$$E(r) = E_{\text{gap}} \cdot \frac{r_0}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Setting the Boundary Condition at the Plasma Edge:**
$$E_{\text{th}} = E_{\text{gap}} \cdot \frac{r_0}{r_{\text{MASI}}}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the Enhanced Radius:**
$$r_{\text{MASI}} = r_0 \cdot \frac{E_{\text{gap}}}{E_{\text{th}}}$$

**Step 4 — Kernel Volume Ratio:**
$$\frac{V_{\text{MASI}}}{V_0} = \left(\frac{r_{\text{MASI}}}{r_0}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{E_{\text{gap}}}{E_{\text{th}}}\right)^3$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Illustration:**
If $E_{\text{gap}} / E_{\text{th}} \approx 2.15$ (typical for 100 W input and Q ≈ 60):
$$\frac{V_{\text{MASI}}}{V_0} = (2.15)^3 \approx 9.94 \approx 10$$

This is the **~10× kernel volume enhancement** quoted in research literature and accepted as a standard KTU reference figure.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Reduction in Cycle-to-Cycle Variation (Statistical Argument)

The Coefficient of Variation of IMEP depends on the early flame-development angle $\Delta\theta_{0\text{–}5}$ through an empirical correlation (Heywood form):
$$\text{COV}_{\text{IMEP}} = k \cdot (\Delta\theta_{0\text{–}5})^{n}, \quad n \approx 1.5\text{–}2$$

MASI shortens $\Delta\theta_{0\text{–}5}$ because the larger kernel reaches the **flame-propagation phase** sooner. If MASI reduces $\Delta\theta$ from, say, 12° CA to 6° CA, with n = 2:
$$\frac{\text{COV}_{\text{MASI}}}{\text{COV}_{\text{conv}}} = \left(\frac{6}{12}\right)^2 = 0.25$$

A **75 % reduction** in COV — consistent with the experimental 40–70 % range reported (the remainder is lost to wall-quench and turbulence fluctuations that MASI cannot control).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Conventional vs. MASI

| Parameter | Conventional SI | Microwave-Assisted SI | Direction of Effect |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Ignition Energy | 30–80 mJ | 30 mJ (spark) + 50–300 mW·s (MW) | Increased total input |
| Kernel Radius | ~1 mm | ~2.2 mm | ↑ ~2× |
| Kernel Volume | ~4.2 mm³ | ~44 mm³ | ↑ ~10× |
| Lean Limit (φ) | 0.65 | 0.50 | Extended |
| EGR Tolerance | 20 % | 30 %+ | Extended |
| COV of IMEP | 5–8 % | 2–4 % | ↓ 40–60 % |
| HC Emissions | Baseline | –15 to –30 % | Reduced |
| NOₓ Emissions | Baseline | –10 to –20 % (lean) | Reduced |
| System Cost | $5–10 | $200–500 | ↑ ↑ |
| Cycle Life of Plug | ~30,000 km | ~15,000 km (electrode erosion) | ↓ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of a complete Microwave-Assisted Spark Ignition system, mapping every signal and energy path from DC supply to in-cylinder plasma kernel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DC[DC Power Supply 12V / 42V] --> INV[High-Frequency Inverter]
    INV --> MWOSC[Magnetron / Solid-State RF Oscillator]
    MWOSC --> CWL[Coaxial Waveguide Line]
    CWL --> ISO[Isolator and Reflected Power Dump]
    ISO --> CPL[Directional Coupler for Diagnostics]
    CPL --> MWP[Modified Spark Plug with Resonator Gap]
    HVPS[Ignition Coil 25kV] --> HVSW[HV Switch IGBT]
    HVSW --> MWP
    MWP --> KERN[In-Cylinder Plasma Kernel]
    ECU[Engine Control Unit] --> INV
    ECU --> HVSW
    ECU --> DIAG[Timing and Combustion Diagnostic Module]
    CPL --> DIAG
    KERN --> CYL[Combustion Chamber and Piston]
    CYL --> CRANK[Crankshaft Position Sensor]
    CRANK --> ECU
    DIAG --> ECU
```

### 4.1 Reading the Block Diagram (Node-by-Node Explanation)

| Block | Engineering Function |
|-------|---------------------|
| DC Power Supply | Provides the raw DC bus (12 V automotive or 42 V mild-hybrid). |
| High-Frequency Inverter | Converts DC to a high-frequency drive signal for the RF source. |
| Magnetron / SS Oscillator | Generates the 2.45 GHz microwave carrier. |
| Coaxial Waveguide Line | Transports microwave energy with minimal loss into the cylinder. |
| Isolator | Protects the source from reflected power (impedance mismatches). |
| Directional Coupler | Samples forward and reflected power for closed-loop control. |
| Modified Spark Plug | Acts as a quarter-wave resonator; concentrates the field at the gap. |
| Ignition Coil + HV Switch | Provides the conventional 25 kV spark pulse, synchronised by ECU. |
| In-Cylinder Plasma Kernel | The non-thermal plasma + spark kernel where combustion initiates. |
| ECU | Master controller; commands microwave timing, spark timing, and power level based on crank angle and load. |
| Diagnostic Module | Logs microwave S-parameters and in-cylinder pressure for adaptive control. |
| Crank Position Sensor | Provides crank-angle reference for ECU synchronisation. |

### 4.2 Operational Timing Chart (Logical Sequence per Cycle)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ECU as Engine Control Unit
    participant INV as Inverter
    participant MW as Magnetron
    participant HV as Ignition Coil
    participant PLG as Spark Plug
    participant CYL as Combustion Chamber
    ECU->>INV: Trigger MW enable at TDC minus 30 degrees CA
    INV->>MW: Gate signal ON
    MW-->>PLG: 2.45 GHz power delivered
    PLG-->>CYL: Pre-ionisation plasma forms
    ECU->>HV: Trigger spark at TDC minus 10 degrees CA
    HV->>PLG: 25 kV pulse
    PLG->>CYL: Spark discharge through pre-ionised kernel
    CYL-->>ECU: Pressure-rise feedback
    ECU->>MW: Cut MW power at TDC plus 5 degrees CA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **microwave pulse must precede the spark** by typically **2–20° CA**, depending on engine speed. The ECU arbitrates this offset as a calibrated map parameter. Examiners frequently ask: *"Why is microwave applied before the spark?"* — the correct answer is that pre-ionisation must be present so the spark finds a low-impedance plasma path and produces a larger kernel.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Dec 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define Microwave-Assisted Spark Ignition (MASI). Mention the typical operating frequency and the physical mechanism by which it enhances combustion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
MASI is an advanced ignition technology in which a high-frequency electromagnetic field, typically at **2.45 GHz**, is superimposed on a conventional spark plug discharge inside the SI engine combustion chamber. *(1 mark)*
The microwave energy creates a **non-thermal plasma** in the air–fuel mixture by accelerating free electrons to energies above the gas-breakdown threshold (reduced field $E/N \geq 100$ Td). *(1 mark)*
This pre-ionised, radical-rich region enlarges the flame kernel by approximately an order of magnitude and shortens the flame-development period, thereby extending the lean and EGR dilution limits of stable combustion. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. List any three advantages of Microwave-Assisted Spark Ignition over conventional spark ignition in SI engines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Extended lean-burn limit** — stable combustion down to φ ≈ 0.5, improving indicated thermal efficiency by 3–5 %. *(1 mark)*
2. **Reduced cycle-to-cycle variation** — COV of IMEP drops by 40–70 %, enabling smoother idle and lower HC emissions during cold start. *(1 mark)*
3. **Higher EGR tolerance** — allows cooled EGR rates above 25 % without misfire, simultaneously reducing NOₓ and pumping losses. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Module-3 Part B questions carry 14 marks, usually split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** the full Q-A or the full Q-B.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**  *(Option for Module 3 – MASI)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question, Set 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a Microwave-Assisted Spark Ignition system. Clearly state the role of the resonator spark plug and the ECU. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MASI system uses a coaxial resonator spark plug with gap capacitance $C_{\text{gap}} = 1.5$ pF, gap distance $d_{\text{gap}} = 0.8$ mm, quality factor $Q = 60$, and a microwave input power $P_{\text{in}} = 100$ W at $f_{\text{mw}} = 2.45 \times 10^9$ Hz. The breakdown threshold of the air–fuel mixture is $E_{\text{th}} = 1.0 \times 10^6$ V/m. Calculate: (i) the gap voltage, (ii) the gap electric field, and (iii) the kernel volume enhancement ratio over a conventional spark kernel of radius 1 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Functional Architecture (7 marks):**
A MASI system consists of three integrated subsystems: a **microwave generation chain**, a **conventional ignition chain**, and a **control and diagnostic chain**, all converging at the **modified resonator spark plug** inside the combustion chamber.

- **Microwave chain:** DC bus → high-frequency inverter → magnetron / solid-state oscillator (2.45 GHz) → coaxial waveguide → isolator → directional coupler → resonator plug. *(2 marks)*
- **Ignition chain:** Ignition coil (25 kV) → HV switch (IGBT) → resonator plug. *(1 mark)*
- **Control and diagnostic chain:** ECU synchronises both chains to crank angle, samples reflected microwave power via the directional coupler, and reads in-cylinder pressure to close the loop. *(2 marks)*
- **Role of the resonator plug:** It acts as a quarter-wave coaxial resonator that concentrates the microwave field at the gap, producing a high $E/N$ for plasma formation. *(1 mark)*
- **Role of the ECU:** Arbitrates MW enable, MW cutoff, and spark timing; performs closed-loop control using crank-angle and pressure sensors. *(1 mark)*

*(A neat block diagram similar to Section 4.1 of these notes should be reproduced on the answer script.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $C_{\text{gap}} = 1.5$ pF $= 1.5 \times 10^{-12}$ F
- $d_{\text{gap}} = 0.8$ mm $= 0.8 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $Q = 60$
- $P_{\text{in}} = 100$ W
- $f_{\text{mw}} = 2.45 \times 10^9$ Hz
- $E_{\text{th}} = 1.0 \times 10^6$ V/m
- Conventional kernel radius $r_0 = 1$ mm

**Step 1 — Gap Voltage (Derivation 1, Section 3.1):**
$$V_{\text{gap}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 Q P_{\text{in}}}{\pi f_{\text{mw}} C_{\text{gap}}}}$$

Substituting values:
$$V_{\text{gap}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 60 \times 100}{\pi \times 2.45 \times 10^9 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$V_{\text{gap}} = \sqrt{\frac{12000}{1.154 \times 10^{-2}}}$$

$$V_{\text{gap}} = \sqrt{1.039 \times 10^6} \approx 1019 \text{ V}$$

**Result:** $V_{\text{gap}} \approx 1.02$ kV. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Gap Electric Field:**
$$E_{\text{gap}} = \frac{V_{\text{gap}}}{d_{\text{gap}}} = \frac{1019}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$E_{\text{gap}} \approx 1.27 \times 10^6 \text{ V/m}$$

**Result:** $E_{\text{gap}} \approx 1.27$ MV/m. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Kernel Volume Enhancement (Derivation 2, Section 3.2):**
$$\frac{V_{\text{MASI}}}{V_0} = \left(\frac{E_{\text{gap}}}{E_{\text{th}}}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{1.27 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times 10^6}\right)^3 = (1.27)^3$$

$$\frac{V_{\text{MASI}}}{V_0} \approx 2.05$$

**Result:** Kernel volume is approximately **2.05×** the conventional kernel volume. *(2 marks)*

**Discussion (1 mark):**
The result (2×) is lower than the ideal ~10× quoted in literature because real plug resonators have additional losses (electrode resistance, wall quench, plasma absorption) that reduce the effective Q below the theoretical value. Designers typically use higher Q cavities or higher input power to push the ratio toward 10×.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**  *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question, Set 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the physical mechanism by which microwave energy creates a non-thermal plasma in the engine cylinder. Explain the role of reduced electric field ($E/N$) and the breakdown threshold. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-cylinder SI engine is being retrofitted with MASI to operate at φ = 0.55 (ultra-lean). With MASI, the COV of IMEP is reduced from 7 % to 2.5 %. The engine delivers an average IMEP of 8.5 bar. Using the empirical relation $\text{COV}_{\text{IMEP}} = k \cdot (\Delta\theta_{0\text{–}5})^{1.8}$ where the constant $k = 0.024$ per degree of crank angle, determine: (i) the flame-development angle with conventional ignition, and (ii) the flame-development angle with MASI. Comment on the significance of the reduction. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B:**

**(a) Physical Mechanism of Non-Thermal Plasma Formation (7 marks):**

In a microwave field, free electrons present in the air–fuel mixture are accelerated by the oscillating electric field $E$. The energy gained by an electron over a mean free path is: *(2 marks)*

$$\varepsilon_{\text{gain}} = e \cdot E \cdot \lambda_e$$

where $\lambda_e$ is the electron mean free path. For plasma formation, this energy must exceed the ionisation energy of the dominant molecules (≈ 12–15 eV for O₂, N₂, H₂).

The **reduced electric field** $E/N$ (where $N$ is the number density of gas molecules, in m⁻³) determines the electron energy distribution function. The critical parameter is: *(2 marks)*

$$\frac{E}{N} \geq 100 \text{ Td (Townsend)}$$

Above 100 Td, the electron-impact ionisation rate exceeds the electron-loss rate (attachment + diffusion), causing **electron avalanche** and breakdown. *(1 mark)*

Unlike thermal plasma (arc discharge), the electron temperature ($T_e \approx 10{,}000$–$50{,}000$ K) far exceeds the heavy-particle temperature ($T_g \approx 300$–$500$ K). This is the **non-equilibrium** state. The energetic electrons dissociate O₂ and H₂ to produce radicals (O, H, OH), which dramatically accelerate the chain-branching reactions of hydrocarbon oxidation. *(2 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- COV$_{\text{conv}}$ = 7 %, COV$_{\text{MASI}}$ = 2.5 %
- Empirical relation: $\text{COV}_{\text{IMEP}} = k \cdot (\Delta\theta_{0\text{–}5})^{1.8}$, with $k = 0.024$

**Step 1 — Solving for $\Delta\theta_{0\text{–}5}$:**
$$\Delta\theta_{0\text{–}5} = \left(\frac{\text{COV}_{\text{IMEP}}}{k}\right)^{1/1.8}$$

**Step 2 — Conventional Ignition Flame-Development Angle:** *(2 marks)*
$$\Delta\theta_{\text{conv}} = \left(\frac{7}{0.024}\right)^{1/1.8} = (291.67)^{0.5556}$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(291.67) = 5.675$; $0.5556 \times 5.675 = 3.153$; $e^{3.153} \approx 23.4°$ CA

**Result:** $\Delta\theta_{\text{conv}} \approx 23.4$° CA.

**Step 3 — MASI Flame-Development Angle:** *(2 marks)*
$$\Delta\theta_{\text{MASI}} = \left(\frac{2.5}{0.024}\right)^{1/1.8} = (104.17)^{0.5556}$$

$\ln(104.17) = 4.646$; $0.5556 \times 4.646 = 2.582$; $e^{2.582} \approx 13.2°$ CA

**Result:** $\Delta\theta_{\text{MASI}} \approx 13.2°$ CA.

**Step 4 — Reduction and Significance:** *(3 marks)*
- Absolute reduction: $23.4 - 13.2 = 10.2$° CA
- Relative reduction: $10.2 / 23.4 \approx 43.6$ %
- **Significance:** A ~44 % shorter flame-development angle means combustion reaches the rapid-burn phase earlier in the cycle. This allows: (i) **earlier spark timing** for the same MBT position, increasing indicated efficiency; (ii) **stable ultra-lean operation** at φ = 0.55 without misfire; (iii) **lower HC and CO emissions** due to more complete early-cycle burn; and (iv) **reduced sensitivity to in-cylinder turbulence fluctuations**, which is the root cause of cycle-to-cycle variation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on MASI Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing MASI with HCCI.** MASI is *spark-assisted by microwaves*; HCCI is *no spark at all*. Do not interchange the two — examiners deduct heavily.
> 2. **Writing "microwave replaces the spark."** Wrong. MASI *augments* the spark; both are present and synchronised.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Always state $V_{\text{gap}}$ in V (or kV), $E_{\text{gap}}$ in V/m, and crank angles in °CA. Marks are lost for missing units even if the number is correct.
> 4. **Using 2.45 MHz instead of 2.45 GHz.** The microwave ISM band is **gigahertz**, not megahertz. This is a very common slip and is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Skipping the ECU synchronisation logic.** Any block diagram that does not show the ECU commanding both the microwave and the spark is incomplete.
> 6. **Treating the plasma as thermal equilibrium.** Non-equilibrium is the entire *raison d'être* of MASI — always specify $T_e \gg T_g$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **MASI = Microwave (2.45 GHz) + Spark (25 kV), applied synchronously in the same plug.**
- [x] **Operating frequency:** 2.45 GHz (ISM band); 915 MHz used in heavy-duty systems.
- [x] **Physical mechanism:** Microwave field accelerates free electrons → $E/N \geq 100$ Td → non-thermal plasma → radical-rich kernel → larger flame kernel.
- [x] **Hardware chain:** DC → Inverter → Magnetron/SS Oscillator → Coaxial line → Isolator → Coupler → **Resonator Spark Plug**.
- [x] **Key equation:** $V_{\text{gap}} = \sqrt{2 Q P_{\text{in}} / (\pi f C_{\text{gap}})}$.
- [x] **Kernel volume enhancement:** Up to **~10×** conventional spark.
- [x] **COV of IMEP reduction:** 40–70 %; flame-development angle reduction: ~40–45 %.
- [x] **Lean-burn limit extension:** Down to φ ≈ 0.5; EGR tolerance > 25 %.
- [x] **Emissions effect:** HC ↓ 15–30 %, NOₓ ↓ 10–20 % (in lean operation).
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Higher cost (~$300 per plug), reduced electrode life (~15,000 km), complex ECU calibration, RF shielding needed.
- [x] **Common error trap:** Microwave is *applied before* the spark, not simultaneously — the pre-ionisation window is 2–20° CA.
- [x] **Exam keywords to use verbatim:** "non-equilibrium plasma", "reduced electric field", "quarter-wave coaxial resonator", "flame-development period", "lean-burn limit extension", "cycle-to-cycle variation".
- [x] **Industrial relevance:** Hydrogen engines, ammonia engines, HCCI bridging, altitude relight of aero-derivative gas turbines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LNT Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** LNT Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lean NOx Trap (LNT) Technology**, also known as **NOx Storage and Reduction (NSR)** catalyst technology, is an advanced exhaust aftertreatment system designed to remove nitrogen oxides (NOx) from the exhaust stream of **lean-burn internal combustion engines** operating under oxygen-excess conditions (λ > 1).

The LNT system traps (stores) NOx during lean operation as nitrate compounds on a barium-based storage material, and then periodically releases and reduces the trapped NOx during a brief rich operating phase, regenerating the trap for the next cycle.

## 1.2 The Problem: Why LNT is Required

| Engine Type | Air-Fuel Ratio (λ) | Excess Oxygen? | Conventional TWC Effective? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric (Gasoline) | λ = 1.0 | No | **Yes** (100% efficient) |
| **Lean-Burn (Diesel/GDI)** | **λ = 1.2 – 2.0** | **Yes (excess O₂)** | **No (cannot reduce NOx)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Limitation:** In lean conditions, abundant oxygen (O₂) reacts preferentially with the reducing agents (CO, H₂, HC) over NOx. Hence, the three-way catalyst (TWC) fails, and NOx slips into the atmosphere unreacted.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine the LNT as a **"parking garage"** for NOx molecules:

1. **Lean Phase (Garage Open for Parking):** During normal driving, the engine runs lean (extra air). The exhaust contains lots of O₂ and some NOx. The LNT "opens its doors" and **stores** the NOx molecules onto special storage sites (like parking cars in spots).
2. **Rich Phase (Garage Closes for Cleanup):** Once enough NOx is stored (after 30–120 seconds), the engine briefly switches to rich operation (less air, more fuel). This creates a reducing atmosphere. The stored NOx is **released**, **broken apart**, and **converted to harmless N₂ and CO₂** — cleaning the storage spots.
3. The cycle repeats.

The storage material is typically **Barium Oxide (BaO)**, and the catalyst coating contains **Platinum (Pt)** for oxidation and **Rhodium (Rh)** for reduction.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Standards)

- **NOx Storage Capacity:** Typically **0.5 – 1.5 g NOx / liter of catalyst**
- **Regeneration Duration:** Approximately **2 – 10 seconds** rich phase
- **Operating Temperature Window:** **300°C – 500°C** (optimal)
- **Conversion Efficiency:** Up to **90%** under well-controlled conditions
- **Standard Metric:** **g NOx / kWh** (for engines) or **g NOx / km** (for vehicles)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LNT Catalyst Architecture (Layered Washcoat Design)

The LNT is a **ceramic honeycomb monolith** (cordierite or SiC substrate) with multiple layers of washcoat:

$$\text{Substrate} \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \text{ washcoat} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Storage Component: BaO / BaCO}_3 \\ \text{Oxidation Catalyst: Pt} \\ \text{Reduction Catalyst: Rh} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Barium?** Barium has a strong chemical affinity for NO₂ (forms barium nitrate), acting as an effective "chemical sponge" for NOx.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Two-Phase Operation

### **PHASE 1: NOx Storage (Lean Operation, λ > 1)**

**Step 1 — NO Oxidation:**
$$\text{NO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt}} \text{NO}_2$$

**Step 2 — Trapping (Storage):**
$$\text{BaO} + 2\text{NO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ba(NO}_3)_2$$

(Barium oxide reacts with NO₂ to form barium nitrate, effectively storing NOx as a solid.)

> Carbon dioxide from exhaust also reacts to form BaCO₃:
> $$\text{BaO} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{BaCO}_3$$

### **PHASE 2: NOx Reduction (Regeneration, λ < 1)**

**Step 1 — Nitrate Decomposition (release of NOx):**
$$\text{Ba(NO}_3)_2 \rightarrow \text{BaO} + 2\text{NO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$

**Step 2 — Reduction of released NOx by Rh:**
$$\begin{aligned}
2\text{NO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 &\rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
2\text{NO}_2 + 4\text{CO} &\rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 4\text{CO}_2 \\
2\text{NO}_2 + 4\text{C}_x\text{H}_y &\rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 4\text{CO}_2 + 2y\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The regeneration phase releases stored NOx, which is then immediately reduced by the Rh catalyst in the same LNT brick — producing only **N₂, CO₂, and H₂O**.

## 2.3 Overall Stoichiometry of Complete Cycle

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Lean Phase:} \quad & \text{NO} \xrightarrow{\text{Pt, O}_2} \text{NO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{BaO}} \text{Ba(NO}_3)_2 \\
\text{Rich Phase:} \quad & \text{Ba(NO}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{} \text{NO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Rh, HC/CO/H}_2} \text{N}_2 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

## 2.4 Sulfur Poisoning — The Major Limitation

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Limitation:** Diesel fuel contains sulfur (even after ultra-low sulfur diesel — ULSD). SO₂ oxidizes more easily than NO and forms stable **barium sulfate**:

$$\text{BaO} + \text{SO}_3 \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4$$

Barium sulfate is **thermally very stable** and cannot be decomposed under normal regeneration conditions. Therefore:

- Sulfur accumulates → storage capacity drops → **DeSOx cycle** (high-temperature 600–700°C rich purge) is needed periodically.
- This is why **LNT is less favored for heavy-duty diesel** but commonly used in **light-duty GDI gasoline engines**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Equation** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units / Typical Value** |
|---|---|---|
| NOx Storage Reaction | $\text{BaO} + 2\text{NO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ba(NO}_3)_2$ | Stoichiometric |
| NOx Reduction Reaction | $\text{NO} + \text{CO} \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\text{N}_2 + \text{CO}_2$ | Stoichiometric |
| Lean Phase Duration | $\tau_\text{lean}$ | 30 – 120 s |
| Rich Phase Duration | $\tau_\text{rich}$ | 2 – 10 s |
| NOx Storage Capacity | $\eta_\text{storage} = \dfrac{m_\text{NOx,stored}}{m_\text{NOx,inlet}}$ | Dimensionless (0.7 – 0.95) |
| Overall NOx Conversion | $\eta_\text{NOx} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_\text{NOx,in} - \dot{m}_\text{NOx,out}}{\dot{m}_\text{NOx,in}}$ | % (typical: 70 – 90%) |
| Regeneration Air-Fuel Ratio | $\lambda_\text{rich}$ | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| Sulfur Poisoning Rate | $\dfrac{dC_\text{trap}}{dt} = -k_\text{SO}_x \cdot C_\text{trap}$ | First-order decay |
| DeSOx Temperature | $T_\text{desulfation}$ | 600 – 700 °C |
| Operating Temperature Window | $T_\text{opt,LNT}$ | 300 – 500 °C |
| Space Velocity (GHSV) | $\text{GHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_\text{exhaust}}{V_\text{cat}}$ | $\text{h}^{-1}$ (typical: 30,000 – 60,000) |
| Lambda (λ) | $\lambda = \dfrac{(\text{A}/\text{F})_\text{actual}}{(\text{A}/\text{F})_\text{stoich}}$ | Lean: >1 ; Rich: <1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** LNT is widely deployed in:
> - **Light-duty diesel vehicles** (Euro 5/6 compliant cars)
> - **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)** vehicles
> - **Lean-burn natural gas engines**
> - As a **compact, cost-effective alternative to SCR** for vehicles with limited under-floor space.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass of NOx Stored per Cycle

We need to derive how much NOx an LNT can store in one lean phase before regeneration.

### Given:

- LNT catalyst volume: $V_\text{cat}$ (liters)
- Exhaust mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_\text{exh}$ (kg/s)
- NOx concentration in exhaust: $C_\text{NOx}$ (ppm or mg/Nm³)
- NOx storage density per unit volume of catalyst: $\rho_\text{NOx,storage}$ (g/L)

### Step 1 — Mass of NOx in Exhaust Stream

$$\dot{m}_\text{NOx} = \dot{m}_\text{exh} \cdot C_\text{NOx} \quad [\text{g/s}]$$

### Step 2 — Maximum Storage Capacity of LNT

$$m_\text{NOx,max} = V_\text{cat} \cdot \rho_\text{NOx,storage} \quad [\text{g}]$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Lean Phase Duration

Setting stored NOx = capacity:

$$V_\text{cat} \cdot \rho_\text{NOx,storage} = \dot{m}_\text{exh} \cdot C_\text{NOx} \cdot \tau_\text{lean,max}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_\text{lean,max} = \frac{V_\text{cat} \cdot \rho_\text{NOx,storage}}{\dot{m}_\text{exh} \cdot C_\text{NOx}}}$$

### Interpretation

This defines the **maximum time** the engine can run in lean mode before the LNT saturates. Beyond this, NOx will slip through unstored.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** If asked to find lean phase duration, identify $V_\text{cat}$, $\rho_\text{NOx,storage}$, $\dot{m}_\text{exh}$, and $C_\text{NOx}$ from the problem data — then apply the formula directly.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A light-duty GDI vehicle is fitted with an LNT catalyst of volume **2.5 L** having a NOx storage density of **1.2 g/L**. The engine exhaust mass flow rate is **0.05 kg/s** with a NOx concentration of **500 ppm (by mass equivalent ≈ 0.82 g/kg)**. Calculate the maximum lean phase duration.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum storable NOx mass:**
$$m_\text{NOx,max} = V_\text{cat} \times \rho_\text{NOx,storage} = 2.5 \times 1.2 = 3.0 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — NOx mass flow rate in exhaust:**
$$\dot{m}_\text{NOx} = 0.05 \times 0.82 = 0.041 \text{ g/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum lean phase duration:**
$$\tau_\text{lean,max} = \frac{m_\text{NOx,max}}{\dot{m}_\text{NOx}} = \frac{3.0}{0.041} \approx 73.2 \text{ s}$$

**Answer:** The LNT can store NOx for approximately **73 seconds** before regeneration is required.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: LNT Operating Zones

| **Operating Regime** | **Temperature** | **Dominant Phenomenon** | **Engine Action** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cold Start | < 200 °C | No NOx storage (kinetically limited) | Engine runs rich to heat catalyst |
| Low-Temp Lean | 200 – 300 °C | Slow NO oxidation; partial storage | Passive regeneration |
| Optimum Storage | **300 – 500 °C** | **Maximum storage efficiency** | **Normal lean operation** |
| Sulfur Accumulation | 300 – 500 °C | Gradual poisoning by SO₂ | Periodic DeSOx required |
| Thermal Deactivation | > 800 °C | Sintering of Pt, Ba compounds | Avoid prolonged high-T exposure |
| Rich Regeneration | 300 – 500 °C | NOx released and reduced | Brief rich pulse (λ ≈ 0.85–0.95) |
| DeSOx | 600 – 700 °C | Barium sulfate decomposed | Long rich purge (5–10 min) |

## 3.4 Derivation: Average NOx Conversion Efficiency

Over one complete LNT cycle (lean + rich), the **average NOx conversion** is:

$$\eta_\text{avg} = \frac{\eta_\text{storage} \cdot \tau_\text{lean} + \eta_\text{reduction} \cdot \tau_\text{rich}}{\tau_\text{lean} + \tau_\text{rich}}$$

Where:

- $\eta_\text{storage}$ = fraction of NOx captured during lean phase (typically 0.95)
- $\eta_\text{reduction}$ = fraction of released NOx converted to N₂ during rich phase (typically 0.90)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $\tau_\text{lean} \gg \tau_\text{rich}$, the system is designed for **high storage efficiency**, while brief rich pulses ensure complete reduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LNT Functional Block Diagram — Aftertreatment System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine\nLean Burn GDI or Diesel] --> EXH[Exhaust Manifold]
    EXH --> DOC[Diesel Oxidation Catalyst\nDOC\nCO, HC to CO2, H2O]
    DOC --> DPF[Particulate Filter\nDPF\nSoot Trap]
    DPF --> LNT[Lean NOx Trap LNT\nBaO + Pt + Rh on Washcoat]
    LNT --> TAIL[Tailpipe Emission\nN2, CO2, H2O]
    ECU[Engine ECU\nLambda Control Unit] -.->|Lambda Modulation\nLean or Rich| ENG
    SENS[NOx and Lambda Sensors] -.->|Feedback Signal| ECU
    SENS -.->|Post LNT Measurement| LNT
```

## 4.2 LNT Internal Washcoat Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MONO[Monolith Substrate Cordierite SiC]
        CHAN[Honeycomb Channel Walls]
    end
    CHAN --> AL2O3[Alumina Washcoat\nHigh Surface Area Porous]
    AL2O3 --> PT[Platinum Catalyst Layer\nNO to NO2 Oxidation]
    AL2O3 --> RH[Rhodium Catalyst Layer\nNOx Reduction Site]
    AL2O3 --> BAO[Barium Oxide Storage Sites\nNOx Trapping]
    BAO --> BANO3[Barium Nitrate BaNO3 2\nStored During Lean Phase]
```

## 4.3 LNT Operating Cycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LeanPhase
    LeanPhase: LEAN PHASE lambda greater than 1\nNO oxidized by Pt to NO2\nNO2 trapped by BaO as BaNO3 2
    LeanPhase --> SaturationCheck: Continuously
    SaturationCheck: Trap Saturated?
    SaturationCheck --> LeanPhase: NO\nContinue Storage
    SaturationCheck --> RichPhase: YES\nRegenerate
    RichPhase: RICH PHASE lambda less than 1\nBaNO3 decomposes to NO2\nNO2 reduced by Rh with HC CO H2\nto N2 CO2 H2O
    RichPhase --> LeanPhase: Storage Sites Cleared\nReturn to Lean
    RichPhase --> Desulfation: Periodic\nSulfur Accumulation
    Desulfation: DESULFATION 600 to 700 deg C\nBaSO4 decomposed to BaO\nSOx released and reduced
    Desulfation --> LeanPhase: Storage Restored\nResume Lean
```

## 4.4 LNT Position in Vehicle Aftertreatment Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Out\nNOx, CO, HC, PM, O2] --> B[DOC\nOxidation of CO, HC\nNO to NO2]
    B --> C[DPF\nPM Soot Removal]
    C --> D[LNT\nNOx Storage and Reduction\nControlled Rich Pulse]
    D --> E[Clean Exhaust\nN2, CO2, H2O]
    style D fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Lean NOx Trap (LNT) technology. Why is it required in lean-burn engines instead of a conventional three-way catalyst?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

LNT is an exhaust aftertreatment system used in lean-burn engines (λ > 1) to reduce NOx emissions. **(1 Mark)**

It consists of a washcoat containing platinum (oxidation), rhodium (reduction), and barium oxide (NOx storage). During lean operation, NOx is oxidized by Pt to NO₂ and stored as barium nitrate on BaO. During brief rich regeneration, the nitrate decomposes and the released NOx is reduced by Rh to N₂. **(1 Mark)**

A conventional three-way catalyst cannot reduce NOx in lean conditions because excess O₂ preferentially oxidizes CO and HC, leaving NOx untreated. **(1 Mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the major components of an LNT washcoat and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Platinum (Pt)** — Catalyzes oxidation of NO to NO₂ during lean phase. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Barium Oxide (BaO)** — Acts as the NOx storage material; traps NO₂ as barium nitrate [Ba(NO₃)₂]. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Rhodium (Rh)** — Catalyzes reduction of released NOx to N₂ during regeneration. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a Lean NOx Trap (LNT) with a neat sketch of the catalyst structure. Describe in detail the chemical reactions occurring during the **lean NOx storage phase** and the **rich regeneration phase**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the phenomenon of **sulfur poisoning** in LNT catalysts. What is the **DeSOx process** and why is it necessary? Mention typical temperatures involved. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A.(a):** **(7 Marks)**

**Working Principle of LNT:**

The LNT operates in two alternating phases on a single catalyst brick containing Pt, Rh, and BaO supported on a porous alumina washcoat over a ceramic monolith. **(1 Mark)**

**Phase 1 — Lean NOx Storage (λ > 1):**

- Engine runs with excess air. Exhaust contains O₂, NO, CO₂, H₂O.
- **Step 1:** NO is oxidized to NO₂ over Pt catalyst:
$$\text{NO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt}} \text{NO}_2 \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$
- **Step 2:** NO₂ is trapped on barium oxide storage sites:
$$\text{BaO} + 2\text{NO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ba(NO}_3)_2 \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$
- The storage continues until trap saturation (typically 30–120 seconds).

**Phase 2 — Rich Regeneration (λ < 1):**

- Engine control briefly enriches the mixture (λ ≈ 0.85–0.95), creating a reducing exhaust.
- **Step 1:** Stored barium nitrate decomposes thermally:
$$\text{Ba(NO}_3)_2 \rightarrow \text{BaO} + 2\text{NO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$
- **Step 2:** Released NO₂ is reduced by Rh using HC, CO, and H₂ as reductants:
$$\begin{aligned} 2\text{NO}_2 + 4\text{CO} &\rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 4\text{CO}_2 \\ 2\text{NO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 &\rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} \end{aligned} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$
- The regeneration lasts 2–10 seconds, after which lean operation resumes.

**Sketch of LNT Catalyst Structure:** A honeycomb monolith with channel walls coated with porous γ-Al₂O₃ washcoat, into which Pt, Rh, and BaO are impregnated. **(1 Mark)**

**Overall Outcome:** NOx is converted to harmless N₂, CO₂, and H₂O. **(1 Mark)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A.(b):** **(7 Marks)**

**Sulfur Poisoning:**

Diesel fuel and lubricating oil contain trace sulfur compounds. During combustion, sulfur forms SO₂ and SO₃ in the exhaust. **(1 Mark)**

These sulfur oxides react **preferentially and irreversibly** with BaO to form **barium sulfate**:

$$\text{BaO} + \text{SO}_3 \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4 \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

Barium sulfate is **thermally far more stable** than barium nitrate and cannot be decomposed under normal regeneration conditions. **(1 Mark)**

**Consequence:** The active NOx storage sites (BaO) become blocked by BaSO₄, **drastically reducing storage capacity** and overall NOx conversion efficiency over time. **(1 Mark)**

**DeSOx Process:**

DeSOx (desulfation) is a **periodic high-temperature rich regeneration** procedure designed to decompose accumulated barium sulfate and restore catalyst activity. **(1 Mark)**

The exhaust temperature is raised to **600–700 °C** (above the normal operating window) and held under rich conditions (λ ≈ 0.85) for an extended period of 5–10 minutes. **(1 Mark)**

At these temperatures, BaSO₄ decomposes:

$$\text{BaSO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\text{rich}} \text{BaO} + \text{SO}_x \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

The released SOₓ is reduced by Rh to H₂S, which is then oxidized in the DOC to SO₂. **Necessity:** Without periodic DeSOx, the LNT would progressively lose its effectiveness, making it impractical for long-term vehicle use. **(1 Mark)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare **LNT** and **SCR** (Selective Catalytic Reduction) technologies for NOx reduction in diesel engines. Discuss their relative merits, demerits, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A light-duty diesel engine produces exhaust at a rate of **0.08 kg/s** with a NOx concentration of **600 ppm (≈ 0.985 g/kg)**. The LNT catalyst volume is **3.0 L** with a storage density of **1.0 g/L**. Calculate:
   (i) The maximum lean phase storage duration. **(3 Marks)**
   (ii) If the rich regeneration phase requires an additional **8% fuel penalty**, calculate the average fuel penalty per cycle assuming $\tau_\text{rich} = 6$ s and $\tau_\text{lean} = ?$ (use your answer from (i)). **(4 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B.(a):** **(7 Marks)**

| **Parameter** | **LNT** | **SCR** |
|---|---|---|
| Reducing Agent | None (uses HC/CO from rich exhaust) | Requires AdBlue (urea → NH₃) |
| Operating Temperature | 300–500 °C (limited) | 250–500 °C (wider range) |
| Conversion Efficiency | 70–90% | 85–98% |
| Sulfur Sensitivity | **High** (deactivation) | Low (more tolerant) |
| Cost | Lower (compact) | Higher (urea tank + dosing system) |
| Maintenance | Periodic DeSOx required | AdBlue refilling |
| Preferred Application | **Light-duty GDI / small diesel** | **Heavy-duty diesel trucks, buses** |

**Merits of LNT:** No secondary fluid tank; compact; lower cost; suitable for tight packaging in passenger cars. **(1 Mark)**

**Demerits of LNT:** Sensitive to sulfur; lower conversion efficiency; periodic DeSOx needed; fuel penalty during regeneration. **(1 Mark)**

**Merits of SCR:** Higher conversion efficiency; tolerant to sulfur; no rich operation needed (no fuel penalty). **(1 Mark)**

**Demerits of SCR:** Requires urea infrastructure (AdBlue tank, dosing pump); risk of ammonia slip; higher cost; bulky system. **(1 Mark)**

**Typical Application:** LNT in light-duty vehicles; SCR in heavy-duty trucks, ships, and large stationary engines. **(2 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B.(b):** **(7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_\text{exh} = 0.08$ kg/s
- $C_\text{NOx} = 600$ ppm ≈ 0.985 g/kg
- $V_\text{cat} = 3.0$ L
- $\rho_\text{NOx,storage} = 1.0$ g/L
- $\tau_\text{rich} = 6$ s
- Fuel penalty during rich phase = 8%

**Part (i) — Maximum Lean Phase Duration: (3 Marks)**

Step 1 — Maximum storable NOx mass:
$$m_\text{NOx,max} = V_\text{cat} \times \rho = 3.0 \times 1.0 = 3.0 \text{ g} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

Step 2 — NOx mass flow rate:
$$\dot{m}_\text{NOx} = 0.08 \times 0.985 = 0.0788 \text{ g/s} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

Step 3 — Maximum lean phase duration:
$$\tau_\text{lean} = \frac{3.0}{0.0788} \approx 38.07 \text{ s} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

**Part (ii) — Average Fuel Penalty: (4 Marks)**

Step 1 — Total cycle time:
$$\tau_\text{cycle} = \tau_\text{lean} + \tau_\text{rich} = 38.07 + 6 = 44.07 \text{ s} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

Step 2 — Fraction of time in rich phase:
$$f_\text{rich} = \frac{\tau_\text{rich}}{\tau_\text{cycle}} = \frac{6}{44.07} = 0.1362 \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

Step 3 — Time-averaged fuel penalty:
$$\eta_\text{fuel,average} = f_\text{rich} \times 8\% = 0.1362 \times 8\% \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_\text{fuel,average} \approx 1.09\%} \quad \textbf{(1 Mark)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** "fuel penalty during rich phase" (8%) with "average fuel penalty" — the average is much lower because the lean phase dominates the cycle. **(Lose 2 Marks commonly)**
> - Always clearly write the **two-step reaction sequence** for NOx storage (NO → NO₂ → Ba(NO₃)₂). Skipping the Pt oxidation step is a frequent mistake. **(Lose 1 Mark)**
> - For numerical problems, remember to **convert ppm to mass fraction correctly** using molar masses (N = 14, O = 16, NO₂ = 46). **(Lose 1 Mark)**
> - In comparison questions, present a **tabular comparison** — KTU examiners award full marks for clear tabulation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LNT Technology**

- **Full Form:** Lean NOx Trap (also called NOx Storage Reduction, NSR).
- **Why needed:** Lean-burn engines (diesel, GDI) operate at λ > 1, where conventional three-way catalyst (TWC) cannot reduce NOx.
- **Three key washcoat components:**
   - **Platinum (Pt)** — oxidizes NO to NO₂.
   - **Barium Oxide (BaO)** — stores NO₂ as barium nitrate Ba(NO₃)₂.
   - **Rhodium (Rh)** — reduces released NOx to N₂ during rich phase.
- **Lean Phase Reaction:** $\text{NO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2$, then $\text{BaO} + 2\text{NO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Ba(NO}_3)_2$
- **Rich Phase Reaction:** $\text{Ba(NO}_3)_2 \rightarrow \text{BaO} + \text{NO}_2$, then $\text{NO}_2 + \text{CO}/\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + \text{CO}_2/\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Operating Temperature Window:** **300 – 500 °C** (optimal storage).
- **Lean Phase Duration:** **30 – 120 s**; **Rich Phase:** **2 – 10 s**.
- **Conversion Efficiency:** **70 – 90%** NOx reduction.
- **Sulfur Poisoning:** $\text{BaO} + \text{SO}_3 \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4$ (irreversible at normal T).
- **DeSOx Process:** High-temperature (600–700 °C) rich purge for 5–10 minutes to decompose BaSO₄.
- **Storage Duration Formula:** $\tau_\text{lean,max} = \dfrac{V_\text{cat} \cdot \rho_\text{NOx,storage}}{\dot{m}_\text{exh} \cdot C_\text{NOx}}$
- **Comparison vs SCR:** LNT is compact, cheaper, no AdBlue needed, but sulfur-sensitive and lower efficiency. SCR is for heavy-duty, more efficient, needs urea.
- **Typical Application:** Light-duty GDI gasoline cars and small diesel passenger vehicles.
- **Key Equation for LNT Regeneration:** $\text{NO} + \text{CO} \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\text{N}_2 + \text{CO}_2$ (on Rh catalyst).
- **KTU Board Focus:** Always write **both phases** of operation with **balanced chemical equations**, mention **sulfur poisoning**, and clearly distinguish LNT from SCR/TWC.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stratified Charge Combustion in Direct Injection SI Engines - GDI Engines Combustion Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stratified Charge Combustion in Direct Injection SI Engines - GDI Engines Combustion Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stratified Charge Combustion in GDI Engines** refers to a mode of operation in which fuel is injected **directly into the combustion chamber** of a Spark-Ignition (SI) engine, producing a **spatially non-uniform air-fuel mixture** where the region surrounding the spark plug is enriched (stoichiometric or slightly rich) while the rest of the chamber is progressively leaner or pure air.

**Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)** is an engine fuel delivery methodology in which a high-pressure injector sprays atomized gasoline **directly into the cylinder** during the compression stroke (or intake stroke for homogeneous mode), bypassing the conventional port-fuel-injection (PFI) path through the intake manifold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stratification Parameter (λ):** In GDI stratified mode, the *overall* (bulk) air-fuel ratio is **extremely lean (λ > 1.4 up to λ ≈ 3.0)**, while the *local* λ at the spark plug kernel is **stoichiometric (λ ≈ 1.0)**. This dual-λ condition is the defining feature of stratified GDI combustion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are hosting a large dinner party in a massive hall, but you only need to cook a small fire for ambiance near the dance floor. Instead of lighting the entire hall uniformly (which wastes fuel), you:
- **Concentrate** finely chopped firewood and a match flame at the dance floor (the spark plug region), and
- **Leave the rest of the hall** filled with just fresh air.

When the match is struck, the small pile of wood catches fire locally, and the surrounding air sustains combustion without you having to burn fuel across the entire hall.

The GDI engine does exactly this:
- A **directed, high-pressure fuel spray** creates a rich cloud precisely where the spark plug sits.
- The rest of the cylinder contains **excess air**, enabling ultra-lean overall operation, superior thermal efficiency, and reduced pumping losses.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric A/F ratio (gasoline) | $(F/A)_{st}$ | **14.7** | - |
| Overall λ (stratified mode) | $\lambda_{ov}$ | **1.5 – 3.0** | - |
| Local λ at spark plug | $\lambda_{local}$ | **0.8 – 1.2** | - |
| Injection pressure | $P_{inj}$ | **5 – 35** | MPa |
| Spray penetration length | $L_{s}$ | **20 – 60** | mm |
| Combustion duration | $\theta_{b}$ | **30 – 60** | °CA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiner's love to test the difference between *overall* and *local* equivalence ratios. Always state **both** values when describing stratified mode.

## 1.4 Why GDI Stratified Combustion Exists — The Engineering Driver

1. **Part-load efficiency improvement:** Conventional SI throttles intake air at part load → pumping loss. GDI stratified operation effectively *de-throttles* the engine by leaning out the mixture, recovering lost efficiency.
2. **Specific fuel consumption reduction:** Up to **15–20 %** improvement in BSFC at part load.
3. **Cold-start hydrocarbon reduction:** Direct injection enables better fuel vaporization control.
4. **Knock margin extension:** Stratified charge permits higher compression ratios (up to **12:1** or more) because end-gas regions are lean and cool.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of GDI Stratified Combustion Process

The GDI stratified combustion process can be decomposed into **five sequential sub-phases**, each occurring over a defined crank-angle window:

### Phase 1 — Spray Injection and Wall/Plume Interaction
- Fuel is injected at high pressure (**5 – 35 MPa**) during the **compression stroke**, typically between **300° – 120°** BTDC.
- Spray atomization is governed by the **Weber number** and **Reynolds number** of the droplet field.
- A spray plume is formed; it may impinge on the piston crown (in *wall-guided* strategies) or remain suspended in air (in *air-guided* strategies).

### Phase 2 — Mixture Preparation and Stratification Build-up
- Droplets evaporate, mix with surrounding air, and form a **non-uniform concentration field**.
- The equivalence ratio field is described by $\phi(x, y, z, t)$, which is highest near the spark plug and falls off radially.
- Turbulence enhances mixing but stratification is preserved if injection is late in compression.

### Phase 3 — Spark Ignition and Kernel Formation
- Spark occurs at the **end of compression stroke** (typically **20° – 5°** BTDC).
- A successful kernel requires **local λ ≈ 0.8 – 1.2** and adequate turbulence intensity.
- Ignition delay in GDI: $\tau_{ign} \approx 1 - 3$ ms (shorter than HCCI but longer than PFI due to local mixture non-uniformity).

### Phase 4 — Premixed/Flame Propagation
- The kernel grows into a **deflagrative flame** consuming the stratified charge.
- Combustion duration: $\theta_b \approx 30° - 60°$ CA.
- Local flame speed is enhanced by **turbulence** and decelerated by **excess dilution** in lean regions.

### Phase 5 — Late-Phase Oxidation and Heat Release Tail
- Slow oxidation of fuel trapped in crevices and oil films.
- This phase dominates **unburned hydrocarbon (UHC)** and **soot formation** in GDI engines.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** All formulas below are directly exam-relevant. Pipe symbols `|` are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Relative air-fuel ratio (lambda) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio (phi) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\lambda \cdot (A/F)_{st}}$ | Fuel mass flow rate from air mass flow | kg/s |
| 4 | $\eta_{c,strat} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \left[ \frac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{in}} \right]$ | Stratified-mode efficiency correction | dimensionless |
| 5 | $L_{s} = 0.39 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P \cdot d_{0}}{\rho_{a}}}$ | Spray penetration (Heywood correlation) | m |
| 6 | $We = \frac{\rho_{a} \cdot v_{rel}^{2} \cdot d}{\sigma}$ | Weber number for droplet breakup | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Re_{d} = \frac{\rho_{a} \cdot v_{rel} \cdot d}{\mu_{a}}$ | Droplet Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 8 | $S_{L}^{0} \propto \left( \frac{T_{u}}{T_{u}^{0}} \right)^{\alpha} \left( \frac{P}{P^{0}} \right)^{\beta}$ | Laminar flame speed (Metghalchi–Keck) | m/s |
| 9 | $\theta_{b} = \theta_{0} + \theta_{d} \cdot \phi^{-n}$ | Burn duration correlation (Rassweiler–Withrow type) | °CA |
| 10 | $S_{T} = S_{L} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \beta \cdot \frac{u' \cdot \lambda_{T}}{S_{L}}}$ | Turbulent flame speed (Damköhler) | m/s |
| 11 | $BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_{f}}{P_{brake}} \times 3600$ | Brake specific fuel consumption | g/kWh |
| 12 | $\eta_{th,BSFC} = \frac{3600}{BSFC \cdot CV}$ | Thermal efficiency from BSFC | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\Delta P_{inj} = P_{rail} - P_{cyl}$ | Injection pressure differential | Pa |
| 14 | $\eta_{v,strat} = \frac{m_{a,actual}}{m_{a,ideal}} \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Stratified volumetric efficiency | dimensionless |
| 15 | $NO_{x} \propto \exp\left(-\frac{E_{a}}{R \cdot T_{flame}}\right)$ | Zeldovich NO formation trend | ppm |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Volkswagen TSI/TFSI, Mitsubishi GDI, Toyota D-4ST, Honda i-VTEC — all production GDI engines employing stratified mode in light-load conditions.
- **Formula-1 Racing:** Direct injection stratified combustion (introduced in **2013 regulations**) enabled 30–35 % thermal efficiency, the highest in mass-production IC engines.
- **Hybrid Powertrains:** GDI stratified operation is ideal for Atkinson-cycle hybrids where the engine operates predominantly at part load.
- **Emissions Compliance:** Stratified mode reduces CO₂, but PM and UHC require GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter) — a known KTU discussion topic.

## 2.4 Stratification Strategies (KTU Hot Topic)

| Strategy | Description | Spark Plug Location | Mixing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wall-guided** | Spray aimed at piston crown wall; rich layer deflects to plug | Central | Wall reflection |
| **Air-guided** | Spray uses in-cylinder air motion to deliver fuel | Central | Tumble / swirl |
| **Spray-guided** | Spray aimed *directly* at spark plug | Offset toward spray axis | Minimal mixing |
| **Jet-guided** (research) | Multiple small spark jets; ultra-lean operation | Multiple | Highly stratified |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall vs. Local Equivalence Ratio in a Stratified Charge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU University Exams. Master the integration form.

Let a cylindrical combustion chamber of radius $R$ and height $H$ contain a charge whose local equivalence ratio varies **radially** as:

$$
\phi(r) = \phi_{0} \cdot e^{-\alpha r^{2}}
$$

where $\phi_{0}$ is the local equivalence ratio at the spark plug axis ($r = 0$) and $\alpha$ is the **stratification decay coefficient** ($m^{-2}$).

**Step 1: Define the local fuel mass in an annular ring of width $dr$**

$$
d\dot{m}_{f} = \rho \cdot Y_{f}(r) \cdot u \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \, H
$$

where $Y_{f}(r)$ is the local fuel mass fraction related to $\phi(r)$ through the stoichiometric fuel mass fraction $Y_{f,st}$:

$$
Y_{f}(r) = \frac{\phi(r)}{1 + \phi(r) \cdot s} \cdot Y_{f,st}
$$

with $s = (A/F)_{st} = 14.7$ for gasoline.

**Step 2: For dilute conditions, $\phi \ll 1$ in far-field, approximate:**

$$
Y_{f}(r) \approx \phi(r) \cdot \frac{Y_{f,st}}{s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute total fuel mass by integrating from $r = 0$ to $r = R$**

$$
\dot{m}_{f,total} = \int_{0}^{R} \rho \cdot \frac{Y_{f,st}}{s} \cdot \phi_{0} \cdot e^{-\alpha r^{2}} \cdot u \cdot 2\pi r H \, dr
$$

**Step 4: Pull out constants and recognize the Gaussian integral form**

$$
\dot{m}_{f,total} = \frac{\rho \cdot Y_{f,st} \cdot \phi_{0} \cdot u \cdot 2\pi H}{s} \int_{0}^{R} r \, e^{-\alpha r^{2}} dr
$$

**Step 5: Apply the standard integral**

$$
\int_{0}^{R} r \, e^{-\alpha r^{2}} dr = \frac{1}{2\alpha} \left[ 1 - e^{-\alpha R^{2}} \right]
$$

**Step 6: Substitute back**

$$
\dot{m}_{f,total} = \frac{\rho \cdot Y_{f,st} \cdot \phi_{0} \cdot u \cdot \pi H}{s \cdot \alpha} \left[ 1 - e^{-\alpha R^{2}} \right]
$$

**Step 7: Total air mass**

$$
\dot{m}_{a,total} = \rho \cdot u \cdot \pi R^{2} H
$$

**Step 8: Overall equivalence ratio**

$$
\phi_{ov} = \frac{\dot{m}_{f,total} \cdot s}{\dot{m}_{a,total} \cdot Y_{f,st}} = \frac{\phi_{0}}{\alpha R^{2}} \left[ 1 - e^{-\alpha R^{2}} \right]
$$

**Step 9: Define Stratification Index (SI)**

$$
\boxed{\, SI = \frac{\phi_{0}}{\phi_{ov}} = \frac{\alpha R^{2}}{1 - e^{-\alpha R^{2}}} \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** As $\alpha \to 0$ (uniform mixing), $SI \to 1$ (homogeneous). As $\alpha \to \infty$ (perfect stratification), $SI \to \infty$. In real GDI engines, $SI$ ranges between **2 and 6**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Indicated Thermal Efficiency of Stratified GDI Engine

**Step 1: Start from Otto cycle baseline**

$$
\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

**Step 2: Apply correction for stratified operation**

Stratified operation allows effective de-throttling, reducing pumping work $W_{p}$. The net indicated work becomes:

$$
W_{i,strat} = W_{Otto} + W_{pump,recov} - W_{heat,loss}
$$

**Step 3: Express $W_{pump,recov}$**

Pumping loss recovered by eliminating throttle:

$$
W_{pump,recov} = \dot{m}_{a} \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{a,throttled} - T_{a,unthrottled})
$$

For typical part-load conditions this equals **15–25 %** of $W_{Otto}$.

**Step 4: Apply the stratified-mode efficiency (Heywood-modified)**

$$
\eta_{i,strat} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{\eta_{mix}}{1 + \frac{1 - \lambda_{local}}{\lambda_{local}} \cdot k} \right]
$$

where $\eta_{mix}$ is the mixing uniformity factor (0 ≤ η_mix ≤ 1) and $k$ is a heat-loss weighting coefficient.

**Step 5: Simplify and tabulate**

| Operating Mode | $\lambda_{ov}$ | Pumping Loss | $\eta_{i}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homogeneous Stoichiometric | 1.0 | High (throttled) | **~ 33 %** |
| Homogeneous Lean | 1.4 | Low | **~ 38 %** |
| Stratified Lean | 2.0 | Very Low | **~ 40 – 42 %** |

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spray Penetration Length in GDI

The **Heywood correlation** for gasoline spray penetration in a quiescent chamber:

**Step 1: Force balance on the spray tip**

$$
\frac{d}{dt}(m_{s} \cdot v_{s}) = \frac{\pi d_{0}^{2}}{4} \cdot \Delta P
$$

**Step 2: For a constant injection pressure differential, integrate to yield:**

$$
L_{s}(t) = 0.39 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot \Delta P \cdot d_{0}^{2} \cdot t^{2}}{\rho_{a}}}
$$

**Step 3: For typical GDI: $\Delta P = 10$ MPa, $d_{0} = 150$ μm, $\rho_{a} = 18$ kg/m³ (at 1.5 MPa cylinder pressure), $t = 1$ ms**

$$
L_{s} = 0.39 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10 \times 10^{6} \cdot (150 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \cdot (10^{-3})^{2}}{18}}
$$

$$
L_{s} = 0.39 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10^{7} \cdot 2.25 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 10^{-6}}{18}}
$$

$$
L_{s} = 0.39 \cdot \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.39 \cdot 1.58 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, L_{s} \approx 62 \text{ μm per ms} \,}
$$

**Step 4: For the full ~2 ms injection event, total penetration:**

$$
L_{s,total} \approx 0.39 \cdot d_{0} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{a}}} \cdot t_{inj} \approx 30 - 60 \text{ mm}
$$

This matches the typical piston-bowl diameter — confirming the **wall-guided** and **spray-guided** strategies are geometrically feasible.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Spark-Ignition Kernel Survival Criterion in Stratified Charge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A 14-mark KTU classic.** The minimum kernel energy $E_{k,min}$ must exceed the energy lost by conduction to surrounding gas.

**Step 1: Energy balance at the kernel**

$$
E_{k,min} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_{k}^{3}}{6} \cdot \rho_{mix} \cdot c_{v,eff} \cdot (T_{ad} - T_{u})
$$

where $T_{ad}$ is the adiabatic flame temperature at local $\phi$.

**Step 2: Conduction loss rate (spherical kernel)**

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = 4\pi k_{mix} \cdot d_{k} \cdot (T_{ad} - T_{u})
$$

**Step 3: For sustained kernel growth, the heat release rate must exceed loss**

$$
\dot{Q}_{release} \geq \dot{Q}_{cond}
$$

$$
\dot{\omega}_{fuel} \cdot V_{k} \cdot \Delta H_{c} \geq 4\pi k_{mix} \cdot d_{k} \cdot (T_{ad} - T_{u})
$$

**Step 4: Solve for minimum kernel diameter (critical radius)**

$$
\boxed{\, d_{k,crit} = \sqrt{\frac{24 k_{mix} (T_{ad} - T_{u})}{\rho_{mix} \cdot \dot{r}_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_{c}}} \,}
$$

**Step 5: For typical GDI conditions:** $d_{k,crit} \approx 0.5 - 1.2$ mm. Hence, spark plug energy of **30 – 80 mJ** is required for reliable kernel formation in stratified mode.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
