# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **"Define stratified charge combustion in a GDI engine. Why is it used at part load only?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Stratified charge combustion in a GDI engine is a combustion mode in which fuel is injected directly into the cylinder to produce a *spatially non-uniform* air-fuel mixture. The region near the spark plug is at or near stoichiometric ($\lambda_{local} \approx 1.0$), while the bulk of the chamber is lean ($\lambda_{ov} \approx 1.5 - 3.0$).
- **Part-load-only rationale (1.5 marks):** At high loads, the bulk mixture must be richer to provide the energy required, defeating the lean-burn advantage and increasing NOₓ and soot. At part load, the throttling loss dominates; stratified mode de-throttles the engine, recovering 15–20 % BSFC, justifying its restricted operating window.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **"Compare homogeneous and stratified modes of GDI engine in terms of λ, BSFC, and emissions."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — Tabular):**

| Parameter | Homogeneous Mode | Stratified Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Overall λ | 1.0 | 1.5 – 3.0 |
| Local λ at plug | Same as overall | 0.8 – 1.2 |
| BSFC (g/kWh) | 260 – 280 | 220 – 240 |
| CO₂ | Higher | Lower |
| NOₓ | High | Low |
| UHC / PM | Low | High |
| Pumping loss | High | Low |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **Module 3 — Internal Choice (A or B). Attempt any ONE full question.**

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the different **mixture-formation strategies** used in GDI stratified engines. Compare wall-guided, air-guided, and spray-guided strategies on the basis of mixture preparation, stratification quality, and emissions tendency. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive an expression for the **overall equivalence ratio** when the local equivalence ratio follows a Gaussian distribution $\phi(r) = \phi_{0} e^{-\alpha r^{2}}$ inside a cylindrical chamber. Define the **stratification index** and comment on its value for homogeneous vs. stratified operation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Wall-guided strategy (2 marks):**
- Injector aimed at piston-crown wall.
- Spray impinges wall, deflects, vaporizes, and rises toward spark plug.
- Creates a rich, stratified cloud near the plug.
- **Drawback:** Wall wetting → high UHC and oil dilution.

**Air-guided strategy (2 marks):**
- Injector uses in-cylinder *tumble* or *swirl* motion to transport fuel.
- Strong organized air motion carries the spray to the plug region.
- **Advantage:** Reduced wall wetting; better fuel economy.
- **Drawback:** Requires high tumble ratio (≥ 2.0).

**Spray-guided strategy (2 marks):**
- Spark plug is **offset** and positioned directly in the spray path.
- Spray aimed *at* the plug, not at the wall.
- **Advantage:** Excellent stratification; lowest wall wetting.
- **Drawback:** Sensitive to spray symmetry; needs multi-hole injectors.

**Comparison summary (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Wall | Air | Spray |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stratification quality | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| UHC emissions | High | Low | Very low |
| Injector complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Spark plug location | Central | Central | Offset |

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Refer to Section 3.1 derivation above.** Step-by-step:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the local equivalence ratio distribution:

$$
\phi(r) = \phi_{0} e^{-\alpha r^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Relate local fuel mass fraction to $\phi(r)$:

$$
Y_{f}(r) \approx \frac{\phi(r)}{s} \cdot Y_{f,st}, \quad s = 14.7
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Set up the integral for total fuel mass:

$$
\dot{m}_{f,total} = \frac{\rho \cdot Y_{f,st} \cdot \phi_{0} \cdot u \cdot 2\pi H}{s} \int_{0}^{R} r \, e^{-\alpha r^{2}} dr
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Evaluate the integral:

$$
\int_{0}^{R} r e^{-\alpha r^{2}} dr = \frac{1}{2\alpha} \left[ 1 - e^{-\alpha R^{2}} \right]
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Obtain overall equivalence ratio and stratification index:

$$
\phi_{ov} = \frac{\phi_{0}}{\alpha R^{2}} \left[ 1 - e^{-\alpha R^{2}} \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\, SI = \frac{\phi_{0}}{\phi_{ov}} = \frac{\alpha R^{2}}{1 - e^{-\alpha R^{2}}} \,}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Comment:
- Homogeneous mode: $\alpha \to 0 \Rightarrow SI \to 1$.
- Stratified mode: $\alpha R^{2} \gg 1 \Rightarrow SI \to \alpha R^{2} \gg 1$ (typically 2 – 6 in real engines).

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **five phases of the stratified combustion process** in a GDI engine, with a sketch of in-cylinder pressure versus crank angle showing the characteristic features of each phase. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A GDI engine operates at 2000 rpm with a displacement of 2.0 L, λ = 2.0, volumetric efficiency = 0.85, and ambient conditions of 1 bar, 30 °C. Calculate (i) air mass flow rate, (ii) fuel mass flow rate, (iii) BSFC, and (iv) indicated thermal efficiency assuming brake specific energy output of 1.8 kWh/kg of fuel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Five Phases (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Spray Injection and Plume Formation** — Fuel is injected at 5–35 MPa between 300°–120° BTDC; spray develops with penetration length 30–60 mm.
2. **Mixture Preparation** — Droplets evaporate; stratification field $\phi(r,z,t)$ builds; bulk charge remains lean.
3. **Spark Ignition** — Spark at 5°–20° BTDC in the local rich pocket ($\lambda_{local} \approx 1.0$); kernel forms.
4. **Flame Propagation** — Premixed burn for 30°–60° CA; heat release rate rises sharply.
5. **Late-Phase Oxidation** — Slow burn of fuel in crevices, wall films; tail in heat release curve.

**P-θ Diagram Description (2 marks):**

- Distinct two-stage heat release: first rapid spike (premixed), then a long tail (diffusion/oxidation).
- Peak pressure slightly retarded compared to homogeneous operation.
- No knock (because end-gas is lean and cool).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $N = 2000$ rpm, $V_d = 2.0 \text{ L} = 0.002 \text{ m}^3$
- $\lambda = 2.0$, $\eta_v = 0.85$, $P_{amb} = 1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $T_{amb} = 303$ K
- $R_{air} = 287$ J/(kg·K), $(A/F)_{st} = 14.7$

**Step 1 — Air mass flow rate (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{V}_{disp} = \frac{V_d \cdot N}{2} = \frac{0.002 \times 2000}{2 \times 60} = 0.0333 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{a,ideal} = \frac{P_{amb} \cdot \dot{V}_{disp}}{R_{air} \cdot T_{amb}} = \frac{10^5 \times 0.0333}{287 \times 303} = 0.0383 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, \dot{m}_{a} = \eta_v \cdot \dot{m}_{a,ideal} = 0.85 \times 0.0383 = 0.0326 \text{ kg/s} \,}
$$

**Step 2 — Fuel mass flow rate (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\lambda \cdot (A/F)_{st}} = \frac{0.0326}{2.0 \times 14.7} = \frac{0.0326}{29.4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, \dot{m}_f = 1.109 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 3.99 \text{ kg/h} \,}
$$

**Step 3 — BSFC (1.5 marks):**

Brake power is given indirectly: 1.8 kWh per kg fuel.

$$
P_{brake} = 1.8 \times 3.99 = 7.18 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, BSFC = \frac{3.99 \text{ kg/h}}{7.18 \text{ kW}} = 0.556 \text{ kg/kWh} = 556 \text{ g/kWh} \,}
$$

**Step 4 — Indicated thermal efficiency (1.5 marks):**

Using gasoline calorific value $CV = 43$ MJ/kg:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{3600 \times P_{brake}}{BSFC \cdot CV} = \frac{3600 \times 7.18}{3.99 \times 43000} = \frac{25848}{171570} = 0.1506
$$

Wait — using the more direct relation: $\eta_{th} = \frac{1.8 \text{ kWh/kg}}{43 \text{ MJ/kg}} = \frac{1.8 \times 3.6 \text{ MJ/kg}}{43 \text{ MJ/kg}} = \frac{6.48}{43}$

$$
\boxed{\, \eta_{th} \approx 0.151 = 15.1 \% \,}
$$

(Indicated efficiency for a small 2 L GDI at 2000 rpm part-load is consistent.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in GDI Combustion Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the difference between *overall* and *local* λ (–2 marks):** Stratified mode is *bulk lean* but *locally stoichiometric at the plug*. Examiners will explicitly check this distinction.
> 2. **Not specifying injection timing range (–1 mark):** Always quote a window (e.g., "300° – 120° BTDC"), not just "during compression."
> 3. **Confusing wall-guided with spray-guided (–1 mark):** Wall-guided = *wall impingement*. Spray-guided = *direct spray-to-plug*. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Skipping the stratification index derivation steps (–3 marks):** Each algebraic step carries partial credit. Never write "and after integration, we get…". Show every substitution.
> 5. **Ignoring pumping-loss recovery in efficiency calculation (–2 marks):** Stratified mode's whole justification is de-throttling. A solution that doesn't mention it scores lower.
> 6. **Using (A/F) instead of (F/A) in formulas (–1 mark):** λ = (A/F)_actual / (A/F)_st — examiners verify direction.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — GDI Stratified Combustion Process**

- **Core Concept:** Fuel injected *directly* into cylinder, creating spatial mixture non-uniformity.
- **Operating Window:** Part-load, low-to-mid speed; *not* used at WOT (Wide Open Throttle).
- **Dual-λ Condition:** Bulk λ = 1.5–3.0, local λ = 0.8–1.2 at plug.
- **Five Phases:** Injection → Stratification → Spark → Propagation → Late-Phase Oxidation.
- **Three Spray Strategies:** Wall-guided, Air-guided, Spray-guided.
- **Spray Pressure Range:** **5 – 35 MPa**; penetration length **30 – 60 mm**.
- **Spark Timing:** **5° – 20° BTDC** in stratified mode.
- **Key Benefit:** BSFC reduction **15 – 20 %** by de-throttling (pumping loss recovery).
- **Key Drawback:** Higher **UHC and particulate matter (PM)** — requires GPF.
- **NOₓ:** *Lower* than homogeneous mode (leaner bulk charge).
- **Spark Energy Required:** **30 – 80 mJ** (higher than PFI's 20–30 mJ).
- **Stratification Index (SI):** $SI = \alpha R^2 / (1 - e^{-\alpha R^2})$; ranges 2–6 in real engines.
- **Heywood Spray Penetration:** $L_s = 0.39 \cdot d_0 \cdot \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho_a} \cdot t_{inj}$.
- **Critical Kernel Radius:** $d_{k,crit} = \sqrt{24 k_{mix}(T_{ad}-T_u) / (\rho_{mix} \dot{r}_{fuel} \Delta H_c)}$.
- **Production Examples:** VW TSI, Mitsubishi GDI, Toyota D-4ST, Honda i-VTEC.
- **F1 Regulation Year:** 2013 — GDI mandated.
- **Compression Ratio:** Can be raised to **12:1** in stratified mode (knock-resistant).
- **Volumetric Efficiency Definition in GDI:** $\eta_v = (\text{actual air mass}) / (\text{ideal air mass at 1 bar, amb T})$.
- **BSFC Formula:** $BSFC = \dot{m}_f / P_{brake} \times 3600$ (g/kWh).
- **Thermal Efficiency from BSFC:** $\eta_{th} = 3600 / (BSFC \cdot CV)$.
- **Key Exam Trick:** Examiner will ask "Why stratified mode is not used at high load?" — answer: energy demand, knock, emissions.
- **Key Diagram Required:** P-θ diagram showing two-stage heat release.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turbocharged Direct Injection SI Engines (TSI) - Problems, Challenges, and Advantages
Direct Injection Gasoline Engines with Auto Ignition Combustion - Principles, Approaches, Operation, Control, and Development of Practical Engines
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direct Injection Gasoline Engines with Auto Ignition Combustion - Principles, Approaches, Operation, Control, and Development of Practical Engines

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Injection Gasoline with Auto-Ignition Combustion (AI-GDI)** refers to a class of advanced internal combustion engine concepts in which a gasoline-like fuel is **directly injected** into the combustion chamber and is **ignited not by an external spark source** but by the **auto-ignition** of a premixed (or partially premixed) fuel–air mixture when the in-cylinder temperature and pressure exceed the fuel's **chemical ignition delay threshold** during the compression stroke.

The term subsumes several related low-temperature combustion (LTC) strategies:

- **HCCI** — Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition
- **CAI / SAE** — Controlled Auto-Ignition / Spark-Assisted Auto-Ignition
- **PCCI** — Premixed Charge Compression Ignition
- **RCCI / PPC** — Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition / Partially Premixed Combustion

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker & the Matchstick"**

Imagine you are cooking **dosa batter** in a pressure cooker. You mix the rice–lentil batter uniformly (homogeneous charge), pour it into the cooker, and lock the lid. As pressure builds inside, the temperature rises. At some critical pressure–temperature combination, the batter begins to cook **on its own** without you lighting a gas flame — purely from the compressed hot environment.

Now imagine the same principle inside a steel cylinder with a piston:
1. **Fuel + air** are mixed and trapped inside.
2. The piston **squeezes** the mixture (compression).
3. When conditions are right, the mixture **self-ignites** everywhere almost simultaneously.

This is **auto-ignition combustion**. A traditional SI engine needs a spark plug (the matchstick) to start the reaction; an AI-GDI engine relies on the **cooking-by-pressure** phenomenon.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics for Auto-Ignition Combustion**
> - **Auto-ignition temperature of iso-octane:** ≈ **255 °C (≈ 528 K)**
> - **Auto-ignition temperature of n-heptane:** ≈ **215 °C (≈ 488 K)**
> - **Typical compression ratio for AI-GDI:** **14:1 to 18:1**
> - **Premixed burn duration in HCCI:** **1 to 5 crank-angle degrees (CAD)**
> - **Combustion efficiency target:** **> 95 %**
> - **BSFC target for HCCI:** **≤ 200 g/kWh** (compared with ~240 g/kWh for port-injection SI)
> - **In-cylinder peak temperature limited to:** **< 1800 K** to suppress NOx

### 1.4 Why This Matters — The Motivation

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises this topic because it sits at the intersection of:
- **Climate targets** (CO₂ reduction)
- **Stringent emission norms** (BS-VI / Euro 7 NOx ≤ 35 mg/km)
- **Renewable fuels** (gasoline + ethanol + bio-fuels blend well in HCCI)

Auto-ignition combustion offers a **theoretical thermal efficiency** approaching that of a **diesel engine** while running on **gasoline**, with **near-zero soot** and drastically reduced **NOx and HC** emissions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Thermodynamic Foundation of Auto-Ignition

Auto-ignition occurs when the integrated thermal history of a charge parcel satisfies the **ignition delay criterion**. The classical interpretation is given by the **Arrhenius-type chemical kinetics model**, integrated over the compression stroke.

### 2.2 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Five-Stage Logic Chain of AI-GDI Combustion**

1. **Mixture Preparation Stage**
   - Fuel is injected directly into the cylinder near top-dead-centre (TDC) or during the compression stroke.
   - A **homogeneous** or **stratified** mixture is formed depending on the injection timing.

2. **Compression & Sensible-Energy Storage**
   - The piston compresses the charge. Temperature rises from ≈ 350 K (at IVC) to ≈ 750–950 K near TDC.

3. **Low-Temperature Heat Release (LTHR) — "Cool Flame"**
   - At ≈ 600–800 K, peroxides and aldehydes form via the **cool-flame chemistry pathway** (2-stage ignition of hydrocarbons).
   - A small LTHR releases 5–15 % of the total fuel energy.

4. **High-Temperature Heat Release (HTHR) — Main Combustion**
   - As temperature crosses ≈ 1000 K, the **second-stage ignition** occurs rapidly throughout the chamber.
   - Combustion duration is **2–5 CAD** — extremely fast compared to spark-ignited flame propagation (≈ 20–40 CAD).

5. **Expansion & Heat Release Completion**
   - Pressure equalises and the working fluid expands doing work on the piston.

### 2.3 Governing Equations

#### 2.3.1 Auto-Ignition Delay — Arrhenius Form

$$
\tau_{ig} = A \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R \, T}\right)
$$

Where:
- $\tau_{ig}$ — **ignition delay** (seconds)
- $A$ — pre-exponential constant (s·Paⁿ)
- $p$ — cylinder pressure (Pa)
- $n$ — pressure exponent (typically 1.0–1.5 for gasoline surrogates)
- $E_a$ — **activation energy** (J/mol)
- $R$ — universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ — unburned mixture temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher compression ratio → higher $T$ at TDC → exponentially smaller $\tau_{ig}$ → earlier auto-ignition. This is why **load control** in HCCI is achieved by **varying $\tau_{ig}$** rather than by throttling.

#### 2.3.2 Knocking vs Auto-Ignition — The Boundary

Knocking in SI engines is **uncontrolled, localised auto-ignition** of the end-gas. In AI-GDI engines, the **entire end-gas is intentionally and uniformly auto-ignited**, which is the **desired mode** rather than a failure mode.

$$
\text{Knock intensity} \;\propto\; \dfrac{\Delta p_{\text{end-gas}}}{p_{\text{bulk}}}
$$

#### 2.3.3 Octane Number vs Auto-Ignition Resistance

A fuel's **Research Octane Number (RON)** correlates inversely with its tendency to auto-ignite:

$$
\text{RON} = 100 - k \ln(\tau_{ig,\text{fuel}})
$$

where $k$ is an empirical constant.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Auto-ignition delay (Arrhenius) | $\tau_{ig}=A p^{-n}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)$ | $A$[s·Paⁿ], $p$[Pa], $E_a$[J/mol], $T$[K] | Time from charge formation to spontaneous ignition |
| Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | $\text{IMEP}=\dfrac{W_{\text{ind}}}{V_d}$ | $W_{\text{ind}}$[J], $V_d$[m³] | Work output per displaced volume |
| Gross Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{it,g}=\dfrac{W_{\text{ind}}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of work to fuel energy input |
| Equivalent Ratio (HCCI) | $\phi_{\text{eq}}=\dfrac{F/A}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | dimensionless | Stoichiometric reference for auto-ignition chemistry |
| Mass Fraction Burned (Wiebe) | $x_b=1-\exp\!\left[-a\!\left(\dfrac{\theta-\theta_0}{\Delta\theta}\right)^{m+1}\right]$ | $a, m$ — Wiebe constants; $\theta$[CAD] | Burn fraction for rapid HCCI combustion |
| Heat Release Rate (apparent) | $\dfrac{dQ}{d\theta}=\dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\,p\,\dfrac{dV}{d\theta}+\dfrac{1}{\gamma-1}\,V\,\dfrac{dp}{d\theta}$ | $\gamma$ — ratio of specific heats | Rate of chemical energy release vs crank angle |
| Combustion Phasing (CA50) | $\theta_{50}$ = crank angle of 50 % mass burned | CAD aTDC | Defines "when" combustion peaks |
| BSFC | $\text{BSFC}=\dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | g/kWh | Brake-specific fuel consumption |
| Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c=\dfrac{\dot m_f - \dot m_{CO} - \dot m_{HC} - \dot m_{\text{soot}}}{\dot m_f}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of fuel fully oxidised |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Note:** All vertical bars in the table above have been replaced with text descriptors or $\vert$ symbols to prevent markdown-table parsing errors. Do **not** use raw $\vert$ in KTU answer sheets — always write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ or `mod(x)`.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Auto-Ignition Delay for a Gasoline Surrogate

**Problem:** Estimate the auto-ignition delay of a stoichiometric iso-octane / air mixture at $p = 20$ bar and $T = 900$ K. Use $A = 1.4 \times 10^{-3}$ s·Paⁿ, $n = 1.2$, $E_a = 1.25 \times 10^5$ J/mol.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing law**

We use the **Arrhenius-type ignition delay** equation:

$$
\tau_{ig} = A \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values**

$$
\tau_{ig} = (1.4 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (20 \times 10^{5})^{-1.2} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{5}}{8.314 \times 900}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the pressure term**

$$
(20 \times 10^{5})^{-1.2} = (2.0 \times 10^{6})^{-1.2}
$$

Take natural log:

$$
\ln\!\left[(2.0 \times 10^{6})^{-1.2}\right] = -1.2 \times \ln(2.0 \times 10^{6})
$$

$$
\ln(2.0 \times 10^{6}) = \ln(2) + 6\ln(10) = 0.693 + 13.816 = 14.509
$$

$$
-1.2 \times 14.509 = -17.411
$$

Therefore:

$$
(2.0 \times 10^{6})^{-1.2} = e^{-17.411} = 2.79 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{Pa}^{-1.2}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the exponential (activation energy term)**

$$
\dfrac{E_a}{R\,T} = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{5}}{8.314 \times 900} = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^{5}}{7482.6} = 16.706
$$

$$
\exp(16.706) = 1.83 \times 10^{7}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply all three factors**

$$
\tau_{ig} = (1.4 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2.79 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.83 \times 10^{7})
$$

$$
\tau_{ig} = 1.4 \times 2.79 \times 1.83 \times 10^{(-3-8+7)}
$$

$$
\tau_{ig} = 7.15 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to crank-angle degrees at 2000 rpm**

$$
\tau_{ig}\text{(CAD)} = \tau_{ig} \times \dfrac{360 \times N}{60} = 7.15 \times 10^{-4} \times \dfrac{360 \times 2000}{60}
$$

$$
\tau_{ig}\text{(CAD)} = 7.15 \times 10^{-4} \times 12000 = 8.58\ \text{CAD}
$$

**Result:** The mixture auto-ignites approximately **8.6 crank-angle degrees** after a reference temperature of 900 K is reached — consistent with observed HCCI burn rates.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Combustion Phasing Control via Negative Valve Overlap (NVO)

**Problem:** Determine the fraction of residual gas trapped in the cylinder when NVO is 30 CAD at 1500 rpm with intake valve closing at –55° aTDC and exhaust valve opening at 135° bTDC.

**Step 1 — Compute total valve-overlap window**

$$
\theta_{\text{NVO}} = \theta_{\text{EVO}} - \theta_{\text{IVC}} = 135 - (-55) = 190\ \text{CAD}
$$

But NVO is the *useful* internal residuals window. The **effective NVO trapping fraction** depends on backflow during overlap.

**Step 2 — Use mass conservation across valve overlap**

$$
x_r = \dfrac{m_{\text{res}}}{m_{\text{total}}} = 1 - \dfrac{\dot m_{\text{backflow}}}{\dot m_{\text{displaced}}}
$$

For a well-tuned NVO strategy with 30 CAD true NVO, the literature gives:

$$
x_r \approx 0.20 - 0.45
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate mass of residuals**

Assuming $m_{\text{total}} = 0.00042$ kg:

$$
m_{\text{res}} = 0.30 \times 0.00042 = 1.26 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Effect on auto-ignition**

The residual gas dilutes the charge, lowering the effective $\phi$ and increasing specific heat. Using a simplified $T_{\text{ad}}$ model:

$$
T_{\text{ad}} \propto \dfrac{\phi \, \text{LHV}}{c_{p,\text{eff}}(1 + x_r/\phi)}
$$

So increasing $x_r$ **delays** auto-ignition (later CA50) — providing load control.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of AI-GDI Approaches

| Approach | Charge Preparation | Combustion Mode | Load Control | Primary Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HCCI** | Early DI, homogeneous premix | Uniform auto-ignition | NVO / EGR / Var. CR | High HC & CO at low load; ringing |
| **CAI / Spark-Assisted** | DI + spark triggers kernel | Spark-induced auto-ignition front | Spark timing | Combustion smoothness |
| **PCCI** | Late DI, partially premixed | Two-stage auto-ignition | EGR + injection timing | Smoke at high load |
| **RCCI / Dual-Fuel** | Port-injected low-reactivity fuel + DI high-reactivity fuel | Reactivity-graded auto-ignition | Fuel ratio | Two-fuel system cost |
| **PPC** | Early CI-like DI of gasoline | Partially premixed compression ignition | Injection pressure / timing | Wall wetting |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AI-GDI Engine Management System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Accelerator Pedal] --> B[ECU Master Controller]
    B --> C1[Air-Management Module]
    B --> C2[Fuel-Injection Module]
    B --> C3[Combustion-Phasing Module]
    B --> C4[Emissions-Aftertreatment Module]
    C1 --> D1[Variable Valve Timing VVT Actuator]
    C1 --> D2[Turbocharger VGT Vanes]
    C1 --> D3[EGR Cooler Valve]
    C2 --> D4[High-Pressure DI Injector 350 bar]
    C2 --> D5[Direct-Injection Rail Pressure Sensor]
    C3 --> D6[In-Cylinder Pressure Transducer]
    C3 --> D7[Crankshaft Position Sensor]
    C4 --> D8[Three-Way Catalyst TWC]
    C4 --> D9[NOx Trap or LNT]
    D1 --> E[HCCI / CAI / PCCI Combustion Chamber]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> F[Combustion-Phasing Feedback CA50 Estimator]
    D7 --> F
    E --> F
    F --> B
```

### 4.2 Crank-Angle Domain Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Intake Stroke 0 to 180 CAD] --> A2[Compression Stroke 180 to 360 CAD]
    A2 --> A3[Auto-Ignition Window 350 to 375 CAD]
    A3 --> A4[Expansion 375 to 540 CAD]
    A4 --> A5[Exhaust 540 to 720 CAD]
    A3 --> B1[LTHR Cool Flame 600 to 800 K]
    A3 --> B2[HTHR Main Burn above 1000 K]
    B1 --> B2
```

### 4.3 Process Block Diagram — Combustion Mode Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1{Engine Load} -->|Low to Mid| S2[HCCI Mode]
    S1 -->|High| S3[PCCI or SI Mode]
    S1 -->|Transient| S4[CAI Spark-Assisted]
    S2 --> S5[Use EGR and NVO for phasing]
    S3 --> S6[Use direct injection timing]
    S4 --> S7[Spark plug ignites kernel that triggers auto-ignition front]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q:** Define Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition (HCCI) and list **two advantages** it offers over conventional SI combustion.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
HCCI is a combustion mode in which a premixed, homogeneous fuel–air mixture is **auto-ignited** by compression-induced high temperature and pressure, without spark initiation.
**Advantages:**
1. **Higher thermal efficiency** — no throttling loss; combustion near TDC; thermodynamic cycle approximates the **Otto ideal cycle with reduced pumping loss**.
2. **Ultra-low NOx and soot** — peak temperatures can be kept < 1800 K via dilution; no flame-front soot formation as there is no rich diffusion flame.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q:** What is **Negative Valve Overlap (NVO)** and how does it enable **load control** in an HCCI engine?

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
NVO is the period (typically **20–60 CAD**) during which **both intake and exhaust valves are closed** at the end of the exhaust stroke, deliberately trapping a fraction (20–45 %) of **hot residual gases**.
**Load control mechanism:** Increasing NVO → more hot residuals → higher $c_p$ of mixture → **lower peak temperature** → **longer ignition delay** → later CA50 → reduces effective pressure work, lowering load. Conversely, reducing NVO brings CA50 earlier and increases load.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle** of direct-injection gasoline auto-ignition combustion. Compare **HCCI, PCCI and RCCI** in terms of mixture preparation, control parameters and emission characteristics.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (3 marks):** In a DI gasoline AI engine, fuel is injected directly into the cylinder. The mixture (homogeneous or partially premixed) is compressed until the bulk gas temperature exceeds the **two-stage auto-ignition threshold** of the fuel (~1000 K). At this instant, **simultaneous multi-point ignition** occurs throughout the chamber, releasing heat in 2–5 CAD.

**HCCI** — fully homogeneous, single fuel, control via EGR/NVO/CR; **NOx low, HC+CO moderate, ringing at high load.**

**PCCI** — partially premixed; injection near TDC; two-stage combustion visible; **moderate NOx, smoke risk at high load.**

**RCCI** — dual fuel (e.g., gasoline DI + diesel or ethanol port); reactivity gradient; **widest load range, best efficiency, complex fuel system.**

| Feature | HCCI | PCCI | RCCI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mixture homogeneity | High | Medium | Stratified reactivity |
| Load control | NVO/EGR/CR | Inj. timing | Reactivity ratio |
| NOx | Very low | Low | Very low |
| HC + CO | High at low load | Moderate | Low |
| Smoke | Negligible | Possible at high load | Low |
| Efficiency | High | High | Highest |

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled **crank-angle diagram**, describe the **combustion-phasing control** using **cooled EGR** in an HCCI engine.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (3 marks):** Cooled EGR introduces inert exhaust gas (mostly N₂ + CO₂) into the intake. This dilutes the charge, increases its **specific heat capacity**, and **lowers the peak temperature** for the same $\phi$. The Arrhenius term $\exp(E_a/RT)$ is reduced, lengthening $\tau_{ig}$, and pushing CA50 later — which **reduces load** in HCCI.

**Crank-Angle Diagram (4 marks):**

```
  P (bar)
   |            HTHR Peak
   |             /\
   |            /  \  ← With EGR (later, lower peak)
   |     LTHR  /    \
   |      /\  /      \
   |     /  \/        \
   |    /   /\         \
   |   /   /  \         \
   |  /   /    \         \
   |_/___/______\_________\____ θ (CAD)
       300   360  CA50
              ↑
         Without EGR (earlier CA50)
```

With increasing EGR rate (0 % → 30 %), CA50 shifts from ≈ 5° aTDC to ≈ 15° aTDC, and peak pressure drops from 80 bar to 60 bar. This is the **principal load-control knob** in modern AI-GDI systems.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **development challenges** that have prevented HCCI from being widely commercialised in passenger-car gasoline engines.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Limited Load Range (2 marks):** At low load, wall-quenching and crevice effects cause high **HC and CO emissions**; at high load, the **pressure-rise rate (PRR)** becomes excessive, causing engine noise and structural stress.

2. **Combustion-Phasing Control (2 marks):** CA50 is extremely sensitive to small changes in $T$, EGR, or fuel composition. Robust closed-loop control requires **fast in-cylinder pressure transducers** (cost issue).

3. **Cold-Start and Transient Operation (1 mark):** Below ≈ 100 °C coolant temperature, the in-cylinder temperature is insufficient for auto-ignition; auxiliary spark SI mode is required during warm-up.

4. **Fuel Sensitivity (1 mark):** Auto-ignition chemistry of gasoline varies dramatically with octane rating, ethanol content, and aging. A fuel-flex system must adapt over a wide RON range (≈ 80–100).

5. **NOx–HC Trade-off at High Loads (1 mark):** Although low-load NOx is near zero, high-load combustion can exceed **BS-VI limits** unless EGR is precisely modulated.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Spark-Assisted Auto-Ignition (SAI / CAI)** with a neat schematic of the combustion sequence.

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (3 marks):** SAI bridges SI and HCCI. A spark plug deposits a **small ignition kernel** which creates a localised high-temperature zone. The auto-ignition chemistry of the surrounding end-gas is sufficiently advanced that this kernel **triggers a propagating auto-ignition front** through the chamber. The result is faster, more controllable combustion than pure HCCI.

**Operating Sequence (4 marks):**
1. Intake — fresh charge + retained residuals (NVO).
2. Compression — bulk gas reaches ~900 K.
3. **Spark event** ≈ 25° bTDC (timed to control CA50).
4. Spark kernel grows into a cool-flame front.
5. Front propagates at 15–25 m/s (much faster than SI flame ≈ 5 m/s).
6. HTHR completes by ≈ 5° aTDC.

**Schematic (mermaid block representation):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Spark Kernel at -25 CAD] --> S2[Cool-Flame Front]
    S2 --> S3[Auto-Ignition Front Propagates]
    S3 --> S4[HTHR by 5 CAD aTDC]
    S4 --> S5[Fast Controlled Burn]
```

**Engineering Utility:** SAI enables **extension of the HCCI operating window** to higher loads without exceeding ringing limits, and provides a **graceful transition** between SI and HCCI modes during transients.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not confuse HCCI with GDI** — HCCI is the *combustion mode*; GDI is the *fuel-delivery mode*. Many students lose marks by treating them as identical.
> 2. **Octane vs Cetane confusion** — Gasoline has HIGH octane (resists auto-ignition in SI) but HCCI **deliberately** uses it as a low-cetane auto-ignition fuel under high compression.
> 3. **Forgetting LTHR/HTHR** — Always mention **two-stage heat release** when describing HCCI; examiners specifically award marks for this.
> 4. **Missing units in the Arrhenius equation** — $A$ has units of s·Paⁿ; students frequently omit pressure units and lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Crank-angle diagram must be labelled** with both axes (P in bar, θ in CAD aTDC) and the CA50 point — a drawing without labels scores ≤ 2/7 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Takeaways — Module 3 AI-GDI**

- **AI-GDI** combines **direct fuel injection** with **auto-ignition combustion**, eliminating the spark plug's role as the dominant ignition source.
- The **Arrhenius ignition-delay equation** $\tau_{ig}=A p^{-n}\exp(E_a/RT)$ is the **central mathematical tool** for this topic — be ready to derive and apply it.
- **Two-stage combustion** is a hallmark of HCCI: **LTHR** at 600–800 K, **HTHR** at > 1000 K.
- **Load control** is achieved by **modulating $\tau_{ig}$** via **EGR, NVO, variable CR, or fuel-reactivity** — *not* by throttling.
- **HCCI, CAI/SAI, PCCI, RCCI** form a family of strategies differentiated by **mixture preparation and reactivity grading**.
- Key challenges are: **limited load range, ringing, cold-start, fuel sensitivity, and closed-loop phasing control**.
- Practical engines require **dual-mode operation** — SI for cold-start and transients, AI-GDI for steady-state mid-load.
- **Crank-angle diagrams** showing CA50 shift with EGR/NVO are **high-mark questions** in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Temperature Combustion Strategies - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Low Temperature Combustion Strategies - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Low Temperature Combustion (LTC)** is an advanced combustion regime in internal combustion engines where the peak in-cylinder gas temperature is deliberately maintained **below approximately 1800 K** (typically **1500–1700 K**), in contrast to the conventional diesel (>2200 K) and spark-ignition (>2000 K) regimes. The principal objective of LTC is the simultaneous, near-zero emission of **NOx** (oxides of nitrogen) and **soot (PM)** while retaining high indicated thermal efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exact Wording):**
> *"Low Temperature Combustion (LTC) strategies refer to a class of advanced, auto-ignition-based combustion modes — including HCCI, PCCI, RCCI, PPC and dual-fuel LTC — in which the charge is significantly diluted (by exhaust gas recirculation, air, or a low-reactivity fuel) and the combustion process is governed primarily by chemical kinetics rather than by flame propagation or diffusion-controlled mixing, leading to near-elimination of NOx and soot."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Slow Cooker vs. The Pressure Cooker"

Imagine two kitchens preparing the same dish:
- A **conventional diesel engine** is like a *high-pressure cooker* — extreme temperatures (>2200 K) cook the fuel rapidly, but produce harmful "scorch" residues (NOx and soot).
- An **LTC engine** is like a *slow cooker* — the temperature is held gently low (≈1600 K), the ingredients (fuel + air + EGR) are pre-mixed in a homogeneous broth, and the "cooking" (auto-ignition) happens gradually and uniformly across the entire cylinder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy works:** LTC doesn't try to make combustion *faster* — it tries to make it **cooler, more distributed, and chemically controlled**. The trade-off is harder combustion phasing control and limited operating range.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of LTC

Every LTC strategy is built on three pillars:

1. **Charge Dilution** — Heavy **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)** rates of **20 %–60 %** or excess air to suppress peak temperature.
2. **Charge Preparation** — Fuel and oxidizer are *partially or fully* premixed before combustion starts.
3. **Kinetics-Controlled Ignition** — Combustion is initiated by auto-ignition at multiple sites simultaneously, NOT by a spark or by a propagating diffusion flame.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Highlighted for Board Exam Recall)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|--------|----------|----------------|
| $T_{\text{peak}}$ | Peak in-cylinder temperature for low-NOx operation | **< 1800 K** |
| $\Phi$ | Equivalence ratio for LTC | **0.3 – 0.7** (lean) |
| $EGR_{\%}$ | EGR fraction by mass | **20 % – 60 %** |
| $\lambda$ | Relative air-fuel ratio | **1.5 – 2.5** |
| $\text{NO}_x$ emission target | Soot-free, near-zero NOx | **< 50 ppm** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of LTC Strategies (Taxonomy)

LTC strategies are classified primarily on **how the charge is prepared** before auto-ignition. The figure below shows the hierarchy used in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 2.1.1 HCCI — Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition
- **Charge State:** Fuel + air + EGR are *fully premixed* before compression.
- **Ignition:** Auto-ignition occurs simultaneously at multiple points when the homogeneous mixture reaches its auto-ignition temperature.
- **Fuel:** Gasoline, ethanol, methanol, DME, natural gas.
- **Advantages:** Very low NOx & soot, high thermal efficiency (compression-ignition-like).
- **Disadvantages:** No direct control of ignition timing; narrow operating range; high HC and CO at light loads.

### 2.1.2 PCCI — Premixed Charge Compression Ignition
- **Charge State:** *Mostly* premixed, but with a small stratified pocket to control ignition timing.
- **Ignition:** Premixed auto-ignition of the bulk charge, triggered by the stratification.
- **Fuel:** Diesel (low reactivity), gasoline-like fuels.
- **Advantages:** Better control than HCCI; lower pressure rise rates (PRR).
- **Disadvantages:** Combustion phasing still difficult; some soot from stratified region.

### 2.1.3 RCCI — Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition
- **Charge State:** Two fuels of **different reactivity** are introduced — a low-reactivity fuel (gasoline, ethanol) premixed early, and a high-reactivity fuel (diesel) injected late.
- **Ignition:** Reactivity gradient inside the cylinder controls combustion phasing *spatially and temporally*.
- **Advantages:** Excellent control of ignition timing; can be implemented on production diesel engines.
- **Disadvantages:** Dual fuel system complexity; fuel reactivity matching critical.

### 2.1.4 PPC — Partially Premixed Combustion
- **Charge State:** Diesel fuel is injected **very early** (50°–90° BTDC) so that most of it has time to premix with the air+EGR charge.
- **Ignition:** Auto-ignition of the partially premixed charge.
- **Advantages:** Works with conventional diesel fuel; moderate modifications to existing engines.
- **Disadvantages:** Highly sensitive to injection timing; HC/CO trade-offs at low loads.

### 2.1.5 Dual-Fuel LTC (DF-LTC)
- **Charge State:** A gaseous low-reactivity fuel (CNG, LPG, H₂) is premixed with air+EGR; a small pilot dose of diesel ignites the mixture.
- **Ignition:** Diesel pilot acts as the ignition source for the homogeneous gaseous charge.
- **Advantages:** Lower NOx, flexibility in fuel use.
- **Disadvantages:** Lower efficiency at high loads; gaseous fuel storage issues.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step "How" an LTC Event Works

1. **Charge Preparation (Intake / Early Compression):** A lean, dilute mixture is formed in the cylinder. Diluents (EGR, residual gases, or excess air) lower the *effective* heat capacity and chemical reactivity.
2. **Compression Stroke:** The dilute mixture is compressed to a temperature just *below* the auto-ignition threshold of the least reactive fuel component.
3. **Low-Temperature Heat Release (LTHR / Cool Flame):** Two-stage auto-ignition begins — first a *cool flame* (T ≈ 800–900 K) producing partial oxidation intermediates.
4. **High-Temperature Heat Release (HTHR):** After ignition delay, the bulk of the mixture auto-ignites simultaneously at multiple sites. Peak temperature is held below **1800 K**.
5. **Heat Rejection & Expansion:** Because temperatures are lower, less heat is lost to walls (relatively) and efficiency can approach **Otto-like values** at part loads.

## 2.3 The "Why" — Why LTC Eliminates NOx and Soot

> [!NOTE]
> **NOx Formation (Extended Zeldovich Mechanism):**
> $$\text{NO}_x \;\propto\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u\, T_{\text{peak}}}\right)$$
> Where $E_a \approx$ **317 kJ/mol** (activation energy for the rate-limiting O + N₂ → NO + N step). Because this is a strong exponential, **dropping peak T from 2200 K to 1700 K reduces NOx by ~95 %**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Soot Formation (Phenomenological):**
> Soot requires *fuel-rich* regions where $\Phi > 2$ locally and temperatures > 1700 K. In LTC, the premixed lean charge never forms these rich pockets, and low peak temperature prevents soot nucleation.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description / Condition |
|---|----------|-------------------------|
| 1 | $\text{NO}_x \;=\; A \,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R_u T}\right)$ | Extended Zeldovich — Arrhenius form |
| 2 | $\tau_i \;\propto\; \Phi^{-a}\, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R_u T}\right)$ | Auto-ignition delay (Arrhenius-type) |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{LTC}} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Ideal Otto efficiency limit for HCCI |
| 4 | $T_{\text{peak, derated}} = T_{\text{peak,diesel}} - \Delta T_{\text{dilution}}$ | $\Delta T_{\text{dilution}} \approx 200\text{–}500$ K with high EGR |
| 5 | $\Phi_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \dfrac{F/A}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Effective equivalence ratio (target 0.3–0.7) |
| 6 | $\text{PRR} \;=\; \dfrac{\gamma \, P_{\max}}{V} \dfrac{dV}{d\theta}$ | Pressure rise rate (must be < 5 bar/deg for smoothness) |
| 7 | $\text{HRR} \;=\; \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, P\, \dfrac{dV}{d\theta} + \dfrac{1}{\gamma - 1}\, V\, \dfrac{dP}{d\theta}$ | First-law heat release rate |
| 8 | $EGR_{\%} = \dfrac{m_{EGR}}{m_{\text{total, intake}}} \times 100$ | EGR fraction (typical 20 %–60 %) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Constants to Memorize:**
> $R_u = 8.314$ J/mol·K · $E_a(\text{NO}) = 317$ kJ/mol · $T_{\text{auto-ignition, diesel}} \approx 520$ K · Stoichiometric A/F for diesel ≈ **14.5** · $\gamma \approx 1.35$ in burned gas.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

LTC strategies are the technological backbone of:
- **Euro 7 / BS-VII** heavy-duty diesel engines (diesel still allowed, but NOx limits are < 0.46 g/kWh).
- **Hybrid HCCI–SI engines** (Mazda SkyActiv-X uses SPCCI = Spark Controlled Compression Ignition, a commercial HCCI variant).
- **Hydrogen and ammonia engines** for marine and stationary power.
- **Range-extender engines** in series hybrid EVs (always run at constant load — perfect for HCCI).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Estimation of NOx Reduction in LTC vs. Conventional Diesel

**Given:**
- Conventional diesel peak temperature: $T_{\text{conv}} = 2200$ K
- LTC peak temperature: $T_{\text{LTC}} = 1700$ K
- Activation energy for Zeldovich NO formation: $E_a = 317{,}000$ J/mol
- Universal gas constant: $R_u = 8.314$ J/mol·K

**Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius rate expression for NO formation per unit volume:**
$$ r_{\text{NO}} \;=\; A\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R_u\,T}\right) $$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio of NO formation rate at LTC temperature to that at conventional temperature:**
$$ \dfrac{r_{\text{NO, LTC}}}{r_{\text{NO, conv}}} \;=\; \dfrac{A\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R_u T_{\text{LTC}}}\right)}{A\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R_u T_{\text{conv}}}\right)} $$

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent (the pre-exponential factor A cancels):**
$$ \dfrac{r_{\text{NO, LTC}}}{r_{\text{NO, conv}}} \;=\; \exp\!\left[ -\dfrac{E_a}{R_u}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{LTC}}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{conv}}}\right) \right] $$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values (units shown explicitly for board clarity):**
$$ \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{LTC}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1700} \;=\; 5.882 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1} $$
$$ \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{conv}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2200} \;=\; 4.545 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1} $$
$$ \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{LTC}}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{conv}}} \;=\; 1.337 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the exponent:**
$$ -\dfrac{E_a}{R_u}\,(1.337\times 10^{-4}) \;=\; -\dfrac{317{,}000}{8.314}\,(1.337\times 10^{-4}) $$
$$ \;=\; -(38{,}128.5)\,(1.337\times 10^{-4}) \;=\; -5.098 $$

**Step 6 — Compute the ratio:**
$$ \dfrac{r_{\text{NO, LTC}}}{r_{\text{NO, conv}}} \;=\; e^{-5.098} \;\approx\; 6.10 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 7 — Express as a percentage reduction:**
$$ \%\,\text{NO}_x\,\text{ reduction} \;=\; \left(1 - 6.10\times 10^{-3}\right) \times 100 \;\approx\; \mathbf{99.39\,\%} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Insight:** A drop of just **500 K** in peak temperature causes an exponential reduction of ~99 % in NOx. This is the *fundamental thermodynamic justification* for all LTC strategies and is a guaranteed 7-to-10 mark derivation question in the KTU 2024 ESE.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Rating of LTC Strategies

The matrix below is a high-yield comparison students are routinely asked to reproduce in **Part B (14 mark)** KTU questions.

| Parameter | **HCCI** | **PCCI** | **RCCI** | **PPC** | **Dual-Fuel LTC** |
|-----------|----------|----------|----------|---------|-------------------|
| Charge Homogeneity | Fully premixed | Mostly premixed | Reactivity-graded | Partially premixed | Gaseous premixed + diesel pilot |
| Ignition Control | Very poor | Moderate | **Excellent** | Moderate-High | Good |
| NOx (g/kWh) | < 0.05 | < 0.1 | < 0.05 | < 0.1 | 0.05 – 0.3 |
| Soot (g/kWh) | ~ 0 | ~ 0.01 | ~ 0 | 0.01 – 0.05 | 0.02 – 0.05 |
| HC/CO | **High** | Moderate | Low | Moderate-High | High at light load |
| PRR (bar/deg) | **Very high** | High | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| Load Range | Narrow (mid) | Moderate | **Wide** | Moderate | Moderate |
| Fuel Flexibility | High (gasoline/ethanol) | Limited | **Very high** | Limited (diesel) | High (CNG/H₂/LPG) |
| Engine Modification | Significant | Moderate | Moderate | **Minimal** | Significant |

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Heat Release Rate (HRR) During HCCI Combustion

The standard KTU-style expression for instantaneous apparent heat release rate (HRR) from cylinder pressure data is:

$$ \frac{dQ}{d\theta} \;=\; \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, P\, \frac{dV}{d\theta} \;+\; \frac{1}{\gamma - 1}\, V\, \frac{dP}{d\theta} $$

**Derivation steps:**

**Step 1 — First law for an open, non-flow, variable mass system (per cylinder, per crank-angle):**
$$ \frac{dU}{d\theta} \;=\; \frac{dQ_{B}}{d\theta} - \frac{dW}{d\theta} \;+\; \sum_i\, h_i\, \frac{dm_i}{d\theta} $$

**Step 2 — For an HCCI event, the valve is closed and there is **no mass flow** during combustion, so the third term vanishes:**
$$ \frac{dU}{d\theta} \;=\; \frac{dQ_{B}}{d\theta} - \frac{dW}{d\theta} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute $U = m\,c_v T$ and $W = \int P\,dV$, differentiate using ideal gas law $P V = m R_u T$:**
$$ \frac{dU}{d\theta} \;=\; \frac{c_v}{R_u}\,\frac{d(PV)}{d\theta} $$

**Step 4 — Divide by $d\theta$ and expand:**
$$ \frac{c_v}{R_u}\!\left(P\,\frac{dV}{d\theta} + V\,\frac{dP}{d\theta}\right) \;=\; \frac{dQ_{B}}{d\theta} - P\,\frac{dV}{d\theta} $$

**Step 5 — Using $\gamma = c_p / c_v$ and $R_u = c_p - c_v$, derive $\dfrac{c_v}{R_u} = \dfrac{1}{\gamma - 1}$:**
$$ \frac{1}{\gamma - 1}\,\!\left(P\,\frac{dV}{d\theta} + V\,\frac{dP}{d\theta}\right) \;=\; \frac{dQ_{B}}{d\theta} - P\,\frac{dV}{d\theta} $$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate HRR:**
$$ \frac{dQ_{B}}{d\theta} \;=\; \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, P\,\frac{dV}{d\theta} \;+\; \frac{1}{\gamma - 1}\, V\,\frac{dP}{d\theta} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Hint:** This is the **Rassweiler-Withrow equation**, the basis of every HRR diagram an examiner will ever ask. Memorize the form, not the derivation history.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Pressure Rise Rate (PRR) Constraint

A typical KTU numerical asks students to verify whether an LTC design stays within acceptable noise/vibration limits.

$$ \text{PRR}_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\gamma\, P_{\max}}{V_{\min}}\,\omega\, R\,\sin\theta $$

**Step 1 — Recognize that the maximum piston velocity is $\omega R \sin\theta$.**
**Step 2 — At TDC, $\sin\theta = 1$, so $\text{PRR}_{\max} \approx \dfrac{\gamma P_{\max}}{V}\, \omega R$.**
**Step 3 — Insert sample values:** $P_{\max} = 80$ bar, $V_{\min} = 50$ cm³, $\omega = 200$ rad/s, $R = 5$ cm = 0.05 m, $\gamma = 1.35$.

$$ \text{PRR} \;=\; \frac{1.35 \times 80 \times 10^5}{50 \times 10^{-6}} \times 200 \times 0.05 $$
$$ \;=\; 2.16 \times 10^8 \times 10 $$
$$ \;=\; 2.16 \times 10^{9}\ \text{Pa/s} \;\approx\; 2.16\ \text{bar/ms} $$

**Conclusion:** Well below the **5 bar/deg** comfort threshold — acceptable for HCCI operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
