# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Low Temperature Combustion (LTC). State any **two** distinguishing features of LTC compared to conventional diesel combustion.*

**Model Answer (Concise — 3 marks format):**
> **Definition:** LTC is an advanced combustion regime in which peak in-cylinder temperature is maintained below ~1800 K through heavy charge dilution and premixing, with auto-ignition as the dominant combustion mode.
>
> **Feature 1:** Combustion is **kinetically controlled** (auto-ignition of a premixed or partially premixed charge) rather than diffusion-flame controlled.
>
> **Feature 2:** NOx and soot are reduced by **>90 %** due to the simultaneous absence of fuel-rich pockets and lower peak temperatures, at the cost of higher unburned HC and CO.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*List any **four** Low Temperature Combustion strategies studied in your syllabus and give one-line descriptions.*

**Model Answer (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks + examples = 3 marks):**
1. **HCCI** — Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition (fully premixed auto-ignition).
2. **RCCI** — Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (dual fuel with reactivity gradient).
3. **PPC** — Partially Premixed Combustion (very early diesel injection).
4. **PCCI** — Premixed Charge Compression Ignition (mostly premixed, slight stratification).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### ⬛ Question A — *(14 Marks)* [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** *Explain the working principle, charge preparation, and ignition process of **HCCI** combustion. Discuss its major **advantages and limitations**. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:** In HCCI, a premixed homogeneous mixture of fuel + air + diluent (EGR) is formed inside the cylinder **before compression begins**. The mixture is then compressed until auto-ignition temperature is reached, after which combustion occurs almost simultaneously at many points throughout the chamber.

**Charge Preparation:**
- Port fuel injection (PFI) or early direct injection.
- Heavy EGR (30–60 %) and/or excess air.
- Target equivalence ratio Φ = 0.3 – 0.7 (lean).

**Ignition Process (Two-Stage):**
1. *Low Temperature Heat Release (LTHR)* at 800–900 K — produces aldehydes and cool-flame intermediates.
2. *High Temperature Heat Release (HTHR)* at ~1100 K — bulk ignition of the homogeneous mixture.

**Advantages (3 marks worth):**
- Near-zero NOx (because peak T < 1800 K).
- Near-zero soot (no rich pockets).
- High part-load efficiency.
- Multi-fuel capability (gasoline, ethanol, DME, natural gas).

**Limitations (2 marks worth):**
- Poor ignition timing control → narrow operating range.
- High HC and CO at low loads (cold walls, crevice effects).
- High pressure rise rates → engine noise/vibration.

**(b)** *Derive an expression showing the **reduction in NOx formation rate** when the peak combustion temperature is reduced from 2200 K to 1700 K. Use the extended Zeldovich mechanism. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (Complete derivation — see Section 3.1 of these notes for full step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Arrhenius form of NO formation rate:**
$$ r_{\text{NO}} = A\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R_u T}\right) $$

**Step 2 — Form ratio at LTC vs. conventional conditions:**
$$ \dfrac{r_{\text{NO, LTC}}}{r_{\text{NO, conv}}} = \exp\!\left[ -\dfrac{E_a}{R_u}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{LTC}}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{conv}}}\right) \right] $$

**Step 3 — Substitute $E_a = 317{,}000$ J/mol, $R_u = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T_{\text{LTC}} = 1700$ K, $T_{\text{conv}} = 2200$ K:**

$$ \dfrac{1}{1700} - \dfrac{1}{2200} = 1.337 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1} $$

**Step 4 — Exponent calculation:**
$$ -\dfrac{317{,}000}{8.314} \times (1.337\times 10^{-4}) = -5.098 $$

**Step 5 — Final result:**
$$ \dfrac{r_{\text{NO, LTC}}}{r_{\text{NO, conv}}} = e^{-5.098} \approx 6.10 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** NOx formation reduces by approximately **99.4 %**, confirming the LTC justification.

---

### ⬛ Question B — *(14 Marks — Alternative Choice)* [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** *Compare the working principles of **RCCI** and **PPC** strategies of low temperature combustion in tabular form with **at least six parameters**. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):**

| Parameter | **RCCI** | **PPC** |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| Charge Preparation | Two fuels of different reactivity (e.g., gasoline PFI + diesel DI) | Single diesel fuel injected very early |
| Reactivity Control | Spatial + temporal reactivity gradient | Achieved by early injection timing |
| Injection System | Dual-loop fuel system | Single high-pressure common rail |
| Engine Modification | Moderate to significant | Minimal — uses existing hardware |
| NOx Level | Very low (<0.05 g/kWh) | Low (<0.1 g/kWh) |
| Soot Level | Negligible | Low to moderate |
| Operating Range | Wide (full load) | Moderate (mid load optimal) |
| Control Complexity | High (two fuel injections) | Moderate (one injection timing parameter) |

**(b)** *Discuss **dual-fuel LTC** strategy using CNG as primary fuel with diesel pilot. Explain how the **reactivity gradient** affects combustion phasing. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Dual-Fuel LTC Concept:**
In dual-fuel LTC, a **low-reactivity gaseous fuel (CNG, LPG, or H₂)** is introduced into the intake manifold to form a homogeneous lean premixture with air and EGR. Near the end of compression, a small **pilot dose of high-reactivity diesel** (~10–20 % of total energy) is directly injected, which acts as the *ignition source* for the bulk CNG mixture.

**Role of Reactivity Gradient:**
- The pilot diesel auto-ignites first because of its high cetane number.
- The diesel combustion ignites the surrounding CNG-air mixture progressively.
- The **combustion phasing is dominated by the reactivity difference** ($\Delta R = R_{\text{diesel}} - R_{\text{CNG}}$) and the **mixing time** of diesel spray with the CNG mixture.

**Performance Benefits:**
- NOx < 0.3 g/kWh (due to lean + EGR dilution).
- Soot near zero (no rich diffusion flame).
- Replaces up to 80–90 % diesel with cheaper gaseous fuel.

**Limitations:**
- Reduced efficiency at high loads due to slow CNG oxidation.
- HC and CO emissions at low loads (unburned CNG).
- Need for gaseous fuel storage and twin fuel system.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Examiner's Pitfall List:**
> 1. **Conflating HCCI with HCCI/SI hybrids** (e.g., Mazda SPCCI) — SPCCI uses a spark plug and is NOT pure HCCI. Mentioning it as "HCCI" will lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Quoting peak temperature incorrectly** — always specify **< 1800 K**, not "low temperature" vaguely.
> 3. **Confusing RCCI with dual-fuel** — RCCI uses *two fuels both in-cylinder* (one premixed + one direct-injected); dual-fuel LTC is broader and may involve *external mixing*.
> 4. **Skipping the two-stage ignition** in HCCI — the cool-flame (LTHR) and hot-flame (HTHR) stages are mandatory 2-mark items.
> 5. **Forgetting to state $E_a$ and $R_u$ units** in NOx derivation — penalised 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Stating that LTC eliminates NOx completely** — it *near-eliminates* NOx (typically < 50 ppm). The word "zero" is technically incorrect and penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **LTC target peak T < 1800 K**, equivalence ratio **Φ = 0.3–0.7**.
- ✅ **LTC strategies:** HCCI, PCCI, RCCI, PPC, Dual-Fuel LTC.
- ✅ **HCCI:** Fully premixed, best emissions, worst control.
- ✅ **RCCI:** Reactivity gradient using two fuels; **most promising** for heavy-duty.
- ✅ **PPC:** Early diesel injection; easiest retrofit on diesel engines.
- ✅ **Two-stage ignition** in HCCI: Cool flame (~850 K) → Hot flame (~1100 K).
- ✅ **NOx reduction formula:** $r_{\text{NO}} \propto \exp(-E_a / R_u T)$ with **$E_a = 317$ kJ/mol**.
- ✅ **Soot is killed** because lean homogeneous charge has no fuel-rich regions.
- ✅ **Major challenge of LTC:** Combustion phasing control + narrow operating range.
- ✅ **Mazda SkyActiv-X** is SPCCI (Spark Controlled CI) — a commercial HCCI variant.
- ✅ **Heavy EGR (20–60 %)** is the most common dilution strategy.
- ✅ **HC and CO increase** in LTC at low loads — trade-off with NOx.
- ✅ **Pressure Rise Rate (PRR)** must remain < **5 bar/deg** for smooth HCCI operation.
- ✅ **Efficiency limit** of HCCI = **ideal Otto cycle** (since combustion is premixed).
- ✅ **Diesel pilot in dual-fuel** = ~10–20 % energy fraction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Compliant Definition

**Principle of Combustion in IC Engines** refers to the fundamental physical, chemical, and thermodynamic processes by which a hydrocarbon fuel is rapidly oxidized within the combustion chamber of an internal combustion engine to release **chemical energy** as **thermal energy**, which is subsequently converted into **mechanical work**.

In SI (Spark-Ignition) engines, combustion is initiated by an external spark and propagates as a **flame front** through a homogeneous premixture of air and fuel.

In CI (Compression-Ignition) engines, combustion is initiated by the high temperature of compressed air, into which fuel is injected as a **spray** — resulting in a **heterogeneous, mixing-controlled** combustion process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Principle):**
> The principle establishes that engine combustion is a **non-equilibrium, unsteady, turbulent reacting flow** problem governed by chemical kinetics (fuel–oxidizer reaction rates), fluid mechanics (turbulence, spray dynamics), and thermodynamics (energy and species conservation).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bonfire vs. The Pressure Cooker" 🔥

Imagine two ways to light a fire:

### 🍳 Analogy 1 — The SI Engine (Premixed Flame — "Bonfire")
- You pour kerosene and air together into a **transparent box** (homogeneous mixture).
- You strike a match (spark plug) → the **flame front sweeps outward** like ripples on a pond.
- The burn is **fast, smooth, and layer-by-layer**.

### 🧯 Analogy 2 — The CI Engine (Diffusion Flame — "Blowtorch")
- You **inject fuel spray** into a chamber of **hot, compressed air** (no match needed — auto-ignites on contact).
- Combustion happens at the **interface** of fuel droplets and air (mixing-controlled).
- The burn is **slower, rougher**, and depends on how finely the spray mixes with air.

### 🎯 Key Insight
Both systems convert chemical energy into heat, but **HOW** that heat is released — by flame propagation (SI) or by spray mixing (CI) — defines the entire **principle** of IC engine combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constant (KTU Frequently Used):**
> **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg**
> **Lower Heating Value (diesel) ≈ 42.5 MJ/kg**
> **Stoichiometric A/F ratio: Gasoline ≈ 14.7 : 1 | Diesel ≈ 14.5 : 1**

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Combustion** | Rapid oxidation of fuel with release of heat and light |
| **Premixed Charge** | Homogeneous mixture of fuel vapor and air before combustion (SI) |
| **Diffusion Flame** | Flame where fuel and air mix simultaneously with burning (CI) |
| **Flame Front** | Thin zone separating unburned and burned gases in SI engines |
| **Ignition Delay** | Time between start of injection and start of combustion in CI engines |
| **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** | Mass ratio of air to fuel in the cylinder |
| **Equivalence Ratio (φ)** | Ratio of actual AFR to stoichiometric AFR |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" — Three Pillars of IC Engine Combustion Principle

### 🧪 Pillar 1 — Chemical Kinetics (The "Why It Burns")
Combustion is governed by the **Arrhenius reaction rate**:

$$
k = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T}\right)
$$

- **Why**: For fuel molecules to oxidize, they must overcome an **activation energy barrier** $E_a$.
- **How**: At SI engine conditions (T ≈ 800–1000 K locally after compression), reactions are slow. A spark provides localized energy to initiate them.
- At CI engine conditions (T ≈ 800–900 K after compression), air is hot enough that the spray **auto-ignites** without a spark.

### 🌪️ Pillar 2 — Fluid Mechanics & Turbulence (The "How Fast It Burns")
Real engine combustion is dominated by **turbulent flame propagation**:

$$
S_T = S_L \, u'
$$

Where:
- $S_T$ = turbulent flame speed (typically 5–25 m/s in engines)
- $S_L$ = laminar flame speed (≈ 0.3–0.5 m/s for hydrocarbons)
- $u'$ = RMS turbulent velocity fluctuation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: Turbulence increases flame speed by a factor of **10–50×** compared to laminar flame speed. This is why engines burn fuel in milliseconds.

### 🔥 Pillar 3 — Thermodynamics (The "How Much Energy")
Energy released is given by the **First Law for an open system (control volume)** applied to the combustion chamber:

$$
\frac{dU}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \dot{m}_f h_f - \dot{m}_e h_e
$$

For the closed combustion chamber (no mass flow during combustion):

$$
\Delta U = Q_{in} - W_{out}
$$

$$
m \, c_v \, (T_3 - T_2) = m_f \times LHV - W_{expansion}
$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested in KTU University Exams** for PEMUT752 Module 4.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application in KTU Exam |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathrm{AFR_{stoich}} = \dfrac{34.5}{12} \cdot C + \dfrac{34.5}{4} \cdot H - \dfrac{34.5}{32} \cdot O$ | C, H, O = mass fractions | kg air / kg fuel | Stoichiometric calculation |
| 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{(\mathrm{A/F})_{stoich}}{(\mathrm{A/F})_{actual}}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless | Rich/lean determination |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(\mathrm{A/F})_{actual}}{(\mathrm{A/F})_{stoich}}$ | Relative air-fuel ratio | dimensionless | λ > 1 = lean; λ < 1 = rich |
| 4 | $Q = m_f \times \mathrm{LHV}$ | Heat released | kJ or MJ | Energy balance |
| 5 | $\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{Q_{released} - Q_{loss}}{Q_{released}}$ | Combustion efficiency | % | Performance |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot \mathrm{N}}{2 \cdot \mathrm{AFR} \cdot 60}$ | Fuel mass flow rate | kg/s | Fuel consumption |
| 7 | $P_i = \dfrac{W_{net}}{n \cdot V_s}$ | Indicated power | kW | Performance |
| 8 | $S_L \approx S_{L,0}\!\left(\dfrac{T_u}{T_0}\right)^{\alpha}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)^{\beta}$ | Laminar flame speed (Metghalchi–Keck) | m/s | Flame propagation |
| 9 | $\tau_i = A \cdot P^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R_u T}\right)$ | Ignition delay (Arrhenius) | seconds | CI engine combustion |
| 10 | $\dfrac{dQ}{d\theta} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \cdot P \dfrac{dV}{d\theta} + \dfrac{1}{\gamma-1} \cdot V \dfrac{dP}{d\theta}$ | Heat Release Rate (HRR) | J/deg | Combustion analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Warning**: When writing equations in answer sheets, **always define each symbol the first time you use it**. Marks are deducted for "undefined variables" in derivation questions.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How "Principle" Is Used |
|---|---|
| **Engine Design (CFD)** | Combustion chamber shape optimized using turbulent flame propagation theory |
| **Fuel Development** | Octane/Cetane ratings designed based on ignition delay chemistry |
| **Emission Control** | Equivalence ratio φ controls formation of CO, NOx, HC, PM |
| **Calibration of ECU** | HRR models used to predict knock and optimize ignition timing |
| **Hybrid Powertrains** | Combustion efficiency directly determines engine ON/OFF strategy |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for a Generic Hydrocarbon

**Given**: A hydrocarbon fuel has the molecular formula $C_x H_y$ with mass fractions $c$, $h$, $o$ of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen.

**Complete Combustion Reaction**:

$$
C_xH_y + \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow xCO_2 + \tfrac{y}{2}H_2O
$$

**Mass of air required per kg of fuel**:

Let the mass of fuel be **1 kg**. Then:
- Mass of carbon = $c$ kg → requires $c \cdot \tfrac{32}{12}$ kg of $O_2$
- Mass of hydrogen = $h$ kg → requires $h \cdot \tfrac{32}{4}$ kg of $O_2$
- Mass of oxygen already present in fuel = $o$ kg → reduces $O_2$ need by $o \cdot \tfrac{32}{32} = o$ kg

$$
m_{O_2,\text{required}} = \tfrac{32}{12} c + 8h - o
$$

Since air contains **23.3 % oxygen by mass** (or $1/0.233 \approx 4.32$):

$$
\boxed{\;\mathrm{AFR}_{stoich} = 4.32 \left[\tfrac{32}{12} c + 8h - o\right] = \dfrac{34.5\,c + 34.5\,h - 4.32\,o}{\text{fuel mass}}\;}
$$

For **pure octane** ($C_8H_{18}$, no oxygen):

$$
\mathrm{AFR}_{stoich} = 4.32 \times \left[\tfrac{32}{12}(1) + 8(0)\right] = 4.32 \times 2.667 = 15.13
$$

(Near standard textbook value for gasoline: **15.13 : 1**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Release Rate (HRR) Using First Law

**Step 1**: Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the combustion chamber (closed system, no mass transfer during combustion):

$$
\delta Q - \delta W = dU
$$

**Step 2**: Express internal energy in terms of temperature and add species energy:

$$
dU = m \, c_v \, dT + \sum_i \left(m_i \frac{dh_i^0}{d\theta}\right) d\theta
$$

Where $h_i^0$ is the standard enthalpy of formation of species $i$.

**Step 3**: Express work done by expanding gas:

$$
\delta W = P \, dV
$$

**Step 4**: Combine:

$$
\delta Q = dU + P \, dV = m \, c_v \, dT + P \, dV + \sum_i h_i^0 \, dm_i
$$

**Step 5**: Use ideal gas law $PV = mRT$, so $P dV = mR dT + V dP$:

$$
\delta Q = m(c_v + R)dT + V dP + \sum_i h_i^0 dm_i
$$

Since $c_v + R = c_p$ and $\gamma = c_p/c_v$:

$$
\delta Q = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} V \, dP + \dfrac{1}{\gamma - 1} P \, dV + \sum_i h_i^0 \, dm_i
$$

**Step 6**: Convert to per-degree crank angle (divide by $d\theta$):

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{dQ}{d\theta} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} V \dfrac{dP}{d\theta} + \dfrac{1}{\gamma - 1} P \dfrac{dV}{d\theta} - \sum_i h_i^0 \dfrac{dm_i}{d\theta}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation Note**: The "minus" sign on the species term arises because fuel is being **consumed** (negative $dm_{fuel}/d\theta$). Board examiners accept either sign convention **provided the student explicitly states it**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Balance for Combustion Efficiency

**Given**: $m_f$ kg of fuel with LHV is burned. Useful heat retained is $Q_{useful}$, while $Q_{loss}$ is lost to coolant, exhaust, and incomplete combustion.

**Step 1**: Total heat input:

$$
Q_{in} = m_f \times \mathrm{LHV}
$$

**Step 2**: Useful heat retained:

$$
Q_{useful} = Q_{in} - Q_{loss}
$$

**Step 3**: Combustion efficiency:

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{Q_{in} - Q_{loss}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{coolant} + Q_{exhaust} + Q_{unburned}}{m_f \cdot \mathrm{LHV}}\;}
$$

**Numerical Example** (typical KTU problem):
- $m_f = 0.002$ kg/cycle
- LHV = 44,000 kJ/kg
- $Q_{coolant}$ = 15 kJ/cycle
- $Q_{exhaust}$ = 20 kJ/cycle
- $Q_{unburned}$ = 2 kJ/cycle

**Step-by-step numerical solution**:

$$
Q_{in} = 0.002 \times 44000 = 88 \text{ kJ/cycle}
$$

$$
Q_{loss} = 15 + 20 + 2 = 37 \text{ kJ/cycle}
$$

$$
\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{88 - 37}{88} = \dfrac{51}{88} = 0.579 = 57.9\,\%
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Combustion Comparison (SI vs CI)

| Parameter | SI Engine (Premixed) | CI Engine (Diffusion) |
|---|---|---|
| Mixture preparation | Pre-formed (homogeneous) | In-cylinder (heterogeneous) |
| Ignition source | Spark plug | Auto (compression heating) |
| Combustion mode | Flame propagation | Spray mixing + reaction |
| Governing physics | Turbulent flame speed | Ignition delay + spray dynamics |
| Flame type | Premixed flame | Diffusion flame (mostly) |
| Equivalence ratio φ | ≈ 0.85 – 1.1 | ≈ 0.7 – 0.9 (locally varies) |
| Heat release duration | ≈ 30–60° crank angle | ≈ 40–80° crank angle |
| Peak temperature | ≈ 2200–2500 K | ≈ 2500–2800 K |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Principle of Combustion Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel + Air Entry] --> B[Mixture Preparation]
    B --> C{Engine Type}
    C -->|SI Engine| D[Homogeneous Premixture]
    C -->|CI Engine| E[High-Temp Compressed Air]
    D --> F[Spark Ignition]
    E --> G[Fuel Injection Spray]
    F --> H[Flame Front Propagation]
    G --> I[Ignition Delay Period]
    I --> J[Mixing-Controlled Combustion]
    H --> K[Turbulent Reaction Zone]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Heat Release to Gas]
    L --> M[Pressure Rise and Expansion]
    M --> N[Mechanical Work Output]
    L --> O[Heat Losses: Coolant and Exhaust]
    O --> P[Emissions: CO, NOx, HC, PM]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stages of Combustion Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Ignition Lag] --> S2[Stage 2: Rapid Combustion]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Controlled Combustion]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: After Burning]
    S1 -.->|CI Engine Only| S1a[Physical and Chemical Delay]
    S2 -.->|Premixed Burn| S2a[Flame Front Sweeps]
    S3 -.->|Mixing Controlled| S3a[Diffusion Burn]
    S4 -.->|Tail Combustion| S4a[Late Burning on Expansion]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Combustion Analysis (Block-Level Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Input: Pressure Crank Angle Data] --> B1[Signal Processing]
    A2[Input: Cylinder Volume Function] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Apply First Law of Thermodynamics]
    C1 --> D1[Calculate Net Apparent HRR]
    D1 --> E1[Apply Heat Transfer Correction]
    E1 --> F1[Final Net Heat Release Rate]
    F1 --> G1[Compute Combustion Duration]
    F1 --> G2[Compute Mass Fraction Burned]
    F1 --> G3[Identify Knock or Misfire]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Note**: When asked to "draw the block diagram of heat release analysis," this **functional flow** (Input → First Law → HRR → Diagnostics) is the **expected answer structure** as per board valuation keys.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

### **Q1. Define the principle of combustion in a Spark Ignition engine.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The principle of combustion in an SI engine is based on **flame propagation through a homogeneous premixture of fuel vapor and air**. Combustion is initiated by an electric spark at the spark plug, creating a kernel that grows into a thin **flame front**. This flame front then propagates outward through the unburned mixture at a **turbulent flame speed** ($S_T \approx 5$–$25$ m/s), releasing chemical energy as heat. The process is governed by:
1. **Chemical kinetics** (Arrhenius reaction at the flame front),
2. **Turbulent fluid mechanics** (eddy transport of reactants), and
3. **Thermodynamics** (pressure rise and work transfer).

> ✅ **Examiner's Note**: Award full marks only if all three governing factors are mentioned.

---

### **Q2. State the principle of combustion in a Compression Ignition engine.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The principle of combustion in a CI engine is based on **auto-ignition of fuel spray injected into hot compressed air**. Since the air is compressed to high pressure (≈ 30–50 bar) and temperature (≈ 800–900 K), the injected fuel spray **auto-ignites** after a short **ignition delay period**. Combustion occurs in a **mixing-controlled** (diffusion) mode, where fuel droplets vaporize, mix with surrounding air, and burn at the **flame sheet surrounding each droplet**. The HRR depends on the rate of fuel–air mixing rather than flame propagation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** *(14 Marks)* `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio for a fuel with carbon content $c = 0.84$, hydrogen $h = 0.16$, and negligible oxygen. **Use molecular weights C=12, H=1, O=16, N=14, Air = 29.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A four-stroke SI engine operates at 3000 rpm with displacement 1.5 L, volumetric efficiency 0.85, and air density 1.2 kg/m³. The equivalence ratio is φ = 0.9 and fuel LHV is 44 MJ/kg. Calculate: (i) Mass of air inducted per cycle, (ii) Mass of fuel per cycle, (iii) Heat released per cycle, (iv) Indicated power if combustion efficiency is 90 %. *(7 Marks)*

---

### **📝 MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A**

#### **Part (a) — Stoichiometric AFR Derivation** *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1** — Write the combustion equation per kg of fuel:
Mass of carbon = 0.84 kg, hydrogen = 0.16 kg.

**Step 2** — Calculate moles of C and H:

$$
n_C = \frac{0.84}{12} = 0.07 \text{ kmol C per kg fuel}
$$

$$
n_H = \frac{0.16}{1} = 0.16 \text{ kmol H per kg fuel}
$$

**Step 3** — Oxygen required for complete combustion:

$$
C + O_2 \to CO_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; O_2 \text{ for C} = 0.07 \text{ kmol}
$$

$$
H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \to H_2O \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; O_2 \text{ for H} = \tfrac{0.16}{4} = 0.04 \text{ kmol}
$$

$$
n_{O_2, \text{total}} = 0.07 + 0.04 = 0.11 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 4** — Mass of oxygen:

$$
m_{O_2} = 0.11 \times 32 = 3.52 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5** — Mass of air (O₂ constitutes 23.3 % of air by mass):

$$
m_{air} = \frac{3.52}{0.233} = 15.11 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\mathrm{AFR}_{stoich} = 15.11 : 1\;}
$$

✅ **Marks Distribution**: (1) Equation (1) (2) Moles (2) (3) O₂ mass (2) (4) Air mass (2) = **7 marks**.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculations** *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1** — Volumetric flow rate of air:

$$
\dot{V}_a = \dfrac{V_d \cdot N}{2 \times 60} \quad \text{(4-stroke)}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_a = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 3000}{120} = 0.0375 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2** — With volumetric efficiency η_v = 0.85, actual air flow:

$$
\dot{V}_{a,\text{actual}} = 0.85 \times 0.0375 = 0.031875 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3** — Mass of air per second:

$$
\dot{m}_a = 0.031875 \times 1.2 = 0.03825 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4** — Air per cycle (2 revs per cycle for 4-stroke):

$$
m_{a,\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{0.03825}{25} = 0.00153 \text{ kg/cycle}
$$

**Step 5** — Actual AFR (using φ = 0.9 and stoichiometric = 15.11):

$$
\mathrm{AFR}_{actual} = \dfrac{\mathrm{AFR}_{stoich}}{\phi} = \dfrac{15.11}{0.9} = 16.79
$$

**Step 6** — Fuel per cycle:

$$
m_{f,\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{0.00153}{16.79} = 9.11 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/cycle}
$$

**Step 7** — Heat released per cycle:

$$
Q_{cycle} = m_f \times \mathrm{LHV} = 9.11 \times 10^{-5} \times 44000 = 4.01 \text{ kJ/cycle}
$$

**Step 8** — Useful heat (η_comb = 0.90):

$$
Q_{useful} = 0.90 \times 4.01 = 3.609 \text{ kJ/cycle}
$$

**Step 9** — Indicated Power (25 cycles/sec for 4-stroke at 3000 rpm):

$$
\dot{W}_i = 3.609 \times 25 = 90.23 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;P_i \approx 90.23 \text{ kW}\;}
```

---

### **Question B** *(14 Marks — Alternative Choice)* `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **three stages of combustion in an SI engine** and indicate on a P-θ diagram where each occurs. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A CI engine operates with global equivalence ratio φ = 0.7. The fuel is C₁₂H₂₆ (dodecane). If 1.5 kg of fuel is burned per hour, calculate: (i) Stoichiometric AFR of dodecane, (ii) Theoretical air required per hour, (iii) Actual air supplied per hour, (iv) Exhaust gas composition (% by volume) assuming complete combustion. *(7 Marks)*

---

### **📝 MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B**

#### **Part (a) — Three Stages of SI Combustion** *(7 Marks)*

**Stage 1 — Ignition Lag (1–2° crank angle):**
The spark creates a small kernel. Initial flame kernel grows slowly due to low temperature and low turbulence. End of stage is marked by rapid pressure rise.

**Stage 2 — Rapid Combustion (≈ 20–30°):**
Flame propagates rapidly through the premixture. Pressure rises sharply. This is the **main heat release phase**. Excessive rapidity causes **engine knock**.

**Stage 3 — After-Burning (tail combustion, ≈ 30–50°):**
Last portion of charge (in crevices, near walls) burns slowly as the piston descends. Controlled by mixture motion and turbulence decay.

**P-θ Diagram Indication**:
- Stage 1: Just after spark, near TDC
- Stage 2: Rapid pressure rise after TDC
- Stage 3: Gradual pressure decay on expansion stroke

✅ **Marks**: (1) Stage 1 (2), (2) Stage 2 (3), (3) Stage 3 (1), (4) P-θ (1) = **7 marks**.

---

#### **Part (b) — CI Engine Air and Exhaust Calculations** *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1** — Stoichiometric AFR for C₁₂H₂₆:

$$
C_{12}H_{26} + \tfrac{35}{2}O_2 \to 12CO_2 + 13H_2O
$$

Molecular weight of fuel = $12(12) + 26(1) = 170$ g/mol.

Moles of O₂ per mole of fuel = $35/2 = 17.5$ mol.

Mass of O₂ = $17.5 \times 32 = 560$ g.

Mass of air = $560 / 0.233 = 2403$ g.

$$
\boxed{\;\mathrm{AFR}_{stoich} = 2403 / 170 = 14.13 : 1\;}
$$

**Step 2** — Theoretical air per hour:

$$
\dot{m}_{air,\text{theor}} = 1.5 \times 14.13 = 21.2 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Step 3** — Actual air supplied (φ = 0.7 means rich mixture):

$$
\mathrm{AFR}_{actual} = \dfrac{14.13}{0.7} = 20.19
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{air,\text{actual}} = 1.5 \times 20.19 = 30.29 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Step 4** — Combustion products per hour:
Per kg fuel: 12/170 kmol CO₂ = 0.0706 kmol; 13/170 kmol H₂O = 0.0765 kmol; 17.5/170 kmol O₂ consumed = 0.1029 kmol; N₂ from air = (0.1029 × 79/21) = 0.387 kmol.

**Exhaust composition (mole fraction basis)**:
- Total moles per kg fuel = $0.0706 + 0.0765 + 0.387 = 0.534$ kmol
- % CO₂ = $0.0706/0.534 = 13.2\%$
- % H₂O = $0.0765/0.534 = 14.3\%$
- % N₂ = $72.5\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing φ and λ** — φ < 1 means RICH mixture (more fuel), φ > 1 means LEAN.
> 2. **Forgetting the air-to-O₂ mass ratio** (use **4.32** or **1/0.233**, not 1).
> 3. **4-stroke vs 2-stroke confusion** in power calculations — always divide N by 2 for 4-stroke.
> 4. **Not converting g → kg or hr → s** consistently in numerical problems.
> 5. **In HRR derivation, missing the species enthalpy term** $\sum h_i^0 dm_i$ — this is a **2-mark penalty** in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Bullets — "Read This in the Last 15 Minutes Before Exam"**

- ✅ **Combustion Principle** = Chemical Kinetics + Turbulence + Thermodynamics.
- ✅ **SI Engine** → Premixed, Flame Propagation, Spark Initiated.
- ✅ **CI Engine** → Diffusion Flame, Spray Mixing, Auto-Ignition.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric AFR** = $4.32 \times \left[\frac{32}{12}c + 8h - o\right]$.
- ✅ **Equivalence Ratio** $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$.
- ✅ **Combustion Efficiency** $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{in}}$.
- ✅ **Turbulent Flame Speed** $S_T = S_L \cdot u'$ (≈ 10–50× laminar).
- ✅ **HRR Equation** uses First Law + Ideal Gas Law — know both forms (apparent and net).
- ✅ **3 Stages of SI Combustion**: Ignition Lag → Rapid → After-Burn.
- ✅ **3 Stages of CI Combustion**: Ignition Delay → Uncontrolled (Premixed) → Controlled (Mixing).
- ✅ **LHV values**: Gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg, Diesel ≈ 42.5 MJ/kg.
- ✅ **Standard A/F**: Gasoline ≈ 14.7, Diesel ≈ 14.5 (often rounded).
- ✅ **Unit Conversions to Remember**: 1 kW = 1 kJ/s; 4-stroke → cycles/sec = N/(2×60).
- ✅ **Memorize the First Law form**: $dQ = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}VdP + \frac{1}{\gamma-1}PdV - \sum h_i^0 dm_i$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEMUT752 – Advanced IC Engines and Combustion (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, Module 4 deals with **Alternative Fuels for Internal Combustion Engines**, covering gaseous fuels (CNG, LPG, H₂), liquid biofuels (ethanol, methanol, biodiesel), and their performance/emission characteristics. The sub-topic **"Advantages"** refers to the engineering, environmental, and economic merits that these alternative fuels offer when compared to conventional petroleum-based fuels (gasoline and diesel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4 – Alternative Fuels):**
> "Advantages and limitations of alternative fuels such as hydrogen, alcohols, biodiesel, natural gas (CNG), and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) as substitutes for gasoline and diesel in SI and CI engines, with emphasis on combustion behavior, emissions, and renewability."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy (The Medicine Cabinet Analogy):**
Think of a conventional IC engine as a patient who has been taking the same strong medicine (petrol/diesel) for over a century. That medicine works, but it has severe side effects — pollution, global warming, and eventual depletion of supply. **Alternative fuels are like switching to a milder, cleaner, more sustainable medicine** — they may not deliver the same raw power instantly, but they heal the patient (the planet) in the long run.

Imagine three fuel "patients":
- **Hydrogen** = A pure-oxygen inhaler (zero carbon, but highly flammable and expensive to store).
- **Biodiesel** = A herbal remedy (renewable, biodegradable, but with storage issues).
- **CNG/LPG** = A balanced generic medicine (cheaper, cleaner, but requires tank space).

### 1.3 Why Study Advantages?

Understanding the **advantages** of alternative fuels is essential because:
- It forms a direct **2-mark / 3-mark KTU exam question** every year.
- It links to **CO (Course Outcome) 4** of the syllabus: *"Understand alternative fuels and their impact on engine performance and emissions."*
- It is the **first half** of any comparative essay question (Advantages + Limitations = 14 marks).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> Examiners frequently award full marks only if students structure the answer as:
> **Environmental Advantages → Engine Performance Advantages → Economic Advantages → Strategic Advantages.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Classification of Advantages

The advantages of alternative fuels can be systematically classified into **four engineering categories**:

#### A. Environmental Advantages
- **Reduction in CO₂ emissions** due to closed carbon cycle (biofuels) or zero carbon content (hydrogen).
- **Lower CO (Carbon Monoxide)** and **unburnt HC (Hydrocarbons)** emissions due to more complete combustion.
- **Lower particulate matter (PM)** and **smoke** in biodiesel blends.
- **Biodegradability** — biodiesel is non-toxic; spillages do not contaminate soil/water.

#### B. Engine Performance Advantages
- **Higher cetane number** of biodiesel → smoother CI engine cold starting.
- **Higher octane number** of alcohols/hydrogen → allows **higher compression ratio** in SI engines → higher thermal efficiency.
- **Higher flame speed** of hydrogen → closer to ideal constant-volume combustion → **better thermal efficiency**.
- **Lubricity** of biodiesel reduces engine wear.

#### C. Economic & Strategic Advantages
- **Renewable** — derived from agricultural produce (ethanol from sugarcane, biodiesel from soybean/jatropha).
- **Energy security** — reduces dependence on crude oil imports.
- **Rural employment** — creates a domestic agricultural market for feedstock.
- **Price stability** — lesser volatility compared to crude oil.

#### D. Operational Advantages
- **CNG/LPG** have higher auto-ignition temperatures → safer handling.
- **Hydrogen** has the **highest energy-to-mass ratio** of any fuel.
- **Biodiesel** is compatible with existing diesel engines with **minimal modification**.

### 2.2 Comparative Numerical Metrics (Standard Reference Values)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following numerical values are **standard textbook metrics** (Heywood, Pulkrabek) and are essential for KTU numerical/semi-numerical questions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | CNG (CH₄) | Biodiesel | Petrol (Ref.) | Diesel (Ref.) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Molecular Weight** | 2.016 | 32.04 | 46.07 | 16.04 | ~292 | ~114 | ~170 |
| **Calorific Value (MJ/kg)** | 142.0 | 20.1 | 26.9 | 50.0 | 37.0 | 44.0 | 42.5 |
| **Octane Number (RON)** | 130+ | 112 | 108 | 120 | N/A | 92 | N/A |
| **Cetane Number** | <10 | 5 | 8 | 0 | 48–60 | 5–20 | 45–55 |
| **Flame Speed (cm/s)** | 250–300 | 50 | 60 | 40 | N/A | 35 | 30 |
| **Auto-Ignition T (°C)** | 571 | 470 | 420 | 540 | ~330 | 280 | 210 |
| **Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoich.)** | 34.3 | 6.4 | 9.0 | 17.2 | 12.5 | 14.7 | 14.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memorization Trick — "FCOCAD":**
> **F**lame speed, **C**alorific value, **O**ctane number, **C**etane number, **A**uto-ignition temp, **D**ensity.
> Always compare these 6 parameters side-by-side for any fuel comparison answer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Fuel | Real-World Application | Key Advantage Exploited |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CNG** | Delhi public buses (India), Italian city cars | Low CO/HC emissions |
| **LPG** | Auto-rickshaws, domestic vehicles | Easy storage, low cost |
| **Ethanol** | Brazil flex-fuel cars (E25–E100) | Renewable, high octane |
| **Biodiesel** | USA B20 blend in heavy trucks | Lubricity, closed carbon cycle |
| **Hydrogen** | Toyota Mirai, BMW Hydrogen 7 | Zero CO₂ tailpipe emissions |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Quantitative Justification of "Higher Thermal Efficiency" Advantage for Hydrogen

**Why does hydrogen give higher thermal efficiency?** Let us derive this from first principles using the **Air-Standard Otto Cycle**.

For an Otto Cycle, thermal efficiency depends only on the **compression ratio (r)** and the **specific heat ratio (γ)**:

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

Hydrogen has a **high resistance to knock** (Octane Number $\geq$ 130), allowing **higher compression ratio (r)** in SI engines.

**Step 1 — Knocking limit of petrol:**
Octane number of petrol $\approx$ 92. Practical compression ratio $r_{petrol} \approx$ 10. For hydrogen, $r_{H_2} \approx$ 14 (limited by mechanical strength).

**Step 2 — Compute γ for hydrogen-air mixture:**
At 1000 K, average $\gamma_{H_2\text{-air}} \approx$ 1.40 (similar to petrol-air).

**Step 3 — Compute efficiency at $r = 10$ (petrol):**

$$
\eta_{petrol} = 1 - \frac{1}{10^{1.40 - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{10^{0.40}}
$$

Computing $10^{0.40}$:

$$
10^{0.40} = \text{antilog}(0.40) \approx 2.512
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{petrol} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.512} = 1 - 0.3981 = 0.6019
$$

So $\eta_{petrol} \approx$ **60.2 %**.

**Step 4 — Compute efficiency at $r = 14$ (hydrogen):**

$$
\eta_{H_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{14^{0.40}}
$$

Computing $14^{0.40}$:

$$
14^{0.40} = 10^{0.40 \times \log_{10}(14)} = 10^{0.40 \times 1.1461} = 10^{0.4584}
$$

Therefore:

$$
10^{0.4584} \approx 2.874
$$

$$
\eta_{H_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.874} = 1 - 0.3480 = 0.6520
$$

So $\eta_{H_2} \approx$ **65.2 %**.

**Step 5 — Gain in efficiency:**

$$
\Delta \eta = \eta_{H_2} - \eta_{petrol} = 0.6520 - 0.6019 = 0.0501
$$

$$
\Delta \eta \approx \mathbf{5.0\%} \text{ absolute gain in thermal efficiency}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Hydrogen's high octane number permits a **30–40% higher compression ratio**, which translates into a measurable thermodynamic efficiency advantage — a **commonly-tested KTU 2024 derivation.**

---

### 3.2 Quantitative Justification of "Reduced CO Emission" for Alcohol Fuels

**Why do alcohols produce less CO than petrol?**

CO is formed due to **incomplete combustion**, primarily when local air-fuel mixtures are too rich (air-starved).

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio comparison:**

Petrol: $(\text{A/F})_{petrol} = 14.7$
Methanol: $(\text{A/F})_{methanol} = 6.4$

**Step 2 — During cold start, the engine runs rich.** Let us assume actual equivalence ratio $\phi = 1.2$ for cold operation.

**Step 3 — For the same mass of fuel, the actual air supplied is:**

$$
(\text{A/F})_{actual} = (\text{A/F})_{stoich} \times \frac{1}{\phi}
$$

For petrol:

$$
(\text{A/F})_{actual,petrol} = \frac{14.7}{1.2} = 12.25
$$

For methanol:

$$
(\text{A/F})_{actual,methanol} = \frac{6.4}{1.2} = 5.33
$$

**Step 4 — Exhaust oxygen (O₂) availability:**

Lower A/F ratio of alcohol means **less nitrogen dilution** in the cylinder, hence more effective combustion even at rich mixtures → **lower equilibrium CO**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** Alcohols have **high latent heat of vaporization** (methanol = 1100 kJ/kg), which cools the intake charge → **higher volumetric efficiency** + denser air charge → more complete combustion → **less CO**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU-Style Comparative Table

| Advantage Category | Hydrogen | Biodiesel | Ethanol/Methanol | CNG/LPG |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| CO₂ Reduction | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Particulate Reduction | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| NOₓ Impact | High (problematic) | Neutral | Reduced | Slightly High |
| Engine Modification | Major | Minor | Minor (FI) | Major (tank) |
| Renewable | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (fossil) |
| Cost Competitiveness | Low | Medium | High | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
