<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Questions (Module 4)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**Q1. List any three advantages of hydrogen as an alternative fuel for IC engines.**

*Model Answer:*

1. **Zero CO₂ emissions** on combustion (only H₂O is produced), making it the cleanest fuel.
2. **Highest calorific value** among all fuels ($\approx$ 142 MJ/kg), giving very high energy density per unit mass.
3. **High flame speed** ($\approx$ 250–300 cm/s), closer to constant-volume combustion, resulting in higher thermal efficiency.

---

**Q2. State two advantages of biodiesel over conventional diesel.**

*Model Answer:*

1. **Renewable and biodegradable** — produced from vegetable oils/animal fats; spillages do not pollute soil.
2. **Higher cetane number** ($\approx$ 48–60 vs 45–55 for diesel) and **better lubricity**, which reduces engine wear and improves cold-start performance.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **environmental and engine-performance advantages** of using **ethanol and methanol** as alternative fuels in SI engines.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **emission characteristics** of ethanol, methanol, and petrol when used in an SI engine, with suitable justification.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Advantages of Ethanol and Methanol:**

**Environmental Advantages:**
- Both alcohols are **renewable** (produced from biomass fermentation).
- Lower CO₂ emissions due to the **closed carbon cycle** — CO₂ released during combustion is reabsorbed by next-generation crops.
- **Biodegradable** and non-toxic; safe for water/soil in case of spillage.
- Lower **CO and unburned HC** emissions because oxygen content ($\approx$ 35% in methanol, 22% in ethanol) promotes **leaner, more complete combustion**.

**Engine-Performance Advantages:**
- **High octane number** (Methanol $\approx$ 112, Ethanol $\approx$ 108) — allows **higher compression ratio** → higher thermal efficiency and power output.
- **High latent heat of vaporization** (Methanol = 1100 kJ/kg, Ethanol = 904 kJ/kg) → cools intake charge → **higher volumetric efficiency** → higher power.
- **Wider flammability limits** → enables lean burn operation → improved fuel economy.
- **High flame speed** compared to petrol → better combustion stability.

**(b) Emission Comparison:**

| Emission | Petrol | Methanol | Ethanol |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| CO | High (rich zones) | Low (oxygenated) | Low (oxygenated) |
| Unburned HC | High | Low | Low |
| NOₓ | Moderate | Slightly Higher (higher flame T) | Moderate |
| CO₂ (net) | High (fossil) | Low (renewable) | Low (renewable) |
| Aldehydes | Low | High (formaldehyde) | Moderate (acetaldehyde) |

**Justification:**
- Lower CO and HC in alcohols: due to **oxygenate effect** (oxygen in fuel molecule ensures complete combustion).
- Higher NOₓ possible in methanol: due to higher adiabatic flame temperature.
- Aldehydes (toxic) appear more in alcohol combustion — a **disadvantage**.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with reasons **why hydrogen is considered the fuel of the future**. Mention any **four advantages** and **three disadvantages**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hydrogen-fuelled SI engine operates on the Otto cycle with a compression ratio of 14:1. Compare its ideal thermal efficiency with a petrol engine at $r = 10:1$ (assume $\gamma = 1.4$).

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hydrogen as the Fuel of the Future — Advantages:**

1. **Zero carbon emissions** — only water vapour is the combustion product. Eliminates CO, CO₂, and unburned HC.
2. **Highest calorific value** ($\approx$ 142 MJ/kg) — more energy per unit mass than any fossil fuel.
3. **Renewable** — can be produced from electrolysis of water using solar/wind energy (green hydrogen).
4. **High octane number** ($\geq$ 130) — permits very high compression ratios, increasing thermal efficiency.
5. **Wide flammability limits** — enables ultra-lean combustion and HCCI (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition) operation.

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Very low density** — requires bulky storage (cryogenic liquid or high-pressure tanks at 350–700 bar).
2. **High auto-ignition temperature** (571 °C) — difficult to use in CI engines.
3. **Production cost** — green hydrogen is currently 2–4× costlier than petrol equivalent.

**(b) Numerical Comparison:**

**For petrol engine ($r_1 = 10$, $\gamma = 1.4$):**

$$
\eta_1 = 1 - \frac{1}{r_1^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{10^{0.4}}
$$

$$
10^{0.4} \approx 2.512 \Rightarrow \eta_1 = 1 - \frac{1}{2.512} = 0.6019 = \mathbf{60.19\%}
$$

**For hydrogen engine ($r_2 = 14$, $\gamma = 1.4$):**

$$
\eta_2 = 1 - \frac{1}{14^{0.4}}
$$

$$
14^{0.4} = 10^{0.4 \times \log_{10} 14} = 10^{0.4 \times 1.1461} = 10^{0.4584} \approx 2.874
$$

$$
\eta_2 = 1 - \frac{1}{2.874} = 1 - 0.3480 = 0.6520 = \mathbf{65.20\%}
$$

**Gain in efficiency:**

$$
\Delta\eta = \eta_2 - \eta_1 = 65.20\% - 60.19\% = \mathbf{5.01\% \text{ absolute}}
$$

$$
\% \text{Relative Improvement} = \frac{0.0501}{0.6019} \times 100 = \mathbf{8.32\% \text{ relative gain}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not** confuse **relative** gain (8.32 %) with **absolute** gain (5.01 %) — examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> - **Do not** assume $\gamma$ changes with fuel unless the question explicitly states it; for SI engines operating near stoichiometric conditions, $\gamma \approx 1.4$ is standard.
> - **Always** state the units (MJ/kg, °C, cm/s) explicitly when quoting fuel properties.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Alternative fuels offer four categories of advantages:** Environmental, Performance, Economic, and Operational.
- **Hydrogen advantages:** Zero CO₂, highest calorific value (142 MJ/kg), highest flame speed, highest octane number — but very low density and high storage cost.
- **Biodiesel advantages:** Renewable, biodegradable, high cetane number, high lubricity — minor engine modification needed.
- **Alcohol advantages:** High octane, oxygenated (cleaner CO/HC emissions), high latent heat (volumetric efficiency) — low energy density.
- **CNG/LPG advantages:** Cheaper than petrol/diesel, lower CO/HC, domestic availability — bulky tanks, power loss.
- **Quantitative Derivation:** Hydrogen's high octane allows $r \approx 14$ vs petrol's $r \approx 10$, giving ~**5% absolute** thermal efficiency gain.
- **Key Formula:** $\eta_{otto} = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$
- **The "FCOCAD" mnemonic** for fuel property comparison: Flame speed, Calorific value, Octane number, Cetane number, Auto-ignition temp, Density.
- **Always structure advantages answer** in the order: Environmental → Performance → Economic → Operational, as per KTU valuation key.
- **Avoid confusing absolute vs relative % gains** in efficiency calculations.
- **Net CO₂ = 0** only for biofuels (closed carbon cycle) and hydrogen — fossil-derived CNG/LPG only reduce emissions, do not eliminate them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HCCI and CAI Engines - Fundamentals - External and Internal Mixture Preparation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HCCI and CAI Engines - Fundamentals - External and Internal Mixture Preparation Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition (HCCI)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
HCCI is a **premixed, low-temperature combustion concept** in which a *homogeneous* fuel–air mixture is prepared prior to (or early in) the compression stroke, and ignition is achieved by **auto-ignition** of the bulk charge through compression heating alone — *without* an external ignition source (spark) or diffusion flame (diesel injection). Combustion therefore occurs as a *distributed, simultaneous multi-point auto-ignition* event governed by **chemical kinetics**, rather than by flame propagation (SI) or spray-controlled mixing (CI).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Board Definition Cue:** HCCI = **SI mixture** (premixed, homogeneous) + **CI ignition** (compression auto-ignition). This hybrid identity is the single most important framing line for a 14-mark question.

### 1.2 Controlled Auto-Ignition (CAI)

**Formal Definition:**
CAI is a **subset / operational variant of HCCI** in which auto-ignition of a premixed dilute charge is achieved using **internally trapped, high-temperature residual exhaust gases** rather than fresh charge alone. CAI is realised through **Negative Valve Overlap (NVO)** — both intake and exhaust valves are closed for a portion of the crank angle around TDC, trapping hot residuals that raise the effective in-cylinder temperature and thereby control auto-ignition timing.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Syllabus Highlight:** CAI is sometimes called **HCCI with internal EGR**. The "control" in CAI comes from actively varying the **amount and temperature of trapped residuals** to dictate ignition timing.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Popcorn vs. Matchstick" Intuition

Think of an SI engine as lighting a *single match* (spark) inside a room full of paper (air-fuel mix) — the flame then **propagates** outward.  
Think of a CI engine as spraying an *oil-soaked rag* onto a hot plate — the fuel burns as it **mixes** with hot air at the spray tip.  
Now think of **HCCI/CAI** as **simultaneously igniting every kernel of corn in a popcorn maker** — each kernel pops on its own when the whole room reaches the auto-ignition temperature. There is no flame front to travel; chemistry, not flame propagation, governs the rate.

- **Controlled room temperature** → controlled pop rate (analogous to combustion phasing).
- **Very dilute fuel-air mix** → low peak temperatures → low **NOx** (< **50 ppm**) and near-zero **PM**.
- **Premixed charge** → no rich diffusion zones → no soot.

> [!TIP]  
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** *"HCCI = Homogeneous + Compressed + Chemically-ignited"* — and *CAI = Controlled + Auto-Ignition (via hot residuals).*

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (used throughout KTU evaluations)

| Metric | Typical Value (HCCI/CAI) | SI Baseline | CI Baseline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equivalence Ratio ($\phi$) | **0.3 – 0.6** | 1.0 | 0.7 – 1.2 |
| EGR / Residual fraction | **30 – 60 %** | < 10 % | 5 – 25 % |
| Peak in-cylinder T | **< 1800 K** | ~2500 K | ~2500 K |
| NOx (raw, ppm) | **< 50** | 500 – 1500 | 800 – 2000 |
| PM (BSFC basis) | **< 0.01 g/kWh** | 0.01 | 0.05 – 0.20 |
| Combustion mode | Chemical kinetics | Flame propagation | Diffusion flame |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Charge Preparation:** A highly *dilute*, well-mixed fuel + air + (EGR/residuals) charge is created.
2. **Compression:** The homogeneous charge is compressed; temperature rises uniformly throughout the volume.
3. **Auto-Ignition Trigger:** When the bulk temperature exceeds the **auto-ignition temperature** of the local fuel-oxidizer-EGR mixture, *multiple* ignition kernels form simultaneously.
4. **Distributed Combustion:** Heat release occurs as a **distributed volumetric reaction**, governed by low-temperature (cool-flame) and high-temperature chemistry.
5. **Phasing Control:** Combustion timing is shifted earlier/later by manipulating:
   - Effective in-cylinder temperature
   - EGR / residual gas mass
   - Compression ratio
   - Fuel reactivity (cetane number, dual-fuel strategies)
6. **Expansion & Exhaust:** Combustion products expand, exhaust valve opens — some residuals are *trapped* (in CAI) for the next cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The "Why" — Why HCCI/CAI?**  
> Simultaneous, distributed combustion means **no localised peak temperature zones** → **NOx falls by 80–95 %** vs SI/CI. Because the mix is **homogeneous and lean**, **PM is essentially eliminated**. Net effect: thermal efficiency comparable to diesel, emissions comparable to (or better than) a catalyst-equipped SI engine.

### 2.2 The Two Mixture-Preparation Pathways (Core Syllabus Topic)

#### A. External Mixture Preparation (EMP)

Fuel is **mixed with the incoming air *before* the inlet valve** — i.e., in the intake port, manifold, or upstream of the cylinder.

- **Mechanism:** Port Fuel Injection (PFI), carburetion-like, or early-cycle Direct Injection.
- **Time Available for Mixing:** Full intake stroke + early compression — typically **0.5 s to 1.0 s equivalent** mixing time.
- **Homogeneity:** Excellent at light/medium loads (low fuel quantity).
- **Problems at High Load:**
  - Excessive in-cylinder mass → over-rapid, high-pressure-rise-rate (PRR > **10 bar/deg**) combustion.
  - Wall wetting (PFI) → UHC and cold-start issues.
  - Knock-like ringing becomes severe.
- **Where Used:** Classic PFI-HCCI engines (e.g., Honda HCCI 2-stroke prototypes, GM's early HCCI demo).

#### B. Internal Mixture Preparation (IMP)

Fuel is **injected directly into the combustion chamber** at a controlled crank-angle moment during the compression stroke.

- **Mechanism:** High-pressure common-rail or piezo injectors (DI), with precise timing (e.g., 60°–120° BTDC).
- **Mixing Time Available:** Short — only the portion of compression stroke *after* injection (often **< 50 ms**).
- **Strategies:**
  - **Premixed DI-HCCI:** Early injection (early compression) → still homogeneous.
  - **Stratified-Charge HCCI:** Late injection → partial stratification, used to extend load range.
- **Advantages:** Better high-load control, no wall wetting, can produce stratified stratification for knock mitigation.
- **Disadvantages:** Need for very high-pressure injectors; risk of smoke at high loads if too rich locally.

> [!TIP]  
> **Board Exam One-Liner:**  
> **EMP → high homogeneity, low-load friendly.**  
> **IMP → controllable timing, high-load friendly.**  
> Modern engines often blend both: *early DI (IMP) into a hot residual charge (CAI strategy)*.

### 2.3 The CAI Mechanism — Negative Valve Overlap (NVO)

| Event | Valve State | Cylinder State |
|---|---|---|
| Exhaust stroke begins | Exhaust valve closes early | Hot residuals trapped |
| End of exhaust / start of intake | Both valves closed (NVO) | Residuals + fresh charge mix, temp rises |
| Intake stroke | Intake valve opens (delayed) | Cool fresh air enters, mixes with residuals |
| Compression stroke | Both valves closed | Auto-ignition near TDC |

The **trapped residual mass fraction** $f_{res}$ is the primary control knob:

$$f_{res} = \frac{m_{res}}{m_{cyl}} \quad \text{(typical: 0.20 – 0.50)}$$

Increasing $f_{res}$ → higher effective $T$ → **earlier** auto-ignition.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all rows below** — KTU board answer books may also use the conditional `\mid` notation.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = (F/A)_{actual} / (F/A)_{stoich}$ | Equivalence ratio (lean: $\phi < 1$) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\lambda = 1/\phi$ | Excess air ratio (lean: $\lambda > 1$) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $f_{res} = m_{res}/m_{cyl}$ | Residual mass fraction (CAI control) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T_{cyl,eff} = (1-f_{res})T_{fresh} + f_{res}T_{res}$ | Effective bulk temperature | K |
| 5 | $\tau_{ign} = A \exp(E_a/RT) [O_2]^{-n}[Fuel]^{-m}$ | Ignition delay (Arrhenius kinetics) | s |
| 6 | $PRR = (1/\gamma) \cdot (dP/d\theta)$ | Pressure rise rate (limit: **< 10 bar/°CA**) | bar/°CA |
| 7 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2}$ | Otto-cycle thermal efficiency (ideal HCCI reference) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $NO_x \propto \exp(-E_{NO_x}/RT_{peak})$ | Zeldovich thermal NOx (suppressed when $T_{peak}<1800$ K) | ppm |
| 9 | $\rho_{mix} = \frac{P}{R_{mix} T}$ | Charge density (for HCCI load limit) | kg/m³ |
| 10 | $\omega_{b} = \frac{dQ}{d\theta}$ / $Q_{total}$ | Mass fraction burned per °CA (kinetic-controlled) | 1/°CA |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of HCCI/CAI

- **Automotive:** Used as a low-load efficiency mode in hybrid powertrains (e.g., Mercedes-Benz DiesOtto concept, Mazda SkyActiv-X Spark Controlled Compression Ignition — SPCCI).
- **Stationary Power:** Gensets running on natural gas — lean-burn HCCI gives > **42 %** thermal efficiency with near-zero emissions.
- **Hydrogen / Renewable Fuels:** HCCI is particularly suited to low-reactivity fuels (methanol, ethanol, H₂) where SI has knock issues and CI has auto-ignition problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Effective In-Cylinder Temperature for CAI

The bulk gas inside a CAI cylinder is a mixture of **fresh charge** (cool intake air + fuel) and **trapped residuals** (hot exhaust gases retained by NVO). We derive the effective temperature that controls auto-ignition timing.

**Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Closed Volume During NVO.**

Assume no work is done during the very short NVO interval (crank-angle variation is negligible at TDC region). The internal energy is conserved:

$$U_{mix} = U_{fresh} + U_{res}$$

**Step 2 — Expand in terms of mass, specific heat, and temperature.**

For an ideal gas with constant specific heats:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{mix} \, c_v \, T_{eff} &= m_{fresh} \, c_v \, T_{fresh} + m_{res} \, c_v \, T_{res}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by the mixture mass** $m_{mix} = m_{fresh} + m_{res}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= \frac{m_{fresh}}{m_{mix}} T_{fresh} + \frac{m_{res}}{m_{mix}} T_{res} \\
T_{eff} &= (1 - f_{res}) \, T_{fresh} + f_{res} \, T_{res}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** This is Formula (4) in the cheat sheet.  
**Interpretation:** Each 1 % increase in $f_{res}$ shifts $T_{eff}$ by $(T_{res}-T_{fresh})/100$. Typical values: $T_{fresh} \approx 320$ K, $T_{res} \approx 1100$ K, so a shift of **~8 K per 1 % residual increase**. Hence, *a small NVO adjustment yields a large timing lever*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Ignition Delay from Arrhenius Two-Step Model

The HCCI auto-ignition is governed by chemical kinetics. The simplest engineering model treats the **low-temperature (cool-flame) induction period** as a single-step Arrhenius reaction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{ign} &= A \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R \, T_{eff}}\right) [O_2]^{-n}[Fuel]^{-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation walk-through:**

**Step 1 — Define the rate constant** for the dominant ignition step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k(T) &= A \exp\!\left(\frac{-E_a}{R T_{eff}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The minus sign inside the exponential — note the **positive** $E_a/RT$ appears *only after inversion* when we solve for time-to-ignition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{ign} &\propto \frac{1}{k} = \frac{1}{A}\exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R T_{eff}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Include oxidizer and fuel concentration dependence** (order $-n$ and $-m$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{ign} &= A \, \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R \, T_{eff}}\right) [O_2]^{-n}[Fuel]^{-m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert concentrations to in-cylinder partial pressures** using $P_i = c_i R T_{eff}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[O_2] &= \frac{y_{O_2}\, P}{R\, T_{eff}} \\
[Fuel] &= \frac{y_{Fuel}\, P}{R\, T_{eff}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{ign} &= A \left(\frac{R\, T_{eff}}{y_{O_2}\, P}\right)^{n} \left(\frac{R\, T_{eff}}{y_{Fuel}\, P}\right)^{m} \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R\, T_{eff}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**  
- Higher $T_{eff}$ (more residuals, less EGR cooling) → **shorter** $\tau_{ign}$ → earlier auto-ignition.  
- Higher dilution ($y_{O_2} \downarrow$) → **longer** $\tau_{ign}$ → later auto-ignition.  
- This is the **fundamental control lever**: $T_{eff}$ controls CA50 (crank angle of 50 % mass burned).

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Load Limit of HCCI via Charge Density

HCCI has a **high-load limit** set by excessive pressure-rise rate. The mass of fuel burned per cycle (and hence the load) is limited by the homogeneous mixture density at ignition:

**Step 1 — Charge density at ignition (TDC for HCCI):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{TDC} &= \frac{P_{TDC}}{R_{mix}\, T_{TDC}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — In-cylinder pressure at TDC using isentropic compression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{TDC} &= P_1 \, r^{\gamma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Temperature at TDC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{TDC} &= T_1 \, r^{\gamma-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Density at TDC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{TDC} &= \frac{P_1 \, r^{\gamma}}{R_{mix} \, T_1 \, r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{P_1 \, r}{R_{mix} \, T_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalence ratio to delivered load** (fuel mass per cycle per unit displacement):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{fuel} &= \rho_{TDC} \cdot V_d \cdot \phi \cdot \left(\frac{F}{A}\right)_{stoich}
\end{aligned}
$$

For natural gas–air: $(F/A)_{stoich} = 1/17.2$ kg/kg.  
For gasoline–air: $(F/A)_{stoich} \approx 1/14.7$ kg/kg.

**Practical Limit:** Above $m_{fuel} \approx 30\text{–}40$ mg/cycle/litre, PRR exceeds **10 bar/°CA** and the engine becomes unacceptably noisy — this defines the **upper HCCI load limit**. To extend load range, *partial stratification (DI-HCCI)* is used.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Effect on $\tau_{ign}$ | Effect on $T_{peak}$ | Effect on NOx | Effect on PRR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $f_{res}$ (CAI) | ↓ Shortens | Slight ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Increase EGR (cool) | ↑ Lengthens | ↓ Lowers | ↓ ↓ | ↓ |
| Increase CR | ↑ Compresses more, $T_{eff} \uparrow$ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Increase $\phi$ | ↓ | ↑ Strongly | ↑↑ | ↑↑↑ |
| Increase fuel reactivity (cetane) | ↓ | — | — | ↑ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — HCCI/CAI Engine Operating System

This diagram maps the **control and energy flow architecture** of an HCCI/CAI powertrain — suitable as a substitute for a physical cylinder drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Fuel Supply System] --> B[Mixture Preparation Module]
    A1[Intake Air Path] --> B
    A2[Recirculated / Trapped Exhaust Residuals] --> B
    B --> C{Strategy Selector}
    C -- External Prep --> D1[Port Fuel Injection Manifold]
    C -- Internal Prep --> D2[Direct Injection Common Rail]
    C -- CAI Mode --> D3[Negative Valve Overlap Controller]
    D1 --> E[Cylinder - Homogeneous Charge]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Auto-Ignition Event - Distributed]
    F --> G[Combustion Heat Release - Kinetic Controlled]
    G --> H[Exhaust Valve - Residual Trapping for CAI]
    H --> A2
    G --> I[Work Extraction Piston]
    I --> J[Crankshaft Output]
    E --> K[Combustion Phasing Sensor - In-Cylinder Pressure]
    K --> L[ECU Closed-Loop Controller]
    L --> C
    L --> A
    L --> D3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The three dashed paths into the cylinder (D1, D2, D3) correspond to the **three mixture-preparation regimes** covered in this module.
- The closed-loop arrow (L → C) reflects the *phasing control problem* — the dominant KTU board topic.

---

### 4.2 Process Flow — Crank-Angle Timeline of a CAI Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Intake Stroke: Cool Fresh Air Enters] --> P2[End of Stroke: Exhaust Valve Closes Early]
    P2 --> P3[Negative Valve Overlap: Both Valves Closed]
    P3 --> P4[Hot Residuals Mix with Fresh Charge]
    P4 --> P5[Effective Temperature Rises]
    P5 --> P6[Compression Stroke Begins]
    P6 --> P7[Intake Valve Opens - Late Intake]
    P7 --> P8[Compression: Auto-Ignition Near TDC]
    P8 --> P9[Distributed Combustion - HCCI Mode]
    P9 --> P10[Expansion - Work Extraction]
    P10 --> P11[Exhaust Stroke: Residuals Trapped for Next Cycle]
    P11 --> P1
```

---

### 4.3 Mixture-Preparation Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EMP[External Mixture Preparation]
        E1[Liquid Fuel in Port] --> E2[Fuel Vaporisation in Intake Manifold]
        E2 --> E3[Long Mixing Time 0.5 to 1.0 s]
        E3 --> E4[Highly Homogeneous Charge]
        E4 --> E5[Enters Cylinder via Intake Valve]
    end
    subgraph IMP[Internal Mixture Preparation]
        I1[High-Pressure Common Rail] --> I2[Direct Injection into Cylinder]
        I2 --> I3[Short Mixing Time 5 to 50 ms]
        I3 --> I4{Injection Timing}
        I4 -- Early Injection --> I5[Premixed HCCI]
        I4 -- Late Injection --> I6[Stratified HCCI - Extended Load Range]
    end
    E5 --> CYL[Shared Cylinder Auto-Ignition]
    I5 --> CYL
    I6 --> CYL
    CYL --> OUT[Distributed Combustion Event]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Distinguish between External and Internal Mixture Preparation in the context of HCCI engines. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **External Mixture Preparation (EMP):** Fuel is mixed with incoming air *upstream* of the cylinder — typically in the intake port via PFI. Mixing time is long (≈ 0.5–1.0 s), producing excellent charge homogeneity. Suited to light/medium HCCI loads; suffers from wall-wetting and UHC issues at high loads.
- **Internal Mixture Preparation (IMP):** Fuel is injected *directly* into the cylinder at a controlled crank angle during compression. Mixing time is short (≈ 5–50 ms). Early injection yields a premixed HCCI charge; late injection produces stratified-charge HCCI, extending the high-load operating range.
- **Key Difference:** EMP delivers homogeneity at the cost of mixing-time flexibility; IMP delivers mixing-time flexibility at the cost of homogeneity.

---

**Q2. Define CAI. How does it differ from HCCI in terms of mixture preparation? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **CAI (Controlled Auto-Ignition):** A variant of HCCI in which auto-ignition of a dilute premixed charge is achieved using *internally trapped, high-temperature residual exhaust gases* retained by **Negative Valve Overlap (NVO)**.
- **Difference from HCCI (Classical):** Conventional HCCI relies primarily on *external EGR* (cool, recycled exhaust) to dilute and warm the charge; CAI relies on *internal hot residuals* (≈ 1000–1100 K) to do the same with **better thermal control** and **no external EGR hardware**.
- **Mixture Preparation Implication:** In CAI, the *trapped residual mass* $f_{res}$ replaces the EGR cooler as the primary control knob, enabling faster, in-cycle adjustment of auto-ignition timing.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module-level choice. The examiner offers two questions; the student attempts one. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the principle of operation of an HCCI engine. Discuss how combustion phasing is controlled and identify the principal operating limits.

**(b) [7 marks]** A four-stroke HCCI engine operates with the following data:
- Compression ratio $r = 14$
- Fresh charge temperature $T_{fresh} = 330$ K
- Residual temperature $T_{res} = 1050$ K
- Residual mass fraction $f_{res} = 0.35$
- $\gamma = 1.35$, $R_{mix} = 287$ J/kg·K, $P_1 = 100$ kPa
- Stoichiometric A/F = 14.7 (gasoline); $\phi = 0.4$ (lean HCCI)

Calculate the **(i)** effective bulk temperature $T_{eff}$, **(ii)** in-cylinder density at TDC, and **(iii)** fuel mass per litre of displacement per cycle. Comment on whether the load is within the typical HCCI limit.

---

**Model Solution — Part A(a):**

1. **Schematic:** Cylinder with piston, homogeneous fuel-air-EGR charge, no spark plug or injector nozzle tip diffusion flame. Auto-ignition occurs simultaneously at multiple sites near TDC, leading to distributed heat release.
2. **Operating Principle:** Premixed, dilute charge; compression heating; auto-ignition when bulk $T$ > auto-ignition $T$; distributed combustion governed by chemical kinetics (no flame propagation).
3. **Phasing Control:** Combustion timing (CA50) is controlled by: (i) effective bulk temperature (via EGR or $f_{res}$), (ii) compression ratio, (iii) fuel reactivity (cetane/RON), (iv) intake temperature / pressure.
4. **Operating Limits:**
   - **Low Load:** Misfire due to too-dilute / too-cool charge.
   - **High Load:** Excessive PRR (> 10 bar/°CA), engine knock/rattle; limit typically **30–40 mg/cycle/litre**.
   - **Cold Start:** Premixed charge cannot auto-ignite reliably below ≈ 350 K wall temperature.

---

**Model Solution — Part A(b):**

**(i) Effective Bulk Temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff} &= (1 - f_{res}) \, T_{fresh} + f_{res} \, T_{res} \\
&= (1 - 0.35)(330) + (0.35)(1050) \\
&= 0.65 \times 330 + 0.35 \times 1050 \\
&= 214.5 + 367.5 \\
&= 582 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Density at TDC:**

Step 1 — Pressure at TDC:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{TDC} &= P_1 \, r^{\gamma} = 100 \times 14^{1.35} \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $14^{1.35}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(14^{1.35}) &= 1.35 \times \ln(14) = 1.35 \times 2.6391 = 3.5628 \\
14^{1.35} &= e^{3.5628} = 35.27
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{TDC} &= 100 \times 35.27 = 3527 \text{ kPa} = 3.527 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Temperature at TDC:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{TDC} &= T_1 \, r^{\gamma-1} = 330 \times 14^{0.35} \\
\ln(14^{0.35}) &= 0.35 \times 2.6391 = 0.9237 \\
14^{0.35} &= e^{0.9237} = 2.518 \\
T_{TDC} &= 330 \times 2.518 = 831 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Density:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{TDC} &= \frac{P_{TDC}}{R_{mix} \, T_{TDC}} = \frac{3{,}527{,}000}{287 \times 831} \\
&= \frac{3{,}527{,}000}{238{,}497} \\
&= 14.79 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Fuel Mass per Cycle per Litre:**

Using $\phi = (F/A)/((F/A)_{stoich})$ → $F/A = \phi \times (1/14.7) = 0.4/14.7 = 0.02721$ kg fuel / kg air.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{air} &= \rho_{TDC} \cdot V_{disp} = 14.79 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg per litre} \\
&= 0.01479 \text{ kg air/litre/cycle} \\
m_{fuel} &= m_{air} \times (F/A) \\
&= 0.01479 \times 0.02721 \\
&= 4.025 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/litre/cycle} \\
&= 40.25 \text{ mg/litre/cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** This fuel mass (≈ 40 mg/L/cycle) is at the **upper edge** of the practical HCCI limit (30–40 mg/L/cycle). At this point, PRR will likely exceed 10 bar/°CA, so **partial stratification (DI-HCCI) would be needed** to extend the load range safely.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a clear schematic, describe the working of a CAI engine using Negative Valve Overlap. Explain how $f_{res}$ controls ignition timing.

**(b) [7 marks]** A CAI engine is operated with $T_{fresh} = 340$ K, $T_{res} = 1100$ K. The driver increases the residual fraction from 0.20 to 0.45 to advance combustion timing. Calculate the change in effective bulk temperature and, using the Arrhenius approximation $E_a/R = 18{,}000$ K, estimate the *ratio* of ignition delays $\tau_{ign,2}/\tau_{ign,1}$ between the two operating points. State your conclusion about combustion-phasing sensitivity.

---

**Model Solution — Part B(a):**

1. **Schematic:** Crank-angle diagram showing four strokes with exhaust valve closing early (≈ 30° BTDC of intake stroke), creating a *Negative Valve Overlap* (NVO) window. Hot residuals mix with fresh charge during NVO; effective temperature rises.
2. **Working:** Hot residuals trapped by NVO raise $T_{eff}$ → earlier auto-ignition near TDC.
3. **Control Knob — $f_{res}$:** Increasing $f_{res}$ (e.g., by closing exhaust valve earlier or delaying intake opening) increases $T_{eff}$ linearly; $T_{eff} = (1-f_{res})T_{fresh} + f_{res}T_{res}$.
4. **Actuation:** Variable Valve Timing (VVT) or cam-phasing controls the *EVO timing* and *IVO timing* independently.

---

**Model Solution — Part B(b):**

**(i) Effective Bulk Temperature at Two Points:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{eff,1} &= (1 - 0.20)(340) + (0.20)(1100) = 0.80 \times 340 + 0.20 \times 1100 = 272 + 220 = 492 \text{ K} \\
T_{eff,2} &= (1 - 0.45)(340) + (0.45)(1100) = 0.55 \times 340 + 0.45 \times 1100 = 187 + 495 = 682 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Change:** $\Delta T_{eff} = 682 - 492 = \mathbf{190 \text{ K}}$.

**(ii) Ignition Delay Ratio:**

Using the Arrhenius form (dominant term only):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\tau_{ign,2}}{\tau_{ign,1}} &= \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{eff,2}} - \frac{1}{T_{eff,1}}\right)\right] \\
\frac{1}{T_{eff,2}} - \frac{1}{T_{eff,1}} &= \frac{1}{682} - \frac{1}{492} = 0.001467 - 0.002033 = -0.000566 \text{ K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\tau_{ign,2}}{\tau_{ign,1}} &= \exp\!\left[18{,}000 \times (-0.000566)\right] \\
&= \exp(-10.18) \\
&= 3.76 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 190 K rise in $T_{eff}$ (achieved simply by increasing $f_{res}$ from 0.20 → 0.45) **reduces ignition delay by a factor of ≈ 26,600**. This is *enormous* sensitivity and explains the central engineering challenge of CAI: **combustion phasing is hyper-sensitive to small thermal perturbations**, demanding **fast closed-loop control** (typically < 1 cycle response time).

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where students lose marks — recurring pitfalls (KTU 2024 valuation patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing HCCI with "diesel + EGR" or "SI without spark."** State the **hybrid identity explicitly**: SI mixture + CI ignition.
> 2. **Mixing up EMP and IMP.** EMP = port; IMP = direct-injection into cylinder. Do not interchange.
> 3. **Forgetting the CAI = NVO** connection — the *Negative Valve Overlap* is the defining mechanism, not just "hot EGR."
> 4. **In numericals, omitting unit conversions** — $P_1 = 100$ kPa must be converted to **Pa** when using $R$ in J/kg·K; failure to do so loses **2 marks** typically.
> 5. **Not commenting on the result** — KTU examiners *expect* a concluding remark (within HCCI limit? sensible? physically reasonable?). Pure calculation without a "comment" line loses **1 mark**.
> 6. **Spelling/terminology errors:** *auto-ignition* (one word, hyphenated) vs *auto ignition* — use the KTU textbook hyphenation.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **HCCI** = *premixed (SI-style) charge + compression auto-ignition (CI-style).*  
- **CAI** = *HCCI variant using trapped hot residuals via Negative Valve Overlap.*  
- **EMP** → PFI → high homogeneity → low-load ideal → wall-wetting & UHC at high load.  
- **IMP** → DI into cylinder → controllable timing → stratified-charge extends high load.  
- **Key Control Knob:** Effective bulk temperature $T_{eff} = (1-f_{res})T_{fresh} + f_{res}T_{res}$.  
- **Ignition Delay:** $\tau_{ign} \propto \exp(E_a / R T_{eff})$ — **exponentially sensitive** to $T_{eff}$.  
- **NOx** suppressed because $T_{peak} < 1800$ K (Zeldovich mechanism quenched).  
- **PM** near-zero due to absence of rich diffusion zones.  
- **Operating Limits:** Misfire at low load; pressure-rise-rate (PRR > 10 bar/°CA) and ringing at high load.  
- **High-Load Extension:** Partial stratification (late DI), dual-fuel reactivity control, hot residuals.  
- **Examiner Triggers:** Always draw a crank-angle / NVO schematic for CAI; always show both $T_{fresh}$ and $T_{res}$ in $T_{eff}$ numericals; always state PRR limit explicitly.  
- **Mnemonic:** *"**H**omogeneous **C**harge **C**ompression **I**gnition — **C**ool but **C**hemically **C**ontrolled."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Use of Exhaust Gas Dilution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of Use of Exhaust Gas Dilution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Exhaust Gas Dilution (EGD)**, more commonly referred to in industry as **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)**, is an advanced emission-control and combustion-modification technique in which a precisely metered fraction of the inert, thermally equilibrated exhaust gases from an internal combustion (IC) engine is intentionally reintroduced (recirculated) into the fresh intake charge — either the induction air stream, the intake manifold, or directly into the combustion chamber.

Mathematically, the dilution/re-circulation ratio is defined as:

$$\text{EGR Ratio (\%)} = \frac{\dot{m}_{EGR}}{\dot{m}_{air} + \dot{m}_{fuel}} \times 100$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_{EGR}$ = mass flow rate of recirculated exhaust gas (kg/s)
- $\dot{m}_{air}$ = mass flow rate of fresh intake air (kg/s)
- $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ = mass flow rate of fuel (kg/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** EGR is examined under the umbrella of "Combustion Chamber Modifications and Emission Control Strategies." Examiners expect students to correlate the **physical chemistry of dilution** (inert gas effect, specific heat, adiabatic flame temperature) with the resulting **emission, performance, and combustion characteristics**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bath-Tub Cap" Intuition

Imagine a small enclosed room with a powerful gas-fired heater (this is your **combustion chamber**). The temperature inside the room represents the **adiabatic flame temperature**. If you introduce fresh oxygen (pure air), the heater burns fiercely and the room becomes very hot (high $\text{T}_{ad}$ → high **NOx**).

Now, suppose you slightly crack open a window and let some already-burnt, cool, inert gas (exhaust) seep back in. Three things happen immediately:

1. The room is now filled with a **mixture of fresh air AND spent gas**, so the heater has less "pure fuel" to react with → the temperature drops.
2. The inert gas acts as a **thermal sponge** because $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ have very high specific heats → they absorb heat without getting chemically involved.
3. Because peak temperature falls, the **thermal-NOx** (Zeldovich) mechanism, which is exponentially sensitive to temperature, is **drastically suppressed**.

**That is exhaust gas dilution in one sentence:** *Replacing a portion of fresh intake air with hot, inert exhaust gas to lower peak combustion temperatures and reshape the heat-release profile, primarily to control NOx and secondarily to influence performance.*

> [!NOTE]
> The exhaust gas is not chemically inert in the strict sense — it contains $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{N}_2$, residual $\text{O}_2$, CO, and unburnt HC. However, for thermodynamic analysis it is treated as an **inert diluent** with a very high effective specific heat.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

The following reference values are used throughout the KTU valuation of EGR-related problems:

| Parameter | Typical Value | Symbol | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight of dry exhaust gas | **28.97 – 30.5** | $M_{exh}$ | kg/kmol |
| Specific heat of $\text{CO}_2$ at 1000 K | **1.13** | $C_{p,CO_2}$ | kJ/kg·K |
| Specific heat of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ at 1000 K | **2.08** | $C_{p,H_2O}$ | kJ/kg·K |
| Specific heat of $\text{N}_2$ at 1000 K | **1.09** | $C_{p,N_2}$ | kJ/kg·K |
| Specific heat of air at 1000 K | **1.10** | $C_{p,air}$ | kJ/kg·K |
| Density of exhaust gas at 800 K | **~0.55** | $\rho_{exh}$ | kg/m³ |
| Typical EGR rate (diesel) | **5 – 35** | $r_{EGR}$ | % |
| Typical EGR rate (SI gasoline) | **5 – 25** | $r_{EGR}$ | % |
| Adiabatic flame temperature (no EGR) | **~2400 K** | $T_{ad}$ | K |
| Activation energy for Zeldovich NOx | **~270 – 320 kJ/mol** | $E_a$ | kJ/mol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the specific heat of **water vapour ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) is nearly twice that of nitrogen**. Because exhaust gas is rich in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ and $\text{CO}_2$, the *effective* $C_p$ of the working charge increases by **15 – 30 %** with EGR. This is the **principal thermodynamic reason** for the temperature drop.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How EGR Works in an Engine

The introduction of exhaust gas into the cylinder modifies the **state, composition, and thermodynamic properties** of the working fluid in a cascading chain of effects. The mechanism is best understood in six discrete steps:

### Step 1 — Compositional Modification
The exhaust gas typically contains (by volume):
- $\text{N}_2$: **65 – 75 %**
- $\text{CO}_2$: **8 – 14 %**
- $\text{H}_2\text{O}$: **8 – 12 %**
- $\text{O}_2$: **2 – 10 %** (residual)
- CO, HC, soot: trace

When mixed with fresh air, the resulting charge has a **lower oxygen mole fraction** and a higher concentration of tri-atomic gases.

### Step 2 — Effective Reduction of Oxygen Partial Pressure
The partial pressure of oxygen in the inducted charge becomes:

$$p_{O_2,\,eff} = p_{O_2,\,air} \times (1 - r_{EGR}) + p_{O_2,\,exh} \times r_{EGR}$$

For a typical diesel with 30 % EGR and exhaust $\text{O}_2$ ≈ 5 %, the effective $\text{O}_2$ drops from **21 % → ~16 %**. This reduces both flame speed and peak temperature.

### Step 3 — Specific Heat Increase (The Core Mechanism)
The molar (or mass) specific heat of the inducted charge is a weighted average:

$$C_{p,\,charge} = (1 - r_{EGR}) \cdot C_{p,\,air} + r_{EGR} \cdot C_{p,\,exh}$$

Since $C_{p,\,exh} > C_{p,\,air}$ (because of $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$), the charge has a **larger heat sink per unit mass** for the same energy release.

### Step 4 — Suppression of Adiabatic Flame Temperature
Using the first law for adiabatic combustion:

$$T_{ad} = T_{in} + \frac{Q_{comb}}{C_{p,\,products} \times m_{charge}}$$

A higher $C_{p,\,products}$ directly produces a **lower** $T_{ad}$. The classic empirical correlation used by Heywood and adopted in KTU textbooks is:

$$T_{ad} \approx T_{in} \left(1 + \frac{\gamma_{eff} - 1}{\gamma_{eff}} \cdot \frac{Q_R}{C_{p,\,eff} \cdot T_{in}} \right)^{-1}$$

where $\gamma_{eff}$ and $C_{p,\,eff}$ are the *effective* specific-heat ratio and specific heat of the diluted charge.

### Step 5 — Disruption of the Zeldovich NOx Pathway
The dominant thermal NOx mechanism follows extended Zeldovich kinetics:

$$\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R_u T} \cdot [\text{O}_2]^{0.5} \cdot [\text{N}_2]$$

Because $E_a \approx 300$ kJ/mol, a **temperature reduction of just 100 K** can cut NOx by **50 – 70 %**. EGR is therefore one of the most cost-effective NOx control technologies available.

### Step 6 — Altered Combustion Phasing
EGR:
- **Increases ignition delay** in CI engines (because of lower $\text{O}_2$ partial pressure and higher $C_p$).
- **Reduces peak heat release rate (PHRR)** in SI engines (because of slower laminar flame speed).
- **Lowers peak cylinder pressure** $P_{max}$ and **mass-averaged peak temperature** $T_{max}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates every equation a student is likely to require for an EGR-related problem in the KTU ESE or internal assessment.

| # | Equation / Expression | Description / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_{EGR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{EGR}}{\dot{m}_{air} + \dot{m}_{fuel}} \times 100$ | EGR ratio definition | % |
| 2 | $C_{p,\,charge} = (1 - r_{EGR}) C_{p,\,air} + r_{EGR} C_{p,\,exh}$ | Weighted specific heat of charge | kJ/kg·K |
| 3 | $T_{ad} = T_{in} + \dfrac{Q_{comb}}{m_{charge} \cdot C_{p,\,prod}}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature (1st law) | K |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R_u T} [\text{O}_2]^{0.5} [\text{N}_2]$ | Zeldovich thermal NOx rate | mol/m³·s |
| 5 | $\text{NOx}_{reduction} \approx 1 - e^{-\alpha (1 - T_{ad,EGR}/T_{ad,0})}$ | Empirical NOx reduction due to EGR | — |
| 6 | $\Delta \theta_{id} = K \cdot r_{EGR} \cdot e^{E_{id}/R_u T_{comp}}$ | Ignition delay increase with EGR (CI) | deg crank |
| 7 | $S_L \propto \left(\dfrac{T_u}{T_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha_1} \left(\dfrac{p_u}{p_{ref}}\right)^{\beta_1} \left(1 - r_{EGR} \cdot \phi_{O_2}\right)$ | Laminar flame speed with dilution | m/s |
| 8 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | Thermal efficiency (Otto baseline) | — |
| 9 | $\rho_{charge} = \dfrac{p_{in}}{R_u \cdot T_{in}} \left( M_{air}(1-r_{EGR}) + M_{exh} r_{EGR} \right)$ | Charge density modification | kg/m³ |
| 10 | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption | kg/kWh |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems:**
> - For **SI engines**, the practical EGR limit is **≈ 10 – 15 %** (above this, misfire and cyclic variability become unacceptable).
> - For **CI engines**, the limit is **≈ 30 – 40 %** (above this, smoke, CO, and HC rise sharply).
> - The **maximum EGR rate** is often defined by either the **smoke limit** (diesel) or the **misfire limit** (gasoline).
> - **Cooled EGR** is preferred over hot EGR for NOx control because the cooler charge has even higher density and lower pre-combustion temperature.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of EGR

In modern automotive and stationary engine applications, EGR is used to:

1. **Reduce NOx emissions** — Primary application; meets Euro VI / BS-VI norms.
2. **Lower peak cylinder temperature** — Protects pistons, valves, and turbochargers.
3. **Improve part-load fuel economy** in SI engines (pumping loss reduction when used with throttle).
4. **Reduce knocking tendency** in SI engines (acts as an anti-knock additive).
5. **Modify heat release rate** for better NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) in CI engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Trade-off Table (Always remember these opposing effects):

| Effect | Direction with EGR | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| NOx | ↓↓ (Strong decrease) | Lower $T_{ad}$, less O₂ |
| Smoke (CI) | ↑ (Increase) | Lower local O₂, slower mixing |
| HC & CO | ↑ (Slight increase) | Incomplete combustion |
| BSFC | ↑ (Slight worsening) | Lower $\eta_{comb}$ |
| Power output | ↓ (Decrease) | Less air, displaced by inert gas |
| Ignition delay (CI) | ↑ (Increase) | Lower T, p, O₂ |
| Knocking (SI) | ↓ (Suppress) | Lower T, slower flame |
| Pumping loss (SI) | ↓ (Reduce) | Allows throttle opening |
| Part-load $\eta_{th}$ (SI) | ↑ (Improves) | Pumping loss reduction |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Quantitative NOx Reduction from EGR

We derive the explicit relationship between EGR rate and the reduction in peak flame temperature, and then use the Zeldovich mechanism to estimate the corresponding NOx reduction.

### Step 1 — Charge Composition with EGR

Let:
- $r$ = EGR ratio (mass fraction, decimal form)
- $x_{O_2,air} = 0.232$ (mass fraction of O₂ in air)
- $x_{O_2,exh} = 0.05$ (typical mass fraction of residual O₂ in exhaust)

The effective mass fraction of oxygen in the diluted charge is:

$$x_{O_2,eff} = (1 - r) \cdot x_{O_2,air} + r \cdot x_{O_2,exh}$$

### Step 2 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio Modification

The required air-fuel ratio for stoichiometric combustion becomes effectively higher because some "air" is replaced by exhaust gas. Define the **relative specific heat ratio**:

$$\gamma_{eff} = \frac{(1-r) \cdot \gamma_{air} \cdot m_{air} + r \cdot \gamma_{exh} \cdot m_{exh}}{m_{charge}}$$

For an ideal gas mixture:

$$\frac{1}{\gamma_{eff} - 1} = (1 - r) \cdot \frac{1}{\gamma_{air} - 1} + r \cdot \frac{1}{\gamma_{exh} - 1}$$

### Step 3 — Adiabatic Flame Temperature with EGR

Using the first law for the constant-pressure combustion process:

$$T_{ad} = T_{in} + \frac{Q_{comb}}{C_{p,eff} \cdot m_{charge}}$$

Substituting the weighted $C_{p,eff}$:

$$T_{ad} = T_{in} + \frac{Q_{comb}}{m_{charge} \left[ (1 - r) C_{p,air} + r C_{p,exh} \right]}$$

The temperature *drop* relative to the no-EGR case is therefore:

$$\Delta T_{ad} = T_{ad,0} - T_{ad} = T_{ad,0} - T_{in} - \frac{Q_{comb}}{m_{charge} \left[ (1 - r) C_{p,air} + r C_{p,exh} \right]}$$

### Step 4 — NOx Reduction via Zeldovich

Integrating the Zeldovich rate over the high-temperature reaction zone:

$$[\text{NO}]_{peak} \approx A \cdot \tau_{res} \cdot e^{-E_a / R_u T_{max}}$$

where $\tau_{res}$ is the residence time at high temperature.

Taking the ratio with and without EGR:

$$\frac{[\text{NO}]_{EGR}}{[\text{NO}]_{0}} = \exp\left[-\frac{E_a}{R_u} \left( \frac{1}{T_{max,EGR}} - \frac{1}{T_{max,0}} \right) \right]$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style)

Assume:
- $T_{max,0} = 2400$ K
- $T_{max,EGR} = 2150$ K (with 20 % EGR)
- $E_a = 300{,}000$ J/mol
- $R_u = 8.314$ J/mol·K

Then:

$$\frac{1}{T_{max,EGR}} - \frac{1}{T_{max,0}} = \frac{1}{2150} - \frac{1}{2400} = 4.651 \times 10^{-4} - 4.167 \times 10^{-4} = 4.84 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{E_a}{R_u} \left( \frac{1}{T_{max,EGR}} - \frac{1}{T_{max,0}} \right) = \frac{300{,}000}{8.314} \times 4.84 \times 10^{-5} = 36{,}085 \times 4.84 \times 10^{-5} \approx 1.747$$

$$\frac{[\text{NO}]_{EGR}}{[\text{NO}]_{0}} = e^{-1.747} \approx 0.174$$

**Therefore, NOx is reduced to ≈ 17.4 % of its original value — a reduction of ≈ 82.6 %.**

This simple calculation is the **"wow" derivation** examiners love. Memorize it.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Effect of EGR on Ignition Delay (CI Engines)

### Step 1 — Arrhenius Form of Ignition Delay

For a diesel engine, the ignition delay $\theta_{id}$ (in crank-angle degrees) follows:

$$\theta_{id} = A \cdot p_{cyl}^{-n_1} \cdot \phi^{n_2} \cdot e^{E_a / R_u T_{comp}}$$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential constant
- $p_{cyl}$ = cylinder pressure at injection
- $\phi$ = equivalence ratio
- $T_{comp}$ = compression temperature
- $E_a$ = global activation energy for auto-ignition (≈ 120 – 170 kJ/kg for diesel)

### Step 2 — Pressure Reduction Due to EGR

The introduction of exhaust gas reduces the effective charge density and hence the compression pressure:

$$p_{comp,EGR} = p_{comp,0} \left( \frac{T_{int,0}}{T_{int,EGR}} \right) \left( 1 - r \cdot \frac{M_{exh} - M_{air}}{M_{exh}} \right)$$

For simplicity, taking a linear approximation:

$$p_{comp,EGR} \approx p_{comp,0} (1 - 0.4 r)$$

### Step 3 — Temperature Modification

The inducted temperature with EGR (recirculated hot gas) increases:

$$T_{int,EGR} = T_{int,0} (1 - r) + T_{exh} \cdot r$$

Assuming $T_{exh} \approx 700$ K and $T_{int,0} = 320$ K, for $r = 0.20$:

$$T_{int,EGR} = 320 (0.8) + 700 (0.2) = 256 + 140 = 396 \text{ K}$$

### Step 4 — Combined Effect on Ignition Delay

$$\frac{\theta_{id,EGR}}{\theta_{id,0}} = \left( \frac{p_{comp,EGR}}{p_{comp,0}} \right)^{-n_1} \cdot e^{\frac{E_a}{R_u}\left( \frac{1}{T_{comp,EGR}} - \frac{1}{T_{comp,0}} \right)}$$

With $n_1 = 1.0$, $E_a = 150{,}000$ J/mol, and typical compression temperatures $T_{comp,0} = 900$ K, $T_{comp,EGR} = 870$ K:

$$\text{Pressure term} = (0.92)^{-1} = 1.087$$

$$\text{Temperature term} = e^{\frac{150{,}000}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{870} - \frac{1}{900} \right)} = e^{18{,}042 \times 3.85 \times 10^{-5}} = e^{0.695} \approx 2.00$$

$$\frac{\theta_{id,EGR}}{\theta_{id,0}} = 1.087 \times 2.00 \approx 2.17$$

**The ignition delay approximately doubles with 20 % EGR.** This is consistent with experimental findings and forms the basis of the **heat-release shape change** in EGR-equipped diesel engines.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Quantitative Effects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for KTU Board Exam:** The following matrix gives the qualitative *and* indicative quantitative effect of EGR on every key engine parameter.

| Engine Parameter | Effect of EGR (↑ ↓ ↔) | Quantitative Order | Governing Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adiabatic flame temperature $T_{ad}$ | ↓↓ | 80 – 150 K drop per 10 % EGR | Higher $C_{p,eff}$ |
| Peak cylinder pressure $P_{max}$ | ↓ | 3 – 8 % per 10 % EGR | Lower $\dot{m}_{air}$ |
| NOx emissions | ↓↓↓ | 60 – 85 % drop at 20 – 30 % EGR | Zeldovich suppression |
| Smoke (CI, diesel) | ↑↑ | Doubles at 30 % EGR | O₂ partial pressure drop |
| HC emissions | ↑ | 10 – 30 % increase | Quench, crevice effects |
| CO emissions | ↑ | 10 – 20 % increase | Incomplete combustion |
| BSFC | ↑ | 2 – 5 % per 10 % EGR | Lower $\eta_{comb}$ |
| Ignition delay $\theta_{id}$ (CI) | ↑↑ | +30 to +100 % at 20 % EGR | $T_{comp}$ and $p_{cyl}$ drop |
| Laminar flame speed $S_L$ (SI) | ↓ | 10 – 25 % at 15 % EGR | Diluent, lower $T_{ad}$ |
| Knocking tendency (SI) | ↓↓ | Major reduction | Lower $T_{ad}$ |
| Part-load BSFC (SI) | ↓ | 3 – 8 % improvement | Pumping loss reduction |
| Specific heat of charge $C_p$ | ↑ | 5 – 15 % at 25 % EGR | $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ content |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — EGR System Architecture

The Mermaid diagram below shows the complete **functional flow** of an EGR system, including the cooler, control valve, and recirculation paths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Cylinder] --> B[Exhaust Manifold]
    B --> C[EGR Cooler]
    C --> D[EGR Control Valve]
    D --> E[ Intake Manifold]
    F[Fresh Air Intake] --> E
    E --> A
    G[ECU / Electronic Control Unit] --> D
    H[Engine Speed and Load Sensors] --> G
    I[Exhaust Temperature Sensor] --> G
    J[Intake Air Temperature Sensor] --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Hot exhaust gas is tapped from the **exhaust manifold** (B) and routed to the **EGR cooler** (C) for heat rejection.
- The **EGR control valve** (D) is electronically actuated by the **ECU** (G), which receives signals from the **speed, load, and temperature sensors** (H, I, J).
- The cooled, metered exhaust is mixed with **fresh air** (F) in the **intake manifold** (E) before entering the cylinder (A).

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Effect Cascade of EGR on Combustion and Emissions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Introduce EGR into Intake] --> B[Lower O2 Partial Pressure]
    A --> C[Higher Charge Cp]
    A --> D[Higher Intake Temperature]
    B --> E[Lower Tad]
    C --> E
    D --> F[Lower Compression Temperature]
    E --> G[Reduced Zeldovich NOx]
    F --> H[Longer Ignition Delay in CI]
    H --> I[Altered Premixed Burn Fraction]
    E --> J[Reduced Peak P]
    G --> K[Lower NOx Emissions]
    I --> L[Modified HRR Profile]
    H --> M[Possible Higher Smoke and HC]
    L --> N[Possible Higher BSFC]
    J --> O[Lower Thermal Stress]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Trade-off Triangle of EGR

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Triangle_Tradeoff
        A1[Low NOx] -->|Achieved by| B1[High EGR Rate]
        B1 -->|Side effect| C1[High Smoke and HC]
        C1 -->|Constrains| A1
    end
    subgraph Control_Loop
        S1[ECU receives Map Signal] --> S2[Calculates Optimum rEGR]
        S2 --> S3[Modulates EGR Valve]
        S3 --> S4[Feedback via NOx and Lambda Sensors]
        S4 --> S1
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **trade-off triangle** is a vital concept: every increase in EGR rate to lower NOx moves the operating point closer to the **smoke/HC limit**. The ECU therefore continuously modulates the EGR valve to keep the engine at the *minimum-NOx*, *maximum-EGR* corner of the triangle without breaching the smoke limit.

## 4.4 Diagram Summary Table

| Diagram | Purpose | Key Insight Conveyed |
|---|---|---|
| EGR System Architecture | Hardware flow | Shows physical location of cooler, valve, ECU |
| Effect Cascade Flow | Cause-effect chain | Connects physics to emissions outcome |
| Trade-off Triangle | Design constraint | Highlights the NOx–smoke–HC balance |
