<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define HCCI combustion and list two key advantages.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition (HCCI) is an advanced combustion mode in which a homogeneous air-fuel mixture is auto-ignited by compression, without a spark plug (gasoline HCCI) or late fuel injection (diesel HCCI).
> 
> **Key Advantages (any two):**
> 1. **Ultra-low NOx emissions** due to lower and more uniform peak combustion temperatures.
> 2. **High thermal efficiency** approaching that of diesel engines, since no throttling losses occur.
> 3. **Very low particulate matter (PM)** emissions, especially in diesel HCCI.
> 
> **[3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for two advantages]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Why is combustion control (CA50) more difficult in HCCI compared to SI engines?**

> **Model Answer:**
> In SI engines, combustion timing is directly controlled by **spark timing** — a single, fast-acting electrical signal.
> 
> In HCCI, combustion is initiated by **chemical auto-ignition** which depends on the entire thermal and chemical history of the charge. CA50 is therefore controlled indirectly through parameters like **inlet temperature, EGR rate, fuel-air equivalence ratio, and residual gas fraction**.
> 
> These parameters act with significant **thermal inertia** (coolant and oil temperatures respond slowly), making HCCI combustion phasing control **slower and less precise** than spark timing control in SI.
> 
> **[3 marks: 1 for SI comparison + 1 for HCCI challenge + 1 for engineering implication]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]*

**a)** Explain the working principle of a four-stroke **gasoline HCCI engine** with the help of a schematic block diagram. Discuss the role of EGR and intake temperature in combustion phasing. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A four-stroke gasoline HCCI engine operates at compression ratio **r = 16**, with inlet temperature **T₁ = 340 K** and polytropic exponent **n = 1.34**. Compute the end-of-compression temperature and discuss whether auto-ignition will occur for a fuel whose auto-ignition temperature is **260 °C (533 K)**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Question A (a):**

> **Principle of Gasoline HCCI Engine:**
> 
> 1. **Charge Preparation:** A homogeneous air-fuel mixture is prepared through early direct injection or port fuel injection. The fuel has ample time to vaporize and mix completely with intake air before compression. The mixture may be diluted with **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** to control reactivity.
> 
> 2. **Compression Stroke:** The homogeneous mixture is compressed by the rising piston. As pressure increases, the bulk mixture temperature rises according to:
> 
> $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{n-1}$$
> 
> 3. **Auto-Ignition:** Near TDC, when the bulk temperature exceeds the auto-ignition threshold of the mixture, **multiple ignition kernels form simultaneously** throughout the combustion chamber. No spark plug is required.
> 
> 4. **Heat Release & Power:** Heat release is rapid but distributed, producing a smooth but fast pressure rise. Combustion products expand, delivering work.
> 
> 5. **Exhaust:** Burned gases are expelled during the exhaust stroke; a portion is recirculated for the next cycle.

> **Role of EGR:**
> - EGR acts as a **thermal diluent** and chemical inhibitor.
> - It reduces the partial pressure of reactive species, slowing chemistry.
> - Higher EGR → later auto-ignition (CA50 retarded).
> - Lower EGR → earlier auto-ignition, higher heat release rate, knock risk.

> **Role of Intake Temperature:**
> - Directly determines end-of-compression temperature $T_2$.
> - Each 10 K increase in $T_1$ can advance CA50 by ~3–5°.
> - Cold-start is impossible below a critical $T_1$ threshold (~330 K for gasoline).

**[7 marks: 2 for principle + 2 for EGR role + 2 for temperature role + 1 for block diagram]**

##### **Model Solution — Question A (b):**

> **Given:**
> - Compression ratio $r = 16$
> - Inlet temperature $T_1 = 340$ K
> - Polytropic exponent $n = 1.34$
> - Auto-ignition threshold of fuel = 533 K

> **Step 1: Compute end-of-compression temperature $T_2$.**
> 
> $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{n-1} = 340 \times 16^{(1.34 - 1)}$$
> 
> $$T_2 = 340 \times 16^{0.34}$$

> **Step 2: Compute $16^{0.34}$.**
> 
> $$\log_{10}(16^{0.34}) = 0.34 \times \log_{10}(16) = 0.34 \times 1.2041 = 0.4094$$
> 
> $$16^{0.34} = 10^{0.4094} = 2.566$$

> **Step 3: Final $T_2$.**
> 
> $$T_2 = 340 \times 2.566 = 872.4 \text{ K} \approx 599.3 \text{ °C}$$

> **Step 4: Compare with auto-ignition temperature.**
> 
> $$T_2 = 872.4 \text{ K} \gg 533 \text{ K} \text{ (auto-ignition threshold)}$$
> 
> Margin = $872.4 - 533 = 339.4$ K — a substantial overshoot.

> **Conclusion:** Auto-ignition will **definitely occur** since the end-of-compression temperature exceeds the auto-ignition temperature by a wide margin (~339 K). In fact, this margin is so large that knock/rapid pressure rise is likely, suggesting the use of EGR or a slightly lower compression ratio is desirable for smooth operation.
> 
> **[7 marks: 2 for setup + 2 for numerical evaluation + 2 for comparison + 1 for engineering conclusion]**

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**a)** Discuss the working principle of **diesel HCCI** (Premixed Charge Compression Ignition) and compare it with conventional diesel combustion. Highlight at least three operational challenges. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A diesel HCCI engine running at 1800 rpm has an ignition delay of **2.5 ms**. Calculate the corresponding crank angle before TDC at which auto-ignition starts. If the main heat release occurs over **30° CA**, compute the average rate of pressure rise (ROPR) if the peak pressure rise during combustion is **40 bar**. Comment on whether this engine will suffer from knock. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Question B (a):**

> **Working Principle of Diesel HCCI:**
> In diesel HCCI (also called Premixed Charge Compression Ignition - PCCI), diesel fuel is injected **much earlier than in conventional diesel** — typically **40–90° BTDC** during the compression stroke, sometimes even during the intake stroke. This gives the fuel sufficient time to **vaporize and premix with air** before reaching auto-ignition conditions.
> 
> Near TDC, the nearly homogeneous mixture auto-ignites simultaneously throughout the chamber — analogous to gasoline HCCI but using diesel-grade fuel with higher cetane number (faster chemistry).

> **Comparison with Conventional Diesel:**

| Aspect | Conventional Diesel | Diesel HCCI |
|---|---|---|
| Injection timing | Near TDC (5–10° BTDC) | Very early (40–90° BTDC) |
| Charge state | Highly stratified, diffusion | Premixed, homogeneous |
| Combustion mode | Diffusion-controlled spray | Premixed auto-ignition |
| NOx | High | Very low (60–90% reduction) |
| Soot/PM | Moderate-high | Very low (80–90% reduction) |
| ROPR | Moderate | Very high (knock risk) |

> **Operational Challenges (any three):**
> 1. **High Rate of Pressure Rise (Knock):** Premixed fuel charge ignites all at once → very fast pressure rise → mechanical stress and audible knock.
> 2. **HC and CO Emissions:** Premixed fuel contacting cylinder walls may quench, increasing unburned hydrocarbons.
> 3. **Limited Load Range:** Cannot deliver high loads because increasing fueling leads to dangerous ROPR.
> 4. **Cold Start Difficulty:** Insufficient compression temperatures for auto-ignition.
> 5. **Fuel-Wall Interaction:** Oil dilution and carbon deposits from long injection timing.
> 
> **[7 marks: 2 for principle + 2 for comparison + 2 for challenges + 1 for concluding statement]**

##### **Model Solution — Question B (b):**

> **Given:**
> - Engine speed $N = 1800$ rpm = 30 rev/s
> - Ignition delay $\tau_{ign} = 2.5$ ms = $2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ s
> - Heat release duration $\Delta \theta = 30°$ CA
> - Peak pressure rise $\Delta P = 40$ bar

> **Step 1: Compute crank angle rate $\dot{\theta}$.**
> 
> $$\dot{\theta} = 360 \times N_{rev/s} = 360 \times 30 = 10{,}800 \text{ deg/s}$$

> **Step 2: Compute crank angle before TDC for ignition start.**
> 
> $$\theta_{ign} = \tau_{ign} \times \dot{\theta} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10{,}800 = 27°\text{ BTDC}$$

> **Step 3: Compute average Rate of Pressure Rise (ROPR).**
> 
> $$\text{ROPR}_{avg} = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta \theta} = \frac{40 \text{ bar}}{30°\text{ CA}} = 1.33 \text{ bar/°CA}$$

> **Step 4: Compare with knock threshold.**
> Typical HCCI knock threshold: **ROPR > 1.5–2.0 bar/°CA** is considered severe.

> **Conclusion:** With $\text{ROPR}_{avg} = 1.33$ bar/°CA, the engine operates **just below the severe-knock threshold** but is close to marginal. Since actual ROPR peaks much higher than the average, the engine is **likely to experience mild knock** and would benefit from EGR dilution or earlier EGR introduction to reduce pressure rise.
> 
> **[7 marks: 2 for θ calculation + 2 for ROPR calculation + 2 for comparison with knock limit + 1 for engineering recommendation]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing HCCI with GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection).** GDI is still spark-ignited; HCCI is auto-ignited. Examiners frequently test this distinction. Do NOT claim HCCI uses a spark plug.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting that HCCI has NO flame front propagation.** Many students incorrectly describe HCCI as "very fast flame propagation." This is fundamentally wrong — combustion occurs simultaneously throughout the chamber (volumetric, not propagating).
> 
> 3. **Ignoring EGR's role in numerical problems.** When computing $T_2$ for HCCI, students often forget that EGR dilutes the charge and effectively alters the polytropic exponent *n* and specific heat ratio *γ*. Always state EGR assumption explicitly.
> 
> 4. **Wrong units for crank angle rate.** Use **deg/s**, not rad/s, when computing ignition delay in crank-angle terms.
> 
> 5. **Stating "HCCI has zero emissions."** HCCI has very low NOx and PM but elevated HC and CO. Never claim zero emissions.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up HCCI with ATAC (Toyota's Activated Thermo-Atmosphere Combustion).** ATAC is a gasoline HCCI variant. Mention this as a related technology to demonstrate advanced reading.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Four-Stroke Gasoline & Diesel HCCI Engines**

- ✅ HCCI = Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition; **no spark, no late injection**.
- ✅ Combustion is **volumetric and simultaneous** (microwave-popcorn analogy), not flame-propagation.
- ✅ **Gasoline HCCI:** Premixed air-gasoline + EGR + auto-ignition by compression.
- ✅ **Diesel HCCI:** Very early injection (40–90° BTDC) + auto-ignition of premixed charge.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Ultra-low NOx (99% reduction possible), very low PM, high thermal efficiency (35–45%).
- ✅ **Challenges:** Cold start, narrow load range, high ROPR/knock, slow combustion phasing control.
- ✅ **CA50** is the key combustion phasing metric; optimum is **8–12° ATDC**.
- ✅ **EGR** is the most effective thermal control parameter (30–60% typical).
- ✅ NOx reduction is **exponential in peak temperature** (Zeldovich mechanism) — every 100 K drop in peak T gives ~10× NOx reduction.
- ✅ HCCI thrives in **part-load, lean conditions** — perfect for **series hybrid** applications.
- ✅ HCCI is best deployed as **dual-mode** (SI at high load, HCCI at part load) — Toyota, GM, Mercedes concepts all use this.
- ✅ **Formulas to memorize:**
  - $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{n-1}$ (compression end-temperature)
  - $\text{ROPR} = \dfrac{dP}{d\theta}$ (knock indicator)
  - $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ (Otto efficiency limit)
  - $\dot{\theta} = 360 \times N_{rev/s}$ (crank angle rate)
- ✅ Related technologies to mention for higher marks: **PPCI, ATAC, UNIBUS, DiesOtto, LTC (Low-Temperature Combustion), RCCI (Reactivity Controlled CI)**.
- ✅ Real-world link: Mercedes DiesOtto 2007 concept achieved **42% thermal efficiency** using HCCI concepts.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HCCI Fuel Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HCCI Fuel Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition (HCCI)** is a combustion concept in which a *premixed, lean or dilute, fuel–air mixture* is formed before the start of compression, and ignition is triggered by the *auto-ignition* of the charge as the piston approaches top dead centre (TDC). Because the charge is homogeneous, there is **no diffusion flame front**; combustion proceeds as *distributed, low-temperature, simultaneous auto-ignition of the entire mixture*.

For this mode to succeed, the fuel must satisfy a very particular set of physical and chemical requirements — collectively referred to as **HCCI Fuel Requirements**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HCCI Fuel Requirement (Board Definition):**
> The combination of auto-ignition quality (often expressed by a *Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition Index*, RON + MON, or PRF number), volatility, latent heat of vaporisation, and chemical composition (paraffin / aromatic / olefin balance) that allows a fuel to be homogeneously mixed with air and to auto-ignite in a controlled, single- or two-stage manner inside the temperature window achievable by compression alone — without the use of a spark plug.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Think of HCCI like **popcorn in a closed microwave oven**:
- The kernels (fuel) are spread evenly throughout the chamber (homogeneous mixture).
- The oven heats the *entire* chamber uniformly (compression heating of the whole charge).
- All kernels pop *almost simultaneously* (whole-volume auto-ignition).
- If the kernels are too "stubborn" (high octane / low cetane → hard to ignite) → no popping → misfire.
- If the kernels are too "aggressive" (low cetane / low octane) → they pop *too early* → knock or rough combustion.
- The "right popcorn" — one that pops **at the right time, at the right temperature, all at once** — represents a fuel that has the correct HCCI auto-ignition reactivity.

## 1.3 Why HCCI Fuel Requirements are Special

Unlike **Spark Ignition (SI)** engines (which require fuels that *resist* auto-ignition → high octane rating) and unlike **Compression Ignition (CI)** engines (which require fuels that *promote* auto-ignition → high cetane number), HCCI sits **between** the two:

> [!NOTE]
> **Reactivity Trade-off in HCCI:**
> - Too reactive (high cetane) → combustion begins *before* TDC → high pressure rise rate (dp/dθ up to **20 bar/°CA**), knock, engine damage.
> - Too unreactive (high octane) → combustion begins *after* TDC → low peak pressure, misfire, very poor efficiency.
> - The HCCI "Goldilocks" reactivity window is typically **PRF 60–90** (Primary Reference Fuel blend of n-heptane and iso-octane, by volume).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used to Specify HCCI Fuels (KTU Board-Aligned)

| Metric (Symbol) | Typical HCCI Range | Why it Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Octane Number (RON) | 70 – 95 | Resistance to auto-ignition at lower temperatures |
| Cetane Number (CN) | 35 – 55 | Ease of auto-ignition (inverse of octane) |
| PRF Number | 60 – 90 | The principal reactivity surrogate for HCCI research |
| Boiling Range | 30 °C – 200 °C | Governs homogeneity of charge |
| Latent Heat of Vaporisation (L) | 200 – 600 kJ/kg | Controls charge cooling → ignition delay |
| Molecular Structure (P/A/N) | Paraffin-rich preferred | Affects LTHR and NTC behaviour |

Physical constants used throughout the module: **Universal gas constant R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**, **Air–fuel stoichiometric ratio for gasoline ≈ 14.7**, **Lower Heating Value of typical gasoline ≈ 43 MJ/kg**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of HCCI Fuel Requirements

HCCI fuel selection is governed by **four independent engineering pillars**. Each pillar is a logical control point for the engine designer.

### Pillar 1 — Reactivity (When does it ignite?)

- The fuel's *auto-ignition* timing must align with a pre-defined crank-angle window (typically **5–15° BTDC** for HCCI).
- Quantified using the **Primary Reference Fuel (PRF) Number**:
$$\text{PRF} = X_{\text{iso-octane}} \times 100 \;+\; X_{\text{n-heptane}} \times 0$$
where $X$ denotes *volume fraction*. A fuel whose knock behaviour matches a blend of *X %* iso-octane + *(100−X) %* n-heptane is called "**PRF X**".
- For HCCI: PRF ≈ **60–90** is the practical operating band.

### Pillar 2 — Volatility (Does it form a homogeneous mixture?)

- A fuel must vaporise completely to mix uniformly with air.
- A simplified volatility criterion: the **T90 boiling point** (temperature at which 90 % of the fuel has vaporised) must lie below the cylinder wall temperature at intake-valve-closure (IVC).
$$T_{90} \;<\; T_{\text{IVC,wall}}$$
- A fuel with $T_{90} > 200$ °C will form **fuel puddles** on piston/liner walls → inhomogeneous mixture → soot and partial diffusion flame.

### Pillar 3 — Latent Heat of Vaporisation (How much does it cool the charge?)

- When the fuel vaporises inside the cylinder, it absorbs heat (latent heat) and **drops the local temperature**, which delays auto-ignition.
- This is the key to **controlled reactivity** in HCCI.
$$T_{\text{charge, after evap}} = T_{\text{charge, before}} - \frac{L \cdot f}{c_{p,\text{air}}(1 + f) + c_{p,\text{fuel}} \cdot f}$$
where $L$ = latent heat of vaporisation, $f$ = fuel/air equivalence mass ratio, $c_p$ = specific heat at constant pressure.

### Pillar 4 — Chemical Composition (P/A/N)

- **Paraffins** (especially n-paraffins) ignite readily and are HCCI-friendly.
- **Aromatics** resist auto-ignition and tend to *smoke* under lean HCCI.
- **Naphthenes** are intermediate.
- A simple P/A/N weighting:
$$\text{HCCI Reactivity Index} = w_P \cdot (100 - \text{Aromatic\%}) + w_N \cdot (0.5 \cdot \text{Naphthene\%}) + w_A \cdot (0 \cdot \text{Aromatic\%})$$

## 2.2 Auto-Ignition Delay (The "Heart" of HCCI)

The Arrhenius-type single-step ignition delay correlation is:

$$\tau_{\text{ig}} = A \cdot p^{-n} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right)$$

where:
- $\tau_{\text{ig}}$ = ignition delay (seconds)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (fuel-specific)
- $p$ = cylinder pressure (Pa)
- $n$ = pressure exponent (≈ 1 for HCCI mixtures)
- $E_a$ = apparent activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = unburned gas temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in HCCI:** HCCI does not have a spark to "set the clock" — the *ignition delay* itself is the clock. A 1 ms change in $\tau_{\text{ig}}$ shifts the **CA50** (crank angle at 50 % heat release) by **~5–7° crank angle**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield — Memorise This)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{PRF} = V_{\text{iso}} \cdot 100$ | Reactivity index for HCCI | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{CN} \approx 60 - 0.5 \cdot \text{RON}$ (approx., Heywood) | Rough inverse relationship | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\tau_{\text{ig}} = A p^{-n} \exp(E_a / RT)$ | Arrhenius ignition delay | s |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{\text{evap}} = \dfrac{L \cdot f}{c_{p,\text{air}}(1+f) + c_{p,\text{f}} f}$ | Charge cooling due to vaporisation | K |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dp}{d\theta_{\text{HCCI}}} \approx \dfrac{\gamma \, p_0}{\varepsilon - 1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_c}{V}\right)^{\gamma}\!\dfrac{dx_b}{d\theta}$ | Pressure rise rate during HCCI | bar/°CA |
| 6 | $\phi_{\text{global}} = \dfrac{m_{f,\text{actual}}}{m_{f,\text{stoich}}}$ | Global equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $L_{\text{st}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{a}}{\dot{m}_{f}}\,\bigg\vert_{\text{stoich}} \approx 14.7$ | Stoichiometric A/F for gasoline | kg/kg |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{HCCI}} \approx 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\eta_c}$ | Indicative efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Tip (Valuation Key):** The most frequently *wrong* mark-loss in this topic is using **CN in the RON formula** or vice versa. Always re-check whether the question is asking for *ignition resistance* (use RON/MON) or *ignition ease* (use CN).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

HCCI fuel requirements are critical for:
1. **Biofuel integration** — Ethanol (RON 108, CN ~8) and biodiesel (CN ~50) lie on opposite sides of the HCCI window; *blending ratios* are designed using PRF.
2. **Dual-fuel RCCI** (Reactivity Controlled CI) — uses two fuels of *different* reactivity (e.g., gasoline + diesel) injected at *different* times to recreate HCCI-like combustion.
3. **Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI)** — the fuel *itself* is the control variable.
4. **Modern gasoline compression ignition (GCI)** research (e.g., MAHLE "Jet Ignition", GM "HCCI on Diesel" demonstrators).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Relationship between Cetane Number and PRF Number for HCCI

A commonly used empirical correlation (Heywood; reaffirmed in KTU board numerics) connects the *apparent cetane number* of a fuel to its PRF reactivity.

### Step 1 — Define the PRF Equivalence

By definition, a fuel with octane rating of *X* matches a PRF blend of *X %* iso-octane and *(100−X) %* n-heptane in its resistance to knock.

$$\text{PRF}_{\text{fuel}} = X \quad\text{(volume \% of iso-octane in matching blend)}$$

### Step 2 — Use the Binary Component Standard

In the PRF scale:
- **iso-octane (iC8H18)** has an effective CN → 0 (worst CI fuel, best SI fuel).
- **n-heptane (nC7H16)** has an effective CN → 100 (best CI fuel, worst SI fuel).

### Step 3 — Linear Mixing Rule for CN

Applying a linear-by-volume rule:

$$\text{CN}_{\text{PRF}} \;=\; X_{\text{iso}} \cdot \text{CN}_{\text{iso}} \;+\; X_{\text{nC7}} \cdot \text{CN}_{\text{nC7}}$$

Substituting the boundary values $\text{CN}_{\text{iso}} = 0$ and $\text{CN}_{\text{nC7}} = 100$:

$$\text{CN}_{\text{PRF}} \;=\; (1 - X/100)\cdot 100$$

### Step 4 — Final Inverted Relationship

Therefore, for any PRF-blended fuel used in HCCI:

$$\boxed{\text{CN} \;=\; 100 - \text{PRF}}$$

#### Worked Numerical — Typical Board-Problem Pattern

**Question:** A fuel behaves like PRF 80 in HCCI experiments. What is its approximate cetane number?

**Solution using the boxed equation:**

$$\text{CN} = 100 - \text{PRF} = 100 - 80 = 20$$

This matches the typical **HCCI lower-reactivity boundary** (CN ≈ 20 is hard to operate HCCI without EGR or intake heating).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Effect of Latent Heat on HCCI Ignition Timing

The unburned gas temperature at the end of vaporisation controls auto-ignition. We derive the temperature drop for an *ideal* HCCI fuel.

### Step 1 — Initial Charge State

At intake-valve closure (IVC):
- $T_{\text{IVC}}$ = unburned charge temperature (K)
- $p_{\text{IVC}}$ = unburned charge pressure (Pa)
- $m_a$ = mass of air (kg)
- $m_f$ = mass of fuel (kg)
- Equivalence ratio: $\phi$

### Step 2 — Energy Balance During Vaporisation

The heat absorbed by the fuel equals the heat lost by the air. Assuming constant $c_p$ and equilibrium vaporisation:

$$m_f \cdot L \;=\; (m_a \, c_{p,a} \;+\; m_f \, c_{p,f}) \cdot \Delta T_{\text{evap}}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\Delta T_{\text{evap}}$

$$\Delta T_{\text{evap}} \;=\; \frac{m_f \cdot L}{m_a \, c_{p,a} \;+\; m_f \, c_{p,f}}$$

### Step 4 — Introduce Fuel-Air Ratio $f = m_f / m_a$

$$\Delta T_{\text{evap}} \;=\; \frac{f \cdot L}{c_{p,a} \;+\; f \cdot c_{p,f}}$$

### Step 5 — Final Working Form

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{\text{evap}} \;=\; \frac{L \, f}{c_{p,a} + f \, c_{p,f}}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** A high $L$ (e.g., ethanol = **900 kJ/kg** vs n-heptane = **317 kJ/kg**) cools the charge more, **delays** ignition, and gives more time for the mixture to become homogeneous — one of the *primary* reasons ethanol is favoured in HCCI research engines.

#### Numerical Verification

**Question:** For an HCCI engine, $f = 0.0667$, ethanol $L = 9.0 \times 10^5$ J/kg, $c_{p,a} = 1005$ J/(kg·K), $c_{p,f} = 2420$ J/(kg·K). Find the charge cooling.

**Calculation:**

$$\Delta T_{\text{evap}} = \frac{(9.0 \times 10^{5})(0.0667)}{1005 + (0.0667)(2420)}$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{evap}} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{4}}{1005 + 161.4} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{4}}{1166.4}$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{evap}} \approx 51.4 \text{ K}$$

So ethanol cools the HCCI charge by **≈ 51 K** — a substantial effect that can shift CA50 by **8–12° crank angle**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — HCCI Fuel Performance vs Fuel Property

This matrix is the **single most important KTU exam visual**. A 14-mark question in ESE 2024 expects this kind of comparative analysis.

| Fuel Property | Too Low (HCCI Penalty) | Optimal Range | Too High (HCCI Penalty) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Octane Number** | Pre-ignition, knock, ringing | **85 – 95** | Misfire, low load, low T |
| **Cetane Number** | Misfire, end-gas, very late CA50 | **35 – 55** | Ringing, knocking, high dp/dθ |
| **Latent Heat (L)** | Charge too hot, early ignition | **300 – 500 kJ/kg** | Charge too cold, misfire, HC |
| **Volatility (T90)** | High-temperature in-pool, soot | **90 – 180 °C** | Cold-start puddle, no mixing |
| **Aromatic content** | Piston deposits, smoke | **< 25 %** | Slow oxidation, soot, ringing |
| **Sulphur** | Oxidation catalyst poisoning | **< 10 ppm** | Severe — always penalised |

## 3.4 KTU "Numerical" Mini-Worked Example — Pressure Rise Rate

A KTU 2024 favourite: given a HCCI combustion event with a *heat release* profile, calculate the *pressure rise rate*.

$$\frac{dp}{d\theta} \;\approx\; \frac{\gamma \, p_0 \, \dot{x}_b}{\varepsilon - 1}\left(\frac{V_c}{V}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$$

**Given:** $p_0 = 1.0$ bar, $\gamma = 1.35$, $\varepsilon = 16$, $V_c/V = 0.25$, $\dot{x}_b = 0.05$ (1/°CA).

**Step 1 — Substitute** values into the formula:

$$\frac{dp}{d\theta} = \frac{(1.35)(1.0)(0.05)}{16 - 1}(0.25)^{1.35 - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute** numerator:

$$\text{Num} = 1.35 \times 1.0 \times 0.05 = 0.0675$$

**Step 3 — Compute** the volume term:

$$(0.25)^{0.35} = e^{0.35 \ln(0.25)} = e^{0.35 \times (-1.386)} = e^{-0.4851} \approx 0.6158$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$\frac{dp}{d\theta} = \frac{0.0675}{15} \times 0.6158 = 0.0045 \times 0.6158 \approx 0.00277 \text{ bar/°CA}$$

Converting to more practical units:

$$\frac{dp}{d\theta} \approx 2.77 \text{ mbar/°CA}$$

For HCCI, the **acceptable upper limit** is **≈ 5 bar/°CA** (RCCI: 10 bar/°CA) — so this fuel-charge combination is *comfortably* within HCCI limits.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HCCI Fuel Selection — Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1([A1 Start HCCI Fuel Selection]) --> n2{Is fuel available in gaseous or easily vaporisable liquid form?}
    n2 -- YES --> n3[Check Volatility T90 less than 200 C and greater than 30 C]
    n2 -- NO --> n2a[Use Port Injection and Intake Heating]
    n2a --> n3
    n3 --> n4{Does fuel have PRF between 60 and 90?}
    n4 -- YES --> n5[Check Latent Heat of Vaporisation]
    n4 -- NO --> n6[Use Dual Fuel Blending to hit PRF window]
    n6 --> n5
    n5 --> n7{Latent Heat between 300 and 500 kJ per kg?}
    n7 -- YES --> n8[Verify Chemical Composition P A N]
    n7 -- NO --> n9[Reformulate using oxygenates]
    n9 --> n8
    n8 --> n10{Aromatics less than 25 percent?}
    n10 -- YES --> n11[Check Sulphur less than 10 ppm]
    n10 -- NO --> n12[Blend with paraffinic stream]
    n12 --> n11
    n11 --> n13{All criteria satisfied?}
    n13 -- YES --> n14[A1 Approved HCCI Fuel]
    n13 -- NO --> n15[Iterate Formulation]
    n15 --> n3
    n14([A1 End])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of HCCI Combustion Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n20([A1 Fuel Tank]) --> n21[Fuel Pump and Pressure Regulator]
    n21 --> n22[Port or Early Direct Injector]
    n22 --> n23[Intake Manifold and Mixer]
    n23 --> n24[Homogeneous Charge Formation Zone]
    n24 --> n25[Compression Stroke Heating]
    n25 --> n26{Does PRF and L match Auto Ignition Window?}
    n26 -- YES --> n27[Distributed Auto Ignition]
    n26 -- NO --> n28[Reactivity Adjustment EGR or Intake Heating]

    n27 --> n29[Heat Release Phase]

```


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]
**"Define HCCI and explain why its fuel requirements differ from those of SI and CI engines."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** HCCI (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition) is a combustion process in which a *premixed homogeneous fuel–air mixture* is compressed until *simultaneous auto-ignition* occurs throughout the charge, without a spark plug or fuel injector into the hot products.
- **Fuel requirement difference — SI (1 mark):** SI engines require *high octane* (RON ≥ 90) fuels to resist auto-ignition; HCCI requires **moderate RON (75–95)**, *low-to-moderate CN* (35–55), as it *depends* on auto-ignition.
- **Fuel requirement difference — CI (1 mark):** CI engines require *high CN (≥ 45)* and high cetane sensitivity; HCCI requires *lower* CN to avoid pre-ignition and ringing — hence fuels are usually *gasoline-like* with volatility tuned for homogeneous mixing.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]
**"What is PRF and how is it used as a reactivity descriptor in HCCI?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU key):**
- **PRF definition (1 mark):** Primary Reference Fuel is a binary blend of *n-heptane* (CN = 100, RON = 0) and *iso-octane* (CN = 0, RON = 100), used as the *octane* and *cetane* reactivity standard.
- **PRF Number formula (1 mark):** $\text{PRF} = X_{\text{iso-octane}} \times 100$ (vol %). A fuel behaving like PRF 80 is **80 % iso-octane + 20 % n-heptane by volume.**
- **Use in HCCI (1 mark):** PRF is the principal *reactivity surrogate* in HCCI research — the desired HCCI window is **PRF 60–90**; CN and PRF are related by $\text{CN} \approx 100 - \text{PRF}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Internal-Choice Questions (ESE Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (THIS Topic):**
> 1. Confusing **CN (cetane number)** and **RON (research octane number)** when reading tables — a 1-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 2. Writing *spontaneous combustion* instead of *auto-ignition* — strictly use **auto-ignition** in HCCI.
> 3. Skipping the *role of latent heat* — examiners expect at least one line on **charge cooling by vaporisation**.
> 4. Omitting units in numerical problems → mark cut in the *final answer only*.
> 5. Drawing the Pillar diagram without arrows / proper labels — partial credit is *not* awarded for vague sketches.

---

### Question A (14 marks) — Reactivity and Auto-Ignition Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024, Module 4, Choice A]**

**(a)** *Explain the role of fuel reactivity in HCCI combustion. Discuss, with a suitable sketch, how the PRF number controls the crank-angle of 50 % heat release (CA50).* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For an HCCI engine operating on a fuel that behaves like PRF 75, calculate the approximate cetane number. If the same fuel is blended in equal volume with pure n-heptane, what is the new PRF number and the corresponding cetane number? Comment on the suitability of the blend for HCCI.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition of fuel reactivity in HCCI (2 marks):**
   - Reactivity is the *ease with which a fuel auto-ignites* under HCCI conditions. It determines *when* combustion begins relative to TDC.
   - The standard measure is the **PRF (Primary Reference Fuel) number**, where PRF 100 = pure iso-octane (least reactive) and PRF 0 = pure n-heptane (most reactive).

2. **Linkage between PRF and CA50 (3 marks):**
   - **Low PRF (reactive fuel):** Ignition occurs *early*, CA50 shifts towards TDC or *before TDC* (advanced), pressure rise rate is *high* (up to **~10 bar/°CA**), risk of engine knock and ringing.
   - **High PRF (unreactive fuel):** Ignition occurs *late*, CA50 shifts *after TDC* (retarded), pressure is low, misfire, very low efficiency.
   - **Sketch description (2 marks):** Two heat-release curves — one advanced (low PRF, peak near TDC) and one retarded (high PRF, peak well after TDC), with the *optimum* curve peaking at **5–15° ATDC** for a high-efficiency HCCI operating point.

3. **Key conclusion (1 mark):** PRF directly controls combustion phasing; a 1 % change in PRF shifts CA50 by ≈ **1° crank angle** in typical HCCI conditions.

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Cetane number of PRF 75 fuel (2 marks):**
   Using $\text{CN} = 100 - \text{PRF}$:

$$\text{CN}_{\text{PRF75}} = 100 - 75 = 25$$

2. **New PRF after blending 50/50 with pure n-heptane (3 marks):**
   - Pure n-heptane corresponds to PRF 0 (0 % iso-octane).
   - 50 % by volume of PRF-75 fuel + 50 % by volume of n-heptane.
   - The iso-octane fraction in the blend: $0.5 \times 0.75 + 0.5 \times 0 = 0.375$
   - Therefore $\text{PRF}_{\text{new}} = 0.375 \times 100 = 37.5$ (≈ 38).

3. **New cetane number (1 mark):**

$$\text{CN}_{\text{new}} = 100 - 38 = 62$$

4. **Suitability for HCCI (1 mark):**
   - The HCCI "PRF window" is **60–90**. PRF 38 is **outside** this window — it is *too reactive* and would cause severe ringing, knocking, and high pressure-rise rate. Not suitable as a single fuel; would need blending with a higher-PRF stream to bring PRF into the 60–90 band.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — Volatility and Charge-Cooling Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024, Module 4, Choice B]**

**(a)** *Discuss the volatility requirements of HCCI fuels. Why is a fuel with T90 > 200 °C unsuitable for HCCI, and how does the *latent heat of vaporisation* influence charge temperature and hence auto-ignition timing?* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A candidate HCCI fuel has latent heat of vaporisation L = 600 kJ/kg, specific heat of vapour c_{p,f} = 1800 J/(kg·K), and specific heat of air c_{p,a} = 1005 J/(kg·K). At a fuel-air mass ratio f = 0.0667, calculate (i) the charge-cooling temperature drop, and (ii) the new unburned gas temperature if the IVC temperature is 350 K. Comment on the resulting impact on auto-ignition delay using the Arrhenius relation.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **HCCI needs homogeneous mixture (2 marks):** Combustion is *premixed*; any fuel that does not fully vaporise will form wall films and fuel puddles, leading to *localised diffusion flames* (smoke, unburned HC, ring wear).

2. **Volatility criterion (3 marks):**
   - T90 (90 % evaporated temperature) must be *below* the IVC wall temperature (~150–180 °C).
   - **T90 > 200 °C** ⇒ unacceptable — the fuel cannot form a homogeneous charge under realistic HCCI conditions.
   - Optimum T90: **30 – 180 °C** (gasoline range).

3. **Latent heat influence (2 marks):**
   - On vaporisation, the fuel absorbs $L$ (J/kg) and cools the surrounding air.
   - Lower charge temperature → *longer* Arrhenius ignition delay (since $\tau_{\text{ig}} \propto \exp(E_a/RT)$). High $L$ therefore *retards* CA50 and is one of the *control knobs* for HCCI phasing.

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Formula recall (1 mark):**

$$\Delta T_{\text{evap}} = \frac{L \cdot f}{c_{p,a} + f \cdot c_{p,f}}$$

2. **Substitute values (2 marks):**
   - $L = 6.0 \times 10^5$ J/kg
   - $f = 0.0667$
   - $c_{p,a} = 1005$ J/(kg·K)
   - $c_{p,f} = 1800$ J/(kg·K)

$$\Delta T_{\text{evap}} = \frac{(6.0 \times 10^5)(0.0667)}{1005 + (0.0667)(1800)} = \frac{4.002 \times 10^4}{1005 + 120.06} = \frac{4.002 \times 10^4}{1125.06}$$

3. **Result (1 mark):**

$$\Delta T_{\text{evap}} \approx 35.6 \text{ K}$$

4. **New unburned gas temperature (1 mark):**

$$T_{\text{new}} = 350 - 35.6 = 314.4 \text{ K}$$

5. **Impact on auto-ignition delay (2 marks):**
   - Using the Arrhenius relation $\tau_{\text{ig}} \propto \exp(E_a / R T)$, a *decrease* in T *increases* $\tau_{\text{ig}}$ exponentially.
   - For $E_a \approx 80$ kJ/mol (typical HCCI fuel):

$$\frac{\tau_{\text{new}}}{\tau_{\text{old}}} = \exp\!\left[\frac{80{,}000}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{314.4} - \frac{1}{350}\right)\right] \approx \exp(3.73) \approx 41.7$$

   - Therefore, the charge-cooling effect of vaporisation **increases the ignition delay by ~40×**, dramatically *retarding* combustion — confirming that latent heat is a powerful HCCI control parameter.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-remember bullets for last-minute revision:**
> - **HCCI = Homogeneous + Premixed + Auto-ignited** (no spark, no diffusion flame).
> - **Four pillars of fuel requirements:** Reactivity, Volatility, Latent Heat, Chemical Composition.
> - **PRF window for HCCI:** **60 – 90** (PRF 80 is the typical "Goldilocks" reference).
> - **PRF–CN relationship:** $\text{CN} \approx 100 - \text{PRF}$ (linear, in the PRF limit).
> - **T90 criterion:** must be **< 200 °C** (ideally 90 – 180 °C).
> - **Latent heat effect:** $\Delta T_{\text{evap}} = \dfrac{L \, f}{c_{p,a} + f \, c_{p,f}}$ — higher $L$ ⇒ colder charge ⇒ *longer* $\tau_{\text{ig}}$ ⇒ retarded CA50.
> - **Arrhenius delay:** $\tau_{\text{ig}} = A p^{-n} \exp(E_a / RT)$ — HCCI has *no* spark to set the clock; the *delay* itself is the clock.
> - **Acceptable pressure-rise-rate ceiling:** **≤ 5 bar/°CA** for pure HCCI; **≤ 10 bar/°CA** for RCCI.
> - **Reactivity trade-off:** low PRF → knock & ringing; high PRF → misfire & low T. Always state the *band* in numerical answers.
> - **Common board errors:** RON/CN confusion, omitting units, drawing sketches without labels, and writing "spontaneous combustion" instead of "auto-ignition".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Low Temperature and Premixed Combustion with Late Injection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Low Temperature and Premixed Combustion with Late Injection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Low Temperature Combustion (LTC)?

**Low Temperature Combustion (LTC)** is an advanced, advanced compression-ignition combustion strategy in which the peak in-cylinder temperature is deliberately kept below the threshold required for the formation of significant quantities of thermal NO_x (typically below **~2000 K**) and soot. The combustion process relies on controlled auto-ignition of a highly diluted and/or highly premixed fuel-air mixture rather than on diffusion-controlled flame propagation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT752 - Module 4):** LTC encompasses multiple sub-modes, namely **HCCI** (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition), **PCCI** (Premixed Charge Compression Ignition), **RCCI** (Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition), and **PPC** (Partially Premixed Combustion). The defining feature is **NOT** the type of fuel delivery, but the **low peak temperature and high degree of premixing**.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of a campfire: traditional diesel combustion is like throwing a wet log onto a roaring fire — you get uneven, smoky, sooty combustion locally. **LTC is like carefully spreading finely powdered, well-mixed sawdust over a thin layer of glowing embers** — the air and fuel are already mixed, the temperature is moderate, the burn is slow, clean, and almost invisible. No big flames, no black smoke, just a clean, controlled thermal release.

## 1.2 What is Premixed Combustion with Late Injection (PCL/LI)?

**Premixed Combustion with Late Injection (PCL/LI)** is a specific subset of Partially Premixed Combustion (PPC) in which fuel is injected **late in the compression stroke** (typically after **60°–90° CA BTDC** and even up to or after TDC). The fuel jet must:

1. Evaporate, mix with air, and form a partially premixed cloud during the remaining crank-angle window.
2. Then auto-ignite once conditions of temperature, pressure, and mixture reactivity are met.

> [!NOTE]
> **Late Injection (LI)** does not mean injection after TDC during expansion — it means *late relative to the conventional diesel injection timing* (which is usually 8°–20° BTDC). PCL/LI sits **between** fully premixed HCCI (very early injection) and conventional diesel (very early-to-mid injection).

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are cooking a thick curry in a pressure cooker. If you add all the spices at the very start (early injection → diffusion controlled diesel), the result is harsh and aromatic. If you add them 80% through the cooking time (late injection → PCL/LI), the spices slowly dissolve and blend, giving a **uniform, smooth, rich** flavor. In PCL/LI, the fuel "blends with the air" before it decides to burn — this is the essence.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Diesel) | **14.5** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Gasoline) | **14.7** | kg air / kg fuel |
| LTC Peak Cycle Temperature | < **2000** | K |
| Conventional Diesel Peak Temperature | **2500 – 2800** | K |
| LTC Ignition Delay | **5 – 25** | ms / °CA |
| Late Injection Timing Window | **−60 to +30** | °CA ATDC |
| Auto-Ignition Threshold (cool flame) | **~850 – 1000** | K |
| NTC Region | **~900 – 1300** | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All LTC modes rely on **low-temperature chemistry** (cool flames) and the **Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC)** region of the fuel's auto-ignition chemistry, NOT on flame structure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of LTC

For any combustion mode to be classified as LTC, **all three** of the following conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

1. **High Dilution (EGR / Excess Air):** The in-cylinder mixture contains a large fraction of inert gases (exhaust gas recirculation, EGR) or excess air. Effective local equivalence ratio is kept **well below 1** in most regions.
2. **High Degree of Premixing:** The fuel and oxidizer are mixed at the molecular level *before* significant heat release begins. No fuel-rich diffusion flames exist.
3. **Premature Quench of Peak Temperature:** The peak combustion temperature is suppressed — typically by dilution, by staged combustion, or by using a low-reactivity fuel — so it does not exceed the **NO_x formation threshold (~2000 K)**.

## 2.2 Two-Stage Auto-Ignition Chemistry

Unlike conventional diesel combustion (which is essentially a one-step high-temperature ignition), LTC depends on the **two-stage ignition** phenomenon governed by the low-temperature and intermediate-temperature chemistry of hydrocarbon fuels.

### Stage 1: Cool Flame (Low-Temperature Oxidation)

- At temperatures around **850 K – 1000 K**, the fuel molecule reacts with O₂ to form alkyl-peroxy radicals:
  $$ \text{RH} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{R} \cdot + \text{HO}_2 \cdot $$
  $$ \text{R} \cdot + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO} \cdot \rightarrow \text{QOOH} \rightarrow \text{Ketohydroperoxides} $$
- Ketohydroperoxides decompose rapidly to release heat, producing a **cool flame** (T rise of **50 – 200 K**).
- This stage **advances** the chemistry: the mixture becomes "primed" for the second stage.

### Stage 2: Hot Flame (High-Temperature Ignition)

- The intermediate species decompose rapidly, releasing the bulk of chemical energy:
  $$ \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{OH} \cdot \quad \text{(chain branching)} $$
- This causes a sharp temperature rise to peak values.

## 2.3 The Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) Region

Between Stage 1 and Stage 2, an interesting behavior is observed in the **~900 K – 1300 K** range:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NTC Phenomenon:** As temperature *increases* in this window, the **ignition delay actually *increases*** (or equivalently, the reaction rate decreases). This is because the alkyl-peroxy radical (ROO·) becomes unstable and reverts to the alkyl radical + O₂, suppressing the low-temperature branching pathway.

For an LTC engine operating in the NTC region, this is **good news**: it gives more time for fuel-air mixing, and it also means a **longer ignition delay**, which translates into a higher degree of premixing before combustion starts.

## 2.4 Classification of LTC Modes (Analytical Matrix)

| Mode | Fuel Type | Injection Timing | Mixing Time | Reactivity Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HCCI** | Single fuel (gasoline, diesel, alcohol) | Very Early (Intake) | Longest | Stratified temperature / equivalence ratio |
| **PCCI** | Diesel | Early-Mid Compression | Long | Multiple injections, EGR |
| **RCCI** | Dual fuel (low + high reactivity) | Port + Direct | Longest | Reactivity stratification |
| **PPC / PCL-LI** | Diesel | Late (60° BTDC → TDC) | Moderate | EGR + Late injection |
| **CDC** (Conventional) | Diesel | Early (8°–20° BTDC) | Short | Diffusion controlled |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{max} < 2000$ K | NO_x suppression criterion | K |
| 2 | $\phi_{local} = \dfrac{(F/A)_{local}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Local equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\tau_{ig} = \tau_{cool} + \tau_{NTC} + \tau_{hot}$ | Total ignition delay (3-stage) | ms |
| 4 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_c$ | Heat release rate | W |
| 5 | $\text{NO}_{x} \propto \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R\,T_{peak}}\right)$ | Zeldovich mechanism (extended) | ppm |
| 6 | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{wall}}{Q_{total}}$ | Combustion efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\theta_{inj, late} = -60° \text{ to } +30°$ | Late injection window | °CA |
| 8 | $F_{EGR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{EGR}}{\dot{m}_{air}+\dot{m}_{EGR}}$ | EGR fraction | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Soot} \propto \phi^{n}\,T^{-m}$ | Empirical soot formation | mg/m³ |
| 10 | $\text{HRR} = \dfrac{\kappa\,P}{R\,T}\cdot A_{flame}\cdot v_{flame}$ | Flame propagation HRR | J/°CA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** LTC and PCL/LI are the foundation of the modern **BS-VI, Euro-VI, and US-EPA Tier 4 Final** emission norms for heavy-duty diesel engines. They are also the enabler for **renewable fuels** (methanol, ethanol, HVO, ammonia) in compression-ignition platforms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Peak Temperature is Suppressed in LTC

### Step 1: Start from the First Law for an Open System (Cylinder Control Volume)

For a closed combustion chamber with negligible mass flow during combustion:

$$ \Delta U = Q_{in} - W_{out} $$

### Step 2: Express the Internal Energy Change

Assuming the working fluid behaves as an ideal gas mixture:

$$ \Delta U = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_{peak} - T_{0}) $$

where $T_0$ is the start of combustion temperature.

### Step 3: Substitute the Released Chemical Energy

The chemical energy released by combustion is:

$$ Q_{in} = m_f \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{comb} $$

where $m_f$ is the mass of fuel burned and $\eta_{comb}$ is the combustion efficiency.

### Step 4: Combine and Solve for Peak Temperature

Equating the energy terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_{peak} - T_{0}) &= m_f \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{comb} \\
T_{peak} &= T_{0} + \dfrac{m_f \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{comb}}{m \cdot c_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Account for Dilution (EGR / Excess Air) Effect

If a fraction $F_{EGR}$ of the in-cylinder gas is inert (from EGR) or if excess air is used, the effective specific heat rises and the mass participating in heating the charge increases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{v,eff} &= c_{v,air} \cdot (1 - F_{EGR}) + c_{v,EGR} \cdot F_{EGR} \\
m_{eff} &= m_{air} + m_{EGR} + m_f
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{peak,LTC} &= T_{0} + \dfrac{m_f \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{comb}}{m_{eff} \cdot c_{v,eff}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Interpretation

> A **higher $m_{eff}$** (more diluent mass) and a **higher $c_{v,eff}$** (more inert heat capacity) both **lower** $T_{peak}$. This is the **fundamental thermodynamic reason** why dilution drives peak temperatures down and consequently suppresses both NO_x and soot.

### Step 7: Numerical Illustration (KTU-style Calculation)

Given:
- $T_0 = 900$ K
- $m_f = 0.005$ kg per cycle
- LHV of diesel = $43 \times 10^6$ J/kg
- $\eta_{comb} = 0.98$
- $m_{air} = 0.072$ kg (stoichiometric for the given fuel)
- $c_{v,air} = 717$ J/kg·K
- $F_{EGR} = 0$ (no EGR, conventional case)

**Conventional Diesel Peak Temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{peak,conv} &= 900 + \dfrac{0.005 \times 43 \times 10^6 \times 0.98}{0.072 \times 717} \\
&= 900 + \dfrac{210700}{51.624} \\
&= 900 + 4081 \\
&\approx 4981 \text{ K} \quad \text{(theoretical, no losses)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In real engines, this is observed around **2500 – 2800 K** due to dissociation, heat losses, and finite-rate chemistry.

**Now with EGR of $F_{EGR} = 0.4$ and $c_{v,EGR} = 850$ J/kg·K:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{EGR} &= 0.4 \times (0.072 + 0.005) = 0.0308 \text{ kg} \\
c_{v,eff} &= 717 \times 0.6 + 850 \times 0.4 = 430.2 + 340 = 770.2 \text{ J/kg·K} \\
m_{eff} &= 0.072 + 0.005 + 0.0308 = 0.1078 \text{ kg} \\
T_{peak,LTC} &= 900 + \dfrac{210700}{0.1078 \times 770.2} \\
&= 900 + \dfrac{210700}{83.03} \\
&= 900 + 2537 \\
&\approx 3437 \text{ K (idealized)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Real LTC engines with the same fuel reach about **1800 – 1950 K** at peak, well below the NO_x threshold — confirming the theoretical foundation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalence Ratio Stratification in PCL/LI

In Premixed Combustion with Late Injection, the fuel jet enters late in compression. The local equivalence ratio at any spatial point $x$ and time $t$ can be modeled as a Gaussian distribution from the spray axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(x, t) &= \phi_{max}(t) \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{2\sigma^2(t)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\phi_{max}(t)$ is the peak equivalence ratio at the spray axis (time-dependent)
- $\sigma(t)$ is the spray width (grows with time due to turbulent mixing)

> **KTU Insight:** When $\sigma$ is large (long mixing time, e.g., due to earlier injection), $\phi$ approaches a uniform low value → **fully premixed**. When $\sigma$ is small (short mixing time, e.g., very late injection), the spray is concentrated → **partially premixed** (this is the PCL/LI regime). When $\sigma \rightarrow 0$ and injection is right at auto-ignition, it becomes **diffusion-controlled** (conventional diesel).

### Critical Delay-to-Injection Relationship

Define the **mixing time** $t_{mix}$ and the **ignition delay** $\tau_{ig}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Degree of Premixing} \propto \dfrac{\min(\tau_{ig},\, t_{window})}{\tau_{ig}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $t_{window}$ is the time between end of injection and start of combustion. For PCL/LI, this is by design **less than full HCCI premixing** but **much more than conventional diffusion** — hence the term **"partially premixed"**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an LTC / PCL-LI Engine System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Intake] --> B[Turbocharger / Compressor]
    C[Exhaust Gas] --> D[EGR Cooler]
    D --> E[EGR Mixer]
    B --> E
    E --> F[In-Cylinder Charge]
    F --> G[Fuel Injector - Late Timing]
    G --> H[Partial Mixing During Compression]
    H --> I[Auto-Ignition Two-Stage]
    I --> J[Combustion Peak T less than 2000K]
    J --> K[Power Stroke]
    K --> L[Exhaust]
    L --> D

    M[ECU Control Unit] --> N[Injection Timing Map]
    N --> G
    M --> O[EGR Valve Position]
    O --> D
    M --> P[Intake Boost Pressure]
    P --> B

    Q[Temperature Sensor] --> M
    R[Pressure Sensor] --> M
    S[Lambda Sensor] --> M
```

## 4.2 Ignition Chemistry Flow (Two-Stage Process)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh Charge T less than 850K] --> B[Stage 1 Cool Flame 850K to 1000K]
    B --> C[Ketohydroperoxide Formation]
    C --> D[NTC Region 900K to 1300K]
    D --> E[Stage 2 Hot Ignition above 1300K]
    E --> F[Rapid Heat Release]
    F --> G[Peak T less than 2000K in LTC]
    G --> H[Clean Combustion]
```

## 4.3 PCL-LI Injection Strategy Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title PCL-LI Injection and Combustion Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section Compression Stroke
    Piston Compresses Charge     :a1, 0, 180

    section Late Injection Event
    Start of Injection at 60 BTDC :a2, 120, 30
    Spray Development             :a3, 125, 35
    Fuel Evaporation              :a4, 130, 30

    section Auto-Ignition and Combustion
    Cool Flame Stage 1            :a5, 155, 15
    NTC Delay                     :a6, 170, 10
    Hot Ignition Stage 2          :a7, 180, 20

```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Conventional vs PCC vs PCL-LI vs HCCI

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONV[Conventional Diesel]
        C1[Early Inj 8 to 20 BTDC] --> C2[Short Mixing Time]
        C2 --> C3[Diffusion Flame] --> C4[High T peak 2500K plus] --> C5[High NOX and Soot]
    end

    subgraph PCC[Premixed Charge Compression]
        P1[Early Inj 30 to 60 BTDC] --> P2[Long Mixing Time] --> P3[Mostly Premixed] --> P4[Moderate T peak 2100K] --> P5[Low Soot Moderate NOX]
    end

    subgraph PCLLI[PCL with Late Injection]
        L1[Late Inj 60 BTDC to TDC] --> L2[Moderate Mixing] --> L3[Partially Premixed] --> L4[T peak 1800 to 2000K] --> L5[Low NOX Low Soot]
    end

    subgraph HCCI[Homogeneous Charge CI]
        H1[In-Cylinder or Port Inj] --> H2[Very Long Mixing] --> H3[Fully Premixed] --> H4[T peak 1700 to 1900K] --> H5[Low NOX Some CO and HC]
    end
```
