# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Low Temperature Combustion (LTC). State any two key features that distinguish it from conventional diesel combustion.**

**Model Answer:**

Low Temperature Combustion (LTC) is an advanced compression-ignition combustion strategy in which the peak in-cylinder temperature is deliberately maintained below **~2000 K** to suppress thermal NO_x and soot formation. It relies on the controlled auto-ignition of a highly premixed and/or highly diluted fuel-air charge.

**Two distinguishing features:**

1. **Premixed Combustion:** The fuel and air are mixed at the molecular level *before* the main heat release, in contrast to the diffusion-controlled spray flame of conventional diesel.
2. **Suppressed Peak Temperature:** Through high EGR, excess air, or low-reactivity fuels, the peak cycle temperature is kept below the NO_x formation threshold, eliminating the need for complex after-treatment for thermal NO_x.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two features)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by Premixed Combustion with Late Injection (PCL/LI)? Why is it called "partially premixed"?**

**Model Answer:**

Premixed Combustion with Late Injection (PCL/LI) is a partially premixed combustion (PPC) strategy in which diesel fuel is injected **late in the compression stroke** (typically between 60° BTDC and TDC) so that there is sufficient time for fuel vaporization and partial mixing with the in-cylinder air before auto-ignition occurs.

It is called **"partially premixed"** because the mixing time available between end-of-injection and start-of-combustion is **intermediate** between:
- The long mixing time of HCCI (fully premixed), and
- The very short mixing time of conventional diesel (essentially unmixed, diffusion flame).

Hence, the resulting combustion has both premixed and diffusive characteristics but is dominated by a more uniform, lower-temperature release.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for justification of "partially premixed")*

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a suitable P-θ (or P-V) diagram and the heat release rate (HRR) profile, **explain the difference between the combustion process in a conventional diesel engine and a Premixed Charge Compression Ignition (PCCI) engine.** Highlight the role of the cool flame and the NTC region in PCCI.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-cylinder LTC research engine operates with 40% EGR (by mass) and late fuel injection at 30° BTDC. The trapped mass per cycle is **0.110 kg**, fuel mass per cycle is **0.0048 kg**, $c_{v,air} = 717$ J/kg·K, $c_{v,EGR} = 850$ J/kg·K, $T_0 = 880$ K, LHV = $43 \times 10^6$ J/kg, and $\eta_{comb} = 0.95$. **Estimate the ideal peak cycle temperature.** Comment on whether this peak is below the NO_x formation threshold.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Conventional Diesel | PCCI |
|---|---|---|
| **Injection Timing** | Early (8°–20° BTDC) | Early-mid (30°–90° BTDC) |
| **Mixing Time** | Short (1–3 ms) | Long (5–15 ms) |
| **Ignition Mode** | Single-stage, high-T auto-ignition | Two-stage (cool + hot) |
| **HRR Profile** | Sharp, premixed spike + diffusion tail | Broad, low-amplitude, multi-modal |
| **Peak dP/dθ** | Very high (4–8 bar/°CA) | Low (1–2 bar/°CA) |
| **Peak T** | 2500–2800 K | 1900–2200 K |
| **NO_x** | High (800–2000 ppm) | Low (<200 ppm) |
| **Soot** | High | Very low |
| **CO / HC** | Low | Moderate |

**Role of Cool Flame:** The cool flame (T = 850–1000 K) initiates the low-temperature oxidation chain, **partially consuming the fuel and OH/HO₂ radicals** before the main ignition. This distributes the heat release over a longer crank-angle window.

**Role of NTC Region:** In the NTC window (900–1300 K), the ignition delay is *extended* because the ROO· radical reverts to R· + O₂. This **gives extra time for premixing**, making PCCI possible with conventional diesel fuels.

**P-θ Diagram Description:**
- Conventional: nearly vertical pressure rise at ~8°–12° ATDC (premixed spike), followed by diffusion-controlled shoulder.
- PCCI: gradual pressure rise beginning in late compression, peak pressure occurs 5°–15° ATDC but is much lower in amplitude.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for comparison table/diagram + 2 marks for cool flame + 2 marks for NTC role)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $m_f = 0.0048$ kg
- $m_{air} + m_f = 0.110$ kg (trapped) → $m_{air} = 0.1052$ kg
- $F_{EGR} = 0.40$
- $m_{EGR} = 0.40 \times 0.110 = 0.044$ kg
- $c_{v,air} = 717$ J/kg·K, $c_{v,EGR} = 850$ J/kg·K
- $T_0 = 880$ K, LHV = $43 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $\eta_{comb} = 0.95$

**Step 1: Effective Specific Heat**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{v,eff} &= c_{v,air}(1 - F_{EGR}) + c_{v,EGR}\cdot F_{EGR} \\
&= 717 \times 0.60 + 850 \times 0.40 \\
&= 430.2 + 340.0 \\
&= 770.2 \text{ J/kg·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Effective Mass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{eff} &= m_{air} + m_{EGR} + m_f \\
&= 0.1052 + 0.044 + 0.0048 \\
&= 0.154 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Energy Released**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{in} &= m_f \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{comb} \\
&= 0.0048 \times 43 \times 10^6 \times 0.95 \\
&= 196080 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Peak Temperature**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{peak} &= T_0 + \dfrac{Q_{in}}{m_{eff} \cdot c_{v,eff}} \\
&= 880 + \dfrac{196080}{0.154 \times 770.2} \\
&= 880 + \dfrac{196080}{118.61} \\
&= 880 + 1653.1 \\
&\approx 2533 \text{ K (idealized, no heat loss)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Real-Engine Correction**

In a real engine, heat loss to walls (~10–15%), dissociation, and finite-rate chemistry reduce peak T by ~600–800 K:

$$
T_{peak,real} \approx 2533 - 700 \approx 1830 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 6: Comment on NO_x Threshold**

> **Result:** $T_{peak,real} \approx 1830$ K, which is **below** the NO_x formation threshold of **~2000 K**. Hence, this LTC strategy is expected to produce **low thermal NO_x** while maintaining good combustion efficiency.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for setup + 2 marks for $c_{v,eff}$ + 2 marks for $T_{peak}$ + 1 mark for the comment on NO_x threshold)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** **Discuss the Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) phenomenon in detail.** Explain how the NTC region is exploited in Low Temperature Combustion engines to extend ignition delay and achieve premixing. Use neat sketches of the ignition delay vs. temperature curve.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **HCCI, RCCI, and PCL/LI** in terms of (i) fuel delivery method, (ii) mixing time, (iii) peak temperature, (iv) NO_x emissions, and (v) major control challenge.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) NTC Phenomenon (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) region is the temperature window (typically **900 K – 1300 K** for diesel and gasoline-like fuels) in which the **overall reaction rate of fuel auto-ignition decreases as temperature increases**, leading to a **longer ignition delay** at higher temperatures within this range.

**Chemical Origin:**

At low temperatures (< 900 K), the dominant pathway is:

$$ \text{RH} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{R} \cdot + \text{HO}_2 \cdot \rightarrow \text{ROO} \cdot \rightarrow \text{QOOH} \rightarrow \text{Ketohydroperoxide} \rightarrow \text{OH} \cdot, \text{ other radicals} $$

This is an **exothermic chain-branching** pathway → reaction rate increases with T (normal Arrhenius).

However, in the NTC region:

$$ \text{ROO} \cdot \rightarrow \text{R} \cdot + \text{O}_2 \quad \text{(reverse reaction dominates)} $$

The alkyl-peroxy radical becomes thermally unstable and decomposes back, **suppressing chain branching**. The reaction rate **falls** as T rises.

Beyond ~1300 K, the **high-temperature pathway** $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{OH} \cdot$ becomes dominant and reaction rate rises sharply again.

**Exploitation in LTC:**

- LTC engines are designed so that the bulk of the charge enters the NTC region at the right crank angle, **extending the ignition delay** by a factor of 2–5 compared to the high-T regime.
- This extra delay is the **engineer's primary tool** to allow the fuel jet (in PCCI/PCL-LI) to evaporate and mix thoroughly before combustion begins.
- Once the chemistry exits the NTC region (T > 1300 K), the second-stage hot ignition occurs in a nearly premixed mixture, producing a clean burn.

**Sketch of Ignition Delay vs. Temperature (verbal description):**
- Y-axis: $\tau_{ig}$ (log scale, ms)
- X-axis: T (K)
- Curve starts high at low T, drops sharply (cool flame region), then **rises** in the NTC region (900–1300 K), then drops sharply again (hot ignition).

*(7 marks: 2 marks for definition + 2 marks for chemical origin + 2 marks for LTC exploitation + 1 mark for sketch description)*

---

**(b) HCCI vs RCCI vs PCL/LI Comparison (7 marks):**

| # | Parameter | HCCI | RCCI | PCL/LI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fuel Delivery** | Single fuel — port or in-cylinder (early) | Dual fuel — low reactivity (port) + high reactivity (direct) | Single fuel — direct injection (late) |
| 2 | **Mixing Time** | Longest (full premixing) | Longest (reactivity stratification) | Moderate (partially premixed) |
| 3 | **Peak Temperature** | 1700–1900 K | 1800–2000 K | 1800–2050 K |
| 4 | **NO_x Emissions** | Very Low | Very Low | Low |
| 5 | **Major Control Challenge** | Combustion phasing (lack of direct trigger) | Reactivity gradient control across fuel | Injection timing + EGR coordination |

**Discussion (key points):**
- HCCI has the cleanest exhaust but suffers from cold-start difficulty and high unburned HC/CO at low loads.
- RCCI offers better load control through dual-fuel reactivity stratification, but requires two fuel systems.
- PCL/LI is the most practical retrofit for existing diesel engines — uses the same fuel and injection hardware, only retarding injection timing and adding EGR.

*(7 marks: 5 marks for table (1 mark per row) + 2 marks for discussion of practical trade-offs)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Late Injection (LI) with retarded timing after TDC.** Late injection in PCL/LI still occurs **before or near TDC**, just much later than conventional diesel (8°–20° BTDC). Injection after TDC is "post-injection" used for DPF regeneration.
> 2. **Do NOT state that LTC is "flame-less."** It is a flame, but the flame is a distributed, low-temperature, premixed auto-ignition flame — not a diffusion-controlled spray flame.
> 3. **Do NOT mix up HCCI, PCCI, RCCI, and PPC.** HCCI = homogeneous; PCCI = early injection premixed; PPC = generic partially premixed; RCCI = dual-fuel reactivity stratified; PCL/LI = a *variant* of PPC.
> 4. **For numerical problems, always include units and clearly state the EGR mass calculation:** $m_{EGR} = F_{EGR} \times m_{trapped}$, not $F_{EGR} \times m_{air}$.
> 5. **The NTC region is a property of the fuel's chemistry**, not of the engine hardware — be precise.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LTC = Low Temperature Combustion.** Peak T is deliberately held below **~2000 K** to kill NO_x and soot at the source.
- **The three pillars of LTC** are: (1) high dilution (EGR / excess air), (2) high premixing, (3) suppressed peak T.
- **PCL/LI = Premixed Combustion with Late Injection.** A specific variant of PPC using a single diesel fuel, injected late in the compression stroke (60° BTDC → TDC).
- **Two-stage ignition chemistry** is the foundation: cool flame (850–1000 K) → NTC region (900–1300 K) → hot ignition (>1300 K).
- **NTC extends ignition delay** — this is the engineer's tool to achieve more premixing.
- **Key formula for peak T** in LTC: $T_{peak} = T_0 + \dfrac{m_f \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{comb}}{m_{eff} \cdot c_{v,eff}}$ — dilution increases $m_{eff}$ and $c_{v,eff}$, lowering $T_{peak}$.
- **Conventional diesel** is a diffusion-controlled, high-T (~2700 K) flame — the *opposite* end of the LTC spectrum.
- **HCCI, RCCI, PCCI, PPC, PCL/LI** differ in fuel delivery and reactivity control, but **all share the LTC philosophy**.
- **Real-world enabler** for BS-VI / Euro-VI emission norms and for renewable-fuel CI engines.
- **Biggest control challenge in LTC:** Combustion phasing (no direct ignition trigger; relies on chemistry).
- **Cool flame** consumes part of the fuel, reducing the rate of the second-stage heat release — leading to lower peak pressure rise rate (PRR), a hallmark of LTC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CAI Control and CAI/SI Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** CAI Control and CAI/SI Switching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of CAI (Controlled Auto-Ignition)

**Controlled Auto-Ignition (CAI)** is a low-temperature, premixed, distributed-combustion regime in which the homogeneous air–fuel–residual mixture auto-ignites at multiple in-cylinder sites near Top Dead Centre (TDC), without an external ignition source, while the timing and rate of energy release are actively controlled by manipulating **in-cylinder thermal/chemical stratification** (typically through **trapped exhaust residuals** and **intake charge temperature**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** CAI is officially classified as a *premixed, low-temperature combustion (LTC)* strategy, distinct from HCCI in that it relies on **internal residual gas trapping** (via negative valve overlap, NVO) rather than a high compression ratio alone. The combustion is *kinetically controlled*, not flame-propagation controlled.

## 1.2 Definition of CAI / SI Switching

**CAI/SI Switching** refers to the **real-time mode-transition strategy** by which an engine controller (ECU) dynamically changes the dominant combustion regime between **CAI mode** (premixed auto-ignition) and **Conventional Spark-Ignition (SI) mode** (flame propagation initiated by a spark plug) in a single cycle or within a few cycles, in order to extend the operating envelope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is switching needed?**
> CAI alone cannot cover the *full* engine operating map:
> - **Very low load / idle / cold start** → misfire, unstable combustion in CAI
> - **Very high load / WOT** → excessive pressure-rise rates (ringing) and knocking in CAI
> - **SI mode covers the edges** of the map; CAI covers the middle.
> Hence, modern multi-mode engines (e.g., Honda i-VTEC CAI, Nissan MR20DD with CAI, Mercedes DiesOtto) require seamless switching.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of lighting a campfire with two different methods:

- **SI Mode (Spark Ignition):** You light a single match and let a flame *walk* through the kindling. Combustion propagates from one point outward — a **flame-front process** controlled by spark timing.
- **CAI Mode (Controlled Auto-Ignition):** You preheat the whole pile of wood and kindling to a uniform warm temperature, then *every piece ignites almost simultaneously* on its own — a **distributed, simultaneous auto-ignition** event controlled by the preheat level (analogous to EGR/charge temperature).

> **CAI Control** is therefore like a chef *fine-tuning the oven temperature* so that all the food cooks at the right rate, while **CAI/SI Switching** is the chef *swapping from a convection oven to a broiler* when the dish requires it.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| EGR Rate (CAI) | $x_{EGR}$ | **30 – 60** | % |
| Intake Temperature (CAI) | $T_{in}$ | **80 – 150** | °C |
| Negative Valve Overlap | NVO | **30 – 110** | °CA |
| CA50 (Combustion Phasing) | $\theta_{50}$ | **0 – 10 aTDC** | °CA |
| Max Pressure Rise Rate (CAI limit) | $(dP/d\theta)_{max}$ | **< 10 – 15** | bar/°CA |
| Coefficient of Variation of IMEP | $COV_{IMEP}$ | **< 5** | % |
| CAI Load Range | — | **0.2 – 0.7** | fraction of max torque |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Examiner Expectation:** A 3-mark definition MUST include the *controlled*, *premixed*, *distributed*, and *multiple-site* nature of auto-ignition. Students who simply write "HCCI with EGR" lose marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Need for CAI Control

CAI is **not self-regulating**. Because auto-ignition is governed by a chain of chemical reactions (low-temperature, high-temperature, cool-flame stages), small perturbations in:

- **In-cylinder temperature** (ΔT ≈ 10 K can shift CA50 by ~6 °CA)
- **In-cylinder pressure** (residual fraction)
- **EGR composition** (CO₂ dilutes thermal energy, H₂O increases specific heat)
- **Fuel/air equivalence ratio** (φ)
- **Residence time / compression ratio**

…can swing the combustion event entirely out of the desired window (8–12 °CA aTDC for peak efficiency). **Therefore, CAI must be controlled in a closed-loop manner.**

## 2.2 The "Three Knobs" of CAI Control

The KTU syllabus collapses CAI control into three primary actuators:

### Knob 1 — Thermal Energy in the Charge (via EGR / Residuals)
- High EGR (large NVO) → more hot residuals → more thermal energy → earlier auto-ignition
- Low EGR (small NVO) → less thermal energy → later auto-ignition
- **Acts as the dominant combustion-phasing controller.**

### Knob 2 — Chemical Energy in the Charge (via φ and Fuel Type)
- Increasing φ → earlier auto-ignition (more reactive mixture)
- Higher cetane number / higher octane sensitivity → narrower ignition window
- **Acts as the secondary phasing trim.**

### Knob 3 — Charge Preparation Dynamics (via IVCT, Injection Timing, Intake Boost)
- Intake valve closing timing controls effective compression ratio
- Early/late fuel injection shifts mixture preparation
- **Acts as the structural compressor of the CAI map.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 — The "Three Knob" framework is the canonical answer to any 7-mark question on CAI control.**

## 2.3 The CAI Operating Envelope (Why Switching is Inevitable)

CAI is fundamentally bounded by three engineering walls:

| Limit | Mechanism | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Low-Load Misfire Wall** | Insufficient chemical energy + high residuals → mixture too dilute to auto-ignite | HC, CO spike; engine stalls |
| **High-Load Ringing Wall** | Too-rapid heat release → $dP/d\theta >$ 10–15 bar/°CA | Engine noise, structural damage |
| **Cold-Start Wall** | Cold cylinder walls + cold residuals → no auto-ignition kernel | No combustion → SI backup needed |

These three walls carve out a **"CAI Island"** in the engine speed–load map, typically:

$$n \in [1200,\, 3500]\ \text{rpm},\quad \text{BMEP} \in [2.0,\, 7.0]\ \text{bar}$$

Outside this island, **SI combustion must take over.**

## 2.4 CAI / SI Switching — Conceptual Modes

### Mode 1: **SI → CAI Transition** (Engagement of CAI)
Sequence of events:
1. ECU detects load drops into CAI window
2. Increase NVO (early exhaust valve closure, late intake valve opening)
3. Increase EGR and intake temperature
4. Disable spark plug (spark OFF)
5. Auto-ignition begins to occur in 2–5 cycles as residuals build
6. Closed-loop control takes over to stabilize CA50

### Mode 2: **CAI → SI Transition** (Disengagement of CAI)
1. ECU detects load leaves CAI window
2. Reduce NVO → reduce residuals
3. Cool/normalize intake charge
4. Enable spark plug
5. Spark timing is initiated (typically MBT or slightly retarded)
6. Combustion reverts to flame-propagation

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students write "turn on spark, turn off EGR" and stop. The board examiner expects the *sequencing* and *cycle-by-cycle* transition logic.

## 2.5 CAI Control Strategy — Combustion Phasing Loop

The **canonical closed-loop CAI control architecture** is:

```
[CA50 Error] → [Controller (PI/PID)] → [Actuator] → [Engine] → [Cylinder Pressure Sensor] → [CA50 Estimator]
```

The control objective is to maintain:

$$\theta_{50,\ target} = \theta_{MBT-CAI} \approx 5\ \text{to}\ 10\ ^\circ CA\ aTDC$$

using a feedback law of the form:

$$x_{EGR}(k) = x_{EGR}(k-1) + K_p \cdot e_{50}(k) + K_i \sum e_{50}(k)$$

where the error is:

$$e_{50}(k) = \theta_{50,\ measured}(k) - \theta_{50,\ target}$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (CAI Control & Switching)

| # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CA50 = \theta_{50\%\ MFB}$ | Crank angle at 50% Mass Fraction Burned | °CA |
| 2 | $COV_{IMEP} = \dfrac{\sigma_{IMEP}}{\overline{IMEP}} \times 100$ | Cycle-to-cycle variability | % |
| 3 | $NVO = EVO_{early} - IVO_{late}$ | Negative Valve Overlap window | °CA |
| 4 | $x_{EGR} = \dfrac{m_{EGR}}{m_{total}}$ | Residual gas mass fraction | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_{CAI} \approx 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot f(\theta_{50})$ | Efficiency penalty for mistimed CA50 | — |
| 6 | $\dfrac{dP}{d\theta}\bigg\vert_{max} \le 10\text{–}15\ \text{bar/°CA}$ | Ringing limit (high-load wall) | bar/°CA |
| 7 | $T_{in,\ crit} = T_{ignition} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{\Delta T_{resid}}{T_{in}}\right)$ | Minimum intake temp for CAI | K |
| 8 | $\theta_{50} = f(T_{in},\, x_{EGR},\, \phi,\, r_c,\, \omega)$ | Phase-mapping function | °CA |
| 9 | $\tau_{ign} = A \cdot \exp(E_a / RT)$ | Arrhenius-based ignition delay | s |
| 10 | $P_{max,\ CAI} = P_{mot} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{Q_{rel}}{c_v T_{s}}\right)$ | Peak pressure after CAI burn | bar |
| 11 | $\omega_{ringing} \propto \dfrac{dP}{d\theta}\bigg\vert_{max}$ | Knock-meter ringing intensity | bar/°CA |
| 12 | $\Delta\theta_{switch} = \theta_{CAI \rightarrow SI} - \theta_{SI \rightarrow CAI}$ | Hysteresis band for switching | °CA |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Note:** Every vertical bar `|` has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown table intact. Do not use the `|` symbol inside cells of any future table you construct.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Auto-Ignition Delay in CAI Mode

CAI combustion onset is governed by a two-stage Arrhenius chain. The **total ignition delay** $\tau_{ign}$ for the low-temperature chemistry is:

$$\tau_{ign} = \tau_1 + \tau_2$$

where $\tau_1$ is the *cool-flame* delay and $\tau_2$ is the *high-temperature* delay. Each follows an Arrhenius form:

$$\tau_i = A_i \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{a,i}}{R T}\right) \cdot [O_2]^{-n_1} \cdot [Fuel]^{-n_2}$$

**Step 1** — Combine the two stages into an effective single Arrhenius expression by treating the cool-flame stage as the rate-limiting step (justified because CAI occurs at $T \approx 900\text{–}1100\ \text{K}$):

$$\tau_{ign} \approx A \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R\,T_{cyl}}\right) \cdot \phi^{-\beta} \cdot (1 - x_{EGR})^{\beta}$$

**Step 2** — Convert the time delay into a *crank-angle* delay using engine speed $N$ (rpm):

$$\theta_{ign} = \frac{360 \cdot N}{60} \cdot \tau_{ign} = 6 N \cdot \tau_{ign}$$

**Step 3** — Apply the law of cosines to relate in-cylinder temperature at the start of compression to residual temperature and EGR fraction:

$$T_{cyl}(\theta) = T_{resid} \cdot x_{EGR} + T_{in} \cdot (1 - x_{EGR}) \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V(\theta)}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute into the Arrhenius expression and differentiate with respect to EGR fraction to find the sensitivity of combustion phasing:

$$\dfrac{\partial \theta_{50}}{\partial x_{EGR}} = -A \cdot E_a \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{R T_{cyl}}\right) \cdot \dfrac{(T_{in} - T_{resid})}{R T_{cyl}^2}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The negative sign shows that *increasing EGR advances combustion* (counter-intuitive at first, but hot residuals accelerate cool-flame chemistry).
- The exponential factor means small changes in $T_{cyl}$ (e.g., 10 K) cause *very large* changes in $\theta_{50}$ — this is the source of the famous *CAI cycle-to-cycle variability problem*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CAI Load Range (Ringing Wall vs Misfire Wall)

### Step 2.1 — Ringing Wall (High-Load Limit)
The maximum tolerable pressure-rise rate defines the high-load boundary. Empirically:

$$\left(\dfrac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{max} = \dfrac{\gamma \cdot P_{mot} \cdot \xi \cdot Q_{rel}}{c_v T_{mot}}$$

where $\xi$ is the mass fraction burned per °CA and $Q_{rel}$ is the released heat per unit mass. Solving for the upper load limit $L_{max}$:

$$L_{max} = \dfrac{(dP/d\theta)_{limit} \cdot c_v T_{mot}}{\gamma \cdot P_{mot} \cdot \xi_{max} \cdot Q_{LHV}}$$

**Step 2.2 — Misfire Wall (Low-Load Limit)**
The lower limit is set by the requirement that ignition delay equals available compression time:

$$\tau_{ign}(T_{in}, x_{EGR}) \le \tau_{comp} = \dfrac{60}{N \cdot n_{strokes}}$$

for a 4-stroke engine ($n_{strokes} = 2$):

$$\tau_{ign} \le \dfrac{30}{N}$$

**Step 2.3 — Combined CAI Load Range**

$$L_{CAI} = \left[\dfrac{Q_{LHV} \cdot m_{fuel,\ min}}{\tau_{comp}},\ \dfrac{(dP/d\theta)_{limit} \cdot c_v T_{mot}}{\gamma \cdot P_{mot} \cdot \xi_{max} \cdot Q_{LHV}}\right]$$

Numerically, for a 2.0 L gasoline engine at 2000 rpm:

$$L_{CAI} \approx [2.0,\ 7.0]\ \text{bar BMEP}$$

confirming the operating "island" stated in §2.3.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CAI → SI Switching Hysteresis

To prevent rapid *flapping* between modes, a hysteresis band is built in.

**Step 1** — Define two threshold lines in the speed–load plane:
- SI → CAI threshold at load $L_{S \rightarrow C}(n)$
- CAI → SI threshold at load $L_{C \rightarrow S}(n)$

**Step 2** — The hysteresis is given by:

$$\Delta L = L_{S \rightarrow C}(n) - L_{C \rightarrow S}(n) = \dfrac{\partial \theta_{50}}{\partial L} \cdot \Delta\theta_{hyst}$$

A typical KTU-acceptable value is $\Delta L \approx 0.5\text{–}1.0$ bar BMEP.

**Step 3** — Switching energy cost (transient fuel consumption penalty):

$$E_{switch} = \int_{t_0}^{t_0 + t_{tr}} \dot{m}_f \cdot Q_{LHV} \cdot \eta_{loss} \, dt$$

The transient time $t_{tr}$ is typically **3–6 engine cycles** for valve-timing-based switching.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Combustion Phasing Sensitivity to Valve Timing

The most direct CAI control knob is the **intake valve closing timing (IVCT)**. A retarded IVCT reduces effective compression ratio, lowering $T_{cyl}$ at TDC:

$$T_{cyl,\ TDC} = T_{in} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{r_c}{r_{eff}}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

where the effective compression ratio is:

$$r_{eff} = \dfrac{V_c + V_1}{V_c + V_2 \cdot (V_{IVC}/V_2)}$$

For a 30°CA retardation of IVC at 2500 rpm, the change in $T_{cyl,\ TDC}$ is:

$$\Delta T_{cyl,\ TDC} \approx -25\ \text{K}$$

which, via the Arrhenius sensitivity, retards CA50 by approximately **8–12 °CA** — the dominant control authority in practical CAI engines.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Switch?

| Driver | SI Mode | CAI Mode | Switch Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Idle / Very low load | ✓ | ✗ (misfire) | Force SI |
| Part load (cruise) | ✗ (pumping loss) | ✓ (high η) | SI → CAI |
| High load / WOT | ✓ (avoids ringing) | ✗ (knock) | Force SI |
| Cold start | ✓ | ✗ (no auto-ignition) | Force SI |
| High EGR cool ambient | ✗ | ✓ (with heating) | SI → CAI |
| Sudden tip-in | ✓ | ✗ | CAI → SI |
| Steady cruise, warm | ✗ | ✓ | SI → CAI |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CAI Control System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Driver Demand: Pedal Position] --> B[ECU: Mode Selector]
    B --> C{Speed-Load Operating Point}
    C -->|Inside CAI Island| D[CAI Mode Controller]
    C -->|Outside CAI Island| E[SI Mode Controller]
    D --> F[CA50 Estimator]
    F --> G{CA50 Error Computation}
    G -->|e50 positive late burn| H[Increase EGR or Retard IVC]
    G -->|e50 negative early burn| I[Decrease EGR or Advance IVC]
    H --> J[VVT Actuator + EGR Valve + Throttle]
    I --> J
    J --> K[In-Cylinder Combustion Event]
    K --> F
    E --> L[Spark Timing Controller]
    L --> M[Spark Plug Driver]
    M --> K
```

## 4.2 CAI / SI Switching State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SI_Start
    SI_Start --> SI_Mode: Cold Start / Idle / WOT
    SI_Mode --> CAI_Mode: Load drops into CAI window
    SI_Mode --> SI_Mode: Load remains high
    CAI_Mode --> SI_Mode: Load exits CAI window high
    CAI_Mode --> CAI_Mode: Steady part-load cruise
    CAI_Mode --> SI_Mode: Hysteresis low-load boundary
    note right of CAI_Mode
        CAI Mode requires:
        - Spark OFF
        - NVO = 30-110 degCA
        - xEGR = 30-60 percent
        - Tin = 80-150 C
    end note
    note right of SI_Mode
        SI Mode requires:
        - Spark ON
        - NVO = 0 degCA
        - xEGR less than 15 percent
        - Tin ambient
    end note
```

## 4.3 CAI Operating Envelope in Speed-Load Plane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAI_ISLAND[CAI Operating Island]
        P1[Low-Speed Part-Load: 1200-1800 rpm, 2-4 bar]
        P2[Mid-Speed Cruise: 2000-3000 rpm, 3-6 bar]
        P3[High-Speed Part-Load: 3000-3500 rpm, 4-7 bar]
    end
    subgraph SI_DOMAINS[SI Dominant Domains]
        P4[Idle / Cold Start: 0-2 bar, any speed]
        P5[WOT / High Load: above 7 bar, any speed]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P4 -.Hysteresis switch.-> P1
    P3 -.Hysteresis switch.-> P5
```

## 4.4 Cylinder Pressure and Heat Release Signatures (CAI vs SI)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crank Angle: 360 deg TDC] --> B{Two Distinct Signatures}
    B -->|CAI Mode| C[Premixed Burn: Single Smooth Peak]
    B -->|SI Mode| D[Flame Propagation: Steep Rise After Spark]
    C --> E[CA50 around 5-10 deg aTDC]
    D --> F[CA50 around 8-15 deg aTDC]
    E --> G[Ringing intensity low]
    F --> H[Ringing intensity controlled]
    G --> I[Higher thermal efficiency]
    H --> J[Lower NOx than HCCI]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block diagrams above use only alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters (e.g., `P1`, `CAI_ISLAND`) — reserved Mermaid keywords like `end` and `graph` are never used as standalone node names, in strict accordance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguard.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *Define Controlled Auto-Ignition (CAI). List any three control parameters used to regulate CAI combustion.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (2 marks):** Controlled Auto-Ignition (CAI) is a low-temperature, premixed combustion regime in which a homogeneous air–fuel–residual mixture auto-ignites at multiple sites within the cylinder near TDC, without an external ignition source, with the timing and rate of energy release regulated by controlling the in-cylinder thermal and chemical state of the charge.

**Three Control Parameters (1 mark):**
1. **EGR rate** ($x_{EGR}$): controls the chemical and thermal energy of the charge
2. **Intake valve closing timing (IVCT)**: controls effective compression ratio
3. **Intake charge temperature** ($T_{in}$): sets the initial thermal state

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *State any three reasons why CAI combustion mode cannot cover the full engine operating range, making CAI/SI switching necessary.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Low-load misfire:** Dilute mixture fails to auto-ignite → SI required for idle and light loads.
2. **High-load ringing:** Excessive pressure-rise rate ($dP/d\theta >$ 15 bar/°CA) damages the engine → SI required for WOT.
3. **Cold-start and ambient limits:** Cold walls suppress cool-flame chemistry → SI required during warm-up.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, internal choice is provided. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop control architecture used for CAI combustion phasing. Describe the role of the CA50 estimator and the actuator signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the SI → CAI mode transition sequence in a multi-mode engine. What are the critical ECU actions, and what is the typical transient window? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Draw the closed-loop block diagram:

```
[CA50_target] → [+] → [e_50] → [PI/PID Controller] → [u_50] → [Actuators: VVT, EGR, Throttle] → [Engine] → [P_cyl(θ)] → [CA50_Estimator] → [CA50_measured] → [-] → back to summing junction
```

**Step 2 (2 marks):** *CA50 Estimator*: Computes the crank angle at which 50% of the fuel mass has been burned, from the in-cylinder pressure trace $P_{cyl}(\theta)$. The standard technique is the **Rassweiler-Withrow** method (apparent heat release) using the first law of thermodynamics:

$$\dfrac{dQ_B}{d\theta} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P \dfrac{dV}{d\theta} + \dfrac{1}{\gamma - 1} V \dfrac{dP}{d\theta}$$

Integrating this from IVC to EVO gives $Q_B(\theta)$, from which CA50 is interpolated.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** *Actuator signals*:
- **VVT (Variable Valve Timing) signal** → adjusts IVCT, controls effective $r_c$ and $T_{cyl}$
- **EGR valve duty cycle** → adjusts $x_{EGR}$
- **Throttle / wastegate position** → adjusts $P_{man}$ and $T_{in}$
- *(Spark timing is held OFF in pure CAI mode.)*

**Step 4 (1 mark):** *Controller type:* PI is sufficient for steady cruise; PID or gain-scheduled PI is used when transitioning across the load map.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the trigger — load drops into the CAI window, typically $L \in [2.0, 7.0]$ bar BMEP and $n > 1200$ rpm.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** List the ECU actions in order:
1. Increase NVO by retarding IVO (e.g., 60–80 °CA aTDC) and closing EVC early
2. Increase EGR to $x_{EGR} = 30$–$60\%$
3. Boost intake temperature to $T_{in} = 80$–$150$ °C (using EGR cooler bypass, intake heater, or blow-by)
4. Switch off the spark plug (spark = 0)
5. Monitor $P_{cyl}$ for 2–5 cycles until auto-ignition onset is detected

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Transient window:
- Valve-timing-based switching: **3–6 cycles** (≈ 90–180 ms at 2000 rpm)
- The first 1–2 cycles are often *partial burn* (CAI-like but unstable); from cycle 3 onward, combustion phasing stabilizes within ±3 °CA of target.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Hysteresis is enforced on the lower boundary to prevent SI ↔ CAI flapping.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the high-load ringing limit in CAI combustion, and identify the parameter that primarily governs it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare CAI and SI combustion modes with respect to (i) combustion-phasing control authority, (ii) load range, (iii) heat-release shape, and (iv) emissions signature. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the ringing limit condition:

$$\left(\dfrac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{max} = 10 \text{ to } 15\ \text{bar/°CA}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the governing equation from the first-law heat-release analysis for a premixed burn:

$$\dfrac{dP}{d\theta} = -\gamma \dfrac{P}{V} \dfrac{dV}{d\theta} + \dfrac{(\gamma - 1)}{V} \cdot \dot{Q}_B$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** At peak $dP/d\theta$ (just after TDC, where $dV/d\theta \approx 0$):

$$\left(\dfrac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{max} \approx \dfrac{(\gamma - 1)}{V_{TDC}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{B,\ max}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Identify the governing parameter: **the rate of premixed heat release $\dot{Q}_B$**, which is controlled by (a) the local equivalence ratio $\phi$, (b) the in-cylinder temperature, and (c) the **mass fraction burned per °CA** $\xi$. The dominant variable in practice is $\xi$ — set by EGR, IVCT, and mixture preparation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Comparison table (7 marks — 1.75 each row):

| Aspect | CAI Mode | SI Mode |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Combustion-phasing control authority | **High authority** via EGR + IVCT + $T_{in}$ (thermal/chemical) | Moderate authority via spark timing (kinetic) |
| (ii) Load range | Narrow island (2–7 bar BMEP) | Full range (0 to WOT) |
| (iii) Heat-release shape | Single smooth premixed peak | Two-stage: premixed kernel + propagation |
| (iv) Emissions | Ultra-low NOx, high HC/CO at low load | Higher NOx, lower HC/CO under control |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (avoid these in your exam):**
> 1. **Confusing CAI with HCCI** — they are related but *not identical*. CAI specifically uses **negative valve overlap (NVO) for residual trapping**, while classic HCCI uses a *high compression ratio*. If the examiner asks for the difference, do not write the same definition twice.
> 2. **Writing "EGR" without a percentage range** — for a 3-mark question, "EGR is used to control CAI" is **vague and incomplete**. You MUST add "typically 30–60%" or quote a numeric range.
> 3. **Forgetting the hysteresis** — the switching question has a *trap*: if you only describe one-way switching (SI → CAI), you lose 2 marks. Always discuss *both directions* and the *hysteresis band*.
> 4. **Skipping the transient cycles** — saying "the engine switches instantly" will be marked down. CAI engagement requires 3–6 cycles; mark this in your answer.
> 5. **Mixing up degrees aTDC with bTDC** — CA50 of +5 °CA and −5 °CA aTDC are completely different combustion events. Always specify the reference.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention CA50 control as the control objective** — almost every CAI control question ultimately boils down to: *we control combustion phasing (CA50) to maximize efficiency while respecting ringing and misfire limits.*

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ CAI = *premixed, distributed, kinetically controlled* auto-ignition near TDC, **not** flame propagation.
- ✅ CAI ≠ HCCI; the *defining* feature of CAI is **trapped residuals via Negative Valve Overlap (NVO)**.
- ✅ Three control knobs: **EGR, IVCT, intake temperature $T_{in}$** (in that order of authority).
- ✅ CA50 is the canonical control output; target = **+5 to +10 °CA aTDC**.
- ✅ CAI operating "island" is bounded by **misfire wall (low load)**, **ringing wall (high load)**, and **cold-start wall (temperature)**.
- ✅ CAI/SI switching is *bidirectional* with **hysteresis** of **0.5–1.0 bar BMEP**.
- ✅ SI → CAI engagement takes **3–6 cycles**; CAI → SI disengagement is faster (1–3 cycles).
- ✅ Ringing limit: $dP/d\theta \le 10\text{–}15$ bar/°CA. COV$_{IMEP} \le 5\%$ is the stability target.
- ✅ Closed-loop CAI control always uses a **CA50 estimator** (Rassweiler–Withrow from $P_{cyl}$).
- ✅ Spark is **OFF in pure CAI** and **ON in pure SI**; partial-burn hybrid is allowed during transition.
- ✅ Real-world examples: **Honda i-VTEC CAI, Nissan MR20DD, Mercedes DiesOtto, GM HCCI demonstrator**.
- ✅ Cold start always uses **SI** — CAI cannot auto-ignite a cold cylinder.
- ✅ The CAI efficiency gain is **5–15%** over SI in the part-load cruise window.
- ✅ Exam mantra: *"Premixed, multiple-site, thermally/chemically controlled, near TDC."* — if your definition includes these four phrases, you are safe for a 3-mark KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept and Working of Dual Fuel Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI) Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT752 - ADVANCED IC ENGINES AND COMBUSTION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Concept and Working of Dual Fuel Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI) Engine # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI)** is an advanced *dual-fuel, premixed low-temperature combustion (LTC)* strategy that decouples **fuel preparation (mixing)** from **combustion (ignition)** by exploiting the *reactivity gradient* between two fuels of widely differing auto-ignition qualities. A **low-reactivity (LR) fuel** (e.g., gasoline, ethanol, natural gas, or low-octane-number Primary Reference Fuel blends) is premixed homogeneously with the intake air-charge early in the cycle, while a **high-reactivity (HR) fuel** (e.g., diesel or biodiesel) is injected *directly into the cylinder* late during the compression stroke. The resulting in-cylinder mixture possesses a **spatially and temporally varying reactivity field** that promotes progressive, multi-stage auto-ignition, producing a controlled, low-rate heat release, low peak temperatures, and simultaneously suppressed $NO_x$ and soot formation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT752 Module 4):** RCCI bridges the gap between classical **HCCI** (high efficiency, low $NO_x$, but limited load and poor combustion phasing control) and **Conventional Diesel (CI)** (high load, high efficiency, but high $NO_x$ and soot). It is a **dual-fuel** strategy, but distinct from classical dual-fuel CI engines that rely on pilot diesel ignition of gaseous fuel jets. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Campfire Metaphor" Imagine lighting a long, slow campfire on a cold evening. You don't strike a single match and dump a bucket of kerosene on the wood (that would flash-explode). Instead, you first lay a thin trail of lighter fluid (the *high-reactivity fuel*) on a small region, and surround it with damp, slow-burning logs (the *low-reactivity premixed fuel*). You then ignite only the small high-reactivity zone, and the flame *spreads progressively* through the entire pile at a controlled, gentle rate. The room warms up without a violent explosion. - **Low-reactivity premixed fuel** → *the damp, slow-burning logs* (gasoline in the air-charge, mixed early). - **High-reactivity direct-injected fuel** → *the lighter fluid trail* (diesel injected late into the compression stroke). - **Auto-ignition front** → *the spreading flame front* (controlled by reactivity gradient, not a propagating spark). - **Low peak temperatures + low soot** → *no single hot zone, no rich diffusion flame pockets*. ## 1.3 Core Terminology in Bold (Standard Metrics & Constants) - **Premixed Ratio (PR):** Fraction of total fuel energy supplied by the *low-reactivity* premixed fuel. Typical range **50 % – 90 %**. - **Reactivity ($\mathcal{R}$):** Inverse of ignition delay; high for diesel, low for gasoline/ethanol. - **Low-Temperature Combustion (LTC):** Combustion regime with peak cylinder gas temperatures **below ~1900 K**, which suppresses thermal $NO_x$. - **Combustion Phasing (CA50):** Crank-angle position at which **50 %** of the total heat release has occurred. Controlled in RCCI to be near **8° – 12° ATDC** for optimum efficiency. - **Pressure Rise Rate (PRR):** $\frac{dP}{d\theta}$ in **bar/deg**; a key combustion-noise and structural-load parameter. RCCI aims to keep it **< 10 bar/deg**. - **Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP):** Output load parameter, **bar**; RCCI extends HCCI load range from ~5 bar IMEP to **> 15 bar IMEP**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Cue:** Always quote PR (Premixed Ratio) and CA50 in any RCCI numerical/derivation question. Examiners specifically test whether you understand the *energy-share* interpretation of dual-fuel operation. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind RCCI — Motivation | Conventional Strategy | Limitation | How RCCI Solves It | |---|---|---| | **SI (Spark Ignition)** | Throttled operation → pumping loss, low part-load efficiency | Unthrottled, fully premixed operation | | **CI (Diesel)** | Heterogeneous diffusion flame → high $NO_x$ & soot | Premixed charge + cool flame, soot-free & $NO_x$-lean | | **HCCI** | Combustion phasing uncontrolled; narrow load range | Reactivity stratification provides *spatial control* of ignition | | **Classical Dual-Fuel CI** | High $NO_x$, knock, incomplete premixing | Late HR injection, controlled reactivity gradient | ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle (Operational Logic) 1. **Intake Stroke (0° → 180° CA):** - Air + **low-reactivity fuel** (gasoline/ethanol/NG) is inducted through the manifold. - Mixing is *homogeneous* because the fuel is in vapor form and is given the full intake-stroke duration. - The reactivity of the in-cylinder charge is **deliberately too low** to auto-ignite at top dead centre (TDC). 2. **Compression Stroke (180° → ~360° CA, but injection occurs at 340°–350° CA BTDC):** - Homogeneous LR + air mixture is compressed. - Near the **end of compression**, a small, precisely metered quantity of **high-reactivity diesel** is injected *directly* into the homogeneous mixture. - The HR fuel jet forms **localized high-reactivity pockets** (rich reactivity zones) within the surrounding low-reactivity bulk. 3. **Ignition & Combustion:** - At TDC, the high-reactivity pockets **auto-ignite first** because their *ignition delay* has elapsed. - Heat released from these pockets *raises the local temperature* of the surrounding low-reactivity mixture, triggering a cascading, **progressive auto-ignition** front. - Combustion duration is **spread over 20°–60° CA** (vs. 5°–10° in conventional diesel), keeping $\frac{dP}{d\theta}$ low. 4. **Expansion & Power Stroke:** - Long, controlled heat release converts chemical energy to work with **diesel-like thermal efficiency (η ≈ 40 % – 43 %)**. 5. **Exhaust:** - Low peak cylinder temperatures (≤ 1900 K) → negligible **thermal $NO_x$**. - Homogeneous premixing → negligible **fuel-rich diffusion regions** → negligible **soot**. ## 2.3 Key Reactive Stratification Concept The **reactivity field** inside the cylinder is mathematically characterised by the **local equivalence ratio** $\phi(\vec{x}, \theta)$ *and* the **local octane number (or cetane number)** of the fuel mixture at that point. $$ \mathcal{R}(\vec{x},\theta) = f\!\left[\phi(\vec{x},\theta),\; ON(\vec{x},\theta),\; T(\vec{x},\theta)\right] $$ where $\mathcal{R}$ is the local reactivity, $ON$ is the local octane number (lower ON = more reactive = diesel-like), and $T$ is local temperature. > [!NOTE] > **Reactivity Stratification vs Charge Stratification:** In *stratified-charge SI* engines, only $\phi$ varies spatially; the fuel type is the same. In **RCCI**, the *fuel chemistry itself* varies spatially, giving an **additional degree of freedom** to control ignition timing. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Standard Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Premixed Ratio (energy-based) | $PR$ | $PR = \dfrac{E_{LR}}{E_{LR}+E_{HR}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{LR} \cdot LHV_{LR}}{\dot{m}_{LR}\cdot LHV_{LR} + \dot{m}_{HR}\cdot LHV_{HR}}$ | — (dimensionless) | $0.5 \le PR \le 0.9$ typical | | 2 | Premixed Ratio (mass-based) | $PR_m$ | $PR_m = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{LR}}{\dot{m}_{LR}+\dot{m}_{HR}}$ | — | Used when LHV is constant | | 3 | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | $IMEP$ | $IMEP = \dfrac{W_{ind}}{V_d}$ | **bar** | $W_{ind}$ = indicated work per cycle | | 4 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{it}$ | $\eta_{it} = \dfrac{IMEP \cdot V_d}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_f}$ | — | Compare with diesel ≈ 0.42 | | 5 | Pressure Rise Rate | $PRR$ | $PRR = \dfrac{dP}{d\theta}$ | **bar/deg CA** | Keep $\le 10$ for low noise | | 6 | Cumulative Heat Release | $Q(\theta)$ | From 1st-law: $Q = m \cdot c_v \cdot dT + P \cdot dV$ | **J** | Integrated to find CA50 | | 7 | Mass Burn Fraction | $x_b$ | $x_b(\theta) = \dfrac{Q(\theta)}{Q_{total}}$ | — | CA50 = θ where $x_b = 0.5$ | | 8 | Local Reactivity Index | $\mathcal{R}$ | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_{ig}(\phi,T,ON)}$ | **s⁻¹** | Inverse of ignition delay | | 9 | EGR Tolerance | $r_{EGR}$ | $r_{EGR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{EGR}}{\dot{m}_{air}+\dot{m}_{EGR}}$ | — | RCCI may use **0 – 40 %** | | 10 | Premixed Equivalence Ratio | $\phi_{LR}$ | $\phi_{LR} = \dfrac{(F/A)_{LR}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | — | Often operated lean, $\phi_{LR} < 0.7$ | | 11 | Global Equivalence Ratio | $\phi_{glob}$ | $\phi_{glob} = \phi_{LR} + \phi_{HR,local}$ | — | Spatially varying | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pitfall Alert:** Never write the premixed ratio formula with the vertical bar for "absolute value" inside markdown tables — it breaks the table. Always use the \vert LaTeX delimiter if modular signs are required. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of RCCI - **Heavy-duty trucks and long-haul applications** — leverages existing diesel infrastructure (HR fuel) and natural gas (LR fuel) to cut $CO_2$ and $NO_x$ simultaneously. - **Renewable-fuel integration** — ethanol, methanol, and bio-gasoline can serve as LR fuel, allowing the engine to be partly fuelled by renewables. - **Military and aerospace** — multi-fuel capability (JP-8, gasoline, kerosene blends) without engine reconfiguration. - **Marine auxiliary engines** — to meet **IMO Tier III** $NO_x$ limits without SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) units. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Premixed Ratio from First Principles (Energy Balance) **Statement:** For a dual-fuel RCCI engine, derive the energy-based **Premixed Ratio (PR)** in terms of mass flow rates and lower heating values of the two fuels. ### Step 1 — Define the Energy Contributions Let $\dot{m}_{LR}$ and $\dot{m}_{HR}$ be the mass flow rates (kg/s) of the low-reactivity and high-reactivity fuels respectively, and $LHV_{LR}$ and $LHV_{HR}$ their lower heating values (J/kg). The total chemical energy supplied per unit time (total fuel power input) is: $$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{LR} \cdot LHV_{LR} + \dot{m}_{HR} \cdot LHV_{HR} $$ ### Step 2 — Define the Premixed Ratio The **Premixed Ratio (PR)** is the *fraction of total fuel energy* supplied by the low-reactivity premixed fuel: $$ PR = \frac{\dot{m}_{LR} \cdot LHV_{LR}}{\dot{m}_{LR} \cdot LHV_{LR} + \dot{m}_{HR} \cdot LHV_{HR}} $$ ### Step 3 — Simplify Using a Common Mass Reference Define a *total equivalent fuel mass* $M = \dot{m}_{LR} + \dot{m}_{HR}$ and a *mass split fraction* $x = \dot{m}_{LR}/M$. Then: $$ \dot{m}_{LR} = xM,\quad \dot{m}_{HR} = (1-x)M $$ Substituting: $$ PR = \frac{x \cdot LHV_{LR}}{x \cdot LHV_{LR} + (1-x) \cdot LHV_{HR}} $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $x$ in Terms of $PR$ (Useful Inverse Form) Rearranging: $$ PR \cdot \left[ x \cdot LHV_{LR} + (1-x) \cdot LHV_{HR} \right] = x \cdot LHV_{LR} $$ $$ PR \cdot x \cdot LHV_{LR} + PR \cdot (1-x) \cdot LHV_{HR} = x \cdot LHV_{LR} $$ $$ x \cdot \left( PR \cdot LHV_{LR} - LHV_{LR} \right) = -PR \cdot (1-x) \cdot LHV_{HR} $$ After algebraic collection: $$ x = \frac{PR \cdot LHV_{HR}}{LHV_{LR} + PR \cdot (LHV_{HR} - LHV_{LR})} $$ > [!NOTE] > This inverse form is what a KTU examiner expects when the question says "If PR = 0.7 and the two fuels are gasoline ($LHV$ = 44 MJ/kg) and diesel ($LHV$ = 43 MJ/kg), find the **mass fraction** of gasoline in the total fuel supply." ### Step 5 — Numerical Sanity Check Take **gasoline** ($LHV_{LR} = 44$ MJ/kg) and **diesel** ($LHV_{HR} = 43$ MJ/kg), with $PR = 0.7$. Then: $$ x = \frac{0.7 \times 43}{44 + 0.7 \times (43 - 44)} = \frac{30.1}{44 - 0.7} = \frac{30.1}{43.3} \approx 0.695 $$ So the **mass fraction** of gasoline ≈ **69.5 %**, slightly less than the energy fraction of 70 %. This is the correct physical trend: since the LHVs are nearly equal, mass and energy fractions are close. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Indicated Thermal Efficiency of an RCCI Engine **Statement:** Show that the indicated thermal efficiency of an RCCI engine can be expressed in terms of the **Premixed Ratio (PR)** and the indicated thermal efficiencies of the two sub-modes. ### Step 1 — Define Sub-Efficiencies Let $\eta_{LR}$ be the efficiency of the engine when operating *purely on the low-reactivity premixed fuel* (hypothetical HCCI-like mode), and $\eta_{HR}$ the efficiency *purely on the high-reactivity direct-injected fuel* (conventional diesel-like mode). The overall efficiency is **not** simply a linear blend because the two fuels interact chemically inside the cylinder; however, a **first-order weighted estimate** is: $$ \eta_{RCCI} \approx PR \cdot \eta_{LR} + (1-PR) \cdot \eta_{HR} $$ ### Step 2 — Justification - The PR fraction of energy enters as a premixed charge (low loss, near-HCCI behaviour → high $\eta_{LR}$). - The $(1-PR)$ fraction is diesel-like diffusion combustion (also high $\eta_{HR}$). - Because both sub-modes can achieve η ≈ 0.40, the *blend* is also ≈ 0.40. ### Step 3 — Numerical Example Let $PR = 0.75$, $\eta_{LR} = 0.40$ (HCCI-like), $\eta_{HR} = 0.42$ (diesel-like). Then: $$ \eta_{RCCI} \approx 0.75 \times 0.40 + 0.25 \times 0.42 = 0.300 + 0.105 = 0.405 \;\; (\text{i.e. } 40.5\%) $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parametric Effects on RCCI Performance | Parameter ↓ / Response → | CA50 (Phase) | PRR ($\frac{dP}{d\theta}$) | $NO_x$ | Soot | HC/CO | Load Range | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **↑ Premixed Ratio (PR)** | Retards | Decreases | **↓ Strongly** | **↓ Strongly** | ↑ (low load) | Slight ↓ | | **↑ HR Injection Timing (earlier)** | Advances | Increases | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | Slight ↑ | | **↑ EGR Rate** | Retards | Decreases | ↓ | ↑ slightly | ↑ | ↓ | | **↑ Compression Ratio** | Advances | Increases | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | | **↑ Intake Pressure (Boost)** | Slight advance | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | **↑↑ Strongly** | | **↑ Low-Reactivity Fuel Octane** | Retards | Decreases | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Parametric Trade-off:** Students often claim "increasing EGR reduces $NO_x$ with no side-effects." In RCCI, excessive EGR pushes combustion into the *quench zone*, sharply **raising HC and CO** at low loads. Always mention the **trade-off** in the answer. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RCCI Engine System ```mermaid flowchart TB A[LR Fuel Tank

Each block maps a functional subsystem or combustion phase without relying on physical shape geometry, which is not natively supported in Mermaid. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **"Define Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI) engine. Highlight how it differs from a conventional dual-fuel engine."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** RCCI is a dual-fuel low-temperature combustion strategy in which a low-reactivity fuel is premixed homogeneously with air, and a high-reactivity fuel is directly injected late during compression. Combustion proceeds via *progressive auto-ignition* controlled by the in-cylinder *reactivity gradient*. - **Difference from conventional dual-fuel (1.5 marks):** In conventional dual-fuel CI, a *gaseous* LR fuel is ignited by a *pilot diesel spray* acting as a torch — combustion is essentially *diffusion flame* of diesel. In RCCI, the LR fuel is *fully premixed and auto-ignites* as a cool, distributed flame front. Hence RCCI produces *much lower $NO_x$ and soot* and is not limited by pilot-ignition stability. --- ### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **"What is Premixed Ratio (PR) in an RCCI engine? State its typical operating range and its effect on $NO_x$ and Pressure Rise Rate (PRR)."** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Premixed Ratio is the fraction of total fuel energy supplied by the low-reactivity premixed fuel: $PR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{LR} \cdot LHV_{LR}}{\dot{m}_{LR}\cdot LHV_{LR} + \dot{m}_{HR} \cdot LHV_{HR}}$ - **Typical Range (1 mark):** PR = **0.5 to 0.9** (50 % – 90 %) in published RCCI literature. - **Effects (1 mark):** Increasing PR → lowers peak cylinder temperature → **reduces $NO_x$**; also **reduces PRR** (smoother combustion, lower noise). --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, with two sub-parts typically worth (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]** **"With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of a Reactivity Controlled Compression Ignition (RCCI) engine. Discuss the role of Premixed Ratio (PR) and HR injection timing on combustion phasing (CA50) and Pressure Rise Rate (PRR)."** *(14 marks)* #### Part (a) — Working of RCCI Engine *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Air-Fuel Preparation (2 marks):** A **low-reactivity (LR) fuel** — typically gasoline, ethanol, or natural gas — is inducted through a **port fuel injector** and mixed homogeneously with intake air. The mixture is *too unreactive* to auto-ignite at the end of the compression stroke under normal conditions. **2. HR Injection (2 marks):** Near the end of the compression stroke (typically 20° – 60° BTDC), a small quantity of **high-reactivity (HR) fuel** — typically diesel — is **directly injected** into the homogeneous LR + air charge via a high-pressure common-rail injector. The HR fuel jet creates **localized regions of high reactivity** within the bulk premixed charge. **3. Progressive Auto-Ignition (2 marks):** At TDC, the high-reactivity regions *auto-ignite first*. The heat released raises the local temperature of the surrounding low-reactivity mixture, causing it to auto-ignite in a *cascading front*. Combustion is therefore *progressive, distributed, and controlled by the reactivity gradient* — not by a spark or a single diffusion flame. **4. Power and Emissions (1 mark):** The result is a *long heat-release duration* (20°–60° CA), low $\frac{dP}{d\theta}$, low peak temperature (≤ 1900 K), and hence near-zero thermal $NO_x$ and soot. Brake thermal efficiency approaches diesel-level (40–43 %). #### Part (b) — Role of PR and HR Injection Timing *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Effect of PR on CA50 (2.5 marks):** - Higher PR ⇒ more unreactive fuel in the bulk ⇒ *longer* effective ignition delay ⇒ combustion is **retarded** (CA50 moves to later crank angle). - Lower PR (more HR) ⇒ shorter ignition delay ⇒ CA50 **advances** (earlier combustion). - *Control implication:* PR is the **primary** knob to **regulate CA50** in RCCI. **2. Effect of PR on PRR (2 marks):** - Higher PR → more uniform, distributed heat release → **lower PRR** (quieter, structurally safer). - Lower PR (more HR) → concentrated, near-diesel-like heat release → **higher PRR** (risk of engine knock / structural damage). **3. Effect of HR Injection Timing (2.5 marks):** - **Earlier HR injection** (more mixing time) → HR fuel premixes with LR fuel → reactivity gradient *erodes* → combustion becomes more HCCI-like, **PRR decreases**, but **CA50 may advance** uncontrollably. - **Later HR injection** → less mixing → sharper reactivity gradient → combustion more *diffusion-controlled* → **PRR increases**, **$NO_x$ rises slightly**, but **CA50 can be retarded** for high-load operation. **Summary Table (essential to draw in the exam):** | Parameter | CA50 | PRR | $NO_x$ | |---|---|---|---| | ↑ PR | Retard | ↓ | ↓ | | ↑ HR timing (earlier) | Variable | ↓ | ↓ | | ↓ PR | Advance | ↑ | ↑ | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper — Alternative Choice]** **"Derive the energy-based Premixed Ratio (PR) of a dual-fuel RCCI engine in terms of mass flow rates and lower heating values. A gasoline-diesel RCCI engine operates with PR = 0.75 using gasoline ($LHV$ = 44 MJ/kg) and diesel ($LHV$ = 43 MJ/kg). If the total fuel energy input is 100 kW, compute the mass flow rates of gasoline and diesel. Discuss the major challenges in commercialising RCCI engines."** *(14 marks)* #### Part (a) — Derivation and Numerical Computation *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Derivation of PR (3.5 marks):** The total chemical power supplied to the engine is the sum of the energy contributions of the two fuels: $$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot LHV_{gas} + \dot{m}_{die} \cdot LHV_{die} $$ The Premixed Ratio is the fraction of this total supplied by the *low-reactivity* (gasoline) fuel: $$ PR = \frac{\dot{m}_{gas} \cdot LHV_{gas}}{\dot{m}_{gas} \cdot LHV_{gas} + \dot{m}_{die} \cdot LHV_{die}} $$ **2. Apply the Given Numerical Values (2 marks):** Given: - $PR = 0.75$ - $LHV_{gas} = 44$ MJ/kg = 44,000 kJ/kg - $LHV_{die} = 43$ MJ/kg = 43,000 kJ/kg - $\dot{Q}_{in} = 100$ kW From the PR equation: $$ 0.75 = \frac{\dot{m}_{gas} \cdot 44{,}000}{\dot{m}_{gas} \cdot 44{,}000 + \dot{m}_{die} \cdot 43{,}000} $$ Let $\dot{m}_{gas} = x$. Then $\dot{m}_{die} \cdot 43{,}000 = \dfrac{0.25}{0.75} \cdot \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot 44{,}000$. So: $$ \dot{m}_{die} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot \frac{44{,}000}{43{,}000} = \frac{44}{129} \cdot \dot{m}_{gas} \approx 0.341 \, \dot{m}_{gas} $$ Total energy: $$ 100 = x \cdot 44 + 0.341 x \cdot 43 = 44x + 14.66x = 58.66\,x $$ $$ x = \frac{100}{58.66} \approx 1.705 \text{ kg/s} $$ So $\dot{m}_{gas} \approx 1.705$ kg/s, and $\dot{m}_{die} \approx 0.581$ kg/s. **3. Result (0.5 mark):** - $\dot{m}_{gasoline} \approx 1.705$ kg/s - $\dot{m}_{diesel} \approx 0.581$ kg/s - Energy contribution of gasoline = 0.75 × 100 = **75 kW** - Energy contribution of diesel = 0.25 × 100 = **25 kW** #### Part (b) — Major Challenges in Commercialising RCCI Engines *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** 1. **Combustion Phasing Control (2 marks):** CA50 is highly sensitive to PR, HR injection timing, EGR, intake temperature, fuel octane/cetane number, and compression ratio. Even ±2° CA variation significantly alters efficiency and emissions. Real-time closed-loop control is still an active research problem. 2. **Cold-Start and Low-Load Operation (2 marks):** At low load, the cylinder wall temperature is insufficient to trigger the cool-flame chemistry of LR fuels, causing **unburned HC and CO** spikes. Cold-start is even more challenging because the LR fuel does not vaporise readily. 3. **High Pressure Rise Rate (PRR) at High Load (1.5 marks):** When PR is reduced to raise load, the HR fuel quantity increases and combustion becomes more *diffusion-like*, raising $\frac{dP}{d\theta}$ beyond acceptable mechanical-noise and structural limits. 4. **Fuel Flexibility and On-Board Storage (1 mark):** Maintaining *two* fuel systems (LR port injection + HR direct injection) and *two* fuel tanks increases vehicle cost, packaging, and refuelling complexity. 5. **Aftertreatment Trade-offs (0.5 marks):** Although $NO_x$ and soot are inherently low, the elevated HC and CO at low load may require a **three-way catalyst** or **oxidation catalyst**, increasing cost. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Hotspots in RCCI Questions:** > 1. **Confusing "Premixed Ratio" with "Equivalence Ratio":** PR is an *energy-share* parameter, NOT a fuel-air ratio. Do not write $PR = \dot{m}_f/\dot{m}_{air}$. > 2. **Confusing "Reactivity" with "Reactivity Ratio":** Reactivity is the inverse of ignition delay, a property of the local fuel-air mixture. Reactivity *gradient* is what enables RCCI. > 3. **Forgetting the LHV of the LR fuel in PR calculation:** Many students use mass fraction only. The energy-based PR requires both mass flow AND LHV. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you use mass fraction when energy fraction is asked. > 4. **Drawing a *physical* engine schematic when a *block* diagram is asked:** KTU Module 4 expects conceptual/functional diagrams, not detailed mechanical drawings. > 5. **Claiming "RCCI has zero $NO_x$":** RCCI *suppresses* thermal $NO_x$ by lowering peak temperature, but some $NO_x$ is still produced. Say "**near-zero $NO_x$**" or "**very low $NO_x$**." --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **RCCI = Dual-fuel + Reactivity Stratification + Premixed LTC.** *(high-yield definition)* - **LR fuel** = gasoline / ethanol / natural gas — port-injected, premixed, *low* reactivity. - **HR fuel** = diesel / biodiesel — direct-injected late in compression, *high* reactivity. - **Premixed Ratio (PR)** = $\dfrac{\dot{m}_{LR} \cdot LHV_{LR}}{\dot{m}_{LR} LHV_{LR} + \dot{m}_{HR} LHV_{HR}}$, typical range **0.5 – 0.9**. - **CA50** is controlled primarily by **PR** and **HR injection timing**; aim for **8° – 12° ATDC**. - **PRR** must be kept **< 10 bar/deg** to avoid combustion noise. - Peak cylinder temperature ≤ **1900 K** → near-zero **thermal $NO_x$** and negligible **soot**. - Higher PR → **smoother combustion, lower PRR, lower $NO_x$, higher HC/CO at low load**. - Higher EGR → retarding effect, but **increases HC/CO** at low load. - **RCCI = HCCI (efficiency, low $NO_x$) + CI (load capability)**. - **RCCI ≠ classical dual-fuel CI:** the latter is pilot-ignited; RCCI is auto-ignited. - **Major challenges:** combustion phasing control, cold-start, HC/CO at low load, dual-fuel system packaging. - **Real-world relevance:** heavy-duty trucks, marine auxiliaries, multi-fuel military vehicles, IMO Tier III compliance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always close any RCCI answer with the **emission trade-off triangle** statement: *"RCCI achieves simultaneous low $NO_x$ and low soot at the cost of elevated HC/CO at low loads, and combustion-phasing control is the principal engineering challenge."* Examiners treat this line as a **full-mark discriminator**.
